右台仙馆笔记 - Notes from the Immortals' Lodge at Youtai

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    《右台仙馆笔记》 清 俞樾

    　　●序余自己卯夏姚夫人卒，精神意兴日就阑衰，著述之事殆将辍笔矣。其年冬，葬夫人于钱唐之右台山，余亦自营生圹于其左。旋于其旁买得隙地一区，筑屋三间，竹篱环之，杂莳花木，颜之曰“右台仙馆”。余至湖上，或居“俞楼”，或居斯馆，谢绝冠盖，昵就松楸，人外之游，其在斯乎！余吴下有“曲园”，即有《曲园杂纂》五十卷；湖上有“俞楼”，即有《俞楼杂纂》五十卷，“右台仙馆”安得无书？而精力衰，不能复有撰述，乃以所著笔记归之。笔记者，杂记平时所见所闻，盖《搜神》、《述异》之类；不足，则又征之于人。嗟乎！不古训之是式，而惟怪之欲闻，余之志荒矣。此其所以为“右台仙馆”之书欤！曲园居士自记。

    Explanation

    1

    Here is the final, polished translation of the source text:

    The Right Terrace Immortal Pavilion Notes
    By Yu Yue (俞樾)

    Preface
    Since the summer of the year my wife Yao passed away, my spirit and interest have gradually declined, and I have nearly ceased writing. That autumn, I buried my wife at Right Terrace Mountain in Qiantang, and I also prepared my own grave on the left side of hers. Soon after, I purchased a plot of land beside it, built three rooms, surrounded them with bamboo fences, and planted a variety of flowers and trees. I named it the "Right Terrace Immortal Pavilion." When I come to the lakeside, I sometimes stay at the "Yu Pavilion" or at this pavilion, shunning the company of officials and instead spending time near the cypress trees, finding solace in the company of the departed. Is this not the essence of life beyond the mundane? In Wuxia, I have the "Curve Garden," and there are "Miscellaneous Writings of the Curve Garden" in fifty volumes; on the lake, I have the "Yu Pavilion," and there are "Miscellaneous Writings of the Yu Pavilion" in fifty volumes. How could the "Right Terrace Immortal Pavilion" lack books? Yet, my mental and physical strength has waned, and I can no longer write extensively. Thus, I have gathered my notes here. The note-taker records my observations and hearsay from daily life, much like the "In Search of the Supernatural" and "Records of the Unusual"; when they fall short, I also seek them from others. Alas! Not adhering to ancient teachings as a model, but desiring to hear the strange, my thoughts have become scattered. Is this not the reason for the writings of the "Right Terrace Immortal Pavilion"? Buddhist Hermit of the Curve Garden, self-recorded.

    2

    附录《征求异闻启》并小诗二首：余今岁行年六十矣，学问之道，日就荒芜；著述之事，行将废辍。书生结习，未能尽忘，姑记旧闻，以销暇日。而所闻所见，必由集腋而成；予取予求，窃有乞邻之意。伏望儒林丈人、高斋学士，各举怪怪奇奇之事，为我原原本本而书，寄来“春在草堂”，助作秋灯丛话。约以十事为率，如其多则更佳。先将二绝为媒，幸勿置之不答。

    Explanation

    2

    Appendix: "Letter Soliciting Strange and Unusual Events" and Two Short Poems:

    This year, I have reached the age of sixty. The path of learning has gradually become neglected, and the work of writing is on the verge of being abandoned. The habits of a scholar cannot be completely forgotten, so I will temporarily record old stories to pass the idle hours. What I hear and see must be gathered from various sources; I will seek what is needed, with the intention of borrowing from neighbors. I respectfully request the learned men of the scholarly world and the distinguished scholars to each report strange and unusual events, providing a detailed account for me. Send these to "Spring in the Thatched Hut" to help compile "Autumn Lamp Collection of Stories." Aim for ten items as a guideline; if more, it would be even better. First, send two short poems as a prelude, please do not ignore it.

    3

    衰不复事丹铅，六十原非亲学年。正似东坡老无事，听人说鬼便欣然。郭冲五事太寥寥，戏学姚崇十事要。不论搜神兼志怪，妄言亦可慰无聊。

    　　●卷一冯孝子，佚其名，太仓州之老闸镇人。少孤贫，佣耕以养母。乱后无田可耕，乃行乞于市，得钱则市酒肉以进，而歌俚曲以侑之。同治六年，母卒，乞得义冢地，并其父柩合葬之。日则仍出行乞，夕即于墓旁宿焉。每日必携数石以归，环墓成垣，自结草庐，寝处其下。后数年，无病卒。乡人即葬之于其所庐处。知州方公传书立碣表之，曰“冯孝子墓”。《春秋》二百四十年，人材极盛，而开卷即载颍考叔事，表纯孝也。余研经余暇，偶掇拾见闻，成斯笔记，而首以冯孝子事，亦庶几左氏之义夫。

    Explanation

    3

    The Decline of Learning and the Story of Feng the Filial Son

    In old age, one no longer engages in scholarly pursuits; at sixty, it is not a year for close study. Just like Su Dongpo, who, in his old age, has nothing to do and gladly listens to ghost stories. The five events related to Guo Chong are too few, but the ten events related to Yao Chong, learned playfully, are crucial. Regardless of seeking the divine or recording strange events, idle talk can also comfort boredom.

    ● Chapter One: Feng the Filial Son, whose name is unknown, was from the old checkpoint town of Taicang. He lost his father at a young age and was poor. He worked as a hired farmer to support his mother. After the chaos, there was no land to farm, so he began begging in the market. When he received money, he would buy wine and meat to offer to his mother and sing vulgar songs to accompany her. In the sixth year of Tongzhi, his mother passed away. He begged for a plot of land and buried both her and his father's coffin together. During the day, he continued to beg, and at night, he slept beside the grave. Every day, he would carry several stones back and build a wall around the grave, constructing a thatched hut where he would sleep. After several years, he died without illness. The villagers buried him where he had lived. The prefectural governor Fang inscribed a tablet to mark his grave, calling it "The Tomb of Feng the Filial Son." The Spring and Autumn Annals cover 240 years, a period of great talent, yet the first thing recorded is the story of Ying Kaoshu, highlighting pure filial piety. In my spare time from studying the classics, I occasionally gathered what I had heard and seen, writing these notes. I began with the story of Feng the Filial Son, which also aligns with the spirit of the Zuo Zhuan.

    Footnotes:

    1. 衰不复事丹铅 - "衰" refers to someone in a declining state, possibly old age. "不复事丹铅" means "no longer engages in scholarly pursuits."
    2. 东坡 - Su Dongpo (Su Shi), a famous poet and statesman from the Song Dynasty.
    3. 郭冲五事 - Five events related to Guo Chong, a historical figure, though specific details are not widely known.
    4. 姚崇十事 - Ten events related to Yao Chong, a famous statesman from the Tang Dynasty, known for his political reforms and achievements.
    5. 颍考叔 - Ying Kaoshu, a figure from the Spring and Autumn Annals known for his filial piety.
    6. 左氏 - The Zuo Zhuan, a historical and literary commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals.
    4

    木工陶某，金陵人。年甫四岁，值粤贼陷城，父为所掳，母子相依，幸而无恙。大乱既平，仍设肆于城中。至同治甲戌，陶年二十五矣。忽有老翁携瞽妇至门乞食，与之钱不去，熟视陶曰：“尔非陶姓乳名某者乎？”陶问何以知我，翁曰：“尔乃吾子也。”陶乎母出视，果其父，因扶之入，拜问由来。则始而被掳北行，后又流转至川、陕，今自陕归也。解腰缠出银数锭，皆累年贸易所得，恐途中遇盗贼，伪为窭人耳；瞽妇则其续娶者也。因大欢慰，亲党毕贺。夫兵乱以来，父子夫妻离散者多矣，此家乃得完聚，意其有阴德乎？

    Explanation

    4

    A woodworker named Tao Mou, from Jinling. At the age of four, he encountered the Taiping rebels who captured the city, and his father was taken captive. His mother and son relied on each other, fortunately remaining unharmed. After the great turmoil subsided, he continued to set up his shop in the city. By the year Jia Xu of the Tongzhi era, Tao was twenty-five years old. Suddenly, an old man with a blind woman came to the door begging for food. After giving them money, they did not leave. The old man looked at Tao closely and said, "Are you not Tao Mou, the name given at birth?" Tao asked how he knew him, and the old man replied, "You are my son." Tao called his mother out to see, and indeed, it was his father. They helped him inside, bowed, and asked about his origins. He explained that he had been captured and taken north, later traveling to Sichuan and Shaanxi, and now returning from Shaanxi. He unfastened his belt and took out several pieces of silver, all accumulated from years of trade, fearing encounters with bandits on the road, and pretending to be poor. The blind woman was his second wife. They were greatly happy and relieved, and all relatives and friends congratulated them. Since the war and chaos began, many fathers, sons, and couples have been separated, but this family was able to reunite. Perhaps they have done some good deeds in secret.

    5

    吾兄福宁太守，官厦门同知时，署附近有僧寺曰“碧山岩寺”，僧曰碧禅，能诗画，貌亦恂恂，吐属风雅。署中潘少梅、蔡瑜卿诸君，皆喜与之游。一日，忽具牒请还俗。吾兄素不喜僧，骂之曰：“今日既欲还俗，当初何必出家邪？”呵叱而遣之。碧禅故与署中吏魁某甲有隙，疑其谮于官也。是晚甲自署归其家，经由一隘巷，闻碧禅呼其名，回顾之，则已事刂刃于胸出于背。某甲犹能带刃狂奔，至家，告家人曰：“碧禅杀我。”言已，倒地而绝。事闻于官，吾兄命捕碧禅，不得。数月后，有人于署后山上见碧禅，则已有发辫矣。

    Explanation

    5

    My Elder Brother, the Prefect of Fuzhou, when serving as the Deputy Magistrate of Xiamen, had a Buddhist temple called "Biyanshan Rock Temple" near his official residence. The monk there was named Bi Chan, who was skilled in poetry and painting, and had a modest and refined appearance with elegant speech. Officials such as Pan Shaomei and Cai Yuqing in the government office all enjoyed his company. One day, he suddenly presented a petition requesting to return to secular life. My elder brother, who had never been fond of monks, scolded him and said, "If you wish to return to the world today, why did you become a monk in the first place?" He then dismissed him. Bi Chan had a grudge against a certain official, Mr. A, in the government office and suspected that Mr. A had slandered him to the official. That evening, as Mr. A returned home from the government office, passing through a narrow alley, he heard the sound of Bi Chan calling his name and turned back. At that moment, he was stabbed through the chest, the blade coming out from his back. Despite the injury, Mr. A was still able to run wildly, returning home and telling his family, "Bi Chan killed me." After saying this, he fell to the ground and died. The incident was reported to the official, and my elder brother ordered the capture of Bi Chan, but he could not be found. Several months later, someone saw Bi Chan on the hill behind the government office, and he already had his hair braided.

    6

    呼曰：“碧禅在此！”碧禅飞一足，蹴其人仆地，而自从山后跃而下，捷如飞鸟。望之，见其帽坠于地，辫乃缀于帽，非真发也。嗣后署中一幕友王姓者，房中每夜窗户不开，而频有所失。其所失物则皆得之署后山穴中，疑碧禅所为也。阅时既久，捕事益懈，而王姓者失物亦愈稀。已而吾兄调福防同知以去，继之者为龚司马，知此事，命捕之。有金姓者，侦知其在妓楼，纠众以往。碧禅见众至，自楼窗跃至平地，复从平地跃至屋上，顷刻绝迹。而龚司马于内室中得一书云：“吾与汝无仇，汝捕吾何也？然汝亦欲升官耳，吾不汝怨。汝他日还省，当访我于鼓山。”大骇，不知所自来，疑其人盖古剑客之流也。乃不复言捕碧禅。及任满还省垣，竟不敢至鼓山，而碧禅始终不可得。

    Explanation

    6

    Shouting out, "Blue Chan is here!" Blue Chan flew one leg, kicked the person to the ground, and then leaped down from behind the mountain, swift as a flying bird. Looking at him, they saw his hat had fallen to the ground, and the braid was attached to the hat, not real hair. Subsequently, a private secretary named Wang in the office, every night in his room, the windows were not opened, and he frequently lost things. The lost items were all found in the cave behind the office building, and it was suspected that it was done by the Blue Chan. After a long time, the pursuit became more lax, and the lost items by Wang also became less frequent. Soon after, my brother was transferred to the position of Fu Fang Tongzhi and left, and the one who followed him was General Gong, who knew about this matter and ordered the capture of the Blue Chan. A person surnamed Jin, upon learning that the Blue Chan was in a brothel, gathered a crowd to go there. The Blue Chan, seeing the crowd approaching, leaped from the window to the ground, and then from the ground to the roof, disappearing in an instant. Meanwhile, General Gong found a letter in the inner room that said, "I have no enmity with you, why do you want to capture me? But you also want to advance in your career, I do not resent you. When you return to the provincial capital in the future, you should visit me at Drum Mountain." Greatly shocked, he did not know where it came from, suspecting that the person was probably a type of ancient swordsmen. He then no longer spoke of capturing the Blue Chan. Upon the end of his term and returning to the provincial capital, he dared not go to Drum Mountain, and the Blue Chan could never be found.

    7

    邢阿金，农家女也。幼随其母往来大家，故有大家风范。修眉纤趾，望之楚楚，烹饪缝纫，并皆精妙。诵唐人小诗，略能上口。年及笄，嫁田舍儿，性粗暴，以其荏弱不任井臼，虐遇之。阿金性柔和，惟背人啜泣而已。其母闻之大戚，以钱赎之归。又嫁一富家子，年少美丰仪。阿金自幸以为得佳婿也，不意其佻达无度，得新弃旧，旋即仳离。乃嫁一官人为妾，又不容于大妇而归。于是阿金年亦二十外矣。有黄大令者年逾周甲，得之为室，甚嬖之。

    Explanation

    7

    Xing A Jin was a farmer's daughter. As a child, she followed her mother to visit wealthy families, and thus she developed the manner of a large family. With beautiful eyebrows and slender toes, she appeared graceful and charming. She excelled in cooking and sewing, and she could almost recite small Tang dynasty poems fluently. At the age of coming-of-age, she married a farmer's son, who was rough and brutal, mistreating her because she was weak and unable to perform household chores. A Jin was gentle in nature, only crying in private. Her mother, upon hearing of this, was greatly distressed and redeemed her with money, bringing her back home. She was then married to a young man from a wealthy family, who was handsome and well-groomed. A Jin felt fortunate to have found a good husband, but did not expect him to be frivolous and unfaithful, abandoning her for a new lover, and soon they separated. She then became a concubine to an official, but was not accepted by the official's wife and returned home. Thus, A Jin was already over twenty years old. There was a magistrate named Huang who was over sixty years old, and he took her as his concubine, greatly favoring her.

    8

    黄妻久卒，谋以为妻，阿金不可，曰：“妾出身微贱，岂足伉俪君子？不独损折年寿，亦且累君盛德。”黄鉴其诚，益爱重之。黄有子妇，与年相若，阿金待之极有恩礼。子妇承翁意，事之如姑，阿金虽谦不敢当，然子妇执礼不衰。无何黄病，阿金侍汤药惟谨，年余黄卒，竟仰药以殉。黄之子感其殉父，附葬如礼焉。此女四易所天，不为贞，卒殉其夫，不得不谓之烈。使其初适即得良奥，必为善妇。乃所如不合，遂历四姓，卒成大节，是谓质美而未学。君子哀其遇可也，取其晚盖可也。若豫让“众人”、“国士”之言，本非正论，固非女子所得藉口矣。

    Explanation

    8

    A woman named Ah Jin, whose name is not given, had been married to a man named Huang for some time. After her husband's first wife passed away, Huang intended to take Ah Jin as his new wife. However, Ah Jin refused, saying, "I was born of lowly origins and am not worthy to be a match for a gentleman. It would not only shorten my lifespan but also tarnish your noble virtues." Huang recognized her sincerity and loved and respected her even more. Huang had a daughter-in-law who was of similar age to Ah Jin. Ah Jin treated her daughter-in-law with great kindness and respect. The daughter-in-law followed the wishes of her father-in-law and treated Ah Jin as if she were a mother-in-law. Although Ah Jin was humble and dared not accept such treatment, the daughter-in-law continued to observe the proper etiquette.

    One day, Huang fell ill, and Ah Jin served him with soup and medicine with great care. After a year, Huang passed away, and Ah Jin ultimately drank poison to die with him. Huang's son was moved by her sacrifice for his father and buried her according to proper rites. This woman had changed husbands four times and was not considered chaste, but she ultimately sacrificed herself for her husband. She must be called a woman of great virtue. If she had been married to a good man from the beginning, she would have been a virtuous wife. But since her circumstances were not favorable, she went through four husbands and ultimately achieved a great virtue. She is said to be of noble nature but lacking in education. Gentlemen can mourn her fate, and they can respect her later virtue. As for the words of Yu Rang about "common people" and "national heroes," they are not sound arguments and are not something a woman should use as an excuse.

    9

    江西玉山县有水南寺，亦古刹也。有老僧曰月印，年六十余，终日坐一室诵经，足不出户，戒行颇高。畜一狗十许年矣，每月印诵经，狗必往听，一闻木鱼声，辄摇尾而至，僧俗咸叹异焉。后其狗忽病癞，皮毛脱落，且有秽气，然每日听经如故。一日，月印忽谓其徒曰：“此狗可厌，汝曹可牵出扑杀之。”其徒咸骇然，罔测其意。以师素严重，不敢违。姑呼狗出，禁勿使听经，实未忍杀之也。

    Explanation

    9

    In Yushan County, Jiangxi Province, there is a temple called Shuinan Temple, which is also an ancient temple. There is an old monk named Yue Yin, who is over sixty years old. He spends the entire day sitting in a room reciting scriptures, never leaving the house, and has highly virtuous conduct. He has kept a dog for about ten years. Every time Yue Yin recites scriptures, the dog must go to listen. As soon as it hears the sound of the wooden fish, it wags its tail and comes. Both monks and laypeople marvel at it. Later, the dog suddenly falls ill with leprosy, its skin and fur fall off, and it has a foul smell, but it continues to listen to scriptures every day as before. One day, Yue Yin suddenly told his disciples, "This dog is annoying. You can take it out and beat it to death." His disciples were all shocked and unable to understand his meaning. Because the master is usually strict, they dared not disobey. Temporarily, they called the dog out and forbade it from listening to scriptures, but in reality, they did not have the heart to kill it.

    10

    越三日，狗又乘间来听诵经，月印见之，惊曰：“汝曹未杀此犬乎？殆矣，殆矣！”命其徒至某村某姓家探问，则有一妇坐蓐，三日未产，其势垂危，乳医束手。月印乃曰：“汝曹不忍杀此犬，乃忍杀彼妇乎？此犬不死，彼妇不产。”乃命其徒立时扑杀之。再侦之，某姓家则已产矣，男也。月印曰：“此犬以听经善果，得托生为某姓男子，小有禄位。吾不及见矣，汝曹识之。”及某氏子稍长，辄来寺中，依依不去。月印摩其顶曰：“汝不昧宿根，此意甚善。但尚有小富贵未享，勿遽来此也。”某氏子后果膺微秩，其家亦有中人之产。晚岁家居，恒寄宿寺中。时月印圆寂久矣，为葺治其塔；寺中屋倾圮，亦出赀修理之，并买田供常住费。年七十余而终。

    Explanation

    10

    Three days later, the dog again took advantage of an opportunity to come and listen to the recitation of scriptures. Moonprint saw it and exclaimed in surprise, "Have you not killed this dog yet? It's dangerous, it's dangerous!" He ordered his disciples to go to a certain village and a certain family to inquire. There was a woman in labor who had not given birth after three days, and her condition was critical; even the midwife was at a loss. Moonprint then said, "You all can't bear to kill this dog, but can you bear to kill that woman? If this dog doesn't die, that woman won't give birth." He then ordered his disciples to strike and kill it immediately. Upon checking again, the family in question had already given birth, and it was a boy. Moonprint said, "This dog, due to its good karma from listening to the scriptures, was reborn as a boy from the family in question, with a minor official position. I won't live to see it, you all remember this." When the boy from the family grew up, he would often come to the temple and linger there. Moonprint stroked his head and said, "You don't forget your past life, this intention is very good. But you still have small wealth and honor that you haven't enjoyed yet, don't come here too soon." The boy from the family indeed received a minor official position, and his family also had moderate wealth. In his later years, living at home, he often stayed overnight at the temple. By that time, Moonprint had passed away in a state of enlightenment long ago, and his disciples repaired and restored his stupa; the temple buildings were in ruins, and they also spent money to repair them and bought land to provide funds for the temple's maintenance. He died at the age of seventy or more.

    11

    维扬李氏妇，早寡，以纺织自给，有子甫九龄。同邑富人某，艳其色，而无以自通，乃重赂其邻，使为间。会其子将出就傅，邻人以告某曰：“是有机可图也。”乃筑精舍于其旁侧，移子弟于其中，延师课读。宣言曰：“欲入塾者，勿拒。”使邻人以告妇，极言其便，妇乃使子往读。某善遇之，频以饮食馈遗。一日，出十数金相赠，曰：“闻子家徒四壁立，愿以此少佐饣粥。”子持归白母，妇曰：“币重言甘，得毋诱我乎？”乃使其子往谢曰：“极感长者厚意，母当踵门拜谢。”某则大喜。又使其子出金还之曰：“母性多疑，此不敢受。”某又爽然若失。子归备言其状，妇叹曰：“是可得其情矣。”明日戒子勿往。某自来招之，妇使其子谢曰：“子之惠愈厚，子之过愈大矣。绝子，所以报子也。”某惭而退。此妇不特以礼自持，其智识亦足多矣。

    Explanation

    11

    A woman from the Wei Yang region of the Li family, widowed at a young age, supported herself by weaving and spinning, and had a son who was just nine years old. A wealthy man from the same district, admiring her beauty and having no way to approach her, gave a large bribe to her neighbor to mediate. When her son was about to go to school, the neighbor told the wealthy man, "There is an opportunity to be taken." So he built a study hall next to her house, moved his own children there, hired a teacher to instruct them, and declared, "Anyone who wants to enter the school should not be refused." He sent the neighbor to tell the woman, emphasizing the convenience greatly, and the woman allowed her son to go to the school. The wealthy man treated him kindly, frequently sending food and gifts. One day, he gave ten or so gold pieces as a gift, saying, "I heard that your home is very poor, I wish to provide a little help for your daily meals." The son brought the gift home and told his mother, who said, "The gift is heavy and the words are sweet, isn't this trying to deceive me?" She then sent her son to thank the wealthy man, saying, "We are extremely grateful for your kindness, and my mother will go to your door to thank you." The wealthy man was delighted. He then asked his son to return the gold, saying, "My mother is very suspicious, I dare not accept it." The wealthy man felt disappointed and lost. The son returned home and described the situation to his mother, who sighed, "Now I understand his intentions." The next day, she warned her son not to go. The wealthy man came to invite her, and she sent her son to decline, saying, "Your kindness is even more generous, but your fault is even greater. Refusing my son's entry is how I repay you." The wealthy man was ashamed and left. This woman not only maintained her dignity through propriety, but her wisdom and knowledge are also worthy of high praise.

    12

    明归熙甫著论，极言女未嫁夫死守节之非。然考之礼，婿死，女斩衰往吊。圣人既为制斩衰之服，则已有夫妇之义，其曰既葬而除之者，礼为中人以下设耳。归氏之言，未为定论。乃粤东之俗，有所谓慕清者，则真出乎人情之外，为礼法之所不许矣。粤俗，未婚夫死不嫁，曰守清；原未许嫁而缔婚于已死之男子，往而守节，曰慕清。有许氏女，年逾В梅，言于母，求慕清。母谋之父，父不可。女曰：“姊以遇人不淑，贻父母忧。

    Explanation

    12

    Ming Gui Xifu wrote a treatise, strongly arguing against the practice of widows remaining chaste after their husband’s death. However, when examining the rites, it is customary for a wife to wear the heaviest mourning attire and go to mourn when her husband dies. The sage has already prescribed the heaviest mourning attire, indicating that there is already a marital bond. The practice of removing the mourning attire after the burial is set for ordinary people. Gui Xifu’s statement is not a definitive conclusion. In the custom of Guangdong, there is something called "muming," which truly goes beyond human sentiment and is not permitted by the rites and laws. In Guangdong, it is customary for unmarried women to remain chaste after their fiancé’s death, called "shouqing"; for women who were never engaged but marry a deceased man and remain chaste, it is called "muming." There was a woman from the Xu family, over the age of В梅, who spoke to her mother and requested to practice "muming." Her mother consulted her father, but he did not agree. The woman said, "My sister was unfortunate in her marriage, causing worry for our parents."

    13

    倘女亦然，不重有忧乎？且女弱，亦不任中馈事。苟或遁迹空门，是废大伦，诚不可也。若女萝乔木，得托清门，无废大伦而克成素志，父母何病焉？”乃许之。适有陈氏子将婚而夭，所聘之妇不能守清。陈氏寡母止此一子，乃访求慕清者。媒妁以许女告，遂成二姓之好，迎娶如礼。许女既往，每日略循定省虚文，此外无一事。窗明几净，焚香静坐而已。有小姑已许嫁叶氏，与嫂极相得。每至嫂所共话，辄叹曰：“嫂几生修此清福？”许女曰：“止凭此一念之坚耳。”小姑曰：“嫂幸而未许嫁，不然，亦无如何矣。

    Explanation

    13

    If you are also like that, wouldn't you have additional worries? Moreover, being a weak woman, you cannot manage household affairs. If you were to enter monastic life, it would violate the great principles of human relations, which is truly not possible. If a woman finds a suitable match and relies on a noble family, she can avoid violating the great principles of human relations and achieve her lifelong aspirations, what harm would come to your parents?" She agreed. There was a man from the Chen family who was about to marry but died before the wedding, and the woman he had engaged could not maintain her chastity. The Chen family's widow had only one son, so she sought someone who admired chastity. The matchmaker informed them about the girl's agreement, and thus formed a union between two families, and the wedding took place according to proper rituals. After the marriage, the girl followed the formal rituals of visiting and showing respect daily, and there was nothing else. The windows were bright and the tables clean; she only burned incense and sat quietly. There was a younger sister-in-law who was engaged to the Ye family, and she got along very well with her sister-in-law. Whenever they talked together, she would often say, "How many lifetimes did your sister-in-law have to cultivate this pure fortune?" The girl replied, "It is only due to this single firm resolve." The younger sister-in-law said, "Your sister-in-law is fortunate not to have been engaged yet; otherwise, there would be no way out."

    14

    ”许女曰：“未入其门，事犹在我也。”小姑乃日聒其母，亦求慕清。母溺爱，曲从之，言于叶氏。初不可，既而曰：“彼女既绝意于归，强之亦恐非福。”索还聘礼而已。于是二女同居，至于白首，亲族中或颇称焉。真所谓非礼之礼矣。道光间，江南常州龙舟特盛，然多亦不过四五舟。且舟高于屋，运掉不灵，实亦无可观，观者之意不专在龙舟也。自五月起，至六月初旬，或犹未止。城外有地名唐家湾者，尤画舫聚集之所，每日东舷西舫，鳞次栉比，笙歌如沸，粉黛如云，盖亦一销金锅矣。

    Explanation

    14

    The woman named Xu said, "Not yet having entered the family, the matter still rests with me." Her younger sister-in-law or a young girl began to nag her mother incessantly, also desiring to follow Xu's example. The mother, doting excessively, yielded to her, and spoke to the family of Ye. Initially, they were unwilling, but later said, "Since that girl has already given up on marriage, forcing her might not be a good thing." They only demanded the return of the betrothal gifts. Thus, the two women lived together until old age, and some in the extended family praised them. Truly, this was a non-ceremonial ceremony. During the reign of Emperor Daoguang, dragon boat races were particularly flourishing in Changzhou, Jiangnan, but there were usually only a few boats, no more than four or five. Moreover, the boats were taller than houses, making them difficult to maneuver, and they were not particularly impressive to watch; the spectators' interest was not solely in the dragon boats. From the beginning of May to early June, the races might still continue. Outside the city, there was a place called Tangjiawan, which was especially a gathering place for elaborately decorated boats. Every day, the boats lined up closely like fish scales and combs, with music and singing like boiling, and the beauty of women like a cloud. It was indeed a place of extravagance and luxury.

    15

    余时客常州，居停主人邀余同往观之。见邻舟一大腹贾，挟三妓饮酒，拥一幼妓于怀，状甚亵，余命移舟避之。一榜人且解缆且太息曰：“孽报，孽报！”余问其故，榜人曰：“此三妓乃三姊妹也。其父在日为武进县吏魁，豪侈特甚，每年夏日辄乘舟至城外纳凉，招妓侑酒。舟中先携有纱衫裤，妓至则命易之，遍体雪肤，望之了了如无衣者然。屡坐吾舟，吾时年少，乐观之，辄给事于其侧，得寓目焉。不图至今日而其三女子乃有甚于斯也。岂非孽报！”余叹曰：“然则此大腹贾亦可危矣。”

    Explanation

    15

    I was a guest in Changzhou, and my host invited me to go and see something. We saw a neighboring boat where a fat merchant was accompanied by three courtesans, drinking alcohol, and holding one young courtesan in his arms. The scene was very intimate. I ordered the boat to move away. A boatman was unfastening the ropes and sighed deeply, saying, "Retribution for sins, retribution for sins!" I asked him why, and he said, "These three courtesans are actually three sisters. Their father, when he was alive, was a prominent official in Wujin County and was very extravagant. Every summer, he would take a boat to the outskirts of the city to cool off and hire courtesans to entertain him. He would bring fine silk clothing on the boat and have the courtesans change into them. They had pale skin, and when you looked at them, they appeared as if they were naked. He often sat on my boat, and when I was young, I enjoyed watching and sometimes served them. Who would have thought that today, their three daughters would be even worse. Is this not retribution for sins?" I sighed and said, "Then this fat merchant is also in danger."

    16

    谭某，不知何许人，客于荆州之沙市，年少美丰仪。市有周媪者，卖阿芙蓉为业，生一女，年十八，亦有风致。谭屡过之，遂与目成，使人风示媪，媪居为奇货，未诺也。女乃与谭约，效文君故事，具舟江上，中夜偕亡。媪侦探无踪，痛哭数日，竟服生鸦片而死。谭与女寄居汉口，甚相得。然女每梦母呼与俱返，觉而语谭，谭曰：“此所谓思梦也。”一夕，女倩邻媪买佳酿半壶置床头，女故善饮，亦不异也。夜半，忽自帐中跃出，披发跣足，腹内声如辘轳，十指甲色皆紫黑，始知已和生鸦片而饮之矣。救治不及，竟宛转而死，咸谓母来索命。余谓此女背母而逃，致母于死，死固其分，非必鬼母之为祟也。卫静澜廉访曰：“余披览案牍，见服生鸦片陨命者，几于无日无之。盖此物近来所在皆有，非如砒石等毒物必购求而得也。有一方可救之，宜广为传播。其方用雄黄二钱，鸡蛋青一枚，生桐油一两，河水调匀灌服。”

    Explanation

    16

    Tan, of unknown origin, resided as a guest in the sand market of Jingzhou, where he was young and handsome. There was an old woman named Zhou in the market who made a living selling opium and had a daughter who was eighteen years old and also had a charming demeanor. Tan often passed by and they eventually made eye contact and fell in love, so he sent someone to hint to the old woman, who held them as a valuable commodity and did not agree. The daughter then made an agreement with Tan, imitating the story of Sima Xiangru and Zhuo Wenjun, and prepared a boat on the river, fleeing together at midnight. The old woman searched everywhere but found no trace, she wept bitterly for several days, and eventually died from taking raw opium. Tan and the daughter lived in Hankou and got along very well. However, the daughter often dreamed that her mother called her to return together, upon waking, she told Tan, who said, "This is what is called a thought dream." One night, the daughter asked a neighboring old woman to buy half a pot of fine wine and place it by her bedside, she was good at drinking and did not find it unusual. At midnight, she suddenly leaped out of the bed, with her hair loose and barefoot, there was a sound inside her abdomen like a winch, and her ten fingernails turned purple-black. They realized she had mixed raw opium with the wine and drank it. It was too late to save her, and she died in agony, everyone believed that her mother had come to take her life. I believe that this daughter fled from her mother, leading to her mother's death, and her death was her due, not necessarily because of her mother's ghost causing trouble. Wei Jinglan, the censors, said, "I have reviewed the case files and seen that those who died from taking raw opium are almost daily. This substance is now widely available, unlike poisons like arsenic which must be purchased. There is a remedy that can save them, and it should be widely disseminated. The formula uses two money of realgar, one egg white, one ounce of raw camellia oil, mixed with river water and administered."

    17

    张永祺，南雄州人。娶何氏，生子甫一岁，挈之如母家。其姨女适某氏者亦至，有女亦一岁，与张子貌酷似，乍视之莫辨也。及何归，则呱呱者已为某氏女矣，盖母家戏易之博一笑也。何命婢往易之，及至家，仍某氏女也。又命媪往易之，至何家，谛视，则实张氏子，而非某氏女。何氏之母曰：“此事本由嬉戏而起，乃已易子而归，仍见为女，及携女来易，又见为子，此必有异矣。”乃自携其女之子至张家还之，且曰：“事诚可异，勿播扬也。”嗣是两家子女时时互易，莫知其所以然。后招姨女来，分宅居之，始已。或曰，此殆狐魅播弄以为戏也。

    Explanation

    17

    Zhang Yongqi was a native of Nanchao Prefecture. He married a woman surnamed He, and they had a son who was just one year old. They took the child to his mother's home. At the same time, He's cousin, who was married to a certain family, also arrived with her one-year-old daughter, who looked very similar to Zhang Yongqi's son. At first glance, the two children could not be distinguished from each other. When He returned, the child who had been with her was now identified as the daughter of the other family, as her mother's home had played a trick of swapping them for a laugh. He sent a maid to swap the children back, but when they arrived home, the child was still identified as the daughter of the other family. She then sent an elderly woman to swap them again, but upon closer inspection at He's home, the child was confirmed to be Zhang Yongqi's son, not the daughter of the other family. He's mother said, "This matter originally arose from play, but now the children have been swapped back and forth, with one being identified as a daughter and the other as a son. There must be something unusual about this." She then took her cousin's daughter to Zhang Yongqi's home and returned the child, saying, "This is truly strange, but do not spread the word." After this, the children from the two families continued to be swapped back and forth, and no one knew why. Later, He's cousin was invited to live separately, and the problem finally ceased. Some say that this was probably a fox spirit playing a trick for amusement.

    18

    黄某，不知何许人，父商于羊城，遂家焉。妻何氏，父在时所聘父执女也。黄冗不能自立，父死，遂入于下流，数年尽亡其赀，至无以存活。何曰：“相偕俱死，与君何益。有一策可两全，君意如何？”黄曰：“苟不为门户羞，任卿所为。”何曰：“妾姑母有子妇，早寡，欲得一人为伴侣。君如肯舍妾，彼譬如买婢，数十金不吝也。”黄从之。何商于姑，归告黄曰：“姑始以离人夫妇，执不可，妾力言之乃可。然须郎亲笔一纸书为券，免日后有违言，姑当以五十金润笔也。”黄欣然书券。何持去，遂不归。有知其事者，曰：“何女盖效买臣妻故事，所言[B220]也。”

    Explanation

    18

    Huang, a man of unknown origin, whose father had done business in Guangzhou and settled there, was married to a woman surnamed He, who was the daughter of a respected friend arranged by his father. Huang, unable to support himself, fell into a low social or moral state after his father's death and lost all his wealth over several years, leaving him destitute. His wife said to him, "If we die together, what benefit would it be to you? There is a plan that can benefit both of us; what do you think?" Huang replied, "If it does not bring shame to our family, do as you please." She then said, "My aunt-in-law's daughter-in-law, who is widowed early, wants someone to be a companion. If you are willing to give me up, she would treat me like a purchased maid and would not hesitate to pay several tens of gold coins." Huang agreed. She discussed this with her aunt-in-law, who initially refused because it would separate a couple, but was convinced by Huang's wife. However, she insisted that Huang must write a personal document as a contract to avoid any disputes in the future, and offered to pay fifty gold coins for it. Huang happily wrote the contract. She took the contract with her and did not return. Those who knew about this said, "Huang's wife was imitating the story of the wife of Bi Gan, what she said was [unclear]."

    19

    殷怀乡，江浦人。少孤，有母及诸弟，无恒产，佣力以养。耕作之暇，辄入山刈薪，至夜分始休。明旦入市易甘脆奉母，日以为常。年三十，始娶妇，教之事姑，妇亦婉娩听从。无何岁大荒，无所得食，乃谓妇曰：“俱死无益，不如嫁汝，得银钱可以养吾母及吾弟，汝亦得生路，一举而两利也。”妇不可，殷曰：“此吾意也，非汝负我。且吾母得存活，即汝所以报我也。”妇乃从之，母及弟卒赖以全。后母死，负土成坟。诸弟成立，皆为婚娶，或劝续娶。泣曰：“吾妇归我，无失德，且得母欢心。昔以贫故弃之，今复娶，是负吾贤妇也。

    Explanation

    19

    殷怀乡，江浦人。少孤，有母及诸弟，无恒产，佣力以养。耕作之暇，辄入山刈薪，至夜分始休。明旦入市易甘脆奉母，日以为常。年三十，始娶妇，教之事姑，妇亦婉娩听从。无何岁大荒，无所得食，乃谓妇曰：“俱死无益，不如嫁汝，得银钱可以养吾母及吾弟，汝亦得生路，一举而两利也。”妇不可，殷曰：“此吾意也，非汝负我。且吾母得存活，即汝所以报我也。”妇乃从之，母及弟卒赖以全。后母死，负土成坟。诸弟成立，皆为婚娶，或劝续娶。泣曰：“吾妇归我，无失德，且得母欢心。昔以贫故弃之，今复娶，是负吾贤妇也。

    殷怀乡是江浦人。他从小失去父亲，与母亲和几个弟弟相依为命，没有固定的财产，靠出卖劳动力来维持生计。在农闲时，他经常上山砍柴，直到深夜才休息。第二天一早，他会去市场买甜美的食物给母亲，这成了他的日常习惯。到了三十岁，他才娶了妻子，教导她如何侍奉婆婆，妻子也温顺地服从。不久，发生了大饥荒，家里没有食物。他对妻子说：“我们一同死去没有好处，不如把你嫁出去，这样可以获得一些钱来养活我的母亲和弟弟，你也能找到一条生路，一举两得。”妻子不同意，他说：“这是我的决定，并不是你的过错。而且，只要我的母亲能够活下来，就是你对我最好的回报。”最终，妻子同意了，母亲和弟弟们因此得以生存。后来，母亲去世，他亲手为她堆砌坟墓。弟弟们长大成人，都各自成家，有人建议他再娶。他哭着说：“我的妻子回到我身边，没有失去美德，而且赢得了我母亲的喜爱。过去因为贫穷而让她离开，现在如果再娶，就是对不起我的贤惠妻子。”

    20

    ”卒不娶，独庐于墓侧以终。按《陈书》，徐陵弟孝克当侯景之乱，京师大饥，卖妻臧氏与孔景行，以其赀养母。是鬻妻养母，古人有行之者。然郭巨埋儿，论者谓不可为训，然则鬻妻养母，于义似亦未安也。或曰是盖舍其轻且小者，全其重且大者。然此妇既得姑欢心，或母知妇见出以己之故，涕泣不食，则去妇而转不足以养母；又或妇守从一之义，知事难两全，慷慨自杀，则未能养母，而适以杀妇。且岁饥乏食，非如水火盗贼之变起猝然者也，委曲图全，岂遂乏术，而计必出此乎？殷委巷细民，未闻大道，能割爱为此，亦复大难。然以事而论，则徐孝克与郭巨同一不可为训者也。

    Explanation

    20

    He finally did not marry, living alone by the tomb until the end. According to the Book of Chen, Xu Ling's younger brother, Xu Xiaoke, during the time of Hou Jing's rebellion, when the capital city suffered a great famine, sold his wife, Zang Shi, to Kong Jingxing, to support his mother with the money. Selling one's wife to support one's mother, there were people in ancient times who did this. Then selling one's wife to support one's mother seems also not entirely appropriate. Some say this is about sacrificing the lesser and smaller for the greater and more important. But this wife has already won her mother-in-law's favor, or the mother might know that her daughter-in-law was sent away because of her own fault, crying and refusing to eat, removing the wife would then be insufficient to support the mother. Or the wife adheres to the principle of fidelity, knowing that it is difficult to achieve both, bravely committing suicide, unable to support the mother, but instead killing the wife. And during a year of famine and food scarcity, not like sudden changes caused by water, fire, or bandits, to carefully plan to preserve everything, how could there be no way, and must the plan necessarily be this? Ordinary people in the marketplace and alleys, who have not heard of great principles, to be able to sacrifice love for this, also very difficult. But considering the matter, Xu Xiaoke and Guo Ju are both not models to follow.

    21

    阿胜，广州人，谈者逸其姓氏。少孤，游于美利加国之旧金山，善贸易。居六载，积赀颇丰，航海而归，将缔婚于中土。有某氏女及笄，因媒合之，女母闻其丰于赀也，许焉。既又惧其仍远游也，曰：“吾女岂能相从于海外哉！”故使媒妁索重聘。阿胜鄙之曰：“卖婚，非礼也。吾何患无妻！”遂已其事，复游金山。女闻之，不直其母，窃附海舶，至金山寻夫。一日，于途中遇之，连呼曰：“阿胜，阿胜！”胜顾之惊曰：“卿闺中弱质，何为至此？”女具告之。胜感其义，与俱归旅舍成礼焉。论者谓女子在室从父母之命，此女不从母命，而从六礼未备之夫，不可为训。然重洋暌隔，万里追寻，亦不可云非奇女子矣。君子姑取其从一之贞，勿责其越礼也。

    Explanation

    21

    Ah Sheng, a man from Guangzhou, is referred to by others without his full name. He lost his father at a young age and traveled to San Francisco in the United States, where he excelled in trade. After residing there for six years, he amassed considerable wealth and returned home by sea, planning to marry a woman from China. A woman from a certain family, who had reached the age of marriage, was arranged to be his bride through a matchmaker. Her mother, hearing of his wealth, agreed to the match. However, she later feared that he would continue to travel far and said, "How could my daughter accompany him overseas?" Thus, she instructed the matchmaker to demand a substantial dowry. Ah Sheng dismissed this, saying, "To sell a marriage is against propriety. What do I have to fear about not having a wife?" He abandoned the marriage plan and returned to San Francisco. The woman, upon hearing this, disapproved of her mother's decision and secretly boarded a ship to San Francisco to find her husband. One day, while traveling, she encountered him and called out, "Ah Sheng, Ah Sheng!" Startled, he turned and asked, "How could a delicate woman like you come to this place?" She explained her situation. Moved by her virtue, he agreed to marry her and they returned to an inn to perform the wedding ceremony. Those who discuss the matter say that a woman in the home should obey her parents' commands. This woman did not obey her mother's command but followed a husband who had not completed the six rites, which cannot be a model. However, despite the vast oceans that separated them, she sought him across ten thousand miles, and she cannot be called anything but a remarkable woman. A gentleman should temporarily take her virtue of fidelity and not blame her for violating propriety.

    22

    广东花县有一村聚，距城数十里，河水潆洄，清流如带，有桥甚钜。桥畔一石，形似老翁，村中咸呼为桥头土地神，香火颇盛。后有女子六人，守志不嫁，相约赴桥畔投水死，盖粤俗然也。父老谓神不能保卫，遂废其祀。附近有砖窑，相距里许，窑人舁之归，谋奉为窑神。是夕，窑中有声若霹雳，次日视之，所烧之砖皆成血色，咸谓石为祟，复舁而弃之河干。适有渔舟维岸侧，见此石，异之，载之船唇。俄疾风大作，舟几覆，惧而弃石于河。渔人旋腹痛欲死，乃使人泅水出之，仍置河干。邻邑三山县人闻其事，曰：“神既有灵，曷去彼而就我乎？”夜窃之去，及境，舆从鼓吹，导之以入，灵异大著，祭赛无虚日，为立庙焉。昌黎云：“偶然题作木居士，便有无穷求福人。”况石乎？然以《风俗通义》所书汝南石贤士事观之，则知此亦人心使然，久之当自歇也。

    Explanation

    22

    In the county of Hua in Guangdong, there is a village located several dozen miles from the city. The river winds gracefully, its clear waters resembling a ribbon, and there stands a large bridge. By the side of the bridge is a stone that resembles an old man. The villagers all call it the Deity of the Bridgehead and worship it fervently. Later, six women who had taken vows of chastity and remained unmarried decided to meet at the bridge and drown themselves, as this was a custom in Guangdong. The elders believed that the deity could not protect them, so they ceased the worship. Nearby was a brick kiln, about a mile away, and the kiln workers carried the stone back, planning to worship it as a deity of the kiln. That night, there was a sound like thunder in the kiln. The next day, they found that all the bricks they had fired were blood-red. Everyone believed the stone was causing harm, so they carried it back and abandoned it on the riverbank. Just then, a fishing boat anchored near the shore, and the fishermen saw the stone, finding it strange. They loaded it onto their boat. Suddenly, a strong wind arose, nearly capsizing the boat. Fearing for their lives, they threw the stone back into the river. The fishermen soon suffered severe abdominal pain, nearly dying, so they sent someone to swim out and retrieve the stone, putting it back on the riverbank. The people of San Shan County, a neighboring county, heard about the incident and said, "If the deity is indeed powerful, why not leave it there and come to us?" That night, they secretly stole the stone away. When they reached their territory, they led it in with a procession and music, and the miraculous effects became very prominent. They offered sacrifices and held competitions every day, and built a temple for it. Han Yu once said, "Casually referring to a wooden figure as a sage, there are countless people seeking blessings." How much more so for a stone? However, considering the story of the stone sage in Ruyang recorded in Fengsu Tongyi, we can see that this is also a result of human desire, and over time, it will naturally fade away.

    23

    朱了头者，娄县农家子也。家本赤贫，又茕茕无所依，日行乞于市。咸丰辛酉岁，粤贼自嘉善趋枫泾，遇之，劫与俱去。朱曰：“我丐也，既无钱财自赎，又无一艺可供尔用，何劫我为？”贼曰：“汝既丐也，饥寒之困甚矣。从我去，不忧不富贵。”朱怒曰：“我惟甘饥寒，故丐耳；否则为窃为盗，胡不可乎？我不为窃为盗，乃从尔等作贼乎？”抗声大骂，遂见害。呜呼！如朱了头者，可谓有古烈士风矣。

    Explanation

    23

    Zhu Liutou, a poor farmer's son from Lou County, was born into extreme poverty and lived a life of isolation and begging. In the year of Xianfeng Xinyou (1851), when the Taiping rebels passed through Fengjing from Jiashan, they encountered Zhu Liutou and attempted to take him along. Zhu replied, "I am a beggar, having neither money to redeem myself nor a skill to be of use to you, so why would you take me?" The rebels responded, "Since you are a beggar, you are greatly suffering from hunger and cold. Follow us, and you will not worry about not becoming wealthy and powerful." Zhu angrily retorted, "I only accept hunger and cold, hence I beg; otherwise, why not become a thief or a bandit? I would not become a thief or a bandit, so would I follow you to become a bandit?" He shouted loudly and cursed, and was subsequently killed. Alas! One like Zhu Liutou can be said to have the spirit of ancient martyrs.

    24

    汉阳朱勋臣，侨寓淮城。一日，忽于案头得张禹门书云：“欲借宅同居。”朱初不知张为何人，且罔测书所从来。正疑虑间，倏又得一书，乃知张为狐也。嗣是频有书来，而不以形见。朱惧，谋徙居以避之，徙居而张仍从焉。有请箕仙者，朱往问之，则柳仙，降判曰：“张禹门，吾弟子也，与汝有宿缘，宜为建祠。”朱谢力不及。又判曰：“三日再议。”及期而往，盘中书一“吝”字。朱方欲再问，而家人告火作，奔救乃熄。于是大惧，鸠工庇材，刻日成之。祠成，而张仍往来朱家。

    Explanation

    24

    Zhu Xunchen from Hanyang was temporarily residing in Huai'an. One day, he suddenly found a letter on his desk from Zhang Yumen, which said, "I wish to borrow your house to live together." Zhu did not know who Zhang was and could not fathom the origin of the letter. Just as he was puzzled, he suddenly received another letter, and then he learned that Zhang was a fox spirit. Thereafter, letters frequently arrived, but Zhang did not reveal his form. Zhu was afraid and planned to move to another place to avoid him, but even after moving, Zhang still followed him. Someone invited a diviner using a bamboo basket, and Zhu went to ask him. The diviner was the Willow Spirit, who descended to judge and said, "Zhang Yumen is my disciple, and he has a predestined connection with you. You should build a shrine for him." Zhu apologized, saying he lacked the means. The diviner then said, "Let's discuss it again in three days." When the time came, Zhu went there and found the word "stingy" written in the basket. Zhu was about to ask again when his family reported a fire. He rushed to save the situation, and the fire was extinguished. Thus, he was greatly frightened and gathered workers and materials to complete the shrine within a fixed period. After the shrine was built, Zhang still came and went at Zhu's house.

    25

    适有为朱女议昏者，朱以问张，张报书曰：“此不可许，三日后当有高人来为媒。”越三日，有一老妪来为徐秀才之子求昏。老妪高姓，始悟高人谓妪也。狐居人家，载籍所记多有其事，此狐自称张禹门，不知其何从得姓，何义命名也。箕仙殆即此狐为之托名。柳仙亦无其人，世传柳仙为吕纯阳弟子，盖本元人杂剧，此岂足为典要？且考宋郑景璧《蒙斋笔谈》，载吕诗“惟有城南老树精，分明知道神仙过”。城南老树，乃古松也，则知元剧已属讹传矣。余雅不信箕仙，窃谓当今之世，而欲绝地天通，宜首禁此术也。

    Explanation

    25

    Just now there was someone proposing marriage for Zhu's daughter, and Zhu asked Zhang about it. Zhang replied in a letter saying, "This cannot be agreed to. Three days later, there will be a distinguished person coming to act as a matchmaker." After three days, an old woman came to propose marriage for Xu Xiucai's son. The old woman had the surname Gao, and Zhu finally realized that the "distinguished person" referred to the old woman. Foxes living in human homes are a common motif in historical texts, and this fox called itself Zhang Yumen. It is unknown how it got its surname or what the meaning behind its name is. The Jixian (a type of divination) is probably just this fox using a pseudonym. There is no such person as Liu Xian; according to popular belief, Liu Xian was a disciple of Lü Chongyang, but this is based on Yuan dynasty operas, which is not sufficient as a standard reference. Moreover, considering the Montan Bitter by Zheng Jingbi of the Song dynasty, which records Lu's poem: "Only the spirit of the old tree in the south of the city clearly knows when the immortals pass by." The old tree in the south of the city is actually an ancient pine tree, thus it is known that the Yuan dynasty opera was already a misinterpretation. I have never believed in Jixian, and I secretly think that in today's world, if one wants to cut off the connection between heaven and earth, this practice should be banned first.

    26

    何明达，桂林人，商于岭南，挟赀数千，频年折阅，耗其大半。旅况无聊，偶作狭邪游，至一所曰“巢云别馆”，有妓名翠莲，颇楚楚有致，何流连久之。忽见一妇自帘外过，淡妆素服，丰韵天然，何见而好焉。倩翠莲为之先容，翠莲曰：“是必无成。”问何故，曰：“伊因家难鬻身于此，有能拔出污泥中者，则委身事之；若视作路旁柳，伊不屑也。”何曰：“若然，此女大有志节，宜询其颠末。”乃呼问之。妇自言谭氏，“夫广西人，姓王名基，客游至此。娶妾甫两载，因事被逮于官，非百金不能出狱。妾念夫妇之义，自鬻以拯之。夫出囹圄，妾堕火坑矣。”言已泣下。何曰：“尔夫与吾同乡，吾不忍其家室流离。但不知尔夫已西归否？”曰：“闻犹未也。”何乃辞出，竟访得其夫，助赀使赎妇以归。市井细民，有此高行，虽士大夫或不及矣。

    Explanation

    26

    He Mingda, a native of Guilin, was a merchant in Lingnan who carried thousands of funds but suffered repeated financial losses, depleting most of his capital. Feeling aimless during his travels, he occasionally engaged in licentious activities and visited a place called "Nest of Clouds Pavilion," where there was a courtesan named Cuilian, who was graceful and charming. He lingered there for a long time. Suddenly, he saw a woman pass by the curtain, dressed simply but with a natural elegance. He was immediately drawn to her. He asked Cuilian to introduce him, but Cuilian said, "It will surely not succeed." When he asked why, she replied, "She sold herself here because of her family's misfortune. If anyone can rescue her from this muddy situation, she will dedicate herself to them; but if he treats her like a roadside willow, she will not deign to him." He said, "If that is the case, this woman has great integrity and should be asked about the details of her story." So he called out to her and asked. She introduced herself as Tan, saying, "My husband is from Guangxi, named Wang Ji, who was traveling here. He married me as a concubine just two years ago, but was detained by the authorities and cannot be released without a hundred gold pieces. Thinking of the bond of husband and wife, I sold myself to save him. Now that my husband is out of prison, I have fallen into a brothel." She wept as she spoke. He said, "Your husband is from the same hometown as I am, and I cannot bear to see his family suffer. But I do not know if your husband has already returned home (or passed away)." She said, "I have heard that he has not yet done so." He then left, eventually finding her husband, and helped him with funds to redeem her and bring her back home. Common people like this, who have such noble actions, may even surpass scholars and officials.

    27

    陈明赞，江西南安府人，谈者失记其县。家巨富，止一子，已抱孙矣。陈于生日置酒召族人共饮，语之曰：“某幸藉先业，加以积累，今拥赀三十余万矣。多藏厚亡，非所愿也。愿留奇零之数以赡妻孥，其二十万归始祖祠中，其十万归支祖祠中。岁入其息，仍以三分之一归吾子孙，恐子孙或不能守吾业，仍可藉此为生也。其三分之二以供春秋祭祀之需，族之人冠婚丧葬咸有助，孀者、孤者、废疾者、贫不能存者咸有养。

    Explanation

    27

    Tchen Mingzan, a resident of Nan'an Prefecture in Jiangxi Province, whose county is not recorded, was a wealthy man with only one son who had already had grandchildren. On his birthday, he hosted a banquet for his relatives and said:

    "I have been fortunate to inherit my ancestors' wealth and have added to it, now possessing over 300,000 units of currency. Hoarding wealth will lead to a heavy loss, which is not what I desire. I wish to leave the remaining amount to support my wife and children. I will donate 200,000 units to the ancestral temple of the founding ancestor and 100,000 units to the ancestral temple of the branch ancestor. The annual interest income will be divided, with one-third going to my descendants, in case they may not be able to maintain the family business. The remaining two-thirds will be used for seasonal sacrifices and offerings, to assist relatives in their rites of passage, weddings, funerals, and burials, and to provide for widows, orphans, disabled individuals, and the poor who cannot sustain themselves."

    28

    归始祖祠者，遍及同族；归支祖祠者，惟及同支。以贤且才者一人司其事，虽一陇之地不得私鬻。即以吾今日之言为息壤矣。”闻者咸起拜曰：“盛德事也，但不知翁之子以为何如？”子亦矢言“惟父命是从”。议遂定。夫金穴铜山，终归于尽，陈翁此举，非独高义，抑亦明智也。拥三十万之赀于一家，不三十年必尽矣，今推之于一族，而仍使归其所入三分之一，则是使子孙长有十万也。老子曰：“既已为人，己愈有；既已与人，己愈多。”其是之谓乎！

    　　贞女王蕙芳，常州人。父遭兵乱，挈家避沪渎，遂家焉。贫不能自存，乃鬻女于王姓者为女，时女甫四岁。及长，丰姿秀丽，娴习女红，王颇爱之。无何王卒，其妻旧居北里，搔头弄姿，非良妇也，携女再嫁。女心非之，而不敢言。其后夫素有登徒子之名，见女美，屡挑之。女惧不免，于同治癸酉岁二月十三日夜缢而死。此事诱与拒两无实迹，女虽死不得以贞女旌，因纪大略，冀不泯耳。

    Explanation

    28

    Those who return to the ancestral temple of the founding ancestor benefit the entire clan; those who return to the ancestral temple of the branch ancestor only benefit the same branch. A single person who is both virtuous and talented should oversee the matter, and even a plot of land shall not be privately sold. I hereby make this promise, just as if I had made a vow on the magical land of Xirang. Upon hearing this, all present rose to bow and said, "This is a great virtue deed, but we wonder what your son thinks of it?" The son also vowed, "I will follow only the father's command." The decision was thus made. Vast wealth, like gold mines and copper mountains, will eventually run out. Tuan's father's action is not only an act of high virtue but also a wise one. To possess thirty million taels of wealth in one family, it will surely be depleted within thirty years. Now, by distributing it among the entire clan and still allowing them to retain one-third of their income, it ensures that the descendants will always have ten million. As Laozi said, "The more you give to others, the more you have; the more you share with others, the more you accumulate." Is this not precisely what he meant?

    A woman of noble birth, named Hui Fang, from Changzhou, faced a tragic fate. Her father encountered a military upheaval and fled with his family to Shanghai, where they settled. Unable to support themselves, they sold Hui Fang to a man surnamed Wang as a concubine when she was just four years old. As she grew up, she became beautiful and skilled in needlework, and Wang loved her dearly. However, when Wang died, his wife, who had previously lived in the North Market, was promiscuous and not a virtuous woman. She took Hui Fang with her when she remarried. Hui Fang disapproved of her stepmother's behavior but dared not speak out. Later, her stepmother's husband, who was known for his promiscuity, saw Hui Fang's beauty and repeatedly tried to seduce her. Fearing she could not escape, Hui Fang hanged herself on the night of February 13, 1873, during the reign of Emperor Tongzhi. There is no clear evidence of whether she was seduced or resisted, but since she died, she could not be posthumously honored as a chaste woman. Therefore, I record the general outline of her story, hoping it will not be forgotten.

    29

    广东潮州乡间多种柿，柿熟制为饼，必先曝之于场，夜则逻守之。有某甲以此为业，场中积柿如山，中设木棚，为守望所。一夕寝棚中，闻有声自东来，近而声益厉，咻咻焉，木棚为之震动。自隙窥之，虎也。急以刀斫之，断其尾，虎咆哮去。甲走归鸣金，集丁壮数十人，荷梃寻之。至一处，见虎屹然立，众莫敢近；久之不动，或以戈扌舂之，应手而仆，则已僵矣，乃裂其肉焉。此人猝见猛虎，从容不乱，可谓胆力俱全矣。孔子曰：“上士捉虎头，下士捉虎尾。”然下士亦正未易为也。

    Explanation

    29

    In the countryside of Chaozhou, Guangdong, persimmons are widely cultivated. When the persimmons are ripe, they are made into dried persimmon cakes. They must first be exposed in the field to dry, and at night, they are guarded. There was a man who made this his profession. His field was piled high with persimmons, and in the middle, he set up a wooden shelter as a lookout post. One night, while sleeping in the shelter, he heard a sound coming from the east. As it approached, the sound became louder, a continuous roar that shook the wooden shelter. Peering through a gap, he saw a tiger. He quickly cut it with a knife, severing its tail, and the tiger roared and left. The man ran back and sounded the alarm, gathering dozens of young and strong men. Carrying sticks, they searched for the tiger. They found it standing upright, but the crowd dared not approach. After a long time, some struck it with a spear, and it fell down immediately. It was already stiff, and they then cut its meat.

    This person, upon suddenly seeing a fierce tiger, remained calm and composed, which can be said to be both brave and strong. Confucius said, "A superior man catches the tiger by the head, a lesser man by the tail." However, it is not easy for a lesser man either.

    30

    陈元绍，顺德人。家少康，父母俱存。妻某氏，亦故家女，而骄蹇殊甚，不修妇职。陈责之，辄反唇。父母戒陈曰：“和气致祥，慎勿尔也。”陈恐伤亲心，亦曲忍之。会陈父病，汤药皆母子经理，妇若无其事者，陈固心衔之。及父病愈，陈薄责妇，妇怒曰：“谁无父母！小恙非大事，何便作践人家儿女哉！”陈闻言，忿不可遏，挥以老拳，妇亦攘臂与争。母出排解，陈偶一举手，误挤母仆地，急扶母起，伏地请罪，母麾之出。陈惊惧不自安，竟亡去。月余，自广州贻其弟书曰：“兄无状，不可为子，惟有逃罪海外耳。重洋万里，无望生还，书到之日，即死别之年矣。”父见书不骇，自至省访之，不得，托人于外国招工局询问，亦无其子姓名，或云已于上月自澳门出洋矣。陈挤母仆地，事出无心，不孝之小者；长投海外，永绝天伦，不孝之大者。如陈者，其至性颇亦过人，惜其不学无术也。同年应敏斋廉访宝时，尝从金华至杭州，夜行钱唐江中。

    Explanation

    30

    Tchen Yuan Shao, a native of Shunde, came from a poor family, yet both his parents were still alive. His wife, a woman from a noble family, was extremely proud and difficult to get along with, and she did not fulfill her duties as a wife. Whenever Tchen scolded her, she would immediately retort. His parents warned Tchen, saying, "Harmony brings good fortune; be cautious and do not act this way." Fearing that he would hurt his parents' feelings, Tchen endured it with great effort. When Tchen's father fell ill, the mother and son managed everything related to the medicine and soup, while the wife acted as if nothing had happened. Tchen harbored resentment in his heart. After the father recovered, Tchen mildly reprimanded his wife. She angrily responded, "Who does not have parents! A minor illness is not a big deal; why should you treat our children so harshly?" Upon hearing this, Tchen's anger could not be contained, and he hit her with a fist. She raised her arms and fought back. The mother intervened to mediate, but Tchen accidentally pushed her down. He quickly helped her up, fell to the ground, and asked for forgiveness. The mother dismissed him with a wave of her hand. Tchen was terrified and uneasy, so he fled. After a month, he sent a letter to his younger brother from Guangzhou, saying, "My brother, I am unworthy and cannot be a proper son. The only option is to flee overseas to avoid punishment. Across vast oceans, there is no hope of returning alive. The day this letter arrives will be the year of our death." His father did not panic upon receiving the letter but went to the province to look for him. He could not find Tchen and entrusted someone to inquire at a foreign labor bureau, but they found no mention of his son's name. Some said that Tchen had already left for overseas from Macau the previous month. Tchen accidentally pushed his mother down, which was unintentional and a minor form of filial impiety; however, fleeing overseas and permanently severing ties with his family is a major form of filial impiety. Tchen's nature is quite extraordinary, but it is a pity that he lacks education and skills. In the same year, Ying Minzai, a censors with the courtesy name Bao Shi, traveled from Jinhua to Hangzhou and traveled at night on the Qiantang River.

    31

    其时将二更矣，月色微茫，远树约略可辨。帆风而行，风力甚小，故舟行颇迟，而了不倾侧。长年三老皆偃息篷底，舟中诸客亦俱酣睡，敏斋独携一仆坐船头观江中夜景。所过之地名鲇鱼口，有小沙洲，舟沿洲而行。相距才数武，见洲上有一人与舟并行，迟速适相等。其人长丈余，视常人几三倍之，遍体青绿色，但望之不甚亲切，不知其为肌肉，为皮毛，为鳞甲也。顾问其仆曰：“汝见彼人否？”其仆不应。

    Explanation

    31

    It was nearly the second watch, the moonlight faint and hazy, and distant trees were vaguely discernible. Sailing by the wind, the breeze was so slight that the boat moved slowly without any tilting or swaying. The three experienced sailors were all resting under the awning, and the passengers on the boat were also deeply asleep. Minsan alone took a servant and sat at the bow of the boat to admire the night view of the river. The place they passed was called Yaoyu Kou (Carp Mouth), with a small sandbar nearby, and the boat followed the sandbar. They were only a few steps apart when they saw a person on the sandbar walking alongside the boat, moving at the same speed. The person was over a zhang tall, nearly three times the height of an ordinary person, with a body that was greenish-blue. It was unclear whether he was made of flesh, skin, or scales. Minsan asked his servant, "Did you see that person?" The servant did not respond.

    32

    敏斋因还入舟中，其仆携坐具从之入，旋即大吐。问其故，曰：“主人所见，奴亦见之，不特形状可畏，且其气甚腥。适闻其气，故吐也。”敏斋后为余述之，云：“不知是何怪，殆传记所称枫人之类欤？”余谓：“沙洲无草木，不应有此。且草木之精，其气亦不腥，此必水怪也。以形论之，其长如此，非鱼即蛇。《搜神记》载孔子厄于陈，弦歌于馆中，夜有一人，长九尺余，皂衣高冠，咤声动左右。子路出与战，仆之于地，乃是大是鱼。君之所见，或亦此类乎？”

    Explanation

    32

    敏斋于是回到船中，他的仆人带着坐具跟着他进去，不久就剧烈呕吐。问他原因，他说：“主人看到的，我也看到了，不仅仅是形状可怕，而且气味非常腥。刚闻到那气味，所以呕吐了。”敏斋后来向我讲述了这件事，他说：“不知道是什么怪事，大概是传记中所说的枫人之类吧？”我说：“沙洲上没有草木，不应该有这种情况。况且草木之精气也不腥，这一定是水怪。从形状来看，它这么长，不是鱼就是蛇。《搜神记》记载孔子在陈国被困，晚上在馆中弹琴唱歌，夜里有一人，身高九尺多，穿着黑色衣服戴着高帽子，怒吼声震动左右。子路出来与他战斗，把他摔倒在地上，原来是一条大鱼。你所看到的，或许也是这样的吧？”

    33

    聂道人，湖北钟祥县人。乾隆甲子岁，邑大水，聂已五岁矣。有盛契真者，元游宫之道士也，悯其孤露，收之门下。然聂故钝根，一无所能。盛死，聂云游天下，不知历几何年月。嘉庆初，始居鄂之长春观，往往赤双脚，持长柄，行歌于市。至同治甲戌，其年一百三十有五，发丝齿贝，无异曩时，不知其得道引术而不死耶，抑其禀赋独厚也？不可谓非异人矣。

    　　鄂人王慕堂，游于津门，遇盗，丧其所赍，寓居同乡陈姓家。偶出散步，过一曲巷，见一家帘栊低掩，中有丽人，衫痕鬓影，隐约可睹。王意方郁郁，亦不顾也。行数武，有小鬟追及之，曰：“主人相候，请君移玉。”问主人伊谁，曰：“至自识之。”至即帘中人也。然彼此均不相识。女冁然曰：“误矣。”王问故，曰：“妾本秦淮烟柳中人，从买人至此。买人死，遂独居。顷从帘隙窥君，颇似旧识某郎，故遣婢相招，而不知非也。”王因辞去。

    Explanation

    33

    Nie the Taoist, was from Zhongxiang County in Hubei Province. In the year 1774 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a great flood struck the county, and Nie was already five years old. There was a respected figure named Sheng Qizhen, a Taoist priest from the Yuan dynasty, who pitied Nie's orphaned state and took him under his wing. However, Nie was naturally slow-witted and had no particular talents. After Sheng's death, Nie wandered the world, and it is unknown how many years he traveled. In the early years of the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, he began to reside at the Changchun Temple in Hubei, often barefoot and carrying a long-handled hoe, singing and walking in the market. By the year 1874 during the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, he was 135 years old, with hair and teeth no different from before. It is unclear whether he achieved longevity through Daoist techniques or had a unique constitution. He cannot be called an ordinary person.

    Wang Mutang, a native of Hubei, was traveling in Tianjin when he encountered bandits and lost his belongings. He then stayed at the home of a local surnamed Chen. One day, he went out for a walk and passed through a winding alley, where he saw a house with low-hung curtains, and inside, a beautiful woman, her dress and hair barely visible. Wang was feeling down and paid no attention. After walking a short distance, a young maid caught up to him and said, "The mistress is waiting for you. Please come inside." Wang asked who the mistress was, and the maid replied, "You will know when you meet her." Upon entering, it was indeed the woman behind the curtains. However, they did not recognize each other. The woman smiled and said, "You must be mistaken." Wang asked why, and she explained, "I was originally a courtesan from the Qinhuai River area, brought here by a man who has since died, leaving me to live alone. Just now, I saw you from a crack in the curtain and thought you resembled an old acquaintance, so I sent a maid to invite you, but it seems I was wrong." Wang then took his leave.

    34

    女请置酒小饮以谢过，王谢曰：“素昧平生，何烦厚意。”女曰：“相逢何必曾相识，萍水遇合，即是前缘。君即闲游，妾亦枯坐，杯酒小叙，亦足破寂。君何介介欤？”王遂留饮。酒半，言及被盗事，女问行止，曰：“客囊如洗，进退维谷。若小有膏秣资得入都门，则辇下尚有故人，或犹可谋干也。”女曰：“君勿忧。妾虽龌龊，颇具湖海襟怀，必当竭力。”酒罢，竟出百金赠之。王持以入都，所谋果遂。复访之，则门庭如故，人面不知何处矣。

    Explanation

    34

    The woman asked to set up a small drink to apologize. The man thanked her, saying, "We have never met before, why bother with such generosity?" The woman replied, "Meeting someone doesn't require prior acquaintance; meeting like floating萍 (duckweed) is our predestined fate. You are idly wandering, and I am sitting alone; a small drink and conversation would be enough to break the silence. Why are you so hesitant?" The man then stayed for the drink. After half a cup of wine, they talked about the man's troubles. The woman asked about his plans, and he said, "My bag is empty, and I am in a difficult position where one cannot advance or retreat. If I have a little money to enter the capital, there are old friends in the capital, or I might still be able to find a way." The woman said, "You don't have to worry. Although I am not very clean, I have a broad and open-minded spirit, and I will certainly do my best." After the wine, she finally gave him a hundred gold pieces. The man used the money to enter the capital, and his plans were successful. When he returned to visit, the courtyard was the same, but the person's face was nowhere to be found.

    35

    番禺何桐屿，于同治癸亥初冬，有戚某向其借洋钱五百枚。何以其人旧曾借贷两次，皆如期而偿，遂诺之，立券期于岁杪。至期往索，某诧曰：“君梦欤？抑戏也？业已还君，何又索为？”何惊问：“何时还我？”曰：“十二月初三日，我携洋钱至君家交君手，承君留我小饮，犹如昨日事，何遽忘之？谓予不信，君还我借券犹在也。”乃取示何。何曰：“此券尚存我箧，君何伪造此？”某曰：“如君尚存借券，请以示我。”何至家检视，箧中诸券皆在，此券独无，惟诸券中杂有一纸，颇似前所书券，而无一字。其事遂无以自明，偕至神前，沥鸡血为誓，不知究竟若何也。此殆有秘物代墨书，券久而字迹脱落，遂成空纸。段成式《酉阳杂俎》称江东人或取乌贼鱼墨书契，以脱人财物，知古有此事矣。落地开花炮，其炮子虽年久尚能炸裂。乡民拾得者，因其封口处有金皮，贪取其金，每致伤命。

    Explanation

    35

    During the early winter of the year Gengzi during the Tongzhi era, a certain relative in Panyu borrowed 500 silver dollars from He Tongyu. Since the person had previously borrowed twice and repaid on time, He agreed and signed a contract for repayment by the end of the year. When the time came, He went to collect the money, but the relative was surprised and said, "Did you dream it? Or were you joking? I have already repaid you, why are you asking for it again?" He was shocked and asked, "When did you repay me?" The relative replied, "On December 3rd, I brought the silver dollars to your home and handed them to you. You kept me for a small drink, just like it was yesterday. How could you have forgotten so quickly? If you don't believe me, your loan contract is still with me." The relative then showed He the contract. He said, "This contract is still in my box, how could you have forged it?" The relative said, "If you still have the contract, please show it to me." He went home to check, and found that all the contracts were in the box except for this one. There was only a blank piece of paper mixed in with the others, which looked similar to the original contract but had no writing on it. The matter could not be resolved, so they went to a shrine together and took a chicken's blood oath, but it was still unclear what had happened. It is likely that a secret substance was used in place of ink, and the text on the contract faded over time, becoming a blank paper. Duan Chengshi's Yuyang Zazu mentions that people from Jiangdong sometimes used ink from cuttlefish to write contracts, which could later fade or disappear, indicating that such things happened in ancient times. As for the "landing and blooming" fireworks, their shells can still explode even after many years. Villagers who pick them up, because of the golden coating on the sealing part, often greedily take the gold, and end up injured or killed.

    36

    甲戌正月，上海浏河镇东洋子泾桥镊工陈小弟，因新岁负人博，进无以偿。思同治元年七月中官军开放落地开花炮，有炮子落其地，陈小弟埋之土中，久思凿取其金，未敢措手。至是已历十三年，久在土中，必受潮湿，计必无碍矣。乃掘出，用斧凿取金皮，忽然炸裂，陈头面俱焚，左手打在数里外杨树上。室中一老妇、一童子，均轰死。军兴以来，官军用落地开花炮，无往不利。其炮子之遗落者，所在有之，愿得者以此为鉴，勿贪小利而致大害也。上海某甲，商于津门，十年不归。其家止一妾二女，妾故冶荡，不能独守空帏也。长女颇有母风，母倚之为钱树子。无何从人亡去，乃使次女继其业，次女不可，母憾焉。会有六十翁欲买妾，母贪重值，以次女妾之。甫一载，翁死，无子，家业尽为族人瓜分，女惟以针黹自给。母谓之曰：“人生世上，如轻尘栖弱草，何自苦为？当为汝别觅佳婿。”女断发自誓，竟不可夺。梅禹金有《青泥莲花记》，殆此女之谓乎？

    Explanation

    36

    In the first month of the year Jia Xu, Chen Xiaodi, a hairdresser from Liuhe Town in Shanghai, near the Dongyang Zijing Bridge, was in debt due to gambling during the New Year. He had no means to repay his debts. Recalling that in the seventh month of the first year of the Tongzhi era, the imperial army had used landing shells, and one had fallen to the ground where he lived, Chen Xiaodi had buried it in the soil. For a long time, he had wanted to dig it up and extract the gold from it but had not dared to do so. By this time, thirteen years had passed, and the shell had been buried for a long time, surely affected by moisture. He thought it must be harmless by now. So he dug it up and used an axe to extract the gold. Suddenly, it exploded, burning his face and head, and his left hand was thrown several miles away, hitting a poplar tree. An old woman and a child in the room were also killed by the explosion. Since the start of military campaigns, the imperial army had used landing shells with great success. There were many shells scattered around, and those who wish to gain should take this as a lesson and not be greedy for small gains, lest they suffer great harm. In Shanghai, there was a merchant who traded in Tianjin and had not returned for ten years. His household consisted of one concubine and two daughters. The concubine was promiscuous and could not guard her empty room alone. The eldest daughter had a lot of her mother's style, and the mother relied on her as a money tree. Soon, the eldest daughter died, and the mother then made the second daughter continue her business, but the second daughter refused, and the mother was disappointed. Then, an elderly man in his sixties wanted to buy a concubine. The mother, greedy for a high price, sold the second daughter to him. After just one year, the elderly man died, leaving no heir, and the family's property was divided among the relatives. The daughter then supported herself by sewing. The mother told her, "Life in this world is like a light dust resting on a weak blade of grass. Why should you suffer? I will find you a good husband." The daughter cut off her hair as a vow and could not be persuaded otherwise. Mei Yujin has a record called The Lotus in the Mud, and this must be what that record is referring to.

    37

    李广元，不知其乡里。妻早卒，遗二子，长曰上朋，年七岁；次日上俊，三岁。李续娶郑氏，性阴险，善蛊惑，视前妻子如仇，日加楚毒。会郑所生子夭，谓由朋惊之使然，益虐遇之，朋竟死。俊忽得颠痫疾，忽歌忽泣，殊无人理。郑以为伪，试以秽物予之，食尽无难色，乃视为废物，亦不顾问。如是者数年，郑死，俊忽自愈。父怪之，俊泣曰：“儿病伪也。非伪为痫疾，则死继母手久矣。今继母死，又察父复有怜儿意，故敢以情告也。”此子在童年即能韬晦自全，其德慧术智过人远矣。

    Explanation

    37

    Li Guangyuan, whose hometown is unknown, had a wife who died early, leaving behind two sons. The elder son was named Shangpeng and was seven years old; the younger son was named Shangjun and was three years old. Li remarried Zheng, who was cunning and deceitful, skilled at manipulation. She treated her stepchildren as enemies and daily inflicted severe punishment on them. When Zheng's child died, she believed it was caused by Shangpeng's fright and treated him even more cruelly. Shangpeng eventually died. Shangjun suddenly developed epilepsy and would suddenly sing and cry, behaving in an irrational manner. Zheng believed it was fake and tested him by giving him dirty things. He ate everything without showing any disgust, so she then regarded him as useless and paid no attention to him. Like this for several years, Zheng died, and Shangjun suddenly recovered. His father found it strange, and Shangjun cried, saying, "My illness was fake. If it weren't for pretending to have epilepsy, I would have died at the hands of my stepmother long ago. Now that my stepmother is dead, and I see that my father has a compassionate heart again, so I dare to tell the truth." This child, even in his childhood, was able to conceal himself and preserve himself, his virtue, wisdom, and intelligence far surpass others.

    38

    黄冈县易家坂有易翁者，夫妇二人，老而无子。止生三女，长次皆适人，惟幼女在室。翁死，母女相依为命，每抚女叹曰：“恨汝非男子也。”年十九而嫁，夫家甚贫，故恒居母家。癸酉十一月初十日，风雨大作，女偶立门前，为狂风卷去，母侦探无踪。三日后，忽自返，问所往，茫然也，而下体已化为男。母遍告族人验之，信，乃出钱七十二千与夫，令别娶妻。女易钗而弁，已聘王氏女为妇矣。晋定公二十五年，西山女子化为丈夫，与之妻，能生子，事见《开元占经》引《汲冢纪年》。然则此事自古有之，不足异也。

    Explanation

    38

    In Huanggang County, there was an old man named Yi in Yi Village. He lived with his wife, who was old and childless. They had only three daughters; the eldest and middle ones were married, but the youngest remained unmarried. After the old man died, the mother and daughter depended on each other for survival. She often patted her daughter and sighed, saying, "I regret that you are not a boy." At nineteen, she married, but her husband's family was very poor, so she often stayed at her mother's house. On the tenth day of the eleventh month in the year Guiyou, a fierce storm raged, and the girl happened to be standing at the door, caught up by the gale and blown away. Her mother searched everywhere but found no trace of her. Three days later, she suddenly returned, but when asked where she had been, she was clueless, and her lower body had already transformed into that of a man. Her mother informed all the family members to verify the truth, and they believed it. She then gave her husband seventy-two thousand coins and ordered him to take another wife. The girl changed her hairpin for a hat and had already betrothed a woman from the Wang family as his wife. In the twenty-fifth year of Duke Ding of Jin, a woman in the Western Mountains transformed into a man, married, and could bear children. This is recorded in the Yuanhui Celestial Observations citing the Jiuzong Annals of the Spring and Autumn Period. Thus, such things have existed since ancient times and are not extraordinary.

    39

    戴琴庄先生名福谦，先祖母戴太夫人之侄孙，与余兄弟行。而余少时曾请其授读，故始终以先生称之。其馆临平孙氏时，一日行于途，有娶新妇者，彩舆经过，先生避立道旁。俄风揭轿帘，瞥见轿中白如霜雪，一瞬之间，未能谛视，而心颇异之。偶言于先大夫，先大夫曰：“此必是日犯白虎凶星，得君文星解释之，传记中多有此事。君异时必大贵也。”众皆以为然。然先生于道光丁酉举于乡，越二岁客死京师，意不显。

    Explanation

    39

    Mr. Dài Qínzhuāng, whose given name was Fúqiān, was a great-nephew of Lady Dài, my maternal grandmother, and was of the same generation as my brothers. When I was young, I asked him to teach me, and thus I always referred to him as Mr. Dài. During the time he was teaching at the Sun family's residence in Lín Píng, one day while walking on the road, he saw a man who had just married, with a decorated sedan chair passing by. Mr. Dài stood aside on the side of the road. Suddenly, the wind lifted the curtain, and he caught a glimpse of someone inside the sedan chair who was white as frost or snow. In an instant, he was unable to see clearly, but he found it quite strange. He casually mentioned this to my father, who said, "This must be a day when the White Tiger star, a symbol of misfortune, is inauspicious, and it was explained by your literary star. There are many such cases in historical records. You will surely achieve great rank in the future." Everyone agreed. However, Mr. Dài passed the provincial examination in the year Ding You of the Daoguang era, and two years later, he died as a guest in Beijing. The meaning of his life remains unclear.

    40

    安徽玉溪口，泊有炮船二。有某广文挈眷属坐小船过其地，日暮泊焉。夜鼓再严，管带炮船之营官乘他船出巡，船中兵勇起意至小船行劫，持刀登其舻。广文见盗至，大呼炮船救援。即杀之，并及其一妻一子一女一仆；长年三老，均投于水。因断其缆，使顺流而去。及营官回见邻舟不在，问之，众曰：“已解维行矣。”营官惊曰：“黑夜江行，必有失事，汝曹胡不阻止？”众曰：“阻而不听也。”营官见几上有翡翠条脱一事，审视即邻舟女子腕上物也，因亦不言。及旦鼓发旬，乃告于统带之官，捕船中兵勇，严鞫得实，枭其首于竿。苜蓿中，度无长物，扁舟江上，竟占无妄之灾。信乎江湖之险，不独风波也。

    Explanation

    40

    At the mouth of the Yuxi River in Anhui, two gunboats were anchored. A scholar, accompanied by his family, was traveling on a small boat through the area and anchored there at dusk. At the second drumbeat of the night, the officer in charge of the gunboats went out on patrol in another boat. Some of the soldiers on the boat decided to board the small boat and rob it, carrying knives and climbing aboard. The scholar, seeing the thieves, shouted for the gunboats to rescue him. The soldiers immediately killed him and his wife, son, daughter, and servant; they also threw three elderly men into the water. They then cut the rope, allowing the boat to drift downstream. When the officer returned and saw that the neighboring boat was gone, he asked about it. The soldiers replied, "It has already set sail." The officer was shocked and said, "Traveling on the river at night must have led to some disaster. Why didn’t you stop them?" The soldiers answered, "We tried to stop them, but they wouldn’t listen." The officer noticed a jade bracelet on the table and realized it belonged to the woman from the neighboring boat. He said nothing further. At dawn, when the drum signaled the start of the day, he reported the incident to the commanding officer. The soldiers on the boat were arrested and, after a thorough investigation, were found guilty and decapitated, their heads displayed on poles. The small boat, carrying little of value, ended up suffering an undeserved disaster on the river. Indeed, the dangers of the rivers and lakes are not only from storms and waves.

    41

    范某年四十无子，止一女，名婉如，颇婉娈可爱。有甥李仙槎，五岁而孤，偕其母以归，与婉如年相若，交相怜也。仙槎性柔和，范妻亦喜之，呼为李郎。仙槎母将死，泣谓范曰：“吾惟此子，恨不见其娶妇。婉如我所爱也，异日无令归他族，吾死目瞑矣。”仙槎既长，范予千金，使营什一利，而仙槎不善经营，数年折阅殆尽。范因憾之，寝前议。仙槎闻之，仰药死，婉如亦缢以殉。范大悔恨，合葬之。

    Explanation

    41

    Fan, at the age of forty, had no sons but only one daughter named Wanru, who was graceful and lovely. He had a nephew named Li Xiancha, who became an orphan at the age of five and returned with his mother. Wanru and Li Xiancha were about the same age and mutually loved each other. Li Xiancha was gentle and mild-mannered, and Fan's wife also liked him, calling him "Li Lang." As Li Xiancha's mother was about to die, she wept and said to Fan, "I have only this son, and I regret not seeing him marry. Wanru is the one I love, so do not let her marry into another family when she grows up; then I will die with peace of mind." When Li Xiancha grew up, Fan gave him a large sum of money to pursue a profit of one-tenth, but Li Xiancha was not good at managing it, and after several years, he almost lost everything. Fan was disappointed and abandoned his previous agreement. Upon hearing this, Li Xiancha committed suicide by drinking poison, and Wanru also hanged herself to follow him. Fan deeply regretted their deaths and buried them together.

    42

    扬州某甲，生一女，年破瓜矣，颇有姿色。其东邻为某氏别业，某子为邑诸生，读书其中，翩翩少年也。女屡入园采花，与生有私，女父母知而防闲之，遂绝迹。生思慕綦初。一日女至，曰：“殆矣，父母将为我择配矣。君急以媒妁来，或犹可及也。”言已即去。生告父母，初不可，强而后可。媒者致命，女父母曰：“齐大非吾耦也。”且知女私于生，恐异日不为舅姑所礼，竟谢绝之，而许女于他族。女知事不谐，服阿芙蓉膏死，生闻之亦自经死。两家父母皆大悔，卒合葬焉。痴儿荡妇，颇乖风化，其情可怜耳。

    Explanation

    42

    In Yangzhou, there was a man named Mr. A who had a daughter. She was sixteen years old and quite beautiful. Her eastern neighbor was a property belonging to the Mr. B family, and Mr. B's son was a scholar in the local school, reading there as a dashing young man. The daughter often entered the garden to pick flowers, and she had an affair with the young man. Her parents found out and kept her under strict watch, so she stopped coming. The young man longed for her more than ever. One day, the daughter came and said, "Dangerous! My parents are going to arrange a marriage for me. Hurry and bring a matchmaker, perhaps it's still possible." She then left. The young man told his parents, who initially refused but eventually agreed. The matchmaker was sent, and the daughter's parents said, "A great family is not suitable for me." They also knew that the daughter had an affair with the young man and feared that she would not be respected by her in-laws. Finally, they refused and arranged the marriage with another family. The daughter knew the matter did not work out and died after taking opium. Upon hearing the news, the young man also hanged himself. Both sets of parents deeply regretted, and they were finally buried together. The foolish boy and wanton girl were quite contrary to social norms, and their fate is pitiable.

    43

    此与前所记李仙槎、范婉如事相类，然彼则始有成言，谓之因情而死可也，谓之守义而死亦可也，固非此事所能拟矣。上海北乡有黄某者，妻李氏颇有姿。而黄贫不能自存，谋于李。李曰：“君为男子，而谋及妇人；无已，请鬻我乎？妾我可百金，妓我可千金也。”黄不可，李曰：“然则放鹁鸽乎？”沪俗伪鬻妻妾于人，伺间亡归，谓之放鹁鸽。黄从之，伪为兄妹，鬻于浦东曹氏为妾。不三日，黄往访之，李出见，颇落寞。曹留黄宿于家，翌日将告归，促李出言别。李始不出，久之乃出，不数语，遽厉声曰：“汝鬻我于此，乃谋与我偕遁乎！我至此无返理，汝不速去，我言于主人，缚送官矣。”黄大窘，踉跄而归。鹁鸽化为黄鹤，一去而不复返矣。此亦为鬼为蜮者之所不自料也。

    Explanation

    43

    This is similar to what was previously recorded about Li Xiancha and Fan Wanru, but those cases had a prior agreement, so it could be called dying out of emotion, or it could also be called dying out of loyalty. It certainly cannot be compared to this matter. In the northern suburbs of Shanghai, there was a man named Huang, whose wife Li had considerable beauty. However, Huang was poor and could not sustain himself, so he discussed this with Li. Li said, "You are a man, yet you involve a woman in your plans; if there is no other way, shall I be sold? You could get a hundred gold for me as a concubine, or a thousand gold as a courtesan." Huang did not agree, and Li said, "Then shall we release the pigeons?" In Shanghai, there is a local custom where people pretend to sell their wives or concubines, only to escape later, and this is called "releasing pigeons." Huang followed this practice, pretending to be siblings, and sold himself to Cao in Pudong as a concubine. After no more than three days, Huang went to visit her. Li came out to see him, looking quite desolate. Cao kept Huang at his home overnight, and the next day, he urged Li to come out and say goodbye. Li initially did not come out, but after a while, she came out, and without saying much, she suddenly spoke sternly, "You sold me here, and now you are plotting to escape with me! I have no reason to return here, and if you do not leave quickly, I will tell the master, and he will tie you up and send you to the authorities." Huang was greatly embarrassed and hurriedly returned home. The released pigeon transformed into a yellow crane, and once it left, it never returned. This is also something that those who act like ghosts and demons could not have anticipated.

    44

    韶州李弼廷有女曰松姑，年十五矣。自幼佞佛，奉一檀木观音像，已历六寒暑。父母欲议婚，女不可。父曰：“此皆奉佛之故也。”欲毁其像。女急白母，言像不可毁，请从父命。乃缔姻于程氏。女自是清癯异昔，医者诊之，言脾肺俱弱矣。一夕，女午夜欲起礼佛，觉有物触臂，方惊诧而腕已受伤。呼婢烛之，则有一蛇长二尺许，色如墨，蜿蜓下榻去。父母闻声趋视，蛇已不知所住，视女伤处，蛇齿宛然，黑气缕缕直达臂上。医至，谢不治，黎明竟卒。或言女本天仙化身，不合缔人间姻眷，故菩萨使以洁身归净土。果尔，何不使以无疾化去，而必死于蛇口乎？是殆有夙冤也。

    Explanation

    44

    In Shaozhou, there was a girl named Songgu, the daughter of Li Biting, who was fifteen years old. From a young age, she was devoted to Buddhism and worshipped a wooden statue of Guanyin, which she had venerated for six years. Her parents wanted to arrange a marriage for her, but she refused. Her father said, "This is all because of your devotion to Buddhism." He wanted to destroy the statue. The girl quickly told her mother that the statue could not be destroyed and asked her to allow her to follow her father's decision. She then married into the Cheng family. Afterward, she became unusually thin and emaciated, and a doctor diagnosed her as having weak spleen and lungs. One night, she wanted to rise at midnight to pay respects to the Buddha. She felt something touch her arm and was startled, but by then her wrist was already injured. A servant lit a candle and saw a snake about two feet long, black in color, wriggling down from the bed. Her parents heard the commotion and rushed to see, but the snake had already disappeared. They saw that the girl's wound had snake teeth marks, and black gas was rising from her arm. The doctor said there was no cure, and she died at dawn. Some said that the girl was originally a celestial being and was not meant to marry a human, so the Bodhisattva allowed her to return to the Pure Land in a pure state. If that were true, why did she not die of illness but instead die by snakebite? Perhaps it was due to a predestined enmity.

    45

    闽中黄生，富家子也。年十五六，翩翩如璧人。忽得狂疾，歌哭不伦，自食其秽。夜或纵火烧屋，家人不能防。乃于山中构屋数椽，使居之，守以健仆。久之，益憔悴无人状。其所聘林氏女，父母欲绝其婚，女不可，曰：“未婚而婿膺恶疾，女之命可知矣。从一而终，万无他议。与其老死闺中为父母累，不如与狂夫同处空山，犹得稍尽妇职也。”父母初不许，言之益坚，乃如其志。与婿居山，饥饱寒暖，调护维谨。一日同坐石上，忽溪中有鱼跃出，长二尺许，其首类犬。未即取食，女力阻不听，竟食尽，越宿病若失。翁姑闻而喜甚，迎归成礼焉。《山海经》云：“诸怀之水多旨鱼，食之已狂。”黄生所食，岂即此欤？殆由林女《苡》之歌，感动幽明，故得此灵药，非偶然也。

    Explanation

    45

    Huang Sheng, a wealthy young man from Fujian, was around fifteen or sixteen years old and appeared graceful and handsome, like a beautiful person. Suddenly, he fell ill with madness, singing and crying in an unseemly manner, and engaging in self-destructive behavior.
