At night, he sometimes set fire to the house, and the family could not prevent him. They then built a small house in the mountains for him to live in, guarded by a strong servant. Over time, he became increasingly emaciated and lost his human appearance.

    His betrothed, Lin's daughter, initially refused to cancel the marriage when her parents suggested it. She said, "Before marriage, the groom contracted a terrible illness. One can foresee the girl's fate. From one and to the end, there is no other opinion. Rather than die an old maid and burden my parents, it is better to live with the madman in an empty mountain, where I can still fulfill the duties of a wife." Her parents initially did not agree, but she spoke more firmly, and they eventually accepted her decision.

    She cared for her husband in the mountains, ensuring he was well-fed, warm, and comfortable. One day, while sitting on a stone together, a fish suddenly leaped out of the stream, about two feet long, with a head resembling a dog. She did not immediately take it to eat, but despite her objections, Huang Sheng ate it all. The next day, his madness seemed to vanish.

    Her parents, upon hearing this, were very pleased and welcomed him back to complete the wedding ceremony. The Classic of Mountains and Seas states, "The waters of the Zhihuai area have many 旨 fish, and eating them cures madness." Could it be that what Huang Sheng ate was this fish? Perhaps due to Lin's daughter's song of Yi, which moved both the visible and invisible worlds, thus obtaining this miraculous medicine, which was not by chance.

    46

    杭州顾茂才，客吴中。甲戌春，春而复苏。自言初死时，但觉飘飘如凌云而上，俄而复下，则仍在吴市也。青衣三人，导至一处，即苏州府城隍庙。神出，略诘数语，便云：“误矣。”遣之还。甫出门，遇故人徐某，吴中诸生，前数年死者也。与偕至玄妙观茶寮小坐，顾问冥中事，曰：“与人世同，亦随时变易。阳间盛传十殿阎罗，此唐制也。唐分天下为十道，故冥中亦设十殿，今则否矣。道光以前，冥官尚有前代遗贤，今则皆本朝贵官，衣冠仪从，悉今制也。”茶罢，徐别去。及家门，如梦忽觉，则已死三日矣，因胸次微温，故未殓耳。考唐太宗分天下为十道。

    Explanation

    46

    A man of talent from Hangzhou, who was a guest in Wu, revived in the spring of the year 甲戌. He recounted that when he first died, he only felt as if floating like a cloud ascending into the sky, but soon descended, and found himself still in Wu City. Three servants in blue guided him to a place, which was the Temple of the City God of Suzhou Prefecture. The deity appeared, briefly questioned a few words, and said, "It was a mistake." Then he sent him back. As soon as he stepped out of the door, he met an old acquaintance named Xu, a scholar from Wu who had died several years ago. They accompanied him to the Xuanmiao Temple's tea house for a short rest, where he asked about matters in the afterlife. Xu replied, "The afterlife is the same as in the mortal world, and it also changes with time. In the mortal world, there is a widespread belief in the Ten Courts of Yama, which is a system from the Tang Dynasty. The Tang Dynasty divided the empire into ten circuits, so the afterlife also set up ten courts, but this is no longer the case. Before the reign of Emperor Daoguang, the officials of the afterlife were still some of the virtuous people from previous dynasties, but now they are all high-ranking officials from the current dynasty, with clothing, attire, and ceremonial attendants following the current system." After finishing tea, Xu left separately. When he reached his home, he was as if waking up from a dream, and it was already three days since he had died. Because his chest was slightly warm, he had not yet been dressed for burial. Investigating that Emperor Taizong of Tang divided the empire into ten circuits, the belief in the Ten Courts of Yama originated during his reign.

    47

    开元二十一年又分山南、江南为东西道，增置黔中道。然则唐制十道为时不久，中叶以后，冥中又当增置阎罗矣，何仍止十殿也。至云冥官皆近代贵官，则理固不妄。盖人死为鬼，人不能无贤愚，鬼亦不能无善恶，非于人鬼中择聪明正直者主之，则不胜纷纷矣。然世间无不死之人，即冥中亦无不散之鬼，盖精气固不能常存也。自夏以上，祀柱为稷，自商以来，祀弃为稷，盖柱之神泯矣。此圣人知鬼神之情状也。前在京师见许文恪公，公言有一人因病至冥中，所见冥中官吏，大半熟识，声音笑貌，一一与生时无异。余曰：“然则生而有官者，死亦有官守。如余者生为闲散之人，死亦宜为闲散之鬼。清风明月，任我邀游，不亦仙乎？”公大笑。

    Explanation

    47

    In the 21st year of the Kaiyuan era, the regions south of the mountains and south of the Yangtze River were divided into eastern and western circuits, and the Qianzhong Circuit was added. Thus, the Tang Dynasty's system of ten circuits lasted for a relatively short period. After the mid-period of the Tang Dynasty, the underworld would have needed to add more Yamas (judges of the dead). Why did it still remain at ten halls? The officials in the underworld were all high-ranking officials from recent times, which is a reasonable assumption. When people die, they become ghosts. People cannot be without wisdom or foolishness, and ghosts cannot be without good or evil. Without selecting wise and upright individuals to govern them among the living and the dead, the multitude would be overwhelming. However, in the world, there are no immortal people, and in the underworld, there are also no ghosts who do not disperse. Vital energy cannot be permanently preserved. Before the Shang Dynasty, the god of agriculture was worshipped as Zhu. Since the Shang Dynasty, the god of agriculture was worshipped as Qi. The spirit of Zhu has vanished. This shows that sages understand the nature of ghosts and spirits. Before, in the capital, I met Xu Wenke Gong, who said that one person, due to illness, went to the underworld. The officials he saw in the underworld were mostly familiar to him, and their voices and smiles were exactly the same as when they were alive. I said, "Therefore, those who had official positions in life also had official duties in death. Like me, who lived as a leisurely person, I should also be a leisurely ghost in death. Breezes and moonlight, allowing me to wander freely. Is this not like being a deity?" He laughed heartily.

    48

    沪上有流寓陈氏妇，嫠也，携一女甚美。妇与某甲私，甲曰：“尔曷以女妻我，则我并迎妻母以归，谁议我者？”妇以语女，女不可。妇惑于甲言，卒以女许之。将成婚，女丑其事，缢而卒。妇惭惧，即迁去，而居此室者，辄见怪异，竟莫敢居。余谓贞烈之魄，固应不泯，然女之烈益著，母之丑益彰。惜无人敷陈此义，以消释其怨气也。

    　　湖北荆门州后港镇有田氏兄弟，皆以猎为业。甲戌初夏，猎于邻村李家台。偶憩林下，同梦一老人告之曰：“午后幸助我一臂。”觉而异之。前行里许，雷雨大作，趋避枯庙中，雷声益厉。仰见{詹}下一物，长二尺余，有四翅，雷下击，则吐黑气御之。乃相谓曰：“梦中求助，岂雷神乎？”发火枪击之，枪发而疾雷骤至，兄弟皆昏不知人。雨过始苏，眉毛辫发各灼去少许，亦无大苦。闻人言五里外茭尾乡雷击死一蜈蚣，长二尺余，有四翅，知即此物矣。

    Explanation

    48

    In Shanghai, there lived a widow of the Chen family, who had a daughter of exceptional beauty. The widow had a secret affair with a man named Jia, who said to her, "Why not give your daughter to me as my wife, and I will bring both her and her mother back home? Who would dare to speak ill of me?" The widow told her daughter about the proposal, but the daughter refused. Influenced by Jia's words, the widow eventually agreed to give her daughter to him. On the eve of their wedding, the daughter found the matter distasteful and hanged herself. Filled with shame and fear, the widow moved away, and those who lived in the house afterward reported seeing strange and supernatural occurrences, so no one dared to live there. I believe that the spirit of chastity and loyalty should not be forgotten, but the daughter's righteousness became even more evident, while the mother's shame became even more apparent. It is a pity that no one has explained this meaning to dissipate her resentment.

    In Hubei Province, in Jingmen Prefecture, there were two brothers of the Tian family, both of whom made their living as hunters. In early summer of the year 1844, they were hunting in a nearby village called Li Jia Tai. They happened to rest under a tree and had a dream where an old man told them, "In the afternoon, please lend me a hand." Upon waking, they found the dream strange. After walking for a few miles, a severe thunderstorm broke out, and they sought shelter in a dilapidated temple, where the thunder grew even louder. Looking up, they saw a creature about two feet long with four wings. When the lightning struck, the creature emitted black smoke to defend itself. The brothers then said to each other, "We asked for help in our dream; could this be the thunder god?" They fired their guns at the creature, and as the guns went off, a sudden and intense lightning strike knocked them unconscious. After the rain stopped, they regained consciousness, with their eyebrows, braids, and hair slightly singed, but they suffered no serious harm. They later heard that five miles away, in Jiao Wei Village, a centipede with four wings had been killed by lightning, and they realized that the creature they had encountered was the same one.

    49

    粤东丁维勋，贾于沪上，娶妇王氏。甫逾年，丁病瘵，王扶持维谨。丁自知不起，语王盍自为计，王以死自誓。越日疾亟，又言之。王曰：“君何不谅之甚。”乃趋别室，缢而死。丁大感恸，将殓，力疾起拜，无何亦死。此妇殉夫于夫未死之前，似乎太早，然因其夫再三劝令自为计，故以死自明，亦田先生不使人疑之意也。余幼时尝作《秋胡妇》诗曰：“桑下相逢本偶然，黄金翻似试婵娟。妾身愿学田光死，一使人疑不值钱。”此妇殆亦此意矣。

    Explanation

    49

    Ding Weixun, a merchant from Guangdong, married a woman surnamed Wang in Shanghai. Just over a year later, Ding fell ill with a wasting disease. Wang took care of him very diligently. Knowing he was dying, Ding told Wang to think about her own future, but Wang vowed to die with him. The next day, when his condition worsened, he again urged her to consider her own future. Wang replied, "How can you not trust me?" She then went to another room and hanged herself. Ding was deeply moved and, despite his illness, got up to pay respects to her. Soon after, he also died.

    This woman chose to die before her husband, which might seem too early. However, because her husband repeatedly urged her to plan for herself, she chose to die to prove her loyalty, much like Tian Xiansheng, who did not want others to doubt his intentions. When I was young, I wrote a poem about the wife of Autumn Hu: "Meeting by the mulberry trees was purely by chance, but the golden reward seemed to test her beauty. I wish to emulate Tian Guang's death, so that no one would doubt my worth." Perhaps this woman had the same intention.

    50

    上海谭家桥有宋姓，寓此有年矣。一女年十六，颇有姿。会有诬以他事者，女无以自明，缢而死，厝其柩于河干。宋旋迁居维扬，女之柩遂无过问者。久之，将陷于河。邻人胡宝素艳女美，心悼之，为改厝于高阜。俄梦女来拜谢，姿态如生。胡大喜，引与并坐。语及绝命之际，哽咽不成声，良久不去，遂与狎。自是无夕不至，胡竟病瘵死。此女可谓以怨报德矣。昔伍员、伯皆以宗族覆灭，逃死于吴，吴王为之报雠雪耻。其后伍员苦口犯颜，所以报吴王也；伯逢迎将顺，亦所以报吴王也。吾安知伯之意，不以子胥之悻悻然为负恩哉？然其报之适以亡之，此女毋乃类是。

    Explanation

    50

    In the Shanghai town of Tanjia Bridge, there lived a family surnamed Song who had resided there for many years. They had a daughter who was sixteen years old and quite beautiful. One day, she was falsely accused of something, and being unable to prove her innocence, she hanged herself and was buried by the riverbank. Soon after, the Song family moved to Yangzhou, and no one paid attention to the daughter's burial. After some time, her coffin began to sink into the river. A neighbor named Hu Bao, who had always admired the girl's beauty, deeply mourned her and reburied her on high ground. Shortly thereafter, Hu Bao dreamt that the girl came to thank him, looking as if she were alive. Overjoyed, Hu Bao invited her to sit beside him. They spoke about the moment of her death, and she choked with sobs, unable to speak, staying for a long time before they became intimate. From then on, she visited him every night, and Hu Bao eventually died from a wasting illness. This girl can truly be said to have repaid kindness with resentment. In the past, Wu Zixu and Bo Yi both saw their families destroyed and fled to Wu for survival, where the King of Wu avenged and restored his honor. Later, Wu Zixu spoke harshly and faced the king directly to repay the King of Wu, while Bo Yi flattered and followed along, also repaying the King of Wu. How can I know that Bo Yi did not view Wu Zixu's stubbornness as a betrayal of kindness? Yet his repayment led to his own demise, and this girl is not unlike him.

    51

    扬州城外二十里有地名湾头，一夕大雷雨，震开一穴。次日，集人缒入，则穴中空洞若夏屋，然一棺其巨。众意其有宝器，发之。一人偃卧，白须及腹，仪容甚伟。得一珠于颐，得一剑于腰，取之出，更入搜索，则尸已化为灰矣。于棺之前斫片木出，隐约有“大业”字，不知隋代何人之墓也。其事在甲戌五月。太仓城北十里地曰毛市，有许姓染店，其染工皆句容、溧水人。许督之严，每年止许一人归。是岁应句容陆姓者归，某工与同邑，托传语其家。及陆返，某问家事，陆嗫嚅久之，曰：“吾负君矣。

    Explanation

    51

    Outside Yangzhou City, twenty li away, there is a place called Wan Tou. One night, a thunderstorm opened a cave. The next day, people gathered and lowered themselves into the cave. Inside, the cave was spacious like a summer house, but there was one coffin, quite large. They decided it might contain valuable treasures, so they opened it. Inside, they found a man lying down, with a white beard reaching his abdomen, and a very dignified appearance. They found a pearl at his chin and a sword at his waist. After taking these items out, they re-entered the cave to search further, but the body had turned to ashes. They cut a piece of wood from in front of the coffin, which faintly showed the characters "Da Ye." It is unknown whose tomb it was from the Sui Dynasty. The event occurred in May of the Jia Xu year.

    Ten li north of Taicang City, there is a place called Mao Shi. There is a dye shop owned by someone named Xu. All the dyers are from Jurong and Lishui. Xu supervises them strictly, allowing only one person to return home each year. This year, it was supposed to be someone from Jurong named Lu who returned. A worker from the same county entrusted me to convey a message to his family. When Lu returned, I asked about his family, and he hesitated for a long time before saying, "I have failed you."

    52

    嫂因久旷，留余共衽席，余不能拒也。”某不信。陆曰：“嫂肋下有两黑子如豆大，然乎？”某遂无语，次日怀刃亡去。许知而追之，已附舟归矣。某抵家叩门，妇出，刃之毙，即至妇翁家言状。妇翁大骇，偕其子同往视之，则妇固无恙。问以前事，妇茫然曰：“吾未见夫归也。”相与骇异。忽见所奉观音像有刀痕数处，乃悟其故。正共罗拜，而许与陆踵至，陆力白前言之妄。盖陆至某家，妇登梯取屋上所曝酱饷客，陆窃窥其腋，故见之也。一言之戏，几至杀人，非菩萨慈悲，则此狱坐死者不止一人矣。

    Explanation

    52

    The sister-in-law, due to her husband's long absence, kept me to share her bed, and I could not refuse. I did not believe this. Lu said, "Does your sister-in-law have two black spots on her ribs like beans?" I remained silent. The next day, he left with a knife. Xu learned of this and pursued him, but he had already boarded a boat and returned. When he arrived home and knocked on the door, the wife came out, and he killed her with the knife, then went to the mother-in-law's house to report the incident. The mother-in-law was greatly alarmed and went with her son to see. They found that the wife was unharmed. When asked about the previous events, the wife茫然said, "I did not see my husband return." They were all shocked and puzzled. Suddenly, they saw several knife marks on the statue of Guanyin, and they realized the reason. Just as they were bowing together, Xu and Lu arrived, and Lu strongly denied the previous statement. It turned out that when Lu came to the house, the wife climbed a ladder to fetch the soy sauce exposed on the roof to serve the guests, and Lu secretly peeked at her armpit, thus seeing the marks. A mere jest almost led to a murder. Without the compassion of the Bodhisattva, this case would have resulted in more than one death.

    53

    绍兴周某，习申、韩家言，馆于某邑。邑有因奸杀夫者，亦旧族也。死者一美少年，年二十余，妇年与相若，国色也。其奸夫年近半百，黑丑可憎。官鞫妇，妇惟俯首饮泣，历其指，痛欲绝。周窥而怜之，私问其故。妇曰：“数年来惘惘如梦，都不自知，殆夙业也。”周曰：“以卿弱质，何以堪此，不如姑认之，徐图一死。”因举小盒示之，盖阿芙蓉膏也。妇亦会意。案将定，周迟回未忍属，而妇仰药死矣。俄见梦于周曰：“妾死不蔽辜，仍堕畜类，毕命パ质，以偿今世之愆。三十年后，君为平阳士人，当侍君巾栉也。”事在隔世，无可证验，或当不妄耳。观其后果，信其前因，妇所云夙业，亦不虚矣。

    Explanation

    53

    A man named Zhou from Shaoxing, who studied the doctrines of Shen and Han, lived in a certain county. In that county, there was a woman from an old family who killed her husband due to adultery. The victim was a handsome young man in his twenties, and the woman was of similar age, a beauty of the nation. The adulterer was nearly fifty years old, ugly and repulsive. The officials interrogated the woman, and she only bowed her head and wept, her finger being touched causing her immense pain. Zhou looked at her with pity and privately asked her the reason. She said, "For several years, I was like a dream, not knowing what I was doing, probably my karmic fate." Zhou said, "With your frail body, how can you endure this? It might be better to admit it and gradually plan for death." Then he showed her a small box, which contained opium paste. She also understood his intention. The case was about to be decided, but Zhou hesitated and did not dare to report it, and the woman drank poison and died. Soon, she appeared in a dream to Zhou, saying, "I die without covering my guilt, still fall into the category of animals, end my life in a certain way to atone for my sins in this life. Thirty years later, you will be a scholar in Pingyang, and I will serve you as your maid." The matter is from another era, there is no evidence to verify, perhaps it is not false. Observing the result, one believes in the cause, the woman's belief in her karmic fate is also not unfounded.

    54

    上海浦东张老达，先世本多田翁。老达性俭啬，未尝妄用一钱。及病笃，命其弟与子析祖产为二，各得其一。室中有银一箧，乃老达手自积累，封识严密。至是命其妻启之，将议分析，而老达已逝。方共惊怛，忽箧中银锭随风飞去，争起扑之，如蛱蝶蹁跹，不可捉摸。或偶得一二，随手即空，俄顷之间，箧中之银乌有矣。乃悟作守钱虏亦正须福，张老达不能用之，犹得守之；其子若弟欲作守钱虏而不得也。

    Explanation

    54

    A Man of Pudong, Shanghai

    The old man Zhang Laoda, whose ancestors were originally a farmer named Dada, was known for his frugality and stinginess. He never spent a single coin frivolously. When he became seriously ill, he ordered his younger brother and son to divide the ancestral property into two parts, each receiving one part. In his room, there was a box of silver that he had accumulated by hand, sealed and marked securely. Upon his death, he instructed his wife to open the box to discuss the division, but by then, Zhang Laoda had already passed away. As they opened the box, they were suddenly startled when the silver ingots inside flew away with the wind. They rushed to catch them, but the ingots moved like butterflies fluttering, impossible to catch. Occasionally, they managed to catch one or two, but immediately, the silver vanished. In a short while, the silver in the box was nowhere to be found. They then realized that being a miser also requires good fortune. Zhang Laoda, who could not use the silver, still managed to keep it; but his son and younger brother, who wished to be misers, could not do so.

    55

    慈溪北乡有叶姓者，于甲戌五月生一男，人身而虎面，骇而弃之野。其邻人往视，犹未死，乃祝曰：“尔三啼，当抱汝归。”儿果三啼，乃抱之归。明日，乡党毕集，争取视之。或有为叶惜者，叶亦悔之，以青蚨一贯赎儿去。越宿，儿竟死。不死于野，不死于邻，而仍不可育，不知此何故也。

    　　宜兴山中一赵姓者，每夕宿火于钅卢，加煤其上，以供明日之用。忽一夜，煤火皆发弃地上，连夕皆然。伺之，则一独足鬼俯钅卢而窥，且笑且发，群起搏之，一跳即逝。或曰此山魈也，是畏爆竹。乃伺其至，然爆竹投之。鬼惊仆，众入执之，于其足旁得一鼗鼓。鬼虽黑丑，殊无所能，惟叩首作乞怜状。或予之饮食，恐怖不敢多食，惟示以所弃鼗鼓，则喜而笑。姑与之，入手即大笑，奋足一跃，倏忽脱去。《抱朴子。登陟篇》：“山精形如小儿，独足向后，夜喜犯人，名曰魈。呼其名，则不能犯也。”此鬼形状，为山魈无疑，但不知所持鼗鼓何用耳。

    Explanation

    55

    In the northern part of Cixi, there was a man surnamed Ye who, in the fifth month of the year Jia Xu, gave birth to a son with a human body but a tiger's face. Frightened by his appearance, they abandoned him in the wild. A neighbor went to see and found that he was still alive. The neighbor then said, "If you cry three times, I will take you back." The child indeed cried three times, and the neighbor took him back. The next day, the neighbors all gathered to see the child. Some felt pity for Ye, and Ye also regretted his decision, using one string of coins to redeem the child. The following night, the child died. He did not die in the wild or with the neighbor, but still could not be raised, for reasons unknown.

    In the mountains of Yixing, there was a man surnamed Zhao who, every evening, lit a fire in a metal container and added coal on top to prepare for the next day. One night, the coal fire suddenly went out, and this happened repeatedly over several nights. When they investigated, they saw a one-legged ghost crouching over the metal container, laughing and playing with the coal. The people gathered and attacked the ghost, but it leaped away in one bound. Someone said it was a mountain demon, and that it fears firecrackers. They waited for it to appear and threw firecrackers at it. The ghost, though black and ugly, was helpless and only knelt and begged for mercy. They gave it food, but it was terrified and dared not eat much. When they showed it the discarded hand drum, it became happy and laughed. They gave it the drum, and as soon as it took it, it laughed loudly and leaped away, disappearing in an instant.

    According to Baopuzi: Ascending to the Heights, "The mountain spirit appears as a small child with one leg facing backward, enjoys causing trouble at night, and is called a mountain demon. If you call its name, it cannot harm you." The ghost's appearance was undoubtedly that of a mountain demon, but the purpose of the hand drum it carried remained a mystery.

    56

    吴中钱氏婢，嫁乡间一农家子。其地有所谓清明山太太者，相传为太湖黑鱼精也。忽附婢言前生为姊妹，故来省视，口操北音，清脆可听。嗣是每数日必一至，积三十余年，惟婢见之。或问婢：“太太作何状？”曰：“年三十许，历年以来，未尝衰老。我今龙钟日甚，而太太仍如初见时也。”钱氏旧主使婢入山进香，并命仆阿勤从之，欲觇其异。甫出门，婢腾身作据鞍状，又举手作扬鞭状，其行甚疾。阿勤追之不及。归而问之，曰：“太太怜我不任跋涉，以仆马来迎也。”婢卒亦无他异。山中初无神祠，惟空屋中一几一椅而已。后村人塑为男像，又有“飞虎城隍”之名。闻高伯足宰吴县，禁绝之。

    Explanation

    56

    A maid from the Wu region who served the Qian family married a farmer's son from the countryside. In her area, there was a deity known as the "Madam of Qingming Mountain," who was said to be a black fish spirit from Taihu Lake. One day, the spirit possessed the maid and claimed to be her sister from a past life, coming to visit her. The spirit spoke with a northern accent, and her voice was clear and pleasant to hear. From then on, she would visit every few days, and this continued for over thirty years, with only the maid seeing her. Someone asked the maid, "What did the madam look like?" She replied, "She was about thirty years old, and over the years, she never aged. I am now increasingly feeble, but the madam still looks as she did when I first saw her." The old master of the Qian family sent the maid to offer incense in the mountain and ordered the servant Ah Qin to accompany her, hoping to observe any unusual occurrences. As soon as they left, the maid leaped onto the saddle, raised her hand as if to whip the horse, and moved very quickly. Ah Qin could not keep up with her. When they returned, they asked her about it, and she said, "The madam pitied me for not being able to endure the journey and brought my servant and his horse to welcome me." The maid ultimately had no other unusual experiences. At first, there was no temple in the mountain, only a table and a chair in an empty house. Later, the villagers made a male statue of her and called her the "Flying Tiger City God." It is said that Governor Gao Bozu of Wu County banned the worship of the spirit.

    57

    吴中某甲，以行买至沪上，偶作狭邪游，悦一妓。酒阑之后，送客留髡，将歌同梦。偶问及家世里居，则即其乱中失去之幼女也，颜色俱变。妓问之，以实告。妓大啼，父摇手戒勿声：“徐谋为汝脱籍。”乃辞去。念客囊羞涩，安得此赎女之赀？而又不忍其沦落，转展无计，竟服阿芙蓉膏死。此人既遇其女，不思援手，而以身死之，亦可谓无策之甚者矣。“

    Explanation

    57

    A certain person from Wu, traveling and buying goods, arrived in Shanghai and occasionally engaged in licentious activities, meeting a prostitute. After the wine had been consumed, he sent off the guests and detained a bald person, planning to sing together in a dream. By chance, he asked about her family background and residence, and she revealed that she was his lost daughter from the chaos, causing both their expressions to change. The prostitute asked him about his identity, and he revealed the truth. The prostitute cried loudly, and the father shook his hand to warn against making a sound: "Let's slowly plan to release you from your register." He then left silently. Thinking of the guest's meager bag, how could one obtain the money to redeem the daughter? And yet, unable to bear her degradation, he had no plan or strategy, and eventually died from taking opium. This person, having met his daughter, did not think to help her, but instead took his own life, which can truly be called an extreme case of helplessness.

    58

    粤人某甲，游于沪，悦一妓名紫鹃，脱籍，携之归，将偕老矣。俄某以讼事破家，鹃自鬻于平康，以其赀料理，讼始解。鹃再入青楼，郁郁不自得。甲时往慰喻之，拟重为脱籍，而苦无资。乃复至沪，将醵于旧友，久而无成。鹃在粤，日夕企望。忽有传甲已在沪物故者，遂服阿芙蓉膏死，同伴觉之，救治复苏。其事传闻至沪，人咸义之，助甲使归，复圆破镜焉。此女以既嫁之云英，为下车之冯妇，未免可惜。然本妓也，不能责以不贞。且其自鬻也，所以解其夫之讼；其再入风尘而不死也，所以待其夫之赎；则其意固未尝一日忘其夫也。善善从长，仍以贞烈许之可矣。

    Explanation

    58

    A man from Guangdong, surnamed Jia, traveled to Shanghai and fell in love with a courtesan named Zijuan. She left the register, and he took her back to his home, intending to grow old together. Soon, however, he lost everything due to legal disputes, and Zijuan sold herself back into the brothels of Pukang to use her earnings to resolve the legal issues. The lawsuit finally ended, but Zijuan returned to the brothel, feeling depressed and discontented. Jia would often go to comfort and console her, planning to help her leave the register again, but he was short of funds. So he returned to Shanghai, planning to raise money from old friends, but after a long time, it was unsuccessful. Zijuan, in Guangdong, looked forward every day and night. Suddenly, there was news that he had died in Shanghai, and she took opium and died. Her companions noticed and saved her. The news spread to Shanghai, and people praised her, helping her return home, and they reunited. This woman, having been married, became a courtesan again, which is indeed a pity. However, she was originally a courtesan, so it cannot be held against her for being unfaithful. Moreover, her selling herself was to resolve her husband's legal issues; her returning to the brothel and not dying was to wait for her husband to redeem her. So her intention was never to forget her husband. Considering all these good deeds, it is appropriate to commend her as chaste and loyal.

    59

    ●卷二姑熟曹氏两孝女，长曰静宜，自幼许嫁姚氏，因兵乱阻隔。及乱定，姚氏子来为赘婿，居久之，始知其已娶妻生子矣。父母怒，责婿无礼，静宜曲为解之。姚氏子羞愤去，遂绝。静宜居家，事父母维谨。并以母病，代操家政，咸井井焉。婉宜者，其第三妹也，幼慧甚，读书过目成诵。父母奇爱之，苛于择婿，故未许人也。同治七年，父卒，婉宜誓以身殉，家人以母在劝止之。乃与静宜长斋奉佛，共诵《金刚经》，为亡父资冥福。无何，母又卒。二女乘人不备，同时仰药死。静宜有临终留别兄弟书曰：“妹不幸为女子身。自有知识，即念女子同受罔极之恩，而独无以为报，深觉歉然。稍长，见母有痰疾，发无定时，必有人昼夜经心，方免不测。诚恐一朝远嫁，不得侍奉母疾。而幼字姚氏，亲命难违，无术分身，焦思万状。及赘婿后，欲令别置媵妾，以便他日归宁。

    Explanation

    59

    Volume Two: The Two Filial Daughters of the Cao Family of Gushu

    The elder daughter, named Jingyi, was betrothed to the Yao family from a young age, but they were separated due to the turmoil of war. When the chaos settled, the son of the Yao family came to be a groomsman, and after living for a long time, they finally found out that he had already married and had children. The parents were angry and blamed the groomsman for his rudeness, but Jingyi tried to smooth things over. The son of the Yao family left in shame and anger, and thus they were separated. Jingyi, at home, served her parents with great care. And because of her mother's illness, she took over managing the household affairs, and everything was well-organized. Wanyi, the third sister, was very intelligent from a young age and could memorize what she read in one glance. Her parents loved her greatly and were strict in choosing a husband, so she was not yet betrothed. In the seventh year of Tongzhi, her father died, and Wanyi vowed to sacrifice herself. The family tried to stop her because her mother was still alive, so she and Jingyi took a long-term vegetarian diet and devoted themselves to Buddhism, reciting the Diamond Sutra together to pray for the deceased father's blessings in the afterlife. Not long after, her mother also died. The two sisters took advantage of someone's absence and both drank poison at the same time. Jingyi left a farewell letter to her brothers before dying:

    "Sister, it is unfortunate to be born a woman. From the time she gained knowledge, she immediately thought that women also receive boundless grace, but there is nothing to repay it with, deeply feeling a sense of inadequacy. As she grew older, she saw that her mother had a coughing illness, which occurred without a fixed schedule, and there must be someone who cares for her day and night to avoid unforeseen dangers. She truly feared that one day she would be married far away and would not be able to serve her mother's illness. And she was betrothed to the Yao family at a young age, it was hard to defy her parents' wishes, she had no way to divide herself, and she was in a state of extreme anxiety. After the groomsman arrived, she wanted to arrange for a concubine so that she could return to her parents' home in the future."

    60

    天幸其先已娶妻，并生子女。维时众欢遇人不淑，咸讥士也无良，而妹独欣天假之缘，得遂养亲之志。是以力阻正名之议，安心在家侍奉，满冀双亲寿享期颐，永叙天伦之乐。何图天降鞠凶，慈父见背；丧服未终，老母又染沈疴，殆将不起。呜呼！吾父吾母，既违定省于堂前；生佛生天，誓必相从于地下。从此色笑常亲，亦复何恨。惟念在室养亲，义非姚氏之人；生事死殉，可谓曹门之女。所存原聘金簪二枝，望即检还姚氏，永断葛藤，以明决绝。三妹于父殁之日，百计求死不得，此番恐亦不免矣。哀哉！兄弟叠遭大故，双折连枝，悲伤之情，不堪设想。窃有一言劝慰者，泉下庭闱，有妹等侍奉，足可放心。兄弟等子职所当尽者，不徒在哀毁二字，但能修身训子，积德显亲，自足慰父母之灵，而妹等亦从旁含笑矣。二妹已为孔氏妇，有其当尽之职，务宜节哀自爱，勿蹈非礼。死别须臾，临颖怆然！”

    Explanation

    60

    Heaven has already arranged for the person to be married and have children. At that time, many people met with misfortune, and all criticized men for being unrighteous, but the sister alone rejoices at the chance provided by heaven, fulfilling her wish to care for her parents. Therefore, she strives to resist the argument for proper naming, settling down at home to serve, fully hoping that both parents will live to a hundred years old and always enjoy the joy of family. Who would have thought that heaven would bring such misfortune, the kind father has passed away; the mourning period is not yet over, the old mother has contracted a serious illness, is likely to die. Alas! My father and mother, have both departed from the hall; may be reborn as a Buddha or ascend to heaven, swear to follow them in the afterlife. From now on, their smiles and laughter will always be close, there is no more regret. But remember that staying at home to care for one's parents, is not the duty of a woman from the Yao family; to serve in life and follow in death, can be called a daughter of the Cao family. The original betrothal gifts, two gold hairpins, hope to return them to the Yao family immediately, to permanently cut off entanglements, to clearly show a final break. The third sister on the day the father died, tried every way to die but could not, this time, it seems she cannot avoid it. Alas! Brothers repeatedly suffer great misfortunes, both branches broken together, the grief is unbearable to imagine. I have a word of consolation, in the afterlife, the family hearth, with the sisters serving, it is enough to put one's mind at ease. The brothers' duties as sons, not just in the words of grief and destruction, but to cultivate oneself and educate one's children, to accumulate virtue and honor one's parents, will be sufficient to comfort the spirits of the parents, and the sisters will also smile from the side. The second sister is now a wife of the Kong family, has her duties to fulfill, must grieve moderately and love oneself, do not violate proper conduct. Death is a brief separation, at the moment of writing, with deep sorrow!

    61

    绍兴老儒王致虚言：乾隆之末，有贾慎庵者，亦老诸生也。尝梦至一处，似大官牙署，重门尽掩，阒其无人。正徘徊间，俄有数人拥一妇自远来，至此门外，将妇人上下衣服尽去之。妇犹少艾，微有姿首，莹然裸立，羞愧之状，殆不可堪。贾素负气，直前叱之曰：“若辈何人，敢肆无礼！”众微笑曰：“此何足异？”言未毕，门忽启，有数人扛一巨桶出，一吏执文书随其后而去。众即拥裸妇人，贾亦随入。历数门，至一广庭，见男女数百，或坐，或立，或卧，而皆裸无寸缕。堂上坐一官，其前设大榨床，健夫数辈，执大铁叉，任意将男妇叉置槽内，用大石压榨之，膏血淋漓。下承以盆，盆满即挹注巨桶中。如是十余次，巨桶乃满，数人扛之出。官判文书付一吏，与同出。贾视吏，乃其已故邻人周达夫也，因前呼之。周惊曰：“子胡在此？此岂可久留邪？速从我出。”贾问桶中何物，周曰：“鸦片烟膏也。

    Explanation

    61

    An Elderly Scholar from Shaoxing, Wang Zhi Xu, recounts: At the end of the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was a man named Jia Shengan, also an elderly student. He once dreamed of arriving at a place that resembled a high official's office, with all the doors closed and the place empty. As he was wandering around, suddenly several people brought a woman from afar to the door. They removed all her clothes. The woman was still young and slightly attractive, standing naked with a face showing extreme shame and embarrassment. Jia, who was always proud, went forward and scolded them, saying, "Who are you people, daring to act so impolitely?" They smiled and said, "What is there to be surprised about?" Before they finished speaking, the door suddenly opened. Several people carried out a large barrel, and an official carrying documents followed behind and left. The group then took the naked woman, and Jia followed inside. They passed through several doors and arrived at a large courtyard. They saw hundreds of men and women, some sitting, some standing, some lying down, all naked. On the dais sat an official, in front of whom was set up a large press. Several strong men held large iron forks and placed men and women at will into the slots. They pressed them with large stones, and blood and fat flowed everywhere. Below, a basin caught the liquid; when it was full, they poured it into the large barrel. This was done over ten times, and the large barrel was finally full. Several people carried it out. The official handed the documents to an official and left with him. Jia looked at the official and saw that it was his deceased neighbor Zhou Dafu. So he called out to him. Zhou was startled and said, "Why are you here? This is not a place to stay long. Come out with me quickly." Jia asked what was in the barrel. Zhou said, "Opium paste."

    62

    ”时鸦片烟未行，贾不知有此名目，因问鸦片烟何物？周曰：“方今承平日久，生齿繁衍，宜有大劫销除。而自来大劫，无过水火刀兵之类。遇此劫者，贤愚同尽，福善祸淫之说往往至此而穷。是以上帝命诸神会议，特创鸦片烟劫，借世间罂粟花汁熬炼成膏，供人吸食。食此烟者在劫中，不食此烟者不在劫中，听其人之自取，不得归咎于造物之不仁。而有此劫以销除繁衍之数，则水火刀兵诸劫，可以十灭五六矣。然罂粟本属草花，自古有之，其汁淡薄，不能熬膏。

    Explanation

    62

    During the time when opium had not yet spread, merchants were unaware of this substance. They asked, "What is opium?" Zhou replied, "At present, peace has lasted for a long time, and the population has grown. There should be a great disaster to reduce the numbers. Historically, the greatest disasters have been those of water, fire, and weapons. Those who encounter such a disaster, whether wise or foolish, perish together, and the saying that good deeds bring good fortune and evil deeds bring misfortune often fails in such cases. Therefore, the Creator commanded the assembly of gods to create a special disaster in the form of opium, using the juice of the poppy flowers from this world to be refined into a paste for people to consume. Those who consume this opium are in the disaster, while those who do not consume it are not. Let people take what they deserve, and one cannot blame the Creator for being unkind. With this disaster to reduce the population, the disasters of water, fire, and weapons can be diminished by tenfold, with five or six of them being eliminated. However, the poppy is originally a grass flower, and it has existed since ancient times. Its juice is thin and cannot be refined into a paste."

    63

    故又命九幽主者，于无间地狱中，择取不忠、不孝、无礼义廉耻诸罪魂，录送此间，榨取膏血，转付地上山陵原隰坟衍之神。使将此膏血灌入罂粟花根内，自根而上达花苞，则其汁自然浓郁，一经熬炼，光色黝然。子试识之，数十年后，此烟遍天下矣。”贾欲更有所问，忽又有人驱数十男妇至，鞭策甚苦，齐声呼号。贾悸而醒，以语人，人无信者。至道光中叶后，鸦片烟果盛行，而贾已前死矣。然其语犹在人耳，故其时皆言鸦片烟中有死人膏血，实由此语传讹也。

    Explanation

    63

    Therefore, the ruler of the nine underworlds was again commanded to select from the Avici Hell those souls guilty of disloyalty, filial impiety, lack of propriety, righteousness, integrity, and shame, and to record and send them to this place. The marrow and blood were extracted from these souls and then delivered to the gods of mountains, hills, plains, lowlands, and mounds on earth. These gods were instructed to pour this marrow and blood into the roots of opium poppies, from the roots up to the flower buds. Then, its juice naturally became rich and thick, and once refined, it shone with a dark luster. You should recognize it; in several decades, this smoke will spread throughout the world. The merchant wanted to ask more questions, but suddenly, someone drove dozens of men and women to the place, whipping and driving them very harshly, all crying out together. The merchant was startled awake and told others, but no one believed him. By the middle of the Daoguang era, opium smoking indeed became widespread, but the merchant had already died before. However, his words still lingered in people's ears, so at that time, it was said that opium contained the marrow and blood of the dead, which was actually a misinterpretation of his words.

    64

    楚士吕凤梧，游于姑苏。于舟中见一女子，美而艳。来桡去楫，一瞬即过，然思之盈盈在目也。是夕就枕，梦有人告曰：“舟中人，汝妻也。”吕固未娶，不能无动，然无可踪迹，亦姑置之。明年，以贡入成均，遂如京师。偶于琉璃厂见一画，画中一女子像，酷似舟中人。上有诗云：“新妆宜面出帘来，共数庭花几朵开。我比敬君差解事，不曾轻去画齐台。”吕不知敬君事，惘然莫测，姑以青蚨一贯买得之。是岁，以知县{}分江西，与同官沈君甚相得。沈君者，苏人也，一日至吕斋中，见画大惊，曰：“此亡妇像，仆所手绘。昔岁在京师，亡一箧，遂失此帧。

    Explanation

    64

    Lü Fengwu, a man from the state of Chu, traveled to Suzhou. On a boat, he saw a beautiful and charming woman. She came and went with swift oars, and in an instant, she was gone, but the memory of her was vivid in his mind. That night, as he lay down to sleep, he dreamed of someone telling him, "The woman on the boat is your wife." Lü had not yet married, and he could not remain unmoved, but there was no trace or lead, so he set the matter aside for now. The following year, he entered the Imperial Academy as a scholar-official and then went to the capital city. By chance, he saw a painting in the famous area of Beijing known for its antique shops and cultural artifacts. The painting depicted a woman who looked very similar to the one he had seen on the boat. On the painting, there was a poem that read: "Freshly made up, she comes out from behind the curtain. Together, we count how many flowers in the courtyard have bloomed. I am more understanding than you, but still not as wise. I did not lightly leave the painted terrace." Lü did not know about the story of "敬君," and he was confused and unable to understand, so he bought the painting for a thousand coins. That year, he was appointed as a county magistrate in Jiangxi Province and got along very well with his colleague Shen, who was also from Suzhou. One day, Shen visited Lü's study and saw the painting, greatly surprised, saying, "This is a portrait of my deceased wife, painted by me personally. In the past years in the capital city, I lost a box, and thus lost this painting."

    65

    君得无于都门市上得之乎？”吕曰：“然则仆曾见君夫人。”因告以吴门舟中相遇事。沈曰：“否，否。吾妇前一年已物故矣。”吕曰：“若然，何相似之甚？”沈曰：“此必吾姨也。吾妻父生二女，面目相同，虽家人不能辨别。长即亡妇，君所见者其妹也。”吕因以梦中语告，沈曰：“吾姨固待聘者，当为君作蹇修。”竟宛转媒合之，一时以为佳话。按《说苑》载齐王起九成之台，募国中能画者，赐之钱。有敬君居常饥寒，其妻妙色，敬君工画台，贪赐画钱，去家日久，思忆其妻，画像对之而笑。沈所用敬君事即此。然事见《艺文类聚》所引，今本《说苑》无此文也。

    Explanation

    65

    Did you not obtain it at the city gate market?" Lü replied, "Then I have seen your wife." He then told him about the encounter in the boat in Wu Gate. Shen said, "No, no. My wife passed away a year ago." Lü asked, "If so, why is the resemblance so striking?" Shen said, "This must be my aunt. My father-in-law had two daughters who looked the same, even family members could not distinguish between them. The elder is my deceased wife, and the one you saw is her younger sister." Lü then recounted the dream to him. Shen said, "My aunt was indeed waiting to be married, and I will arrange the match for you." Finally, he arranged the marriage through intermediaries, and it became a tale of beauty. According to the Shuo Yuan (Book of Sayings), King Qi of Qi built the Nine-Tiered Terrace, recruited talented painters from the country, and rewarded them with money. There was a man named Jing Jun who often suffered from hunger and cold, and his wife was beautiful. Jing Jun was skilled in painting terraces and coveted the reward money, so he was away from home for a long time. He missed his wife and painted her likeness, smiling at it. The story of Jing Jun used by Shen is this. However, this story is found in the Yiwen Leiju (Classified Collection of Literature and Art) but not in the current edition of the Shuo Yuan.

    66

    滁州府来安县民刘某，夜起如厕，闻墙外语曰：“不食三日矣，蒙神许我明日以前村张氏童养妇供我一饱。汝与三郎亦得与焉，幸无他往。”刘讶其语，梯而观之，则墙外二狼相对坐，大骇而下。因思张氏妇，其族女也，天明趋往告之。女甫十三岁，是日遂闭门不出。姒妇笑谓之曰：“诳汝耳，岂有狼而人语者。”及暮，姒使至门外曳柴供晚炊，女不可。姒乃自往门外取之，且四顾曰：“狼何在？”言未既，一狼自积薪中出，冲姒倒，入门，衔女去。姒惊呼，邻人咸集，见狼犹未远，逐之。将及矣，忽路旁又出二狼，遂并驱而去。

    Explanation

    66

    A Man from Lai'an County, Chuzhou Prefecture

    One night, a man named Liu from Lai'an County, Chuzhou Prefecture, got up to go to the toilet. He heard someone speaking from outside the wall: "We have not eaten for three days, and the gods have promised us that we can be fully fed by the betrothed daughter of the Zhang family in the village tomorrow. You and the third son can also partake, please do not go elsewhere." Liu was surprised by the words and climbed the ladder to look. He saw two wolves sitting face to face outside the wall, and he was greatly frightened and descended. Thinking of the Zhang family's betrothed daughter, who was his clan's daughter, he went to inform her early in the morning. The girl was just thirteen years old, and that day she closed the door and did not leave. Her older sister-in-law laughed and told her, "You are just being fooled; how could there be wolves that speak like humans?" By evening, the older sister-in-law sent someone to fetch firewood for the evening meal, but the girl refused. The older sister-in-law then went herself to fetch it and looked around, saying, "Where is the wolf?" Before she finished speaking, one wolf emerged from the pile of firewood, charged at the older sister-in-law and knocked her down, entered the house, and carried the girl away. The older sister-in-law screamed, and neighbors gathered. They saw that the wolf was still nearby and chased after it. They were about to catch up, when suddenly two more wolves appeared from the side of the road, and they all fled together.

    67

    吴宾香者，湖南沅陵士族女也。幼孤，依其舅寓居武陵。咸丰四年，粤寇至，舅家咸避去，独女随其舅居危城中。城破，女谓舅速去。舅曰：“若母以汝托吾，吾舍汝去，何面目见汝母地下乎？”女曰：“甥累舅非一日，今危急之际，岂忍复以孱躯为舅累哉！”入室，雉经死。越数日，贼入其居。时方酷暑，讶其尸久而不臭。解视之，衣皆缝纫，胸前得一诗云：“城破身难辰，途危志愈贞。千秋名节重，两字死生轻。未报君亲德，羞怀儿女情。愿将三尺组，毕此百年身。”汉口镇袁家湾有老木工，年六十三矣。二子未娶，俱执父业，而兄弟不相能。偶因口角，兄举斧斫弟之头，弟即死。邻人欲缚其兄送官，父泣曰：“老朽止此不肖子二，今幼子已死非命，而长子复抵法，则暮年何恃焉？”众哀其言，各散去，其事竟已。余按纪文达笔记中载有一事，与此相类，天下事竟有情与理两妨者。律载重囚无子者许其妻子入狱，此或可以通其变乎？

    Explanation

    67

    Wu Binxiang, a woman from a scholarly family in Hunan's沅陵 County, was orphaned at a young age and lived with her uncle in Wu Ling. In the fourth year of Xianfeng (1854), when the Taiping rebels arrived, her relatives all fled, but she stayed with her uncle in the besieged city. When the city fell, she urged her uncle to leave quickly. Her uncle replied, "Your mother entrusted you to me. How can I face your mother in the afterlife if I abandon you?" She responded, "I have been a burden to you for a long time. How could I bear to be a burden to you again in this critical moment?" She then went into the room and committed suicide by hanging. Several days later, the rebels entered her residence. It was a hot summer, and they were surprised that her body had not decomposed after so long. Upon examining her, they found that her clothes were still neatly sewn, and on her chest was a poem: "The city fell, and it was a difficult time for me. The journey was dangerous, but my resolve became even more steadfast. The reputation for integrity is valued for a thousand autumns. The words 'life' and 'death' are light. I have not yet repaid the kindness of my sovereign and parents. I am ashamed to harbor feelings of love and affection. I wish to end my life with a three-foot length of rope, to complete my hundred years of life."

    In Yuanjiawan, Hankou, there was an elderly carpenter who was sixty-three years old. His two sons had not yet married and both followed their father's profession, but they did not get along well. One day, due to a quarrel, the older brother raised an axe and struck his younger brother's head, killing him instantly. The neighbors wanted to tie up the older brother and take him to the authorities. The father wept and said, "I am an old man with only these two unworthy sons. Now the younger son has died an unnatural death, and the older son will also face legal consequences. What can I rely on in my old age?" Moved by his words, the people dispersed, and the matter ultimately ended.

    I have read in Ji Wen Da's notes about a similar incident, showing that there are situations in the world where both emotion and reason are at odds. The law allows the wife and children of a serious prisoner to enter the prison. Perhaps this can be adapted to such situations?

    68

    山右侯氏，在国初钜富，传闻其始富甚奇。有夫妇二人，穷而无子，然每日必祷于神，愿得横财，即死无恨。如是数十年无验，而夫妇则已老矣。一夕就寝，忽闻地上有声鬻然，如釜溢起，视之则遍地皆元宝涌出。惊喜，捉取之，而愈取愈多，至于不胜取，二老皆力竭而仆。有族子者，少孤未娶，所居相距不远，每日至其家助炊汲。是日至，而门不启，叩之不应，逾垣入视，则老翁已为元宝压死，老妇仅存一息，尚能言，未几亦死。族子葬埋之，拥其所有，为富人，乾隆末始衰。

    Explanation

    68

    The Shan You Hou Family was immensely wealthy at the beginning of the dynasty, and it is said that the origin of their wealth was quite extraordinary. There was a couple who were poor and childless, yet they prayed to the gods every day, wishing for unexpected wealth, even if it meant dying without regret. For several decades, their prayers went unanswered, and by then, the couple had grown old. One night, as they were about to sleep, they suddenly heard a sound like a pot overflowing, and upon looking, they saw gold ingots spilling all over the ground. Overjoyed, they grabbed the ingots, and the more they took, the more there was, until they could no longer take any more, and both of them collapsed from exhaustion. There was a nephew, who was young, orphaned, and not yet married, living nearby and often helping them with cooking and fetching water. On that day, when he arrived, the door was not opened, and when he knocked, there was no response. He climbed over the wall and looked inside, only to find the old man crushed to death by the gold ingots, and the old woman barely alive, still able to speak, but she died shortly after. The nephew buried them, took possession of all their wealth, and became a wealthy man, but his fortune began to decline at the end of the Qianlong era.

    69

    英吉利国有帆船名夭仙尼，于同治十三年四月中开往亚甸。行六十余日，舟中失火，救之不得。舟师乃放小艇三，纵人逃命，登小艇者凡三十一人。行数日，一艇独后，望前两艇不复见。所赍粮食俱尽，死者二人，尚存六人，惟饮&lt；卤咸&gt；水度日，中有一人创议曰：“同死无益，苟一死而五人得生，是亦杀身成仁也。”乃相议以人为粮，削木为筹，掣得最短者任众杀食。已而创议之人掣得最短之筹。

    69

    The Kingdom of England had a fleet ship named Tiaoxianni, which set sail for Aden in the middle of the fourth month of the 13th year of the Tongzhi era. After more than sixty days of sailing, a fire broke out on the ship and could not be extinguished. The ship's crew then launched three small boats to allow people to escape for their lives, and a total of thirty-one people boarded the small boats. After several days, one boat lagged behind, and looking ahead, the other two boats were no longer visible. The supplies of food were all exhausted, and two people died, leaving six people alive. They only drank brackish water to survive. One person proposed an idea: "Dying together would be of no benefit; if one person dies so that five others can survive, this is also sacrificing oneself for righteousness." They then discussed using one person as food, cutting wood into sticks, and drawing the shortest stick to determine who would be killed and eaten. Soon after, the person who proposed the idea drew the shortest stick.

    70

    时有同载之水手名阿加士藐剌者，自以饮卤水多，恐不能活，愿代之死，而掣得短筹者执不可。乃杀而饮其血，食其肉，五人者遂不死。越数月，英国又有一大火轮船于亚非利加洲之南失火。二人逃小艇中，随风漂荡，数日不得崖岸，饥甚，竟食其同伴者以延残喘。观此二事，亦见航海之险矣。按晋杨泉《物理论》，汉末有管秋阳与弟及伴一人避乱俱行。天雨粮绝，谓其弟曰：“今不食伴，则三人俱死。”乃与弟共杀之，得粮达舍。后遇赦无罪。孔文举论此，以食之为是。梁元帝《金楼子》则以文举之论为悖逆之言。夫弱肉强食，禽兽之事，使饥而相食，则人何以异于禽兽乎？

    Explanation

    70

    There was a sailor named Agas Smolits who, having drunk too much saltwater, feared he could not survive and offered to die in place of his companions. However, the one who drew the short straw refused. They then killed him, drank his blood, and ate his flesh, and all five survived. Several months later, another large British steamship caught fire off the coast of Africa. Two people escaped in a small boat, drifting with the wind for several days without reaching land. They were starving and, in desperation, ate their companion to prolong their lives. These two incidents also illustrate the dangers of sea travel. According to the Wu Li Lun by Yang Quan of the Jin dynasty, during the late Han period, there was a man named Guan Qiuyang who, along with his brother and a companion, fled during a time of chaos. Rained out and running out of food, he told his brother, "If we do not eat the companion, all three of us will die." They then killed their companion, obtained food, and reached safety. Later, they were pardoned and found not guilty. Kong Wenju argued that eating the companion was justified, but Liang Yuandi, in his Jin Lou Zi, criticized this view as contrary to proper conduct. Indeed, the weak being prey to the strong is a matter of animal behavior; if people resort to cannibalism when starving, what distinguishes them from animals?

    71

    华亭顾秉藻，幼而慧，父母皆奇爱之。咸丰十一年，与诸昆弟奉其母避兵沪上，得疾而卒。临终，牵母衣，请以仲兄子礼枢为嗣，母泣而许之。无何，母亦卒。及乱定还里，诸昆弟将如母命，而以秉藻未娶，不得有嗣。适金山钱氏有女，未许嫁死，与秉藻年相若也，遂媒合之。仿迎娶之礼，迎其柩归，合葬于秉藻之墓。好事者为赋催妆诗。按此事自古有之。迁葬嫁殇，媒氏职其明禁，而郑司农注《周官》曰“今时娶会”是也，则汉代固有此风。魏武帝为邓哀王聘甄氏亡女合葬，亦循世俗之见而已。

    Explanation

    71

    A Man from Hua Ting Named Gu Bin Cao

    Gu Bin Cao, a man from Hua Ting, was young and intelligent, and both his parents loved him extraordinarily. In the eleventh year of the Xianfeng era, he accompanied his mother to Shanghai to avoid the war, and fell ill and died. On his deathbed, he pulled his mother's clothes and requested that his second brother's son, Li Chu, be adopted as his heir. His mother wept and agreed to it. Shortly after, the mother also died. When the chaos subsided and they returned home, the other brothers intended to follow the mother's command, but because Bin Cao had not married, he could not have an heir. Fortunately, there was a daughter of the Qian family from Jinshan who died before being betrothed, and she was about the same age as Bin Cao. They then arranged a marriage, following the wedding ceremony to welcome her coffin back, and buried her together with Bin Cao's tomb. Those who were fond of such things composed a poem about the wedding preparations. It should be noted that such things have existed since ancient times. To move a grave and marry a deceased person, the matchmaker's role is to enforce the clear prohibition, and Zhang Shi, the Agricultural Official, commented on the Rites of Zhou saying 'the current practice of marrying a deceased person' is correct. The Han Dynasty indeed had this custom. Emperor Wei Wu arranged for the marriage and burial of a deceased daughter of the Zhen family for the Duke of Deng, simply following the common practice.

    72

    苏州袁氏女许嫁吴氏子，未婚嫁而遇庚申之乱，两家咸徙避。及乱定，女从父兄复还。父故业贾，复理旧业，居积颇赢。而吴氏则人亡家破，仅存老孀妇，即女之姑也。女请于父，愿适吴氏，事孀姑。父不可，且谋别嫁之，女断发自誓，因亦不强也。同治十三年，有吴氏亲串自秦中归，言与吴氏子同被掠，转展至江西，吴氏子于某年月日死。女闻大恸，谓父曰：“今日当从儿志矣。如不许，愿死之。”父不得已，归诸吴焉。此女不以贫富生死易心，可谓贤矣。明归熙甫著论，极言女未嫁夫死守节之非，近时汪容甫亦主此说。余颇不谓然。《列女传》曰：卫寡夫人者，齐侯之女也，嫁于卫。至城门，而卫君死。女人，行三年之丧，作诗曰：“我心匪石，不可转也。我心匪席，不可卷也。”如执归、汪之说，则卫女之诗，圣人何取而录之哉！忆前在沪上，应敏斋同年以所记张贞女事见示，余书其后，说此义颇详，今存文集中。

    72

    A young woman from the Yuan family in Suzhou was engaged to a young man from the Wu family. Before they were married, they encountered the Gengshen Uprising. Both families fled to take refuge. When the chaos subsided, the young woman returned with her father and brother. Her father, who had previously been a merchant, resumed his old business and accumulated a considerable amount of wealth. However, the Wu family was devastated, with only an elderly widow remaining, who was the young woman's mother-in-law. The young woman requested her father to allow her to marry the young man and serve her widowed mother-in-law. Her father initially refused and considered finding another match for her. The young woman cut her hair as a vow and persisted in her request. Unable to dissuade her, her father eventually agreed to the marriage. This young woman did not change her mind due to poverty, wealth, or the death of her fiancé, and she is truly virtuous. In the Ming dynasty, Gu Xiancheng wrote extensively criticizing the practice of widows remaining chaste after their husband's death, and in recent times, Wang Rong also supported this view. I do not agree with these perspectives. The Lienü Zhuan states: "The widow of the State of Wei was the daughter of the ruler of Qi and married into the State of Wei. When she reached the city gate, the ruler of Wei died. She observed the three-year mourning period and composed a poem: 'My heart is not a stone, it cannot be turned; my heart is not a mat, it cannot be rolled up.' If one follows the views of Gu and Wang, why did the sages select and record this poem?" I recall that before in Shanghai, my contemporary Ying Minzai showed me the story of Zhang贞女, and I wrote about it in detail in my collection of essays.

    73

    广东开平县城外有张桥村，皆张姓所居，钜族也。有张某者，聘杨氏女，未娶而卒。杨女衰经入门，拜见舅姑，筑室于夫之坟茔，居三日，乃还。张氏因择近支一人为之后，杨抚之，至于成人，为聘苏氏女。婚有日矣，而子又卒。苏女请仍以其日至夫家成服。杨曰：“是又一我也，此事不易。”固却之，苏女不可，乃如其志。姑妇二女并以未嫁女守义，可谓难矣。闻苏女嗣子已有声黉序间，或两节妇之报乎？

    　　苏州城外有小家女，幼受某氏聘。及女长，父母俱死，独与兄居。闻婿游荡，人于下流，心鄙之，有背盟意。乃以所积女红赀洋钱十枚付其兄，属交原媒偿男氏聘币，索还婚书，其兄亦无赖，尽以供饮博，而伪为婚书以绐其妹。女不知也，以为真与绝矣，示意邻媪，使为媒妁，遂与城中护龙街王姓者成婚。其前聘夫知女归王氏，谋劫之。以王氏有备，不果，即于其日讼于官。官鞫得实，笞其兄三百，判女仍归前聘夫。

    Explanation

    73

    In the outskirts of Kai Ping County, Guangdong, there is a village named Zhangqiao, inhabited by the Zhang family, a prominent clan. There was a man named Zhang who had engaged a daughter of the Yang family, but he died before the marriage could take place. The Yang woman entered her husband's home in mourning attire, paid respects to her father-in-law and mother-in-law, built a house near her husband's tomb, and stayed for three days before returning. The Zhang family then chose a close relative to be the heir, and the Yang woman raised the child until he grew up and arranged for him to marry a daughter of the Su family. Just before the wedding, the son died. The Su woman requested to go to her husband's family and put on mourning clothes. The Yang woman said, "This is another instance like mine; this matter is not easy." She firmly refused, but the Su woman insisted, and ultimately, the Yang woman agreed to her wishes. Both the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law remained chaste widows, which is indeed a difficult feat. It is said that the Su woman's son has already made a name for himself in the schools, perhaps as a reward for the two chaste widows?

    Outside Suzhou City, there lived a girl from a small family who had been engaged to a man since her youth. When she grew up, her parents both died, and she lived alone with her brother. She heard that her fiancé was a wastrel and had fallen into disrepute, and she despised him, harboring thoughts of breaking off the engagement. She gave her brother ten silver dollars, which she had saved from her needlework, and instructed him to give it to the original matchmaker to repay the dowry and request the return of the marriage contract. Her brother, being a good-for-nothing, spent all the money on gambling and forged a marriage contract to deceive his sister. The girl did not know about this, believing that the engagement had truly been broken off. She indicated to a neighboring woman to act as a matchmaker and arranged to marry a man from the Wang family on Hulong Street in the city. Her former fiancé, learning that she had married the Wang family, plotted to kidnap her. However, the Wang family was prepared, and the plan failed. That same day, the matter was taken to court. The judge investigated the truth and, after beating her brother with a hundred lashes, ruled that the girl should return to her former fiancé.

    74

    以律以礼，固应尔也，然女之不能安其室，则可卜矣。番禺大石乡有某甲，生三子一女，次子早卒。某年为长子娶妇何氏，而以其女许嫁陈氏子，即何氏之从母昆弟也。何与女极相得，女偶刺绣，何见而欢曰：“工妙至此，可谓针神，如有才无命何！”女怪其语。固问之。何曰：“姑之婿乃寒家葭莩亲也，虽美如冠玉，然狎游无度，已染恶疾，亏其体矣。”女闻之，不食累日。母问之，以告，母曰：“或传闻误也。”而女以嫂言为信，一日自断其发。
    母大惊，呼妇至，咎其多言，何亦大惊，曰：“姑太不为我地矣！我与姑相爱如姊妹，故隐以相闻，而姑遽为此决绝之事。今尊章见责，我何以堪！”甲夫妇乃使原媒至婿家求绝。婿家不可，反欲讼甲背盟。甲夫妇追事所由起，日诟何。何不能忍，亦翦发投地曰：“新妇知罪矣，请偕小姑长斋绣佛以赎罪，可乎？”于是举家大详。甲子适他出，促之归，婉劝其妇与妹，而仍使人卑词求绝于陈。陈不得已，许之。

    Explanation

    74

    According to law and propriety, it should indeed be so, yet if a woman cannot settle in her household, it can be predicted. In the Dashi village of Panyu, there lived a man named Mr. A, who had three sons and one daughter. His second son died young. In a certain year, he married his eldest son to a woman from the He family, and arranged for his daughter to marry a son of the Chen family, who was also a cousin of the He family. The woman and the girl got along very well. Once, when the girl was embroidering, the woman saw it and said happily, "Skillful to this extent, you may be called a needle god! What a pity if you have talent but no luck!" The girl found her words strange and asked her why she said that. The woman replied, "Your sister-in-law's husband is a distant relative of a poor family, although he is as beautiful as jade, he indulges in promiscuous behavior without restraint, has contracted a venereal disease, and damaged his body." Upon hearing this, the girl refused to eat for several days. Her mother asked her about it, and she told her. The mother said, "Perhaps the news was wrong." But the girl believed her sister-in-law's words and, one day, cut off her own hair.

    The mother was greatly shocked, called her wife over, blamed her for talking too much, and the woman was also greatly shocked, saying, "My mother-in-law is not being fair to me! I love my sister-in-law like a sister, so I hinted indirectly, but my sister-in-law did this extreme action immediately. Now that my husband's mother is blaming me, how can I bear it!" Mr. A and his wife then sent the matchmaker to the groom's family to request the termination of the engagement. The groom's family refused and instead wanted to sue Mr. A for breaking the engagement. Mr. A and his wife investigated the cause of the incident and scolded the woman daily. The woman could no longer endure it and also cut off her hair and threw it on the ground, saying, "I, the new wife, know I have sinned. May I observe a long-term vegetarian diet and sew Buddhist images with my younger sister to atone for my sin, is that acceptable?" Thus, the whole family was in a state of mourning. Mr. A went on a journey, urged them to return, gently persuaded his wife and sister-in-law, and still sent someone to humbly request the termination from the Chen family. The Chen family, unwillingly, agreed to it.

    75

    此女未达韩诗《苡》之义，殊不可训，亦未知其卒如何也。李绳式字肯堂，广东韶州府人。本窭人子，以居积起家，暮年居然素封矣。生平所值，多是九数，盖其父于四十九岁生绳武，至六十九岁而卒。绳武十九岁始出学贾，五十九岁归老于家。生九子九女。六十九岁时，其继室年三十九，又生一子。未几，其第四子卒，仍为九子。子各娶一妇，无娶妾者，女之夫亦无娶妾者。九子九妇九女九婿，有孙十九人，外孙男女二十九人。光绪元年正月初九日，其九十生日也。是亦熙朝人瑞矣。

    　　江北一妇，与其姑不相得，常勃溪。一日，妇吞金指环自尽，宛转欲绝。有人教以用羊胫骨烧炭研末，饣易饣唐调服。次日，金环从大便出，竟无恙。张少渠为余言，因书之以广其传。

    Explanation

    75

    This woman did not understand the meaning of the poem "Yi" from the Han dynasty, and it was very difficult to correct her, and she also did not know what would happen to her in the end. Li Shengshi, known as Kenting, was from Shaoguan Prefecture in Guangdong Province. Originally a poor man's son, he became wealthy through hoarding goods, and in his old age, he unexpectedly became a wealthy man. Throughout his life, he encountered many things related to the number nine. His father was forty-nine years old when he gave birth to Reng Wu, and he died at sixty-nine. Reng Wu started learning trade at nineteen and returned home to live out his days at fifty-nine. He had nine sons and nine daughters. At sixty-nine, his second wife was thirty-nine and gave birth to another son. Soon after, his fourth son died, but he still had nine sons. Each son married one wife, and none took concubines, and the husbands of the daughters also did not take concubines. There were nine sons, nine wives, nine daughters, and nine sons-in-law, with nineteen grandchildren and twenty-nine grandnieces and grandnephews. On the ninth day of the first month of the first year of Guangxu's reign, it was his ninetieth birthday. This is also a rare blessing of the prosperous era.

    A woman in the north of the river had a bad relationship with her mother-in-law and often quarreled. One day, the woman swallowed a gold ring and tried to commit suicide, writhing in pain. Someone taught her to use burned and ground sheep leg bones mixed with food to take. The next day, the gold ring came out through her stool, and she was fine. Zhang Shaqu told me this, so I wrote it down to spread its fame.

    76

    某甲家小康，而数遭讼事，耗其财，郁结成疾，殆将不起。有丐踵门，自言能医。延之入视，已不食三日矣，气垂欲绝，而腹中尚咻咻动。丐曰：“是中有物，非吾所能治，当请吾师来。”以二十日为期，其家人曰：“旦夕不能保，可再旬乎？”丐出药授之，曰：“服此可待矣。”服之，果如熟寐者然，虽不食，竟不死。越二十日，一僧来曰：“丐所荐也。”入视病者，于钵中出一丸药，用铁丝系之，纳病者喉间，频牵曳之，复以手摩其腹。良久，病者口吻微动，僧摩腹益亟，仍牵曳其铁丝。忽突然掣出，即有一物随药而出，遽投诸地，乃一小蛇也。僧以药少许糁之，蛇化为水，病良已。人问蛇何自生，僧曰：“怨毒之气所结也。老僧不惟治病，且消其怨气，毋令与所讼者来生再冤孽纠缠也。”其家厚酬之，不受而去。此僧此丐，殆仙佛化身欤？

    Explanation

    76

    A family named A lived in moderate prosperity, but they frequently encountered lawsuits that depleted their wealth, leading to a buildup of resentment that turned into illness, almost causing them to die. A beggar knocked on their door, claiming he could cure them. They invited him in to examine the patient, who had not eaten for three days and was on the verge of death, though there was still a faint movement in his abdomen. The beggar said, "There is something inside, which I cannot treat; I must ask my teacher to come." He set a period of twenty days. The family responded, "We cannot guarantee survival day or night; can we extend it to another ten days?" The beggar gave them a medicine, saying, "Take this, and you will be able to wait." After taking the medicine, the patient seemed to be in a deep sleep, and although he did not eat, he did not die. After twenty days, a monk came, saying, "The beggar recommended me." He examined the patient and took a pill from his bowl, tying it to an iron wire and inserting it into the patient's throat, then pulling it back and massaging his abdomen. After a long time, the patient's mouth began to move slightly, and the monk massaged his abdomen more vigorously while continuing to pull the iron wire. Suddenly, he pulled it out, and a small snake followed the medicine out, falling to the ground. The monk sprinkled a little more medicine on the snake, and it turned into water. The patient was completely cured. Someone asked the monk where the snake came from, and he replied, "It was the accumulation of toxic resentment. I not only treat the illness but also dissipate the resentment, so that the person involved in the lawsuit will not be entangled in karmic retribution in future lives." The family offered a generous reward, but the monk refused and left. Could this monk and beggar be divine or Buddhist incarnations?

    77

    姑苏某寺僧，每岁举行佛会，托邻比一妪招致众善信。凡入会者咸输香钱数百文，僧与妪瓜分之。妇日以饶裕，而僧清贫如故。某岁除夕，索逋者踵于三门，僧无以应，竟亡去。明岁，里人以他僧主之。上元之夕，循例举佛会，仍托邻妪代为招致。事已，僧向妪索钱，妪迁延，久不与。一日，僧归自外，将入房，闻有声。入之，则有人在床，拥衾而卧。呼问之，妪也。问何至此，不应。僧将曳出之，甫一启衾，妪裸无寸缕。僧大惊，趋出，至妪家，招其子。子初若不信者，强拉之至。妪知事不谐，反诬僧。

    Explanation

    77

    A Monk from a Temple in Suzhou

    Every year, a monk from a temple in Suzhou held a Buddhist assembly, entrusting a neighbor, an old woman, to attract many devout believers. All who attended the assembly paid incense money, amounting to hundreds of coins, which the monk and the old woman divided equally. The old woman became increasingly wealthy over time, while the monk remained as poor as before. On New Year's Eve of one year, those demanding payment gathered at the temple's three gates, and the monk, unable to respond, eventually fled away. The following year, the locals appointed another monk to lead the assembly. On the evening of the Lantern Festival, they continued the tradition, still entrusting the old woman to act as an intermediary to attract attendees. After the event, the monk demanded payment from the old woman, but she procrastinated and refused for a long time. One day, the monk returned from outside, about to enter his room, when he heard a sound. Entering the room, he found someone on the bed, covered with a quilt and lying down. Calling out and asking, he discovered it was the old woman. He asked why she was there, but she did not respond. The monk, about to drag her out, just as he lifted the quilt, found the old woman naked, not wearing a single thread. Greatly shocked, he hastened out, went to the old woman's house, and called her son. At first, the son seemed not to believe, but the monk forcefully pulled him there. Knowing the matter was not going well, the old woman turned around and accused the monk.

    78

    僧入厨取刀，断一指，忍痛示之曰：“以此自明！”妪母子踉跄去。乃悟前僧之堕妇术中也。若有定力如后僧者，佛矣。有孝廉下第南归，病于逆旅，不携仆从，惟一车夫与之周旋。病月余乃愈，而负逆旅主人及车夫钱已数十缗，无以为计。车夫曰：“君行既无资，住又不可。此间有一馆，吾托人先容，当可成。君曷就之，稍积馆谷，再谋归计，何如？”孝廉喜而从之。已而车夫来告曰：“事谐矣。惟距此尚百余里，明日吾御君行也。”迟明首涂，所行殊非恒境，始则阡陌纵横，继则山径丛杂，间有小村聚，亦不知名。行三日，始达其家，雕墙峻宇，规制甚宏。然无多人，亦无与交一语者。

    Explanation

    78

    A monk entered the kitchen, took a knife, and cut off one of his fingers. Enduring the pain, he showed it to them and said, "This is to prove my innocence!" The woman and her son staggered away. He then realized that the previous monk had fallen into the woman's trap. If one had the steadfastness of the latter monk, they would be a Buddha.

    There was a scholar-official who failed his examination and returned to the south. He fell ill at an inn, having brought no servants or attendants with him. Only a coachman accompanied him. After being ill for over a month, he recovered, but he owed the innkeeper and the coachman tens of strings of cash and had no way to pay. The coachman said, "Since you have no means to travel, and staying here is also not an option, there is a guesthouse nearby. I have arranged for you through someone, and it should work out. Why don't you go there, save some money, and then plan your return? What do you think?" The scholar-official was pleased and agreed. Soon after, the coachman came to tell him, "The arrangement has been made. However, it is still over a hundred miles away. Tomorrow, I will drive you there." At dawn, they set out on their journey. The path they took was far from the usual. At first, they passed through fields with intersecting paths, then through winding mountain trails, occasionally passing small villages that they did not recognize. After traveling for three days, they finally reached his home. The walls were carved, the buildings were grand, and the layout was very extensive. However, there were not many people, and no one spoke to him.

    79

    车夫导之入，历十余重屋，至一院落，花木翳然，窗明几净，乃语之曰：“此下榻所也。所司惟笔墨事。主人适他出，并笔墨事亦无之，君但居此，无苦也。君所负钱，巳悉为君偿之，勿以为念。惟此间仆御不多，苦无伺候之人，但于壁间置轮盘以通饮食。君有所需，扣盘而语之，即得也。”言已辞去。孝廉独居是室，供馔颇丰。然居有余，不见一人，殊深疑虑。偶出散步，则诸屋悉加扃锁，不得而入。独一室未扃，入之，则有书十余匮。因携一册归，将以遣日。书面大书一“水”字，中多符咒，不可通晓，殊无意味。

    Explanation

    79

    The coachman led him in, passing through more than ten layers of houses, until they reached a courtyard where flowers and trees were lush and dense, with bright windows and clean tables. He then told him, "This is where you will stay. Your duty is only to handle writing and calligraphy. The master has just gone out, and there are no writing materials either. You can just stay here, there's no need to worry. The money you owe has already been paid off for you, do not worry about it. Only there are not many servants here, it is difficult to find someone to serve. There is only a wheel plate on the wall to order food. If you need anything, knock on the plate and speak, and you will get it." After saying this, he left. The scholar-gentleman lived alone in this room, with a plentiful supply of food. However, there was no one around, and he felt very suspicious. Occasionally, he went out for a walk, and then all the rooms were locked, preventing him from entering. Only one room was not locked, and upon entering it, he found more than ten cabinets of books. He took one volume back to pass the time. The cover had a large 'water' character, and it contained many talismans and incantations, which were not understandable and very uninteresting.

    80

    明日拟往易之，而迷其处所，因复持归，姑置案头，又居数月，朔风戒寒，木叶尽脱，乡思颇切。偶啜茗，手披是书，见一符，屈曲如蛇，旁有咒语。戏以指醮杯中茗，画其符，并诵咒语。忽觉身在大水中，风涛澎湃，茫无畔岸。大惧，自分必死，姑闭目听其所之。食顷，忽履平地，衣履初不沾湿。道有行人，就之，问此何地，曰：“绍兴府也。”骇甚。孝廉本吴中人，距家非远，适有同年生宦于越，乃往谒之，助以资斧而归，既抵家，妻子迎问曰：“君馆某所，何遽言归？”问何以知之，曰：“数月前有客持百金来，言君之脯，寄家中，供薪水，因事冗故无书也。”孝廉益怪之，乃语其事，又虑为所踪迹，移家避之，后亦无他。或曰其符乃术家水遁法，孝廉已不能记忆，且亦不敢试也。

    Explanation

    80

    Tomorrow, I planned to go and exchange it, but I lost its location and brought it back, temporarily placing it on the desk. After a few months, the north wind warned of the coming cold, and all the leaves fell off, making my longing for home very intense. Casually drinking tea, I opened the book with my hands and saw a talisman twisted like a snake, with a spell beside it. Playfully, I dipped my finger in the tea cup and drew the talisman, reciting the spell. Suddenly, I felt myself in a large body of water, with waves surging and boundless shores. Greatly afraid, I expected to die, so I temporarily closed my eyes and listened to where it took me. After a meal, I suddenly walked on flat ground, and my clothes and shoes were not wet. There were passersby on the road, and I asked them what place this was. They said, "It is Shaoxing Prefecture." I was very shocked. The scholar-official is originally from Wu, not far from home, and just happens to have a fellow student who is an official in Yue. I then went to visit him, helping him with money and returning home. After arriving home, my wife and children greeted and asked, "You were staying at a certain place, why suddenly say you're returning?" They asked how they knew this. I said, "A few months ago, a guest brought a hundred gold pieces and said that you were mending your skin, sending it home to provide for daily expenses, and because of busy affairs, there was no letter." The scholar-official finds it even more strange and tells the story. He also worries about being followed and moves the family to avoid it, later finding nothing else. Someone says that the talisman is a water遁 method of the magical arts, and the scholar-official cannot remember it anymore, and also dare not try it.

    81

    嘉庆间，有选人谒选，得贵州某县令，挈家赴任，从者颇多。至省会，谒大府毕，同僚语之曰：“此县瘠苦异常，万不可以眷属往。”乃留眷属于会垣，独行至郡，见太守。太守曰：“君所官非人境也。幸其印在吾处，君便可接印，不必前往矣。吾幕中适缺一书记，君文士，必娴翰墨，暂以奉屈，可乎？”令自念一官虽瘠，终胜幕下客，固欲往。太守乃命首县具车徒，且募一曾至此县者导之行。行数日而失涂，所遇皆苗僚异族，无可问。计其地不过五六百里，而行十许日未到。一日，导者告曰：“前途似是矣。”因共前进，至则贵垣环绕，若有城者。其中荆榛塞路，行颇不易。

    Explanation

    81

    During the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, there was an official candidate who was selected to serve as the magistrate of a certain county in Guizhou. He brought his family to take up the post, accompanied by many attendants. Upon reaching the provincial capital, he paid his respects to the high-ranking official and was advised by his colleagues that the county was extremely poor and harsh, and it was not advisable to bring his family along. He therefore left his family in the provincial capital and proceeded alone to the county, where he met the governor. The governor said, "The place you are to govern is not a human settlement. Fortunately, the seal is here with me, so you can take it and do not need to go there. My office is currently lacking a secretary, and as a scholar, you must be skilled in writing. Would you be willing to serve temporarily in this role?" The magistrate thought to himself that although the position was meager, it was still better than being a guest in the governor's office, so he decided to accept it. The governor then ordered the head of the county to prepare a carriage and attendants, and also hired someone who had been to this county to guide the journey. They traveled for several days and lost their way, encountering only ethnic minorities who could not be asked for directions. Calculating the distance, it should not have taken more than five or six hundred miles, yet they had been traveling for about ten days without reaching their destination. One day, the guide said, "The way ahead seems to be correct." They continued forward and eventually arrived at a fortified enclosure surrounded by walls, as if it were a city. The path was blocked by thorny bushes, making the journey quite difficult.

    82

    良久，见有土屋数间，其坏墙有字迹，审视之，则某县也，诧曰：“岂即公署乎？”呼其门，有人自草中出。问谁何，告之，其人叩首曰：“吾即县吏也。已二十余年无县令矣，不图今日复见令君。”问有隶役乎，曰：“曩固有之，今久无官，此辈皆散处各乡，不相闻久矣，不知其在亡也。”乃导之入，室中积土数尺，曰：“此公案也。”又其内荒冢累累。问此何人，吏曰：“皆前令君也，死于此，不能归骨，小人穴土瘗之耳。”令闻之丧气，即日言归。至郡中，太守以其始之固请往也，诮让之，流落数载，始得量移焉。此事曩在京师时闻之友人吴文南，并能言其县名，今记忆不真矣。边徼荒凉，事容有之，或谈者亦过其实乎？

    Explanation

    82

    For a long time, they saw several earthen houses, with damaged walls bearing inscriptions. Upon careful examination, they realized it was a certain county. In surprise, they exclaimed, "Could this be the government office?" They called out to the door, and someone emerged from the grass. When asked who he was, he identified himself. The man bowed his head and said, "I am the county official. There has been no county magistrate for over twenty years, and I did not expect to see you again today." They asked if there were any servants, and he replied, "There were indeed such things in the past, but now there has been no official for a long time. These people have all dispersed to various villages and have not been heard from for a long time; I do not know if they are still alive or dead." He then led them inside, where they found several feet of accumulated soil in the room, which he identified as the public desk. Inside, there were numerous abandoned graves. They asked who these people were, and the official replied, "They are all the previous county magistrates who died here and could not be returned to their hometowns for burial. We dug holes and buried them." The magistrate felt disheartened upon hearing this and decided to return immediately. Upon reaching the prefecture, the governor blamed him for having initially insisted on going, reproached him, and exiled him for several years before finally allowing him to be transferred to a less distant place. This story was heard from a friend named Wu Wenna in the capital, who could also name the county, but the details are now unclear. Such things may indeed occur in the remote and desolate frontier, or perhaps the storyteller is exaggerating?

    83

    文南言此时，因言薄宦远游，踪迹孤危，事有不可问者，国家定制，仕宦不得在五百里内，窃谓一命之员，从事万里，亦殊可怜，何不为之限制，使不得在一二千里外乎？乾、嘉间有某甲者，以县尉至滇南，莅任未一年而卒。无子，止一妻、一妻弟、一仆、一媪。居无何，妻弟亦死，仆媪皆散去，妻尚少艾，寄居民舍。久之无食，为人浣濯衣服以自给，十指流血，而不免饥寒。有邻媪者，在官之媒氏也，一日过而谓之曰：“何自苦乃尔。

    Explanation

    83

    In the southern region, the speaker addresses a specific moment, saying that a low-ranking official who travels far away has a solitary and precarious existence, with matters that are beyond inquiry. The state has a regulation that officials cannot hold positions within 500 miles of their hometown. The speaker suggests that even a low-ranking official serving far from home is pitiful and proposes imposing a stricter rule that officials should not be allowed to serve more than 1,000-2,000 miles away from their hometown.

    During the reigns of Emperors Qianlong and Jiaqing, there was a certain individual, a county magistrate, who traveled to Southern Yunnan. He died shortly after taking up his post. He had no children, and only one wife, one sister-in-law, one servant, and one old woman remained. After a while, the sister-in-law also died, and the servant and the old woman left, leaving the wife alone. She was still young and beautiful, living in a rented house. After a long time, she had no food and earned a living by washing clothes. Her hands bled, but she could not escape hunger and cold. One day, a neighbor, an old woman who was involved in matchmaking or social services related to officials, passed by and said to her, "Why are you suffering so much?"

    84

    今有一策，可暂救饥寒，能从之乎？”妇问何策，媪曰：“新到县官，少年佻，而慕道学名。喜笞妓，笞必去衣。妓耻之，以多金求免不得，又以多金募代己者，亦无其人。若能代之到官，吾当与诸妓约，受杖一，予钱千也。伍百诸人皆受妓赂，行杖必轻。且形体是而名氏非，初不为泉下人羞也。”妇以贫失志，竟从其策。嗣后邑有妓女应到官，悉此媪为介绍而代之，县中皂隶无不识者，皆笑其顽钝无耻也。然妇竟积二百余金，以其夫之丧归葬。余谓此妇受辱虽甚，然究未失身，不得谓之不贞。不惜父母之遗体，以归其夫之遗骸，不得谓之不义。君子哀其志，悲其遇，未可重訾之也。

    Explanation

    84

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    There is a strategy that can temporarily alleviate hunger and cold—would you be willing to follow it?” The woman asked what the strategy was. The old woman replied: “The new county official is a young man who is frivolous and seeks fame for his learning. He enjoys flogging prostitutes, and always strips them of their clothes. The prostitutes are ashamed of this, and though they try to bribe their way out, they fail. They also offer large sums to find someone to take their place, but no one is found. If you can take their place before the magistrate, I will make an agreement with the other prostitutes: each flogging earns a thousand coins. The five hundred officials all accept bribes from the prostitutes, so the floggings are always light. Moreover, while the body is there, the name is not, and you won’t be ashamed in the afterlife.” The woman, impoverished and desperate, ultimately followed the plan. Afterwards, whenever a prostitute needed to appear before the magistrate, they all came to this woman to arrange it. The officials in the county all knew her, and they all laughed at her for her foolishness and lack of shame. But the woman eventually accumulated over two hundred gold pieces, and she used the money to return and bury her husband. I say that although she suffered great humiliation, she did not lose her virtue, and cannot be called unchaste. She did not spare her parents' body to return and bury her husband's remains, and cannot be called unjust. Gentlemen should pity her resolve and lament her fate, and not harshly criticize her.

    85

    金陵乡间有某翁者，生一子，与邻村某氏联姻。兵乱，失其子。翁故为行贾，因转徙四方，收得一小儿，与其子年相若也，遂子之，而即名以子之名。及金陵收复，翁仍行贾于外，而某氏则已归矣。以女年长，将遣嫁，访得某翁所在，贻书告之。翁思己子虽失，幸有养子，可以赝代真也，乃作复书，订以是年夏秋间携子而归。及期果至，而故居无存，乃赁屋为娶妇计。两家均有成说，娶有日矣。翁之子忽归，归而不得其家，乃至邻村造妇翁之门而求见。妇翁大骇，因亲送至某翁家。子见父母，牵衣恸哭。翁欲不认，则真其子也；欲认之，又碍有妇翁在。乃曰：“此吾兄子，非吾子也。自幼失散，彼记忆不真耳。”妇翁归而疑焉。

    Explanation

    85

    In a rural area of Jinling, there lived an old man who had a son who was married to a woman from a neighboring village. During a period of military unrest, he lost his son. The old man, who was a trader by profession, traveled extensively around the four directions and adopted a little boy who was of similar age to his original son. He then adopted him as his own son and named him after his original son. When Jinling was recovered, the old man continued his trading activities outside, while the woman from the neighboring village had returned. As her daughter was growing up, she was to be married off. The old man was informed of this through a letter, and he thought that although he had lost his original son, he had a foster son who could serve as a substitute. He wrote a reply, agreeing to bring his son back during the summer or autumn of that year. When the time came, the son indeed arrived, but their old home was no longer there, so they rented a house to prepare for the wedding. Both families had already made arrangements, and the wedding was scheduled. Suddenly, the old man's original son returned, but he could not find his home and went to the neighboring village to seek out his father-in-law. The father-in-law was greatly surprised and personally escorted him to the old man's house. The son saw his parents, tore at their clothes, and cried bitterly. The old man was in a dilemma: if he did not recognize him, then he was truly his son; if he recognized him, he would be embarrassed by the father-in-law. So he said, "This is my brother's son, not my own son. He has been lost since childhood, and he doesn't remember accurately." The father-in-law returned home and became suspicious.

    86

    旋侦访得实，乃使媒氏告曰：“原聘者，吾婿也，他人子安得婿吾女？”某翁正&lt；止寺&gt；屠无计，而其养子知事且不谐，于人定后怀刃出门，突入妇翁之家。妇翁出，因左手把其袖，而右手以刃拟之曰：“尔女不我嫁，吾先杀尔及尔女。归杀婿，吾亦自杀，四人同日死矣！”妇翁见事急，请如期。曰：“不能待矣，事在今夕！”妇翁曰：“然则当释吾入内，略为小女治装，子姑小坐。”妇翁既入，谋集健仆缚而送之归。其妻曰：“非计也。

    Explanation

    86

    Soon after investigating, the truth was found, and he sent the matchmaker to inform him: "The one who originally betrothed my daughter is my son-in-law. How can someone else's son become my daughter's husband?" The old man was at a loss, but his adopted son knew the matter and it was not harmonious. After midnight, he carried a knife and went out, suddenly entering his mother-in-law's house. The father-in-law came out, and the adopted son grabbed his sleeve with his left hand, while holding a knife in his right hand and pointing it at him, saying: "If you do not marry me, I will first kill you and your daughter. Then I will return and kill the groom, and I will also commit suicide. All four of us will die on the same day!" Seeing the urgency, the father-in-law asked to proceed as scheduled.
