The adopted son replied, "It cannot be delayed, the matter is for tonight!" The father-in-law said, "Then you should let me go inside, and prepare my daughter for a bit. You may sit down for a while." After the father-in-law went inside, they planned to gather strong servants to bind and send him back. His wife said, "It is not a good plan."

    87

    如此，彼不愈恨乎？吾婿吾女，终必死其手。”“然则奈何？”妻曰：“彼有假子，吾何妨有假女。”乃潜自后户送其女至其叔父家，而饰一婢居青庐中，招婿入行礼。既成婚，乃语之曰：“汝不告而成婚，汝父必怒，毋遽言归，请留此满月何如？”喜曰：“诺。”妇翁使媒氏告某翁曰：“尔子已婿吾女矣。尔尚有兄之子，吾亦有弟之女。再以相配可乎？”翁亦喻其意，使其子为赘婿于女之叔父之家。既满月，妇翁择吉日送两婿两女偕归，其伉俪各相得，遂相安无事。

    Explanation

    87

    If so, wouldn't he hate us even more? Our son-in-law and daughter will surely die at his hands." "What then should we do?" The wife replied, "He has a stepson; why can't we have a stepdaughter?" Then she secretly sent her daughter through the back door to her uncle's house and dressed a maid to stay in the blue tent, bringing the groom in to perform the wedding rituals. After the marriage was completed, she then told him, "You didn't tell anyone and got married; your father will surely be angry. Don't rush to say you're leaving. How about staying here for a full month?" He happily said, "Yes." The father-in-law sent a matchmaker to tell Mr. So-and-so, "Your son has already married my daughter. You still have your brother's son, and I also have my younger brother's daughter. Can we match them again?" The father-in-law understood his intention and made his son a son-in-law at his daughter's uncle's house. After a full month, the father-in-law chose a good day to send the two sons-in-law and two daughters back together. They each got along well with their spouses and lived peacefully thereafter.

    88

    江西广昌人丘某，幼读书，困于童子试。忽梦至冥中，冥吏方据案决事，见丘曰：“此十名相公也，何遽至此？”麾之出，遂寤，以语人。或曰：“子必以第十名入学矣。”甲私念：“若果以第十名入学，则竟呼秀才可矣，何言十名相公乎？此殆非佳兆。”乃弃儒而贾，为同乡之卖茶者司笔墨事。春出秋归，岁以为常，绝意进取矣。一岁，归途过郡城，适值郡试，同学之友及亲串咸在，怂恿入试。丘不可，而众已为之在县注册，在府纳卷，乃勉就之。及府出圆榜，招覆试，果第十名也。丘见之惊悸，觉体中不佳，因不覆试而归。未至家，卒。噫！府试招覆，是何大事，其名次乃亦注冥籍乎？

    Explanation

    88

    A man from Guangchang, Jiangxi, named Qiu, read books as a child but struggled in the imperial examinations for children. One day, he suddenly dreamed of arriving in the underworld, where an official was seated at a desk adjudicating cases. Seeing Qiu, the official said, "This is a scholar who will pass as tenth place. Why are you here so soon?" He then beckoned Qiu out, and Qiu awoke, telling others about the dream. Someone said, "You will surely pass as the tenth candidate and enter the academy." Qiu thought to himself, "If I indeed pass as the tenth candidate, I can just call myself a scholar. Why would they call me a tenth-place scholar? This cannot be a good omen." He then abandoned his studies and became a merchant, serving as a clerk for a fellow villager who sold tea. He traveled to the capital in spring and returned in autumn, making this an annual routine, and he gave up on further advancement. One year, on his way home, he passed through the prefectural capital, where the prefectural examination was taking place. His classmates and relatives were all there, urging him to take the exam. Qiu refused, but they had already registered him in the county and submitted his papers to the prefecture. Reluctantly, he took the exam. When the prefecture issued the final list, they called for a re-examination, and Qiu was indeed ranked tenth. Seeing this, Qiu was filled with shock and unease, feeling unwell, and decided not to take the re-examination. Before he could return home, he died. Alas! The prefectural re-examination was such a major event, yet his name was already recorded in the records of the underworld.

    89

    桐乡沈宝樾，字茂庭，富而好施与。凡遇善事，必勇为之，远近有善士之名。庚申、辛酉之乱，避寇远出，其族姻从之者数十家。辛苦垫隘，卒不相舍，人尤称焉。初无子，中年后纳妾，生丈夫子二。邻有不戒于火者，火及其闾，天反风以免，咸曰为善之报也。乃于光绪元年冬日墙坏压死，向之称善者，至是而不能无疑。严芝生庶常，其友也，求余为文以释疑。余乃据《援神契》遭命之说及王充《论衡》福虚祸虚之义，为文以应之，娓娓千言。然王充所谓福虚祸虚者，余雅不以为然。

    Explanation

    89

    Shen Baoyue of Tongxiang, courtesy name Maoting, was wealthy and fond of giving. Whenever he encountered good deeds, he would bravely undertake them, earning a reputation as a virtuous person in both far and near places. During the chaos of the Gengshen and Xinyou years, he fled to avoid bandits, and dozens of his relatives and in-laws followed him. Enduring hardship and cramped conditions, they never parted, and people especially praised him for this. He had no son at first, but after middle age, he took a concubine and had two sons. A neighbor who was careless with fire saw his house burn, but the wind changed direction, sparing him; everyone said it was a reward for doing good. However, in the winter of the first year of Guangxu, he was crushed to death by a collapsing wall. Those who previously called him virtuous could not help but doubt him by then. Yan Zhisheng, a scholar-official and his friend, asked me to write an article to dispel doubts. I based my argument on the Yuan Shenqi (The Book of Divine Contracts) theory of fate and Wang Chong's Lunheng (Critical Essays) discussion of the illusory nature of fortune and misfortune, and wrote an article to respond, elaborating in a thousand words. However, I do not agree with Wang Chong's notion of the illusory nature of fortune and misfortune.

    90

    夫余殃余庆之文，著于《周易》；天人相应之理，备于《春秋》。苟祸福皆虚，则劝惩无寄矣。同其时，适有张少渠之事。少渠名豫立，光绪元年以县丞奉檄与于海运之役。凡从事海运者，皆至沪渎，附火轮船以行。有轮船名“福星”者，行有日矣，江苏海运局之官大半附是舟。少渠初亦预焉，适有一轮船先“福星”二日而首涂，少渠舍而从之。同袍之友争掺其祛，卒不为留。已而少渠安抵丁沽，“福星”轮船竟沉于海，坐是船者皆死焉。少渠平日亦乐为善事者也。光绪二年，少渠行年五十，乞言于余。

    Explanation

    90

    The text of Fu Yu and Yu Qing, concerning calamity and blessing, is recorded in the Zhouyi; the principle of correspondence between heaven and humanity is fully detailed in the Spring and Autumn Annals. If blessings and misfortunes were all illusory, then there would be no basis for encouragement or punishment. At the same time, there happened to be the case of Zhang Shaoyu. Zhang Shaoyu's given name is Yuli, and in the first year of Guangxu, he was appointed by edict as a county magistrate to participate in the maritime transport mission. All those involved in maritime transport went to Shanghai and boarded steamships to proceed. There was a steamship named "Fuxing" that was scheduled to depart, and most of the officials from the Jiangsu Maritime Transport Bureau boarded this ship. Zhang Shaoyu had initially planned to join, but just as another steamship departed two days before "Fuxing," Zhang Shaoyu changed his plan and boarded the other ship. His fellow officials tried to persuade him, but he did not change his mind. Later, Zhang Shaoyu safely arrived at Dinggu, while the "Fuxing" sank at sea, and all those on board perished. Zhang Shaoyu was someone who usually enjoyed doing good deeds. In the second year of Guangxu, when Zhang Shaoyu was fifty years old, he asked me for advice.

    91

    余因言少渠行善而得福报，宜益加勉，而力避王充之说。恐后之读者有矛盾之疑，故掇举其事，著于此编。杭州有老儒，以教读为生。一妻一女，家有余屋，因赁与某甲居之。居数岁，某甲迁居他处，然犹通问如常时，其眷属亦相往来也。一日，某甲使二人荷轿来迎其女去。及暮，老儒自馆中归，女犹未返，使人至某甲所迓之，则空无人矣。问其邻，言今日午前尽室而去，不知所之。老儒侦探无踪，惧为门户羞，不复深究。是女已许嫁，乃以暴卒告其婿氏，存亡听之矣。某甲挟女至江苏无锡，据为妻。

    Explanation

    91

    I have completed the translation of the source text. Here is the final, polished translation:

    I then said that Shaoqu had done good deeds and received blessings, and should strive even harder while avoiding Wang Chong's views. I feared that future readers might have doubts about this, so I gathered and recorded these events in this compilation. There was an elderly scholar in Hangzhou who earned a living by teaching. He had a wife and a daughter, and his home had an extra room, which he rented to a man named "A." After living there for several years, A moved to another place, but they still communicated as usual, and their families continued to visit each other. One day, A sent two people to carry a sedan chair to welcome his daughter away. By evening, the elderly scholar returned from his teaching post, but his daughter had not yet come back. He sent someone to A's residence to fetch her, but found it empty with no one there. When he asked his neighbors, they said that A and his entire family had left before noon, and no one knew where they had gone. The elderly scholar could find no trace of them, and fearing that this would bring shame to his family name, he did not pursue the matter further. The daughter had already been betrothed, so he informed her fiancé's family that she had died suddenly, leaving her fate to be decided by them. A took the daughter to Wuxi, Jiangsu, and claimed her as his wife.

    92

    而某甲故窭人，又慵惰，不能治生，转仰食于女。女工针黹，佣于苏州一宦家，月得洋钱二以畀其夫。而甲殊未餍，谋鬻女于妓家。女微闻之，无以自脱。忽有浙人来诣其主，女见其名纸，曰：“此吾杭之大姓，吾父尝教授于是家者也。”访知其所在，次日至其家求见，言家世及通家之谊，甚有条理。乃为招其父至，讼于官，笞逐某甲，而判归其女于父。老儒愀然不乐曰：“此女归，辱门户矣。”邵君步梅亦杭人官于苏者，旧与老儒相熟也，乃于吴下为此女相攸嫁一官人焉。余闻此事于步梅，以非美事，故不书其姓氏。

    Explanation

    92

    A Poor Man's Wife and Her Rescue

    There was a man named Mr. A, who was originally a poor and lazy person, unable to support himself, and thus relied on his wife for sustenance. His wife worked as a seamstress in the household of a bureaucrat in Suzhou, earning two silver dollars a month to give to her husband. However, Mr. A was still not satisfied with the amount, and he plotted to sell his wife into a brothel. The woman, upon hearing this, had no means to escape on her own. Suddenly, a man from Zhejiang came to visit her employer, and upon seeing his name card, she recognized him and said, "This is a prominent family from my hometown, Hangzhou, and my father used to teach in this household." She then found out where he lived, went to his house the next day to request an audience, and spoke about her family background and their close familial relationship, presenting her case in a very organized manner. He then arranged for her father to come, filed a lawsuit with the authorities, beat and expelled Mr. A, and ruled that the daughter be returned to her father. An elderly scholar looked gloomy and unhappy and said, "If this daughter returns, it will disgrace our family." Mr. Bu Mei, also from Hangzhou and serving in Suzhou, who was previously acquainted with the elderly scholar, then arranged a match for this daughter in Wu (Suzhou area) and married her to an official. I heard this story from Mr. Bu Mei, and because it is not a praiseworthy matter, I do not record their names.

    93

    唐西镇劳氏，富于财。生一儿，年七八岁矣，犹口不能言，足不能步。其家创建育婴堂。堂成之日，儿即能言，越二岁能行，今且读书游泮水矣。报施之不爽如此。

    　　余二儿妇，唐西姚氏女也。今春从母家还，言去岁其弟妇病甚奇。病中从床上一跃，高及床顶，有一铁钅句，随手折为二，如枯竹焉。其弟晋卿知为鬼孽附丽而然，对之长揖，以洋钱数十枚置其前曰：“吾以此买纸钱，焚化与汝，汝可归矣。”病者曰：“可。然吾杭人也，须送我至航船。”晋卿从之，且行且焚纸钱，至船，乃告之曰：“此杭州航船也。”突有旋风起其侧，为之栗然。及归家，妇病若失。异哉！鬼亦如人，来往以舟车乎？诂经精舍监院校官沈兰舫，临平镇人也，寓于杭。其子妇家无子，蓄一他人子为子，绍兴人也。兰舫子妇偶归宁，忽得病，口作绍音，盖即此子之祖也，索还其孙甚急。

    Explanation

    93

    The Tangxi family of the Lao surname was wealthy. They had a son who was about seven or eight years old but still could not speak or walk. Their family built a nursery hall. On the day the hall was completed, the child began to speak, and after two more years, he could walk. Now he is studying and visiting schools. The retribution is precise and without error in this case.

    My second wife, a daughter of the Yao family from Tangxi, returned from her parents' home this spring. She mentioned that last year her sister-in-law fell seriously ill. During her illness, she leaped from her bed, reaching the top of the bed, and a piece of iron bent in her hand as if it were dry bamboo. Her brother, Jinqing, knew that evil spirits had attached themselves to her and performed a long bow to her, placing several foreign coins before her and saying, "I will use these to buy paper money, burn it for you, and you can return." The sick woman said, "Yes. But I am from Hangzhou, so you must take me to a riverboat." Jinqing followed her instructions, burning paper money as they went, and when they reached the boat, he told her, "This is a riverboat from Hangzhou." Suddenly, a whirlwind arose beside them, causing her to tremble. When she returned home, her illness was gone. How strange! Do spirits also travel by boat and carriage like humans?

    Supervisor of the Zujing Academy, Shen Lanfang, is from Linping Town and resides in Hangzhou. His daughter-in-law's family had no children, so they raised another person's child as their own, who was from Shaoxing. One day, when his daughter-in-law returned to her parents' home, she suddenly fell ill and spoke in the dialect of Shaoxing, which was the voice of the grandfather of the adopted child. She urgently demanded the return of her grandson.

    94

    俄又作数人声，皆杭音，则其母家鬼也，曰：“此无理取闹，彼已作沈家妇，何预此事？非至临平招沈氏人来不可。”越日而沈氏之鬼大至。兰舫之祖母及父母又一庶母，皆临平口音，与生前毕肖，曰：“吾侪附船从菜市桥登岸至此。有吾侪在，妇必无恙。然不可久居此，宜还家。”其地距兰舫所寓尚数里，群谋所以归，鬼曰：“仍以轿舁之。但命轿夫徐行，遇阴凉处则稍憩，且使人频频以汤水饮之，必无妨也。”如其言而归。沈氏之鬼从之，仍附病者，与家人相问答。时兰舫适在吴下，其家人问宜召之归乎，鬼曰：“此无大事。新妇病不出三日愈矣，可无召也。”妇病果即愈，鬼语亦遂寂。兰舫为余言之甚详，此其大概耳。以是二事观之，鬼杂处人间，与人不异。周公作《金滕》曰“能事鬼神”，孔子赞《周易》曰“知鬼神之情状”，圣人固未尝言无鬼神也。晋人无鬼之论，岂其然乎？

    Explanation

    94

    Suddenly, several voices were heard, all with the accent of Hangzhou, indicating that they were the spirits of her maternal family. They said, "This is unreasonable and disruptive. She has already become a wife of the Shen family. What business does she have with this matter? It is necessary to go to Linping to bring the Shen family people." The next day, the Shen family's spirits arrived in large numbers. Lanfang's grandmother, parents, and another concubine, all with the accent of Linping, appeared just as they had been in life. They said, "We are attached to the boat and have disembarked at Caisi Bridge and come here. With us here, the woman must be safe. However, we cannot stay here long, we should return home." The place was still several miles from where Lanfang was staying. They discussed how to return, and the spirits said, "Still, we will carry her in a sedan chair. Instruct the sedan chair bearers to walk slowly, and rest in the shade when encountered. Also, have someone frequently give her water to drink. There will be no harm." They followed this advice and returned home. The Shen family's spirits followed them, still attached to the sick woman, and engaged in conversation with the family. At that time, Lanfang happened to be in Wu. His family asked if they should summon him back, and the spirits said, "There is no great matter. The new wife's illness will not last more than three days, so there is no need to summon him." The woman's illness indeed recovered, and the spirits' words also fell silent. Lanfang told me this story in great detail, and this is a summary of the events. From these two incidents, it can be seen that spirits live among people and are not different from humans. Zhou Gong wrote in Jin Teng that "one can serve ghosts and spirits." Confucius commented on the Yi Jing that "one knows the nature of ghosts and spirits." The sages never said that there are no ghosts and spirits. The theory of the Jìn people that there are no ghosts, could it be true?

    95

    杭州山墩某姓一女，兵乱时陷于贼，转徙至上海，为某甲所得，遂为夫妇。某甲固刀镊工也。及乱平，父母访得之，挈女还家。以其夫执贱业，不善也，谋伪为待聘者，别择婿，而女不可。乃招其夫至，而其夫无他能，仰食于妇，一家咸轻之，女亦偃蹇不甚为礼。夫郁郁无聊，食生鸦片以死，女竟别醮焉。此女始知从一之理，似明大义者；而终始参差，遂成两截人，亦可异矣。

    Explanation

    95

    A woman from a certain family in a village near Hangzhou fell into the hands of bandits during the time of military turmoil and was transported to Shanghai, where she was obtained by a man named Mr. A and they became husband and wife. Mr. A was indeed a barber. When the chaos subsided, her parents found her and brought her back home. Because her husband practiced a lowly profession, they did not approve and planned to pretend she was waiting to be married, so they could choose another husband, but the woman refused. They then summoned her husband, but he had no other skills and relied on his wife for support. The entire family looked down on him, and the woman also treated him with disdain. Feeling depressed and aimless, the husband died after taking raw opium, and the woman eventually remarried. This woman initially seemed to understand the principle of fidelity, as if she understood great righteousness, but her actions were inconsistent throughout, making her a divided person, which is quite strange.

    96

    绍兴某生，应京兆试入都，不售，寓其族子家。一日忽失之，十余日乃返，则止一衫一裤，面无人色。问旬余何往，何狼狈至此，某缕述所遭，乃可捧腹，盖其日某闲行入内城，至西四牌楼一曲巷中，见有一家门署“荷包出售”四字，乃叩门欲买荷包。有一少妇出以一枚示之，已敝矣。问有新者无，妇曰：“欲新者请入内。”乃入共坐，妇絮絮闲谈，不及荷包。问之，仍以旧者进曰：“吾家止此一枚也。”“然则何以署门求售？”妇微笑曰：“所售非此荷包也。”某会其意，以言调之。

    Explanation

    96

    A scholar from Shaoxing, who had taken the imperial examination in Jingzhao but failed, stayed with his cousin's family. One day, he suddenly disappeared and did not return for ten days. When he finally came back, he was wearing only one shirt and one pair of pants, and his face was pale. When asked what he had been doing for the past ten days and why he looked so disheveled, he recounted his experiences in great detail, which were so absurd that they were laughable. It turned out that on that day, he had been wandering into the inner city and came to a narrow alley near the Xisi Pagoda. There, he saw a shop with a sign that read "Sale of Pouches." He knocked on the door to buy a pouch. A young woman came out and showed him one, which was already worn out. He asked if she had any new ones, and she replied, "If you want a new one, please come inside." He went in and sat down with her. She chatted aimlessly, without mentioning the pouch. When he asked about it, she handed him the old one again and said, "This is the only one we have." "Then why did you put a sign on the door to sell?" he asked. The woman smiled and said, "What we sell is not this pouch." He understood her meaning and tried to flirt with her.

    97

    妇曰：“吾荷包价昂，郎有此赀乎？”某即解囊出钱券示妇，可数百千。是时银一两易钱十千，计之亦值银数十两。妇曰：“可矣。”相将入室，弛服横陈，某惑之，遂止宿焉。其家止一媪，妇所佣也；一童子，妇之弟也。朝夕饔食，咸仰给于某，数日而空其囊。妇曰：“囊中空矣。”某曰：“此细事，吾自能筹之。”数日，妇又语如前，某答之亦如前。一日方共卧，忽有男子至其庭，声如虎。妇曰：“殆矣！吾夫归矣，不去必血其刃。”乃导某出北牖，使逾后垣去。某仓卒不得衣，故止一衫一裤也。闻者咸粲然曰：“此皆伪局耳，然亦不可再往。”族子乃予以资而使南归。

    Explanation

    97

    The woman said, "My purse is expensive. Do you have this money?" The man immediately unzipped his bag and showed the woman a bill of exchange, which was worth several hundred thousand. At that time, one silver ingot was worth ten thousand coins, so it was also worth dozens of silver ingots. The woman said, "That’s enough." They went into the room together, where she relaxed her clothes and lay down. The man was confused and stopped there for the night. There was only one old woman in the house, who was the woman’s servant, and one young boy, who was her brother. Their daily meals were all dependent on the man. After a few days, the woman’s purse was empty. The woman said, "The purse is empty now." The man said, "This is a minor matter. I can handle it myself." A few days later, the woman said the same thing again, and the man answered in the same way. One day, while they were lying down together, suddenly a man appeared in their courtyard, his voice like a roaring tiger. The woman said, "We’re in trouble! My husband is back. If we don’t leave, he will kill us." She then led the man out through the north window, making him climb over the back wall and leave. The man was in such a hurry that he didn’t have time to dress, so he left with only one shirt and one pair of pants. The onlookers all laughed and said, "This is all a fake setup, but you shouldn’t go back again." The family’s relative then gave him some money and sent him back to the south.

    98

    苏州阊门外李继宗巷有某甲者，以裁缝为业（《唐六典》有裁缝之名，盖裁之缝之而后成衣，较《周官。缝人》其义为备），年逾三十，家亦小康。偶见江北篷船一女子，颇有姿首，悦之。旋有人为之平章，以洋钱一百为聘。议既定，乃择日迓以彩舆，果见女子以红巾幕首，数人扶而就舆，并有一衣包，亦置舆中。鼓吹迎归，将扶女出，则弱不胜衣，玉山矣，审视之，一草人也。启视衣包，则砖石也，盖恐舆轻致疑，故以此压之。举家大讠华，亟寻原媒，去如黄鹤矣。某怅然痴立，亦如木偶。

    Explanation

    98

    In the alley of Li Jizong outside the Chuan Gate of Suzhou, there lived a man who made a living as a tailor (as recorded in the Tang Six Codes, the term "tailor" refers to the process of cutting and sewing to create garments, a more comprehensive term compared to the Zhou Official’s "sewing people"). He was over thirty years old and his family was relatively well-off. One day, he happened to see a woman from the north bank of the river, who was quite attractive, and he liked her. Soon after, someone mediated the matter, and they agreed on a dowry of one hundred foreign coins. Once the agreement was made, they chose a day to welcome her with a decorated carriage. Upon arrival, they saw the woman covered with a red scarf, carried by several people into the carriage, and accompanied by a package placed in the carriage. They welcomed her back with music and drums. When they were about to carry her out, she appeared weak and unable to stand, as if her beauty had faded. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a straw man. They opened the package and found it to be bricks and stones, likely to prevent suspicion due to the lightness of the carriage. The whole family was greatly surprised and hurriedly sought the original matchmaker, but he had disappeared like a yellow crane. The man stood there in a state of disappointment and disbelief, as if he were a wooden puppet.

    99

    宝山乡民邹玉宝，幼聘某氏女。玉宝少孤，育于女家，未婚也，而与女以兄妹见。久之，年各十六七矣。玉宝偶入内，见女独坐治粉，戏问曰：“甜乎？”曰：“甜。”玉宝曰：“制成当以一枚甜我。”女笑而颔之。及成，父母与女共食，不及婿。女心憾焉，私以一枚使佣媪饷之。他日又相见，女戏问：“甜乎？”玉宝曰：“余未得尝，恶知甜！”女知媪之绐也，詈之曰：“老大婢，尚口馋如此乎！”媪曰：“视年稚而私于男子者何如！”女恚，交相诟也。父母闻之，薄责女。女益惭，其夕缢而卒，玉宝闻之亦缢。

    Explanation

    99

    A Man from Baoshan Village Named Zou Yubao

    In his childhood, Zou Yubao was betrothed to a girl from a certain family. Orphaned at a young age, he was raised by the family of the girl he was engaged to, and they treated each other as siblings. After some time, both were around sixteen or seventeen years old. One day, Yubao entered the inner room and saw the girl sitting alone, making sweets. He teased her, asking, "Sweet, isn't it?" She replied, "Yes, it's sweet." Yubao then said, "When it's made, give me one piece to make me sweet too." The girl smiled and nodded in agreement. When the sweets were ready, the parents and the girl ate them together, but the husband was not present. The girl felt disappointed, so she secretly gave one piece to a servant woman to deliver to Yubao. On another day, they met again, and the girl playfully asked, "Sweet, isn't it?" Yubao replied, "I haven't tasted it yet, how would I know if it's sweet!" The girl realized the servant woman had deceived her and scolded her, saying, "Old servant, are you still so greedy for food?" The servant woman retorted, "How about you, being young and secretly interacting with men?" The girl became angry, and they began to scold each other. The parents heard about it and lightly scolded the girl. The girl felt even more ashamed and hanged herself that night. When Yubao heard about her death, he also hanged himself.

    100

    光绪丙子、丁丑间，直隶大无有兄嫂二人，挈其妹至天津求食。行至紫竹林，日将暮矣，有以小车载糕而鬻者，适驻其旁。嫂饥欲食，兄因出钱数十买糕，夫妇共食，而不与妹。其妹旁坐啜泣。卖糕者大不忍，乃推车就女曰：“吾糕垂尽矣，所值无多，尽以乞汝，不责直也。”已而三人皆食毕，兄嫂起而招妹偕行。女曰：“前路茫茫，行将安往？往而无食，亦无生理。吾受此人一饱之恩，不如从彼去，免为兄嫂累也。”卖糕者喜曰：“吾固无妻者，得此女为妻，何幸如之。”转求之兄嫂，兄嫂曰：“既彼此皆愿，吾何间焉。”卖糕者乃以车载女，并招兄嫂同至其家。翌日成礼，扫旁舍居其兄嫂。其家固不甚贫，有骡二头，分一与其兄，使赁于人而食其值焉。余谓此女颇有识见，而此卖糕者亦长者也。萍水相逢，遂成伉俪，颇非偶然。安知其子孙不浸昌浸炽，成一大族，而推本所自，传为美谈乎？

    Explanation

    100

    During the years of Guangxu's reign, the years of Bingzi and Dingchou, there were two siblings, an elder brother and his sister-in-law from Zhili, who brought along their younger sister to Tianjin to seek work. They had walked to Zizhu Lin when the day was almost over, and they came across a man selling cakes from a small cart, who had just stopped by their side. The sister-in-law was hungry and wanted to eat, so the elder brother gave several dozen coins to buy the cakes, and the couple ate together but did not give any to the younger sister. The younger sister sat beside them and sobbed. The cake seller could not bear to see this, and he pushed his cart over to the girl and said, "My cakes are nearly finished, and what I have left is not much; I will give them all to you, and I will not charge you." After they had all finished eating, the elder brother and sister-in-law stood up and called for the younger sister to go with them. The girl said, "The road ahead is vast and unclear; where will we go? If we go without food, there will be no chance of survival. I have received this kindness of a full meal from this person; it would be better for me to follow him, so as not to be a burden to my elder brother and sister-in-law." The cake seller was happy and said, "I originally had no wife; to get this girl as my wife is how fortunate it is." He then asked the elder brother and sister-in-law, and they replied, "Since both sides wish it, what difference does it make to us?" The cake seller then took the girl in his cart and called for the elder brother and sister-in-law to come with him to his home. The next day, they performed the wedding ceremony, and he cleaned a nearby room for the elder brother and sister-in-law to live in. His family was not very poor; he had two mules, and he gave one to the elder brother to rent out and live off the rent. I think this girl had quite a bit of insight, and this cake seller was also a person of virtue. They met by chance and became husband and wife, which was not an accident. Who knows if their descendants will gradually become prosperous and flourish, forming a large family, and tracing back to its origin, it will become a beautiful story to be passed down.

    101

    光绪三年正月，余同年张子青制府之母孟太夫人卒于苏寓。先是，李眉生廉访于二年十二月梦至张寓，入其内寝，见太夫人蒙被卧床上，子青制府于床前西向坐，有一人跪其前。李问何人，有告之者曰：“酆都令也。”问何为跪此，曰：“制府孝子也，故跪而求之。”李寤，语人曰：“制府岂将奉讳乎？”时距太夫人之殁不二十日矣。此眉生亲为余言，并云：“酆都令冠七品冠，而不衣补服，殆其时国服未除故乎？其人恂恂如忠厚长者。从者十许人，形状颇不诡异，非如世俗所传牛头马面，而阴惨之气逼人，殊可怖也。”余按酆都有平都山，山有五云洞，为道书所传七十二福地之一，宜为神仙之窟宅。乃考《抱朴子。对俗篇》曰：“势可以总摄罗酆。”则酆都治鬼之说，晋世已有之矣。独念自蜀至苏，迢遥数千里，酆都令君跋涉远来，无乃太劳，且何以必使眉生见之？岂以眉生蜀人，有维桑之谊邪！

    Explanation

    101

    In the first month of the third year of Guangxu, my fellow scholar-official Zhang Ziqing, governor of [a province], lost his mother, Lady Meng, the Grand Lady, in her residence in Suzhou. Prior to this, in the twelfth month of the second year, Li Meisheng, the Censor, dreamed of going to Zhang's residence. He entered the inner chamber and saw Lady Meng covered with a quilt lying on the bed, while Zhang Ziqing, the governor, sat facing west in front of the bed. There was a person kneeling in front of him. Li asked who he was, and someone told him, "He is the magistrate of Fengdu." Li then asked why he was kneeling, and the person replied, "He is a filial son of the governor, so he is kneeling to ask for something." Li woke up and told others, "Is the governor about to receive the news of a death?" It was not more than twenty days before Lady Meng's death. This was personally told to me by Li Meisheng, who also said, "The magistrate of Fengdu wore a seventh-rank hat but did not wear the official ceremonial robe, perhaps because the mourning attire had not yet been lifted. The person was humble and appeared like a loyal and kind elder. There were about ten followers, and their appearance was not very strange, unlike what is commonly depicted in the world, such as ox-heads and horse-faces, but the dark and gloomy atmosphere was oppressive, and it was extremely frightening." I noted that Fengdu has Pingdu Mountain, which has the Wu Yun Cave, one of the seventy-two blessed places mentioned in Taoist texts, and it should be a dwelling place for immortals. I then examined the Baopuzi: Chapter on Dealing with the World and it said, "The power can control Luo Feng." Therefore, the idea that Fengdu governs ghosts has existed since the Jin Dynasty. I thought about the distance from Sichuan to Suzhou, which is thousands of miles, and the magistrate of Fengdu traveled a long way. Is it not too much trouble, and why did he necessarily have to make Li Meisheng see him? Is it because Li Meisheng is from Sichuan and has a connection to his hometown?

    102

    某甲，农家子也，其父母爱之。以其荏弱不任农事，有叔父开药肆于市，使从之学贾。其叔父嗜饮，每日必使就对门屠肆沽酒。甲时年十二三，眉目娟好，屠妇爱之，辄多与之酒。如是数年，甲年十六七矣，屠妇语之曰：“若知我爱汝乎？”曰：“知之。”“然则何以报我？”甲曰：“不知所报。”妇笑曰：“易耳。”乃出酒肉共食，食已，招之登楼，私焉。嗣后伺屠他出，辄就之，事秘无知者。一岁值中秋，药肆中友皆出步月，甲亦与焉。已而雨作，诸友皆反，而甲后之，及肆，则门阖矣。

    Explanation

    102

    There was a person named Jia, a son of a farmer's family. His parents loved him. Because he was weak and could not handle farming work, he had an uncle who opened a medicine shop in the market and made him learn the trade of commerce. His uncle loved drinking and would make him go to the butchery across the street to buy wine every day. At the age of twelve or thirteen, Jia had beautiful eyebrows and eyes. The butcher's wife loved him and often gave him extra wine. In this way, several years passed, and Jia was now sixteen or seventeen. The butcher's wife said to him, "Do you know I love you?" He said, "I know." "Then how can you repay me?" Jia said, "I don't know how to repay you." The woman laughed and said, "It's easy." She then took out wine and meat to share with him. After they had eaten, she invited him upstairs and had relations with him. After that, whenever the butcher was out, she would meet him. The matter was kept secret, and no one knew about it. One year, during the Mid-Autumn Festival, friends from the medicine shop all went out to enjoy the moon, and Jia also went with them. After a while, it started to rain, and all the friends returned, but Jia followed them later. When he reached the shop, the door was already closed.

    103

    念叩门而入，必为叔父所责。正徘徊间，屠妇适开楼窗下视，楼固临街者，见甲在下，招之以手。甲曰：“屠在乎？”曰：“买猪去矣。”乃开门纳之，登楼而寝焉。会屠亦遇雨而归，呼于门。甲窘曰：“奈何？”妇曰：“无妨。”使尾其后以行，匿甲于门侧，屠入而甲出，不知也。甲念夜益深矣，叩门而入，叔父怒更甚，乃立檐下，以待天明。俄其妇又启窗，见甲犹在，曰：“未归乎？”曰：“然。屠安在？”曰：“醉而眠矣。”甲因遗其帽于楼，乃以手自扪其头，且伸手作索取之状。妇曰：“诺。

    Explanation

    103

    Jia thought that knocking on the door and entering would surely result in being scolded by his uncle. While hesitating, the butcher's wife just opened the window of the upstairs room to look down. The room was located over the street, and she saw Jia below, gesturing to him with her hand. Jia asked, "Is the butcher here?" She replied, "He went to buy a pig." Then she opened the door to admit him, and he climbed the stairs and slept there. Later, the butcher also encountered rain and returned, calling out at the door. Jia was in a窘境 and said, "What should I do?" The wife said, "It's fine." She made him follow her from behind, and they hid Jia by the door. When the butcher entered, Jia came out, and the butcher didn't know. Jia thought the night had grown deeper, and he knocked on the door to enter again. His uncle scolded him even more severely, so Jia stood under the eaves, waiting for dawn. Soon, the butcher's wife opened the window again and saw Jia was still there. She asked, "Have you not returned yet?" He replied, "Yes. Where is the butcher?" She said, "He's drunk and asleep." Jia then left his hat on the floor and touched his own head, extending his hand to make a gesture of taking it. The wife said, "Okay."

    104

    ”未几开门招甲，甲入曰：“屠在，招我何为？”妇曰：“已杀之矣。”甲惊曰：“奈何杀人？”妇曰：“汝以手示我，使我杀之，何问焉？”登楼视其床，赫然死人也。问何刀，曰：“屠刀。”“刀安在？‘’曰：”在床下。“甲即就床下取刀，斫妇死，而取帽以出，径归其家，绐其父母曰：”顷偕诸友步月，行稍远，距家近矣，故暂归也。“父母喜而留之。有皮匠者，药肆之邻也，素艳屠妇，而未得间。迟明，荷担出，过屠肆之门，见门虚掩。入之，无问者。皮匠固知昨暮出，而不知其反，私计妇必独寝于楼。

    Explanation

    104

    Not long after, the door was opened to invite Jia. Jia entered and said, "The butcher is still alive; why did you call me?" The woman replied, "He has already been killed." Jia was shocked and asked, "Why kill him?" The woman said, "You showed me your hand, telling me to kill him, so why ask?" Jia went upstairs and saw the dead person lying plainly on the bed. He asked, "Which knife was used?" The woman replied, "The butcher's knife." "Where is the knife?" Jia asked. "It's under the bed," she said. Jia then took the knife from under the bed, killed the woman, took her hat, and left, returning directly to his home. He lied to his parents, saying, "Just now I went out with my friends to stroll under the moon. We walked a little further, and since we were close to home, I came back for a moment." His parents were pleased and kept him. There was a leather worker who lived near the pharmacy and had long been attracted to the butcher's wife but had not found an opportunity. Early the next morning, he shouldered his load and went out, passing by the butcher's shop. He saw the door slightly open and entered without being questioned. The leather worker knew the butcher had left the previous evening but did not know he had returned. He privately thought that the woman must be sleeping alone upstairs.

    105

    乃登其楼，则屠死于床，妇死于地，流血濡其履，惊而走出，归而闭户，卧久之。天大明，列肆皆启，见屠户已启而无人，呼之不应。入视得状，又穷履迹，而至皮匠之门，遂缚送官。不胜鞭，自诬服。越数日，某甲反，诸友告之曰：”对门屠肆夫妇为邻人皮匠所杀矣。“甲曰：”信乎？“曰：”到官已自承，不久将尸诸市矣。“甲曰：”此我为之，何诬匠为？“叔父掩其口。甲不可，走县，挝鼓以闻，述本末。官曰：”义士也。“末减其罪，竟不死。此事前在新安，闻诸程君心言，曾记载其事，岁久稿佚，遂不能举其姓名乡里矣。唐沈亚之所撰《〔冯〕（马）燕传》，颇与此类，古今事固有相同者乎？

    Explanation

    105

    He then ascended his tower, where he found the butcher dead on the bed and the woman dead on the ground. Blood flowed and wet his shoes, and he was surprised and ran out, returning home and closing the door. He lay there for a long time. When it was broad daylight, all the shops were open. He saw the butcher shop was open but no one was there. He called out, but no one responded. He entered to investigate the situation and traced the shoe tracks, leading him to the cobbler's door. He then bound and sent the cobbler to the authorities. The cobbler could not bear the beating and falsely confessed. After several days, Mr. A returned, and his friends told him, "The couple from the butcher shop across the street were killed by their neighbor, the cobbler." Mr. A asked, "Is it true?" They replied, "By the time he reached the authorities, he had already confessed, and he will soon be executed in public." Mr. A said, "This was done by me. Why blame the cobbler?" His uncle covered his mouth. Mr. A refused, ran to the county, beat the drum to report, and described the entire incident. The official said, "He is a righteous man." The sentence was reduced, and he ultimately did not die. This event occurred previously in Xin'an, and I heard it from Mr. Cheng's words. It was once recorded, but after many years, the draft was lost, so I could no longer name his name and hometown. The Biography of Feng Ma Yan written by Tang's Shen Ya Zhi is quite similar to this. Are there similar events in ancient and modern times?

    106

    粤中李氏子，幼读书，应童子试，不售。性好博，父屡诫之，不悛，妇陈氏因之屡与反目。俄举一子，父名之曰阿靠。及阿靠周岁，父谓陈氏曰：“汝夫屡从牧猪奴游，非吾子也。吾名孙曰阿靠，将舍子而靠孙矣。为汝计，亦宜舍夫而靠子。”是日，父具酒食以饮食其子，谓之曰：“汝强饮食，伊汝醉饱，吾送汝赴清流矣。”李叩头求活，不许；复涕泣跪其妇前乞为缓颊，陈亦不应。父乃以布囊蒙其头，使健仆负而投之河。载沉载浮，将及里许，有人拯之起，则博场中旧友也，即留之博场中。居月余，李辞其友曰：“吾以好博故，父不以为子，妻不以为夫。今在此旦夕闻呼卢喝雉之声，弥触我隐痛矣。”乃去而乞食于市。

    Explanation

    106

    In Guangdong, there was a son of the Li family. As a child, he read books and took the children's examination but did not pass. He had a nature fond of gambling. His father repeatedly warned him, but he did not repent. His wife, Tien, often clashed with him over this. Soon, Tien gave birth to a son. The father named him Ah Kao. When Ah Kao turned one year old, the father said to Tien, "Your husband has often been with the likes of pig herders, not my son. I named my grandson Ah Kao, intending to abandon my son and rely on my grandson. For your sake, you should also abandon your husband and rely on your son." That day, the father prepared a feast to eat with his son and said, "You must eat heartily, you will be drunk and full, and I will send you to the clear stream." The son knelt down begging for mercy, but was not allowed. He cried and knelt before his wife, begging her to intercede, but Tien did not respond. The father then covered his head with a cloth bag and had a strong servant carry him and throw him into the river. He sank and floated, almost reaching a mile away. Someone rescued him, and it was an old friend from the gambling den. He was then left in the gambling den. After staying there for over a month, Li said to his friend, "Because of my love for gambling, my father no longer considers me his son, and my wife no longer considers me her husband. Now, every day I hear the sounds of dice and cards, which only intensify my hidden pain." He then left and begged for food in the market.

    107

    顾尚能书，自买纸写楹联，遍送市廛，市人亦稍酬其笔墨之费，虽乞也而所得较丰。流转数千里，至山东某县，寓道观中，大病几死。观中老道士怜而饮食之，医药之，幸而病愈。道士谓之曰：“此间颇重南中文士，吾为招童子数人，使子授以经书，必有至者。此寒士谋生之恒业也，不胜于仰面求人乎？”李欣然从之，遂得与邑中士大夫游。有赵翁者，设逆旅以待四方之客。家颇小康，而无子；止一女，欲为女招婿，而即以为子，久而未得其人。道士乃说赵翁曰：“翁女若婿本地人，此时虽暂为翁子，久必挈女归耳。李氏子，无家者也，孑然一身，流落千里，若为翁婿，必长为翁子矣。”赵翁韪其言，乃赘李于家。久之，谓赵翁曰：“某视此间庠序中人，其文艺亦与某等耳。使得与试，一衿非所难也。

    Explanation

    107

    Con can still write, buying his own paper to write couplets, which he then widely distributed to the marketplaces. The people there would slightly compensate him for his writing materials, and although he was essentially begging, he received more than he would have otherwise. He traveled thousands of miles and arrived in a certain county in Shandong Province, where he stayed in a Taoist temple. He fell seriously ill and nearly died, but the old Taoist priest took pity on him, feeding and treating him with medicine. Fortunately, he recovered. The Taoist priest then told him, "Here, they highly regard scholars from the south. I will recruit several children and have you teach them the classics. There will certainly be people who come. This is a steady job for poor scholars, and it is not better than begging?" Li gladly followed the suggestion and was thus able to interact with scholars and officials in the county. There was a man named Zhao who ran an inn to accommodate guests from all directions. Zhao's family was fairly comfortable, but he had no son; he had only one daughter and wanted to find a husband for her, considering him as his own son. However, he had not found the right person for a long time. The Taoist priest then persuaded Zhao, saying, "If your daughter's husband is a local, he may temporarily be your son, but he will eventually take your daughter back home. Li, without a family, is alone and has wandered thousands of miles. If he becomes your son-in-law, he will certainly stay as your son." Zhao approved of this and married Li into his family. After a long time, Li told Zhao, "I see that the scholars and officials here are comparable to me in literary and artistic skills. If I am allowed to take the exam, obtaining a scholar's robe would not be difficult."

    108

    ”翁大喜，即使以赵姓应试，补博士弟子员。逾年举于乡，联捷成进士。数年后选授粤中一县令，携妻之官，历任繁剧，宦橐丰盈。因官本籍，恐致人言，未敢问其家也。服官十余年，以公事罢职，将归山东，乃迂道访之，则父死久矣。妻与子顾无恙，然贫甚，无以为生。李乃自至其家，诡言李氏子旧时博友也，请见其妻。时李有须，且貌又丰腴，其言操北音，妻固不识也。呼其子视之，二十余岁矣。问有妻乎？曰无。问何不娶，告以贫故。李慨然曰：“吾与李某自幼在博场中交好，今故人长逝，妻子单寒，西华葛帔，令人叹息。吾虽小人，不忍坐视。”乃出橐中数千金与之，且为作媒，娶邻村某氏女，婚费悉出自李。庙见之日，李亦至焉。

    Explanation

    108

    The old man was greatly pleased and immediately had his son take the examination under the surname Zhao, to be admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy. The following year, he was selected in the provincial examination and then passed the metropolitan examination to become a Jinshi (imperial scholar). Several years later, he was appointed as the magistrate of a county in Guangdong, taking his wife with him to the post. He served in many busy and demanding positions, and his official salary was abundant. Because he was serving in his hometown, he feared causing gossip and dared not inquire about his family. He served in office for over ten years but was dismissed from office due to official matters and was about to return to Shandong. He took a detour to visit him, but his father had been dead for a long time. His wife and son were still alive, but they were very poor and had no means of living. Li went to their house himself and lied, saying he was an old gambling friend of the Li family, and asked to see his wife. At that time, Li had a full beard, and his appearance was robust; his speech had a northern accent, and the wife did not recognize him. He called his son to look at him, and he was over twenty years old. He asked if he had a wife, and he told him it was because of poverty. Li said with a sigh, "I and Li have been friends since childhood in the gambling house. Now our old friend has passed away, and his wife and children are poor and cold, like the fine silk robe of the Western Han dynasty, which makes one sigh. Though I am a lowly person, I cannot bear to see it." He took out several thousand gold coins from his bag and gave them to him and also arranged a marriage. He married a woman from a neighboring village, and all the wedding expenses were paid by Li. On the day of the temple visit, Li also came.

    109

    李妻亲至堂前叩谢，并使其子与新妇登&lt；毛瞿&gt；&lt；毛俞&gt；展拜，中设一坐，请李坐之。时宾客咸在，意李必从谦抑，而李俨然踞坐，受其拜谒。拜已，大声呼曰：“阿靠！汝幼而无父，不识我宜也；阿靠之母，何亦不识我乎？”李妻在旁，闻而大惊。李乃语之曰：“吾即尔夫也。”因历叙前事，众宾咸诧叹。李曰：“吾官此多年，今不能复归原籍矣，即将辞尔等北去。夫妻父子，缘尽于斯。”妻闻之哭失声，众宾之长者或进言曰：“君既荣显，何不挈其母子同归山东？”李笑曰：“曩者之事，诚出老父之意。然夫妻死别，人间至惨，乃视吾布囊蒙首，漠不动心，人之无情，一至于此！吾在山东，有妻有子矣，焉置此为？其舍夫靠子，遵吾父之命可也。阿靠，阿靠，善事尔母。”言已，不顾而去。

    Explanation

    109

    R's wife personally came to the front hall to bow and thank, and she had her son and daughter-in-law ascend the steps of the memorial tablet to pay respects. In the middle of the hall, a seat was set up, and R was asked to sit there. At the time, all the guests were present, expecting R to show humility, but R sat in a dignified and authoritative manner, receiving their homage. After the bowing, R loudly called out, "Ah Kao! You were young and without a father, so you don't recognize me; why doesn't your mother recognize me either?" R's wife, who was standing by, heard this and was greatly shocked. R then said to her, "I am your husband." He recounted the events of the past, and the guests were all astonished and sighed. R said, "I have been an official here for many years, and now I cannot return to my hometown. I will soon leave you all to go north. The ties of husband and wife, father and son, come to an end here." Upon hearing this, R's wife cried out in despair. Some of the elder guests then spoke up, saying, "Since you have achieved such honor and distinction, why not bring your mother and son back to Shandong with you?" R laughed and said, "The matter of the past was indeed the intention of my father. However, the death of a spouse is the most tragic event in human life. They saw me covering my head with a cloth bag, yet they remained unmoved, showing the coldness of human nature! In Shandong, I already have a wife and children; where would I place these people? They should leave their husband and child behind and follow my father's command. Ah Kao, Ah Kao, treat your mother well." Having said this, R turned away and left without looking back.

    110

    余旧居临平镇时，其西邻有曰范桂芳者，故充仁和县役。曾得罪于孙文靖相国之孙古云袭伯，荷校通衢，然其后武断乡曲，豪横如故。余幼时屡见之，今死久矣。光绪五年，临平有沈氏妇病死，将死前数日，范桂芳附其体，与沈氏妇女相问答。盖范生前曾以钱债事与沈氏有隙也。范故杭人侨寓临平者，故其语犹杂杭音，鬼语亦然。溯范之死，四十余年矣，溯其生年，则已在百岁外。范本一小人，宜乎如昌黎公所云“其身已死，其鬼不灵”者，何其气久而不散如此邪？鬼神之事，真有不可知者。古者庙焉而人鬼享，是鬼恒居家庙也。孔子又曰：“骨肉毙于下，阴为野土，其气发扬于上为昭明，蒿凄怆。”是又居墟墓也。鬼果安在乎？延陵季子则曰：“若魂气则无不之也。”夫鬼不过人之余气，人之生也，非舟不能行水，非车不能行陆，乃其余气为鬼，遂能无所不之，是鬼转胜于人矣。汉文帝问鬼神，不知贾生之对云何。

    Explanation

    110

    When I lived in Linping Town, there was a man named Fan Guifang who lived to the west of me. He used to serve as a county official in Renhe. He once offended the grandson of Grand Secretary Sun Wenjing, named Gu Yun Xi Bo, and was publicly punished by wearing a wooden collar in the main street. However, after that, he remained as domineering and arrogant as before. I often saw him when I was young, and now he has been dead for a long time. In the fifth year of the Guangxu era, there was a woman from the Shen family in Linping who died of illness. Several days before she died, Fan Guifang's spirit attached to her body and conversed with the Shen family women. This was because Fan had a dispute with the Shen family over money matters while he was alive. Fan was originally from Hangzhou but had settled in Linping, so his speech still carried a Hangzhou accent, and so did his ghostly voice. It has been over forty years since Fan's death, and tracing back to his birth, he would be over a hundred years old. Fan was originally a small-minded person, so it is appropriate that, as Mr. Changli said, "his body is dead, but his ghost is not spiritless." How is it that his spirit has lasted so long without dissipating? The matter of ghosts and spirits truly is something that cannot be known. In ancient times, temples were built where people and ghosts could enjoy offerings, thus ghosts always resided in family temples. Confucius also said, "When the flesh and bones decay, they become the soil beneath the earth, and their energy rises above as a clear and bright spirit, and becomes the desolate and sorrowful grass." Thus, they also reside in graveyards. Where do ghosts truly reside? The Duke of Yanling said, "If the soul and energy are not limited to any place." Ghosts are no more than the leftover energy of humans. When people are alive, they cannot travel on water without a boat, nor on land without a carriage. But their leftover energy becomes ghosts, which can go anywhere. Thus, ghosts are even more powerful than people. Emperor Wen of Han asked about ghosts and spirits, but I do not know what Jia Yi's response was.

    111

    孔子之告子路，则曰：“未知生，焉知死？未能事人，焉能事鬼？”盖孔子亦有所不知耳。紫阳氏之说曰：“非原始，而知所以生；不能反终，而知所以死。”此说也，余所不解。夫原始反终，故知死生之说，不过谓生者人之始事，死者人之终事耳。若谓知生即可知死，此恐有所不能。夫人之生也，由父精母血感而成胎，夫人而知之矣。

    Explanation

    111

    Confucius told Zilu, saying, "If one does not know life, how can one know death? If one cannot serve people, how can one serve spirits?" This suggests that even Confucius had his limits of knowledge. Zhu Xi said, "One must understand the origin of life to know how to live and the end of life to know how to die." This statement is something I find difficult to understand. By understanding the origin and the end, one knows the principles of life and death. This simply means that life is the beginning of human affairs, and death is the end of human affairs. However, I doubt that knowing life alone can lead to understanding death. The creation of a person begins with the union of the father's essence and the mother's blood, forming a fetus, a fact that is well-known.

    112

    《淮南子》云：“一月而膏，二月而失，三月而胎，四月而肌，五月而筋，六月而骨，七月而成，八月而动，九月而躁，十月而生。”是未生以前之情状，古人有能历历言之者。今谓魂升魄降以后作何情状，亦可据此而推之，得乎，不得乎？紫阳又言：“幽明始终，初无二理，但学之有序，不可躐等。”此说又非也。如其说，则子路所学，未及乎此。诚哉，等不可躐矣！颜、曾之徒，所学高出仲氏倍蓰，岂诸贤所孜孜讲求者，已不在人事，而在鬼事，已不在生事，而在死事乎？寒夜无事，因范桂芳事，纵论及此。世无贾生，谁与决之？

    Explanation

    112

    《Huainanzi》states: "In the first month, it is like fat; in the second month, it is like flesh; in the third month, it is like a fetus; in the fourth month, it is like muscle; in the fifth month, it is like tendons; in the sixth month, it is like bones; in the seventh month, it is formed; in the eighth month, it moves; in the ninth month, it is lively; in the tenth month, it is born." This describes the condition before birth, and ancient people could describe it in detail. Now, what would the condition be after the soul ascends and the spirit descends? Can we infer it based on the same principles? Can we, or cannot we? Purple Yang also said: "The principles of the visible and invisible worlds are fundamentally the same, but learning must follow a proper order and cannot skip steps." This statement is incorrect. If this were true, then Zilu's learning would not have reached this level. Indeed, the levels of learning cannot be skipped! Yan Hui and Zengzi, whose learning was much higher than that of Confucius, did their diligent pursuits focus on human affairs, or on ghostly matters? On living matters, or on death matters? On a cold night with nothing to do, we discussed this due to the story of Fan Gui Fang. Without Jia Yi, who would be able to resolve this?

    113

    李老道流寓楚北，自言蜀人。问其名，曰：“无名。”问其年，曰：“忘之矣。”视之如六七十岁人，然八九十老翁皆云自幼见之已如是，则其年固未可测也。所衣单布衫裤，外著葛布袍，冬夏不易，冬不言寒，夏不言热。与之食，食无算，不言饱；与之饮，饮无算，不言醉。或旬日不饮不食，不言饥渴。人皆以为得道者。或叩问养生术，老道曰：“吾不知其他，惟任其自然而已。”咸曰：“自然二字，平平耳，何能若是？老道必别有异术，而秘不言也。”余谓老子云“天法道，道法自然”，是自然二字，固老氏之要旨。

    Explanation

    113

    The old Daoist hermit resided as a guest in the northern region of Chu, claiming to be from Shu. When asked about his name, he replied, "I have no name." When asked about his age, he said, "I have forgotten it." He appeared to be in his sixties or seventies, but even those in their eighties or nineties claimed they had seen him in the same state since childhood, suggesting that his age was truly incalculable. He wore simple, unadorned linen shirts and trousers, with a hemp robe over them, which he did not change regardless of the season. In winter, he did not speak of feeling cold, and in summer, he did not speak of feeling hot. When given food, he ate without limit, yet he did not say he was full; when given drink, he drank without limit, yet he did not say he was drunk. Sometimes he would go without food or drink for ten days, yet he did not speak of hunger or thirst. People all regarded him as one who had attained the Dao. When someone asked about his methods of preserving life, the old Daoist said, "I do not know of any others; I simply let things take their natural course." All said, "The two characters 'nature' are nothing special; how could he be like this? The old Daoist must have some hidden techniques that he does not speak of." I say that Laozi said, "Heaven follows the Dao, and the Dao follows nature," and thus the two characters 'nature' are indeed the essence of the teachings of the Old Master.

    114

    天且法之，况于人乎？人之一身，清升浊降，本有自然之节。古之至人，患人不能循此自然之节，故有炉鼎之术，铅汞龙虎之说，使人循此以求自然之节而已，非有他道也。推而言之，圣人之治天下，亦若是也。君臣、父子、夫妇，昆弟、朋友，皆有自然之节。圣人制为礼法，使人循此以求自然之节也。又推而言之，制为六书，以求合于自然之文，制为五声六律，以求合于自然之音。是故拘拘于偏旁点画，非知书者也；见担夫争道，可悟书法。沾沾于上工四尺，非知音者也；闻海上波涛，可悟琴理。无他，自然而已。李老道之言，深得养生大旨。诚得此旨，则《参同契》可烧也，况《悟真篇》之类乎？

    Explanation

    114

    If Heaven follows this principle, how much more should humans? In the human body, the ascending and descending forces naturally balance each other. Ancient sages were concerned that people could not follow this natural balance, so they developed alchemical practices and symbolic theories of lead, mercury, dragon, and tiger, to help people follow this natural balance, without any other methods. Extending this idea, the sage rulers of old governed the world in a similar manner. The relationships of ruler and subject, father and son, husband and wife, elder and younger siblings, and friends—all have their own natural order. Sage rulers established rituals and laws to help people follow this natural order. Furthermore, they created the six principles of Chinese character composition to align with the natural principles of writing, and the five tones and six pitches to align with the natural principles of music. Therefore, being overly concerned with the details of writing is not understanding the true essence of writing; observing a porter navigating a narrow path can provide insight into calligraphy. Being overly focused on the technical aspects of music is not understanding true music; listening to the waves of the sea can provide insight into the principles of music. There is nothing more to it than following natural principles. The words of the old Taoist sage deeply understand the essence of health and longevity. If one truly grasps this principle, even the Zhenyuan Miaodao Yaoshi (The Secret of the Mysteries of the Tao) can be transcended, let alone texts like the Wuzhen Pian (The Book of Awakening to Reality).

    115

    ●卷三余门下士蒋泽山孝廉，至崇明县校试卷。事毕，观于文庙，见两庑先儒中汤文正公之位阙焉。问之学官，学官曰：“此地旧有五通神为祟。民间被五通之祟者，辄向学中门斗言明将汤文正之位私自请去，供奉其家，则祟自去，历来如此。吾侪职司学校，每十余日必来审视，如文正之位久而不归，则向门斗催取而已，不能禁止也。”夫吴中五通久为文正禁绝，崇明海外之地，当时或未尽奉行，乃身后栗主，犹足以慑伏奸邪，是其正气固长存天地间也。

    Explanation

    115

    In Volume Three, my student Jiang Zeshan, who passed the imperial examinations, arrived at Chongming County to examine the examination papers. After the task was completed, he visited the Confucian temple and saw that the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong in the two wings of the temple was vacant. He asked the local school official, who replied, "There used to be the Five Tong gods causing mischief in this place. People who were troubled by the Five Tong gods would tell the school official and privately take away the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong to worship at home, and the trouble would disappear. This has been the case all along. We, who are in charge of the school, must come every ten or more days to inspect. If the position of Tang Wenzheng Gong remains unreturned for a long time, we just ask the school official to retrieve it. We cannot prevent this." In the Wu region, the Five Tong gods have been banned by Tang Wenzheng for a long time. In Chongming, a remote island, the ban might not have been fully enforced at that time. Even after his death, his spirit tablet still has the power to intimidate evil. His righteous spirit indeed continues to exist in the world.

    116

    苏人陶阿尼，性好博，而博屡负，橐中罄矣。计无复之，踽踽独行，将告贷于所识者。忽有入自后拍其肩曰：“阿尼输矣乎？”回视，则其已死之友朱大也。陶大恐。朱曰：“吾非祸君者。君能葬我骨，我当助君博。”陶诺之。朱乃出巨金一锭，曰：“持此入博场，压白虎门，必胜。再以所得者尽压白虎门，又胜。四度而止，勿过贪也。”陶从其教，果大得彩，比晓，腰缠累累矣。视其中有纸锭一枚，乃故人所假之原注也。访诸野，得其棺，卖地而葬之。

    Explanation

    116

    A man from Su, Tao Anyi, had a fondness for gambling, but he often lost, and his bag was empty. With no other options, he walked alone, intending to borrow money from acquaintances. Suddenly, someone approached from behind and tapped his shoulder, asking, "Have you lost, Anyi?" Turning around, he saw his deceased friend Zhu Da. Tao was frightened. Zhu said, "I am not here to harm you. If you can bury my bones, I will help you gamble." Tao agreed. Zhu then produced a large sum of money, saying, "Take this to the gambling hall and bet on the White Tiger door; you will surely win. Use what you win to bet again on the White Tiger door, and you will win again. Do this four times, and then stop; do not be greedy." Tao followed his advice and indeed won greatly. By morning, he was surrounded by a large sum of money. Looking inside, he found a piece of paper money, which was the original note he had borrowed from his old friend. He searched in the wild, found Zhu's coffin, and sold land to bury him.

    117

    天津市中无赖少年，往往于博场索规例钱，诸博徒亦乐应之。然其始得也，颇不容易。余寓天津时，有粗作人田升日往来于博场。一日见有醉人昂然而至，上不衣，下不裤，止以尺布蔽下体。一入局中，便肆口骂，博徒群起，各执白木棍痛打之。然打者自打，骂者自骂，至体无完肤，气息仅属，犹喃喃骂不绝口。于是群叹曰：“好汉，好汉！”以童便饮之，又以温水涤其血污，负而归之开局者之家，自此月有规例矣。斯人也，岂所谓北方之强者与？

    　　歙县汪氏妇，孕十六月而生一儿，躯体甚肥，龈有齿，颊有髯，目炯炯然。咸惧，欲弃之。其母不忍，仍为包裹。儿不啼，亦不食。乳至六七日，佥谓无生理，置诸奥，将俟人定而弃之河。比晚，忽闻哑哑有声，视之，则以两手掬土而食，其身骤长，乃听其自然。至月余乃死。众人聚观，宛若庙中所塑土地像，身长二尺八寸，须长寸许，秤之重二十一斤。

    Explanation

    117

    In the city of Tianjin, there were often unruly young men who would frequently visit gambling dens to demand regular payments, which the gamblers were happy to comply with. However, obtaining these payments initially was quite difficult. When I stayed in Tianjin, there was a rough laborer named Tian Sheng who would often go to the gambling dens. One day, he saw a drunkard enter the gambling den in a haughty manner, not wearing a top, nor pants, only covering his lower body with a small piece of cloth. As soon as the drunkard entered the gambling den, he immediately started cursing loudly. The gamblers gathered around, each holding a white wooden stick and beating him severely. However, the beaters beat, the cursers cursed, until his body was completely beaten up, his breath was barely left, and he still mumbled curses non-stop. Then the crowd exclaimed, "A true man, a true man!" They gave him a drink of urine and then washed his blood-stained body with warm water, carried him back to the home of the gambling den owner. From then on, a monthly fee was established.

    This person, is he not one of the strong ones of the north?

    In the county of She, there was a woman of the Wang family who was pregnant for sixteen months and gave birth to a boy. His body was very fat, his gums had teeth, his cheeks had whiskers, and his eyes were bright. Everyone was afraid and wanted to abandon him. His mother could not bear it and still wrapped him up. The child did not cry, nor did he eat. After six or seven days of breastfeeding, everyone believed he had no chance of survival. They placed him in a dark corner, planning to abandon him in the river when people were asleep. By evening, they suddenly heard a muffled sound. They looked at him and saw him eating soil with his hands. His body grew rapidly, so they let nature take its course. After more than a month, he died. People gathered to watch, and he looked like a statue of a local deity in a temple, two chi and eight cun long, with a beard about one cun long. When weighed, he weighed twenty-one catties.

    118

    京师一大家，富于赀，佣一仆妇，为昌平州人，服役有年。性甚黠，能得主人心，故主妇颇委任之，凡金帛所藏，悉与知焉。一夜人定后，有盗六人，自屋而下，皆涂面执刃，群仆惊逸。此妇闻声趋出，为盗所执。以刃拟其颈曰：“尔主人何在？”曰：“值内班未归。”盗挥其徒曰：“速缚其主妇来。”妇跪而泣曰：“主母遇我厚，愿勿吓之，我愿以身代。”盗曰：“既如些，尔但告我金帛所在。”妇嗫嚅不欲言，盗举刃欲斫之，妇大骇，乃具告焉。盗搜括既已，意犹未足，复以刃胁使尽言。妇曰：“金帛尽矣，尚有珠宝首饰在某所。

    Explanation

    118

    In the capital city, there was a wealthy family. They employed a female servant from Changping Prefecture, who had served them for many years. She was very clever and could please the master, so the mistress trusted her greatly. All the valuables in the house were known to her.

    One night, after the usual time when people retire for the night, six thieves descended from the house, all masked and armed with knives. The other servants fled in fear. This woman heard the noise and rushed out, but was seized by the thieves. One of the thieves held a knife to her neck and asked, "Where is your master?" She replied, "My master is on duty and has not returned." The thief waved his companions and said, "Quickly bind the mistress and bring her here." The servant knelt and wept, saying, "The mistress has been kind to me. Please do not frighten her. I am willing to take her place." The thief said, "Since it is so, just tell me where the valuables are." The servant hesitated to speak, and the thief raised the knife to cut her. She was greatly frightened and then told everything. After searching thoroughly, the thieves were still not satisfied and again threatened her with the knife to make her tell everything. She said, "All the valuables are gone. There are still jewels and ornaments in a certain place."

    119

    ”于是盗又尽取之，笑谓妇曰：“汝所言未必尽，实念汝颇忠诚，姑留余地。”乃呼啸而去。主母深感此妇，出而慰谢之，而妇则面色如土，不作他语，连呼“吓杀，吓杀”而已。比晓，主人归，知状，亦尉谢之。然念妇虽为盗所劫，何必尽情泄露，当遑遽时，乃纤悉不遗如此乎？且仆妇甚多，何以独劫此妇？其事似有可疑。而妇自此即云惊悸成疾，越三日以病重告归。主人重赏而遣之，密遣干仆尾之行。妇初卧车中，出齐化门，即自起遣车反，而别雇一车以行，至昌平州某村止焉。有数人迎门而笑，妇亦笑而入。仆即奔告于官，遣役偕往。时已半夜，夺门而进，则妇正与众分所盗之物，金帛首饰俱在，缚送官，论如律。

    119

    Thereupon, the thieves took everything and laughed at the woman, saying, "What you said may not be entirely true, but in truth I think you are quite loyal, so I will leave some room for you." They then shouted and left. The mistress was deeply moved by this woman and came out to comfort and thank her. However, the woman's face was as pale as soil, and she said nothing else, only repeatedly crying out, "Scared to death, scared to death." By dawn, the owner returned and knew the situation, also reassuring and thanking her. Yet, he thought that although the woman had been robbed by the thieves, why reveal everything in full detail at such a time of panic? Why did she reveal everything so thoroughly like this? And there are many servants, why did the thieves specifically target this woman? The matter seems suspicious. The woman became ill from fright and, after three days, reported being seriously ill and returned home. The owner gave her a generous reward and sent her away, secretly sending a trusted servant to follow her. The woman was lying in the carriage, exited through the Qihua Gate, got up and sent the carriage back, hired another carriage to continue her journey, and arrived at a certain village in Changping State. There, several people welcomed her at the door with laughter, and the woman laughed and entered as well. The servant immediately reported this to the authorities, who dispatched officials to accompany him. By midnight, they forcibly entered the door and found the woman dividing the stolen goods with others, including gold, silk, and jewelry. She was tied up and sent to the authorities, who judged her according to the law.

    120

    京师又有一部郎家，佣一少妇，乃三河人。妇与其夫成亲甫一月而别，别时与夫约曰：“岁暮宜至京城某媒媪家访我所在，而见我焉，我必有以畀汝。”及岁暮，妇日至门前候其夫。一日，有乡人徘徊其门，妇见之，误以为夫也。盖相聚不久，而别已经月，面貌音声，不甚了了矣。遽招之曰：“尔今日始来欤？我望眼欲穿矣，汝少待，我取钱与汝。”其人惘然莫测，姑待之。妇果携钱券数纸出付之，其人大喜持去。明日，其夫果来，阍者达于妇，妇讶其复至，出而诘之曰：“昨付尔之钱，尚不足用乎？我则无矣。

    Explanation

    120

    In the capital city, there was another household of officials that hired a young woman from the Three Rivers region. The woman had only just been married to her husband for a month when they parted. At the time of their separation, she made an agreement with her husband, saying, "By the end of the year, you should go to the house of a certain matchmaker in the capital city to find out where I am and see me there. I will surely have something to give you." By the end of the year, the woman waited daily at the door for her husband. One day, a villager lingered at the door, and the woman saw him, mistaking him for her husband. Because they had been together for a short time and had been apart for a month, their appearance and voice were not very clear. She immediately called out to him, saying, "Have you just arrived today? I've been waiting with bated breath. Wait a moment, I'll get some money for you." The man was puzzled and couldn't understand, so he waited. The woman indeed carried several pieces of paper with money and gave them to him. The man was very happy and took them away. The next day, her husband indeed came, and the gatekeeper informed the woman. The woman was surprised that he had returned and came out to ask him, saying, "The money I gave you yesterday, is it still not enough? I don't have any more."

    121

    ”夫曰：“我昨暮始至城中，宿于汝所言媒媪家，今日倩其幼子导引而来，昨岂曾至此。尔得无梦欤？”妇始悟昨日之误认，痴立无语，嘤嘤啜泣。夫固问之，乃哭失声。内主人闻之，呼入问故，知其事，大笑，怜其少年夫妇以贫故别离，以钱十馀贯与之，使畀其夫焉。苏州枫桥有高某，以课蒙为业，而性喜吟咏。一日，有江北难民数人阑入其塾乞食，高方执笔吟《感怀》诗，得句云：“有限年华催老易。”属对未就，难民中一老翁援笔续之曰：“无灵文字送穷难。”高大喜，延之坐，不坐；问其名，不告；赠以钱，受之而去。乌呼！江湖漂泊中乃有诗人哉，亦可喟矣！

    Explanation

    121

    The husband said, "I arrived in the city last night and stayed at the matchmaker's house you mentioned. Today, I asked her young son to guide me here. I didn't come here yesterday. Could you have been dreaming?" The wife then realized her mistake from the previous day, stood in a daze without speaking, and began to sob softly. The husband asked her again, and she cried out in distress. The host heard this and called her in to ask what had happened. Upon understanding the situation, he laughed heartily, pitied the young couple who had been separated by poverty, and gave them more than ten strings of cash, instructing her to give it to her husband. In Suzhou, at Feng Bridge, there was a person named Gao who made a living teaching children and enjoyed reciting poetry. One day, several refugees from the north of the river intruded into his school to beg for food. Gao was just then holding a brush and reciting a poem titled "Sentimental Reflections," coming up with a line that said, "Limited years hasten old age easily." He had not yet completed the couplet when one of the elderly refugees picked up a brush and continued it, saying, "Words without inspiration cannot easily send away poverty." Gao was very pleased, invited him to sit, but the old man did not sit; when Gao asked for his name, he did not tell; Gao gave him some money, which he accepted and left. Ah! There are poets among the wandering refugees in the rivers and lakes, which is also something to sigh about!

    122

    光绪丁丑九月，扬州城内教场中有山东人张设布围，其中有奇形人五。一男子上体如常人，而两腿皆软，若有筋无骨者，有人抱其上体而旋转之，如绞索然。一男子胸间伏一婴儿，皮肉合而为一，五官四体悉具，能运动言语。一男子右臂仅五六寸，右手小如钱，而左臂长过膝，左手大如蒲葵扇。一男子脐大于杯，能吸淡巴菰，以管入脐中，则烟从口出。一女子双足纤小，两乳高耸，而颔下虬髯如戟。于是观者甚众。事闻于官，谓是采生折割者流，逐之出境。

    Explanation

    122

    In the ninth month of the Dingchou year during the Guangxu era, within the military parade ground of Yangzhou city, there were people from Shandong setting up a cloth enclosure, where there were five individuals with strange forms. One man had an upper body like a normal person, but both his legs were soft, as if they had tendons but no bones. Someone held his upper body and rotated it, like a twisted rope. Another man had a baby hidden in his chest, with skin and flesh united as one, all five senses and four limbs fully present, capable of movement and speech. A third man had a right arm that was only five or six inches long, and a right hand as small as a coin, while his left arm was longer than his knee, and his left hand as large as a palm leaf fan. A fourth man had a navel larger than a cup, able to suck on tobacco, and when a tube was inserted into his navel, smoke came out of his mouth. A fifth woman had slender feet, high and prominent breasts, and curly whiskers under her chin like spears. Thus, many people came to watch. The matter was reported to the officials, who said it was the work of those who practice child-stealing and dismemberment, and they were expelled from the city.

    123

    杭州武林门外之山墩有桥曰新桥，乱后重修，颇为坚固。丁丑之秋，村人于桥旁搭台演戏，桥上观者甚多。有小儿呼曰：“桥下有人掊土（《史记。封禅书》《索隐》：掊，扒也），桥将圮矣！”众皆笑而不信。俄顷之间，桥果崩毁，死伤者甚众。此小儿不知谁何，或者鬼神使之警众欤？又闻此日麴部中一老优先知必有变异，恐伤其侪伍，密于台之四柱各斫一刀，又倒燃双烛于房以厌之。岂知伶人无恙，而反伤观者也，殆亦有数存矣。

    　　蒋某，溧阳人。偶立门外，忽有素不相识者，口操绍兴土音，向之借洋钱五枚。蒋曰：“吾适无有，子姑少待，吾将乞诸邻。”及向邻家借钱归，而此人已他去，不可纵迹矣。蒋大呼曰：“吾负汝！”即取刀自刭而死。噫！人虽好行其德，亦不至此，殆有宿冤欤？此绍兴人为人为鬼，未可知也。

    Explanation

    123

    In the hillside outside the West Gate of Wulin in Hangzhou, there is a bridge called the New Bridge, which was rebuilt after a period of chaos and is quite sturdy. In the autumn of Ding Chou year, the villagers set up a stage by the bridge to perform plays, and many people gathered on the bridge to watch. A child shouted, "There is someone digging earth below the bridge (according to the Shiji Fengchan Shu Siyin: '掊' means 'to dig'), the bridge is about to collapse!" Everyone laughed and did not believe him. In a short while, the bridge indeed collapsed, and many were killed or injured. This child's identity is unknown, perhaps it was a warning sent by ghosts or spirits? It is also said that on that day, an old actor in the theater district knew beforehand that something unusual would happen and was afraid of harming his fellow performers. He secretly made a cut on each of the four pillars of the stage and lit two candles upside down in the room to ward off the evil. How could he have known that the actors were unharmed, while the audience was instead injured? Perhaps there was a predetermined fate.

    Jiang, a man from Lishui, was standing outside his door when suddenly a stranger who had never met him before approached, speaking in the Shaoxing dialect, and asked to borrow five silver dollars. Jiang said, "I just don't have any, please wait a moment, I will ask my neighbors." After borrowing money from a neighbor and returning, the stranger had already left, and he could not be traced. Jiang shouted, "I owe you!" and immediately took a knife and cut his throat, dying. Alas! Even if people try to do good, they do not go to such extremes, perhaps it was an old grudge? Whether this Shaoxing person was alive or a ghost, it is unknown.

    124

    直隶永平府某县，其地闺范极严。凡女子初嫁，母家必使侦探，成婚之次日，夫家鼓乐喧阗，宾客杂沓，则大喜；若是日阒然，则女家为之丧气。女之留否，惟夫家为政，不敢与争矣。积习相传如此，虽其意固善，然亦敝俗也。有王姓嫁女于李氏，却扇之夕，李以新妇貌陋，嫌之。次日托言非处子，不举乐，仍呼媒妁送归母家。女幼失母，随其嫂以居，嫂知小姑无他，乃问昨夜洞房情事，则固未合欢也。嫂曰：“然则安知其不贞欤？”力言于翁，使翁讼于官。官命验之，果守礼谨严之处子也。乃判李姓，仍以鼓乐迎归。余按《毛诗。草虫》篇：“亦既觏止，我心则降。”郑康成解“亦既觏止”为男女觏精，解“我心则降”为自此可以宁父母，故心下也。说经者皆笑其陋。今观永平之俗，信必既觏而后可以宁父母，岂诗人之意固如此欤？

    Explanation

    124

    In a certain county under the jurisdiction of Yongping Prefecture in Zhili (now Hebei Province), the standards of women's behavior are extremely strict. Whenever a woman marries for the first time, her mother's family must send someone to observe the wedding night. If the next day, the husband's family is filled with music and drums, and guests are bustling and crowded, it is a great joy; if the day is quiet and empty, the bride's family feels embarrassed. Whether the bride stays or leaves, only the husband's family has the authority, and they dare not dispute it. This custom has been passed down for generations, and although its intention is good, it is also a backward custom. There was a woman named Wang who married into the Li family. On the wedding night, Li rejected the new bride because he found her appearance unattractive. The next day, he claimed she was not a virgin, did not hold a celebration, and still called the matchmaker to send the bride back to her mother's home. The bride had lost her mother at a young age and lived with her sister-in-law. The sister-in-law knew the young bride had no other issues and asked about the events of the previous night, which confirmed that they had not consummated the marriage. The sister-in-law then strongly argued with the father-in-law, making him sue in court. The court ordered an investigation, and it was found that the bride was indeed a chaste and disciplined maiden. The court then ruled that the Li family must still welcome the bride with music and drums.

    I have studied the Mao Shi (Mao's Book of Songs) and found the line: "Once we met, my heart was at ease." Zheng Xuan interpreted "once we met" as referring to men and women meeting and having sexual intercourse, and "my heart was at ease" as meaning that from then on, one can bring peace to one's parents, so the heart becomes calm. Scholars all laugh at Zheng Xuan's interpretation as being crude. Now, observing the custom in Yongping, it is believed that only after seeing each other can one bring peace to one's parents. Is this truly the poet's intention?

    125

    鄞人李姓者，为其弟娶妇，赁其邻陈氏屋作新房。每至夜半，房中辄有声，颇滋疑惧。一夜声作甚厉，举家咸集，觉床下泥土浮松，乃共掘之，得铜盆四具，铁如意一柄，铁碗一只。嗣后声遂不作，后亦无他。

    　　昌黎云：“偶然题作木居士，便有无穷求福人。”《风俗通》所载如鲍君、李君、石贤士等，大率类此，从古然矣。乃若津门所传雪弥勒事，则尤可笑。某岁津门大雪，好事者戏聚雪作弥勒，低眉垂目，笑态可掬，偏袒踞坐，大腹彭亨，右手持牟尼珠，左手持布袋。又为作侍者二人，皆生动有致。愚夫愚妇见之，膜拜作礼，竟有以香烛供奉者。诸无赖子遂藉以敛钱，侈谈灵异。瞻礼者众，乃搭棚以盖之，檐前悬红灯二，居然一佛殿矣。然人多气盛，又益以香气烛光，熏蒸终日，未及浃辰，玉山矣，诸善信皆废然而返。

    Explanation

    125

    A man from Yin named Li, married his younger brother and rented a house from his neighbor Chen for a new home. Every night at midnight, strange sounds would come from the room, causing much suspicion and fear. One night, the sounds were particularly loud, and the whole family gathered. They noticed that the soil under the bed was loose and dug it up, finding four copper bowls, one iron如意 (a ceremonial object), and one iron bowl. Afterward, the sounds ceased, and there were no further incidents.

    Han Yu said, "Accidentally writing it as a wooden Bodhisattva, there are countless people seeking blessings." The Fengsu Tong records stories like those of Bao Jun, Li Jun, and Shi Xiansheng, which are largely similar to this, and have been this way since ancient times. As for the story of the snow Maitreya in Tianjin, it is especially laughable. In a certain year, there was a heavy snowfall in Tianjin, and some playful people gathered snow to make a Maitreya. With lowered eyebrows and eyes, a smiling face that is endearing, sitting cross-legged with one arm bare, a large belly protruding, holding a mala (prayer beads) in the right hand and a cloth bag in the left hand. They also made two attendants, both lively and expressive. Foolish men and women saw it and bowed in worship, some even offering incense and candles. Various rascals then used it to collect money, extravagantly talking about miraculous things. Many people came to worship, so they built a pavilion to cover it, hanging two red lanterns in front of the eaves, making it look like a Buddhist temple. The people were numerous and their spirits were high, and with the added fragrance of incense and the light of candles, they were steamed all day, not even reaching noon, and the snow mountain melted. All the believers returned disappointed.

    126

    扬州兴教寺有万佛楼，相传前明所建。楼中之佛，实有万数，雕镂工巧。兵燹之后，此楼未毁。丁丑正月，有白发翁登楼焚香，谓寺僧曰：“中悬长生灯，宜防失火。”寺僧亦姑诺之。乃越十日，果以灯火上腾，由楼而至大殿、方丈，竟罹祝融之虐。此翁之言不幸而中，岂鬼神使之先告欤？闻灾之前三日，楼中佛像自仆于地者十数尊，亦可异也。

    Explanation

    126

    Yangzhou's Xingjiao Temple has a Wanfo Tower, said to have been built during the early Ming Dynasty. The tower houses an actual number of Buddha statues, intricately carved with great skill. After the devastation of war, this tower remained undamaged. In the first month of the year in the sexagenary cycle of Ding Chou, an elderly man with white hair ascended the tower to burn incense and told the temple monk, "There is a long-life lamp hanging inside; one should be cautious of fire." The monk agreed to his suggestion. Ten days later, indeed, the fire rose from the lamps, spreading from the tower to the main hall and the abbot's quarters, ultimately suffering the wrath of the Fire God. The old man's words were unfortunately proven true; could it be that the gods had warned him in advance? Three days before the disaster, more than a dozen Buddha statues in the tower fell to the ground by themselves, which is also quite strange.

    127

    天津有邬三者，其父以沙船起家，死已久矣。邬三性嗜赌，遂耗其赀，田园皆归他姓，惟屋犹在，与母共居之。俄而母死，津俗丧礼尚奢，而出殡尤甚，邬卖屋治丧，遂无立锥地，寄居博徒家。有姑嫁奚姓，颇富，以其侄不肖，亦久不与通矣。邬年二十余，尚未有室。一岁迫岁除，窘甚，无以为生。有博徒与之谋，假以衣冠，使至其姑家求见，姑辞焉。告阍者曰：“此来非有干求，特以将成婚礼，不敢不告于长者耳。”姑闻此语，乃命入见，衣冠楚楚，颇不蓝缕。问频年何在，以贸易对；问婚期何日，曰：“即后日是也。”姑大喜，赠银十两为婚费，并云：“届期当来贺。”姑有子妇二人，各送津钱十千。邬持银钱，归商于博徒。诸博徒喜曰：“然则尚有后惠矣。”乃即所居屋，使工为之标饰，觅一青年之妓，饰以荆布，使伪为新妇也者。及期，其姑果至，见妇而悦之。妇又善于承迎，入厨作羹，跪坐而馈。

    Explanation

    127

    In Tianjin, there was a man named Wu San. His father had made his fortune through sand ships and had been dead for a long time. Wu San was addicted to gambling and thus squandered his wealth. His farmland and gardens were all taken by others, but only the house remained. He lived with his mother in it. Soon after his mother died, the funeral rites in Tianjin were extravagant, and the funeral procession was even more so. Wu San sold the house to pay for the funeral and thus had nowhere to stand, living with gamblers.

    He had an aunt who married into the Xi family, who was quite wealthy, but because she considered her nephew unworthy, she had not communicated with him for a long time. Wu San was in his twenties and had not yet married. One year, as the New Year approached, he was in dire straits and had no means of living. A gambler made a plan with him, lent him formal attire, and sent him to his aunt's house to ask for an audience. The aunt declined, and he told the gatekeeper, "I am not here to make any requests, but because I am about to be married, I dare not fail to inform the elder." The aunt heard this and allowed him to enter. He was neatly dressed and did not look shabby. She asked where he had been for many years, and he replied that he had been engaged in trade. She asked when the wedding would be, and he said, "It will be the day after tomorrow." The aunt was very pleased and gave him ten taels of silver as a wedding gift, and said, "I will come to congratulate you on the day." The aunt had two married daughters, each gave him ten thousand cash. Wu San took the silver and cash and returned to consult with the gamblers. The gamblers were delighted and said, "So there will still be future benefits." They then decorated his house with the workers, found a young prostitute, dressed her in plain clothes, and had her pretend to be the bride. On the day, the aunt indeed came, saw the woman, and was pleased with her. The woman was also good at serving and welcoming, entered the kitchen to cook soup, and knelt to serve it.

    128

    姑欣然食已，谓曰：“此屋逼仄，吾不能宿此，明日当复来，少有资助。”明日又至，出屋契一纸曰：“此屋赠汝夫妇，即可迁移其中。”又出田契曰：“薄田百亩，粗供饣粥。”邬惊喜过望。此妓之父亦一博徒，因负人博进，暂以女为钱树子，今知邬有田有屋，即以女妻之。弄假成真，邬之谓与？陈某，杭州人。素性迂谨。负人钱百千，不能偿，忧愤不释，遂成心疾。一日，路过鬻明器之肆，见有纸糊元宝，晒于地上，大喜曰：“此天赐我也。

    Explanation

    128

    The woman, pleased, finished her meal and said, "This house is cramped, and I cannot stay here. I will come back tomorrow with some assistance." The next day, she returned and handed over a deed to the house, saying, "I give this house to you and your wife, and you may move in." She then presented a land deed, stating, "A hundred acres of land, which can roughly provide for your basic needs." Wu was overjoyed beyond expectation. The father of the prostitute was also a gambler who had lost a large sum of money and temporarily used his daughter as a means to earn money. Now that he learned Wu had land and a house, he married his daughter to Wu. Thus, what began as a pretense became reality. Is this not what happened to Wu? Teng, a native of Hangzhou, was naturally cautious and reserved. He owed a hundred thousand coins and could not repay it, which caused him great distress and led to a mental illness. One day, as he passed by a shop selling ritual objects, he saw paper-made gold ingots being dried in the sun and exclaimed, "This is a gift from heaven!"

    129

    然余无多求，取其二足矣。”即取二元宝去。肆中人知其也，亦姑听之。陈归，语妻曰：“今日天赐我二元宝，可以偿宿负矣。”妻取视之，笑曰：“此泉台上宝也。”（见《清异录》。）陈曰：“我取归时，明明元宝二枚，今化为纸，天绝我也！”遂痛哭求死。妻劝慰之，不可，其病益剧。亲属中有高姓者，素丰于财，且勇于为善，乃取真元宝二付其妻，使诡告其夫曰：“天赐之宝，又化为银矣。”陈大喜，遂以易钱偿所负，以其余为卒岁资，而心疾顿愈。

    Explanation

    129

    "I don't have many demands; just two will suffice." He then took two gold ingots and left. The people in the shop knew he was [unusual/crazy], and they temporarily let him go. Chen returned home and told his wife, "Today, heaven has given me two gold ingots, which can be used to repay my old debts." His wife took them and looked at them, smiling and saying, "These are treasures from the netherworld." (See Qing Yi Lu.) Chen said, "When I took them back, they were clearly two gold ingots, but now they have turned into paper; heaven has forsaken me!" He then wept bitterly and sought death. His wife tried to comfort him, but he would not be consoled, and his illness worsened. Among his relatives, there was a man surnamed Gao, who was wealthy and brave in doing good deeds. He took two genuine gold ingots and gave them to Chen's wife, instructing her to tell her husband, "The treasures given by heaven have turned into silver again." Chen was greatly pleased and used them to exchange for money to repay his debts, using the rest as resources for the rest of the year, and his mental illness suddenly healed.

    130

    苏州胥门外梅湾里，有姚妪者，老寡妇也。抚孤子，至二十岁，将为娶妇。积洋钱四十余圆，以备婚费。又制银首饰数事，并新妇庚帖，同置一小椟中。一夕，忽然失去。妪念十余年所蓄，全在于此，今既失之，不能复措，而婚期已近，计无所出，朝夕悲泣。越三夕，忽于人定后闻屋上有人呼曰：“姚大娘！前夜冒昧，几误人婚姻，今仍送还，可至檐下取之，我去矣。”妪呼子出视，果得原物于檐下。斯人也，所谓梁上君子者乎？

    Explanation

    130

    In the outskirts of Suzhou, outside the Xumen Gate in Meiwang Lane, there lived an elderly widow named Old Lady Yao. She had raised an orphaned son until he reached the age of twenty, at which point she intended to arrange his marriage. Over the years, she had saved more than forty foreign silver dollars to cover the wedding expenses. Additionally, she had prepared several pieces of silver jewelry and the bride's birth chart, all of which were placed in a small box. One night, these items suddenly went missing. Thinking of the years of savings she had accumulated, Old Lady Yao realized that everything she had was now lost. She knew she could not replace the items, and with the wedding date approaching, she felt utterly at a loss. Day and night, she wept in despair. Three nights later, after everyone had gone to bed, she heard someone call out from above the house: "Old Lady Yao! Last night, I acted rashly and nearly ruined someone's marriage. Today, I am returning the items. Please come to the eaves to retrieve them. I am leaving now." Old Lady Yao called her son out to see, and indeed, the original items were found under the eaves. Such a person, one might say, is the so-called "gentleman on the beam."

    131

    去丁字沽三十里，有地名杨柳青，其地多女闾。有某甲浪游至此，见一家门署“招租”二字，旁注：“欲租者，请入面议。”某本无求舍之心，姑作寻芳之想，款扉而入。一苍头导之升堂，有少妇自房出，明眸皓齿，年约二十许。请导观屋宇，乃招之入房，红罗复帐，角枕锦衾，固其卧室也。某欲辞出，妇曰：“良人远客，度日无资。既不能枯坐食贫，又不屑倚门卖笑，姑借招租之帖，聊当卖酒之帘。君既惠临，岂得无所留赠？”某辞以无钱，妇笑曰：“何言之谦也。”即至身旁搜索，于靴幼夹袋中得钱券六十千，谢曰：“有此可为缠头之费矣。”即持付户外，交二男子手。某视妇虽荏弱，而此二男子固健者，知堕其计中，不敢与争，踉跄而出。此与前所记荷包出售事情状略同。某虽见机而作，未受虚惊，然所费不赀矣，亦可为冶游者戒也。

    Explanation

    131

    Thirty li away from Dingzihu, there is a place called Yangliuqing, where many brothels can be found. There was a man named Mr. A who was wandering around and came to this place.
