He saw a sign on a door that read "For Rent," with a note beside it that said, "Those who wish to rent, please enter to discuss in person." Mr. A had no intention of seeking shelter, but he decided to go in, thinking he might find some pleasure. An old servant led him upstairs, and a young woman emerged from a room. She had bright eyes and a radiant smile, and she appeared to be about twenty years old. He asked the servant to show him the house, and the woman invited him into her room. The room was adorned with a red silk double-layered canopy, a horn pillow, and a brocade quilt, clearly her bedroom. Mr. A wanted to leave, but the woman said, "My husband is a distant guest, and we have no means to live on. We cannot sit idly and live in poverty, nor do we wish to sell ourselves for money. We borrow the 'for rent' sign to serve as a substitute for a 'selling wine' sign. Since you have kindly come, how can we not offer something in return?" Mr. A tried to refuse, saying he had no money, but the woman laughed and said, "How modest of you to say that." She immediately searched through his belongings and found sixty thousand coins in his boot pocket. She thanked him and said, "With this, we can have enough for entertainment." She then handed the money to two men outside. Mr. A saw that although the woman seemed weak, the two men were strong, and he realized he had fallen into their trap. He did not dare to argue and left in a hurry. This situation was similar to what happened when he tried to buy a bag. Although Mr. A acted quickly and avoided a false alarm, he still spent a lot of money, which serves as a warning to those who indulge in pleasure-seeking.

    132

    粤西有姚三者，幼时不异常人。年十八时，偶钓于池，得一鱼，无鳞，烹而食之。忽暴病，月余病愈，则躯体骤长尺许。已而屡病屡愈，病愈，体必加长。数年之间，长及一丈矣。然其首仍与常人无异。询其故，则食鱼时弃鱼头未食，一犬食之，俄而犬首亦大倍于前。惜此犬旋为人扑杀，否则亦必有可观矣。江西丰城乡间，某年春夏间，路毙一媪。众人悯之，醵钱买棺以敛，舁至义冢。未及葬埋，忽雷电大作，风雨暴至，众皆趋避。俄而霹雳一声，棺截为两段，此媪起坐棺中，视之则已生矣。余谓此媪偶然气闭，非真死也，为雷所震荡，气复流通，故仍生耳。然其棺无故为迅雷所劈，亦非偶然，殆命不应绝，如虢太子之遇扁鹊矣。

    Explanation

    132

    In the western part of Guangdong, there was a man named Yao San. As a child, he was no different from others. At the age of eighteen, he once fished in a pond and caught a fish without scales, which he cooked and ate. Suddenly, he fell gravely ill and remained sick for over a month. When he recovered, his body had suddenly grown by about a foot. After that, he suffered many illnesses and recovered many times, and each time he recovered, his body would always grow longer. Over the course of several years, he grew to a height of one zhang (about 3.3 meters). However, his head remained the same as that of ordinary people. When asked about the reason, it was explained that when he ate the fish, he left the fish head uneaten, and a dog ate it. Soon, the dog's head also became much larger than before. Unfortunately, the dog was soon killed by people; otherwise, it would have been a remarkable sight. In a rural area of Fengcheng, Jiangxi, during a certain spring and summer season, an elderly woman died on the road. The people felt pity for her and collected money to buy a coffin for burial, carrying her to a public cemetery. Before the burial was completed, a great thunderstorm and violent wind and rain came, and everyone fled. Soon there was a loud clap of thunder, and the coffin was split into two pieces. The elderly woman stood up in the coffin, and when looked at, she was already alive. I believe the elderly woman had a sudden case of asphyxia, not that she was truly dead. The thunder shook her, and her breath returned to circulation, so she remained alive. However, her coffin being split by a sudden clap of thunder was not accidental; it was likely that her fate was not to die, like Duke Guo's son being revived by Bian Que.

    133

    鄞有汪翁，富于财。妻死无子，纳一妾，生子而殇。及翁死，族人利其财，逼嫁其妾。翁死后三七之日，其家作佛事，亲族毕集，妾出遍拜之，泣而言曰：“未亡人虽小家女，亦知从一而终之义，誓死不出此门，愿长斋奉佛，以了余生。但求诸尊长见怜，与我一室之地，我便削发修行，家中赀产，所不问也。”言已，袖中出翦刀，翦发投地，哭而入内，时其年甫二十也。众人中亦有为感泣者，乃扫除屋西偏一小楼，使居之。呜呼！是亦居然一燕子楼矣。

    Explanation

    133

    In Yin there was a Mr. Wang, who was wealthy in wealth. He was widowed with no son and took a concubine, who had a son who died young. When the old man died, clan members coveted his wealth and forced to marry off his concubine. On the twenty-first day after the old man's death, his family performed Buddhist rites, and all relatives gathered. The concubine went out to greet them all and wept and said, "I, though a woman from a small family, also understand the principle of fidelity to one's husband until death, swore to never leave this door, wished to observe a long fast and worship Buddha, to end my remaining life. Only asked the respected elders for mercy, to give me a room, I will then shave my head and practice Buddhism, the family's wealth, I will not inquire about." After speaking, she took a scissors from her sleeve, shaved her head and threw it to the ground, wept and went inside, at the time she was just twenty. Some in the crowd were moved to tears, they then cleaned a small tower in the western part of the house, and made her live there. Alas! This is also a Yanzi Tower.

    134

    有丐者乞食于吴市，忽有武弁遇之，问曰：“子非某乡某氏子乎？”丐曰：“然。”弁曰：“吾尝受子之惠，今何一寒至此？”乃携之至茶肆，使之啜茗，小坐以待，未几取衣服帽履而至，悉与易之。又于怀中出银十余两与之，曰：“子有此，可作归计，勿久吹吴市箫也。”丐者茫然不测所自来，向弁问故。弁曰：“事虽久远，子忘之，我不敢忘也。”竟别去。此弁感恩报德，殊有风义，使其得志，则淮阴侯之一饭千金，岂所难哉！

    Explanation

    134

    A beggar was begging for food in Wu city when a low-ranking military officer suddenly encountered him. The officer asked, "Are you not the son of a certain family from a certain place?" The beggar replied, "Yes." The officer said, "I once received your favor, but how have you fallen into such poverty?" He then took the beggar to a tea house, had him drink tea, and sat with him for a short while. Shortly after, he brought clothes, a hat, and shoes, and exchanged them all with the beggar. The officer then took out more than ten taels of silver from his pocket and gave it to him, saying, "With this, you can make plans to return home. Do not stay here begging for long." The beggar was completely puzzled and unable to guess where it came from, so he asked the officer why he had done this. The officer replied, "Though the matter is long past, you have forgotten it, but I have not." He then left. This officer showed great gratitude and a sense of righteousness, and if he had succeeded, he would have easily repaid a favor as Han Xin did with the "thousand gold pieces for a single meal."

    135

    番禺李氏女，许嫁赵氏子。赵子以贫故，谋食海外，岁久不归，音问遂绝。女待至二十八岁，父母欲别嫁之，女不可。乃使媒妁言于赵氏，先娶妇入门，以待其子之归，盖亦粤俗然也。女于是或居夫家，或居母家，又历十余年。而赵氏子果由海外附轮船以归，行至中途，轮船飘没，同舟之人皆死于海。惟赵抱一木浮游数十里，遇他船救之，得生还家。后与女重行合卺之礼，夫妇皆年将五十矣。赵之遇难不死，或鬼神哀怜贞女，故阴相之，使得完聚欤？

    Explanation

    135

    A woman from the Li family in Panyu was engaged to marry a son of the Zhao family. Because of poverty, the Zhao son went overseas to make a living and did not return for many years, and news ceased entirely. The woman waited until she was twenty-eight years old, and her parents wanted to arrange another marriage for her, but she refused. They then sent a matchmaker to speak to the Zhao family, and a proxy wife was married into the household to wait for the Zhao son's return, which was also a custom of the Guangdong region. The woman thus sometimes lived with her husband's family and sometimes with her mother's family, and this continued for over a decade. The Zhao son eventually returned by steamship, but on the way, the ship drifted away and was lost, and all those on the same ship died at sea. Only the Zhao held onto a piece of wood and drifted for dozens of miles, where he was rescued by another ship and survived to return home. Later, he renewed the wedding ceremony with the woman, and both the couple were approaching fifty years old. Perhaps the gods or spirits pitied the chaste woman and helped her in secret, allowing them to reunite completely.

    136

    山左程姓者，寓于吴中，有一婢，嫁农家叶氏子。庚申之乱，程全家避于叶氏，器用财贿悉寄焉。不数年，程氏相继死，止遗一幼子在襁褓中，婢抚以为子，使与诸子齿。俄而其夫亦死，婢守义不嫁，抚程子及其子俱成立。为程子聘邻村一女为妻。成婚之日。请姑出，将以新妇见，而婢遽出，自房登&lt；毛瞿&gt；&lt；毛俞&gt；而先拜焉。程子大惊，婢对众自陈曰：“我非新郎之母，乃程氏之婢也。主人不幸遭乱流离，死亡殆尽，我以郎君年幼无人管束，故十余年越主婢之分，冒母子之名，今敢不道其实欤？”于是具述颠末，并出资财，尽以归之。程子欲分其半以与婢，不受，乃使其妻以姑事之，而己仍呼为母焉。

    Explanation

    136

    A Person from Shandong Surnamed Cheng

    A person from Shandong surnamed Cheng temporarily resided in Suzhou. He had a servant girl who married a man from a farming family surnamed Ye. During the tumultuous period of 1640, the Cheng family took refuge with the Ye family, entrusting all their household goods and wealth to them. Within a few years, the Cheng family members died one after another, leaving behind only a young child in swaddling clothes. The servant girl raised the child as if he were her own son, making him grow up with her other sons. Soon after, her husband also died. The servant girl upheld her virtue and did not remarry, raising both the Cheng family's son and her own son to adulthood. She arranged a marriage for the Cheng family's son to a girl from a nearby village. On the wedding day, she asked her aunt to come out to introduce the new bride. However, the servant girl hurriedly came out from her room, climbed up to a high place, and bowed first. The Cheng family's son was greatly surprised. The servant girl explained to the crowd, "I am not the bridegroom's mother; I am a servant of the Cheng family. Our master unfortunately encountered turmoil, and almost all of us died. I raised the young master because he was too young and had no one to take care of him. So, for over ten years, I crossed the boundaries of master and servant, pretending to be mother and son. Now, dare I not reveal the truth?" She then fully described the events, contributed her money and property, and returned everything to the Cheng family's son. The Cheng family's son wanted to give her half of what she had contributed, but she refused. Instead, he made his wife treat her as an aunt, and he continued to call her mother.

    137

    甲与乙，兄弟也，同室而异爨。甲鳏而无子，乙有子，已十余龄矣。甲欲嗣以为子，而乙惑于后妻之言，执不可。一日，乙之子至河干淅米，失足堕于河，后妻望见之，惟大声呼救，坐而不动。甲适有小疾卧床，闻声趋出，一跃入河，拯之而起。子得免，而甲之疾则加剧。乙归知状，感焉，延医治甲疾，以其子子之。乌呼！乙之始惑于后妻之言，必以后妻为爱其子，而以甲为不爱其子焉，不遇事变，真伪乌乎见哉！

    Explanation

    137

    Brothers 甲 and 乙 lived under the same roof but had separate hearths. 甲 was a widower with no children, while 乙 had a son who was already over ten years old. 甲 wished to adopt 乙's son as his own, but 乙 was bewitched by the words of his concubine and insisted on refusing. One day, 乙's son went to the riverbank to wash rice, lost his footing, and fell into the river. The concubine saw him, shouted for help, but sat still and did not move. 甲, who just had a minor illness and was lying in bed, heard the sound, rushed out, leaped into the river, and rescued the boy, lifting him up. The son was saved, but 甲's illness worsened. When 乙 returned and learned the situation, he was deeply moved. He invited a doctor to treat 甲's illness and adopted his son as his own. Alas! Initially, 乙 was bewitched by the words of his concubine, thinking that she loved his son and that 甲 did not. Without this incident, how could the truth and falsehood have been revealed!

    138

    “有病不治，恒得中医”，贾公彦引此入《周礼疏》，非惟古谚，直是经义矣。潘玉泉方伯尝为余言，有病者延医治之，医言宜用麻黄少许以发汗。持方至药肆，而肆中适缺麻黄，以伪品予之，服之无效。次日医至，诧曰：“岂用麻黄太少，不足以发之乎？”乃倍其数，而肆中已购得真麻黄，如方服之，大汗不止而死。然此药之误也。又有兄弟二人，庚申、辛酉间，避乱于沪渎，同时而病，医者各授以方，且戒曰：“病异药异，切勿误投。”而其家止一爨，婢煎药，竟误投焉，次日皆愈。设使不误，不将俱死欤？医之不足恃如此。余谓医所凭者脉也，而脉之失传久矣。《史记。扁鹊传》言扁鹊饮长桑君所与药，“视见垣一方人，以此视病，尽见五藏症结，特以诊脉为名耳。”又曰：“至今天下言脉者，由扁鹊也。”夫扁鹊特以诊脉为名，则其精于医，非精于脉也。而至今言脉者宗之，则是扁鹊特以为名，而后人乃真以治病，即此知其不足恃矣。

    Explanation

    138

    “If a disease is not treated, one often ends up with a mediocre cure.” Jia Gongyan included this saying in his commentary on the Rites of Zhou, noting that it is not only an ancient proverb but also a principle of the classics. Pan Yuquan, a high-ranking official, once told me about a case where a sick person invited a doctor to treat him. The doctor prescribed a small amount of ephedra to induce sweating. The patient carried the prescription to the pharmacy, but it just happened to be out of ephedra, so they gave him a counterfeit version. Taking it had no effect. The next day, the doctor arrived, surprised and said, “Is it because too little ephedra was used, so it wasn’t enough to induce sweating?” He then doubled the amount. By then, the pharmacy had already purchased genuine ephedra. The patient took it according to the prescription and sweated profusely, dying as a result. However, this was the mistake of the medicine. There were also two brothers who, during the years 1660 and 1661, fled to Shanghai to escape the chaos and fell ill at the same time. The doctor gave each of them a different prescription and warned them, saying, “Since the illnesses are different, the medicines are different; do not mix them up.” But their household had only one kitchen, and the maid was preparing the medicine. She accidentally mixed them up, and the next day, they both recovered. If she hadn’t made a mistake, wouldn’t they both have died? The unreliability of doctors is shown in this way. I say that doctors rely on pulse diagnosis, but the art of pulse diagnosis has been lost for a long time. The Biography of Bian Que in the Records of the Grand Historian states that Bian Que drank the medicine given to him by Chang Sang Jun, “and could see people on the other side of a wall. Using this ability, he could see all the internal organs and their ailments. He was known only for pulse diagnosis.” It also says, “Until today, those who talk about pulse diagnosis are all followers of Bian Que.” Bian Que was known only for pulse diagnosis, but even today, those who talk about pulse diagnosis follow him. This shows that Bian Que was known only for pulse diagnosis, while later generations truly used it to treat diseases. From this, we can see that it is unreliable.

    139

    《素问》有三部、九候论，所谓三部者，岂今所谓三部乎？所谓九候者，岂今所谓九候乎？脉法既已失传，医道亦可不讲，而悬壶之客，遍满通衢，衙推之名，被之屠酤。又以其书传自黄帝，其职列于《周官》，从古相承，莫之敢废。父母之于子女，子孙之于祖父，苟医药之不具，即慈孝之有亏，而人之不获终其年命者多矣。医师、卜师，并列《周官》，卜亦圣人之所重，而唐李华有废龟之论。

    Explanation

    139

    The Suwen has discussions on the Three Sections and Nine Pulses. Are the Three Sections referred to today the same as those of old? Are the Nine Pulses referred to today the same as those of old? Since the methods of pulse diagnosis have been lost, one might argue that the study of medicine could be neglected. Yet doctors, symbolized by the gourds they hang, are everywhere in the streets, and titles such as "衙推" (a low-ranking official) are even bestowed upon butchers and wine sellers. Moreover, the Suwen is said to have been passed down from the Yellow Emperor, and its role is listed in the Zhouli (Rites of Zhou), a tradition that has been maintained since ancient times, and no one dares to abandon it. Parents owe their children care, and descendants owe their ancestors respect; if medicine is lacking, then filial piety and benevolence are compromised, and many people fail to reach the end of their natural lives. Both doctors and diviners are listed in the Zhouli, and divination was also considered important by the sages. However, during the Tang Dynasty, Li Hua proposed the idea of abandoning the use of tortoise shells for divination.

    140

    卜可废，医亦何不可废之有？余曾有《废医论》，刻入《俞楼杂纂》，虽骇俗听，不顾也。北地严寒，冬日则水泽腹坚，舟楫不通，虽通洋诸口，不能不停桡以待，谓之封河。若南中，则向无是也。乃光绪丁丑腊月大雪之后，气候凛洌，河中冰厚尺许，来桡去楫，为之停滞者旬有余日。苏城有某姓子，聘胥门外某氏女为妻，期于是月初八日迎娶。乃至是而冰雪交阻，将由陆路，则雪深没胫，舆不能行；将由水路，则冰坚如石，舟不能进。两家父母令新郎新妇望空交拜，以应吉时，越七日而黄姑织女乃得相见。是亦婚礼之中，亡于礼者之礼也。

    Explanation

    140

    Divination can be abandoned, and why not medicine as well? I once wrote The Discourse on Abandoning Medicine, which was included in Yulou Miscellaneous Compilation. Though it may shock the common ear, I remain unconcerned. The northern lands are severely cold, and in winter, the water freezes solid in the middle, making it impossible for boats to pass. Even at the open ports, one cannot help but stop and wait, a phenomenon known as river freezing. In contrast, the southern regions have never experienced such conditions. During the twelfth month of the Dingchou year in the Guangxu era, after a heavy snowfall, the climate became extremely cold, and the ice on the river grew to be about a foot thick. Boats coming and going were delayed for more than ten days. A man from a certain family in Suzhou had engaged a woman from a certain family outside Xumen Gate as his wife, planning to marry on the eighth day of that month. However, snow and ice blocked the way. If they took the land route, the snow would be deep up to the knees, and carriages could not move. If they took the water route, the ice would be as hard as stone, and boats could not proceed. The parents of both families made the bridegroom and bride perform a ceremony of mutual bowing in the air to meet the auspicious time. After seven days, the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl were finally able to meet. This is also a form of wedding ritual that has been lost.

    141

    鄞有卜者，设肆于市。一日，有男子在肆中大骂，势将用武，众人环集问故。其人曰：“夏间因人口不安，就彼问卜。彼问灶何向，我对曰：”南向。‘彼曰：“宜改西南。’我谨如其言。乃至秋而仍多疾病，又来问卜，彼仍问灶何向，我曰：”西南。‘彼曰：“宜改正西。’我亦如其言。今入冬后，病者未愈，加以贸易折耗，无聊之至，姑再卜之。彼问如前，及我告之，则曰：”宜改南向。‘是仍复其初矣。自夏徂冬，我奉彼为蓍龟，乃颠倒如此乎！“众大笑，为解劝之而去。余因忆亲串中有从相宅者之说，一岁之中，三易其灶而复其初，与此相类。然彼则三人三说，此则一人而前后异说，尤可笑也。江湖术士之说，固无足据矣。

    Explanation

    141

    In Yinzhou, there was a diviner who set up a stall in the market. One day, a man in the stall began to shout loudly, and it seemed he was about to use violence. The crowd gathered around to ask what was happening. The man said, "During the summer, because my family was not well, I went to him for divination. He asked which direction the stove was facing, and I said, 'South.' He said, 'It should be changed to southwest.' I followed his advice. But by autumn, my family was still sick, so I went to him again for divination. He asked again which direction the stove was facing, and I said, 'Southwest.' He said, 'It should be changed to west.' I followed his advice again. Now that winter has arrived, the sick have not recovered, and my business has suffered losses, leaving me very bored. So I decided to consult him again. He asked the same question, and when I told him, he said, 'It should be changed back to south.' This brings me back to where I started. From summer to winter, I have treated him as a diviner, and his advice has been so inconsistent! The crowd laughed and tried to calm me down before I left. I remembered that among my relatives, there was someone who had consulted a house surveyor, and in one year, the stove's orientation was changed three times and returned to its original position, similar to this case. However, in that case, there were three people giving three different opinions, whereas in this case, it was one person giving inconsistent advice, which is even more laughable. The sayings of itinerant diviners are indeed not worth relying on."

    142

    苏有某翁，于庚申之乱，失其子，旋于乱中收得他人子。及乱定复归，屋庐幸而无恙，乃即以他人子为子，而为之娶妇焉。俄而此子死，将为之敛，忽欠伸而起，遍拜其父母曰：”别父母久矣，幸无恙乎？“又指其所娶之妇而问曰：”此伊谁也？“怪而察其声，则即其所失之子也。问曰：”汝故吾子欤？今从何来？“其子曰：”吾自与父母相失，转徙至某所某所，今甫趁某人之船而归耳。“言未已，其所谓某人者自外叩门而入，曰：”我载尔子归，将至家而暴病死，今其尸尚在舟也。“父母趋而视之，则果其子之尸，始悟其子死，而借他人子之尸以生也。因即以所具之棺佥之，共相庆幸，谓假子死而真子生矣。乃其所娶之妇，则以其夫声音举止与昔顿殊，耻与同室。舅姑无如何，请其父母来再三劝导，始为夫妇如初。

    Explanation

    142

    There was an old man named Su, who lived through the Geng Shen disaster. During this chaos, he lost his son. Soon after, he found and adopted another child. When the chaos subsided and he returned home, he was fortunate that his house and property remained undamaged. He then took the adopted child as his own and arranged for him to marry. Not long after, the adopted son died. Just as they were preparing to bury him, the son suddenly sat up, stretched, and bowed to his parents, saying, "I have been away from you for a long time. Are you both well?" He then pointed to the woman he had married and asked, "Who is this?" Surprised, the parents listened carefully to his voice and realized it was their own son. They asked, "Are you really our son? Where have you been?" The son replied, "After we were separated, I wandered to various places and finally boarded a ship with someone to return home." Before he finished speaking, the person who had brought him home knocked on the door and said, "I brought your son back, but he suddenly fell ill and died on the way. His body is still on the boat." The parents rushed outside to see the body and realized that it was indeed their son's body. They then understood that their original son had been presumed dead, and the body of the adopted son had been used as a substitute. They placed the body in the coffin and were relieved that their true son had returned. However, the woman he had married was ashamed of her husband's changed voice and mannerisms and refused to live with him. The old man's in-laws could not do anything to resolve the situation, so they repeatedly asked the parents to come and persuade her. Finally, she agreed to return to her husband, and they resumed their life together as before.

    143

    光绪丙子夏秋之交，浙中大雨，临安一县凡出蛟二十七尾，其北乡有二山忽合而为一。徐花农孝廉书来述之，言其邑人来赴乡试者所言，当不妄也。余按《明史。五行志》，秦州有二山相距甚远，民居其间者数百万家。一日地震，两山合，居民并入其中。然则此事亦前史所有，不足为异矣。

    　　绍兴某翁，生一子而妻死；乃续娶焉，又生一子。两子并长成，皆有室矣，而翁死。遗产千金，族人为均分之，俾兄弟同居而异爨。乃其弟慵惰，耗其所分之产，遂与母谋，将逐兄而据其所有。于是母子、兄弟、娣姒间，终日勃溪矣。兄不堪其逼，一夕谓其妻曰：“某所某所有钱若干，尔可抚遗孤度日，我已市有生鸦片烟，拚一死以解冤矣。”妇夺其烟，曰：“君死，我何赖焉？不如我死为愈耳。”夫初以为劝解之词，亦不留意。未几，闻妇在房中声息有异，入视之，则已宛转床蓐间，灌救无及，竟死。妇故妊身数月矣，死后腹中大动，逾时乃止，见者恻然。

    Explanation

    143

    During the summer and autumn of the Guangxu Bingzi year, there was heavy rain in Zhejiang, and in Lin'an County, twenty-seven dragons emerged. In the northern part of the county, two mountains suddenly merged into one. Mr. Xu Huannong, a juren, wrote to describe this, stating that it was what people from his county who came to take the provincial exams had said, and it was likely true. I refer to the "Wuxing Zhi" section of the Ming History, which records that in Qin Prefecture, there were two mountains very far apart, with several million households living between them. One day, an earthquake occurred, and the two mountains merged, with the residents all being swallowed up. Thus, this event also has precedents in historical records and is not unusual.

    A certain elderly man from Shaoxing had one son and his wife died; he then remarried and had another son. Both sons grew up and got married, but the elderly man died. He left behind an inheritance of a thousand gold pieces, which the clan divided equally, allowing the brothers to live together but cook separately. However, the younger brother was lazy and wasted his share of the inheritance. He then plotted with his mother to expel the elder brother and take everything. As a result, mother, son, brothers, and sisters-in-law quarreled all day long. The elder brother, unable to bear the pressure, one night told his wife, "There is a certain amount of money at a certain place. You can raise the orphaned child and make a living. I have already bought opium and am willing to die to resolve the grudge." The wife took the opium and said, "If you die, what will I rely on? It would be better if I died." The husband initially thought it was just words of comfort and did not pay attention. Soon after, he heard strange sounds from the room. He went to see and found her writhing on the bed. Rescue efforts were too late, and she died. The wife was pregnant for several months, and after her death, there was a large movement in her belly, which stopped after a while. Those who saw it felt pity.

    144

    杭有郑姓者，年四十余。妻始有孕，甚爱护之。佣一媪于家，凡爨汲浣濯之事，皆使代之，命其妻安坐勿劳顿。一日，曝衣于庭，而雨骤至，佣媪适他出，乃自起收之。还入室，闻呱呱声，以为邻儿也，察之则出己腹中，大惊而卧。夫归语之，延乳医治之，亦无效。时妊甫七月，儿啼腹中，月余乃止。及期而产，竟无他异。

    　　绍兴张某，自幼聘陈氏女为妇。旋以父母双亡，又遭回禄之虐，无以为生，遂入天台削发为僧。而陈氏待婿不来，访知其遁迹空门，乃别嫁其女于沈氏。成婚之日，沈已大病，勉强成礼，未久即卒，女实犹处子也。其姑怜之，将为择婿。时张某在天台三年矣，有旧友入山遇之，劝其还俗，遂从之归。访所聘妻，已别嫁矣。其友为平章，娶沈氏之妻，而实即其元聘妻也。故剑复得，殊非偶然。但多此周折，不知造物者果玉成之欤，抑播弄之欤？

    Explanation

    144

    In Hangzhou, there was a man surnamed Zheng who was in his forties. His wife was pregnant, and he took great care of her. He hired an old woman to work in his home, and all cooking, fetching water, washing, and cleaning tasks were done by her. He told his wife to sit comfortably and not overwork herself. One day, she was hanging clothes in the courtyard when it suddenly rained heavily. The old woman happened to be out, so she got up herself to collect the clothes. She returned to the room and heard the sound of a baby crying. She thought it was a neighbor's child, but upon checking, she realized it was her own. She was greatly surprised and lay down. Her husband came back and told her, and they called a midwife to treat her, but it was ineffective. At the time, she was seven months pregnant, and the baby cried in her womb for over a month before stopping. When the time came, she gave birth, and there were no other unusual events.

    In Shaoxing, there was a man named Zhang who had been engaged to a woman from the Chen family since childhood. Soon after, he lost both his parents and suffered a fire disaster, leaving him with no means of living, so he went to Tian台 to become a monk. Meanwhile, the Chen family waited for their son-in-law, but when they learned that he had taken refuge in the monastic life, they arranged for their daughter to marry the Shen family instead. On the day of the wedding, Shen was already gravely ill, and the ceremony was carried out with great difficulty. Shortly after, Shen passed away, and the daughter remained a virgin. Her mother-in-law felt sorry for her and planned to find her a new husband. By then, Zhang had been in Tian台 for three years. An old friend of his encountered him in the mountains and persuaded him to return to secular life. Zhang followed his friend's advice and returned home. Upon inquiring about his former wife, he learned that she had already remarried. His friend, who was a high-ranking official, married the woman from the Shen family, and it turned out to be the same woman Zhang had originally been engaged to. It was as if they had found their lost sword again, which seemed quite extraordinary. However, with all these twists and turns, it is unclear whether fate truly wanted to bring them back together or was merely playing with them.

    Now, using the research and context we gathered above, produce a final, polished translation of the source text.

    Your goal is to deliver a final English translation that is both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and reads naturally in English. Apply the insights from the pre-drafting research directly — handle idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as you planned.

    Here are 5 examples showing the expected translation quality and style:

    Example 1

    Source: 爰及農商工賈，廝役奴隸，釣魚屠肉，飯牛牧羊，皆有先達，可為師表，博學求之，無不利於事也。

    Translation: As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    Example 2

    Source: 兩頭四臂女

    靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。

    Translation: A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，

    145

    某孝廉，家贫落魄，无以为生，贷于亲友，皆莫之应。有一博徒，独善遇之，时有馈遗，以资薪米。及公车北上，又为治装，且赡其家。未几，孝廉捷南宫，授县令，感念旧恩，使人招之。谢不往，曰：“吾侪呼卢喝雉，席地帷天，放浪久矣。一入朱门，则束缚欲死，非所以爱我也。使我居君之所，仍日日外出，从牧猪奴游，不于君官声有损乎？又非所以爱君也。”孝廉乃使人赠之千金，亦不受，曰：“君虽日赠我千金，亦不过供我博场之一掷而已，徒伤君惠，而无救我贫，不如其已也。”此博徒见识甚高，使淮阴侯能见及此，则无鸟尽弓藏之叹矣。谁谓市井中无英雄哉！

    Explanation

    145

    A certain scholar-official, impoverished and destitute, had no means of making a living. He borrowed money from relatives and friends, but none responded to his pleas. There was a gambler who alone treated him kindly, occasionally giving him gifts to support his daily needs. When the scholar-official set out for the imperial examination in the capital, the gambler helped him prepare his travel arrangements and even provided for his family. Shortly after, the scholar-official succeeded in the imperial examination and was appointed as a county magistrate. Grateful for the gambler's past kindness, he sent someone to invite him. The gambler refused to come, saying, "We who play dice and indulge in a carefree life have long been unrestrained. Once I enter the red doors of the wealthy and powerful, I will feel constrained to the point of death, which is not what I desire. If I were to stay in your household and still go out every day, associating with lowly people, would that not harm your reputation? And that is not how I could truly show my affection for you." The scholar-official then sent him a thousand gold pieces as a gift, but the gambler refused to accept it, saying, "Even if you give me a thousand gold pieces every day, it would only be enough to fund one round of gambling. It would only hurt your generosity and not alleviate my poverty. It would be better to leave it alone." This gambler had a very high level of insight. If Han Xin (淮阴侯) had been able to see things this way, he would not have lamented the fate of "when the birds are caught, the bow is put away." Who would say that there are no heroes among the common people!

    146

    宁波孙氏，有屋一区，忽于戊寅之春不时火起，虽旋起旋灭，而梁柱窗棂均有焦灼痕。孙氏之子亦猝然颠狂，时发时愈。愈时问之，云见有红袍纱帽者五，自云兄弟五人，皆明季翰林，欲孙氏为之立祠。又不时有字纸从梁上飞下，间有诗句。一日得七言诗一首，用四豪韵，而有跳字，群以为出韵，或以为误笔。少顷梁间飞下一纸云：“《集韵》：跳与逃通。”又问古人用过否？次日临睡，问者于床头得一纸曰：“元微之诗‘主帅惊跳弃旄钺’。”众皆惊服。余按《汉书。高帝纪》：“汉王跳。”如淳曰：“音逃。”此鬼不读古书，但以元微之诗句为证，信乎明季翰林也。

    Explanation

    146

    The Sun Family of Ningbo had a house that suddenly caught fire at irregular intervals during the spring of the year of the Tiger (戊寅). Although the fires started and stopped quickly, the beams, columns, and window frames were all marked with burn scars. The Sun family's son also suddenly went mad, experiencing episodes of madness and recovery. When he recovered, he was asked what he had seen, and he claimed to have seen five men in red robes and official hats, who identified themselves as five brothers, all late Ming dynasty scholars of the Hanlin Academy, and requested that the Sun family build a shrine for them. Additionally, there were times when written papers would fall from the ceiling, some of which contained poems. One day, a seven-character poem was found, using the "Four Heroes" rhyme scheme, but with a missing word. Some thought it was out of rhyme, while others believed it was a writing error. Shortly after, a paper fell from the ceiling, stating, “According to the Jiyun: '跳' and '逃' are interchangeable.” They then asked if this usage had been used by ancient scholars. The next day, before going to sleep, the person who had been asking received a paper from the bed saying, “Yuan Zhen's poem: 'The commander was so startled that he jumped and abandoned his flag and battle-axe.'” Everyone was amazed and impressed. I checked the Book of Han (漢書), specifically the biography of Emperor Gaozu (高帝), and found the commentary by Rú Chún (如淳): “The pronunciation is '逃'.” These ghosts, despite not reading ancient books, used Yuan Zhen's poem as evidence, confirming their identity as late Ming dynasty scholars.

    147

    红兰，苏妓也，与某生订嫁娶，而生无力脱其籍，红兰郁结成疾。有费媪者，佣于妓家者也，谓曰：“娘子倾城姿，何患无藏娇金屋，乃恋恋一穷措大乎？”兰曰：“秦楼楚馆中所往来者，率皆纨绔儿、大腹贾，谁似某郎之甘苦相怜者。彼也力绵，我也命薄，茫茫孽海，不知伊于何底矣。”媪曰：“果尔，吾当为娘子玉成之。”一夕，乘假母他出，负红兰至某生所。生惧，不敢受。媪出红兰身契付生，曰：“吾已为盗得此纸，彼无如何矣。”媪归，乃迹假母所在，而告以红兰逃。寻觅数日，始同至生处见之。假母促兰归，兰誓死不从。妪曰：“此女心变矣，速归取身契，讼于官，必得直，我请为证。”假母归觅契，则无矣，不得讼。媪乃为调停，使生酬假母百金，而红兰竟归生矣。此媪者，其亦古之许俊、昆仑奴欤？

    Explanation

    147

    Hong Lan, a courtesan from Suzhou, had promised to marry a certain scholar, but the scholar was unable to free her from her status as a courtesan, and Hong Lan became ill due to her emotional distress. There was an old woman named Fei who worked as a servant in the courtesan's household. She said to her, "Your beauty is unmatched in the city. Why worry about not having a golden house to keep a beauty? Why are you so attached to a poor scholar?" Hong Lan replied, "Those who come and go in the brothels of Qin and Chu are mostly idle rich and fat merchants. Who is like this scholar who understands and cares for me through thick and thin? He is weak in strength, and I am weak in fate. The vast sea of karma, it is unknown where it will end." The old woman said, "If that is so, I will help you achieve your marriage." One night, while the fake mother was out, she carried Hong Lan to the scholar's place. The scholar was afraid and did not dare to accept. The old woman gave Hong Lan's contract to the scholar and said, "I have already stolen this paper, so she cannot do anything about it." The old woman returned and then found the fake mother's location, and told her that Hong Lan had escaped. After searching for several days, they finally went to the scholar's place to see her. The fake mother urged Hong Lan to return, but she swore to die rather than comply. The old woman said, "This woman's heart has changed. Quickly go back and get the contract, sue in court, and you will definitely win. I will be the witness." When the fake mother returned to look for the contract, it was gone. She could not sue. The old woman then mediated, making the scholar pay the fake mother a hundred gold pieces, and Hong Lan finally married the scholar. This old woman must be the ancient Xu Jun or Kunlun Slave.

    148

    苏人某甲，无赖恶少年也。屡与人斗殴，为人痛打而不悛改，人皆呼之曰“石臼”，以其耐打也。一日，饮于酒家，饮毕径出。酒保索钱，甲曰：“乃翁适乏杖头资，俟诸异日可也。”肆中人恶其无状，群出诟骂，ㄏ而殴之，如舂如揄，血流漂杵。视之几无生理，乃纵之去。越数日复至，则伤痕已愈，咆哮如故。群叹曰：“真石臼也。”予以千钱而去。

    Explanation

    148

    A rascal and wicked young man from Suzhou, named Su, was known for his persistent bad behavior. He had been beaten many times before but never repented or changed. People called him "Stone Mortar" because he could withstand physical punishment. One day, he drank at an inn and, after finishing his drink, left without paying. The bartender demanded payment, and Su said, "Your father is short of money for the stick, wait for another day." The patrons in the tavern were furious and began to insult and beat him, pounding and dragging him until his blood flowed like water. When they saw that he was nearly dead, they let him go. A few days later, he returned, and his injuries had already healed. He continued to act as he had before. The patrons marveled, saying, "He is truly a stone mortar." I gave him a thousand coins, and he left.

    149

    阿保者，小家女也。父母早卒，育于比邻金氏媪。及长，颇有姿，媪欲倚之为钱树子。阿保泣曰：“豢养之恩，没齿不忘，虽鬻我为妾为婢，我不敢怨。若令堕入烟花，则刀锯鼎镬，不能夺我志也。”媪无如何，鬻于某姓为妾。而大妇妒甚，日加鞭笞，体无完肤，阿保惟饮泣而已。一日，妇乘夫外出，送之归金媪，而以逃亡告其夫。阿保俟夫归，作书自陈，杳无复书，亦未知其得达否。金媪乃伪为其夫书，命其他适。阿保得之，即日仰药死。乌呼！此女节烈之行如此，而淹没无闻，谈者并姓氏里居而失之，是可慨矣。

    Explanation

    149

    阿保，是一个出身卑微的女子。她的父母早逝，由邻居家的金氏老妇抚养长大。待到她长大成人，容貌颇为美丽，金氏老妇想把她当作摇钱树来利用。阿保哭着说：“您养育我的恩情，我终身难忘，即使把我卖作妾或婢女，我也不会抱怨。但如果让我沦落风尘，刀锯鼎镬，我也不能改变我的志向。”金氏老妇无可奈何，将她卖给了某户人家作妾。那家的正妻非常嫉妒，每天对她鞭打，打得她体无完肤，阿保只能默默流泪。有一天，正妻趁丈夫外出，把她送回了金氏老妇家，并告诉丈夫说她已经逃跑了。阿保等丈夫回来后，写信为自己辩解，但没有收到任何回信，也不知道信是否送达。金氏老妇于是伪造了丈夫的信，命令她另嫁他人。阿保收到信后，当天就服毒自尽了。唉！这位女子的贞烈行为如此，却默默无闻，连她的姓名和籍贯都被人遗忘，真是令人叹息。

    150

    山东藩司管库之吏某甲，生女甚美，自幼许嫁其从母之弟。未嫁而婿死，问名者踵相接，而女誓不他适。父母劝谕之，不从，遂为别筑一静室居之，长斋事佛者数年，心如古井矣。俄其父盗用官钱事发，系囹圄，当死，计无所出。有富家子谓之曰：“吾丧耦未娶，尔能以女妻我者，我必为代偿官钱。”父母商之女，女执不可。母泣曰：“尔父无生理矣。

    Explanation

    150

    A Certain Official in Shandong, in Charge of the Treasury, Had a Daughter Who Was Very Beautiful. From a Young Age, She Was Betrothed to Her Mother's Younger Brother. Before She Could Marry, Her Fiancé Died, and Those Asking for Her Hand in Marriage Came One After Another, but She Vowed Not to Marry Anyone Else. Her Parents Tried to Persuade Her, but She Did Not Comply, So They Built a Separate Quiet Room for Her to Live In. She Observed Long-Term Vegetarianism and Devoted Herself to Buddhism for Several Years, and Her Heart Became as Still as an Ancient Well. Soon, Her Father Was Caught Using Official Money, and He Was Imprisoned and Sentenced to Death, with No Way Out. A Wealthy Young Man Told Him, "I Have Lost My Wife and Have Not Remarried. If You Can Give Your Daughter to Me as My Wife, I Will Definitely Pay Back the Official Money on Your Behalf." Her Parents Discussed This with Her, but She Firmly Refused. Her Mother Wept and Said, "Your Father Has No Hope of Survival Anymore."

    151

    父死，我亦必死，尔守未嫁之夫，而不顾父母之死乎？今舍此无他法，而尔执不可，我先死尔前矣！”女饮泣数日，从之。既嫁，事果得解。而女以违其初愿，居恒郁郁，不数年，竟死。自来表扬节妇者，必题以节孝二字。盖女子奉父母之命，以身事人，不负其夫，即是不负其父母，故节即孝也。乃如此女，则节孝不能两全，是亦居人伦之变矣。然女子为未婚夫守义，历来议论，颇有异同。此女改归他族，于礼本无不可，使其执志不从，父死而母随之，此恨岂有极欤？勉从亲命以全孝，而又郁郁以死，则仍所以徇其节。君子于此女无讥焉。

    Explanation

    151

    When my father passed away, I was determined to follow him in death, asking you to remain loyal to your betrothed husband without considering the death of our parents. Now, if there is no other way but you insist on not complying, I will take my own life before you!" The woman wept for several days and eventually agreed. After she married, the matter was indeed resolved. However, because she went against her original wishes, she lived in constant sadness and passed away within a few years. Traditionally, virtuous women are praised with the titles of "chastity" and "filial piety." It is said that a woman, following her parents' command, dedicates herself to another, remaining faithful to her husband is equivalent to being filial to her parents, thus chastity is filial piety. Yet, in this case, the woman could not fulfill both her duty of chastity and filial piety, which is an exception to the usual human relationships. Nevertheless, a woman maintaining her loyalty to an未婚夫 (betrothed husband) has been a subject of varied opinions throughout history. This woman remarried another family, which is not against the rules, but if she had insisted on not complying and her mother also died, the sorrow would be immeasurable. She complied with her parents' wishes to fulfill her filial duty, and yet her subsequent depression and death were a way of maintaining her chastity. A gentleman would not criticize this woman.

    152

    津门流妓，多出杨柳青、独流诸处，其地淫风流行，过于溱洧矣。乃有刘氏妇者，亦杨柳青人，其姑佣于某氏。某氏子知其子妇之美，乃以重金其姑，使招子妇来供缝纫之役。其子妇初意不愿，姑强之乃往。始至无所事，针黹稀少，而饮馔丰腆，亦颇相安。数日后，某氏子于薄暮入其室，挑以言，妇不从；直前犯之，妇夺门而出，奔回其家。某氏子乃使其姑劝谕之，姑告其子，子亦以为可。母子二人朝夕聒焉，妇投水死。嗟呼！碧玉小家之女，又居采兰赠芍之乡，而坚白自持如此，人之贞淫固出于天赋欤？

    152

    Translation:
    In Tianjin, floating prostitutes are mostly from Yangliuqing and Duliu, where licentious customs are rampant, even surpassing those of the rivers Zhen and Wei. There was a woman surnamed Liu, also from Yangliuqing, whose mother-in-law worked for a certain family. The son of this family, knowing of his wife's beauty, offered a large sum of money to her mother-in-law to bring her to do sewing work. Initially, the woman did not wish to come, but her mother-in-law persuaded her, and she went. At first, there was little work, and the sewing tasks were sparse, but the food was abundant and rich, so she was somewhat at ease. After several days, the son of the family entered her room at dusk, tempting her with words. She refused, but he forcibly advanced and tried to assault her. She rushed out of the door and hurried back home. The son of the family then had his mother-in-law persuade her, and she informed her son, who also thought it was acceptable. The mother and son nagged her day and night, and in despair, the woman drowned herself. Alas! A woman of low status, living in a place known for the exchange of gifts, yet she remained steadfast and pure in such a licentious environment. Is a person's chastity or licentiousness truly determined by nature?

    153

    苏州阊门外高版桥，一富翁晚年得子，甫三岁，忽患惊风之疾，一厥不醒。翁痛甚，举家惶骇，四出求医。有道人踵门求见，自言能医。问所须，令于静室中为坛，取白米一斗，金银各十两，为镇坛之用。道人入室，诵咒良久，令抱儿出。道人以手抚摩数四，又以中指重按儿鼻，儿忽作一嚏。道人曰：“生矣，生矣！”翁大喜，留之饭，不肯，匆匆别去。视镇坛金银，封裹依然，而已易以瓦砾矣。正共惊疑，小儿倏复惊厥，竟于是日死。

    Explanation

    153

    In the western suburb of Suzhou, outside the Chuan Gate, there was a wealthy man who had a son in his old age. The child was only three years old when he suddenly fell ill with a severe condition known as "jingfeng" (a type of convulsion or epilepsy), and after one fainting spell, he did not regain consciousness. The old man was deeply grieved, and the entire family was in a state of panic, searching far and wide for a doctor. One day, a Taoist monk knocked on the door, claiming he could heal the child. When asked what he needed, the monk instructed that a shrine be set up in a quiet room, and that one peck of white rice, ten taels of gold, and ten taels of silver be used to anchor the altar. The monk entered the room, recited incantations for a long time, and then ordered the child to be brought out. The monk stroked the child's body several times and then firmly pressed his middle finger against the child's nose. Suddenly, the child sneezed. The monk exclaimed, "He is alive! He is alive!" The old man was greatly relieved and invited the monk to stay for a meal, but the monk refused and hurriedly departed. When they looked at the gold and silver used to anchor the altar, they found that the wrapping was still intact, but the precious metals had already been replaced with tiles and gravel. Just as they were astonished and suspicious, the child suddenly suffered another convulsion and died on that very day.

    154

    苏人某甲，清狂不慧。母死未逾月，即欲纳妾，遍求佳丽。其亲串某乙知之，出一西洋法所照女子像视之曰：“君视此美否？”甲曰：“美甚。”乙曰：“此某氏女，可图也。然须重聘耳。”甲即托乙平章，往返数四，乃报甲曰：“事成矣。”议定聘银五百两，先付二百为女治奁具。甲如数付之。数日无耗，使人问之，则乙已远出矣。留书别甲曰：“君甫遭大故，即纳小星，非特人言可畏，抑亦国法不容。此事宜徐之。天下多美妇人，俟君服阕，再为留意。

    Explanation

    154

    Mr. Su, a man from Suzhou, was eccentric and lacking in wisdom. Shortly after his mother's death, he immediately wanted to take a concubine and sought out beautiful women everywhere. His relative, Mr. Yi, learned of this and showed him a photograph of a woman taken by Western methods, saying, "Do you think she is beautiful?" Mr. Su replied, "Very beautiful." Mr. Yi said, "This is the daughter of a certain family; she can be arranged for you. However, a substantial dowry is required." Mr. Su then entrusted Mr. Yi to negotiate, and after several back-and-forth exchanges, Mr. Yi reported to Mr. Su, "The matter is settled." They agreed on a dowry of 500 taels of silver, with an initial payment of 200 taels to prepare the concubine's dowry. Mr. Su paid the amount as agreed. After several days with no news, he sent someone to inquire, and found that Mr. Yi had already left far away. Mr. Yi left a letter for Mr. Su, saying, "You have just suffered a great loss and are immediately taking a concubine; not only is there the fear of public opinion, but it is also forbidden by national law. This matter should be handled gradually. There are many beautiful women in the world; wait until you have completed the mourning period, and we will consider it again."

    155

    仆适有远行，前银暂借一用。”甲得书，惘惘累日。或慰之曰：“费此二百金，省却几许事，亦值得也。”又有人于学使者按试时，诡言是使者葭莩戚，可为买通关节。一生惑其说，乃与议定，用银二百两，先付四之一。及试毕无效，再访其人，杳无踪迹。忽得其一书云：“学使者乃朝廷大臣，岂可干以私？士子读书，当知义命，万无夤缘幸进之理。承惠银两，暂充杖头资。愿此后努力下帷，以求上进，勿图侥幸，致犯刑章。”斯言也，可为药石之言矣。以五十金买得之，亦不得谓之不值也。

    Explanation

    155

    I have just the one head and two arms. I am just about to go on a long journey, so I need to borrow some money in advance for use. Upon receiving this letter, Jia felt disoriented for several days. Someone comforted him, saying, "Spending this two hundred taels of silver saves so much trouble, it's worth it." Another person, during the provincial examiner's examination period, falsely claimed to be a distant relative of the examiner and could facilitate connections. A student was deceived by this claim and agreed to pay two hundred taels of silver, giving one-fourth upfront. After the exam, it proved ineffective, and when he tried to find the person again, they were nowhere to be found. Suddenly, he received a letter stating, "The provincial examiner is a high-ranking official of the court; how could one influence him with private matters? Students should understand their duty and fate; there is no way to advance through connections. Thank you for the silver, which I will temporarily use for casual expenses. I hope you will strive to study diligently to advance, do not seek shortcuts, and avoid breaking the law." These words can be considered as advice that is like medicine. Spending fifty taels of silver to get it, it cannot be called not worth it.

    156

    某甲，铜匠也，居苏州阊门外，孑然一身，而颇有积蓄。然性俭啬，衣履不完，所居破屋两间，中惟草荐败絮而已。一夕，独坐未寝，时风雨交作。忽闻门外有泣声，启视，一少年女子也，颇姣丽。问所自，此女操江北口音，自言：“父母俱亡，为族人诳诱至此，将鬻我于倡家。恐陷入火坑，故冒雨而逃，然我无归矣。”言已又泣，且出金簪一枝付甲，求为之计。甲既艳其色，又利其财，乃招之入室，曰：“室止一榻，奈何？”女然曰：“既至此，固惟君所为矣。”甲喜，拥之而卧。及天明，女起为执爨，若甚相安者。俄有江北人数辈排闼而入，见女曰：“在此矣！”乃并执甲曰：“汝诱藏良家女子，当缚送官。”女俯首不一言，甲亦无从置辩。众人遍搜室中，得金簪并金约指十六枚，洋钱数十，问女曰：“簪与此等皆汝所携来乎？”女点首，乃尽攫之，挟女出。复欲拉甲去，一人故相解劝，乃舍之，哄然而散。

    Explanation

    156

    Mr. Jia was a copper craftsman who lived alone outside the阊gate of Suzhou. He had some savings but was thrifty, and his clothes and shoes were incomplete. He lived in two dilapidated rooms, inside which there were only straw mats and old rags. One night, he sat alone without sleeping, as a storm with wind and rain raged outside. Suddenly, he heard a sobbing sound at the door. Upon opening it, he saw a young woman, quite beautiful. He asked where she came from, and the woman, speaking with a northern Jiangsu accent, said, "My parents are both dead, and I was lured here by relatives, who were going to sell me to a brothel. Fearing that I would fall into a fire pit, I ran away in the rain, but now I have no place to go." After speaking, she wept again and handed a gold hairpin to Mr. Jia, asking him to help her. Mr. Jia was attracted by her beauty and also lured by her wealth, so he invited her into the room and said, "The room only has one bed, what should we do?" The woman replied, "Since I am here, I will do whatever you say." Mr. Jia was pleased and lay down with her. When it was morning, the woman got up to cook, as if they were very comfortable. Soon, several people from northern Jiangsu forced their way in, saw the woman, and said, "Here she is!" They then also seized Mr. Jia and said, "You lured and hid a virtuous woman; you should be bound and sent to the authorities." The woman lowered her head and said nothing, and Mr. Jia was also unable to argue. The group searched the room thoroughly and found a gold hairpin, sixteen gold rings, and several dozen foreign coins. They asked the woman, "Did you bring these items with you?" She nodded, and they took everything, taking the woman out. They also wanted to take Mr. Jia away, but one person tried to mediate and persuade them. They then let him go and dispersed.

    157

    山东人朱翁，商于姑苏，性好施与，有以缓急告，辄假贷之，不责其偿。及病且死，其子问父：“外人所假贷者，亦有契券乎？”翁曰：“此可勿问也。吾枕函中有一小匣，吾死，汝取视之，一生吃著不尽矣。”俄而翁死，子发匣视之，中无他物，惟《朱子家训》一篇。此翁大有晏子凿楹之意，其所见高出士大夫上矣。

    Explanation

    157

    A man from Shandong named Zhu, who traded in Suzhou, was by nature fond of giving. If someone came to him in urgent need, he would lend them money without demanding repayment. When he was about to die, his son asked him, "Were the loans you gave out recorded with contracts or bonds?" Zhu replied, "You need not ask about that. In my pillow box, there is a small chest. After my death, you may open it and see; it will provide you with a lifetime of sustenance." Soon after, Zhu died, and his son opened the chest. Inside, there was nothing but a single copy of the Zhu Zi Jia Xun (Instructions for the Family of Zhu). This man had the same intention as Yanzi, who carved a message into the lintel of his house, and his wisdom surpassed that of ordinary scholars and officials.

    158

    有于薄暮见一中年妇人哭于路，问之，曰：“吾某处人，附航船至此，投一亲戚，而其家已迁至别处。我欲归家，则夜航开矣，进退无所，是以悲耳。”其人为恻然，乃招之至家，饭以脱粟，而止宿焉。天明，不辞而去，则失去衣服什物无算，乃悟是此妇所为：是妇女能为贼也。有一家，每日必有所失，虽所值无多，然日日如此。而室中实无外人往来，惟邻舍一幼子，日或数至，嬉戏良久乃去。年未十龄，固不疑也。一日，此子将去，逾阈而仆，急为掖之起，则怀中堕出零物数事，皆其家物也，乃悟是此子所为：是童子能为贼也。金陵有某氏子，应童子试有年，考凤池书院，居然高等。乃其素性佻达，母又溺爱，不加防范。与邻女有私，丁娘十索，苦无以应。适其同居朱姓一老寡妇，颇饶于赀，某氏子乃乘夜入其室，窃得洋钱数十，黄金三十两，珠十余颗。质明，朱告于官，官往勘验，曰：“此非外贼也。

    Explanation

    158

    At dusk, I saw an older woman crying on the road. I asked her, and she said, "I am from a certain place and boarded a ferryboat here to visit a relative, but their family has already moved to another location. I want to return home, but the night ferry has already departed. I am at a loss, not knowing what to do, and that is why I am sad." Moved with pity, I invited her to my home, fed her coarse millet, and let her stay overnight. At dawn, she left without saying goodbye, and I realized that she had lost countless clothes and belongings. It dawned on me that she was responsible for the theft: women can also be thieves. There was a household where something was lost every day, though the value was not much, yet it happened every day. There were no outsiders coming and going in the house, only a young neighbor boy who came several times a day, played for a long time before leaving. He was not yet ten years old, so it was not doubted. One day, when this boy was about to leave, he tripped over the threshold and fell. I quickly helped him up, and several items fell out of his pocket, all of which were from his home. I realized that this boy was responsible for the theft: children can also be thieves. In Jinling, there was a man who had taken the examination for young scholars for many years and studied at Fengchi Academy, unexpectedly ranking high. However, by nature, he was frivolous and unrestrained, and his mother spoiled him, not taking precautions. He had a secret affair with a neighbor girl, and the girl's mother demanded ten taels of silver, but he could not pay. Just then, a widowed old woman named Zhu who lived nearby and was quite wealthy, the man then sneaked into her room at night, stealing several dozen taels of silver, thirty taels of gold, and more than ten pearls. At dawn, the woman reported it to the authorities, and the officials went to investigate, concluding, "This was not done by an outsider."

    159

    ”大搜其家，于某氏子书室得金珠焉，洋钱则无矣，乃絷之去：是士子能为贼也。苏城道前街有一客寓，多楼屋，每一间以版隔之，分为前后二间，客各居其一。有前楼之客，扃户外去，及归，启钥而入，则后楼之客正在其室，去箧探囊发匮矣。盖版不甚高，累卓倚而登，即可逾而过焉。然后楼之客寓此已经月，平日冠五品之冠，乘肩舆而出，一仆应门，一仆从之，居然一官也：是官亦能为贼也。嗟乎！外户不闭之风，固难望于中古以下矣。金陵杨立庵，医生也。

    Explanation

    159

    A thorough search of his home revealed gold and jewels in the study of a certain family's son, but no foreign coins were found. The person was then bound and taken away: this scholar could be a thief. On the front street of Suzhou, there was a guesthouse with many buildings, each room divided by wooden boards, creating front and back sections, with guests occupying separate rooms. A guest from the front room left, locked the outer door, and upon returning, opened the lock and entered to find the guest from the back room in his room, having already gone through his boxes, bags, and chests. The wooden boards were not very high, so he stacked chairs and leaned on them to climb over, easily jumping over them. The guest who lived in the back room had been there for a month, usually wearing a fifth-rank official's cap, riding out in a sedan chair, with one servant answering the door and another following him—clearly an official: this official could also be a thief. Alas! The custom of leaving outer doors unlocked is indeed difficult to expect from the middle ages and beyond. Yang Lian'an of Jinling was a doctor.

    160

    一夜已二更矣，有人打门甚急，问之，曰：“求医者。”乃使佣媪启入之，则一盗也。涂面而持刀，径入杨室。杨见之，惶遽失措，顾曰：“尔何必涂面来。”其意谓我无所有，尔来此无谓耳。盗闻斯言，误谓杨识其面，不敢行劫而去。又滁州一张姓者，亦医生也。一夜，有盗入室，亦涂面而执刀。张固识其人，谓之曰：“尔何必涂面来。”盗知主人识己，惧其明日告官，竟杀张而去。同一涂面之盗，同一何必涂面之言，而一以获免，一以见杀，天下事其可以一律论哉？

    Explanation

    160

    It was already the second watch of the night when someone pounded on the door with great urgency. Upon asking, the person said, "I seek a doctor." The owner then sent a hired woman to open the door, only to find that it was a thief. The thief, masked and holding a knife, directly entered Yang's room. Upon seeing the thief, Yang felt panic and at a loss, and turned to say, "Why do you need to wear a mask to come here?" His words implied that he had nothing valuable to offer the thief, making the thief's visit pointless. The thief, hearing these words, mistakenly believed that Yang recognized his face, and thus did not dare to rob and left. Another doctor surnamed Zhang from Chuzhou also encountered a similar situation one night. A thief, also masked and holding a knife, entered his room. Zhang, who recognized the thief, said to him, "Why do you need to wear a mask to come here?" The thief, realizing that the owner recognized him, feared that he would report to the authorities the next day, and eventually killed Zhang and left. Both thieves were masked, and both were asked the same rhetorical question, yet one escaped unharmed while the other was killed. Can all things in the world be judged by the same standard?

    161

    徽之绩溪县有殷翁，与柳翁者相善也。柳翁且死，托其子于殷翁。及其子长，则流荡不肖，日以饮博为事。殷翁述乃父之遗言，苦口劝戒，至于流涕痛哭而罢，如是者非一次矣，而其子终不悛。殷翁知其不可教也，乃使其客日从之博，博大负则又教之卖田，而殷翁出己金托他人之名以贱值买之。其田之所入，则别置一处，不侵蚀其丝粟。凡柳子此后鬻田、鬻地、鬻金玉器皿、鬻字画玩好，殷翁悉以其田所入之钱，一一购买之。未数岁，良田美产，悉归殷翁。而除购买柳氏物外，又无他用，所积益饶，柳子固不知也。久之，并其所住之屋亦归于殷，柳遂无立锥地。其初寄宿亲串家，大受残杯冷炙之辱。又转徙至僧庐道观，亦不见容纳。遂行乞于市，夜则席地帷天，与群丐为伍。

    Explanation

    161

    In the county of Jixi, there was an old man named Yin, who was friends with Old Man Liu. Before Old Man Liu died, he entrusted his son to Old Man Yin. As the Liu son grew up, he became a vagrant and unworthy, making drinking and gambling his daily affairs. Old Man Yin recounted his father's last words, earnestly advising and warning him, weeping and crying bitterly, but the Liu son never repented. Old Man Yin knew that he could not teach him, so he made his guests follow him in gambling every day. When the Liu son lost a lot, Old Man Yin then taught him to sell his land, and he paid his own money, under someone else's name, to buy it at a low price. The income from the land was set aside in a separate place, and he did not touch even a grain of it. All of the Liu son's sales of land, property, gold and jade, and fine objects, Old Man Yin bought with the money from the land. Within a few years, all the good land and fine property belonged to Old Man Yin. Except for buying items from the Liu family, he had no other use for the money, and his savings became even more abundant, which the Liu son did not know. After a long time, even the house where he lived also belonged to Yin, and the Liu son had no place to stand. At first, he stayed in the homes of relatives, suffering great humiliation by receiving cold food and wine. He then moved to temples and monasteries, but was not accepted. He ended up begging in the market, and at night, he slept on the ground with the sky as his canopy, joining the ranks of beggars.

    162

    殷翁乃招之至家，熏沐之，饮食之，谓曰：“若亦忆我向者之言乎？”柳子痛哭自责。殷翁曰：“所失之物，不可复返矣。姑努力读书，或可望晚成耳。”柳子嗣是果发愤下帷，期年之后，得隶诸生籍。殷翁乃以所买田宅，一一归之，曰：“曩见足下迷而不悟，不可以口舌争，非山穷水尽，不知变计，故不得已出此背水之计，置之死地而生之。曩与子共饮博之某甲某乙，皆吾所遣；曩买子田宅之某甲某乙，即老夫托名也。今此计果成，郎君前程未可限量。老夫年迈，不久就木，死见尊翁，亦无愧九泉矣。”柳子感极，厥角稽首，不知所云。里党闻之，咸叹殷翁高义而又有远见，非寻恒所及也。余按元人杂剧中有一事与此相类，疑即古事而误传为今事，然言者凿凿，以为不诬，姑为记之。卑官小说家，固不必拘拘于事之真伪，但取其足以风世而已矣。

    Explanation

    162

    Mr. Yin then invited Li Zi to his home, bathed and fed him, and said, "Do you also remember what I said before?" Li Zi burst into tears and blamed himself. Mr. Yin replied, "The lost thing cannot be returned. Just try to study hard, and you may hope for success in the future." From then on, Li Zi indeed became determined and studied diligently. After a year, he was enrolled as a student. Mr. Yin then returned all the land and houses he had bought, saying, "Before, I saw you lost and not understanding. It cannot be resolved by words alone. Unless one reaches the end of their resources, they do not know to change their plan. I had no choice but to use this risky strategy, placing you in a desperate situation to survive. Before, I played drinking and gambling games with someone named 某甲某乙, and those were my agents; the people who bought your land and houses were my aliases. Now this plan has succeeded, your son's future is limitless. I am old and will soon die. When I die, I will have no shame in facing your father in the underworld." Li Zi was so moved that he knelt and bowed deeply, unable to speak. The villagers all praised Mr. Yin for his noble character and foresight, noting that not even ordinary people could achieve such things. I have read a similar story in a Yuan dynasty play, and I suspect it is an ancient story that was mistakenly passed down as a modern one. However, the storytellers are very convincing, and they believe it is not a lie, so I will write it down. As a minor official and a novelist, I do not need to be overly concerned with the truthfulness of the story, but I will only take what is sufficient to influence the world.

    163

    有客自宁波来，言其地有焚死女婴一事。于空地积薪，置女婴其上，举火而焚之。始则呱呱啼，继则动，久之皮骨俱焦，不复成人形矣。乃纟遂以石而投之江，观者数百人，咸为叹息。问其故，则此家已生二女，皆溺死之。至是复生女，故不毙之水，而毙之火，冀其魂魄知惧，不敢复来也。嗟乎！溺女已为敝俗，乃更以一炬了之。赤子何辜，惨罹王莽焚如之刑，为民牧者如何不为之厉禁也！

    　　金少伯枢部之祖母某太夫人，享高寿，几及百龄而终。其末年神识日衰，见其子妇孙妇，辄呼大姊二姊，以为是亲串也。每至家祭之日，子妇具馔，则以既食辞。初谓是老耄谬误耳，已而每祭皆然，怪之。因问所食何物，则皆祭品也。始悟其形神已离，家祭之日，已与祖先一同受享矣。又缘此知“吉蠲为馆”，实有来享之者。圣人知鬼神之情状，而制祭祀之礼，《诗》所谓“芬孝祀，神嗜饮食”者，初非虚语也。

    Explanation

    163

    A traveler from Ningbo reported an incident of a girl child being burned to death. In an open field, they piled up firewood and placed the infant on top, then lit the fire and burned her. At first, she cried out pitifully, then moved weakly, and after a long time, her skin and bones were completely charred, and she no longer resembled a human form. They then wrapped her remains in cloth, tied them with stones, and threw them into the river. Hundreds of spectators were moved to sigh. When asked why this was done, it was explained that the family had already drowned two daughters. Now, with the birth of another daughter, they chose not to drown her but to burn her, hoping her spirit would be afraid and not come back. Alas! Drowning girls has already become a shameful custom, yet they resort to burning her with a single flame. What crime has this innocent child committed, to suffer such a cruel fate akin to the punishments of Wang Mang? How could the people in charge not impose a strict ban on such practices?

    The respected lady, the grandmother of Minister Jin Shao, lived to an advanced age and passed away just short of her hundredth year. In her later years, her mental faculties declined, and she would call her daughter-in-law and granddaughter-in-law "big sister" and "second sister," thinking they were her relatives. On the day of family rituals, when the daughter-in-law prepared the food, she would say she had already eaten. At first, it was thought to be the ramblings of old age, but this happened every time the rituals were performed, which was strange. Upon asking what she had eaten, it turned out to be the sacrificial offerings. They realized that her physical and spiritual essence had already departed, and on the day of the family rituals, she was already sharing in the enjoyment of the ancestors. This led them to understand that "preparing the hall with purity" indeed means that the spirits do come to partake. The sages understood the nature of the spirits and established the rituals of sacrifice. As the Book of Songs says, "The fragrant offerings of filial piety are enjoyed by the spirits," which is not an empty statement.

    164

    秦娘者，维扬勾栏中人。其父固老诸生也，谈者失其姓。生而国色，幼失怙恃，依其舅以居。而其舅负官逋，不得已，议鬻其甥女，为媒者所诳，遂入青楼。女守贞不辱，假母好言劝之，不从，恫忄曷之，挞楚之，惟以死自誓。假母计穷，议转鬻之他所，而以其貌美，未忍也。或为假母谋曰：“凡为女子，孰无情欲？宜广觅少年美男子，勿责以缠头之费。苟有当女意者，任留一二宿，此后事易为计矣。”假母从之，凡所交好者，皆托其物色。于是裘马少年，日有至者。女见之辄哭泣，稍近之则怒詈。假母不能忍，日以鞭朴从事，女决意求一死。夜梦老翁曰：“吾，尔父也。汝慎无死，吾已为汝觅佳婿，明日当可谐秦晋之好矣。”吴下有蒋生者，以应京兆试，道出芜城，初无意寻芳也。蒋有友，平时亦尝受假母之托，以蒋貌美，导之往。蒋始不可，友固怂{臾心}之。及至，女向壁哭如故。

    Explanation

    164

    Qin Niang was a courtesan from the entertainment district in Yangzhou. Her father was an elderly scholar, and no one knows his surname. She was born with extraordinary beauty, but she lost her parents at a young age and lived with her uncle. Her uncle was in debt to the government, and out of necessity, he considered selling his niece. A matchmaker deceived him, and Qin Niang was taken into a brothel. Qin Niang remained chaste and refused to compromise her virtue. Her foster mother tried to persuade her with kind words, but she refused. The foster mother then resorted to intimidation and severe beating, but Qin Niang only swore to die rather than yield. The foster mother, at her wit's end, considered selling Qin Niang to another place, but she hesitated because of Qin Niang's beauty. Someone suggested to the foster mother, "All women have desires. It would be better to find a wealthy young man and not demand payment for her services. If he finds her pleasing, allow him to stay with her for a night or two, and then it will be easier to arrange a marriage." The foster mother agreed and entrusted her contacts to find a suitable match. Soon, wealthy young men began visiting the brothel daily. Qin Niang would cry whenever she saw them, and she would angrily curse them if they approached her. The foster mother could no longer bear it and began to punish her daily with beatings. Qin Niang resolved to die. One night, she dreamed of an elderly man who said, "I am your father. Do not die. I have already found a good husband for you, and tomorrow you will be married." There was a young man named Jiang from Wu who was traveling to take the imperial examination in the capital. He had no intention of visiting the brothel. Jiang had a friend who had previously been entrusted by the foster mother to find a match for Qin Niang. The friend, seeing Jiang's beauty, persuaded him to visit the brothel. Jiang initially refused, but his friend persisted. When Jiang arrived, he found Qin Niang crying into her pillow, as she had been doing before.

    165

    蒋调之曰：“闻卿名秦娘，小生则小字晋郎，秦晋自宜为姻好，何拒我之深也？”女闻言，忆梦中父语，秋波斜睇，见蒋风度不凡，不觉哭声顿止。假母喜曰：“大好，大好！今日仙女思凡矣。老身且去料理酒食。”女与蒋同坐房中，虽无一言，亦无愠意。须臾酒食至，假母招女同坐，女亦盈盈而至，然泪痕固涔涔也。蒋见旁无他人，乃问之曰：“观卿情状，必有隐怀。仆虽交浅，何碍言深。

    Explanation

    165

    Jiang Diao Zhi said, "I have heard of your name, Qin Niang. I am known by the nickname Jinlang. A match between Qin and Jin is naturally a good alliance. Why do you reject me so deeply?" Upon hearing this, the woman recalled her father's words in a dream. She cast a sidelong glance, noticing Jiang's extraordinary demeanor, and without realizing, her crying suddenly stopped. The woman's foster mother was delighted and said, "Very good, very good! Today, the immortal woman has thoughts of the mortal world. I will go prepare the wine and food." The woman and Jiang sat together in the room, though they exchanged no words, there was no sign of resentment. Soon, the wine and food arrived, and the foster mother invited the woman to sit with them. The woman gracefully joined them, though tears still streaked her face. Jiang, seeing that no one else was present, asked her, "Observing your emotional state, you must have hidden feelings. Though our acquaintance is shallow, what prevents us from speaking deeply?"

    166

    ”女细述己志，且告以梦，又哽咽而言曰：“郎君若能为百年之计，梦中父命，敢不敬从。若以为风尘中人，苟遣一时意兴，则虽死不从也。”蒋叹曰：“有志女子哉！小生固未娶，然贫无金屋，奈何？”女曰：“苟许相从，荆布无恨。但求先矢天日，然后再陪杯勺。”蒋许之，共誓于神。是夜遂同宴好。假母喜女意转，坚留小住，乃流连三日。女谓蒋曰：“郎君别后，假母必不容独居，宜早为计。君家有何人？所居何处？可详告妾。”蒋曰：“家中无人，惟一寡姊相依，所居则姑苏某巷也。

    Explanation

    166

    The woman spoke at length about her own goals and shared her dream with him, then spoke tearfully, saying, "If you can commit to a lifelong partnership, I will obey the command of my father in my dream. But if you consider me a woman of low social standing, merely a fleeting whim, then even death would not make me follow you." Jiang sighed and said, "What a determined woman you are! I have not yet married, but I am poor and cannot provide a luxurious home. What can I do?" The woman replied, "If you consent to be with me, I will not resent a simple life. I only ask that we first swear by the sky and the sun, and then share meals together." Jiang agreed, and they made a vow before the gods. That night, they shared a feast. The foster mother was delighted with the daughter's new resolve and decided to keep her for a few more days, so they spent three days together. The woman said to Jiang, "After you leave, the foster mother will not allow me to live alone. You should make plans early. Who lives with you in your household? Where do you reside? Please tell me everything." Jiang replied, "There is no one at home except for my widowed sister, who relies on me. We live in a certain alley in Suzhou."

    167

    ”女喜曰：“妾得计矣。君宜为一书与令姊，详述妾事，妾自有策脱此火坑。”蒋悉如其言。及蒋去三日，假母果别招一客至，女强笑承迎，醉之以酒。乃服客之衣帽蔑履，诈为客状，启户迳出，大骂曰：“何物婢子，如此倔强，令人愤气填膺！”假母疑女又有变，得罪于客，追出谢之，则扬长竟去矣。入房审视，客固醉卧未醒，而女兔脱，乃始追。女甫出门，而暴风骤起，灯烛皆灭。盖女之出也，默祷于父，有阴相之者也。追者皆悚然而返。女独行昏黑中，若有导之出者，遂附船至苏州，竟至蒋家，投书于姊。姊审书不谬，留之，而女已有身。及期，产一男。姊始犹狐疑，视所生男酷似其弟，乃大喜焉。蒋自别女入京，应京兆试，不售。或荐之就四川学政幕，甫至而学使者卒，蒋留蜀不得归。俄值川楚教匪之乱，益困顿。适大帅欲延一书记之友，蒋遂入其幕府，宾主甚相得。始惟司笔札之事，居久之，灰盘密谋，罔不参预，以军功保举训导。

    Explanation

    167

    The concubine was delighted and said, "I have a plan now. You should write a letter to your elder sister, detailing my situation, and I will have a way to escape this dangerous situation." Jiang followed her advice. Three days after Jiang left, the stepmother indeed invited another guest, and the concubine forced a smile to welcome him, getting him drunk with wine. She then put on the guest's clothes and shoes, pretending to be the guest, and opened the door, walking out directly. She shouted loudly, "What kind of maid are you, so stubborn, making me furious!" The stepmother suspected that the concubine had changed again and offended the guest, chasing her out to apologize, but the concubine walked away proudly and left. When they entered the room to inspect, the guest was indeed drunk and asleep, and the concubine had already escaped. They then began to chase her. Just as she was about to leave the door, a strong wind suddenly arose, and all the lamps and candles went out. Because the concubine had silently prayed to her father, there was someone secretly helping her. The pursuers were all shocked and returned. The concubine walked alone in the darkness, as if someone was guiding her, and she boarded a boat to Suzhou, finally arriving at Jiang's house. She sent a letter to her sister. After her sister examined the letter and found it true, she kept her, but the concubine was already pregnant. At the due time, she gave birth to a boy. Her sister was initially doubtful, but when she saw that the boy looked very much like her brother, she was greatly pleased. Jiang, after parting from the concubine and going to the capital, took the examination in the capital but failed. Someone recommended him to join the education office in Sichuan, but just as he arrived, the education commissioner died, and Jiang stayed in Sichuan and could not return. Soon, he encountered the uprising of religious bandits in Sichuan and Hubei, which made him even more difficult. Just then, a general wanted to invite a friend who was a secretary, and Jiang entered his office. The host and guest got along very well. At first, he only managed writing and correspondence, but after a long time, he participated in secret plans and discussions, and was recommended for the position of tutor with military merit.

    168

    是时道路梗塞，鱼雁罕遇，而蒋亦从事戎旃，置家事不问，遂与家人久绝音问。及川楚平，叙功以知县铨选，始乞假而归。自辞家北行至此，将二十年矣。遥望故山，颇有近乡情怯之意。乃至所居坊巷，则门庭如故，且红灯双挂，彩幕高张，鼓吹喧阗，溢于户外，不知其有何事。入门则坐上客满，多不相识。有少年，就问客从何来。蒋诧曰：“吾故蒋某，此吾家也。”少年大骇而入。无何，有中年妇人出，则其姊也，惊且喜曰：“吾弟归欤？”引少年就蒋曰：“此吾弟之子也。”盖其子年已弱冠，是日适为毕姻耳。坐客皆大惊叹，以为巧遇。姊曰：“正有一事为难。弟妇已将作阿婆，而犹垂发作女儿装束，使之改妆，不可。今吾弟幸而归来，事当如何？”一客曰：“何不趁此吉日，使父母、子妇同日完姻，亦佳话也。”满堂轰然曰：“然。”于是青庐之内，花烛高烧，翁姑拜前，儿妇拜后。观者皆啧啧，谓为未有之盛事，好事者为作《秦晋配传奇》。

    Explanation

    168

    At that time, the roads were blocked, and it was rare to encounter fish and geese, indicating the difficulty of communication. Jiang was also engaged in military service, neglecting family affairs, and thus had been out of touch with his family for a long time. After the pacification of Sichuan and Hubei, he was awarded for his service and selected for the position of magistrate, and he finally asked for leave and returned home. He had left home and traveled north to this place, nearly twenty years ago. As he looked far into the distance at his hometown, he felt a sense of apprehension as he approached. Upon reaching his neighborhood, he found that the house was as it was before, with red lanterns hanging and colorful curtains hanging high, drums and music filling the air, spilling out into the streets, but he did not know what was happening. Upon entering, he found the house full of guests, most of whom were strangers. A young man approached and asked where the guest came from. Jiang was surprised and said, "I am the former Mr. Jiang, and this is my home." The young man was greatly startled and entered. Soon, a middle-aged woman came out, and she was his sister, who was surprised and delighted and said, "My younger brother has returned?" She led the young man to Jiang and said, "This is my younger brother's son." It turned out that Jiang's son was already twenty years old, and on this day, he was just getting married. The guests were all greatly amazed, considering it a coincidence. His sister said, "There is one matter that is difficult. My sister-in-law is about to become a grandmother, but she is still wearing her hair down and dressed as a young woman. Making her change her appearance is not possible. Now that my younger brother has fortunately returned home, what should we do?" One guest suggested, "Why not take advantage of this auspicious day and let the parents and the son's wife complete their marriages on the same day? It would also be a good story." Everyone in the hall exclaimed, "Yes." Inside the blue tent, the candles were lit brightly, and the father-in-law and mother-in-law bowed first, followed by the son and his wife. The spectators all murmured, considering it an unprecedented grand event. Those who enjoy telling stories wrote a legend called "The Legend of the Qin and Jin Pairing."

    169

    苏州乡间有某甲者，饶于赀而无子，年四十而鳏，乃谋买妾。偶入阊门，遇一媪，似曾相识，邀至其家。略叙寒暄，便问：“君今丧偶，当续弦乎，抑买妾也？”甲曰：“亦思买妾耳。”媪力以蹇修自任。言次，有女子奉茶出，媪曰：“此弱息也，君视可抱衾否？”甲睨之，女颇娟秀，乃问聘金几许，媒妁伊谁，媪曰：“老身无夫无子，久思托足空门，正苦此女为累。若得所归，便大慰矣，何敢多求。止望洋钱一百，为老身瓶钵之资。君如许可，则觌面一言，人财两易，媒妁奚为？”甲大喜，囊中适有此数，即以付妪，携女迳出，同舟而归。甫出城，有数人飞棹而至，中有老翁，哭而呼女，女亦哭而呼父，两舷切近，一拥登舟，翁抱女去。众人匈匈，势将用武。甲无婚书，无以自明。问女，则女无一言。知为媪所绐，但呼咄咄，而众已一哄而散矣。苏谚所谓“放白鸽”者也。

    Explanation

    169

    In a rural area of Suzhou, there was a man named Jia who was wealthy but had no children. At the age of forty, he was widowed and decided to seek a concubine. One day, he chanced upon the city gate of Changmen and encountered an old woman whom he seemed to recognize. She invited him to her home, where they exchanged polite greetings. She then asked, "Since you have lost your wife, are you considering remarrying or buying a concubine?" Jia replied, "I am also thinking of buying a concubine." The old woman volunteered to act as a matchmaker. As they spoke, a young woman brought tea. The old woman said, "This is my young daughter. Do you think you could take her as a concubine?" Jia glanced at the girl and found her quite beautiful and graceful. He then asked about the dowry and who the matchmaker would be. The old woman explained, "I am childless and have been a widow for a long time, and I have been thinking of becoming a nun. However, I am troubled by this daughter. If she finds a suitable home, I will be greatly relieved. I do not dare to ask for much. I only hope for one hundred taels of silver, which will be used for my alms and begging bowl. If you agree, we can make a face-to-face agreement, exchange the person and money, and there is no need for a matchmaker." Jia was delighted and found that he had the exact amount in his bag. He immediately gave the money to the old woman, took the girl with him, and returned on the same boat. Just as they left the city, several people arrived in boats, among them an old man who cried out for his daughter. The girl also cried out for her father. The two boats were very close, and the old man rushed onto the boat, taking his daughter away. The crowd became noisy, and the situation seemed to turn violent. Jia had no marriage contract and could not prove himself. When he asked the girl, she remained silent. He realized he had been deceived by the old woman and cried out in frustration, but the crowd had already scattered in a hurry. This is what the Suzhou saying refers to as "releasing a white pigeon."

    170

    ●卷四松江邹生，娶妻乔氏，生一子名阿九，甫周岁而邹死。乔守志抚孤，家尚小康，颇足自存。而是时粤贼已据苏、杭，松江亦陷于贼，乔虑不免，思一死以自全。而顾此呱呱者，又非母不活，意未能决。其夜，忽梦夫谓之曰：“吾家三世单传，今止此一块肉，吾已请于先亡诸尊长矣，汝宁失节，毋弃孤儿。”乔寤而思之，夫言虽有理，然妇人以节为重，终不可失，意仍未决。其夜又梦夫偕二老人至，一翁一媪，曰：“吾乃汝舅姑也。汝意大佳，然为汝一身计，则以守节为重；为吾一家计，则以存孤为重。愿汝为吾一家计，勿徒为一身计。”妇寤，乃设祭拜其舅姑与夫曰：“吾闻命矣。”后母子皆为贼所得，从贼至苏州。

    Explanation

    170

    Chapter Four: A Woman from Songjiang

    A man from Songjiang, named Zou, married a woman named Qiao, and they had a son named Ajiu. When Ajiu was just one year old, Zou died. Qiao maintained her virtue and raised the orphan. Her household was modestly comfortable, sufficient for their needs. At this time, the Taiping rebels had already occupied Suzhou and Hangzhou, and Songjiang had also fallen to the rebels. Qiao feared she could not escape, and considered ending her life to preserve her virtue. However, thinking of her crying infant, she realized that the child could not survive without her, and her mind remained undecided. That night, she suddenly dreamed that her husband told her, "Our family has had only one child per generation for three generations. Now we have only this one child. I have already asked our deceased ancestors, and you should rather lose your chastity than abandon the orphan." Upon waking, Qiao thought about her husband's words, which made sense, but she believed that a woman's virtue was paramount and could not be compromised. Her mind was still undecided. That night, she dreamed again that her husband came with two elderly people, an old man and an old woman, who said, "We are your father-in-law and mother-in-law. Your intention is good, but for your own sake, it is more important to maintain your virtue; for the sake of our family, it is more important to preserve the orphan. We wish you to consider the family's welfare rather than just your own." Upon waking, Qiao set up an offering and paid respects to her in-laws and husband, saying, "I have understood your wishes." Later, both mother and child were captured by the rebels and taken to Suzhou.

    171

    乔有绝色，为贼所嬖，而乔抱阿九，无一日离。语贼曰：“若爱妾者，愿兼爱儿。此儿死，妾亦死矣。”贼恋其色，竟不夺阿九。久之，以乔为贞人，以阿九为公子。贞人者，贼妇中之有名号者也。方是时，贼踞苏、杭久，城外村聚，焚掠殆尽，鸡豚之类亦皆断种。贼中日用所需，无不以重价买之江北。于是江北诸贫民，率以小舟载杂货渡江，私售于贼。有张秃子者，夫妇二人操是业最久，贼尤信之，予以小旗，凡贼境内，无不可至。乔闻之，乃使人传贞人命，召张妻入内与语，使买江北诸物。往来既谂，乃密以情告之，谋与俱亡。乘贼魁赴湖州，伪言己生日，醉诸侍者以酒，而夜抱阿九，登张秃子舟以遁。舟有贼旗，无谁何者，安稳达江北。

    Explanation

    171

    Qiao was exceptionally beautiful and was favored by the bandit leader. While holding her child, Ajiu, she never left his side. She told the bandit, "If you love me, please also love my child. If this child dies, I will die too." The bandit was so infatuated with her beauty that he did not take away Ajiu. After a long time, Qiao was regarded as a woman of high moral integrity, and Ajiu was treated as a nobleman's son. A "贞人" is a woman among the bandits who holds a notable position. At that time, the bandits had occupied Suzhou and Hangzhou for a long time, burning and looting the villages outside the city walls almost completely, and even chickens and pigs had been exterminated. The bandits bought all their daily necessities at high prices from the north of the river. Therefore, the poor people in the north of the river often used small boats to carry various goods across the river and sell them secretly to the bandits. There was a man named Zhang Tuzi, whose wife and he had been in this business for the longest time, and the bandits trusted them the most. They were given a small flag, and they could go anywhere within the bandits' territory. Qiao heard about this and sent a message to the "贞人," summoning Zhang's wife to speak with her and ask her to buy various goods from the north of the river. After frequent meetings, she secretly told her the plan and plotted to escape together. They pretended to celebrate Qiao's birthday while the bandit leader was traveling to Huzhou, got the servants drunk with wine, and at night, they took Ajiu and boarded Zhang Tuzi's boat to flee. The boat had a bandit flag, so no one questioned them, and they safely reached the north of the river.

    172

    而张夫妇意乔居贼中久，必有所赍；侦之无有，颇失望，乃载之扬州，鬻乔于娼家，乔不知也。倡家率多人篡之去。乔仍抱阿九不释，语倡家曰：“汝家买我者，以我为钱树子耳。此儿死，我亦死，汝家人财两失矣。若听我抚养此儿，则我故失行之妇，岂当复论名节。”倡家然之。乔居倡家数年，阿九亦长成。乔自以缠头资为束修，俾阿九从塾师读。俄而贼平，乔自蓄钱偿倡家赎身，挈阿九归松江，从其兄弟以居。阿九长，为娶妇，乃复设祭拜舅姑与夫曰：“曩奉命存孤，幸不辱命。然妇人究以节为重，我一妇人，始为贼贞人，继为倡，尚何面目复生人世乎？”缢而死。此妇以不死存孤，而仍以一死明节，不失为完人。程子云：“饿死事小，失节事大。

    Explanation

    172

    While Zhang's wife believed that Qiao, having lived among the bandits for so long, must have brought something with her; they investigated and found nothing, quite disappointed, they then took him to Yangzhou and sold Qiao to a brothel, unaware of what was happening. The brothel owner, along with many others, took him away. Qiao still held Ajiu tightly and said to the brothel owner, "The one who bought me from your family treats me as a money tree. If this child dies, I will die too, and your family will lose both person and wealth. If you let me raise this child, then I, a woman who has lost her virtue, how can I still be concerned with reputation and morality?" The brothel owner agreed to it. Qiao lived in the brothel for several years, and Ajiu also grew up. Qiao used the money she earned as a prostitute to pay for her own education and made Ajiu study under a private teacher. Soon after the bandits were defeated, Qiao saved up money to repay the brothel and buy her freedom, and took Ajiu back to Songjiang, where she lived with her brothers. When Ajiu grew up, Qiao arranged for him to marry, and she then set up an altar to worship her in-laws and husband, saying, "I was once entrusted with the task of caring for an orphan, fortunately, I did not fail my mission. But for women, chastity is the most important thing. I, a woman, first was a chaste person among the bandits, then became a prostitute, how can I still face the world?" She hanged herself and died. This woman, by not dying but caring for the orphan, and still by dying to uphold her chastity, did not lose her integrity. Chengzi said, "Dying of hunger is a minor matter, losing one's chastity is a grave matter."

    173

    ”然饿死、失节，皆以一身言耳，若所失者一身之名节，而所存者祖父之血食，则又似祖父之血食重，而一身之名节轻矣。管仲有一匡天下之大功，则不必徇沟渎之小节，特非长乐老一流人所得藉口耳。咸丰三年，山东幅匪起，掠费县之仲村集。有一贼骑马走荒郊，遇少妇独行，遽下骑推妇于地，将淫之。不知此妇固倡也，殊不惭惧，乃反笑曰：“汝骑将逸，奈何？”贼思其言良是，而四顾无可系马处。妇又笑曰：“拙哉，贼也！何不即系于汝足？”贼亦笑曰：“诺。”乃引马缰系己足上，解衣就妇。妇猛起拾地上贼刀，力斫马尾。马惊，又负痛，狂奔十余里不止。贼为其牵曳而去，颅碎肋折，生死不可知矣。妇望之，鼓掌大笑，检贼衣，得巨金数锭，怀之归。

    Explanation

    173

    However, dying of hunger and losing one's moral integrity, both affect the individual alone. If one loses personal reputation and moral integrity, but preserves the family honor, it seems that family honor is more important than personal reputation. Guan Zhong had great achievements in stabilizing the state of Qi, so he did not need to follow minor moral lapses. This comparison is not applicable to officials who prioritize comfort and longevity.
