    In the third year of the Xianfeng era, bandits rose in Shandong and raided the village of Zhongcun in Feixian County. One bandit rode a horse in the wilderness and encountered a woman walking alone. He quickly dismounted and pushed the woman to the ground, intending to rape her. Unaware that this woman was a prostitute, she was not at all ashamed or fearful, instead laughing and saying, "Your horse will escape, what then?" The bandit thought her words were true, but looked around and found no place to tether the horse. The woman then laughed and said, "What a fool, you bandit! Why don't you tie it to your own foot?" The bandit also laughed and said, "Okay." He then pulled the horse's reins and tied them to his own foot, undressed, and approached the woman. The woman suddenly picked up the bandit's knife from the ground and struck the horse's tail with force. The horse was startled and in pain, racing wildly for more than ten miles without stopping. The bandit was dragged away, crushed skull and broken ribs, whether alive or dead is unknown. The woman watched, clapped and laughed loudly, searched the bandit's clothes, and found several large pieces of gold. She carried it back home.

    174

    徽有富翁徐某，将葬其亲，广招堪舆家，足茧千山，未得吉壤。偶过一樵舍，有地师曰：“此地众山环抱，风藏气聚，真吉兆也。”徐翁曰：“然则其家何贫窭乃尔？”地师曰：“宜阴宅，不宜阳宅耳。”徐使人谋诸樵叟，叟曰：“先人之敝庐，百金不易也。”徐门下客有周姓者，工心计，善口辩，复使往说之。叟卒不可。将辞出，见有垂髫女荷薪而入，面目颇娟好。周曰：“此叟之掌上珠乎？”叟曰：“然。老夫妇无子，止此一女。

    Explanation

    174

    A wealthy man from Huizhou named Xu, intending to bury his parents, extensively sought geomancers, traveling to countless mountains, yet failed to find an auspicious burial site. By chance, he passed by a woodcutter's hut, where a geomancer remarked, "This land is surrounded by mountains, with wind concealed and energy gathered—truly an auspicious sign." Xu asked, "Then why is his family so poor?" The geomancer replied, "It is suitable for a burial site, not a residential one." Xu sent someone to consult the woodcutter, who said, "My ancestors' old hut is invaluable, worth a hundred gold pieces." Xu's follower, a man surnamed Zhou, skilled in mental calculation and eloquent speech, was sent again to persuade him. The woodcutter finally could not be persuaded. As Zhou was about to leave, he saw a young girl with braided hair entering, carrying firewood, her face graceful and beautiful. Zhou remarked, "Is this the woodcutter's cherished daughter?" The woodcutter replied, "Indeed. We have no son, only this daughter."

    175

    ”问许嫁乎，曰：“未也。”周妇告徐曰：“得之矣。渠有女，与君家季子年相若也，盍聘为儿妇，此地必可得矣。”徐翁曰：“樵耳，奈何与为婚姻！”周曰：“君何迂之甚！得地之后，此女去留，固在君也。”乃使周为媒。叟初以非耦辞，往返数四，始许之。逾岁，即草草毕姻。徐翁谓樵叟曰：“吾家颇有旷宅，盍移家来此，使婿女得朝暮见，差不落寞。”叟从之，与媪俱来。始至，亦甚相得。徐乃复以地为请。叟曰：“既成至亲，何爱敝庐，竟以奉赠可也。

    Explanation

    175

    "Is she engaged to be married?" asked Xu. "No," replied the man. The woman from Zhou informed Xu, "I have found it. Chan has a daughter whose age is similar to your youngest son. Why don't you consider marrying her as your daughter-in-law? This place can definitely be obtained." Xu's father said, "He is just a woodcutter. How could he be married into our family?" Zhou replied, "How narrow-minded you are! After obtaining this land, whether this girl stays or leaves is entirely up to you." So she asked Zhou to act as a matchmaker. The woodcutter initially refused, saying they were not a suitable match, but after several back-and-forth discussions, he finally agreed. A year later, they hastily completed the wedding. Xu's father told the woodcutter, "We have quite a spacious house. Why don't you move your family here so that the bride and groom can see each other every day, and they won't feel lonely." The woodcutter agreed and moved with his wife. When they first arrived, they got along very well. Xu then asked the woodcutter again about the land. The woodcutter said, "Now that we are close relatives, why should we cherish our humble abode? You may accept it as a gift."

    176

    ”徐乃择吉日，招地师定穴开圹，掘数尺许，得一断碑曰：“居此绝，葬此吉。”乃悟地师宜阴宅不宜阳宅之言不谬也。既葬数年，家益富厚，长子入粟，仕秦中为县令，次子亦登贤书，居然绅矣。而徐之季子颇喜狎游，旋娶一倡归以为妾，樵女微有违言，辄与反目，樵女诉于舅姑，舅姑顾不直之，曰：“汝小家女，眼孔浅耳。而夫大家子，东眠西宿，自是常事，岂如田舍儿止一妇哉！”妾闻之，意益骄，日进谗言于夫，又诬以阴事。樵女无以自明，雉经死。媪痛女亦死，乃逐樵叟。

    Explanation

    176

    Mou then chose an auspicious day, hired a geomancer to determine the grave site and dig the tomb. After digging several feet deep, they found a broken stele that said, "This place is unsuitable for living, but it is auspicious for burial." Then he realized that the geomancer's statement that it is suitable for a tomb but not for a house was not wrong. After the burial for several years, his family became richer and more prosperous. His eldest son entered public service through the grain donation system and became a county magistrate in the Qin region. His second son also passed the imperial examinations and became a scholar-official. But Mou's youngest son greatly enjoyed idling and playing. Soon, he married a courtesan as a concubine. The woodcutter's daughter had slight disagreements, and they would immediately quarrel. The woodcutter's daughter complained to her in-laws, but they did not think she was right. They said, "You are a girl from a poor family, with shallow eyes. But my husband is a son of a wealthy family, sleeping east and west is a common thing, how can it be like a farmer's son who has only one wife!" The concubine heard this and became even more arrogant. She daily spread slander against her husband and falsely accused him of private matters. The woodcutter's daughter had no way to prove her innocence and hanged herself. The old woman, grieving for her daughter, also died. Then they drove away the woodcutter.

    177

    叟归，徘徊于徐氏新茔，痛哭其下，触墓石死。未几，天大雷雨，震徐氏之茔，发其穴，破其棺。徐翁不得已，易棺而更葬之，于其穴下又得断碑曰：“义则吉，不义则绝。”与前所得碑语意相承，盖一石也。改葬之日，季子暴卒。其明年，次子应礼部试入都，坠车死。长子官秦中，以侵冒军饷，伏法。徐翁暮年失所，亦以忧死。而门下客周姓者，偶过徐氏茔，见樵叟揖之人，则仍昔日樵舍也。悸而觉，恍然如梦，奔还其家，与家人言所见，未竟，忽曰：“樵叟又至矣！”欧血而死。

    Explanation

    177

    The elderly man returned, lingering at the newly constructed tomb of the Xu family, weeping bitterly at its foot, and died upon touching the tombstone. Not long after, a great thunderstorm struck the mounds of the Xu family, uncovering the burial site and breaking open the coffin. The Xu elder, having no choice, replaced the coffin and re-buried the remains, and in the process, found a broken stone tablet inscribed with the words: "Righteousness brings good fortune, unrighteousness leads to ruin." This inscription was in harmony with the previous one, indicating that they were part of the same stone. On the day of the reburial, the youngest son suddenly died. The following year, the second son, who was taking the imperial examination in the capital, died from a fall from a carriage. The eldest son, who was serving as an official in the Qin region, was executed for embezzling military funds. The Xu elder lost his home in his old age and died of grief. Meanwhile, a guest of the Xu family, surnamed Zhou, happened to pass by the Xu family's tomb and saw the woodcutter bowing to someone. It was still the same place where the woodcutter used to stay. Zhou woke up in a state of fear, feeling as if he were still in a dream. He rushed back home and told his family about what he had seen, but before he could finish, he suddenly said, "The woodcutter has come again!" He then coughed up blood and died.

    178

    有巫者名就地滚，能以术致亡者之魂。其为术也，先伏地喃喃诵咒，诵毕，就地一滚，则亡者之魂已附其身，与家人问答如生时，其术甚验，故得是名，而其真姓名转不甚著矣。一日，有士人托致其父之魂，良久，竟不至。巫者甚愧，往见其师而问焉。师曰：“其人之父必大恶人也。”巫曰：“此亦儒流，未闻其有大罪孽。”师曰：“然则其人必生天矣。”巫请其说。师曰：“汝但能行召亡之术，而未能知亡者之情状也。夫人之生也，为血肉之躯，其质重浊。故虽圣贤如孔、孟，有蟠天际地之学；神勇如贲、获，有裂兕曳牛之力；而离地一步，即不能行。及其死也，此块然之质埋藏地下，而其余气尚存，则轻清而上升矣。大凡其气益清，则其升益高。故孔、孟、颜、曾，千秋崇祀，而在人间绝无，盖其气已升至极高之地，去人甚远也。

    Explanation

    178

    A witch named Jiudi Gun, known for his ability to summon the spirits of the deceased with a spell, performed his ritual by first kneeling down and muttering incantations softly. After finishing the incantation, he rolled on the ground once, and the spirit of the deceased would then attach itself to his body, allowing him to converse with the family as if the deceased were still alive. His method was very effective, which is why he gained this name, though his real name is less well-known now. One day, a scholar asked him to summon his father's spirit. After a long time, the spirit did not come. The witch felt ashamed and went to see his master to ask for an explanation. The master said, "The scholar's father must have been a very wicked person." The witch replied, "He is also a Confucian scholar, and I have not heard of any major sins." The master then said, "Then the scholar must have been reborn in heaven." The witch asked for an explanation. The master replied, "You can only perform the ritual to summon the dead, but you do not understand the circumstances of the deceased. When a person is alive, they are a body of flesh and blood, and their substance is heavy and turbid. Even sages like Confucius and Mencius, who have knowledge that encompasses heaven and earth, and heroes like Bi Gan and Yu Hui, who have the strength to tear apart buffaloes and drag oxen, cannot move if they leave the ground by even a step. When they die, this solid mass is buried underground, but their residual energy still exists, and it is light and clear, rising upwards. Generally, the clearer the energy, the higher it rises. So Confucius, Mencius, Yan Hui, and Zengzi are revered for thousands of years, and in the world, there is no trace of them. Their energy has risen to a very high place, far from people."

    179

    苟有一分浊气未净，即不能上与太清为体，于是有赫然森列而为明神者焉。其品愈下，则浊气愈多，而去人亦益近。至于寻常之人，则生本凡庸，死亦冗，不过依其子孙以居。汝平时所一召而即至者，皆此等鬼也。若夫凶恶之人，清气久绝，纯乎浊气。生前有形有质，尚可混迹人间；死后形质既离，便非大地所载。其气愈沉愈下，堕入九幽，去入亦远。吾始疑其人之父为大恶人，恐其堕入九幽，故非吾术所能召也；既非此类，则必其人之气浊少而清多，已超然在声臭之外，故吾知其已生天也。”余谓此论极精，非他巫觋所能见及。佛家天堂地狱之说，不外乎此。余尝疑匹夫匹妇，死犹有鬼；而士大夫一经易箦，则反寂然，殊不可解。得此巫师之论，乃释然矣。若夫有恩报恩，有怨报怨，其心既有所系著，则无论气之清浊，而皆不能上升，故往往能见形为祸福。此巫师所未言，而可推论及之者也。

    Explanation

    179

    If there is even a trace of impure qi remaining, one cannot be in harmony with the Great Void. Thus, there are those who stand out clearly and in an orderly array as bright deities. The lower their rank, the more impure qi they have, and the closer they are to humans. As for ordinary people, they are born ordinary and die without significance, relying on their descendants to reside. Those whom you summon and immediately appear are such spirits. For extremely evil people, having lost their pure qi for a long time, they are purely impure qi. In life, they have a form and substance, which allows them to mingle with the human world; but after death, their form and substance separate, and they are no longer borne by the earth. Their qi becomes heavier and sinks deeper, falling into the Nine Lower Regions, and they are far from the human realm. I initially suspected that the father of this person was a great evil-doer, fearing that he had fallen into the Nine Lower Regions, which is beyond the reach of my art to summon. If not such a person, then it must be that this person has more pure qi than impure qi, having transcended the realm of the mundane, so I know that he has been born into heaven. This argument is extremely precise, something that other shamans cannot comprehend. The Buddhist concepts of heaven and hell do not go beyond this. I have often wondered why ordinary people still have spirits after death, while scholars and officials, upon their deathbed, become silent, which is hard to understand. With this shaman's argument, I now understand. If one repays kindness with kindness and resentment with resentment, and their hearts are attached to something, then regardless of the purity of their qi, they cannot ascend, and they often manifest to bring about good or evil. This is something the shaman did not mention, but it can be inferred.

    180

    胡某，苏州人，役于冥中。其赴役也，每不自知，或行于通衢，忽然倒地；甚或自楼梯坠下，头面破坏，甚以为苦。哀于冥中主者，愿预示期日。主者曰：“他人皆不尔，而汝如是，是有夙谴也。宜自谶悔乃可。”甲自矢从此永不杀生，嗣后遂预知期日，先就床榻安卧，昏沉若死，或一二日始寤。其入冥为何事，则不与人言，惟言冥中城郭市廛与人间无异。然阴晦无天日，途中之人肩相摩也，而从无交一言者。又言冥中境域有数等，其所治事之处，最下一层也；由最下一层望次上一层之人，犹外邑之人视省会中人也；望再上一层之人，犹京师中人也；若更上，则不可得见矣。最下一层者，可以暂至次上一层，而不能至再上一层；其再上一层之人，则可至最下一层，但至者少耳。所言若此，与巫师之言可相印证矣。

    Explanation

    180

    Hu, a native of Suzhou, serves in the underworld. Whenever he goes on duty, he is never aware of it; sometimes he walks down a busy street and suddenly falls to the ground; at other times, he may fall from a staircase, breaking his face, which causes him great suffering. He laments to the master of the underworld, asking to be forewarned of the dates when he will be called. The master says, "Others do not experience this, but you do, which indicates a predestined punishment. You should make a vow of repentance." Hu vows never to take another life from then on. Afterward, he begins to know in advance when he will be called, and he prepares by lying down on his bed, falling into a deep and death-like sleep, sometimes waking up after one or two days. As for what he does in the underworld, he does not speak of it to others, only saying that the cities and markets there are no different from those in the human world. However, the underworld is dark and without sunlight, and the people along the way rub shoulders with one another, yet they never speak a word to each other. He also says that the underworld has several levels, and the place where he serves is the lowest level; looking from the lowest level to the next higher level is like looking from a small town to the provincial capital; looking from the next higher level to the one above it is like looking from the provincial capital to the imperial city; if one goes even higher, it cannot be seen. Those on the lowest level can temporarily reach the next higher level but cannot reach the one above that; those on the higher levels can reach the lowest level, but few actually do. What he says can be corroborated with the words of the witch.

    181

    江西南昌乡间有童子。放鸭于河。一鸭遁入田塍，其田主乃某秀才也，适行田畔，遂捕以归。童子寻至索鸭，秀才怒麾之以肱，坠门前池水中。池阔水深，童竟溺死。其母哭而至，秀才大言：“尔子自堕水死，与我何涉！”母见其词色甚厉，不敢与辨，又痛子死，亦自沉于池。事闻于官，秀才竟拟抵。临刑之日，一妻三子均来哭送，而秀才默无一言，但流涕而已。环观者甚众，见其人可四十余，貌亦闲雅，无凶悍之色。以一鸭之故，竟罹大辟，岂前生冤孽欤？亦可为小不忍者戒也。

    Explanation

    181

    In the countryside of Nanchang, Jiangxi, there was a young boy. He released ducks into the river. One duck secretly entered a field ridge, and the field owner, a certain scholar, was walking along the edge of the field and captured it to take back. The boy came to claim the duck, but the scholar angrily waved his arm at him, causing the boy to fall into a pond in front of the door. The pond was wide and deep, and the boy drowned. His mother arrived crying, and the scholar loudly declared, "Your son fell into the water and died; what do I have to do with it?" The mother saw his stern expression and words, and being afraid to argue, and also grieving over her son's death, she also drowned herself in the pond. The matter was reported to the authorities, and the scholar was sentenced to death. On the day of his execution, his wife and three sons all came to cry and bid him farewell, but the scholar remained silent, weeping alone. There were many onlookers who saw that he was around forty years old, calm and elegant in appearance, with no sign of being fierce or violent. Because of one duck, he suffered the death penalty; was this karmic retribution from a past life? It can also serve as a warning to those who lack patience.

    182

    汉口一钱肆中，以钱一千置钱版上，转瞬间并版失之。时无他人，惟一妇在柜前小立而去。乃使数人分东西追寻，果遇之于隘巷，方倾钱入袖中，犹未尽也。即拉之归，尽取其钱，将纵之去。一少年曰：“男子作贼已可恶，况乃妇人，宜重惩之。”乃尽褫妇上身之衣，缚之于柱，加笞楚焉。少年之意，非恶其作贼，盖以妇年止二十余，饶有姿首，欲观其袒露耳。方缚妇时，少年故意以手触其乳。鞭笞既久，复曰：“此妇顽钝无耻，宜并褫下衣。”众人咸曰：“不可。

    Explanation

    182

    In a money shop in Hankou, a woman placed one thousand coins on a money board. In an instant, both the coins and the board disappeared. At that time, there were no other people around, only one woman stood briefly in front of the counter and then left. So several people were sent to search east and west. Indeed, they found her in a narrow alley. She was just pouring the coins into her sleeves, but not all of them were in yet. They immediately pulled her back and took all her money. They were about to let her go. A young man said, "A man committing theft is already despicable, let alone a woman. She should be severely punished." So they completely stripped her of her upper clothing, tied her to a pillar, and beat her with rods. The young man's intention was not to hate her for stealing; it was because she was only in her twenties and had a beautiful appearance. They wanted to see her exposed. As they tied her, the young man deliberately touched her breast. After beating her for a long time, he said, "This woman is stubborn and shameless; she should also be stripped of her lower clothing." Everyone present said, "No, we cannot do that."

    183

    ”少年乃手脱其舄，解其双行缠，掷置通衢，曰：“聊以示辱。”众人咸劝，乃始释之。妇披衣拾履，号哭而去。妇本良家，素无遗行，偶以贫失志，遘此大辱。既归家，又饱其夫老拳，既羞且愤，夜竟投缳焉。明日，少年在肆中，忽曰：“此妇又来窃钱矣！”众以为戏语，而数数言之，乃始骇间，少年忽张目作妇言曰：“吾岂来窃钱邪？来索命耳！吾诚不合窃汝钱，然何至辱吾如此？吾死，汝岂得生邪！”言已，以穿钱之草绳向喉下作自经状。众方惊顾，而少年已气绝矣。

    Explanation

    183

    The youth then removed his shoes by hand, unfastened his double bindings, and threw them into the main road, saying, "Just to show humiliation." Everyone tried to dissuade him, and he finally let go. The woman put on her clothes and picked up her shoes, cried out, and left. She was from a respectable family, always had a good reputation, but due to poverty, she lost her resolve and suffered this great humiliation. After returning home, she was beaten by her husband again, feeling both shame and anger, and at night, she ultimately hanged herself. The next day, the youth was in the market when he suddenly said, "This woman has come again to steal money!" Everyone thought it was a joke, but as he repeated it, they began to be alarmed. Suddenly, the youth opened his eyes and spoke in the woman's voice, "I didn't come here to steal your money! I came to seek retribution! I truly did not intend to steal your money, but why did you humiliate me so much? If I die, how can you live?" Having said this, he used the grass rope used for threading coins and made a gesture of hanging himself. Everyone was shocked and turned to look, and the youth had already lost consciousness.

    184

    高邮县某村，有农家姊妹二人，其姊嫁而死。婿请继室以其妹，父母皆喜，女不可，父母固命之，执不可。母私问之，女曰：“闺房之内，夫妇之私，天下之至耻，幸无他人得与闻耳。若姊妹俱事一夫，则吾姊之私，吾得而知之，是重吾姊之耻也。”父母不能强，乃已。按《明史。列女传》，虞凤娘，义乌人，其姊嫁徐明辉而卒。明辉闻凤娘贤，欲聘为继室。女知，泣谓父母曰：“兄弟未尝同妻，即姊妹可知。”父执不听，女自经死。余谓虞凤娘事不足为法，其所言亦于义未精，盖同夫与同妻迥别。妻不可同，非独兄弟为然，即非兄弟，亦无可同妻之理，夫则固有可同者。《春秋公羊传》曰：“诸侯娶一国，则二国往媵之，以侄娣从之。侄者何？兄之子也；娣者何？女弟也。”然则姊妹同夫。古礼固然。使谓姊妹不可同夫，则尧不当以二女妻舜矣。今此女所见，与虞凤娘正合。古今事固有相类者，然皆一偏之行，非中道也。

    Explanation

    184

    In a certain village in Gaoyou County, there lived two peasant sisters. The elder sister married and died. Her husband asked to take her younger sister as his second wife, and both parents were pleased with the idea, but the younger sister refused. The parents insisted, but she firmly refused. The mother privately asked her why, and the sister replied, "The private relationship between husband and wife is the greatest shame in the world, fortunately no one else knows about it. If both sisters were to serve the same husband, then my elder sister's private affairs would be known to me, that would be adding to my elder sister's shame." The parents could not force her, so they gave up.

    According to the "Biographies of Exemplary Women" in the History of Ming, there was a woman named Yu Fengniang from Yiwu. Her elder sister married Xu Minghui and died. Xu Minghui heard of Yu Fengniang's virtue and wanted to marry her as his second wife. Upon hearing this, Yu Fengniang wept and told her parents, "Brothers have never shared a wife, so it is the same for sisters." The father insisted, and Yu Fengniang hanged herself.

    I believe Yu Fengniang's actions are not worthy of emulation, and her words are also not fully precise in terms of propriety. It is fundamentally different to share a husband versus sharing a wife. A wife cannot be shared, not only for brothers, but even for non-brothers, there is no reason to share a wife. A husband, however, can be shared. The Gongyang Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals states, "When a feudal lord marries a princess from one state, two other states send brides to accompany her, with her niece and younger sister following. What is a niece? She is the son of one's elder brother; what is a younger sister? She is one's younger sister." Thus, sisters sharing a husband is an ancient custom. If we say that sisters cannot share a husband, then Yao should not have married two of his daughters to Shun. Today this girl's view aligns with Yu Fengniang's. There are indeed similar cases in ancient and modern times, but they are all one-sided actions, not the middle way.

    185

    奚呆子，鄂人也，以樵苏为业，贫未有妻。然性喜淫，每负薪入市，遇妇女问价，辄贱售之，不与论所直，故市人呼曰“奚呆子”。市有某翁者，生女及笄，有姿首。奚见而艳之，每日束薪，卖之其门。俄而翁女死，奚知其瘗处，乘夜发冢负尸，归与之媾焉。翼日键户出采薪，而遗火于室，烟出自笮。邻人排闼入，扑灭之，顾见床有卧者，笑曰：“喧呶如此，尚未寤欤！此伊谁也？”发其衾，则一裸妇，迫而视之，死人也。乃大惊。有识者曰：“此某翁女也。”翁闻奔赴，验之信，闻于官，论如律。异哉！天下竟有好色如此人者！乃叹宋孝武帝为殷淑仪作通替棺，欲见辄引替睹尸，尚非异事。

    Explanation

    185

    Xi Dāizi was a man from E who made his living by cutting and selling firewood. He was poor and had no wife. However, he had a strong inclination towards sexual desires. Every time he carried firewood into the market, when he met women asking for the price, he would always sell them at a low price without discussing the actual value. Therefore, the people in the market called him "Xi Dāizi" (foolish Xi).

    In the market, there was an old man who had a daughter who had reached marriageable age and was beautiful. Xi saw her and found her attractive. Every day, he would bundle the firewood and sell it at her house. Soon after, the old man's daughter died. Xi knew where she was buried and took advantage of the night to dig up the grave and carry the body away. He returned home and had intercourse with her.

    The next day, he locked the door and went out to cut firewood, leaving a fire in the room. Smoke came out from the chimney. Neighbors forced their way in through the door and extinguished the fire. They saw someone lying on the bed and laughed, saying, "So noisy, and still not awake! Who is this person?" They lifted the blanket and closely examined it. It was a dead person. They were greatly surprised.

    Someone recognized and said, "This is the daughter of a certain old man." The old man heard about it and rushed to the scene. Upon verification, it was confirmed, and it was reported to the authorities. Punished according to the law. How strange! There is someone so obsessed with sex. Thus, one can only sigh that Emperor Song Wu of the Southern Dynasties made a coffin with interchangeable lids for his favorite concubine, Yin Shuyi, so that when he wanted to see her, he could pull out the lid to look at her body. It was still not an unusual thing.

    186

    有夫妇二人者，广东人，寓于苏。妇每至岁终，积针黹所储，并向其夫索洋钱数枚，寄广东遗其母。一岁索之，夫不之应，妇忿泣数日，雉经而死。有一子，甫两岁，夫以乳哺乏人，鬻之刘氏。刘抱子去，越二日，妇之鬼忽附其子，而语刘曰：“速召我夫来。”刘大惊，召其夫至。鬼曰：“尔不恤我之母，并不自恤其子，何无良至此！我今来挈儿去，无使谓他人父也。”言已，其子竟绝。此妇可谓有灵矣。然怼其夫遂杀其子，自是一悍妇，宜其轻以自戕也。

    Explanation

    186

    There was a couple from Guangdong who lived in Suzhou. The wife, every year at the end of the year, would save her earnings from needlework and request a few pieces of foreign currency from her husband to send to her mother in Guangdong. One year, when she made the request, her husband refused to give her the money. The wife was furious and wept for several days before committing suicide by hanging. She had a son who was just two years old. The husband, lacking a wet nurse or caregiver, sold the child to the Liu family. The Liu family took the child away. Two days later, the wife's ghost suddenly possessed her son and spoke to Liu, saying, "Quickly summon my husband." Liu was greatly alarmed and summoned the husband. The ghost then said, "You do not care for my mother, and you do not care for your own son. How wicked you are! I have come to take the child away, so that he will not be called someone else's father." After saying this, the child died. This woman can truly be called a spirit. Yet, her resentment toward her husband led her to kill her son. She was indeed a fierce woman, and it is appropriate that she chose to harm herself.

    187

    光绪丁丑正月，余姚城中失火，延烧屋宇四百余间。有某姓之听事毁矣，而大门岿然独存，其门额乃前明王文成所书也。故老相传，此门三遇火灾，而皆无恙，岂大儒翰墨，固有神物护持欤？

    　　苏州齐门外黄姓嫠妇，汲水于河，其子甫五岁，随之行。妇失足坠水，儿大啼，亦匍匐入于水。适有舟过，拯妇起，而儿已随流去。至对岸，始救出之，则腹彭亨而气已绝矣。姑依法治之，覆儿大栲栳上，研皂角为末，吹入其鼻，未几儿哇然有声，吐水无数，竟得复生。孩提之童，从母入水，此赤子之真性也。绝而复苏，殆鬼神阴相之欤？

    Explanation

    187

    In the first month of Ding Chou in the Guangxu era (January 1897), a fire broke out in the city of Yuyao, spreading to burn over four hundred rooms. The hall of a certain family was destroyed, but the main gate remained standing alone and unscathed, with the plaque above the gate inscribed by Wang Yangming of the Ming dynasty. According to the old people, this gate had encountered fires three times before and remained unharmed each time. Could it be that the writings of great scholars are indeed protected by divine beings?

    A widow from the Huang family, living outside the Qi Gate of Suzhou, went to draw water from the river. Her five-year-old son followed her. The woman slipped and fell into the water, and the child cried loudly, also crawling into the water. Just then, a boat passed by, and they pulled the woman out, but the child had already been swept away by the current. They reached the other side of the river and managed to rescue the child, who was found with a swollen abdomen and no breath. Following the method, they placed the child on a large bamboo basket, ground soapberry into a fine powder, and blew it into the child’s nose. Soon, the child made a loud cry, vomited out a large amount of water, and finally revived. A young child, following his mother into the water, acted out of his true nature. To die and then revive, could it be that the ghosts and spirits were secretly helping him?

    188

    徐烈妇，名仲婉，平湖人，嫁同里赵宝善。夫兄宝源，亦娶于徐，于妇为从姊妹。甚相得也。其舅宦于蜀，姑从之。妇每以手书问起居，书法秀劲，舅姑得之甚喜。宝善能画花鸟，妇辄摹其粉本，绣之于缣，生动有致，见者咸叹美焉。生子女各一。宝善卒，妇曰：“姒，吾姊也。吾儿不患无母，惟逝者无侣，吾当相从地下耳。”家人共守之，不得死。日久，守稍懈，竟自经焉。发其箧，得数纸书，留别其父母舅姑者。又为韵语训其孤，并处分身后事，戒勿以佛事溷丧礼。夫烈妇殉夫，世多有之，若徐者贤而有才，尤难得也。

    Explanation

    188

    Xu Zhongwan, a virtuous woman from Pinghu, married Zhao Baoshan, a man from the same village. Zhao Baoshan's brother, Zhao Baoyuan, also married into the Xu family, making Xu Zhongwan and Zhao Baoyuan cousins. They were very close. Zhao Baoshan's father-in-law served as an official in Shu, and his mother-in-law followed him. Xu Zhongwan often wrote letters to inquire about Zhao Baoshan's well-being, and her elegant handwriting impressed her in-laws. Zhao Baoshan was skilled in painting flowers and birds, and Xu Zhongwan would copy his sketches and embroider them onto silk. The embroidery was vivid and well-executed, and those who saw it all praised it. She gave birth to one son and one daughter. When Zhao Baoshan died, Xu Zhongwan said, "My sister-in-law is my sister. My children will not lack a mother, but the deceased has no companion. I will follow him into the afterlife." The family guarded her, preventing her from dying. After a long time, the guard became lax, and she eventually hanged herself. Opening her box, they found several sheets of paper left for her parents and in-laws. She also left poetic instructions for her orphaned children and arranged matters after her death, warning against mixing Buddhist rituals with funeral rites. Women who follow their husbands into the afterlife are common in the world, but Xu Zhongwan, who was both virtuous and talented, is especially rare.

    189

    顾孝女，名嗣徽，字亚芬，金匮人。年十六，随父母避寇乡间。父或以诗文课诸子，孝女辄效为之。每出一语，隽永可喜，患难中以是博父母欢焉。及乱平还里，父母相继病，女臂肉，和药以进，卒无效。父母死，女欲殉之。或尼之曰：“母病，以弱弟托汝。今家庭之间，栎釜燃萁，情事孔亟。汝死，如汝弟何？”女乃不复言死。而兵燹后室庐尽毁，所居一室，黑暗如漆。女又厌人事相扰，杜门塞窦，足不逾阈，日坐其中，焚膏读书，服素而食淡。或劝以事佛，则曰：“非吾所愿也。”弟自塾归，必呼之问所读书。衣服饮食，调护惟谨。或弟有所需，而主者吝不与，则拔钗搜箧以给之。及为弟毕姻，乃叹曰：“今而后可以见父母于泉下矣。”仰药死。所著有《绿香吟草》，临终，投之火。此与徐烈妇皆贤而且才，故并录之。虽《虞初》小说，而未始不以表章孝义为主也。

    Explanation

    189

    Gu Xiaonü, named Siwei and courtesy name Yafen, was from Jinkui. At sixteen, she followed her parents to the countryside to escape bandits. Her father sometimes taught the children with poetry and prose, and Gu Xiaonü would imitate and write them. Every word she wrote was elegant and pleasing, and in times of hardship, she used this to win her parents' affection. When the chaos subsided and they returned home, the parents fell ill one after another. Gu Xiaonü cut off her arm's flesh and mixed it with medicine to give to them, but it was ultimately ineffective. When the parents died, Gu Xiaonü wanted to sacrifice herself. Someone stopped her and said, "Your mother is ill and has entrusted her weak younger brother to you. Now, in the family, there is strife and chaos, and the situation is very urgent. If you die, what will happen to your younger brother?" She then no longer spoke of dying. After the war, the houses and buildings were all destroyed, and the room she lived in was as dark as ink. Gu Xiaonü also disliked being disturbed by people, so she closed the door and blocked the windows, and her feet did not cross the threshold. She sat there every day, burning oil to read, wearing plain clothes, and eating simple food. Someone advised her to worship the Buddha, but she said, "It is not what I wish." When her younger brother returned from school, she would call him and ask about his studies. She took great care of his clothing and food. If the younger brother needed something, and the head of the household was stingy and did not give it to him, she would then pull out her hairpins and search her box to give to him. When she arranged the marriage for her younger brother, she then sighed and said, "Now, I can finally see my parents in the netherworld." She drank poison to die. She wrote a collection of poems called Green Fragrance Anthology, and at the end of her life, she threw it into the fire. This and the case of Xu's chaste wife are both virtuous and talented, so they are recorded together. Although it is a Yuchu novel, it has always been about promoting filial piety and righteousness.

    190

    汉口镇有刘氏女，许嫁张氏子，彼此往来嬉戏，从不避忌，亦其乡俗然也。有富家儿，见女而艳之，谋于父，将以千金买为妾，父漫应之。女以为实然，是夜，启户潜出如张氏告之，乃即夕成礼焉。质明，父失女，奔告于张，则女及婿双双而出拜矣。夫六礼不备，贞女不行，此女所为殊乖礼法。然既聘，则非奔也。宋儒云“权而得中，是乃礼也”，或可为此女解嘲欤？

    Explanation

    190

    In Hankou Town, there was a girl from the Liu family who was betrothed to a son of the Zhang family. They interacted and played together without any taboo, as this was also the custom of their village. There was a wealthy son who found the girl attractive and planned with his father to buy her as a concubine for a thousand gold pieces. The father casually agreed. The girl thought it was true, so that night, she secretly left her home to go to Zhang’s house to inform him, and they immediately performed the wedding ceremony that night. At dawn, the father discovered his daughter was missing and rushed to tell Zhang, and then the girl and her husband came out together to greet him. A virtuous woman would not proceed without the six rites, and this girl’s actions are particularly contrary to proper etiquette. However, since she was already engaged, it is not considered elopement. The Song Confucian scholars say, “to act with moderation and find the middle way is the essence of propriety,” perhaps this can be used to excuse the girl.

    191

    苏州三乡庙有一僧曰皆通，年六十矣。除夕，其生日也。先期一月，大会僧俗，盛设酒面，以庆六十之寿。而即于是日备豫后事，绞纟今衾冒，手自料量。客问其故，曰：“吾不腊矣。”众莫之信。乃至十二月二日，竟趺坐而逝。此僧平日饮酒食肉，了不异人，而来去分明如是，可异也。

    　　痴道士，卖药于苏州阊门外之渡僧桥，不知所自来，其言语不伦，人咸以为痴。每夜栖宿一草屋中。丁丑冬，大雪盈尺，而其草屋之上无雪。或问其故，道士曰：“明日饮吾酒，吾告妆。”次日，有好事者携酒而往，则道士不知所之矣。岂其异人溷迹市廛者欤？

    Explanation

    191

    In the three villages of Suzhou, there was a monk named Jietong, who was sixty years old. On New Year's Eve, which was also his birthday, he had a grand assembly of monks and laypeople a month in advance, lavishly setting up wine and food to celebrate his sixtieth birthday. On the same day, he prepared for his afterlife, personally measuring and preparing bedding and shrouds. When guests asked him why, he said, "I won't make it through the winter." Nobody believed him. However, on the second day of the twelfth month, he finally sat in meditation and passed away. This monk usually drank alcohol and ate meat, no different from ordinary people, but his departure was so clear and definite, which is indeed strange.

    There was also a foolish Taoist priest who sold medicine at the Du Seng Bridge outside the city gate of Suzhou. No one knew where he came from, and his speech was incoherent, so people all thought he was foolish. Every night, he lived in a thatched hut. In the winter of Ding Chou, heavy snowfall covered the ground up to a foot deep, but there was no snow on top of his hut. When someone asked him why, the priest said, "Tomorrow, drink my wine, and I will tell you." The next day, a curious person carried wine to visit, but the priest was nowhere to be found. Could he be an extraordinary person blending in among the common people?

    192

    山右何君，谈者失其名，以丁卯孝廉人赀为郎中，官农部。屡试春官，不得志。至丁丑春，忽梦一人向之诵诗曰：“十载莺花春夜梦，八年鱼水镜中欢。”寤而思之，似非佳语，居恒怏怏。俄而感疾，遂卒。计丁卯至丁丑十载，而其续娶之妻适止八年，梦中诗句，年数正符，亦足异矣。

    　　李玉桂者，妓也。故蜀产，不知其姓氏，流转至汉皋，姓假母之姓，故曰李，颇有声北里间。有李孝廉者，长沙人也，计偕北上，道出汉皋，为友人拉作狭邪游，遂与妓相遇。妓屡目之。友曰：“若爱李郎乎？是固将买妾而未得其人也。”妓私于李曰：“信乎？果信也，妾有私赀如干，当出以佐君，为脱籍费。”李感其意，诺之，而请俟之礼闱捷后。已而春风失意，旅食京华，遂失前约。妓偃蹇风尘中，未尝一日忘李也。有富商某，艳其色，强委千金于其假母，劫之去。妓不食七日，不死，仰药死。嗟乎！节烈如是，此女不妓矣。

    Explanation

    192

    A man from Shanxi, whose name is unknown, entered public service through financial means and was appointed as a mid-level official in the Ministry of Agriculture. He repeatedly failed to advance in his career despite his efforts. In the spring of the year Ding-Chou, he suddenly dreamed of someone reciting a poem to him: "Ten years of a dream in the spring night of flowers and birds, eight years of joy like fish and water in the mirror." Upon waking, he thought the words seemed ominous, and he felt discontented. Soon after, he fell ill and died. From Ding-Mao to Ding-Chou, it was ten years, and his second wife had been with him for exactly eight years. The numbers in the dream matched his life events, which was indeed remarkable.

    Li Yugu was a courtesan. Originally from Sichuan, she did not know her own surname, and after being taken in by a foster mother, she adopted her surname, hence the name Li. She was quite popular among the courtesans. There was a scholar from Changsha named Li, who was traveling north and passed through Han-Gao. His friend took him to visit a brothel, and he met Li Yugu there. She often looked at him. His friend said, "Do you like Li Lang? He is indeed planning to buy a concubine but has not yet found the right person." Li Yugu privately told Li, "Is it true? If it is true, I have some private funds, and I will give them to you to help you pay the fee to free me from my status." Li was moved by her words and promised to fulfill her request after passing the imperial examinations. However, he failed to pass the examinations and ended up living in poverty in the capital, thus breaking his promise. Li Yugu lived a difficult life in the brothel but never forgot Li. A wealthy merchant, captivated by her beauty, forced her foster mother to give him a thousand gold coins and took her away. Li Yugu refused to eat for seven days, but she did not die. She eventually committed suicide by drinking poison. Alas! Her chastity and loyalty were so great; she was no longer just a courtesan.

    193

    民间呼县尉曰四衙，盖以县令之下，有丞、簿、尉，故尉次第四也。通州德兴镇有毛某者，武断乡曲，俗有土四衙之号。畜一犬，甚爱之，名之曰阿生，饮食寝处，辄与共。每年六月六日，相传为狗生日，则具酒面为寿焉。一岁，为邻人王长林击毙。毛大怒，迫使具棺以葬之，斩衰而送之。且使手书讣状，榜诸通衢。其文曰：“不孝狗男王长林，罪孽深重，不自陨灭，祸延狗父阿生府君，于某年月日寿终。即日成服治丧，谨此讣告。”此真未有之奇文也。昔楚庄王所爱马死，使群臣丧之，以大夫礼葬之，因优孟之谏而止。阿生竟得成礼以葬，土四衙之豪横亦可想见矣。

    Explanation

    193

    People in the countryside call the county magistrate's assistant "Fourth Office," because under the county magistrate, there are the deputy, the clerk, and the assistant, so the assistant is fourth in rank. There was a man named Ma in the town of De Xing, Tongzhou, who was a bully in the countryside, and people called him "Local Fourth Office." He kept a dog, loved it very much, and named it Ah Sheng. He shared his food, drink, sleep, and living space with the dog. Every year on the sixth day of the sixth month, it is said to be the dog's birthday, and he would prepare wine and noodles to celebrate. One year, the dog was killed by a neighbor named Wang Changlin. Ma became very angry and forced the preparation of a coffin to bury the dog, dressed in mourning clothes to send it off. He also had the death notice handwritten and posted in public places. The notice reads: "Unfilial dog son Wang Changlin, burdened with heavy sins, did not die naturally, but brought disaster to his dog father Ah Sheng, who passed away on a certain date. Funeral arrangements will be made immediately. This notice is respectfully given." This is truly an unprecedented strange text. In the past, when King Zhuang of Chu's beloved horse died, he ordered the ministers to mourn it and bury it with the rites of a high-ranking official, but this was stopped by the advice of You Meng. Ah Sheng was ultimately buried with proper rites, and the arrogance of the local "Fourth Office" can be imagined.

    194

    河南有一县，谈者忘其县名，其俗喜为少子娶长妇，欲以操井臼、持门户也。有农家子甫十三四，而所娶妇年长以倍矣。新昏之次日，贺者毕集，而寝门未辟，日且旰矣。舅姑呼于门外，闻其子应声而不见其出，穴窗视之，则麇缚于床足。惊而问故，其子曰：“昨暮人定后，有男子自床下出，缚我于此，而拥新妇睡。”问何故不言，曰：“言则杀我。”语未竟，男女二人皆启帐出，男子抗声曰：“吾与尔新妇自幼有交，昨乘人乱入此室处，当容我尽欢而去。

    194

    In Henan province, there is a county whose name is forgotten by those who talk about it. The local custom is to marry a younger son to an older woman, with the intention of having her manage household chores and household affairs. There was a young farmer's son who was just thirteen or fourteen years old, and the wife he married was twice his age. On the day after their wedding, guests had gathered to congratulate them, but the bedroom door had not been opened, and the day was getting late. The husband's parents called from outside the door, and they heard the son respond but did not see him come out. They looked through a window hole and saw the son bound to the bed legs. They were surprised and asked why. The son said, "Last night after the evening meal, a man came out from under the bed, tied me here, and held the new wife while sleeping." When asked why he did not speak, he said, "If I speak, he will kill me." Before the words were finished, the man and woman both pulled back the curtains. The man loudly said, "I have known your new wife since childhood. Last night, taking advantage of the chaos, I came into this room. I should be allowed to enjoy myself and leave."

    195

    如敢破扉而入者丨”袖中出白刃指其子曰：“吾事刂刃尔子之腹矣！”举家惊异，罔知所措。而男子在室中索酒肉，索饭，索汤饼，曰：“不我与者，杀而子，与我而不丰美，亦杀而子。”其家不得已，一一与之。男子使自窗中置案上，而以长绳系其子，使就窗间取之。先命其子品尝，食然后食，以所余食其子。食毕，置虚命撤。于是观者云集，皆恐伤其子，计无所出。相持三日，闻之于官。官亦骇异，亲诣视之，信。官问新妇有父母乎？曰：“有。”乃逮之至，使呼其女，女不应。官命隶笞其父臀，批其母颊。父母呼吁，哀号甚惨，复使呼其女，仍不应。

    195

    If anyone dares to break in through the door, I will cut open your child's belly!" he said, drawing a white blade from his sleeve and pointing it at his son. The whole family was shocked and at a loss what to do. The man in the room demanded wine and meat, food, and soup dumplings, saying, "If you do not give me what I demand, I will kill your child. If you give me what I demand but it is not rich and delicious, I will also kill your child." The family had no choice but to give him everything one by one. He then tied his son with a long rope, making him fetch it from the window. He first ordered his son to taste it, eat after him, and then gave the leftovers to his son. After eating, he set down the empty dishes and ordered them to be removed. Then the onlookers gathered in crowds, all fearing that he would harm his son, with no plan in mind. They held him for three days, and reported it to the authorities. The official was also shocked, personally went to see it, and believed it. The official asked if the new wife had parents. She replied, "Yes." He then brought them in, made her call her daughter, and the daughter did not respond. The official ordered the clerk to whip the father's buttocks and slap the mother's cheeks. The parents cried out, with deep sorrow. He then made her call her daughter again, but the daughter still did not respond.

    196

    如是者三，母颊批至百，父臀笞至二百，流血漉漉。父母跪窗外哀其女，使开门，若罔闻知。官无如何，命人逻守之，絷其父母去。是时狱中有一贼，善穴人壁。官命之至其家，先伏人于门外，而使此贼伺男女皆睡熟，从屋后穴而进，潜以刀断其子之绳，曳之走。而门外伏者破扉突入，男女皆就缚，天明送之官。其事轰动一邑，观者自大门至大堂皆满，官坐堂皇鞫之。男子乃屠者，面麻有须髯，黑丑可憎。女则娟娟白皙，纤腰弓足，颇有风致。

    Explanation

    196

    Three times, the mother slapped her cheek a hundred times, and the father beat her buttocks two hundred times, causing blood to flow continuously. The parents knelt outside the window, pleading with their daughter to open the door, but she seemed indifferent. The officials had no choice but to order someone to guard her and bind her parents and take them away. At that time, there was a thief in the jail who was skilled at digging through walls. The officials sent him to the house, and he waited outside the door while the thief waited for both the man and woman to fall asleep. The thief then entered from behind the house, secretly used a knife to cut the rope binding the son, and dragged him away. Meanwhile, the person outside the door broke the door and rushed in, capturing both the man and woman. The incident caused a great stir in the entire district, and the crowd filled from the main gate to the hall. The official sat in the main hall and interrogated them. The man was a butcher, with a pockmarked face and a beard, appearing black and ugly. The woman was graceful and fair-skinned, with a slender waist and arched feet, and had a certain charm.

    197

    官呼女至前，戟手厉声大骂之曰：“吾见人多矣，从未见无耻如尔，不孝如尔者！犬彘之不若，非人类也。律载妇女犯奸，去衣受杖，况尔岂可以人理论乎！”命左右尽去其上下衣，不留寸缕。先批颊如其母数，再笞臀如其父数，然后科以奸罪，决大杖四十，命其父母领去，而归聘礼于夫家。父母扶裸女出县门，各脱衣衣其女，而观者无虑数千人，争前褫夺，竟不得衣而归。屠者笞二千，不死，次日又笞二千，未毕，死。女父母羞且愤，相继死。女杖创寻愈，无恙。后数岁有人于逆旅中见此女为人行酒，风致如故，而颊则有批痕矣。或指以问女，女缕述前情，不少讳。至公堂受辱情状，尤言之历历如绘，人多乐听之，藉博缠头以饣胡口焉。

    197

    The magistrate summoned the woman to the front, brandishing his hand and shouting at her in a loud and harsh voice: "I have seen many people, but I have never seen anyone as shameless and ungrateful as you! You are not even fit to be compared to dogs and pigs, you are not human! According to the law, women who commit adultery must have their clothes removed and be beaten with a stick. How can you possibly be treated as a human being?" He ordered the attendants to strip her completely, leaving no piece of clothing on her. First, he slapped her cheeks as many times as her mother had been slapped, then he whipped her buttocks as many times as her father had been whipped. Afterward, he convicted her of adultery and sentenced her to forty strokes with a heavy stick, ordering her parents to take her away and return the dowry to her husband's family. The parents led the naked woman out of the county gate, each removing their own clothes to cover her. The crowd, numbering in the thousands, rushed forward to try to take the clothes away, and in the end, she returned home without any clothes. The butcher was beaten two thousand times, but he did not die. The next day, he was beaten another two thousand times, and he died before the punishment was completed. The woman's parents, ashamed and furious, died one after the other. The woman's wounds from the beating healed over time, and she remained unharmed. Several years later, someone saw her serving drinks at an inn, still graceful as before, but with marks of the slaps on her cheeks. Someone pointed this out to her, and she recounted her previous ordeal in detail, without any concealment. Her description of the humiliation she suffered in the courthouse was vivid and detailed, and many people enjoyed listening to her story, using it as a form of entertainment to earn a living.

    198

    江宁徐某，为赘婿于汪氏，生一子矣。汪以其贫也，庸奴视之，徐不能堪而归。其妇翁商于皖，将移家至皖。徐闻之，往迎其妇，妇不可。徐知其将从母远去矣，谋于族人，议夺其子归，族人咸以为然。乃将七八人偕往，先遍拜其邻比，告以故，已而直入妇室，劫其子去。妇之母大呼有盗，而邻人无应者。责其坐视不救，邻曰：“徐氏之子，为徐氏父兄抱去，此正理耳，吾侪何与焉？”妇母又至徐氏索之，徐氏群起诟谇，不敌而返。斯人斯举，大有张飞、赵云勒兵截江夺回阿斗之意，想亦从看演义得来也。

    　　维扬王氏妇，孕六月矣。适有邻猫来攫其笼中之鸟，妇起逐之，仆于地，震动胎气，势甚危殆。一邻媪曰：“可速觅一二纸过期不赎之当票，烧灰和开水服之，则胎即安矣。”如其言，果无恙。夫过期当票，已成废纸，乃有安胎之妙用，其理不可解，亦见天下之无弃物也。

    Explanation

    198

    In Jiangning, there was a man named Xu who was a son-in-law to the Wang family and had a son. The Wang family, due to Xu's poverty, treated him like a slave. Unable to bear the mistreatment, Xu returned home. His father-in-law was engaged in business in Anhui and planned to move the family there. Upon hearing this, Xu went to try to persuade his wife to come back, but she refused. Realizing that his wife would leave with her mother, Xu discussed the matter with his family members and decided to take the child back. The family agreed. Xu then took seven or eight people with him, first visiting all the neighbors and explaining the situation, and then directly entering his wife's room to seize the child. Xu's mother-in-law shouted that there were thieves, but the neighbors did not respond. When questioned about their inaction, the neighbors said, "Xu's son is being taken by Xu's father and brothers; this is the right thing to do, what business is it of ours?" Xu's mother-in-law then went to Xu's house to retrieve the child, but Xu and his family members all shouted at her, and she returned without success. This act of Xu and his family had the same intention as Zhang Fei and Zhao Yun leading troops to intercept and reclaim Liu Bei's son, Liu Shan (A Dou), from Sun Quan, likely inspired by reading the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

    In Yangzhou, there was a woman from the Wang family who was six months pregnant. One day, a neighbor's cat tried to snatch a bird from her cage. The woman got up to chase the cat and fell to the ground, causing a disturbance in the fetus. The situation was very dangerous. A neighbor advised her to quickly find one or two expired and unredeemed pawn tickets, burn them into ash, and mix them with boiling water to drink. If she followed this advice, the fetus would be safe. Indeed, she followed the advice and had no further issues. An expired pawn ticket, once considered useless, had this unexpected use in ensuring the safety of the fetus, a mystery that also illustrates that nothing in the world is truly useless.

    199

    扬州之俗，婴儿初生，必服化毒丹以涤胎毒。有何氏者，生一子，命老妪至药肆买化毒丹。此妪乃金陵人，口音与扬州有异，药肆中人听之不审，误会为买活络丹者，即以付之。婴儿服后，忽大病，不食乳，数日竟死。偶检点得蜡丸，刻“活络丹”三字，穷究之，乃得其故，然无及矣。是故买药不可不慎也。有卖柴老媪，挈其子入城，至一富家。其子年十八九，未娶也。富家适有隔夜冷粥一盂，有蜈蚣死其中，议弃之于河，其子正苦馁，即曰：“此不足为患。”乃以指捉去蜈蚣，而啜之立尽。富家有女睨之而笑。其子以为悦己也，归而思之不置，遂成疾，日以羸瘦，几不起矣。媪知其情，偶以语富家之婢，遂达于女。女曰：“然则速召此子来。”其子喜甚，力疾奔赴。女一见大骂曰：“天下有此妄人邪！我见汝饥不择食，是故哂之，汝敢遽萌妄想乎！”命婢媪辈批其颊数十，其子叩头服罪，始叱之出。及归，病若失矣。

    Explanation

    199

    In Yangzhou, it is customary for newborn infants to be given 化毒丹 to detoxify the body and eliminate 胎毒. A woman named He gave birth to a son and sent an elderly woman to a pharmacy to buy 化毒丹. This old woman was from Nanjing, and her accent differed from that of Yangzhou. The pharmacy staff misheard her and mistakenly gave her 活络丹 instead. After the infant took the medicine, he suddenly fell severely ill, refusing to nurse, and died within a few days. Upon inspection, a wax pill was found, inscribed with the words "活络丹." After a thorough investigation, the cause of the tragedy was discovered, but it was too late to change the outcome. Thus, one must be extremely cautious when purchasing medicine. There was an elderly woman who sold firewood and brought her son into the city to a wealthy household. Her son was in his late teens and had not yet married. The wealthy family had a bowl of leftover cold porridge from the previous day, in which a dead centipede was found. They considered discarding it into the river, but the son, who was starving, said, "This is not a problem." He then picked up the centipede with his finger and ate the porridge immediately. The wealthy daughter saw him and laughed. The son thought she was attracted to him and could not stop thinking about her, eventually falling ill and becoming increasingly weak, nearly unable to recover. The old woman learned of his feelings and told the servant girl, who relayed the information to the daughter. The daughter said, "Then quickly summon this boy." The son was very happy and hurried to the house despite his illness. The daughter saw him and scolded, "Is there such a foolish person in the world? I saw you eating without discrimination, so I laughed at you. How dare you suddenly develop such foolish thoughts?" She ordered the servants to slap him across the face many times. The son bowed deeply in submission, acknowledging his guilt, and was then ordered to leave. Upon returning home, his illness seemed to vanish as if it had never existed.

    200

    陈广辅、陈广弼，安徽巢县人，兄弟也。一日，因喧传天半有龙见，因登城观之。先有一人在城上，天雨泥滑，仆于地，压广辅之足。辅举足欲使挽之而起，不意足一举而其人滚落城下，头面破损，奄然毙矣。于是城门之兵役咸集，执二人，将送官。辅曰：“是诚我之罪也，与弟何与？”弼曰：“否，此我为之，非兄也，宜执我。”兄弟两争不已。辅曰：“我一时卤莽，致有此事，安可诬弟？”弼曰：“兄甫有室，兄死，嫂必嫁；我又无力娶妇，则宗祀绝矣。不如我死之愈也。”于是众皆叹曰：“义士，义士。彼人虽绝，或尚有救，姑试救之。

    Explanation

    200

    Tien Guangfu and Tien Guangbi were brothers from Chaohu County in Anhui Province. One day, due to the widespread rumor that a dragon was seen in the sky, they climbed the city wall to observe. First, there was a person on the city wall. It was raining, making the ground slippery, and the person fell to the ground, pressing on Tien Guangfu's foot. Tien Guangfu lifted his foot to try to pull the person up, but unexpectedly, the person rolled down from the city wall, sustaining head and face injuries and dying suddenly. As a result, the guards at the city gate gathered, seized the two brothers, and prepared to hand them over to the authorities. Tien Guangfu said, "This is truly my fault, and what does it have to do with my younger brother?" Tien Guangbi said, "No, this was my doing, not your fault, and you should take me instead." The two brothers continued to argue. Tien Guangfu said, "I acted rashly at the time, leading to this incident. How could I blame my younger brother?" Tien Guangbi said, "My elder brother has just married, and if he dies, his wife will remarry; I have no means to marry, and then our ancestral worship would be cut off. It would be better for me to die instead." The guards all sighed, saying, "A righteous man, a righteous man. Even if that person is dead, there might still be a way to save them. Let us try to save them."

    201

    ”移时果苏，而其母亦奔至，见其子已苏，众又告以辅、弼之言，母亦泫然曰：“吾子幸而得生，即不幸，吾不责汝偿也。”辅、弼咸厥角谢之。道路嗟叹，以为三义。高邮一农家，衣食粗足。生一女，极美，父母爱之，择婿殊苛。其后门临大河，有宦家子泊舟河干，见女而艳之，停桡不去。一日，见有尼自其门出，遂尾之至庵，告以故，许以重赂。尼诺之。越数日，醉女以酒，而送之至舟，遂扬帆而去。女醒大啼，宦家子曲意抚慰之，矢以白首。比至家，则有翁在，女哭诉于翁。翁曰：“是吾儿之过也。然既至此，毋戚戚，吾为汝玉成其事。”乃倩媒妁，具彩币至其父母家，聘为子妇焉。农家失女，正愁苦无策，至是喜出望外，遂结朱陈之好。好事者为谱《意外缘传奇》。

    Explanation

    201

    After some time, the man indeed revived, and his mother also rushed to him. Seeing that her son had come to life, the crowd informed her of the words of the doctors, and the mother wept, saying, "I am fortunate that my son has survived. Even if he had not, I would not blame you for the debt." The doctors bowed and thanked her. People on the road sighed and praised it as three acts of righteousness.

    In Gao You, there was a farming family whose basic needs were met. They had a daughter who was extremely beautiful, and her parents loved her dearly, setting very high standards for her future husband. Later, their home was near a large river, and a son of an official anchored his boat by the riverbank. He saw the girl and was captivated by her beauty, so he stayed and did not leave. One day, he noticed a nun leaving their house and followed her to a temple, where he explained his intentions and offered a large bribe. The nun agreed. A few days later, he drugged the girl with wine and sent her to his boat, then sailed away. When the girl woke up, she cried loudly, and the official's son comforted her gently and swore to live with her until old age. By the time they arrived home, there was an old man waiting for them. The girl cried to her father, who said, "It is my son's fault. But since it has happened, do not be sad; I will make this happen successfully." He then hired matchmakers and prepared gifts, going to the girl's parents' home to formally betroth her to his son. The farming family, having lost their daughter, was in deep distress and had no solution, but they were overjoyed when this happened, and thus they formed a marital alliance. Those who heard about it composed a legend titled The Legend of Unexpected Fate.

    202

    王士淮，皖人，而家于扬，其妻则扬之仙女庙镇人。生一子一女，而妻卒。子年甫及冠，聘某氏女为妇矣，以学贾于质库，故恒不在家，惟女依父以居。一日，有从母至其家，谓女曰：“吾将归宁于仙女镇，汝曷从我至外家乎？”士淮适他出，从母乃告之邻妪，而携女去。士淮归，不见其女，邻妪亦他出，无从问讯。而大江以南，自经兵燹，妇女绝少，欲娶妇者辄谋之江北，奸人逐利，往往有略卖妇女之事。士淮疑其女为人所略，即渡江而南，踪迹之。至丹阳县境一小村聚，闻村人偶语曰：“可惜此好女子嫁此白头翁也。”王惊询其故，村人告以村中李翁年将六十，今日新买得一妾。

    Explanation

    202

    Wang Shih Ch'uan, a native of Anhui, settled in Yangzhou. His wife was from Xian Nu Town in Yangzhou. They had one son and one daughter, but his wife passed away. The son was just about to come of age and had betrothed a daughter of a certain family as his wife. He was learning trade in a pawnshop, so he was often away from home, leaving only the daughter to live with her father. One day, his stepmother came to the house and said to the daughter, "I am going to visit my hometown in Xian Nu Town. Why don't you come with me to your mother's family?" Wang Shih Ch'uan was away on other business, so the stepmother told the neighbor's old woman and took the daughter away. When Wang Shih Ch'uan returned home, he found that his daughter was missing. The neighbor's old woman was also out, so he had no one to ask. In the area south of the Yangtze River, after the chaos of war, there were very few women. Those who wanted to marry had to look north of the river. Unscrupulous people, pursuing profit, often kidnapped and sold women. Wang Shih Ch'uan suspected that his daughter had been kidnapped, so he crossed the river to the south and traced her. He reached a small village near DanYang County and heard the villagers talking, "It's a pity that such a good woman is married to this old man." Wang was shocked and asked the villagers why. They told him that there was an old man named Li in the village, nearly sixty years old, who had recently bought a concubine.

    203

    王即访至其家，伪为贺喜者。闻内室有啼泣声，王遽出奔城中，诉于官。官命役偕往，闯入其室，大呼索女。女出，则非己女也。王骇愕，方自咎卤莽，女哭曰：“吾扬州某氏女，许嫁王氏。王士淮，吾翁也。”王惊且喜曰：“汝吾儿妇邪？吾即王士淮也。”拉李赴公廷。李愿还女，且以洋泉二百为谢，乃罢讼。寄女于尼庵，而遣急足告知女父。女家失女，正共惊扰，闻信奔至，得女，大喜，谓王曰：“吾昨至君家，闻之邻妪，君女固无恙，乃从姨至外家耳。然使君早闻妪言，则必不渡江寻女，不渡江寻女，则吾女不可得矣。此吾两家大幸也。”王亦喜曰：“此诚天。吾叨天之，而因以罔人之财，可乎？”归洋泉于李翁偕女父挈女归。

    Explanation

    203

    The man then visited her home, pretending to be a celebrator. Upon hearing crying inside the room, he rushed out to the city and appealed to the official. The official sent men with him, who broke into the room and shouted loudly to find the girl. The girl emerged, but she was not his own daughter. The man was shocked and blamed himself for his carelessness. The girl wept and said, "I am a daughter of a family from Yangzhou, betrothed to the Wang family. Wang Shihuai is my father-in-law." The man was both surprised and joyful, saying, "Are you my daughter-in-law? I am Wang Shihuai." He took Mr. Li to the public court. Mr. Li agreed to return the girl and offered two hundred taels of silver as compensation, thus ending the lawsuit. The girl was left at a nunnery, and a messenger was sent to inform her father. The girl's family, having lost her, was in a state of panic. Upon hearing the news, they rushed to the nunnery, found the girl, and were overjoyed. They told the man, "Yesterday we came to your house and heard from a neighbor that your daughter was safe, having gone to her aunt's house. Had you heard the neighbor earlier, you would not have crossed the river to search for her, and if you had not crossed the river, we would not have found her. This is a great fortune for both our families." The man also said joyfully, "This is truly a blessing from heaven. Having benefited from heaven's blessing, should I defraud someone's wealth?" He returned the silver to Mr. Li and, accompanied by the girl's father, took the girl back home.

    204

    某甲以盐官需次维扬。家藏一大珠，重二钱有奇，置之暗中，有光荧荧，古所称夜光者也。一日，有同官数人踵门求观，咸共赞叹，先后辞去，惟一庄姓者未去。某送客复入，则庄姓者犹踞隐囊而吃鸦片，盘中之珠已失所在。大惊问庄，庄亦大惊曰：“室无他人，岂我窃邪？”乃自解其衣，不留寸缕，使大索之，且曰：“如疑我藏珠衣中，请留此衣，君假我衣以归可也。”某失珠大懊恼，然初不疑庄，乃越月而庄之仆以其珠来归。盖庄幼无赖，习吞刀、吐火诸幻术。是日乘某出送客，吞珠腹中，归则哇而出之也。后以虐遇其仆，事遂败。古人剖腹藏珠，以为大愚，此则匪夷所思矣。

    Explanation

    204

    Mr. A, a salt official, was waiting for a position in Yangzhou. He had a large pearl, weighing slightly more than two qian, which emitted a faint glow when placed in the dark. It was the anciently described night pearl. One day, several colleagues came to his door to see it, all praising it highly before leaving, except for one man surnamed Zhuang, who stayed behind. After Mr. A sent the guests away and returned, he found that Zhuang was still sitting on a cushion, smoking opium, and the pearl was missing from the tray. Mr. A was greatly surprised and asked Zhuang, who also looked shocked and said, "There were no other people in the room, could it have been me who took it?" Zhuang then undressed, leaving not a single thread of clothing, and asked Mr. A to search him thoroughly. He added, "If you suspect I hid the pearl in my clothes, please keep this garment, and you can lend me your clothes to go back." Mr. A was very upset at losing the pearl but did not initially suspect Zhuang. A month later, Zhuang's servant returned the pearl. It turned out that Zhuang, having been a rascal in his youth, had learned various magic tricks like swallowing swords and spitting fire. On that day, he took advantage of Mr. A leaving to send the guests away and swallowed the pearl. Upon returning, he vomited it up. Later, due to mistreating his servant, the truth came out. The ancients said that hiding a pearl in one's belly was extremely foolish, but this case was truly beyond comprehension.

    205

    苏州葑门内有王七者，与富仁坊巷某姓有连，自其父在时，即呼某姓妇为乾阿奶。父卒后，某姓抚育之，视犹子也。妇有一女，与年相若，初意即以为婿。及王七年长，则一流荡子也，妇乃悔前议，许嫁其女于胥门外某生，娶有日矣。王七闻之，纠合无赖少年十余辈，劫其女归。女至王七家，闭门号泣，久之无声。或自门间窥之，则雉经矣。破门入，救之复苏，女遂绝食求死。事闻于官，官以王七劫婚，非礼也。笞之百，且谕之曰：“汝谓某姓先曾有婚姻之议，然空言无实据。女既誓死不汝从，汝又何爱焉？男子岂患无妇哉！”乃判某姓妇以洋泉五十畀王七，使为异日婚娶之资，而全曩时抚育之义，女则归之某生云。

    Explanation

    205

    In the inner part of the Fuzhong Gate in Suzhou, there was a man named Wáng Qī, who had a familial or social connection with a certain family in the Furen alley. His father had called the woman of that family "Dry Aunt" (a term of respect). After his father's death, the family took care of Wáng Qī, treating him like a son. The woman had a daughter who was about the same age as Wáng Qī, and initially, it was intended that she would marry him. However, when Wáng Qī grew up, he became a reckless young man. The woman then regretted her previous arrangement and agreed to marry her daughter to a man named Mǒu Shēng outside the Xuhu Gate. The wedding was set to take place soon. Wáng Qī heard about this and gathered a group of about ten young men who were known for their misbehavior. They forcibly took the woman and brought her back to Wáng Qī's house.

    When the woman arrived at Wáng Qī's house, she locked herself in a room and cried loudly. After a while, there was no more sound. Someone peeped through the door and saw that she had hanged herself. They broke down the door, rescued her, and she came back to life. She then refused to eat, seeking death. The incident was reported to the authorities, who ruled that Wáng Qī's act of forcibly taking the woman for marriage was improper. He was whipped one hundred times and the officials told him, "You thought that the family had previously agreed to marry you, but there is no solid evidence for this. Since the woman has vowed to die rather than marry you, what do you care? Men are not lacking in wives!" The officials then ordered the woman's family to give Wáng Qī fifty units of currency (洋泉) as support for his future marriage, acknowledging the past kindness and nurturing provided by the family. The woman was then returned to her original fiancé, Mǒu Shēng.

    206

    何阿双，金陵人，以负贩为业。畜一犬，竟体白毛如雪，光洁可爱。出入寝食，必与之俱，室无他人，以犬为侣。一夕，携犬归卧，至明日，门不启，人犬无声。邻人疑焉。又越一夕，门闭如故，呼之不应。排闼入，则流血满床，阿双已为犬啮断喉管而死，犬犹据其腹，大嚼不止。乃醵钱殡之，即杀犬祭焉。圣人云“鸟兽不可与同群”，信夫！

    　　张阿福，绍兴人，寓于杭。自幼聘王氏女为妻，年三十矣，贫不能娶，女亦年二十有七。其母屡托媒媪趣阿福成婚，媪曰：“彼贫奈何？”母曰：“彼无婚费，我亦无嫁资，无已，其抢亲乎？”媪以告阿福。阿福大喜，乃期于某月日纠众劫女去。母故招集邻比至张氏夺女，则合卺已毕，贺客盈门矣。媒媪劝曰：“事已至此，夫复何言？明日当令其来谢罪也。”母若为悻悻者而归。夫抢亲，恶俗也，律有明禁，乃此则由女氏招之使抢，亦事之创见者矣。

    Explanation

    206

    He Aishuang, a man from Jinling, was engaged in the business of carrying goods for sale. He kept a dog whose entire body was covered in white fur like snow, bright and clean, lovable. Whenever he went out or came in, ate or slept, he was always with it. There was no one else in the room, so he took the dog as his companion. One night, he brought the dog back to sleep. By the next day, the door remained closed, and there was no sound from either the man or the dog. The neighbors became suspicious. Another night passed, the door remained closed as before, and no one responded when they called. They then forced their way in and found blood everywhere on the bed. It turned out that He Aishuang had been bitten in the throat by the dog and died. The dog was still sitting on his belly, eating voraciously. They then collected money to bury him and immediately killed the dog to offer it as a sacrifice. Sages say that one should not live among birds and beasts. Indeed.

    Zhang Aifu, a native of Shaoxing, lived in Hangzhou. From a young age, he had been betrothed to a woman from the Wang family, but at thirty years old, he was too poor to marry her. She was also twenty-seven years old. His mother repeatedly asked the matchmaker to urge Zhang Aifu to marry, but the matchmaker said, "What can he do if he is poor?" The mother replied, "He has no marriage expenses, and I have no dowry. If nothing else works, perhaps we should resort to eloping?" The matchmaker told Zhang Aifu, who was greatly pleased. He then planned to gather a group of people to kidnap the woman on a certain day. The mother deliberately gathered neighbors to Zhang Aifu's house to take the woman, but by the time they arrived, the wedding had already taken place, and the house was filled with guests. The matchmaker advised, "The situation is already this way; what more can be said? Tomorrow, we should make him come to apologize." The mother reluctantly returned home. Kidnapping for marriage is a vile custom, explicitly forbidden by law. Yet in this case, it was the woman's family who invited the kidnapping, which is a novel and unprecedented occurrence.

    207

    律载将妻妾典雇与人为妻妾者，杖八十。而宁波乡间往往有此事，亦恶俗也。有唐某者，以采樵为业。一母一妻，以捆屦织席佐之，而常苦不给。值岁歉，饔飧缺焉。闻邻村有王姓者，无子，欲典人妻以生子。唐谋于母，将以妻典焉。妻不可，唐曰：“妇人失节，固是大事，然使母饿死，事更大矣。”妇乃诺之，典于王，以一年为期。而妇有姿，王嬖之。及期往赎，王将典契中一字改为十字，唐不能争。妇告众曰：“吾隐忍为此者，以为日无多而可以活姑与夫之命也。若迟至十年，吾行且就木矣，其奚赎焉？”乃投水死。此妇以养姑失节，卒至破镜难圆，黄泉赍恨，其亦可悲也已！

    Explanation

    207

    The legal code prescribes that those who hire out their wives and concubines to others as wives and concubines shall be sentenced to eighty strokes of the rod. Yet, in the countryside of Ningbo, such incidents often occur, making it a bad custom. There was a man named Tang who made a living by gathering firewood. With his mother and one wife, he supplemented their income by binding sandals and weaving mats, but they often suffered from not having enough. During a year of famine, their meals were scarce. He heard that a man named Wang in the neighboring village, who had no children, wanted to hire a wife to bear a child. Tang consulted his mother, planning to hire out his wife. The wife refused, but Tang said, "A woman losing her virtue is indeed a serious matter, but if my mother were to starve to death, that would be an even greater matter." The wife then agreed, and was hired out to Wang for a period of one year. However, the woman was beautiful, and Wang favored her. When the term was up, Tang went to redeem her, but Wang changed one character in the contract to ten characters, and Tang could not argue. The woman told everyone, "I endured this because I thought that with a short time left, I could save my mother-in-law and my husband's lives. If it were delayed for ten years, I would soon be dead, so what would there be to redeem?" She then drowned herself. This woman lost her virtue to support her mother-in-law, ultimately ending up in a situation where the broken mirror could not be mended, carrying her grievances to the netherworld, which is truly pitiful.

    208

    金陵城中有以芦席覆一尸者，其上半身不可见，下半身则裤仅及膝，双足不履不袜，挺然不动。尸旁立一丐者，向众涕泣，募钱收敛，云死者其父也。于时观者甚众，颇有哀而予钱者，或百或数十，一时积有千余。丐犹未足，请益哀。有老翁持筒吸淡巴菰，余烬适坠于尸足，足为之蹄。众大惊，既而悟其伪也，乃大笑。死者掀开芦席而起曰：“愈矣，愈矣！”卷芦席，向众叩头谢，共荷钱而去。

    　　苏有汪氏子者，贾于常州，托其友金姓者寄家书于其母。金故酒徒也，既至苏，先沽饮大醉，然后如汪氏。汪所居在虎丘山后，金行至望山桥，失足坠于河，觉有人坚握其手不令起。金大惊，正与相持，忽有人诃曰：“此人为汪节妇送家书，尔曹安得留之！”金始得攀援登岸，疾行至汪氏，衣服淋漓，手腕青黑，汪母问故，具告之。母曰：“不图匹妇一节之善，感动鬼神也。”

    Explanation

    208

    In the city of Jinling, there was a corpse covered with reed mats. The upper half of the body was not visible, while the lower half was only partially covered, with the pants reaching just below the knees. Both feet were bare and without socks, and the body was stiffly motionless. Beside the corpse stood a beggar who wept before the crowd, raising money for the burial, claiming that the deceased was his father. At that time, there were many onlookers, and some, moved by pity, gave money, ranging from a hundred to dozens of coins, accumulating to over a thousand in total. The beggar still felt it was insufficient and pleaded for more. An old man, holding a tube and smoking tobacco, accidentally dropped the embers onto the corpse's feet, causing them to twitch. The crowd was greatly surprised, but soon realized it was a deception and burst into laughter. The "corpse" then lifted the reed mats and stood up, saying, "I am better, I am better!" He rolled up the reed mats, bowed to the crowd in gratitude, and carried the money away.

    In Suzhou, there was a merchant named Wang who traded in Changzhou. He entrusted his friend, surnamed Jin, to deliver a letter to his mother. Jin was a habitual heavy drinker. After arriving in Suzhou, he first bought and drank heavily, then went to Wang's residence. Wang lived behind Tiger Hill. Jin, while walking to the bridge named Wàng Shān Bridge, slipped and fell into the river. He felt someone holding his hand tightly and preventing him from rising. Jin was greatly alarmed, and as he struggled, someone suddenly shouted, "This person is delivering a letter for Mrs. Wang, how could you keep him!" Jin then managed to climb up to the shore, hurriedly made his way to Wang's residence, his clothes soaked, and his wrists bruised and black. Wang's mother asked him about the incident, and he explained everything. She said, "I never expected a woman's virtue to be so great, it has moved the spirits."

    209

    浙右某生，年少美风姿，有声庠序间。以早丧父母，故弱冠而未有室。某年，以乡试至省城，寓亲串家。其家有女，貌美而能文，两相慕悦，遂有割臂之盟。女父已亡，其母微知之，不禁也。及生使媒妁来求婚，母问生年，则长于女者六岁，以俗有六冲之忌，辞焉。女知事不谐，终日悲泣。母始而慰论，继而谯诃，女遂雉经死。生闻之，亦仰药自尽。拘牵俗忌，遂使男女并以情死，虽非礼所许，其情亦可怜也。

    　　龚某，不知何处人，入都应京兆试。场前纳一妾一婢，皆购自牙人，其姓氏里居，固无稽也。寓宅在前门外三里河，分前后二院。将入闱，以妾独居，虑有他虞，乃请其友部郎某君居其前院。部郎素迂谨，承友朋之托，一步不敢离，日坐前院读书而已。场毕，龚归，部郎告以谨守管键，幸不辱命。龚再三致谢，乃入后院，则阒其无人，并室中什物俱归乌有矣。窗户完好，惟后垣颓焉。始悟妾与婢皆从此去。防其前，未防其后也。

    Explanation

    209

    A young man from Zhejiang, handsome and graceful, was well-known in the local schools. Having lost his parents at an early age, he remained unmarried at the age of twenty. In a certain year, he traveled to the provincial capital for the regional examination and stayed at the home of a relative. The relative's daughter was beautiful and talented, and the two fell deeply in love, making a blood oath to each other. The girl's father had already passed away, and her mother, upon learning of their relationship, did not forbid it. When the young man sent a matchmaker to propose marriage, the mother asked about his age and discovered that he was six years older than her daughter. Due to the traditional belief that a marriage in the sixth year is inauspicious, she declined the proposal. The girl, realizing that their union was doomed, wept endlessly. Her mother initially tried to comfort her but then scolded her, leading the girl to take her own life by hanging. Upon hearing of her death, the young man also took poison and ended his life. Bound by superstitious taboos, both the man and the woman died out of their love for each other, though their actions were not sanctioned by propriety, their story is indeed pitiable.

    As for Gong, whose origins are unknown, he came to the capital to take the provincial examination. Before the examination, he purchased a concubine and a maid from a broker, but their names and origins are unknown. He rented a house near the front gate of the city, divided into front and back courtyards. As he was about to enter the examination hall, he was concerned that his concubine, left alone, might be in danger, so he asked a friend, a certain Departmental Officer, to stay in the front courtyard. The Departmental Officer, known for his cautious and conservative nature, accepted the request and stayed in the front courtyard, reading books all day. After the examination, Gong returned home, and the Departmental Officer informed him that he had carefully guarded the premises, fulfilling his duty. Gong thanked him repeatedly and then went to the back courtyard, where he found it empty, with all the belongings gone. The windows were intact, but the wall at the back had collapsed. He then realized that his concubine and maid had escaped through the back wall. He had guarded the front but failed to protect the back.

    210

    扬州东乡十里田有看青苗人，野宿棚内。遥见二里外河边土地庙前有火光，或大或小，忽明忽暗，疑之，约壮夫数人持械同往。至则一僧席地坐，旁置土锉，支以石，聚薪燃之。问何事，僧曰：“吾朝南海还，过此腹饥，故煮食耳。”众亦将返矣，一人笑曰：“和尚得毋食肉？”戏揭其盖，则所煮者两婴儿胎也。众大惊，争以械击僧。僧倒地，叹曰：“吾数尽矣！凡吾曹炼金刚禅者，须食男胎三十六个，大道乃成。吾止食其七，而遇公等，此吾福薄也。速毙吾，无多言。”众怒甚，击杀之，而投其尸于河。

    Explanation

    210

    Ten li east of Yangzhou, in the eastern outskirts, there was a man watching over young crops, sleeping in a shelter in the wild. He saw a firelight two miles away at the riverbank in front of a land temple, fluctuating in size and brightness. Suspicious, he gathered several strong men with weapons and went together. Upon arrival, they found a monk sitting on the ground, with a tripod placed on stones nearby, gathering fuel and lighting it. They asked what he was doing, and the monk replied, "I returned from the South Sea and was hungry, so I am just cooking food." The men were about to return when one of them laughed and said, "Are you not eating meat, monk?" Playfully lifting the lid, they discovered that what the monk was cooking were the fetuses of two infants. The men were greatly shocked and fought to kill the monk with their weapons. The monk fell to the ground and sighed, "My fate is sealed! All of us who practice the Diamond Sutra meditation must consume 36 male fetuses to achieve enlightenment. I have only consumed seven, and now I have met you all. This is because my fortune is thin. Kill me quickly, don't waste words." The men became very angry and killed him, then threw his body into the river.

    211

    无锡有哑丐者，行乞于市，多历年所，人皆识之。一日，至南门内一绅家乞食。其阍人拒之曰：“吾主人因幼子病危，自朝至日昃，不遑暇食，焉有食食汝乎？”哑丐忽出言曰：“何不谋之于我，我能治之。”阍者以其哑而能言，大异之，入言于主人，姑延入试之。切脉处方，具有条理。一服而有起色，服三剂，病者霍然。主人喜出望外，问其生平。丐自言：“父在日曾开药肆。我虽生而哑，然自幼识字，喜观医书，故颇知医。父殁家贫，又以不能言，有术无可施，故流而为丐耳。”问今何以能言，则亦不自知也。由是医名大噪，县壶城隍庙，就诊者日以千百计。然是岁之冬，有杭州黄质文路过无锡，访之，见其所开脉案不成文理，所书药名亦多别字。恐其术止一时偶中，竟以为高手医，吾不信也。或曰：“哑子能言，亦大奇事。”然安知其始非伪为哑以行乞欤？

    Explanation

    211

    In Wuxi, there was a mute beggar who had been begging in the market for many years, and everyone knew him. One day, he went to a wealthy family in the southern part of the city to beg for food. The gatekeeper rejected him, saying, "Our master has been busy since morning until noon because his young son is gravely ill. He hasn't had time to eat, so how can we feed you?" Suddenly, the mute beggar spoke and said, "Why not consult me? I can treat him." The gatekeeper was astonished that the mute beggar could speak and reported this to the master. The master invited the beggar in to try his skills. The beggar took the pulse, prescribed medicine, and his treatment was well-organized. After one dose, the patient showed signs of improvement, and after three doses, he recovered completely. The master was overjoyed and asked about the beggar's background. The beggar said, "My father used to run a pharmacy. Although I was born mute, I learned to read since childhood and enjoyed reading medical books, so I know a lot about medicine. After my father's death, our family became poor, and because I couldn't speak, there was no way to apply my skills, so I became a beggar." When asked why he could speak now, he said he didn't know. As a result, his medical reputation became very famous, and he set up a clinic at the Temple of the City God. Thousands of patients came to see him every day. However, in that winter, a man named Huang Zhiwen from Hangzhou passed through Wuxi and visited the beggar. He saw that the prescriptions the beggar wrote were not coherent, and the names of the medicines were often misspelled. He feared that the beggar's skills were just a lucky coincidence and yet he was considered a great doctor. He did not believe it. Someone said, "A mute speaking is also a great wonder." But how can we know if he was pretending to be mute to beg?

    212

    道光丁酉乡试之年，湖州双林镇人沈涛在家昼寝，梦有数人哗嚣而入曰：“沈涛中式矣！”正惊喜间，又有一人狂奔而至曰：“误矣，误矣！非此沈涛也。”乃轰然俱出。其步履之声，始则自外而内，继则自内而外，行于石，然，行于木，登登然，皆历历可辨。是科沈响泉同年中式。响泉名涛，湖州城中人，所谓非此沈涛也。殆鬼神以此人名姓相同，故意戏弄之欤？

    Explanation

    212

    In the year of the provincial examination during the Ding You year of the Daoguang era, Shen Tao of Shuanglin Town in Huzhou was napping during the day when he had a dream. Several people suddenly entered, shouting, "Shen Tao has passed the examination!" Just as he was filled with surprise and joy, another person suddenly ran in and said, "Mistaken! Mistaken! This is not Shen Tao." They then suddenly left all together. The sound of their footsteps was first from outside to inside, then from inside to outside, clattering on stone and echoing on wood, all clearly distinguishable. That year, Shen Xiangquan, who passed the examination, was the one who actually passed. Xiangquan's name is also Tao, and he is from the city of Huzhou, which is the person referred to, not this Shen Tao. Perhaps the ghost or deity played a trick on him due to the similarity in names?

    213

    扬州之北鄙雷塘乡，即隋炀帝葬处也。近年忽出一物女，（“物女”二字见《春秋繁露。王道篇》，曰：“干溪有物女，水尽则女见。”）与村中少壮者交。交辄病，病重者死。光绪二年六月二十四日，日加午，忽阴晦如夜，雷雨大作。电光中见一女子，白衣红抹额，手执双叉，与霹雳斗，雷竟不能下击。相持良久，大声忽发，有雷火从地出，伤女一足。女稍逡巡，一迅雷从空劈下，其声猛烈异常，观者皆晕仆。俄而雨霁，则有一物震死于地。如猪而无尾，如牛而无角，周身白毛，两背至胁有黑毛成如意形，腹下一肉条长二尺余，其气膻腥，不可向迩。称之重二百余斤，众莫之识。其地有观音庵，一老僧出视曰：“此螭虎也。

    Explanation

    213

    The northern frontier of Yangzhou, in Leitang Village, is where Emperor Yang of Sui was buried. Recently, a creature known as a "thing-woman" (物女) appeared there. ("Thing-woman" is a term found in the Spring and Autumn Annals (春秋繁露), which states, "In the dry creek, there is a thing-woman; when the water dries up, the woman appears.") She engaged with the young and strong men of the village. Those who engaged with her fell ill, and those who became severely ill died. On the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month in the year of Guangxu (光绪二年), at midday, the sky suddenly grew dark as if it were night, and there was a heavy thunderstorm. In the lightning, a woman was seen, dressed in white with a red headband, holding a pair of forks, fighting against the lightning. The thunder could not strike her. They fought for a long time, and then a loud sound suddenly erupted, and a bolt of lightning shot from the ground, injuring one of her feet. The woman hesitated for a moment, and then a swift bolt of lightning struck down from the sky, its sound being exceptionally loud, causing all the spectators to faint. Soon after the rain stopped, a creature was found dead on the ground. It was like a pig without a tail, like a cow without horns, with white hair all over its body, and black hair forming a wish-fulfilling pattern on its back and sides. It had a fleshy strip on its belly, two feet long, and its smell was pungent and unpleasant to be near. It weighed over two hundred pounds, and no one could identify it. There is an观音庵 (Guanyin Temple) in that area, and an old monk came out to look and said, "This is a chīhǔ (螭虎)."

    214

    ”乡人脔割而焚之，臭闻里外。愚按《说文》，螭若龙而黄。《汉书。杨雄传》《音义》引韦昭云：“螭似虎而鳞。”此物白毛黑文而无鳞，未必其为螭也。京师十刹海烟袋斜街，有山西刘姓之女，小字香珠，自幼许嫁同乡黄某。黄学贾于外，久无音问，而刘姓中落，女遂流落烟花。已而黄某积累得数百金，挟之至都，将求故剑，舍于西河沿逆旅。主人利其腰缠，诱与冶游。黄年少，堕其计。妓有秀兰者，与黄极相得。无何，黄之金尽，妓家将有逐客之令。秀兰乃托言赴庙会，与黄偕逃至良乡，投宿一老媪家。媪熟视曰：“汝非山西黄某欤？此女其刘家香珠欤？”叩媪姓氏，乃黄之从母，而刘女幼时呼为干阿奶者也，于是始知秀兰即香珠。媪为买花烛，具鼓乐，而成夫妇。

    Explanation

    214

    A local resident dismembered and burned the body, and the stench spread throughout the neighborhood. According to the Shuowen Jiezi, a chi resembles a dragon but is yellow. The Han Shu (History of the Han Dynasty), specifically the biography of Yang Xiong, with annotations by Wei Zhao, states that a chi resembles a tiger with scales. However, this creature had white fur and black markings but no scales, so it may not have been a chi. In the area of Ten Temples Sea and Yandaixie Street in the capital, there lived a girl named Xiangzhu from Shanxi. She was betrothed to a local man named Huang from a young age. Huang went to learn trade elsewhere and lost contact. The Liu family fell on hard times, and the girl became involved in the entertainment industry. Later, Huang accumulated several hundred gold coins and returned to the capital with them, intending to find his former betrothed. He stayed at an inn near Xihéyán. The innkeeper coveted his wealth and lured him into a life of debauchery. Being young, Huang fell for the innkeeper's scheme. There was a courtesan named Xiulan who became very close to Huang. Soon after, Huang spent all his money, and the courtesan's household planned to expel him. Xiulan pretended to go to a temple fair and escaped with Huang to Liangxiang, where they sought refuge in the home of an elderly woman. The elderly woman looked at them closely and asked, "Are you not Huang from Shanxi? Is this girl Xiangzhu from the Liu family?" When they asked her name, she revealed that she was Huang's aunt and was called "gàn ānai" (a kind of godmother) by Xiangzhu when she was young. Thus, they realized that Xiulan was indeed Xiangzhu. The elderly woman arranged a wedding ceremony with wedding candles and music, and they became husband and wife.

    215

    扬州左卫街一大宅，乱时为贼中大头目据为伪府。乱平后，某姓居之。其堂前地，每逢阴雨，辄见一女子形，洗之不去，天晴即没，遂扃闭不处。偶有亲串自远来，仆从众多，遂入居之。夜半，忽见砖动不已，顷刻坟起，一仆践之，晕仆于地。天明，言于主人，发而视之，则一女尸也。衣裙未坏，面目如生，遍体生绿毛，长寸许，栩栩欲动。闻于官，官命焚之。其邻有攻皮之工某叟，曰：“此事吾知之。我陷贼中，即隶此贼帐下。贼获此女，欲污之，骂詈不从。杖数百，骂益厉，遂活埋之。不意其今为祟也。”余谓此女既以烈死，毅魄贞魂，久已归之太虚，此特其遗蜕耳。久而成僵，或地气使然，与烈女初不涉矣。惜叟知其事而不能举其姓氏，不得为之表彰也。

    Explanation

    215

    During a period of chaos, a large mansion on Left Guard Street in Yangzhou was occupied by a prominent bandit leader as a counterfeit government office. After the chaos subsided, a family moved into the house. In the courtyard in front of the main hall, a woman's image would appear every time it rained, and it could not be washed away; it would disappear when the sky cleared. The house was then sealed and not used. One day, a distant relative came to visit, accompanied by many servants, and they moved into the house. At midnight, they suddenly saw bricks moving incessantly, and in a moment, a mound rose up. One servant stepped on it and fainted, falling to the ground. The next morning, they reported this to the owner, who dug it up and found a female corpse.
