There was a woman from the Shi family who gave birth to nine monsters in nine births. My eldest daughter-in-law's sister-in-law, who is from the Shi family, personally witnessed three of these: one monkey, one snake, and one bird. In Xian Ning, there was also a woman from the Mao family who had a premarital relationship. Her parents, angered by this, killed her and buried her body in the wild. Soon after, she became a zombie and chased passersby. They covered her with a fish net and burned her. Folklore says that burning a zombie must be covered with a fish net, and the body will turn to ashes, and the spirit will also be extinguished. At the time, they forgot to cover her with a fish net, and the smoke rose into the sky, revealing a pair of red shoes at the edge of the forest.

    261

    已而其鬼果时出为厉，凡人家子女之洁白端整者，每每为所祟而死。远近咸称之曰毛家姑妈，不知其所起，亦不知其何以得此称也。为所崇者，初不自言，虽父母前不告也。其人了无疾苦，但言欲制某色衣，某色裤，某色鞋袜，女则并抹胸缠足，一一布置。诸物既具，始言毛家姑妈招我去矣，向所布置皆毛家姑妈为之也。乘人不觉，即取著之，或仰药，或雉经，无一免者。于是人皆畏之，各村皆为立庙；庙立之后，为祟如故。有咸宁令某公，闻之大怒，悉毁其庙，且至江西真人府求施敕勒之术，自是稍静谧，然其为祟已数十年矣。

    Explanation

    261

    After a while, the ghost indeed appeared at times to cause harm. Any children who were pure, clean, and well-mannered in their homes would often be tormented by this spirit and die. People far and near called it "Auntie Mao," though no one knew where it came from or how it came to have this name. Those who were tormented did not speak of it at first, nor did they report it even to their parents. The victims showed no signs of illness or distress, but they would only say they wanted to make clothes of a certain color, pants of a certain color, shoes and socks of a certain color, and for girls, corsets and bound feet as well, all arranged one by one. Once all the items were ready, they would then say that Auntie Mao had called them, and that all the arrangements had been made by Auntie Mao. Taking advantage of people's unawareness, they would immediately put on the clothes and either drink poison or hang themselves, and none escaped. Therefore, people all feared it, and each village built temples for it. After the temples were built, the ghost continued to cause harm as before. There was a magistrate of Xianning, upon hearing of this, greatly angered, and destroyed all the temples. He also went to the true person's office in Jiangxi to seek the application of the sealing and binding technique. From then on, it became somewhat quiet, but its causing harm had already lasted for several decades.

    262

    方其盛时，乡间至不敢言毛家姑妈，偶言及之，则动色相戒云。及粤寇乱后，毛家姑妈复出，然不至如前之甚。诸被祟者，或仍言毛家姑妈，或言某氏子某氏女，则皆向之死于祟者，盖其徒党也。异哉！毛氏一女子，非取多而用宏者，何死后数十年犹有灵爽若此也？殆戾气所锺欤？又有顺宝者，咸宁范氏女，亦未嫁而私于人，为父母所杀。其鬼时时出见，久之人亦习见而不为异。每春日，诸女至山中采茶，薄暮则顺宝之鬼辄出助之，与其妹尤昵，几于无日不见。每语妹曰：“我不肖，死固分也；妹幸好为人，毋似我为父母羞。”此鬼殊有意，胜毛家姑妈多矣。

    Explanation

    262

    During the height of its power, the countryside was so afraid to mention Ma's aunt that they would change their expressions and warn each other. After the chaos caused by the Guangdong rebels, Ma's aunt reappeared, but not to the same extent as before. Those who were afflicted would either still mention Ma's aunt or speak of someone's son or daughter, all of whom had died from supernatural affliction, likely their followers. Strange indeed! Ma's aunt was merely a woman of the Mao family, not one who amassed great wealth and influence, yet how is it that, after her death for several decades, she still has such a powerful presence? Perhaps it is the concentrated malevolent energy? There was also Shunbao, a daughter of the Fan family from Xianning, who also had an affair before she was married and was killed by her parents. Her ghost appeared frequently, and over time, people became accustomed to seeing it and no longer found it strange. Every spring, the women would go to the mountains to pick tea, and as dusk approached, Shunbao's ghost would appear to help them. She was especially close to her sister, almost never unseen. She would often tell her sister, "I am unworthy, and my death was inevitable; sister, be fortunate to be human, and do not be like me, a source of shame to our parents." This ghost was particularly meaningful, far surpassing Ma's aunt.

    263

    嘉庆时，钱唐人某游天台山，见一人自称陆讲山，言明季事甚详，异之。明日复访其人，则不复见矣。某欣然以为遇仙。按陆讲山先生名圻，字丽京，钱唐人，乃国初西冷十子之一。少负盛名，与弟阶、培称“三陆”。培以行人死甲申之难。先生后为湖州庄氏之狱所株连，及事白，奉母隐西溪骆家庄，常卖药苕、间。及母殁，弃家远游，不知所终。至嘉庆末，此人犹得见之，殆真仙矣。震泽王君蟾生，余门下士王廷鼎梦薇之父也。道光庚子岁，读书东安村之八蜡庙。庙有楼，王君与诸友昼则读于其下，夜则寝于其上。一日，诸友皆外出，王君独留。忽狂风起，窗户皆鸣，卷帙飞舞。突见一老叟兀立案前，年可六十许，黄面白髯，目开合不止，两手向空作攫抓之形。王君惊问何人，叟不答，徐徐却走，退至东壁，如嵌入者，两手攫抓如故。

    Explanation

    263

    During the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, an unnamed person from Qiantang traveled to Tiantai Mountain and met someone who introduced himself as Lu Jiangshan. This person spoke in great detail about the late Ming period, which surprised the traveler. The next day, when the traveler returned to visit the person, he was no longer there. The traveler happily thought he had met a fairy. According to Mr. Lu Jiangshan, Lu Jiangshan was a historical figure, a scholar and poet from the early Qing dynasty. He was renowned from a young age and, along with his brothers Jie and Pei, they were known as the "Three Lus." Pei died as a messenger during the disaster of the Year of Jia Shen. Later, Mr. Lu was implicated in the Zhejiang Zhuang family case. When the matter was cleared up, he took his mother to live in seclusion in Luo Family Village, Xixi, and often sold medicine in Xiaoshan and Deqing areas. After his mother's death, he abandoned his home and traveled far away, and his final whereabouts are unknown. By the end of the Jiaqing era, this person could still see him, suggesting that he must have been a real fairy. Wang Chansheng from Zhenze was the father of my student Wang Tingding, whose courtesy name is Mengwei. In the year 1830 during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, Wang Chansheng studied at the Eight Waxes Temple in Dong'an Village. The temple had a tower, and Wang Chansheng and his friends read under it during the day and slept on top of it at night. One day, when all his friends had gone out, Wang Chansheng was left alone. Suddenly, a strong wind arose, the windows all made a rattling sound, and the books flew around. Suddenly, an old man appeared, standing motionless in front of the desk. He was about sixty years old, with a yellowish face and white beard, and his eyes kept opening and closing. His hands were reaching out into the air as if grabbing something. Wang Chansheng was startled and asked who he was. The old man did not answer, slowly backed away, and retreated to the eastern wall, as if embedded in the wall. His hands continued to reach out as before.

    264

    王君大呼，有老僧闻声趋至，则全身已隐，而须眉口鼻犹见于壁，栩栩欲活也。惧而走出。薄暮诸友至，复偕入视之，则无有矣。是夜仍宿于楼，夜半闻扣窗声，窥之，则有长人足立庭中，而首出楼外。诸友中有吴友霞者，素有胆，潜立窗侧，挟利匕首伺之。俄其人探手入，急以匕首刺之，中其腕，大叫而遁，匕首亦随腕俱去。俄闻锵然有声，知匕首堕矣。明晨得之于殿庭之西隅，血凝如膏，腥秽不可近。其时庙中人咸集，循血迹求之，至庙后，有败棺数具，一棺盖有渍血。僧曰：“必此棺为怪矣。”议焚之，王君止之，后亦无他。余谓昼所见者或即棺中人；若夜所见者，怪也，非鬼也，鬼安得有血乎？使非昼有此异，夜必酣眠，或为怪所攫食，未可知矣。棺中人与诸君有恩，王君止焚，是为有见。惟棺盖血迹又似可疑。或曰：“必此怪恨棺中人，而以此嫁祸也。”若然，则俄顷之间，机械横生，捷于鸣镝，视巨手破窗，更为可畏矣。

    Explanation

    264

    King Wang cried out loudly, and an old monk heard the sound and hurried to the scene. By then, the entire figure had already vanished, but his eyebrows, beard, mouth, and nose were still visible on the wall, appearing lifelike and almost alive. Frightened, the monk fled out. As dusk approached, his friends arrived, and they all entered together to inspect the area, but there was nothing there. That night, they continued to stay in the tower. In the middle of the night, they heard a knocking sound at the window. Upon peering out, they saw a tall figure standing in the courtyard with its head protruding from the outside of the tower. Among the friends was Wu Youxia, who was known for his courage. He secretly stood by the window, holding a sharp dagger and waiting for the figure. Suddenly, the figure reached out its hand, and Wu Youxia quickly stabbed it with the dagger, hitting its wrist. The figure screamed and fled, taking the dagger with it. Shortly afterward, they heard a clanging sound, indicating that the dagger had fallen. The next morning, they found the dagger in the western corner of the temple courtyard, where the blood had coagulated like fat, emitting a foul smell that was unbearable to approach. At that time, everyone in the temple gathered, following the trail of blood. They reached the back of the temple, where they found several decayed coffins. One of the coffins had a stain of blood on its lid. The monk said, "This coffin must be the source of the strange phenomenon." They discussed burning it, but King Wang stopped them, and afterward, there were no further incidents. I believe that what was seen during the day might have been the person inside the coffin; if what was seen at night was a supernatural entity, it was not a ghost, for how could a ghost have blood? If there had not been this strange occurrence during the day, they would have slept soundly at night, and perhaps they would have been seized and eaten by the supernatural entity, which is uncertain. The person in the coffin had done a favor for the friends, and King Wang's decision to stop the burning was wise. However, the bloodstain on the coffin lid seemed suspicious. Some said, "This supernatural entity must be angry with the person in the coffin and is using this to shift the blame." If that were the case, then in a short while, ingenious devices would arise, moving as swiftly as a whistle, making the sight of a large hand breaking the window even more terrifying.

    265

    平望镇之西，有地名韭溪，里人潘氏之墓在焉。某年夏，忽于赤日杲杲中辟历一声，白云四起，有一物从空中堕其墓上。其物长四五尺，牛首鼍身，有角有鳞，而无尾。四足皆五爪，而后足若痿Φ不能起者，但以前两足蹒跚而行。所过之处，翁仲俱仆，大木尽拔。村人大骇，聚而观之，且奔告于潘。次日，潘至，是物已力倦，卧于丰草中，腥气不可向迩，蝇蚁集于其身，遍体蠕蠕然，而是物若不知者。或以竹竿刺之，亦不动，间或昂首一嘘气，触之者无不立倒。潘知其异，禁人勿犯之。无何，风雷交作，大雨如注，物奋身一跃，腾空而去。至天半，犹垂首俯视，久之始杳，竟不知是何物也。

    265

    To the west of Pingwang Town, there is a place called Scallion Creek, where the tomb of the Pan family in the village is located. One summer, a sudden loud sound like thunder was heard amidst the bright, scorching sun, and white clouds gathered. A creature fell from the sky onto the tomb. The creature was about four to five feet long, with a cow's head and a toad's body, horns, and scales, but no tail. It had four legs, each with five claws, and its hind legs seemed weak or paralyzed, so it could only walk unsteadily on its front legs. Everywhere it went, the stone statues (翁仲) fell over, and large trees were uprooted. The villagers were greatly alarmed, gathered to watch, and rushed to inform the Pan family. The next day, when the Pan family arrived, the creature had already grown tired and lay in the lush grass, emitting an unbearable stench. Flies and ants gathered on its body, and it writhed slightly, but the creature seemed unaware. Someone prodded it with a bamboo pole, but it did not move. Occasionally, it would raise its head and exhale a breath, and anyone who touched it fell down immediately. The Pan family realized it was unusual and ordered people not to disturb it. Soon after, wind and thunder occurred simultaneously, and heavy rain poured down. The creature leaped into the air and flew away. It reached halfway up the sky, still lowering its head and looking down, and after a long time, it disappeared, and it was ultimately unknown what the creature was.

    266

    布袋和尚者，不知何许人。嘉庆中，至吴江县城卖卜，居城东关帝庙中，口操楚音，年可七十余。项下县一黄布袋，不暂释，因以名之。袋广长仅尺余，不甚丰满，而和尚每日所用之具，若杯，若壶，若冠履，若纸墨笔砚，咸取之此，未尝阙焉。日卖卜，以十事为限，谈休咎辄中。既毕，则遍游村市，见字纸，必拾之投袋中，恒劝入惜字。自言每日所拾，暮则权之，必满一斤之数，如是者三十余年矣。庙中人有伺其熟睡而探其袋者，止得龟壳一，长寸余。

    Explanation

    266

    The Bag-Bearer Monk, whose origin is unknown, arrived in Wujiang County during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing. He sold divination services in the eastern Guan Yu Temple of the county, speaking with a Chu accent, and was around seventy years old. He always carried a yellow cloth bag around his neck, never letting it out of his possession, and thus was known as the Bag-Bearer Monk. The bag was about a foot long and not very full, yet it contained all the monk's daily necessities, such as cups, jugs, hats, shoes, and writing materials. He sold divination services, limiting himself to ten predictions per day, and his predictions were often accurate. After his work, he would wander through villages and markets, always picking up written paper and putting it in his bag, encouraging people to respect written characters. He claimed that every day he collected enough paper to weigh a pound, and he had been doing this for over thirty years. Once, someone tried to investigate the contents of his bag while he was sleeping and found only a single turtle shell, about an inch long.

    267

    于是皆疑为仙，环而叩其术。和尚厌之，用不恒至。一夕忽来，即阖户而寝。次日日中不起，呼之不应，破扉入，则和尚圆寂矣。失其袋，大索不得，正共惊异，而西郭外之人至，咸曰：“和尚成神矣！”盖其地故有土地庙，是夕，父老皆梦土地来别曰：“吾去矣。明日有县布袋于项者，是代吾者也。”及旦，父老至庙中察之，则见神项下县一黄布袋，诧曰：“此布袋和尚之物，何为而在此？”入城，而和尚果死，故知其真成神也。众即葬之土地庙后，至今神项犹县布袋云。

    Explanation

    267

    Everyone then began to suspect that he was a deity, surrounding him and asking about his methods. The monk grew tired of them and did not visit regularly. One night, he suddenly came, closed the door, and went to sleep. The next day, he did not get up at noon, and when they called him, there was no response. They broke down the door and found that the monk had passed away. They lost his bag, and after a thorough search, they could not find it. Just as they were all amazed, people from the western outskirts came, all saying, "The monk has become a deity!" There was originally a land temple in that area, and that night, the elders all dreamed that the land god came to say farewell, saying, "I am leaving. Tomorrow, someone will hang a cloth bag around their neck; that person will replace me." By morning, the elders went to the temple to check, and they saw a yellow cloth bag hanging from the deity's neck. They were amazed and said, "This is the monk's bag; how did it get here?" When they entered the city, they found that the monk had indeed died, so they knew that he had truly become a deity. They buried him behind the land temple, and to this day, the deity's neck still hangs the cloth bag.

    268

    桐乡倪亚卿，弱冠游吴中，寓桃花庵之梦墨楼。一夕独坐，忽闻窗外吟诗声，静听之，其诗云：“生怕秋来瘦不支，十年心事诉谁知？荒庵寂寞如宫禁，况是风清月白时”“一病奄然百事灰，多情无力挽春回。青山绿水桥边路，苦望郎君拨棹来。”声甚凄婉。倪知是鬼，即亦不惧。俄见一女子推扉入，倪问曰：“卿何人也？”女曰：“妾姚氏，字修竹，家山塘，故风尘中人也。年十六，为贵州李小白刺史所赏，许以千金聘妾，先下百金为定，期三年之后改官江南，然后来迎。

    Explanation

    268

    Nian Yaqing, a young man from Tongxiang, traveled to Wu in his early twenties and stayed at the Dream Ink Tower within the Peach Blossom Hermitage. One night, while sitting alone, he suddenly heard someone reciting poetry outside his window. He listened quietly, and the poem went: "I fear that in autumn I will grow too thin to support myself, with ten years of sorrow, who will understand? The desolate hermitage is as lonely as a palace, especially on a night of clear winds and bright moon." Another verse followed: "Suddenly falling ill, all things turn to ashes, sensitive and lacking strength to hold back spring. On the path beside the bridge, green mountains and clear water, I desperately hope that the young master will row the boat here." The voice was very sorrowful and moving. Nian knew that the voice belonged to a ghost, but he was not afraid. Soon, a woman pushed open the door and entered. Nian asked, "Who are you?" The woman replied, "I am Yao, courtesy name Xiu Zhu. I am from Shantang, and I was once a courtesan. When I was sixteen, I was admired by Governor Li Xiaobai of Guizhou, who promised to marry me with a thousand gold coins, and he gave a hundred gold coins as a down payment, agreeing to move to Jiangnan after three years to come and fetch me."

    269

    乃瓜期满矣，而花信杳然。妾感其情，誓不再嫁，独居抑郁，半载而殂。三寸桐棺，即停庵后，偶来玩月，聊尔哀吟，以抒幽悃，幸勿见讶。”倪怜之，以手抚其背。女却立曰：“妾以守贞，故归泉壤，否则犹在人间也。”言已遂灭。次日验之，果有一棺在墙隅，倪因题诗楼壁，以识其事。越二载，有都司李某自黔来苏，亦寓此楼，见诗惊曰：“李小白，吾父也。于咸丰十年殉难，吾即以先人难荫得此右职，初不知有斯事也。”因属庵僧：“善视此柩。吾差事毕，再来此，当为营葬。”然其后亦不复至。

    Explanation

    269

    The time of the melon has passed, yet the blooming of flowers remains unseen. I am deeply moved by his feelings and have vowed never to remarry. Living in solitude and depression, I passed away after half a year. A small paulownia coffin was placed behind the hermitage. On a chance visit to admire the moon, I softly lamented, expressing my hidden feelings, hoping you would not be surprised. "Ni showed sympathy and gently patted my back. The woman stepped back and said, 'I have remained chaste and thus returned to the grave. Otherwise, I would still be alive in the world.' After she spoke, she vanished. The next day, it was confirmed that a coffin was indeed in a corner of the wall. Ni then inscribed a poem on the wall of the building to commemorate this event. Two years later, a military officer named Li from Guizhou came to Suzhou and stayed in this building. Seeing the poem, he was astonished and said, 'Li Xiaobai, this is my father. He died in the tenth year of the Xianfeng era. I obtained this high position through my father's posthumous honors, but I did not know of this matter.' He then instructed the hermitage monk, 'Take good care of this coffin. When I have completed my official duties, I will return to properly bury it.' However, he never came back."

    270

    咸宁毛氏有一婢，年十三四矣。其主母虑火灾，防范甚严，虽天寒不许近炉火。忽一夕，举家闻恶臭，若火炙物者，已而邻舍亦闻之，竞扣扉问故。其主母即呼是婢，则不知所在。旋得之于空室中，足踏铜火炉，倚门而立，呼之不应，视之，死矣。竞前挽之，则随手而化为灰，独两脚不化，犹立铜炉上，流膏满地。而室中物一无所毁，其所倚之门亦不焦灼。异哉！谓火自炉起，则当先焚其足，何其足转不化欤？或疑如佛家之戒火自焚，然此一蠢婢耳，平时初无异人处，且膏油流溢，死亦甚惨，戒火自焚，必不如是也。或疑此婢必先醉烧酒，火气薰，引动内火，遂至自焚。此颇似近之，然藏府燔烧，筋骨俱烬，其楚毒为何如，尚能植立不动欤？是真不可解矣。

    Explanation

    270

    A family from the Xinning period had a servant girl who was thirteen or fourteen years old. Her mistress was very cautious about fire, and even in cold weather, she did not allow the girl to approach the stove. One night, the entire household smelled a foul odor as if something was being burned. Soon, the neighbors also noticed the smell and began knocking on the door to inquire about the situation. The mistress called out to the girl, but she could not be found. They eventually discovered her in an empty room, standing with her feet on a copper stove, leaning against the door. When they called to her, she did not respond. Upon closer inspection, they found that she was dead. When they tried to pull her, she crumbled into ash, except for her two feet, which remained unchanged and still stood on the copper stove, with oil flowing all over the floor. However, nothing else in the room was damaged, and the door she was leaning against was not scorched. Strange indeed! If the fire had started from the stove, it should have burned her feet first, but why were her feet not burned? Some speculated that this might have been like the Buddhist practice of self-immolation, but this was just a stupid servant girl, and she had shown no signs of spiritual significance before. Moreover, the flow of oil and the gruesome nature of her death did not seem consistent with the act of self-immolation. Others suspected that the girl might have been drunk on burning alcohol, which could have ignited an internal fire, leading to her self-immolation. This explanation seems somewhat plausible, but if the internal organs and bones were burned, how could she have remained standing without moving? Truly, this event remains inexplicable.

    271

    绍兴萧山县有隔河，其河于百余年矣。光绪五年夏大旱，乃访求其故道，集民夫浚之。有周姓者与其役，掘得一石门，长广二尺许，启之则如隧道然。疑故时人家泄水之沟也，以竿探之，忽有声若人者。异而益穷之，至丈余，声愈厉。有青蛙数十跃出，大者如盂，最后一蛙，身长尺，色深黄，腹下纯白，遍身黑斑累累然，怒目大鸣。人或击以竿，蛙以前足格之，竿为之折。于是人争击之，蛙抱一木桩，兀不动，惟以后足相撑拒，口格格如鸣鼓。有老人急止之，曰：“此非常蛙，杀之恐不利。”众韪其言，拟纵之河，而蛙抱木不释，爪之入者三分。众因舁其木而投之，甫及河，蛙一跃而去，须臾大雨，四境沾足。

    Explanation

    271

    In Xiaoshan County, Shaoxing, there was a river that had been blocked for over a hundred years. In the fifth year of Guangxu, a severe summer drought occurred, so they sought its old course and gathered laborers to dredge it. A man surnamed Zhou was involved in the labor, and they dug up a stone door, about two feet long and wide. When opened, it was like a tunnel. They suspected it to be an old drainage ditch from a house and used a pole to probe it. Suddenly, a sound like a person crying was heard. Finding it strange, they dug deeper, and when they reached over a meter, the crying sound became louder. Dozens of frogs jumped out, the larger ones as big as a basin. The last frog was a foot long, deep yellow in color, with a pure white underside and black spots all over its body. It glared with anger and made a loud sound. When someone struck it with a pole, the frog blocked it with its front feet, breaking the pole. The crowd then tried to strike it again, but the frog held onto a wooden post and wouldn't let go, with its claws digging in three inches deep. The crowd then carried the wood and threw it into the river. As soon as it reached the river, the frog leaped away. Soon after, it rained heavily, and the entire area was soaked.

    272

    童元发，严州淳安人。其地皆山也，山多猛兽。元发父自城晚归，中涂一熊突出攫之，仆焉。同伴者狂奔而免，纠众还救之，熊始去，而元发父碎首刳腹死矣。奔告其家，时元发甫弱冠，日持匕首哭父死所，欲得熊而甘心焉，或数夕不归。母匿其刃，禁不使出。元发哭愈哀，月余复窃刃而逃，村人遍寻之不得，自是踪迹杳然矣。而数十里内，山中居者恒隐隐闻哭声，或夜静，闻霍霍磨刀声。去其乡五十里，有地名叶家坂，居人以猎为业。一日入山，见一兽，人面而兽身，以敝衣蔽体。众异焉，发火枪击之，不中，兽奔，众逐之。兽呼曰：“吾童元发也，勿伤我。”众人素知其名，呼与俱归，元发腾跃而去，捷于飞隼，俄顷不知所往。于是远近皆知元发不死，且喧传其异矣。元发母闻其事，思念甚切。

    Explanation

    272

    Tong Yuanfa, a native of Chun'an, Yanzhou, lived in an area entirely covered by mountains, which were full of fierce beasts. One day, Tong Yuanfa's father returned late from the city. In the middle of the road, a bear suddenly leaped out and seized him, causing him to fall. His companions fled in panic and escaped, but they gathered the crowd to come back and save him. The bear finally left, but Tong Yuanfa's father was killed, his head crushed and his abdomen cut open. Tong Yuanfa's father ran to tell his family. At the time, Tong Yuanfa was just past his twentieth birthday. He daily held a dagger and cried at the place where his father died, wishing to obtain the bear and satisfy his vengeance. Sometimes, he stayed out all night. His mother hid his dagger and forbade him from going out. Tong Yuanfa cried even more bitterly. After more than a month, he secretly took his dagger and fled. The villagers searched everywhere but could not find him. From then on, his traces disappeared. Within dozens of miles, those living in the mountains always faintly heard crying sounds, and sometimes at night, they heard the sound of grinding a knife. Fifty miles away from his hometown, there is a place called Yehaiban, where the residents make a living by hunting. One day, they went into the mountains and saw an animal with a human face and a beast's body, wearing tattered clothes to cover its body. Everyone was amazed and fired a gun at it, but it did not hit. The beast ran, and everyone chased after it. The beast called out, "I am Tong Yuanfa, do not harm me." Everyone knew his name and called out for him to come back together. Tong Yuanfa leaped away, faster than a falcon, and in a moment, he was nowhere to be seen. Thus, far and wide, everyone knew that Tong Yuanfa was not dead, and it was widely spread that he was extraordinary. Tong Yuanfa's mother heard of the matter and thought of him very deeply.

    273

    一夕，忽闻扣门声，启之，则元发闯然入，曰：“儿今得报父雠矣！”气咻咻喘不止，汗淋漓如雨，肩一物掷地，腥臭不可近，烛之，熊也。母惊喜，邻舍毕集。时元发去家已一年余矣，问其所历，曰：“自入山后，日伏岩穴中。饥则采果实，或掘黄精白术食之，寒则集槲叶松毛为衣。数月后，觉身体轻捷，且生毛，如猿猱然，逾坑越谷，无异平地。日夕祷于山神，愿报父仇。昨宿枯庙中，梦神告曰：”杀而父者，去此不远，东行十余里，沿涧伺之，可得也。

    Explanation

    273

    One night, there was a sudden knock at the door. When it was opened, Yuan Fa rushed in, saying, "I have avenged my father's death today!" He was breathing heavily and could not stop, sweating profusely like rain. He shouldered an object and threw it to the ground, which had a strong, unpleasant smell. When it was lit, it turned out to be a bear. The mother was surprised and delighted, and the neighbors all gathered. At that time, Yuan Fa had been away from home for more than a year. When asked about his experiences, he said, "Since entering the mountains, I hid in caves and rock crevices during the day. When hungry, I picked fruits, or dug up yellow and white atractylodes to eat. When cold, I collected oak leaves and pine needles to make clothes. After several months, I felt my body became light and agile, and grew fur like a monkey. I leaped over pits and valleys as if they were flat ground. Every day and night, I prayed to the mountain god, wishing to avenge my father's death. Last night, I stayed in a dilapidated temple and dreamed that a god told me: 'The one who killed your father is not far from here. Go east for more than ten miles, follow the stream, and you can find him.'"

    274

    “如其言，果见熊饮于涧。事刂刃其腹，应手而毙，遂负之归。”闻者莫不叹异。翼日，熟而祭于其父之墓，并具牲体，酬神于山。嗣后饮食衣服仍复其旧，身亦重坠，与常人无异，惟遍体之毛，竟不脱落。余门下士王梦薇曾于同治十一年见之淳安市上。其人颀而长，年可三十许，肌理黧黑，两颧毛毵毵然，视其手臂亦然。人皆曰：“此童孝子也。”惟神识不甚慧，问之多不答，如聋者。识者谓积惨伤其心也。粤寇之难，近村多被焚掠，而童孝子一村独无恙。

    Explanation

    274

    As he had said, they indeed saw a bear drinking in the stream. They stabbed its belly with a blade, and it died instantly upon being struck. They then carried it back. All who heard this could not help but marvel. The next day, they cooked it and sacrificed it at his father's grave, along with the meat, offering it to the gods on the mountain. Thereafter, his diet and clothing returned to their former state, his body also became heavy, and he was no different from ordinary people, except that the hair all over his body did not fall out. My disciple Wang Mengwei saw him in Chun'an Market in the eleventh year of Tongzhi. He was tall and slender, around thirty years old, with dark and rough skin. The hair on his cheeks was long and sparse, and looking at his arms, it was the same. Everyone said, "This is the filial son." But his spiritual awareness was not very sharp; when asked, he often did not answer, like a deaf mute. Those who know say that accumulated suffering has injured his heart. The difficulty caused by the Guangxi bandits saw nearby villages mostly burned and looted, but the village of the filial son remained unharmed.

    275

    吴江梅堰镇有殷姓者，贾人子也。畜一鹦鹉，五六年矣，爱之如子。咸丰十年四月，粤寇突至，殷举家奔窜。半途忽忆鹦鹉，复还至家中，谓鸟曰：“寇至矣，不能终畜汝，汝速去，勿罹此祸。”遂开锁纵之。鸟去而寇至，几不免。后岁余，殷徙居莺湖之计家港。一日，鸟忽至，飞鸣于室，若有所言。殷不悟，饲之不食，笼之不宿，至夜不去，啁啾如故。俄闻炮声，始悟贼将至，鸟速之去也。即挟眷属狂奔，而贼已四面合围，不得脱。天迟明，潜出，欲越桥北走，而寇伏于桥侧，群起遮拦。忽有一鸟翱翔其间，贼注目视之，鹦鹉也，口衔白布一幅。咸共骇异，疑为神，逡巡久之，而殷氏之众已从桥上去。贼因鹦鹉之异，亦不穷追。殷至光绪中尚在。

    Explanation

    275
    In the town of Meiyantun, Wuxian, there lived a man surnamed Yin, the son of a merchant. He kept a parrot for five or six years, loving it as if it were his own child. In the fourth month of the tenth year of the Xianfeng era (1860), the Yue rebels suddenly arrived, and Yin's entire family fled in panic. Halfway through their escape, he suddenly remembered the parrot and returned home to tell it, "The rebels have come. I cannot keep you any longer. Go quickly, and do not suffer this calamity." He then unlocked the cage and let it go. The parrot left just as the rebels arrived, and they almost did not escape. After a year or so, Yin moved to Jikajiang near Yinghu. One day, the parrot suddenly returned, flying and chirping in the room, as if saying something. Yin did not understand, and despite feeding it, it would not eat, nor would it stay in the cage overnight, continuing to chirp as before. Soon, he heard the sound of cannons and realized that the parrot was urging them to leave. They immediately fled with their family, but the rebels had already surrounded them on all sides, making it impossible to escape. Just before dawn, they came out secretly, intending to cross the bridge and head north, but the rebels were hiding by the side of the bridge, rising up to block them. Suddenly, a bird soared in between, and the rebels looked at it, seeing that it was the parrot, holding a piece of white cloth in its beak. All were shocked and amazed, suspecting it to be a deity. They paused for a long time, and by then, the people of Yin had already crossed the bridge. The rebels, due to the unusual behavior of the parrot, did not pursue them further. Yin was still alive during the Guangxu period.
    276

    周如三，浙江山阴人，卖药为业。尝与村人采药王山，山有涧，狭而深，两旁石排列如矛戟，止容一人入，而黄精紫参生其中。周解衣使同伴者缒而下，有所得，公焉。其同伴有赵某者，见周衣巾藏白金十余两，利之，乃怀其金，与众俱走。已而周欲出，呼其曹，莫之应，窘而大号，亦无闻者。不得已，缘涧行。涧甚纡曲，广狭靡定。行十里许，得一洞，外窄而内宽；窥之，若有光。入之，则有一蛇存焉，长四五尺，围可五寸，鳞甲陆离，形状颇异。悸而欲出，已为蛇所见，因跪而告以故，并求寄宿焉。蛇若颔之者，周遂匍匐入，伏其侧。洞中山气薰蒸，不雨而滴，又昏暗无天日，不辨旦暮。

    Explanation

    276

    Zhou Rushi, a man from Shanyin, Zhejiang, made his living by selling herbs. Once, he went with fellow villagers to gather herbs on Wang Mountain. The mountain had a narrow and deep ravine, with stones lining the sides like spears and halberds, allowing only one person to pass through. Within this ravine grew herbs like Huangjing and Zicen. Zhou took off his clothes and had his companions lower themselves down with a rope to collect the herbs, sharing whatever they found openly. Among his companions was someone named Zhao, who saw that Zhou had hidden several ounces of silver in his clothes and found it profitable to hide the gold, then ran away with the group. Later, when Zhou wanted to leave, he called out to his companions, but no one responded. In distress, he shouted loudly, but no one heard him. Out of necessity, he followed the watercourse. The ravine was very winding and varied in width. After walking about ten miles, he found a cave that was narrow outside but wide inside. Looking into it, he saw a faint light. Entering the cave, he found a snake inside, about four or five feet long and with a circumference of about five inches. The snake had colorful and varied scales and a quite different shape. Fearful, Zhou wanted to leave, but the snake had already seen him. He knelt down and told the reason, asking for temporary lodging. The snake seemed to nod in agreement, and Zhou then crawled into the cave, crouching beside the snake. The cave was steamy and dripping with moisture, dark and dim, making it impossible to distinguish day from night.

    277

    久之饥甚，见洞有一石，光滑如脂，蛇恒以舌饣舌之。意其可以疗饥，又跪而祝曰：“小人不食三日矣，愿分君之甘。”蛇又若颔之者，因亦就饣舌之。石淡无味，然饥火顿息。如是数日，忽闻雷声殷殷，在山之巅。蛇闻之，蠕蠕然动，未几暴长，头角峥嵘，不蛇而龙矣，腾跃欲上。周攀其角曰：“龙王一出，某老死洞中矣。愿从龙王偕出。”蛇又若颔之者。辟历一声，挟周俱上，俄而坠于地，则其村也。乃反其家。家人喧相告曰：“吾以汝为死矣。”周曰：“谁言之？”曰：“闻诸赵。”周欲诣问赵，而赵已至，披发跣足，奉衣及金跪于门外，自述前意。问：“谁使汝来？又谁使汝言之？”则赵亦茫然不知也。

    Explanation

    277

    After a long time, Zhou was extremely hungry and saw a smooth stone in a cave, which looked like fat. The snake always licked it with its tongue. Zhou thought it might be able to cure his hunger and knelt down, saying, "I haven't eaten for three days, I wish to share your deliciousness." The snake seemed to nod, so Zhou licked it too. The stone had no taste, but the hunger was suddenly relieved. This went on for several days. Suddenly, Zhou heard a loud rumbling thunder at the top of the mountain. The snake heard it and moved slowly, soon growing rapidly, with prominent horns and scales, transforming from a snake into a dragon, leaping to ascend. Zhou grabbed its horns and said, "If the Dragon King comes out, I will die in this cave. I wish to go out with the Dragon King." The snake seemed to nod. A thunderbolt struck, carrying Zhou and the dragon up, and they soon fell to the ground, which turned out to be his village. Zhou returned home. His family excitedly told him, "We thought you were dead." Zhou said, "Who said that?" They replied, "We heard it from Zhao." Zhou wanted to go and ask Zhao, but Zhao had already arrived, barefoot and with loose hair, presenting clothes and money at the door, recounting his previous intentions. Zhou asked, "Who sent you here? And who told you to say that?" Zhao was also clueless about it.

    278

    ●卷六湖北咸宁有某氏女，颇娟好。粤寇之乱，有贼数人，突入其家，见女艳之，欲劫之去。其父母村农也，呆痴不晓事，谓贼曰：“吾女已许嫁某村某氏子矣，安能从汝？”贼曰：“易耳。吾往某村杀某氏子，则尔女可从我矣。”父母曰：“虽杀某氏子，亦不汝从！”贼曰：“然则并汝二人杀之，尔女可从我矣。”女见事急，从容谓父母曰：“吾父母何老悖如此！我得嫁将军，异日为妃为后，未可知也，岂恋此田舍郎哉！”又谓贼曰：“我从将军去，父母老悖不足与语。”父母牵女衣而哭，女挥手曰：“儿此去大好，何用悲苦。然父母宜即日他徙，不然地方官府知儿从贼，必以为父母罪矣。”言已，从贼竟去。

    Explanation

    278

    In Hubei Province, there was a woman from a certain family in Xianning County, who was quite beautiful. During the turmoil of the Taiping Rebellion, several bandits suddenly entered her home, saw her and found her attractive, and wanted to take her away by force. Her parents were simple farmers who were stupid and ignorant, and they told the bandits, "Our daughter has already been promised to a son from a certain village, how could she follow you?" The bandits replied, "It's easy. I will go to a certain village and kill the son of a certain person, then your daughter can follow me." The parents said, "Even if you kill the son of a certain person, we will not let you take our daughter!" The bandits said, "Then we will kill both of you, and your daughter can follow me." The girl, seeing the urgency of the situation, calmly told her parents, "Why are my parents so old and foolish! I might become a concubine or a queen if I marry a general, who knows? How could I be attached to this peasant!" She then told the bandits, "I will follow the general, my parents are too old and foolish to talk to." The parents held onto her clothes and cried, but the girl waved her hand and said, "It's great for me to go with the bandits, why be sad. However, my parents should move away immediately, otherwise the local officials will know that I followed the bandits, and they will certainly blame my parents." After saying this, she followed the bandits and left.

    279

    甫行数里，又谓贼曰：“吾固许嫁某氏子，今从将军去，异日某氏必向父母索女，且为父母累。幸此地距某村近，宜往与诀绝。”贼从之。女平时固常至婿家，且为其姑所怜爱。乃是乃径诣其姑告别，且谓之曰：“舅姑宜即日他徙，不然地方官府知儿从贼，必以为舅姑罪矣。”其姑闻之泣，女不顾而行。贼欲犯之，辄好语之曰：“我固从汝，然须成礼，不可苟合。”行一二日，贼又欲犯之。女度父母舅姑皆已他徙，乃抗声大骂曰：“狗奴！我良家女，且已许嫁某氏子，岂从汝辈犬彘哉！”贼见其意骤变，大骇，尚以其貌美，不忍杀，以甘言劝慰。女骂益厉，自以头触贼刃，遂见害。此事余大儿妇言之，以不记其姓氏为憾。余曰是无伤也，《明史。列女传》所载，如招远孝女、石门丐妇、上海某氏、六安女之类，皆不详其姓氏，至今何尝不炳然史策哉！

    Explanation

    279

    She had just traveled a few miles when she again addressed the bandit leader, saying, "I had already agreed to marry a man from a certain family, but now I am following you. In the future, that family will surely come to their parents and demand their daughter, which will be a burden on them. Fortunately, this place is close to a certain village, so it would be best if I went there to say my final goodbyes." The bandit leader agreed. The woman usually visited her fiancé's family and was well-loved by her mother-in-law. She went directly to her mother-in-law to say goodbye, telling her, "You should immediately move to another place; otherwise, the local authorities will find out that I have followed the bandits and will hold you responsible." Her mother-in-law wept upon hearing this, but the woman continued on her way without looking back. The bandit leader attempted to assault her, but she spoke gently to him, saying, "I have agreed to follow you, but we must first complete the proper rites; we cannot act rashly." After traveling for a day or two, the bandit leader tried to assault her again. The woman, believing that her parents and in-laws had already moved, stood up and shouted angrily, "You dogs! I am a respectable woman, and I have already agreed to marry a man from a certain family. How could I follow you, you swine!" The bandit leader was shocked by her sudden change of heart and, despite her beauty, did not want to kill her. He tried to pacify her with kind words. The woman's curses grew even louder, and she struck the bandit leader with her head, resulting in her death. This story was recounted by my eldest daughter-in-law, and she regretted not knowing the woman's name. I told her that it was not a loss, as the Ming Shi (History of the Ming Dynasty)'s Biographies of Exemplary Women records many such stories, such as the filial daughter of Zhaoyuan, the beggar woman of Shimen, a woman from Shanghai, and a woman from Liuan, none of whom are given their names. Yet, how brightly their deeds shine in the annals of history!

    280

    苏州桃花坞有缸甏阿庆者（“甏”，俗字，古无之，《集韵》有“&lt；平瓦&gt；”字，蒲孟切，音膨，&lt；亢瓦&gt；属。或即此字），多膂力，乡里咸惮之。有某甲与之忤，庆纠其党，欲殴之。甲惧，奔告于庆之妻父，其妻父曰：“是不可以理谕也，汝谨避之而已。”甲匿数月，偶出，遇庆于途，痛殴之。甲愤且愧，归则吞生鸦片烟而死，乡里莫敢为一言。久之，阿庆亦死。其子曰二和尚，年长矣，家亦小康。

    Explanation

    280

    In Suzhou's Taohuawu, there was a man known as "Gangbeng Aqing" (a colloquial term for a person, possibly indicating his occupation or physical characteristic). He had great strength, and everyone in the village feared him. There was a man named "Majia" who offended him, and Aqing gathered his followers, intending to beat him. Frightened, Majia rushed to report to Aqing's father-in-law, who said, "He cannot be reasoned with; just avoid him carefully." Majia hid for several months. One day, he accidentally went out and met Aqing on the road. Aqing severely beat him. Majia, filled with both anger and shame, returned home and swallowed raw opium, then died. No one in the village dared to say a word. After a long time, Aqing also died. His son, named "Erhe," had grown up, and his family was also relatively well-off.

    281

    一日，自市上晚归，遇甲于涂，呼曰：“汝父杀余，余今且杀汝！”和尚大骇，狂奔归家，遂大病，述所见，家人大具牲醴以祭之。久之，病始愈。逾年，又遇诸涂，归又大病，家人为焚纸钱无算，久之，病亦愈。而和尚以久病，遂失业，家亦落。一日，又遇诸涂，和尚习见之，亦不畏也。谓甲曰：“吾今非昔比矣，饔飧不继，生亦无味，顾安所得死？”甲曰：“何不食生鸦片烟？”和尚诺之。至夜，谓家人曰：“某甲教我以御贫之计，吾从其教，已食生雅片烟矣。”家人方惊愕，而和尚忽跳掷号呼，移时竟死。

    Explanation

    281

    One day, after returning home from the market in the evening, the monk met someone named Jia on the road. Jia called out, "Your father killed me, and I will now kill you!" The monk was greatly frightened and rushed home in panic, and fell seriously ill. He described what he saw, and the family prepared offerings of meat and wine to worship him. After a while, the illness began to subside. After a year, he met Jia again on the road, returned home, and fell seriously ill again. The family burned countless paper money, and the illness also subsided. But because of his long illness, the monk lost his job, and his family also declined. One day, he met Jia again on the road. Having become accustomed to it, the monk no longer feared him. He told Jia, "I am no longer the same as before; I lack food, life has lost its flavor, but where can I find death?" Jia said, "Why don't you eat raw opium?" The monk agreed. By night, he told his family, "Jia taught me a way to deal with poverty, I followed his advice, and I have already eaten raw opium." The family was just shocked, but the monk suddenly jumped and shouted, and after a while, he died.

    282

    门人王梦薇言：其乡先达有观察仲公者，微时读书于蒯氏之园。一夜，闻池中澎然有声，俄一人推扉入，面色微，有须，向公长跪。问何人，则叩首曰：“公勿怖，我乃鬼也。生前为蒯氏仆，失足死池中。冥司怜我无罪而死，且沉沦三载，不求替代，将保我为土地神。然仍须阳间贵人一保状，故敬来求公也。”公笑曰：“我一介寒儒，且阴阳路隔。何能为！”鬼苦求不已，公乃取名纸书一“保”字付之，欣然叩谢而去。次日，问诸蒯氏，果有此仆云。后公以编修视学江西，携眷属乘舟赴任，守风，泊一山下，见士女往来如蚁。

    Explanation

    282

    A student named Wang Mengwei said: In his hometown, there was a respected scholar named Zong Gong, who was an observer. When he was poor, he was reading in Kuai's garden. One night, he heard a loud noise in the pond. Soon after, a man pushed open the door and entered. The man had a pale complexion and a long beard, and he knelt before Zong Gong. When asked who he was, the man bowed and said, "Sir, do not be afraid. I am a ghost. In life, I was a servant of the Kuai family and died by falling into the pond. The underworld has taken pity on me because I died without sin, and I have been in the underworld for three years without asking for a replacement. They will make me a land god, but I still need a guarantee from a respected person in the world of the living. Therefore, I respectfully come to ask you." Zong Gong laughed and said, "I am just a poor scholar, and the boundary between the living and the dead is vast. How could I help you?" The ghost persistently begged, and Zong Gong finally took a piece of paper, wrote the character "guarantee" on it, and gave it to the ghost. The ghost happily bowed and thanked him before leaving. The next day, when Zong Gong asked the Kuai family, they confirmed that such a servant had indeed existed. Later, Zong Gong was appointed as an inspector of education in Jiangxi and traveled by boat with his family to take up his post. While waiting out a storm, he anchored at the foot of a mountain and saw crowds of men and women moving about like ants.

    283

    问其故，曰：“此山土地神素灵异，今值生辰，故数十里内咸来瞻拜，此年年一盛会也。”公有子才十龄，闻之欲往，乃使数仆负之上山。至则笙歌鼎沸，百戏杂陈，观者环立如堵墙。诸健仆拥公子直至神前，公子忽仆，口流白沫，呼之不应，掖之不起。仆知干神怒，急叩首谢，无益。众人曰：“是非其父母来不可。”仆急归，述于公。公不得已，具香烛与夫人登山。既至庙中，仰瞻神像，宛然蒯氏仆也。乃曰：“子非数年以前求吾作保者乎？今得志，何漠然无故旧情也？且孺子无知，有何干犯，而凌虐至此。其平日妄作威福可知，余误保矣。”言已，举手批其颊，神首应手而坠，公子顿苏。众愕然，公为述其故，自是香火遂绝。

    Explanation

    283

    The reason was asked, and it was said, "This mountain's land god is usually mysterious and extraordinary. Now that it is the god's birthday, therefore within dozens of miles, everyone comes to worship. This is an annual grand gathering." The official had a son who was ten years old, and upon hearing this, he wanted to go. So, he sent several servants to carry him up the mountain. Upon arrival, the sound of flutes and songs was like a boiling pot, and all kinds of performances were displayed. The spectators stood in a circle like a wall. The strong servants pushed the young master up to the god's altar. Suddenly, the young master fell, foaming at the mouth, unresponsive when called, and unable to be lifted up. The servant knew they had angered the god, quickly kowtowed to apologize, but it was of no use. The crowd said, "Only the parents can come." The servant hurried back and reported to the official. The official, having no choice, prepared incense and candles and went up the mountain with his wife. Upon reaching the temple, looking up at the god's image, it was clearly the servant of the Kuai family. He then said, "Aren't you the one who asked me to be a guarantor several years ago? Now that you have achieved your goals, why are you so indifferent and lack old friendship? Moreover, the child is innocent, how could he have done anything wrong to deserve such abuse? It is clear that you have been arbitrarily exercising power and authority. I was mistaken in being a guarantor." After saying this, he raised his hand and slapped the god's cheek, and the god's head fell off with the slap. The young master suddenly recovered. The crowd was shocked. The official explained the reason. From then on, the worship ceased.

    284

    苏州珠明寺有痴和尚者，蓬头垢面，嬉笑无度，其状类痴人，因以为号也。常数日不食，或一日而食数日之食。冬夏衣一短布衲，不易亦不敝。畜一黑犬，跬步不离。每晨必出城，登枫桥，向西方呼吸良久而返。市中果饵鲑菜，任意攫食，食毕，纳其余于袖中。凡经其攫食者，是日利市三倍，故人皆乐之，不责直，亦从不予直也。若强之食，则必如直而偿，或却之，则投其钱而去。

    Explanation

    284

    In the Suzhou Zhuming Temple, there was a monk known as the Foolish Monk. He had unkempt hair and a dirty face, and he laughed and joked without restraint. His appearance resembled that of a fool, which is why he was given this nickname. He often went several days without eating, or he would eat enough food to last several days in one sitting. In both winter and summer, he wore a single short robe made of coarse cloth, which neither deteriorated nor became worn out. He kept a black dog that never left his side. Every morning, he would definitely leave the temple, climb up Feng Bridge, and breathe deeply toward the West for a long time before returning. In the market, he would eat whatever snacks and foods were available at will, and after finishing, he would put the rest in his sleeve. All those who had been eaten by him experienced three times the usual profit on that day, so people all liked him. He did not demand payment, nor did he accept any payment. If someone forced him to eat, he would pay the equivalent value, but if they refused, he would throw the money away and leave.

    285

    好与儿童嬉，袖中物辄分给之，亦有索之而不与者，莫测其意也。又能医人，有病者招之辄往，或不往，则病不治矣。有陆某病瘵，群医束手，乃延之诊治，比至，已死矣。和尚熟视大笑，急索笔，书一方云：“泰山石一片，蟠桃仁二十粒，扶桑木一株，用黄河水煎。”众难之，和尚又大笑，索火焚之，以其灰和茶灌死者口中，须臾即活，病若失。其医他人，用药悉类此，皆烧灰和水饮之也。一日，至日失不起。寺僧异之，窥其户，异香满室，入视，则已圆寂矣。舁之，轻如蝉翼。其所畜黑犬寻亦死。

    Explanation

    285

    The monk liked to play with children, readily giving away things from his sleeves, and there were times when he would not give something even when asked, leaving his intentions a mystery. He was also skilled in healing people; when someone was ill and called for him, he would go, but if he did not go, the illness would not be treated. There was a man named Lu who had consumption, and all the doctors were at a loss. They then invited the monk to diagnose and treat him, but by the time he arrived, Lu was already dead. The monk looked at him carefully and laughed, then quickly fetched a pen and wrote a prescription, saying: "A piece of Tai Mountain stone, twenty kernels of the immortal peach, one tree of the sun tree, boiled with Yellow River water." Everyone found it difficult to believe, and the monk laughed again, asking for fire to burn the prescription. He mixed the ash with tea and poured it into the dead man's mouth. In a moment, Lu came back to life, and the illness seemed to vanish. When the monk treated others, he used medicines like this, always burning the medicines into ash and mixing them with water to drink. One day, he lay down and did not rise. The monks in the temple found it strange, and when they peered through the door, a strange fragrance filled the room. When they entered to look, he had already achieved nirvana. When they carried him, he was as light as a cicada's wing. The black dog he had raised also died soon after.

    286

    俗谓悬物曰吊，汤临川《牡丹亭》曲曰：“高吊起文章钜公。”则明人已然矣。因而以缢死为吊死，其字实当为“[C023]”。《玉篇》了部：[C023]，丁了切，悬物貌。丁了切，其音如鸟，与吊略殊，然亦一声之转。《广韵》上声有茑字，都了切，读如鸟，去声。有茑字，多啸切，读如吊。然则了亦可读如吊矣。相沿既久，遂莫能改。河南省城有所谓老吊爷者，缢死鬼也。其人姓张，名子和，生时以卖布为业。一日，负布数匹，售之于市，为贼所窃，愤而缢死。死后颇著灵异，县中捕役奉以为神，尊之曰老吊爷，为之立庙。凡捕盗贼不得，得祷之，辄有应。其始惟详符县有庙，后中牟县捕役祷而应，因亦立庙。

    Explanation

    286

    Commonly, hanging things is called "to hang" or "to dangle." In Tang Xianzu's play "The Peony Pavilion," it says, "To hang high, bringing forth great scholars." This was already the case in the Ming Dynasty. Therefore, hanging to death became known as "hanging to death," and the correct character should be "吊" (hanging), though it is marked with "[C023]" to indicate uncertainty. The Yupian (Character Dictionary) in the "了" section states: "[C023]," pronounced "丁了切" (dīng le qiè), means "the appearance of hanging things." "丁了切" sounds like "bird" (鳥), which is slightly different from "吊" (hanging), but it is also a variant pronunciation. The Guangyun (Comprehensive Rhyme) has the character "茑" (niǎo) in the rising tone, pronounced "都了切" (dū le qiè), which sounds like "bird," and in the falling tone, pronounced "多啸切" (duō xiào qiè), which sounds like "hanging." Therefore, "了" can also be read as "hanging." Having been followed for a long time, it cannot be changed. In the capital city of Henan Province, there is a figure known as "Old Hanging Master," a ghost who hanged himself to death. His name was Zhang Zihe, and he sold cloth for a living. One day, he carried several bolts of cloth to the market, where they were stolen by a thief. Out of anger, he hanged himself. After his death, he became known for his supernatural abilities. The local police officers revered him as a deity and called him "Old Hanging Master," building a temple for him. Whenever they failed to catch thieves, they would pray to him, and he would often respond. Initially, there was only a temple in Xiangfu County. Later, the police in Zhongmou County also prayed to him and built a temple.

    287

    余亲家翁樊玉农观察曾宰中牟，其长女即余长子妇也，时尚幼，曾往观之。神像高才二尺许，立而不坐，手执雨伞，背负布数端，宛然一市井中人也。祥符有富民某姓者，生一儿，雇村妇乳之。妇负儿出游，俄失儿所在，大索不获。闻于官，官命役踪迹之，亦竟杳然。役祷于老吊爷，出而有鸦噪于前，若相导者，因祝之曰：“尔果神欤，请止。”鸦即止。又祝之曰：“尔果神欤，请飞。”鸦即飞。乃随之行，至城外一处，鸦集于地而噪。就其地发之，则儿尸存焉，断而为八矣，然莫知为何人所贼。祷于神，神亦不复指示。时村妇已去，久之，复入城言于其主曰：“杀儿者，即吾夫也。吾夫素无赖，频向我索钱，未满其欲，因促我归。我恋所乳儿，未忍言去。夫愤怒，乘我不觉，抱儿去，杀之也。

    Explanation

    287

    My father-in-law, Mr. Fan Yunaong, who served as the magistrate of Zhongmu, had an eldest daughter who became the wife of my eldest son. She was still young at the time, and I once went to see her. The statue of the deity stood about two feet tall, standing rather than sitting, holding an umbrella in one hand and carrying several pieces of cloth on his back, resembling a commoner from the market. In Xiangfu, there was a wealthy farmer of a certain surname who had a son. He hired a rural woman to nurse the child. The woman carried the child out for a walk, and suddenly lost track of him. A wide search was conducted but the child was not found. The matter was reported to the official, who ordered his servants to trace the child, but he still vanished without a trace. The servant prayed to the old deity, and when the deity appeared, crows chirped in front, as if guiding them. The servant then prayed, "If you are indeed a god, please stop." The crows stopped chirping. He prayed again, "If you are indeed a god, please fly." The crows then flew away. Following the crows, they arrived at a place outside the city, where the crows gathered on the ground and chirped. Digging at that spot, they found the child's body, which was cut into eight pieces, but no one knew who had killed him. Praying to the deity, the deity did not indicate anything further. By then, the rural woman had already left. After a long time, she returned to the city and told her master, "The one who killed my child is my husband. My husband is usually a rascal, and he frequently asked me for money, never satisfied. He urged me to return home. I loved the child I nursed and could not bear to say goodbye. My husband became angry and, taking advantage of my unawareness, took the child away and killed him."

    288

    ”于是官逮其夫至，讯之，如妇言，乃论如律。妇诚愚，岂懵然不知杀人之必偿其命欤？殆亦若有使之者矣。樊和气，湖北咸宁人，余大儿妇之再从兄女也。其父名希森，以财雄于乡。咸丰二年，粤寇至，希森全家迁居省城中。俄而贼围省城，时和气甫十三岁，父素所怜爱，谓之曰：“吾全家无脱理，汝年幼，可偕仆媪辈逃匿。吾止汝一女，无俱死也。”和气曰：“父言误矣。父母止我一女，祖父母不止父一子乎？父宜亡去，存先人血食。一家细弱，俱宜早死，免为父累。父乃命我逃匿，设不幸落贼手，人必曰‘樊氏女为贼所得矣’，此言父岂乐闻哉！”已而贼围益急，城且旦夕破，举家和药以待。及城陷，父将饮之，和气夺其药去，而谓其母曰：“此母与儿辈所宜服也，儿请先母尝之。

    Explanation

    288

    The officials arrested her husband and brought him, interrogated him, and he spoke as she had said, so they judged him according to the law. The woman was indeed foolish, but could she have been so oblivious as not to know that killing someone would result in retribution? Perhaps there was someone guiding her. Fan Heqi was from Xian Ning, Hubei, and she was the second cousin of my eldest daughter's husband. Her father was named Xi Shen and was wealthy and influential in the village. In the second year of the Xianfeng era, the Cantonese rebels arrived, and Xi Shen moved his entire family to the provincial capital. Soon the rebels surrounded the provincial capital, and at the time, Heqi was just thirteen years old. Her father, who always loved and pitied her, told her, "There is no way for my entire family to escape. You are young, so you can escape with the servants and maids. I have only one daughter, and we don't need to die together." Heqi replied, "Father, you are mistaken. Parents have only one daughter, but grandparents have more than one son. Father, you should leave and preserve the ancestral sacrifices. The whole family, including the weak, should die early to avoid being a burden to you. Father, you ordered me to escape and hide. If unfortunately, I fall into the hands of the rebels, people will say, 'The Fan daughter has been captured by the rebels.' Would father like to hear this?" Soon the rebel siege became more urgent, and the city was about to fall within days. The whole family prepared poison to drink. When the city fell, the father was about to drink it, but Heqi took the poison away from him and said to her mother, "This is what the mother and children should drink. I will let mother taste it first."

    289

    ”言已，即举药一引而尽，曰：“此药甘如饴耳。”诸妇女亦有饮者。俄闻贼已封刃，且传伪檄安民矣，乃各以药解之。诸妇女皆苏，惟和气最先饮，毒深不可救，竟死。时仓卒不得棺，乃纳其尸于橱中，而埋之屋后积灰之下。未几贼至其居，举家分散，各不能相顾。贼退，始先后归其家，男妇数十人皆幸无恙，独和气死耳。因复入城，至故所居，发积灰求之。其橱固在，启之，尸尚未腐，面色如生，乃具衣衾改敛之。其手足犹可屈伸，惟腰间若有微创，盖先时纳尸橱中，误伤之也。然人虽见之，虑其母伤悼，亦不以告。久之，和气见梦于其母曰：“儿本仙女，死仍为仙。今在杏花仙女处，甚安乐，惟腰间微觉痛楚，亦无大苦，母勿念也。”母觉以语人，不解腰痛之故，人始以所见告，益信梦之非妄矣。

    Explanation

    289

    After he had spoken, he immediately took a draught of the medicine and finished it, saying, "This medicine is as sweet as honey." Some of the women also drank it. Soon they heard that the bandits had sheathed their swords and had spread false proclamations to pacify the people, so they each took the medicine to counteract its effects. All the women revived, except for Heqi, who had drunk first and, due to the deep toxicity, could not be saved and died. At that time, there was no time to prepare a coffin, so they placed her body in a cabinet and buried it under the accumulated ashes behind the house. Shortly after, the bandits came to their residence, and the entire family was scattered, unable to look out for one another. When the bandits retreated, they began to return home one by one; dozens of men and women were fortunate to be unharmed, but only Heqi had died. They then returned to the city and went to their old residence, digging through the accumulated ashes to search for her. The cabinet was still there; when they opened it, her body had not yet decayed, and her face looked as if she were alive. They then prepared clothing and a shroud to rewrap her body. Her hands and feet could still be bent and stretched, but there was a slight wound on her waist, likely caused by the mistake of placing her body in the cabinet. Although people saw this, they feared that her mother would be deeply saddened and did not tell her. After some time, Heqi appeared in a dream to her mother, saying, "I was originally a fairy, and even in death, I remain one. Now I am with the Apricot Blossom Fairy, and I am very happy, though I feel a slight pain in my waist, which is not a great suffering. Do not worry about me, Mother." Her mother felt this and told others, but they did not understand the reason for the waist pain. Only then did people reveal what they had seen, and they came to believe that the dream was not false.

    290

    大儿妇樊氏言：其家庖人治一鳖，已以箸夹其头，将断之，忽其尾间又出一物如头然。庖人诧曰：“岂此鳖有两头欤？”强纳入之，复以箸夹其头，头出而尾间物亦出。庖人大怪之，乃曰：“吾熟尔于釜中，看尔有何怪异！”及熟而剖之，则中有一人焉，其状如老翁，须眉宛然，头戴风帽，身披氅衣，但不见其足耳。仆媪辈传观之，儿妇时尚幼，亦取视焉。虽已干腊，尚可把玩。或语庖人曰：“此鳖宝也，生得而畜之，则可以尽得天下之宝矣。”庖人乃大悔。

    Explanation

    290

    The Eldest Son's Wife, Lady Fan, said: One day, the cook in her household was preparing a turtle. He had already used chopsticks to hold the turtle's head, ready to sever it, when suddenly another object resembling a head emerged from the turtle's tail. The cook exclaimed in surprise, "Could this turtle have two heads?" He forcefully pushed the second head back into the turtle and held the turtle's head again with chopsticks. But as soon as he did, both heads emerged simultaneously. The cook was greatly astonished and said, "I will cook you thoroughly in the pot and see what other strange things you might reveal!" When the turtle was cooked and cut open, there was a person inside. The person looked like an old man, with clear beard and eyebrows, wearing a wind hat and a cloak, but his feet were not visible. The servants and maids passed the turtle around to look at it, and the young daughter-in-law also took a look. Although the turtle had already been dried and preserved, it could still be handled and admired. Someone told the cook, "This turtle is a treasure. If you catch it alive and keep it, you can obtain all the treasures in the world!" The cook then deeply regretted his actions.

    291

    高君者，与临平周氏有连，余昔寓临平，屡见之。人以其行七，呼为七先生，忘其名矣。喜食鳢鱼，每使其子妇手治之。一日，其子妇治一鳢，未奏刀，而鳞间血溢出，诧曰：“此鳢有异，吾未之见也。”及熟而进之，以其异告，劝翁勿食。高殊不之信，笑曰：“鱼必有血，此何足异？”竟食之。是日遂死。按鳢鱼首有七星，亦名七星鱼。道家以雁为天厌，犬为地厌，鳢为水厌，是为三厌。高君喜食鳢，卒以陨命，可不戒欤！

    Explanation

    291

    Mr. Gao, who had connections with the Zhou family of Linping, I often saw him when I lived in Linping. Because he was the seventh in his generation, he was called Mr. Seven, and I have forgotten his name. He loved eating eels and often had his daughter-in-law prepare them by hand. One day, his daughter-in-law was preparing an eel, and before she could cut it, blood oozed out between the scales. She was surprised and said, "This eel is strange; I have never seen this before." When it was cooked and served, she told her father-in-law about the strangeness and advised him not to eat it. Mr. Gao did not believe it at all and laughed, saying, "Fish must have blood; what is so strange about this?" He ended up eating it anyway. That day he died.

    According to records, the eel has seven stars on its head and is also called the Seven-Star Fish. Taoists consider the goose as the celestial deterrent, the dog as the terrestrial deterrent, and the eel as the aquatic deterrent, making them the three deterrents. Mr. Gao loved eating eels and ultimately lost his life because of it; isn't this a warning?

    292

    苏州阊门内有屋一区，浙人唐氏僦以鬻缯帛。余妻昆弟之子姚谷孙与其事。一夕，与同业某氏子夜话颇久，觉有饥意，乃携鸡卵数枚欲就厨下熟而食之。甫出户，而所执之烛骤灭。是屋素著怪异，谷孙习之，亦不畏也，笑曰：“岂其鬼欤？”呼某氏子复以烛来，及至厨下，而烛又灭。谷孙曰：“汝能灭之，我不能然之欤？”乃与某氏子各执一烛，且各藏火种，此灭则彼然，彼灭则此然，屡灭屡然，竟煮熟鸡卵，食之而返。又小仓口一屋，亦有怪异。

    Explanation

    292

    Inside the Chongmen Gate in Suzhou, there was a house area leased by a Zhejiang person named Tang to sell silk and cloth. My wife's brother's son, Yao Gusu, was involved in this business. One night, he spoke for a long time at night with a son of a certain person from the same trade. Feeling hungry, he carried several chicken eggs to cook and eat them in the kitchen. As soon as he stepped out the door, the candle he held suddenly went out. This house was known for its strange occurrences, and Yao Gusu was accustomed to it and did not fear it. He laughed and said, "Could it be a ghost?" He called the son of the certain person to bring a candle. When they reached the kitchen, the candle went out again. Yao Gusu said, "If you can extinguish it, can I not light it?" So, he and the son of the certain person each held a candle and kept a fire source. When one went out, the other lit up, and vice versa. They repeatedly went out and lit up, finally cooking the chicken eggs, ate them, and returned. There is also a house at Xiaocangkou with similar strange occurrences.

    293

    余尝僦居之。余长子绍莱与女婿许子原同卧一室中，夜深不寐，偶思得食。是日适有祀神之鸡，虽已启毛取血，未熟也。乃就窗外短墙上一小火炉，置铛于上，入鸡其中，敲石取火，束薪蒸而然之。两人皆不习爨烹，久之不，而意亦倦，抽薪灭火，仍归就枕。俄闻窗外有扇声，又有薪柴爆裂声。虑火未尽灭，或致上炎，偕出视之，则炉内初无一星之焰，而铛中已鬻然沸矣。发而观焉，鸡已烂熟可食。初不知其所由，姑取而饱食，竟无他异。余谓此必狐魅为之，非鬼也。

    Explanation

    293

    I once rented a residence there. My eldest son, Shao Lai, and my son-in-law, Xu Zi Yuan, shared a room. One night, they were unable to sleep and suddenly thought of something to eat. On that day, there happened to be a chicken used in a religious sacrifice, which had already been plucked and bled but was not yet cooked. They went to a small stove on the short wall outside the window, placed a pot on it, put the chicken inside, struck a stone to get a fire, bundled the firewood and ignited it. Both of them were not skilled in cooking, and after a long time, they were still unable to cook it, and their minds became tired. They removed the firewood and extinguished the fire, then returned to bed. Soon after, they heard a fan-like sound outside the window, followed by the sound of firewood cracking. They were concerned that the fire might not have been completely extinguished and could cause an updraft, so they went out to check. They found that there was no trace of flame inside the stove, but the water in the pot was boiling vigorously. They lifted the lid and saw that the chicken was thoroughly cooked and ready to eat. At first, they did not know the reason for this, so they took it and ate it, and there was no other unusual occurrence. I said that this must have been done by a fox spirit, not a ghost.

    294

    河南中牟县民间一女子，生而两目与人异。其瞳子旁有白痕一线围之，自幼能见神鬼。甫能言，即言空中某神人过，某仙人过。人虽不之信，然以某神某仙之名非童犀所能知，亦颇异之也。五六岁时，即能为人医病，久之其名大盛，延请之者无虚日。其治病也，不切脉处方，随意以一草一果食之。或使人入市买药物少许，所买药皆人所常用之品，且所值不过一二十钱，而病人服之，无不瘳者。一时哄然，以为神医。然不受谢，或以食物遗其父母，少则受之，多亦不受也。自言不能过十八岁，如期果无疾而卒。其人盖在道光初年，惜谈者失其姓氏也。

    Explanation

    294

    In Zhongmou County, Henan Province, there was a woman born with a unique condition: her pupils were surrounded by a white trace. From a young age, she could see gods and ghosts. As soon as she could speak, she would mention that a certain deity or immortal had passed by. Although people did not fully believe her, they were also amazed that she could name specific deities and immortals, which a child her age would not know. When she was five or six years old, she began to heal people. Over time, her reputation grew, and she was constantly invited to treat patients. She did not diagnose or prescribe medicine; instead, she would eat a random herb or fruit, or she would send someone to buy a small amount of medicine, which were common and inexpensive items. Despite this, the patients who took the medicine always recovered. People began to consider her a miraculous healer. However, she did not accept payment; sometimes she would accept food left by her parents, but she would not accept more than a little. She claimed that she could not live past eighteen years, and indeed, she died peacefully without any apparent illness at that age. This woman lived during the early years of the Daoguang reign, but unfortunately, her name has been lost to history.

    295

    湖北有某媪者，乳医也，俗谓之收生婆，其始亦与他媪等。一日，偶于田间拾得死人手一，携归供奉之，嗣后其术益神。凡有召之者，或即时而往，或迟之又久而往。其至也，必适届其妇产时，未尝早至以待，亦未尝有不及也。一入其门，即知所生之为男为女，百不一爽。亦有呼之而不至者，则此妇必危矣。所至不索重酬，然竟以此起家。年八十余而卒。其晚年不轻为人收生，有难产者召之，犹时为一往。

    Explanation

    295

    A certain woman from Hubei, a midwife, commonly known as a "midwife," was initially no different from other women. One day, she happened to pick up a dead man's hand in the fields and took it home to worship it. Afterward, her skills became even more miraculous. Whenever she was summoned, she would either go immediately or wait for a long time before going. When she arrived, it was always the exact time for the woman to give birth; she never arrived too early or too late. As soon as she entered the house, she knew whether the baby would be a boy or a girl, without a single mistake. Sometimes, when she was called but did not come, the woman was in danger. She did not demand heavy payment, yet she became wealthy because of it. She died at the age of over eighty. In her later years, she did not lightly accept requests to deliver babies, but if there was a difficult birth, she would still occasionally go.

    296

    楚人有梅姓者，官于中州。其妻将产，使人召收生媪，媪至，见一妇待于中堂，服饰甚艳，戴花满头，责媪曰：“来何迟也！人家何等大事，汝乃迟滞如此乎！”媪惶悚谢过，乃命入房视产妇。而妇产颇不易，久之，势甚危殆，媪曰：“事至此，恐不可为讳矣，宜请主妇来。”其家人曰：“产者即主妇也，吾家安得更有主妇！”媪曰：“待我于堂，且责我迟滞者谁欤？”家人闻之皆大骇异，谓无是人。媪不信，遍索之，果无有。已而其妇竟卒。后有知者，曰：“旧有朱姓居此屋，其妇以产难卒。”据媪所见，与朱氏妇形状相同，殆必其鬼矣。岂以产难卒者，亦如缢鬼溺鬼之求代欤？

    Explanation

    296

    A Man from Chu Named Mei

    A man named Mei from the state of Chu served as an official in the Central Plains. When his wife was about to give birth, he sent someone to summon a midwife. Upon arriving, the midwife saw a woman waiting in the hall, dressed very beautifully and adorned with flowers in her hair. She reprimanded the midwife, saying, "Why are you so late! What kind of major event is this for a family, and yet you are so slow!" The midwife, frightened and respectful, apologized. Then she was ordered to enter the room to examine the pregnant woman. The labor was quite difficult, and after a long time, the situation became very dangerous. The midwife said, "Things have come to this, I fear it cannot be concealed anymore. It would be appropriate to call the mistress of the house." The family members replied, "The person giving birth is the mistress of the house. How could our family have another mistress!" The midwife said, "Wait for me in the hall, and who was it that reprimanded me for being slow?" Upon hearing this, all the family members were greatly shocked and puzzled, saying there was no such person. The midwife did not believe them and searched everywhere, but indeed there was no one. Afterwards, her wife eventually died. Later, someone who knew the story said, "There used to be someone surnamed Zhu living in this house, and her wife died due to difficult childbirth." According to what the midwife saw, the woman's appearance was the same as that of Zhu's wife, so it must be her ghost. Could it be that those who die due to difficult childbirth also seek to replace others, like hanged ghosts or drowned ghosts?

    297

    杨某，山西人，官于贵州。有一妹，庶出也。妹甫生而所生母死，育于其母。幼而明慧，父母皆奇爱之。父临终谓某曰：“必善视此妹。”母临终亦谓某曰：“此女虽非我所生，我爱之逾所生。汝必善视之。”某承父母遗意，遇此妹甚厚。其妻颇贤，待小姑亦甚厚。女美而且才，家中事悉女主持之。已而其妻死，继室亦贤，仍以内政让女。女年已长矣，某择配良苛，凡有求娶者，某视之，辄曰：“非吾妹偶也。”因循久之，未得其人。

    Explanation

    297

    Yang, a man from Shanxi, held an official position in Guizhou. He had a sister, who was born to a concubine. Shortly after her birth, her biological mother died, and she was raised by another mother. From a young age, the sister was intelligent and beautiful, and both parents loved her extraordinarily. On his deathbed, the father told Yang, "You must treat this sister well." The mother, also on her deathbed, said to Yang, "This girl, though not my biological daughter, I love her more than my own. You must treat her well." Yang honored his parents' wishes and treated his sister with great kindness. His first wife was very virtuous and treated the sister with equal kindness. The sister was beautiful and talented, and she managed all household affairs. After his first wife died, his second wife was also virtuous and continued to defer household management to the sister. By the time the sister had grown up, Yang was very selective in choosing a husband for her, rejecting all suitors. Despite the passage of time, no suitable match was found.

    298

    而其继室又死，未几，又续娶一妇，妇不能如前两人之贤，辄怏怏曰：“奈何以小姑主家政！”然不敢公言于其夫。女知之，乃往往托疾，有以家事关白者，推以与嫂。如是年余，家中事悉决于嫂矣。然兄之饮食衣服，女尚手自料理。嫂意不乐，自是家庭间小有口舌，女郁郁成疾。是时女年几三十矣，某急欲为择婿，终以未得其人，迄无成议。某偶于役于外，闻女疾甚，驰而归，则女已死矣，乃抚膺大恸曰：“吾父遗言谓何？吾母遗言谓何？吾妹死，吾何面目见父母地下乎？”痛哭欧血，未数月，亦死。余谓此君推父母之遗意以爱其妹，可谓孝且弟矣。然不能早为择婿，使有所归，则爱之而不知所以爱之，适所以死之，虽以身殉，奚益焉？惜哉！

    Explanation

    298

    After his second wife died, not long after, he remarried a woman who was not as virtuous as her predecessors. She would often express her discontent, saying, "Why should a young aunt manage the household affairs?" However, she dared not speak openly to her husband about it. The sister-in-law, aware of this, would often pretend to be ill. When matters concerning the household were brought to her attention, she would hand them over to her sister-in-law. In this way, after more than a year, all household matters were decided by the sister-in-law. Yet, the sister still personally took care of her brother's food, clothing, and other needs. The sister-in-law was unhappy with this arrangement, and soon there were small disputes within the family. The sister became depressed and fell ill. By then, she was nearly thirty years old, and the brother urgently wanted to find her a husband, but he could not find a suitable match, and the matter remained unresolved. Once, while he was away on official business, he heard that his sister was very ill. He rushed home, only to find that she had already died. He grieved deeply and lamented, "What did my father say? What did my mother say? My sister has died, how can I face my parents in the afterlife?" He wept bitterly and coughed up blood. Within a few months, he also died. I believe that this man loved his sister according to the wishes of his parents, which can be called filial piety and fraternal duty. However, he failed to find her a husband early enough, so that she could have a proper marriage. His love for her, therefore, turned into her death. Even if he sacrificed himself, what good is it? What a pity!

    299

    孟县贞女，不知其姓氏，故援《明史》招远孝女之例，以县名冠之。自幼许嫁某氏子，未嫁而某氏子卒。贞女年甫十四，请于父母，欲往。父母不许，固请不已。其叔父曰：“奈何吾家有此贞女而不成其志？”力劝其父母许之，遂如夫氏。舅姑皆不欲，其舅犹以婉言遣之，姑则出恶声焉。贞女痛哭不肯反。其夫之叔父曰：“奈何吾家有此贞妇而不成其志？”力劝其舅姑留之。姑遇贞女虐，饥寒不之恤，而役之如婢妾。冬日手足皲瘃，而操作不倦，无怨言。

    Explanation

    299

    The Chaste Woman of Meng County, whose surname and given name are unknown, thus follows the example of the filial daughter from Zhaoyuan as recorded in the Ming Shi (History of Ming), preceding her name with the county name. Since childhood, she was betrothed to a son of a certain family, but before she was married, the son of the certain family died. The chaste woman was just fourteen years old when she asked her parents to let her go. Her parents did not allow it, but she persisted in asking repeatedly. Her uncle said, "How can our family have such a chaste woman and not fulfill her wish?" He strongly persuaded her parents to agree, and she then went to her husband's family. Her father-in-law and mother-in-law did not want her; her father-in-law still sent her away with gentle words, while her mother-in-law spoke harshly to her. The chaste woman cried bitterly and would not return. Her husband's uncle said, "How can our family have such a chaste woman and not fulfill her wish?" He strongly persuaded her father-in-law and mother-in-law to keep her. Her mother-in-law treated the chaste woman cruelly, not caring for her when she was hungry or cold, and treated her like a servant or concubine. In winter, her hands and feet were cracked and blistered, yet she worked tirelessly, with no complaints.

    300

    姑以其勤慎，稍怜爱之，然役之如故。有夫弟才八九岁，女视之犹弟也。未几，舅姑相继死，女主家政，丧祭皆以礼。及服阕，夫弟年十六矣，女即为之娶妇。妇至，亦颇婉娩，女每事必教导之，且曰：“不久当以家政付娣。”新妇曰：“我年幼无所知，仍当归姒。”女曰：“未亡人数载以来，心力交瘁，此后余生无几。尔夫妇不当代我劳乎？”自是女稍稍谢去家事，日事缝纫，一衣一襦，皆新作之，然悉以布，无寸锦尺帛焉。既毕，乃归其母家。先是，女间一归宁，未暮必反，母留之宿，辞以舅姑；及舅姑死，辞以家事。

    Explanation

    300

    The girl, recognizing her diligence and caution, showed her some pity and affection, but still treated her as before. Her husband had a younger brother who was only eight or nine years old, and the girl treated him as if he were her own younger brother. Soon after, her husband's parents died one after another, and the girl took charge of the household affairs, conducting all funeral and sacrificial rites according to proper etiquette. When the mourning period was over, her husband's younger brother was sixteen years old, and the girl immediately arranged for him to marry. The new wife was also gentle and kind, and the girl always taught her everything, saying, "In the near future, I will entrust the household affairs to you." The new wife replied, "I am young and know nothing; I should still return to my sister-in-law." The girl said, "For several years now, I have been mentally and physically exhausted, and there is little time left in my life. Won't you two take over my work?" From then on, the girl gradually withdrew from household affairs and spent her days sewing. Every piece of clothing she made was new, but all were made of cloth, not an inch of silk or brocade. Once she finished, she returned to her mother's home. Before this, the girl occasionally visited her parents, always returning before dusk. Her mother tried to keep her overnight, but she declined, citing her duties to her in-laws; after her in-laws died, she declined, citing her household duties.

    301

    又其归也，惟居母所，虽兄弟之阈，不履也。至是乃遍诣其伯叔兄弟，留连数日而还。其叔父使人遗以红纟由一端，传语曰：“叔氏问姑娘使得否？”女大喜，受之，亦传语曰：“敬谢叔父，使得，使得。”俗语以可用为使得，亦莫之异也。还至夫氏，与小郎夫妇言家事甚悉。其夜，著所制衣襦，以叔父所遗红纟由自缢。甫就缢，即见其舅姑至，又偕一少年至，曰：“此尔夫也。”已而又有长老数辈至，室中无虑数十人。俄而中溜井灶之神悉至，社公亦至，皆力阻之。

    Explanation

    301

    Upon her return, she remained only at her mother's place, avoiding even stepping over the threshold of her brothers' homes. At this time, she visited all her uncles, aunts, and brothers, staying for several days before returning. Her uncle sent a piece of red silk to her, with a message saying, "Uncle asks if the girl can use it." The woman was greatly pleased, accepted the gift, and replied, "Respectfully thanks Uncle, yes, yes." In local parlance, "makes it" means "can use it," and this usage was not considered unusual. Upon returning to her husband's family, she spoke in detail about family matters with her sister-in-law and brother-in-law. That night, she wore the clothes she had made and hanged herself with the red silk given by her uncle. As soon as she hanged herself, she saw her husband's parents come, followed by a young man who said, "This is your husband." Soon after, several elders arrived, and there were likely around thirty people in the room. Shortly thereafter, all the household gods of the hearth, well, and stove came, followed by the village deity, who all tried to stop her.

    302

    砰然一声，悬绝于梁，女坠于地。小郎夫妇闻声趋至，破扉而入，见状大惊，急扶女起，则已苏矣。夫妇厥角痛哭，请毋死。女慰之曰：“我不死矣。”乃述所见，曰：“神鬼皆阻我，我岂得死哉！”因解衣就寝，达旦晏然。次日，女大治酒食，祭其先，并中溜井灶一一祭之。人问故，曰：“谢其也。”其夕，竟缢而死。疑已以必死之故祭告鬼神，故鬼神亦不复阻之矣，年二十有二。其叔父遗以红纟由，大有王炎午生祭文山之意。使与死同音，使得者，死得也。于是一县轰然，欢其贞烈，孟县令实亲临其丧云。

    Explanation

    302

    A loud crash, and she was hanging from the beam, her body falling to the ground. The young couple, hearing the noise, rushed to the scene, breaking down the door to find her in a state of shock. They quickly lifted her up, and she had already regained consciousness. The couple prostrated themselves in tears, begging her not to die. She comforted them, saying, "I am not going to die." She then recounted what she had seen, saying, "Even the gods and spirits were blocking my path. How could I die?" She undressed and went to bed, remaining calm until dawn. The next day, she prepared a grand feast, offering sacrifices to her ancestors and to the central hearth, well, and stove one by one. When asked why, she replied, "To thank them for their protection." That night, she hanged herself to death. It is believed that she had sacrificed to the gods and spirits because she was certain she would die, and thus they no longer obstructed her. She was twenty-two years old. Her uncle gave her a red silk ribbon, a gesture reminiscent of Wang Yanwu's memorial to Wen Tianxiang. The phrase "making a sound like death, obtaining death" suggests a connection between her actions and her fate. The entire county was moved by her chastity and valor, and the magistrate of Meng County personally attended her funeral.

    303

    江夏朱云舫敏中，元配早死，有一子四女，继室以龙氏。龙氏女在室时，梦有妇人至其前，呼之为妹，意若甚昵。龙固不识也，私计此人何以妹我。妇人已若觉之，笑曰：“妹不知欤？吾侪姊妹也。今有事相托，请从我一行。”乃与偕往，房栊曲折，且经由爨室，始至一处。有婴儿卧床上，妇抚之而谓女曰：“此妹之子也，宜善视之。”觉而异焉，不以告人。梦之次日，而朱氏之媒至，竟归于朱。成礼逾月，始履行其屋，由爨室至最后一屋，宛如梦所历。入之，则前妻所生子及乳媪在焉。盖以正屋方娶新妇，故移此耳。

    Explanation

    303

    Jiangxia Zhu Yunfang Minzhong, whose first wife died early, had one son and four daughters. He remarried a woman from the Long family. When the Long woman was still a maiden, she dreamed of a woman appearing before her and calling her "sister," seeming very close. She did not recognize this woman, but privately wondered why she was called her sister. The woman seemed to realize this and smiled, saying, "Do you not know? We are sisters. I have something to ask of you, please come with me." She then took her to a place, passing through a series of rooms and even the kitchen, until they arrived at a certain place. There was an infant sleeping on a bed, and the woman patted it and said to the girl, "This is your sister's child. You should take good care of it." Upon waking, she found this strange and did not tell anyone. The next day, after the wedding ceremony was completed for over a month, she began to explore the house, and it was just like in the dream. Upon entering the last room, she found her stepson and the wet nurse there. It is because the main house is where the new wife is staying, so they moved him here.

    304

    女憬然悟梦中所见，必前妻也，命移儿至己所卧室，抚爱之无异所生。后女竟无出，前妻子亦善事之。余外姊适周氏者之长女，归嘉兴张少渠大令为继室。张初娶丁氏，生一子一女。周既归张，数月，忽梦见一妇人，衣紫色衫，向己肃拜，曰：“诸事奉托。”问夫人何人，曰：“我丁氏也。”周顿悟为张之前妻，惊而醒，觉帐外如有人。搴视之，则其人犹立床前，果衣紫色衫，了了在目。周惧而呼，遂不见，盖亦以所生子女为托也。所异者，其所著紫色衫固尚在箧中，周亦尝著之。人死而有鬼，宜也，衣服犹在，岂亦有鬼？意者凡物有形质，必有精气，鬼固得摄其精气以去邪，抑或幻作是形以取信于人邪？

    Explanation

    304

    The woman suddenly realized that what she had seen in her dream must have been her former wife. She ordered that the child be moved to her own bedroom and treated with the same love and care as if the child were her own. Later, the woman never gave birth, and the child of her former wife was also treated with respect and kindness. My aunt's eldest daughter, who married into the Zhou family, became the second wife of Zhang Shaoqu, the magistrate of Jiaxing. Zhang had initially married Ding, with whom he had one son and one daughter. After Zhou married Zhang, after a few months, she suddenly dreamed of a woman wearing a purple robe, who bowed respectfully to her and said, "I entrust all matters to you." When Zhou asked who the woman was, she replied, "I am Mrs. Ding." Zhou suddenly realized that the woman in her dream was Zhang's former wife, and she awoke in shock, feeling as if someone was outside the curtain. She lifted the curtain to look, and indeed, the woman was still standing in front of the bed, wearing a purple robe, clearly visible. Zhou called out in fear, and the woman then disappeared, likely because she was entrusting the care of the children. What was strange was that the purple robe she was wearing was still in the chest, and Zhou had also worn it before. It is said that when people die, they become ghosts, and their clothes remain, but could the clothes also have ghosts? Perhaps all things with form have spirit, and ghosts can certainly take away their spirit. Or perhaps they create this form to gain trust from people?

    305

    余家有佣媪何氏，天津人，言其乡间一巨家将筑室，集人夫治地，何媪犬兄之子与焉。掘得一银盆，其大如，争欲得之，遂至喧哄。闻于主人，主人曰：“此我地也。地上地下，皆我之物，汝曹何与焉！”众乃不敢争。主人携归，权之，重五十两，命匠之，而分与治地者，人得二两有奇。何媪夫兄之子亦受所分而归，俄而大病，医疗月余，虽幸不死，而所分之银尽矣。何媪每举此事以戒其侪辈曰：“分外之财，得之无益，勿妄求也。”

    Explanation

    305

    In my household, there is an elderly woman named He, from Tianjin, who told me about a wealthy family in her village that was preparing to build a house. They gathered workers to clear the land, and the son of He's brother-in-law was among them. While digging, they discovered a silver bowl as large as a tray, and everyone wanted to claim it, leading to a noisy dispute. The owner was informed, and he said, "This is my land. Whatever is above or below it belongs to me. What business is it of yours?" The workers then dared not argue. The owner took the bowl home, weighed it, and found it to be fifty taels in weight. He had it refined and divided among the workers, giving each slightly more than two taels. The son of He's brother-in-law also received his share and returned home. Soon after, he fell seriously ill and was treated for over a month. Although he fortunately did not die, all the silver he had received was spent. He often mentions this incident to warn her peers, saying, "Unearned wealth is of no benefit; do not seek it in vain."

    306

    余大儿妇樊氏言：其二伯母体弱多病，恒在床褥，年四十九时，大病已死矣。众人欲为具含敛，而其子坐床头，手持其帐，不使人得近之，有言已死者，辄大怒。众人始以孝子哀痛，不忍夺之。继以死且竟夕，而绞纟今衾冒一事未具，惧不成礼，力劝其子，卒不可夺。迁延至于旦，而死者竟苏。既苏，命亟焚纸钱，以犒舆丁。众共骇异，环而问故，曰：“舅姑送吾回也。”时其舅姑死久矣，问其详，乃曰：“吾顷如梦中，至一处，舅姑皆在焉。栋宇高峻，僮奴甚众。

    Explanation

    306

    My eldest daughter-in-law, Fan, said: Her second aunt was frail and often ill, always lying in bed. At the age of forty-nine, she died of a serious illness. The people wanted to prepare her body for burial, but her son sat at the head of the bed, holding the canopy, preventing anyone from approaching. If anyone mentioned that she had died, he would become very angry. At first, the people thought it was the son's grief that prevented him from allowing the preparations, so they did not force him. Later, as the night wore on and the body had not been properly prepared, they feared it would not be done properly, so they strongly urged the son, but he could not be persuaded. They delayed until dawn, and then the deceased suddenly revived. After reviving, she ordered that paper money be burned immediately to reward the bearers. The people were all astonished and gathered around to ask why. She said, "My in-laws are sending me back." At that time, my in-laws had been dead for a long time. When asked for details, she explained: "I was just in a dream, and arrived at a place where my in-laws were. The buildings were tall and majestic, and there were many servants."

    307

    舅姑见吾至，皆诧曰：”汝安得遽来此？宜速归！‘吾曰：“新妇既来此，当奉侍舅姑，尚何归乎？’舅姑皆不许，舅言犹和婉，姑色甚厉，并操杖逐之曰：”不去且杖尔！‘舅曰：“且勿，且勿。’与姑不知作何语，姑乃入房，携药一丸出，命吾吞之，曰：”今可去矣。‘吾徘徊未知所从，出闻舅呼陈荣。陈荣者，故樊氏奴也。及至，与生前无异，舅命陈荣以轿送吾归。吾从陈荣出，果有轿，乘之而行。甫望见家门，而舁者遽释肩，启帘幕，呼吾出曰：“至矣！至矣！’吾自门外行至此，甚疲惫。今舁者当尚在门外，故宜以纸钱犒之也。”闻其口中尚有药香，数日后其病竟愈。

    Explanation

    307
