Once all the ghosts gather, my two sons will stand at the alley entrance to block their exit, and my younger brother will hold a broom and strike the air vigorously inside. This way, we can punish them severely, so that henceforth ghosts will not dare to come." They agreed. That night, they arranged everything according to ritual, and Liu sat high up, reciting spells to summon the ghosts. His two sons stood at the front and back of the alley entrance, blocking their exit, while his younger brother held a broom and struck the air vigorously inside. Liu also rang a cymbal to assist. The ghostly sounds reached the alley entrance and then turned back, like this for a long time.

    350

    其长子呼其叔曰：“叔惫矣，且来守此巷口，我代叔击鬼。”叔往立未定，忽仆于地，若为人推倒者，盖鬼已奔去矣。后二子皆成进士，其叔以明经终。或曰：“鬼殆以其可轻而侮之欤？”余曰：“不然，鬼之畏人，畏其镇定耳；一移足，则气不固矣。敌之至也攻其瑕，病之来也乘其虚，皆此理也。”余同年丁濂甫绍周，自言少时曾戏学缁流施攻焰口，祗习召请之文，未得退送之诀。鬼聚其室，灯光如豆，濂甫置之不问，拥被而眠，但闻啾唧之声，达旦始寂，竟无他异。此则由濂甫根柢深厚，故鬼不得而侮之，否则未有不为所困者矣。

    Explanation

    350

    The eldest son called out to his uncle, saying, "Uncle, you are tired. Come and guard this alley entrance, and I will take your place to fight the ghost." The uncle went to stand there but had not yet settled when he suddenly fell to the ground, as if pushed down by someone. It seemed that the ghost had already fled. Later, the other two sons both became jinshi (advanced scholars), while the uncle ended his life as a scholar of the classics. Someone said, "Perhaps the ghost considered them too easy to mock?" I replied, "Not so. Ghosts fear humans because they fear their composure; once they move, their spirit becomes unstable. When the enemy comes, they attack the weak points; when illness comes, it takes advantage of the vulnerability. All this is the same principle." My contemporary, Ding Lianfu, also known as Shao Zhou, said that in his youth, he once playfully learned from Buddhist monks to perform the ritual of attacking the mouth of fire. He only learned the texts for summoning and did not master the techniques for dismissing. Ghosts gathered in his room, and the light was dim like a small soybean. Lianfu ignored them and did not inquire, wrapping himself in his quilt and going to sleep. He only heard the chirping sounds until dawn when it finally became quiet, and there was no other strange occurrence. This is because Lianfu had a deep foundation, thus the ghosts could not mock him. Otherwise, there would be no one who would not be trapped by them.

    351

    濂甫又言：道光辛丑岁入都会试，绕道走徐州，投宿旅店。店中人满，主人导至后屋中，扫榻布席，将就偃息。俄闻有臭气甚恶，周观四壁，无所见。以烛照床下，则一尸存焉，两手两足以四巨钉钉于床版。濂甫噤不敢声，危坐达旦，束装而去。此必孤客挟重赀为所戕害者，使见之而惊呼，必且身罹其害。危险之中，镇定如此，其胆其识，均足多也。

    351

    Lianfu said: In the year Xinchou of Daoguang (1831), I was on my way to the capital for the imperial examination and took a detour through Xuzhou. I checked into an inn, but it was full. The owner led me to a back room, where he swept the bed and spread the mat, preparing for me to lie down and rest. Soon, I heard a very foul smell. I looked around the four walls but saw nothing. I lit a candle and looked under the bed, and there was a corpse there, with both hands and feet nailed to the bed with four large nails. Lianfu remained silent in fear, sitting upright until dawn, then packed up and left. This must have been a lone traveler carrying heavy wealth who was killed. If he had seen it and cried out, he would surely have been harmed himself. In such danger, to remain so calm, his courage and wisdom are both commendable.

    352

    镇江甘露寺，一巨刹也。嘉庆间，长白策公守是郡，不信佛法。一日至寺，寺僧与之畅谈内典，并侈陈福田利益。公曰：“佛法深微，固难遽喻。至如慈云甘露，普拯幽冥，则宜实有征应。明日烦吾师施放焰口，吾亲临观览，必有所见，方释吾疑；不然，则是师之慧力未足洞彻幽明，当别选高僧拄持此寺。”僧唯唯，谋于其徒。有小沙弥颇狡狯，谓主僧曰：“市有丐者二人，形容如鬼，俟召孤魂时，使之至踱庭中，自东而西，太守远观，必以为鬼也。”主僧从之，乃与丐约，每人予之钱二百，使伪为鬼以欺太守。孰意次日日未暮，太守使人封闭三门，丐不得入。

    Explanation

    352

    Zhenjiang Ganlu Temple, a grand Buddhist monastery, stood in the city. During the Jiaqing era, Governor Changbai Cegong, who was stationed in this prefecture, did not believe in Buddhism. One day, he visited the temple, and the monks engaged him in a lengthy discussion about the Buddhist scriptures and the benefits of cultivating virtue. The governor remarked, "Buddhism is profound and subtle, and it is difficult to understand it immediately. However, if teachings like the compassionate clouds and rain, which universally save the souls in the underworld, are truly effective, there must be tangible evidence of their efficacy. Tomorrow, I would like you to perform the ritual of releasing the hungry ghosts. I will personally observe it, and if I see something, my doubts will be dispelled; otherwise, it will indicate that your wisdom is insufficient to comprehend the visible and invisible realms, and I will have to choose a different high monk to uphold this temple." The monks agreed, and the head monk consulted with his disciples. One of the young novices, who was quite cunning, suggested to the head monk, "There are two beggars in the market whose appearances are like those of ghosts. When we summon the spirits of the dead, we can have them wander back and forth in the courtyard, moving from east to west. The governor, observing from afar, will surely think they are ghosts." The head monk followed this advice and made an agreement with the beggars, giving each of them two hundred coins to pretend to be ghosts and deceive the governor. However, the next day, before sunset, the governor had the three gates of the temple sealed, preventing the beggars from entering.

    353

    群僧无如何，晚间姑循照常仪登坛施食，果有人影往来庭庑。僧疑三门封闭，丐不知以何术而来，乃太守则耸然异之，布施优渥，顶礼而去。次日僧持钱往酬丐，则已僵毙道旁矣。始悟昨暮来者，丐之鬼也，因使人瘗之。镇江邹氏，在乾、嘉间颇称殷富。其第宅宽大，四旁小屋空者居多。一日，有佣工缢死其中，邹氏不知也。会其家作佛事，至夜施放焰口，缁流正在坛上诵经，忽阴风大起，满堂灯烛暗淡无光。群僧惶骇，皆逃入廊屋，惟主僧曰希圣者仍危坐不动，口中默念，不知是何神咒。良久灯烛复明，群僧亦集。佛事毕，希圣语主人曰：“今日事颇怪异，檀越宜各处检寻，有无他故。”次日寻至空屋，始知佣工缢死已数日矣。

    Explanation

    353

    The monks, at a loss, decided to follow the usual ritual and perform the food-offering ceremony at night. Indeed, human-like shadows appeared moving around the temple courtyard. The monks were puzzled because the temple gates were closed, and they wondered how the beggar could have entered. The magistrate, greatly alarmed, regarded the event as strange, made generous offerings, and left in reverence. The next day, when the monks went to give the beggar money, they found him lying stiff and lifeless by the roadside. They finally realized that the one who had come the previous night was the ghost of the beggar, and they had him buried.

    In Zhenjiang, the Zou family was quite wealthy during the Qianlong and Jiaqing reigns. Their mansion was spacious, with many small rooms around it that were often empty. One day, a hired worker was found hanged in one of the empty rooms, but the family was unaware of this. When the family held a Buddhist ceremony, at night they performed the ritual of offering food to hungry ghosts. As the monks were reciting sutras on the altar, suddenly a strong cold wind arose, and the candles in the hall dimmed and went out. The monks were terrified and all fled into the side rooms, except for the head monk, named Xisheng, who remained seated calmly, muttering something under his breath, though it was unclear what he was reciting. After a while, the candles lit up again, and the monks gathered back. After the Buddhist ceremony ended, Xisheng told the host, "Today's events were quite strange. You should search everywhere to see if there is any other matter." The next day, when they searched the empty room, they discovered that the hired worker had been hanged there for several days.

    354

    丹徒有沈某者，工举业，而家甚贫，无所得食，乃为人作讼牒。一夕，梦其父痛责之，始辍不作。其后举于乡，成进士，以知县分发河南。十余年后，宦成而归，橐中颇丰。至岁除日，夜将半矣，其妻闻听事前似有人聚语，乃自门隙窥之。见古衣冠者数人列坐堂上，一人曰：“沈某不义之财，宜付之一炬。”一人曰：“此似过重，绝其嗣可也。”一人曰：“沈某作县令，听断尚无枉挠，绝嗣亦太过，但当散其财耳。”其妻悚然而入。后其家果日贫。

    Explanation

    354

    In the district of Dantu, there was a man named Shen who was skilled in the imperial examinations but was very poor, having nothing to eat, and so he wrote legal documents for others. One night, he dreamed that his father severely scolded him, and from then on, he stopped writing legal documents. Later, he passed the provincial examination, became a jinshi (imperial scholar), and was appointed as a county magistrate, sent to Henan. After more than ten years, he completed his official career and returned home, with a bag quite full of wealth. On New Year's Eve, as the night was almost half over, his wife heard what seemed to be people gathering and talking in front of the main hall, and she looked through the door crack to see them. She saw several people in ancient attire sitting in a row on the hall, one of whom said, "Shen's unjust wealth should be burned." Another said, "This seems too harsh; cutting off his descendants would suffice." A third said, "Shen, as a county magistrate, still judged cases without being unfair; cutting off his descendants is too severe; it is better to disperse his wealth." His wife trembled with fear and entered the house. After that, his family indeed became increasingly poor.

    355

    扬州包某，酷信扶箕之术。懋迁有无，悉决之于箕，久之家大富。一日，箕仙告之曰：“某日扬州有水灾，汝宜早为计，勿及于难。”包疑信参半。然念时届秋初，正淮水盛涨之时，且箕仙灵异素著，当不吾欺。乃先期具舟，托言有事于镇江，载眷属俱去。是日天气阴晦，大雨如注，甫出瓜步江中，风浪大作，波涛汹涌，几濒于危。幸而至于京口，雨霁，风亦稍止。次日探问维扬消息，则并无水灾，城中安堵如故。数日后，仍移家而归。叩问箕仙，盘中大书曰：“是日江中风浪可怕否？”殆以其屡渎，故戏之也。

    　　京口有蔡姓者，其妻张氏，病中见一鬼来索命，言前世冤仇，今日既遇，必不相舍。蔡因具牒诉于城隍神。张见有冥隶持票来捉鬼去，俄又有一隶来拉张俱去，张遂昏然若死者。二日而苏，语其夫曰：“不可为矣！我至冥中与鬼对质，彼直而我曲，神不吾也。”翌日果死。

    Explanation

    355

    Bao, a man from Yangzhou, firmly believed in the practice of fengshui divination. He made all his trading decisions based on the results of these divinations, and after a long time, he became very wealthy. One day, the fengshui spirit warned him, "On a certain day, Yangzhou will experience a flood. You should prepare early and avoid the disaster." Bao was doubtful and believing at the same time. However, he thought it was the beginning of autumn, the time when the Huai River was at its highest water level, and since the fengshui spirit was known for its supernatural powers, it would not deceive him. Therefore, he prepared boats in advance, pretending to have business in Zhenjiang, and took his family with him. On that day, the weather was overcast, and it rained heavily. As soon as they left the Gua Bu section of the river, the wind and waves became very strong, and the waves were surging, putting them on the verge of danger. Fortunately, they reached Jingkou, and the rain stopped. The wind also calmed down a bit. The next day, he inquired about the news in Weiyang and found out that there was no flood; the city remained peaceful as usual. A few days later, he returned home with his family and asked the fengshui spirit. The spirit wrote a message in the divination tray, asking, "Were the wind and waves in the river frightening on that day?" It seemed that the spirit was teasing him for asking too many questions.

    In Jingkou, there was a man surnamed Cai whose wife, Zhang, saw a ghost come to demand her life while she was ill. The ghost said they had a grudge from a past life, and now that they had met, it would not let go. Cai wrote a petition to the city god. Zhang saw a ghostly official holding a ticket to take the ghost away, and soon another official came to take her away too. Zhang then fainted as if she had died. Two days later, she revived and told her husband, "It is not possible to continue! I went to the underworld and faced the ghost, and he was right while I was wrong. The gods will not protect me." The next day, she indeed died.

    356

    扬州有某翁者，以禺起家，为诸商领袖，家中巨富，年迹逾六十矣。偶乘肩舆过钞关义渡，值漕艘连樯来，风帆甚猛，顷刻而至，渡舟几为冲覆。同舟之人惶怖号呼，翁坐舆中，亦甚惊惧。有一舆夫在旁，见舆中所坐竟非翁也，青面赤发，目大如鸡卵，齿&lt；齿&gt；々出唇外，若庙中所塑鬼判者然。大惊，不敢言。俄顷之间，仍复其故，舟亦不覆。此翁以惊惧故，神出于舍，见此怪状，殆亦魔君转世者欤？

    　　史兰奇，海宁之硖石镇人。无子，有一女名大姑，年二十，未许嫁。同治甲戌岁，史患痢疾甚剧，诸医束手。同里有戴生者，善扶箕之术，乃使求方于仙。仙判用大黄四两，佥疑不可用，请易之。又判用猪脾一具，实以糯米及莲实芡实，煮烂食之。大姑素慧，见仙方如此，叹曰：“吾父病必不起矣。

    Explanation

    356

    In Yangzhou, there was an old man who became wealthy through pearls and jewels, becoming the leader among merchants, with a vast fortune and an age surpassing sixty. Once while riding in a sedan chair passing by the customs checkpoint at the ferry, he encountered a large number of ships coming quickly with strong winds, arriving in a moment, almost capsizing the ferry boat. The people on the boat were terrified and shouting, and the old man in the sedan chair was also very frightened. There was a sedan chair bearer nearby who saw that the person in the chair was not the old man, but rather a monstrous figure with a green face, red hair, large eyes, and teeth protruding from the mouth, resembling a ghost or demon from a temple. The bearer was greatly shocked and dared not speak. In a short while, everything returned to normal, and the boat did not capsize. It seems that the old man, due to his fear, had his spirit leave his body, and seeing this strange appearance, he might have been a reincarnated demon king.

    Shi Lanchi was a man from Xieshi Town in Hai Ning. He had no sons but one daughter named Dagu, who was twenty years old and not yet married. In the year Jia Xu of Tongzhi (1874), Shi suffered from severe dysentery, and all the doctors were at a loss. There was a local man named Dai who was skilled in the art of fumigation (a type of divination), and he was asked to seek a remedy from the gods. The gods prescribed four ounces of rhubarb, but everyone doubted that it could be used and requested a change. Another prescription involved a pig's spleen filled with glutinous rice, lotus seeds, and water caltrop, cooked until soft and eaten. Dagu was usually wise, and seeing the prescription like this, she sighed, "My father's illness must be fatal."

    357

    ”其夜，戴生在家，忽箕笔自摇动若欲书者，大惊，试扶之，则疾书曰：“此地竟有此大好事邪？史女大姑以身代父，其孝可嘉，惜其父禄已尽，不能延也。上帝悯之，赐其嗣子成名。又录大姑为泰山侍书，羽葆导引，从此经过，犹未远也。”众方惊诧，忽闻香气酷烈，且隐隐有音乐声。戴与史相距里许，乃走问史氏。史氏犹不知大姑已死也，急入视之，则大姑卧床上，其体已冰，推寻死状，盖服生鸦片烟而死也。次日，史病小愈，越十四日竟死。既服阕，其所嗣子补博士弟子员，所谓赐嗣子成名者信矣。则大姑之为泰山侍书，亦必不妄也。余雅不信扶箕之术，然如此等事，虽近无稽，而有足劝孝。

    Explanation

    357

    On that night, Dai Sheng was at home when suddenly a brush began to move on its own, as if it wanted to write. Startled, he tried to steady it, but it wrote quickly: "Who would have thought that such a great good thing could happen here? Shi Nu Dagu sacrificed herself for her father-in-law; her filial piety is commendable, but alas, her father's fate has already been determined and cannot be prolonged. The Heavenly Deity showed compassion, bestowing success upon his successor. She was also recorded as a servant of Mount Tai, with a ceremonial procession led by feathered banners passing by here not long ago." The crowd was amazed, and suddenly they smelled a strong fragrance, accompanied by faint music. Dai and Shi were about one li apart, so he went to inquire about Shi. Shi still did not know that Dagu had died, so he hurriedly went inside to see. There, Dagu lay on the bed, her body already frozen. Investigating the cause of death, it turned out that she had died from consuming raw opium. The next day, Shi's illness slightly improved, but after fourteen days, she finally died. After the period of mourning, her successor was admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy, confirming the prophecy of the successor's success. Thus, Dagu's role as a servant of Mount Tai must also be true. Although I am skeptical of spirit writing, such events, though seemingly unverifiable, serve to encourage filial piety.

    358

    稗官小说，何妨妄言而妄听哉！扶箕或谓之扶乩，余按《夷坚志》“沈承务紫姑”一条云：紫姑，仙之名，古所未有，至唐乃稍见之。世但以箕插笔，使两人扶之，或书字于沙中。据此，则当作扶箕为是。离硖石十余里，地名长阡，有王翁者，人颇长厚。一子弱冠，应童子试矣。为之娶妇，甫娶而妇死；续聘某氏女，未娶而女又死。其子旋发狂疾，翁亦久病。乃问于箕仙，仙判云：“此夙孽也。尔前生陈姓，家巨富。有范生者，寒士也，负尔百金。

    Explanation

    358

    Minor or unofficial historical records, what harm is there in speaking and listening recklessly! The practice of "fú jī" is also called "fú jī." I have examined the entry "Shen Chengwu and the Deity Zigu" in Yijianzhi, which states: "Zigu is the name of a deity, unknown in ancient times, but became known during the Tang Dynasty. The world merely uses a writing tool inserted into a tray, with two people supporting it, or writing characters in the sand." According to this, it should be called "fú jī." In a place named Changqian, about ten miles from Xiazhi, there was an old man named Wang, who was a kind and honest person. He had a son who was just coming of age and had taken the examination for young scholars. After he was married, his wife died; when he was about to marry another woman from a certain family, she too died. His son then fell ill with madness, and the old man also fell seriously ill. They then asked the deity for judgment, and the deity ruled: "This is a matter of past karma. You were named Chen in your previous life and had a large fortune. There was a poor scholar named Fan who owed you a hundred gold coins."

    359

    尔索之急，范缢死；妻徇夫死；子失母，亦死；其家遂绝。范讼之冥司，冥司以为负债必偿，索非无理。但阴律首在问心，范生本有偿意，而陈操之过急，致范生一家三命同时而死，情殊可悯。陈本素封，此金为数无多，在陈不足为得失。乃铢锱必较，为富不仁，其情又甚可恶，故判今生以一子、两子妇偿其三命，亦使绝后，所以报也。惟念今生尚不失为长者，倘能力行善事，或尚可挽回。”王翁乃矢于神，愿力行善事，且诵《高王经》满万遍。复叩问箕仙，仙曰：“冥司嘉尔善念，令范生宽二十年限，俟尔子生子，再偿范命矣。”已而子病果愈。

    Explanation

    359

    Your urgent demand led to Fan hanging himself; his wife followed him in death, and his son, orphaned by his mother's death, also died, thus ending the family line. Fan appealed to the underworld officials, who believed that debts must be repaid and that the demand was not unreasonable. However, the laws of the underworld first consider one's heart. Fan had originally intended to repay the debt, but Chen was too hasty, causing Fan's entire family of three to die simultaneously, a situation that is particularly pitiable. Chen was originally a wealthy person, and this amount of money was not significant for him, not a significant gain or loss. But he was penny-pinching and unkind because of his wealth, and his actions were especially detestable. Therefore, it was decreed that in this life, one son and two daughters-in-law would repay the three lives, and also cause them to have no descendants, as this is how it was repaid. But considering that Chen was still a respected elder in this life, if he can perform good deeds, perhaps it can still be redeemed. The underworld officials praised his good intentions and granted Fan a twenty-year reprieve, until his son has a son, then the repayment will be made. Later, the son's illness indeed recovered.

    360

    刘云椒孝廉廷杰，直隶通州人，道光戊子科举人，中年不得志，放浪诗酒以卒。仁和许信臣中丞乃钊、宛平史叔平观察致康，其同年中最契合者也。死后附箕仙降于许氏，成诗文数百言，酷肖其生前所为。中丞愕然曰：“君是云椒欤？何以至是？”曰：“然，廷杰死三日矣，以赴状未至，故君未知也。前身本紫府吏，今世无大罪恶，一诣城隍司，不复拘束，因念君故，一来耳。”又一日，复附箕言：“妻孥茕苦，诸君存恤之，甚善。闻叔平有卵翼孤儿之意，尤可感也。”时叔平方谒选京师，得云椒凶问，念其子尚幼，恐以贫废学，欲挈之至官。甫起是念，未以告人，而鬼神于数千里外已预知之，亦可异矣。

    Explanation

    360

    Liu Yunjiao, the scholar-official Tingjie, was from Tongzhou under the direct jurisdiction of the central government. He passed the imperial examination during the Wu Zi year of the Daoguang era but did not achieve much success in his middle years, living a life of unrestrained indulgence in poetry and wine until his death. Xu Xincheng, the high-ranking official from Renhe with the given name Zao, and Shi Shuping, the inspector from Wanping with the given name Zhi Kang, were among his closest friends from the same examination year. After his death, Liu Yunjiao's spirit attached itself to a divination tool and descended to Xu's household, composing hundreds of words of poetry and prose that closely resembled his works from life. Xu Xincheng was startled and said, "Are you Liu Yunjiao? How did you come to this?" Liu replied, "Yes, I died three days ago. The official report has not yet arrived, so you were unaware. My former life was as an official in the Purple Palace. In this life, I have committed no great sins. I have gone to the City God's office and am no longer bound by earthly constraints. Because I thought of you, I came here." On another day, he again attached himself to the divination tool and said, "My wife and children are left alone and suffering. It would be very kind of you gentlemen to look after them. I have heard that Shi Shuping has the intention of protecting the orphan like a bird protects its young, which is especially touching." At that time, Shi Shuping was visiting the capital for selection into an official position and received the news of Liu Yunjiao's death. He thought about how Liu's son was still young and feared that poverty might prevent him from continuing his education, so he wanted to take him along to his official post. He had just had this thought but had not yet told anyone when the ghosts and spirits, thousands of miles away, already knew about it. This is indeed a remarkable occurrence.

    361

    杭州艮山门外有地曰横塘，其地有明兵部尚书胡端敏公祠。肇自明季，至国朝二百余年，列入祀典，春秋致祭。粤寇之乱，胡氏子姓凋零，祠亦毁于兵火。余门下士高海鹏年与胡氏有连，议复之。乃纠合仁和、钱唐两县绅士，具呈请于大府。时杨石泉中丞为浙抚，以乱后无可稽考，咨询闽浙总督衙门。展转年余，始于同治十三年二月复建胡端敏公祠，由杭州府遣官致祭，悉如故事。祭后三日，海忽梦有古衣冠者向之拱手。胡氏后人于是夕同得此梦者十人而九，是公之灵爽犹未泯也。公名世宁，字永清，弘治六年进士。正德中官江西副使，忤宸濠得罪，戍辽东。宸濠诛，复起至兵部尚书、太子太保。《明史》有传。

    Explanation

    361

    In the eastern suburbs of Hangzhou, there is a place called Hengtang, where the ancestral shrine of Hu Duanmin, the Ming Dynasty Minister of War, stands. Originating in the late Ming period, this shrine was included in the official rites for over two hundred years, with annual spring and autumn sacrifices. During the chaos of the Cantonese rebels, the Hu family's descendants dwindled, and the shrine was destroyed by fire. One of my disciples, Gao Haiyan, known as Pengnian, who had connections to the Hu family, proposed rebuilding it. He rallied gentry from Renhe and Qiantang counties and submitted a petition to the provincial administration. At that time, Yang Shiquan, the Viceroy of Zhejiang, found no records to verify the matter after the turmoil, so he consulted the Fujian-Zhejiang Governorate. After over a year of deliberation, the Hu Duanmin shrine was rebuilt in February of the thirteenth year of Tongzhi, and the Hangzhou government sent officials to offer sacrifices as per tradition. Three days after the ceremony, Gao Haiyan suddenly dreamed of an anciently dressed figure bowing to him. That very night, nine out of ten Hu family descendants shared the same dream, indicating that the spirit of the public official was still present. His name was Shining, with the courtesy name Yongqing. He passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi in the sixth year of Hongzhi. During the Zhengde era, he served as Deputy Governor of Jiangxi, where he offended Chen Hua and was exiled to Liaodong. After Chen Hua's execution, he was reinstated and eventually rose to the position of Minister of War and Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince. His biography can be found in the History of Ming.

    Example 6

    Source: 杭州艮山门外有地曰横塘，其地有明兵部尚书胡端敏公祠。肇自明季，至国朝二百余年，列入祀典，春秋致祭。粤寇之乱，胡氏子姓凋零，祠亦毁于兵火。余门下士高海鹏年与胡氏有连，议复之。乃纠合仁和、钱唐两县绅士，具呈请于大府。时杨石泉中丞为浙抚，以乱后无可稽考，咨询闽浙总督衙门。展转年余，始于同治十三年二月复建胡端敏公祠，由杭州府遣官致祭，悉如故事。祭后三日，海忽梦有古衣冠者向之拱手。胡氏后人于是夕同得此梦者十人而九，是公之灵爽犹未泯也。公名世宁，字永清，弘治六年进士。正德中官江西副使，忤宸濠得罪，戍辽东。宸濠诛，复起至兵部尚书、太子太保。《明史》有传。

    Translation: In the eastern suburbs of Hangzhou, there is a place called Hengtang, where the ancestral shrine of Hu Duanmin, the Ming Dynasty Minister of War, stands. Originating in the late Ming period, this shrine was included in the official rites for over two hundred years, with annual spring and autumn sacrifices. During the chaos of the Cantonese rebels, the Hu family's descendants dwindled, and the shrine was destroyed by fire. One of my disciples, Gao Haiyan, known as Pengnian, who had connections to the Hu family, proposed rebuilding it. He rallied gentry from Renhe and Qiantang counties and submitted a petition to the provincial administration. At that time, Yang Shiquan, the Viceroy of Zhejiang, found no records to verify the matter after the turmoil, so he consulted the Fujian-Zhejiang Governorate. After over a year of deliberation, the Hu Duanmin shrine was rebuilt in February of the thirteenth year of Tongzhi, and the Hangzhou government sent officials to offer sacrifices as per tradition. Three days after the ceremony, Gao Haiyan suddenly dreamed of an anciently dressed figure bowing to him. That very night, nine out of ten Hu family descendants shared the same dream, indicating that the spirit of the public official was still present. His name was Shining, with the courtesy name Yongqing. He passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi in the sixth year of Hongzhi. During the Zhengde era, he served as Deputy Governor of Jiangxi, where he offended Chen Hua and was exiled to Liaodong. After Chen Hua's execution, he was reinstated and eventually rose to the position of Minister of War and Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince. His biography can be found in

    362

    仁和魏芸阁士龙，余甲辰乡榜同年也。魏为是科解元，其岁年已六十矣。生平邃于经史，而旁通禅学。每日晨起，必先诵《金刚经》一遍，寒暑不辍。道光庚戌岁，年六十有六，凭几诵经，端坐而逝，右手犹作展卷之状。卒后有传其为神者。其门人蔡小西孝廉祖武为龙游校官。一夕梦入公廨中，见魏与项侮侣先生并坐于上，旁列小几。蔡至，为之起，且曰：“君来大好，此间案牍甚繁，可分劳也。”其时蔡尚无恙，越三日即逝。项先生名名达，亦仁和人，嘉庆丙子科举人，先通奉公同年也。

    Explanation

    362

    Renhe Wei Yunguo Shilong was a fellow examinee of mine from the Jiachen provincial examination. Wei was the top scholar of that year, and he was already sixty years old. Throughout his life, he was deeply versed in the classics and histories, and he also had a profound understanding of Chan Buddhism. Every morning, he would rise and first recite the Diamond Sutra once, without interruption throughout the cold and hot seasons. In the Gengxu year of Daoguang, when he was sixty-six, he was leaning on a table reciting the sutra when he passed away while sitting upright, with his right hand still in the posture of holding open a book. After his death, there were those who claimed he had become a deity. His disciple, Cai Xiaoxi, who was a successful candidate in the local examination and served as an official in charge of education in Longyou, had a dream one night in which he entered the public office and saw Wei and Mr. Xiang Wulü sitting together on the upper seat, with small tables placed beside them. When Cai arrived, they rose to greet him, and Mr. Xiang said, "It's great that you've come. There is a great deal of official paperwork here, and you can share the labor." At that time, Cai was still in good health, but three days later, he passed away. Mr. Xiang's name was Mingda, also from Renhe, and he was a successful candidate in the Bingzi year of Jiaqing, the same year as Mr. Tongfeng.

    363

    仁和魏条三农部大纲，僦居宣武门外，历有年所矣。道光二十年，忽来一狐仙，不见形状，但在空中与人言语，自称姓名曰胡美髯。颇解诗，与农部倡和甚欢，其诗皆书片纸从空飞下。诗句清新，书法亦遒劲，一时传为美谭。有术士过其门曰：“此室有妖气，请为驱除。”农部不之信，术者叹息而去。未几，都中大疫，农部与三子俱死。殆所谓妖孽者欤？

    　　杭城孙同伯，诸生也。元聘施氏，未娶而卒。光绪五年，复聘同里某氏女。婚有日矣，孙忽得疾，施氏之鬼附其体而言：“将与孙俱去。”孙父母苦求之，许迎其柩以葬，且立主家庙，皆不可。俄而孙竟卒。此鬼何强悍如此，或亦孙之数当尽欤？

    Explanation

    363

    Wei Tiao from Renhe, who was in charge of agricultural affairs, rented a residence outside the Xuanwu Gate and had lived there for many years. In the year 20 of the Daoguang era (1840), a fox spirit suddenly appeared. It did not have a visible form but communicated with people in the air, introducing itself as Hu Meiran, with "Meiran" meaning "beautiful beard." The fox spirit was quite knowledgeable in poetry and engaged in poetic exchanges with great joy with Wei Tiao. Its poems were written on small pieces of paper and flew down from the sky. The verses were fresh and elegant, and the handwriting was strong and vigorous, making the story a topic of beautiful conversation at the time. A magician passed by the door and said, "This room has an eerie atmosphere. I can remove the supernatural presence." Wei Tiao did not believe him, and the magician sighed and left. Shortly after, a major epidemic broke out in the capital, and Wei Tiao and his three sons all died. This might be what is meant by "supernatural evil."

    Sun Tongbo from Hangzhou was a student. He originally married into the Shi family but died before he could marry. In the year 5 of the Guangxu era (1879), he remarried a woman from the same hometown. The wedding was scheduled, but Sun Tongbo suddenly fell ill. The spirit of his first wife attached itself to his body and said, "I will leave with Sun Tongbo." Sun's parents pleaded with him, offering to bring her coffin for burial and set up a memorial tablet in the family temple, but neither request was accepted. Shortly after, Sun Tongbo died. How strong and persistent this spirit was, perhaps this was Sun's destiny.

    364

    道光十五年，杭城大疫，死者甚众，市中棺为之一空。武林门外有地名仓基，其地有金姓者于前一年除夕，闻门外有鬼声，俄又闻若有人言：“此家有节妇。”及元旦开门，则见墙上画一大红圈，异之，然亦谓是儿童辈所为耳。及夏间疫盛，邻比诸家无一免者，而金姓独无恙，始悟除夕红圈，乃鬼神为之以识别也。节妇姓钱氏，为金子梅都转之伯母，时守节已三十余年矣。余门下士高海乃节妇之外孙，为余言之。

    　　海又言：钱唐黄成甫茂才维城，博学工诗文，年三十余而卒。平生著作，零落无存。同治十二年，仁和钟雨辰殿撰将编诗话，致书海，托为搜罗。海与黄君有旧，遍搜其诗不得，耿耿于心。一夕，忽梦黄君来，次日即于旧帙中得其七律一首，且骇且喜，录寄雨辰，并述其梦。文士名心至死不泯，亦可悲也。昔顾侠君选元诗竣，见古衣冠者数百人来谢，当非[B220]语矣。

    Explanation

    364

    In the fifteenth year of the Daoguang era, a great epidemic broke out in Hangzhou, resulting in many deaths, and the city's coffins were emptied. Outside Wulin Gate, there is a place called Cangji, where a man surnamed Jin, living there, heard ghostly sounds outside his home on New Year's Eve of the previous year. Soon after, he heard someone speaking, saying, "This household has a chaste widow." On the first day of the new year, when he opened the door, he saw a large red circle drawn on the wall, which he found strange, though he also thought it was done by children. By summer, the epidemic worsened, affecting all neighboring households, but the Jin family remained unharmed. He then realized that the red circle on New Year's Eve was made by gods and spirits to identify the family. The chaste widow was surnamed Qian, the aunt of Jin Zimei, a vice minister, and she had been a widow for over thirty years. My student Gao Hai, who is the great-grandson of the chaste widow, told me this story.

    Gao Hai also mentioned: Huang Chengfu, a scholar from Qiantang, was highly learned and skilled in poetry and prose, but he died at the age of thirty or so. His life's works were scattered and lost. In the twelfth year of the Tongzhi era, Zhong Yuchen, a palace scholar from Renhe, was compiling a collection of poetic anecdotes and wrote to Gao Hai, asking him to gather materials. Gao had an old acquaintance with Huang and searched thoroughly for his poems but could not find them, keeping worried about it. One night, he suddenly dreamed that Huang came to him, and the next day, he found one seven-character regulated verse in old books, feeling both surprised and delighted. He copied and sent it to Zhong Yuchen, also describing the dream. A scholar's ambition persists until death, which is also pitiful. In the past, when Gu Xiajun finished selecting Yuan dynasty poetry, he saw hundreds of anciently dressed people come to thank him, which is not a fabrication.

    365

    钱唐有贝翁者，少有膂力，素以意气自负。一日，自城外被酒夜归，憩于白蜡桥下。瞥见一妇人趋过，觉有异，尾之行。抵一村舍，妇忽不见。叩门入，则其家止妇姑二人，是夜适反唇。因使视其妇，已扃户雉经矣，亟解县救之，得不死。感翁高义，以夜深止之宿。翁以其家无男子，不可，遂携灯独行。俄寒风自后来，林叶皆簌簌落。翁知为鬼，不之顾，鬼忽作声若相詈者。翁怒，返击之，鬼乃退。及翁行，又詈如初。翁益怒，穷追不已，复至于桥下，而鸡声四起，东方白矣。

    Explanation

    365

    In Qiantang, there was an old man named Bei who, when young, possessed great physical strength and always boasted of his spirit and courage. One day, after drinking late at night outside the city, he rested under the White Wax Bridge. He caught a glimpse of a woman passing by and felt something was strange, so he followed her. They reached a village house, and the woman suddenly disappeared. He knocked on the door and entered, where he found only two women, a daughter-in-law and her mother-in-law, who were quarreling that night. He asked to see the woman, and discovered that she had already hanged herself by closing the door and tying a rope around her neck. He quickly untied her and saved her, and she did not die. Grateful for the old man's noble deed, they kept him to stay for the night because it was late. The old man thought there was no man in the house, so it was not right, and he took a lamp and left alone. Soon, a cold wind came from behind, and the leaves in the forest fell with a rustling sound. The old man knew it was a ghost, and he did not pay attention to it. The ghost suddenly made a sound as if cursing. The old man became angry, turned around and hit it, and the ghost then retreated. When the old man continued walking, the ghost cursed again as before. The old man became even angrier and chased it relentlessly, returning to under the bridge. And the roosters crowed all around, and the east began to turn white.

    366

    仁和钟古春明经凤书，生平颇多善行。咸丰辛酉岁，杭城再陷，死之，其二子皆陷于贼。次子登甫孝廉世培时年甫十二，为贼所胁，已出城十余里，贼以其年幼力弱，弃之于途。是夜宿邮亭中，若有人推其背曰：“速行，速行！”登甫惊起，而昏黑不辨南北，其前隐隐有灯光，遂从之行。天明，至北新关，其地有吴姓者，知其为明经之子，收养于家。乱定后，于光绪元年中式副榜，次年登贤书。杭之人咸叹善人之有后也。

    Explanation

    366

    Renhe Zhonggǔ Chūnmíng Jīng Fèngshū, throughout his life, had many good deeds. In the Xin You year of the Xianfeng era, Hangzhou was recaptured, and he died. His two sons were captured by the rebels. His second son, Dengfu, who was a scholar-official named Shipai, was only twelve years old. He was coerced by the rebels and had already left the city for more than ten li. The rebels, thinking him too young and weak, abandoned him on the road. That night, he stayed at a post station. As if someone pushed his back and said, "Hurry up, hurry up!" Dengfu was startled and woke up, but it was dark and he couldn't tell north from south. In front of him, there was a faint light, so he followed the light. When it was light, he arrived at Beixin Pass. There was a person surnamed Wu in that place who knew him to be the son of a scholar-official. He took him in and raised him in his home. After the chaos was settled, in the first year of Guangxu, he passed the imperial examination as a supplementary candidate. The following year, he was listed as a talented scholar. The people of Hangzhou all praised that good people have good descendants.

    367

    杭有高君者，勇于为善，其少壮时已不杀生，晚年并断荤血。然不奉佛，而好言养生，能辟谷数日不饥。年八十余，无疾而逝。有友人客于成都，一日，忽遇高于途，惊曰：“君高年来此何为？”时其友适将旋里，因约与偕。高曰：“我尚思游峨嵋山，君其先归。有扇一握，麈尾一柄，烦君携至我家，并为传语儿曹，我此来安好，勿念也。”其友抵家访之，则高死五月矣，成都相见之日，即其死日。一扇一麈尾，皆殉葬物也。此事载高氏家谱。予门下士高海，其族孙也，为余言之。

    Explanation

    367

    In Hangzhou, there was a gentleman named Gao who was courageous in doing good. In his youth, he abstained from killing living creatures, and in his later years, he also stopped consuming meat and blood. Although he did not follow Buddhism, he was fond of discussing health preservation and could fast from grains for several days without feeling hungry. He lived to be over eighty years old and passed away peacefully without any illness. One day, a friend of his was visiting Chengdu when he unexpectedly met Gao on the road. The friend was surprised and asked, "Why have you come here, Master Gao?" At the time, his friend was about to return home, so he invited Gao to join him. Gao replied, "I still wish to visit Mount Emei. Please go back first. I have a fan and a fly whisk. Could you bring them to my home and convey to my children that I am well here and they should not worry." When his friend returned home and visited, he discovered that Gao had already died five months ago. The day they met in Chengdu was the day Gao had died. The fan and fly whisk were objects buried with him. This story is recorded in the Gao family genealogy. My student, Gao Hai, is a descendant of Gao and told me about it.

    368

    同治八年五月，苏城大风，民间所セ衣服（“セ”，俗字。然香山诗云“中庭セ服玩”，则此字唐已有之），皆随风而起，飞舞满天，有堕十里外者。苏俗率以稻秆为薪，十余斤为一束。有陆氏积薪于庭，亦被吹去，愈上愈高，望之若止三四寸者，移时不见。余门人王梦薇廷鼎时居护龙街，忽堕一竹筐，筐外书某年某月琴川黄氏置，盖常熟人家物也，距苏已七十余里矣。

    　　梦薇又言：其家旧住平望镇柘湖之滨，门前多古树，最古者一槐，已二百年矣。一日大雷雨，槐为迅雷所击，分而为二。雷所劈处有烟焰上腾，越日乃止。邻比聚观，并无他异，惟于树根下掘得黄脂斗许，大小不等，而皆坚凝如石，椎而碎之，又甚黏腻。世称茯苓、琥珀生于松下，猪零生于枫下，此或其类欤？惜不知何名，亦未详所用也。

    Explanation

    368

    In the eighth year of the Tongzhi era (1869), a fierce wind swept through Suzhou. Clothes stored in bamboo or wooden boxes (笥) were carried into the sky, dancing and swirling everywhere, and some even fell as far as ten miles away. The people of Suzhou generally used rice straw as fuel, bundling it in quantities of ten or more pounds. A man named Lu had accumulated a large pile of firewood in his courtyard, which was also carried away by the wind. The firewood rose higher and higher, appearing to be only a few inches high from a distance, and eventually disappeared after some time.

    One of my students, Wang Mengwei Tingding, lived on Hulong Street at that time. Suddenly, a bamboo basket fell on his house. The basket was marked with the year and month and indicated that it belonged to the Huang family of Qincun, a place in Changshu, which is more than seventy miles from Suzhou.

    Wang Mengwei also recounted that his family used to live near Zhehu Lake in Pingwang Town. There were many old trees in front of their house, the oldest being a locust tree that was already 200 years old. One day, a heavy thunderstorm occurred, and the locust tree was struck by lightning and split into two parts. Where the lightning struck, smoke and flames rose, but it stopped the next day. Neighbors gathered to watch, and there were no other strange occurrences. However, they found about a dou of yellow fat at the base of the tree. This substance varied in size, all hard and solid like stone, and when struck with a hammer, it broke apart, revealing a very sticky and greasy texture. It is said that poria and amber grow under pine trees, and a type of fungus grows under maple trees. Perhaps this substance is of a similar kind, though its name and uses remain unknown.

    369

    寿昌多山，故多虎豹。有童姓者居于眉林，其家畜一母猪甚大，一岁生子十余，内有一头，短颔修尾，与凡豕绝异，数日顿长大，食倍他豕。童老恶之，屏勿使食，欲使自毙。其邻叟曰：“此豕浅毛被体，粗具文理，状颇彪炳，得毋虎豹之属欤？”童老曰：“若然，亟当杀之，为虺弗摧，为蛇奈何？”磨刀霍霍，亲赴豕{艹立}.豕忽突出，追之不及，入于山中。数月后，有一豹径诣童氏，入其旧阑，若甚驯扰。群豕见之，&lt；豕句&gt；然惊走，豹徘徊良久乃去。嗣后月或数至，且与他豹及虎俱来，出入村舍，遇人不噬。然村中人甚厌苦之，聚众驱逐，如是数次，乃不复来。

    Explanation

    369

    Shouchang is a region with many mountains, hence it is home to many tigers and leopards. There lived an old man named Tong in Meilin, who kept a large female pig. In one year, the pig gave birth to more than ten piglets. Among them, one had a short snout and a long tail, distinguishing it from ordinary pigs. Within a few days, the piglet grew rapidly and ate twice as much as the other pigs. The old man disliked the piglet and refused to feed it, hoping it would die of starvation. His neighbor remarked, "This pig has thin fur covering its body, with a pattern that makes it look somewhat striking. Could it be of the tiger or leopard kind?" The old man replied, "If so, it should be killed immediately, lest it grow into a dangerous creature." He sharpened his knife and prepared to kill the piglet. However, the piglet suddenly dashed out, and the old man could not catch it. It escaped into the mountains.

    Several months later, a leopard came directly to the old man's house and entered the old pig pen. The leopard seemed very tame and peaceful. The other pigs saw the leopard and ran away in fear. The leopard lingered for a while before leaving. Afterward, the leopard visited occasionally, sometimes accompanied by other leopards and tigers. They entered and left the village without attacking people. However, the villagers found the animals annoying and drove them away several times until they no longer returned.

    370

    江西李某，以贸易往来苏杭，亦老于江湖者也。尝自杭州归，途遇少年求载，同舟者咸拒之。李见其衣服蓝缕，踯躅江岸，心怜之，语同舟者曰：“孤客无归，大可怜悯，何惜此一席地乎？”乃招之登舟。及至常山，将舍舟而陆，少年顾诸客曰：“萍水相逢，幸附船骥。今行将分手，颇思稍尽微意，供诸君一饱，愿闻所嗜。”诸客曰：“昨过某处，见市中馒头颇佳，今思之，食指犹动。既承雅意，得此足矣，他不敢请。”时距其地已远，诸客姑以此难之，且知其贫，必不能办也。少年曰：“可。”乃还至其所卧处，蒙被而卧。众呼之，曰：“毋扰清梦。”众曰：“吾曹饥矣，馒头安在？”曰：“诸君何汲汲若是！顷方起馊裹馅耳。”卧如故。久之，觉热气缕缕自其被中出，众异焉。或曰：“此渴睡汉鼻息耳。

    Explanation

    370

    A merchant from Jiangxi, named Li, traveled back and forth between Suzhou and Hangzhou for trade. He was a seasoned traveler, having seen much of the world. Once, on his way back from Hangzhou, he encountered a young man on the shore of the river, asking to be taken along. The other passengers on the boat all refused him. Li saw that the young man was poorly dressed and standing alone on the riverbank, feeling sorry for him. He said to the others, "This traveler has no place to go, which is very pitiful. Why not let him have a seat here?" And so, he invited the young man to board the boat.

    When they reached Changshan and were about to disembark, the young man looked at the passengers and said, "We met by chance, and you have been kind enough to allow me to ride with you. Now we are about to part ways, and I would like to show my gratitude by providing you all with a meal. Please tell me what you would like to eat." The passengers replied, "We passed by a certain place yesterday and saw some excellent steamed buns in the market. We still remember them, and we are very hungry. If you can provide us with those, we will be very grateful. We don't dare to ask for anything more." At this point, they were already far from the place where the steamed buns were sold, and the passengers were jokingly making it difficult for the young man, knowing that he was poor and could not possibly fulfill their request.

    The young man said, "Very well." Then he returned to his sleeping place and lay down under the blanket. The passengers called out to him, saying, "Don't disturb our peaceful sleep." One of them said, "We are all hungry. Where are the steamed buns?" The young man replied, "Why are you so eager? I just got up to wrap the filling." He continued to lie there as before. After a long time, hot steam began to rise from under the blanket. The passengers were surprised. Someone said, "This must be the snoring of a thirsty sleeper."

    371

    ”俄少年起曰：“馒头出笼矣！”发其衾，累累者皆是也。众客饱啖，咸果其腹。异而叩其术，笑不言。食已登陆，独约李会于三里外。李至，少年已先在，出数纸为赠。视之，自玉山起至李之乡里止，一路舟车悉为代雇，此则各牙郎之票据也。李怪问之，少年曰：“不敢相欺，某乃江湖所谓铁算盘者也。不必探囊去箧，而能以术取人财，舟中诸客所赍，已各分其半矣。以君长者，故丝毫未取，且为君代雇舟车，以报厚意。”李大惊，又甚感之，再三致谢。少年曰：“舟车之费，皆取之诸客，何谢焉？”李曰：“相距且数百里，何能咄嗟而办？”少年曰：“我辈于千里外物不难立致，况数百里内，直咫尺耳！”又谓李曰：“江湖间如我辈者不少，君此后橐中宜置五谷少许，或官府印花，方不为术士所算也。”遂别李而去。李持票据示牙行，无不合契，沿途舟车，悉应付如数。盗亦有道，斯之谓与？

    Explanation

    371

    A Young Man's Gift

    Soon, a young man stood up and said, "The steamed buns are out of the steamer!" He opened his quilt, and there were steamed buns piled up in abundance. The guests ate their fill, and all were satisfied. They found it strange and asked about his method, but he just smiled and did not speak. After eating, they went ashore, and the young man invited Li separately to meet three miles away. When Li arrived, the young man was already there, and he took out several pieces of paper as a gift. Looking at them, they were from Yushan to Li's hometown, and all the boats and vehicles were hired on his behalf. These were the tickets from the various brokers. Li was surprised and asked him, "I dare not deceive you," the young man said, "I am known in the world as the Iron Abacus. Without searching through bags or chests, I can use my skills to take people's money. The money brought by the guests on the boat has already been divided in half. Because you are a gentleman, I have taken nothing, and I have hired the boats and vehicles for you, to repay your kindness." Li was greatly surprised and very grateful, and he thanked him repeatedly. The young man said, "The cost of the boats and vehicles was taken from the guests, why thank me?" Li said, "It is hundreds of miles away, how can it be done so quickly?" The young man replied, "We can easily obtain things from thousands of miles away, let alone hundreds of miles, it is just a short distance!" He then told Li, "There are many like me in the world. You should keep a little grain or official stamps in your bag, so that you won't be tricked by magicians." He then bid farewell to Li and left. Li showed the tickets to the brokers, and they all matched. All the boats and vehicles along the way were provided as promised. Even thieves have their ways, isn't this what it means?

    372

    门人汉阳邬梅仙，言其家好为扶箕之戏，名其坛曰“驻云坛”。有明五先生者屡降是坛，自言乃元时济州一狐也。少年时曾蛊某氏女，为雷神追击，几殆，其后潜修二百余年，始挂名仙籍。又言修仙之道，其过莫大于犯淫，其功莫重于惜命，故二百年来惟以惜命为事，虽一草一木，有生意者均不忍攀折也。叩以休咎，多不答，喜谈诗及古事，如云宸濠胁有肉鳞，王阳明体颀而黑，皆世所不知也。或问：“先生何不以狐为讳？”曰：“由狐而仙，譬如白屋中出公卿，方以为荣，何讳之有！”

    　　临平有某氏嫠妇，独居悲花庵中，长斋奉佛有年矣。畜一猫，亦不食荤血，每食饲以白饭一盂，上置豆腐一方，呼而戒之曰：“猫，尔其省穑而食之！”猫嗷然若有知者，先食白饭，饭尽，乃食豆腐，日日如是。余外姊适周氏者之女实亲见之。余长女闻而笑曰：“此妇所修未知何如，此猫必成正果矣。”

    Explanation

    372

    Disciple Wu Meixian from Hanyang mentioned that his family enjoyed the practice of spirit writing, naming their altar "Zhu Yun Tan." Five enlightened masters, known as "Ming Wu Xianshen," frequently descended to this altar, claiming to be a fox spirit from Jizhou during the Yuan dynasty. In his youth, this fox had bewitched a woman from a certain family and was pursued by thunder deities, nearly perishing. Afterward, he spent over two centuries in hidden cultivation, eventually being registered in the books of immortals. He also spoke of the path to immortality, stating that the greatest sin is committing sexual misconduct, and the most significant virtue is cherishing life. Therefore, for the past two centuries, he has devoted himself solely to cherishing life, refraining from harming even the smallest living things, such as grass and trees. When asked about auspicious or inauspicious signs, he often did not respond, but he enjoyed discussing poetry and ancient events, such as the rumor that Liu Ji (Chenghuo) had a fleshly scale on his chest and that Wang Yangming was tall and dark-skinned—details unknown to the world. Someone asked, "Why don't you hide your fox identity?" He replied, "Transforming from a fox to an immortal is like rising from humble origins to high social status, a source of honor, so why should I hide it!"

    In Linping, there was a widow from a certain family who lived alone in the small temple called "Bei Hua An," practicing long-term vegetarianism and devoting herself to Buddhism for many years. She kept a cat that also did not eat meat or blood. Every meal, she fed the cat a bowl of white rice with a piece of tofu placed on top, calling out to it and warning, "Cat, you must save and eat this!" The cat seemed to understand, first eating the rice and then the tofu, every day. My cousin's daughter, who married into the Zhou family, actually witnessed this. My eldest daughter, upon hearing about it, laughed and said, "It is unknown what this woman has practiced, but this cat will surely achieve enlightenment."

    373

    有某氏者祟于狐，延道士驱之。道士星冠象简，伏而拜表，两童子执剑侍。乃道士未起，而童子忽失所在。俄闻有声出自瓮，视之，则二童在焉。瓮固不甚大，不知何以能容二童子，而二童子又何从入瓮也。共议破而出之，一椎甫击，而童子在瓮中呼痛。于是道士术穷，不得已，祷于狐。闻空中语曰：“既尔知罪，可避去，我出童子。”乃各避他所，而童子出矣。瓮初不破，亦不知其何以出也。余前记慈溪小灵峰关牒事，兹又闻有一庙，淫祀也。庙中之神不知何姓，塑一女像配之，则张氏女也。初张氏女从母至小灵峰进香，归而自言神悦其貌，将以为妻。

    Explanation

    373

    A certain family was plagued by a fox spirit, so they invited a Taoist priest to exorcise it. The priest wore a star crown and an elephant tablet, prostrated himself, and offered a petition, with two children holding swords in attendance. But before the priest could rise, the children suddenly disappeared. Soon, a sound was heard coming from a jar. When they looked, the two children were inside. The jar was not very large, and it is unclear how it could accommodate the two children, or how the children managed to enter the jar. They discussed breaking the jar to let them out. As soon as one struck it with a hammer, the children cried out in pain from inside the jar. The priest then found himself at a loss, and was forced to pray to the fox. A voice from the air said, "Since you know your fault, you may leave, and I will let the children out." So everyone moved to another place, and the children emerged. The jar was not broken, and it is still unclear how the children came out. I previously recorded the story of the Cixi Xiaoling Peak and its temple, and now I have heard of another temple, one that engages in excessive and improper worship. The god in the temple is unknown by name, but a statue of a woman was paired with it, which turned out to be a girl from the Zhang family. The girl first went to the Xiaoling Peak with her mother to burn incense, and upon returning, she said that the god was pleased with her appearance and intended to marry her.

    374

    自是闭门不与人接，而时有香气出其房中，儿女燕亵语亦往往闻焉。家人患之，莫可如何。居数月，女腹彭亨矣。母以告其父，父曰：“处子而有孕，是为门户羞。”立逼女死。神凭女而言：“苟杀是女，必祸尔家！”其父怒曰：“吾不畏也，必杀女！”女知不免，泣曰：“吾为此一块肉而死，凡妇女怀任者我必之。”乃于夜中投水死，尸逆流达于庙前。其夕庙祝梦神曰：“起，起，迎夫人。”不解所谓。次日，其家失女，疑其逃也，四出迹之，而得其尸。其兄愤怒，至庙中，批神颊无算，又以指爪碎其&lt；面甫&gt；。灵峰人闻其事，乃塑女像，称曰夫人，至今黾妇辄祷焉，香火甚盛。二月某日为夫人生日，红裙绿鬓相望，而神面爪痕竟不可去，屡经改塑，瘢如初。

    Explanation

    374

    From then on, she closed herself off and did not interact with anyone, yet there were sometimes fragrances emanating from her room, and the intimate conversations between husband and wife were often heard. Her family was troubled by this, but could do nothing about it. After several months, her belly swelled. Her mother told her father, who said, "A virgin who is pregnant is a disgrace to the family." He immediately forced his daughter to die. The spirit possessed the daughter and spoke, "If you kill this girl, you will surely bring disaster upon your family!" The father angrily said, "I do not fear it, I will definitely kill the girl!" The daughter knew she could not escape, and wept, saying, "I will die for this piece of flesh, and all women carrying a pregnancy will be protected by me." She then drowned herself in the water at night, and her body floated upstream to the temple. That night, the temple attendant dreamed that the deity said, "Rise, rise, welcome Madame." He did not understand what this meant. The next day, the family lost their daughter, suspecting she had fled, and searched for her everywhere, but found her body. Her brother was furious and went to the temple, slapping the deity's face countless times, and with his fingers and claws, he shattered the face of the deity. The people of Lingfeng heard about the incident and sculpted a statue of the daughter, calling her "Madame." Until today, women who are pregnant or giving birth always pray to her, and the incense and fire are very prosperous. On a certain day in February, it is Madame's birthday, and red skirts and green hair are seen everywhere, yet the marks of the deity's face and claws could not be removed, even after multiple re-sculptings, the scars remained as they were.

    375

    杭有徐翁者，家于清波门之水沟巷。每岁暮祀神，必以彘首一、鸡一、鱼一，俗所谓三牲也。既撤，辄失之，初疑奴辈所窃，又疑猫犬食之，然无全失之理，又不应岁岁如是。一岁，祀神毕，徐翁亲以筠筐盛之，而县之于梁，自设卧榻于侧，卧而侦之。人定后，忽有瓦甓声出自厨下。无何，一人于于然来，头角崭然，若庙中所塑牛头之状，以头戴筐而去。翁徐起迹之，则至厨下而没，乃以物识其处。明日，集家人掘之，不数尺，见有阶级，徐翁疑其中有窖藏之物，自入视之。

    Explanation

    375

    In Hangzhou, there lived an old man named Xu, who resided in Shuigou Lane near Qingbo Gate. Every year at the end of the year, he would offer a pig's head, a chicken, and a fish to the gods, which were traditionally known as the "three sacrificial animals." After the offerings were removed, they would always disappear. Initially, he suspected that the servants had stolen them, or that cats and dogs had eaten them. However, it didn't make sense for everything to be completely lost, and it shouldn't happen every year. One year, after the ritual was completed, Old Xu personally placed the offerings in a bamboo basket and hung it from the beam. He set up his bed beside it and lay down to watch. After the hour of man, there was a sudden sound of tiles and bricks coming from the kitchen. Soon, a person came slowly and deliberately, with a prominent head and horns, resembling the head of a cow carved in the temple. The person wore the basket on his head and left. Old Xu followed the trail slowly and found that the person had gone to the kitchen and disappeared. He marked the spot with an object. The next day, he gathered his family to dig at the marked spot. They did not dig very deep, but soon saw steps. Old Xu suspected that there might be hidden treasures inside and went in to see.

    376

    阶尽，忽隐隐有光，曲折而进，屋宇俨然，漆灯荧荧未灭，光盖从此出也。又进，则有朱棺一具，巨绠县之，明器之属罗列左右。徐取其铜爵一藏之怀中，尚徘徊其间，冀有所得。忽左室门辟，牛头人自其内出，徐骇极而奔，牛头人亦从之，及阶而止。众援翁出，即以土掩之，顾视翁色若死灰，汗犹涔涔也。探怀中，出铜爵，古色黝然，以插花，无不活，且能结实，洵非近代物也。此必唐宋人古墓，但牛头人不知何怪。《周官》方相氏职曰：“及墓，入圹，以戈击四隅，&lt；区攴&gt；方良。”此牛头人其诸所谓方良与？

    Explanation

    376

    At the end of the steps, a faint light suddenly appeared, and the path twisted and turned forward. The buildings stood in orderly rows, and a lacquer lamp still glowed dimly, its light seemingly emanating from there. Proceeding further, they found a red coffin suspended by a thick rope, with burial objects arranged on both sides. Slowly, they took one of the bronze wine vessels and hid it in their chest, still lingering in the place, hoping to find something more. Suddenly, the door of the left room opened, and a figure with a cow's head emerged from inside. The person ran in extreme fear, and the cow-headed figure followed, stopping at the steps. The crowd helped the old man out, immediately covering the site with soil. Looking at the old man, his face was ashen, and he was still sweating profusely. They reached into their chest and pulled out the bronze wine vessel, which had an ancient and dark color. When planted with flowers, they all came to life, and the vessel could even bear fruit—indeed, it was not a recent object. This must be an ancient tomb from the Tang or Song dynasty, but the cow-headed figure was an unknown monster. The Zhou Li records the duties of the fangxiangshi (ritual official): "Upon reaching the tomb, entering the grave, striking the four corners with a halberd, to ward off the fangliang (evil spirits)." Could this cow-headed figure be the fangliang mentioned in the text?

    377

    张某，绍兴人，携妻子至杭赁屋而居。其妻自是多病，张疑其不服水土，使医治之。一夕，忽作杭音，语张曰：“此老妇之屋，汝何得居此？”张知有异，以屋券示之曰：“我所赁也。”曰：“屋虽汝赁，然其下我藏有金银，今尔居此，或盗我财，奈何？”张闻其语，艳之，乃曰：“尔附我妻而言，必为泉下人矣。金银皆人间所用之物，尔无用焉，苦守奚为？不如指以示我，我得尔财，为尔广作佛事，则彼此均有利益。

    Explanation

    377

    Zhang, a native of Shaoxing, took his wife and children to Hangzhou to rent a house. His wife fell ill frequently, and Zhang suspected that she was not adapting to the local climate and environment. He had a doctor treat her. One night, she suddenly spoke in the Hangzhou dialect and told Zhang, "This is an old woman's house. How can you live here?" Zhang knew something was amiss and showed her the lease agreement, saying, "This is what I rented." She replied, "Even though the house is rented by you, there are hidden treasures (gold and silver) below. You might steal my wealth. What shall I do?" Zhang was tempted by her words and said, "You speak through my wife, so you must be a ghost. The treasures are things used by people in the world, and you have no use for them. Why keep them? It would be better if you show them to me. I will get your wealth and make many Buddhist rituals for you, so both of us will benefit."

    378

    不然藏地下，虽至百年，不获其用，甚无谓也。”曰：“汝言亦有理，但须以汝子为我子，我始以所藏付之。”张欣然呼其子至，拜于床下。其妻抚摩者再，仍作杭语曰：“所藏即在床下。”如言掘之，得一坛，缄封甚密。发之，惟洋钱三枚，簪数事而已。以告，则曰：“老妇耄而善忘，固不在此，当在户限下耳。”复掘之，得二瓮，一则满盛清水，一则贮铁钉无数，已生锈矣。又以告，其妻作杭音，呶呶不已，谓乾没其所藏金。张无如何，尽以前所得者易纸钱焚之，扰如故，乃别赁屋而迁居焉，其妻之病始瘳。

    Explanation

    378

    Otherwise, if hidden underground, even after a hundred years, it would not be used, which is very pointless." He said, "Your words are also reasonable, but you must consider your son as my son, and only then will I entrust my hidden treasure to him." Zhang happily called his son over, and the son knelt before the bed. Zhang's wife patted him twice and then spoke in Hangzhou dialect, saying, "The hidden treasure is right under the bed." Following her words, they dug and found a jar, tightly sealed. Upon opening it, there were only three foreign coins and a few hairpins inside. When they told her, she said, "The old woman is forgetful and has already forgotten it, so it must not be here; it should be under the threshold." They dug again and found two jars, one filled with clear water and the other containing countless rusted iron nails. When they told her again, she continued to nag in Hangzhou dialect, accusing Zhang of defrauding her hidden gold. Zhang had no choice but to exchange all the previously obtained items for paper money and burn them. Despite this, the situation remained troublesome, so he rented another house and moved. Only then did his wife's illness finally subside.

    379

    邬梅仙言：其友金某者，死后凭其家女奴而哭告其母曰：“儿寿不止此，特死于庸医耳。阴律，凡寿未尽者，必置之枉死城中，阴风冷雾，惨不可言。幸为儿多焚纸钱，庶可买他鬼自代。”问或为主者所觉奈何？曰：“每日以洋钱六枚赂主者，即无事矣。他鬼亦主者所顾募也。”问：“冥中亦有洋钱乎？”曰：“阳世之物，冥中皆有之，但多焚纸钱，一一可致也。”其母许之，女奴乃苏，问之茫然也。

    Explanation

    379

    Wu Mei Xian said: Her friend Jin, after his death, appeared through the family's female servant to cry and report to his mother, saying, "My life span was not supposed to end here; I only died due to a mediocre doctor. According to the laws of the underworld, anyone whose life span has not ended is placed in the City of Wrongful Death, where the cold winds and fog are too terrible to describe. Fortunately, if you burn a lot of paper money for me, perhaps you can buy another ghost to take my place." She asked, "What if the supervisor notices?" He replied, "Give six silver dollars to the supervisor every day, and there will be no trouble. The other ghosts are also hired by the supervisor." She asked, "Are there silver dollars in the underworld too?" He replied, "Everything in the world of the living also exists in the underworld. Just burn a lot of paper money, and you can obtain them one by one." Her mother agreed, and the female servant then woke up, but she had no memory of what happened.

    380

    海盐西门外有吴姓者，饶于赀。一婢名秋华，年二十矣，颇有姿首。光绪己卯岁四月中，忽有美丈夫据而淫之，既乃谓婢曰：“为我告尔主，明日扫除后屋以待，屋中什物好为陈设。倘有不备，或备而不华美，祸且立至。”婢告其主母，于是一家尽知之。吴翁以为妾，不之信。次日午后，有肩舆入其门，至中庭而止。舆丁启其帘，则虚无人也，惊曰：“吾在东门外，有少年赁吾舆，令舁至尔家，今安在欤？岂其鬼也？”因述其人年二十余，衣罗衫，手纨扇，丰采丽都，听其语，为冠盖中人，非本地乡音也。翁知有异，入内探问，则婢已失所在。翁室中有床一具，金钩翠幔，颇极富丽，亦转瞬失之。因觇之后屋，则沙石飞击，人不能近。

    Explanation

    380

    In the western gate of Haiyan, there lived a man surnamed Wu who was rich in wealth. He had a maid named Autumn Flower, who was twenty years old and quite beautiful. In the fourth month of the year 1889 during the reign of Emperor Guangxu, a handsome man suddenly came and took advantage of her. Afterward, he told the maid, "Tell your master to clean and prepare the back room for me tomorrow, and arrange the household items beautifully. If anything is missing or not luxurious enough, disaster will come immediately." The maid informed her mistress, and soon the entire household knew about it. The old man, Wu, thought it was a concubine and did not believe it. The next afternoon, a sedan chair was brought into his door and stopped in the courtyard. The sedan chair bearer opened the curtain, but there was no one inside. He was shocked and said, "I was at the eastern gate, and a young man rented my sedan chair, ordering it to be carried to your house. Where is he now? Could he be a ghost?" He described the man as being in his twenties, wearing a silk robe, holding a white fan, with an elegant and handsome appearance. His speech was that of someone from the upper class, not speaking the local dialect. The old man knew something was strange and went inside to inquire, but the maid had already disappeared. In the old man's room, there was a bed with golden hooks and green curtains, extremely luxurious, but it also disappeared instantly. He then looked into the back room, where sand and stones were flying around, and people could not approach.

    381

    有顷，婢自内出，传少年之语，属主人唤傅婢一人为之上头；又须备盛馔一席，次者两席；又须青蚨四贯，以备赏犒。“不从其言，且火尔屋！”翁惧，如言备之，而家中衣服器皿，其佳者悉被摄去，虽什袭深藏，亦不得免。明日，婢又传言，须循俗例，饣Й女三日，酒食之外，宜有杂戏侑觞。一日为十番鼓，二日为口技，三日为盲女弹词。亦曲从之。有好事者请入视新妇，亦不之拒，但见婢盛饰坐床头，房中纸醉金迷，五色眩目，余无所见。嗣是每日必有所需，间数日辄一出，出必舆，舆者但闻其语，不见其形也。吴翁苦之，将使人走江西诉于真人府。一日，少年忽谓婢曰：“我今且去矣。室中物具在，汝苟念伉俪情，一月之内，慎勿移动；一月不至，则永诀矣。”言已而去。吴翁即时移其物于他所，呼婢出嫁之，其后竟不复至。

    Explanation

    381

    After a while, a maid came out from inside and conveyed the young man's message: "Order the master to call a female servant to do the new bride's hair; also, prepare a lavish feast for one table and two secondary tables; additionally, prepare four strings of money for rewards. 'If you do not follow my words, I will burn your house!'" The old man, fearing the threat, prepared everything as instructed. However, all the fine clothes and utensils in the house were taken away, even those carefully stored away. The next day, the maid relayed another message: "Follow the custom, feed the bride for three days, and besides the wine and food, there should be various entertainments to accompany the drinking. On the first day, it will be ten drum performances; on the second day, oral performances; and on the third day, a blind woman will perform narrative songs." The old man reluctantly complied. Someone who was curious asked to see the new bride, and the maid did not refuse. They saw only the maid, who was elaborately dressed and sitting at the head of the bed. The room was filled with luxurious decorations, dazzling with colors, but they saw nothing else. From then on, something was needed every day, and every few days, the maid would leave in a sedan chair, and those who carried her could only hear her voice but not see her form. The old man Wu was greatly troubled by this and intended to send someone to report to the Taoist priest's office in Jiangxi. One day, the young man suddenly told the maid, "I will be leaving now. Everything in the room is still here. If you remember our marital affection, do not move anything within one month; if I do not return within one month, it will be our final farewell." After saying this, he left. The old man Wu immediately moved the items to another place, called the maid to marry her off, and after that, the young man never returned.

    382

    海宁硖石镇之南有地曰大虹村，其地有吴姓者，本农家子也。后以水旱荒其田，弃而逐什一之利。久之，家稍裕，遂服贾于外，恒数月不归。其妻陈氏，虽农女而颇有姿，邻里诸少年皆艳之，然陈贞洁白守，无可乘也。有小姑，色仅中人，而愁眉龋齿，作诸媚态，惑之者颇众。性狡工谗，常于母前短其嫂，母信之，故陈恒郁郁，久之成疾。每疾发，心辄大痛，四体如冰，终日不暖，惟夜卧而痛止，而体亦温和，心异之。一夕睡醒，觉有人并枕卧，大骇，推枕而起，则杳矣。惶怖无措，解带欲自经，忽见少女向之敛衽，笑而言曰：“姊何卤莽，以侬为男子欤？”陈问：“卿人邪？鬼也？”女曰：“侬非人非鬼，实则狐耳。已为姊按摩四夕矣，再如是三夕，病即全愈。

    Explanation

    382

    To the south of Hai Ning Xiaoshi Town, there is a place called Da Hong Village. In this village, there was a man named Wu, who was originally a farmer's son. Later, due to drought and flood, his fields became barren, and he gave up farming to pursue a profit of one-tenth, which meant he engaged in trade and was often away for months. His wife, Chen, though a farmer's daughter, had quite a beautiful appearance. The young men in the neighborhood all admired her, but Chen was virtuous and pure, and there was no way to take advantage of her.

    She had a younger sister-in-law who was of average appearance, with a frowning face and crooked teeth, and she made various alluring gestures, deceiving many. She was cunning and skilled at slander, often speaking ill of her sister-in-law in front of their mother, who believed her, thus Chen always felt depressed and eventually developed an illness.

    Every time the illness struck, she would feel intense pain in her heart, her limbs would feel as cold as ice, and she would not feel warm all day. Only when she slept at night would the pain subside, and her body would feel warm. She found this strange.

    One night, after waking up, she felt someone lying beside her and was greatly frightened. She pushed the pillow aside and got up, but then she was gone. Terrified and at a loss, she untied her belt and was about to hang herself. Suddenly, she saw a young girl folding her sleeves and smiling at her, saying, "Are you a person? Or a ghost?" The girl replied, "I am neither a person nor a ghost. In fact, I am a fox. I have been massaging you for four nights. If you continue for three more nights, your illness will be completely cured."

    383

    ”陈见其修眉秀项，笑靥嫣然，甚爱之。与俱坐，问所自来，曰：“侬亦吴姓，小字细细，旧住海宁潮神宫，今宫圮，故一家俱他徙。侬与姊有缘，来相伴耳。”自是女不去，惟陈得见之。小姑闻嫂与人絮语，疑有所私，告其母。母怒，执梃而往，先于窗隙窥之。陈独坐灯下，方缝纫故衣，寂无他人。母以女诳己也，将还问之，至其室，则见女正与邻子狎，状甚亵。母愤甚，奔入，奋梃挞之，邻子尚抱持女不释。女号呼，母恐声闻于外，忍怒走出。女哭詈终夕，罔测其故。盖女虽与邻子私，是夕固独宿也，细细幻形以挫辱之耳，然自此百口莫辨矣。居月余，细细忽辞陈去，曰：“明日郎君至矣。”明日，吴果至，陈为述其事。吴欲一见，陈曰：“渠约一月复来，且俟其来谋之。

    Explanation

    383

    Tchen saw her with long eyebrows and a graceful neck, her smile charming and radiant, and he loved her greatly. He sat with her and asked where she came from. She replied, "I am also of the Wu surname, my pet name is Xi Xi, and I used to live in the temple of the Tide God in Hai Ning. Now that the temple has collapsed, our family has moved elsewhere. I have a predestined connection with my sister, so I came to accompany you." From then on, the woman did not leave, and only Tchen could see her. The younger sister heard her sister-in-law talking casually with someone and suspected they had a secret affair, so she told their mother. The mother became angry and went with a stick in hand, first peeping through a crack in the window. Tchen was sitting alone under the lamp, sewing old clothes, and there was no one else present. The mother thought the woman had deceived her and was about to go back to ask her, but when she reached her room, she saw the woman was indeed engaging in intimate behavior with the neighbor's son, the situation being very indecent. The mother was extremely furious, rushed in, and raised her stick to beat them, but the neighbor's son still held the woman tightly. The woman cried out, and the mother feared the noise would be heard outside, so she suppressed her anger and walked out. The woman cried and cursed all night, unable to understand the reason. It turned out that the woman was indeed alone that night, and Xi Xi transformed herself to humiliate the neighbor's son, but from then on, no one could explain what happened. After living together for more than a month, Xi Xi suddenly bid farewell to Tchen and said, "The young master will arrive tomorrow." The next day, Wu indeed arrived, and Tchen recounted the events to him. Wu wanted to see her, and Tchen said, "She promised to return in about a month, so let's wait for her to come and discuss it."

    384

    ”及匝月，陈方独坐，而细细至，握手笑语如故。陈述其夫求见，意不可，再三言之，乃许于道旁一见。戒勿萌他念，若恶作剧，则与姊从此绝矣。陈谨诺之。其日，吴自外归，忽见道旁大树杈桠中坐一女郎，衣浅碧色衫，淡墨色裙，罗袜锦鞋，纤不盈握，对吴微笑，百媚横生。吴大喜，趋而前，将缘树而上。忽飞尘眯目，痛不可启，掩面而归，为妻白其故。陈方咎吴狂妄，忽闻空中语曰：“与姊从此绝矣！”聆其音，细细也。陈拉吴俱跪叩头谢罪，泣而留之。曰：“缘定不能强也，明年二月尚可一见。”言已寂然。是时为咸丰己未八月。至庚申二月，杭城陷，乡间盗贼公行。吴外出未返，陈已有孕。其邻村有某甲者，素无行，且习邪术，恒取孕妇胎以为药。

    Explanation

    384

    After a full month, Chen Fang sat alone, and Xixi arrived, greeting each other with smiles and laughter as before. She told him that her husband wanted to see him, but he was unwilling. She repeated it several times, and he finally agreed to meet once on the side of the road. He warned him not to have other thoughts, and if he played a prank, then he would sever ties with his sister forever. Chen solemnly promised. On that day, Wu returned from outside, and suddenly saw a young lady sitting in the branches of a large tree by the roadside. She wore a light green blouse and a pale blue skirt, her silk stockings and embroidered shoes barely fitting her delicate feet. She smiled at Wu, radiating a hundred charms. Wu was overjoyed, hurried forward, and was about to climb the tree. Suddenly, dust flew into his eyes, causing such pain that he couldn't open them. He covered his face and returned home, explaining the reason to his wife. Chen Fang blamed Wu for being reckless. Suddenly, a voice from the air said, "We will sever ties forever!" Listening to the voice, it was Xixi. Chen pulled Wu down, and both knelt to apologize, crying and begging him to stay. She said, "Fate is fixed and cannot be forced, but we can meet once more in February next year." After speaking, the voice fell silent. It was the eighth month of the year Xianfeng Jiwu (1859). By the second month of the year Gengshen (1860), Hangzhou fell, and bandits roamed openly in the countryside. Wu went out and did not return, and Chen was already pregnant. In the neighboring village, there was a man named Mr. A, who had no good conduct and practiced dark arts, often taking the fetuses of pregnant women to use as medicine.

    385

    久闻陈美，且知其孕，白昼将三人入其室，思奸之而堕其胎。陈骇欲死，忽皆反奔而出，跪而自投，又以手自批其颊，颊尽肿，口流血。邻里聚观，甲等誓从此不敢犯陈，且愿保卫吴氏，使不受他害。众亦为代求，始得扶携而起，归各大病数月，后遂敛戢。是夜，陈梦细细来曰：“今日之事，我救姊也。上帝以我好善而贞，命为碧霞宫侍书。从此真与姊绝矣。”咸丰乙卯科浙江乡试，头场有绍兴某生于卷上大书一绝云：“绣鞋踏遍几回寻，相会当年未了因。记否红栏明月夜，楼头偷占一枝春。”人询其故，摇首不言。至初十日黎明，缴卷而出。余门人高海与同坐一号，先一日相与论文，极相契合。其三艺皆已脱稿，读之甚佳，乃夜间忽有此异，是必于温柔乡中失足者也。诸书言科场果报事，类此者极多，此则海实亲见之。

    Explanation

    385

    For a long time, I had heard of Chen Mei, and I knew she was pregnant. During the day, he brought three people into her room with the intention of seducing her and causing her to miscarry. Chen was terrified and almost died. Suddenly, they all ran back out, knelt down, and threw themselves to the ground. They then hit their own cheeks with their hands, causing their cheeks to swell and their mouths to bleed. The neighbors gathered to watch. Jia and others swore they would never offend Chen again and promised to protect Wu, ensuring she would not suffer any other harm. The crowd also interceded on her behalf, and she was finally helped up. She returned home and was gravely ill for several months, but later, they all became more restrained. That night, Chen dreamed that Xixi came to her and said, "Today's matter, I saved my sister. God has made me good and chaste and appointed me as a scribe in the Palace of Green Clouds. From now on, I will truly be separated from my sister." During the provincial examination in Zhejiang in the year Yimao of Emperor Xianfeng, in the first round, a scholar from Shaoxing wrote a quatrain on his scroll: "I have searched many times with my embroidered shoes. We met in the past, and our unfinished business. Do you remember the red railing on a moonlit night? We secretly enjoyed a branch of spring on the rooftop." People asked him why, but he shook his head and said nothing. On the tenth day at dawn, he handed in his scroll and left. My disciple Gao Haiyu sat in the same room with him. The day before, we discussed literature and were very compatible. His three essays were all finished, and they were very good. But at night, there was this strange occurrence—it must be someone who has stumbled in the land of pleasure. Many books talk about the retribution in the examination hall, and there are many similar cases. This is what Haiyu actually saw.

    386

    仁和高君桂山，与其兄啸萝，读书西湖之葛林园。其邻为梁文庄公祠，祠中有棺数十具，皆他姓所寄也。一夕，闻外有吟哦声，启视无人，闭户而声又作，乃笑曰：“如有吟魂，盍来相见？”久之杳然。隔数夕，又闻之，其声自西南来，浸至窗外。审听之，则嗟叹之声，非吟哦也。俄而哗然一声，其门自启，二人惊顾，以为鬼来，然实无所见。及卧，梦见一叟曰：“我粤东钱某，老诸生也，以谋食来浙，死于此。明日断桥堍下有著青布衣者，吾子也。吾棺停梁家祠内东廊下，烦两君指示之。”二人寤，述所梦皆同，相与愕然。次日，至白堤伺之，未至断桥，果有一人来，如梦所言，迎问之曰：“尔姓钱乎？”其人惊问：“何以知我？”乃告以梦。其人果访寻父柩者也。导至梁公祠，指示所在，并厚赠之使归。

    Explanation

    386

    Gao Guishan from Renhe, along with his brother Xiaoluo, studied in the Ge Lin Garden by West Lake. Their neighbor was the temple of Liang Wen Zhuang Gong, which housed dozens of coffins belonging to other families. One night, they heard the sound of recitation outside. They opened the door to look, but saw no one. When they closed the door, the sound resumed. They laughed and said, "If there is a spirit reciting, why don't you come and meet us?" After a long while, the sound disappeared. Several nights later, they heard it again, coming from the southwest and gradually reaching the window. Upon careful listening, they realized it was the sound of sighing and lamenting, not recitation. Suddenly, a loud sound echoed, and their door opened by itself. The two of them were startled and looked around, thinking a ghost had come, but they actually saw nothing. When they lay down to sleep, they both dreamed of an old man who said, "I am Qian from Yue Dong, an old scholar. I came to Zhejiang to make a living and died here. Tomorrow, there will be someone wearing a blue cloth coat at the foot of the broken bridge; he is my son. My coffin is resting under the east veranda inside the Liang family temple. Please help me show him the way." Upon waking, they recounted their dreams, which were the same, and were both startled. The next day, they went to the White Causeway to wait for the person. Before reaching the broken bridge, indeed, a person came as described in the dream. They greeted him and asked, "Are you of the surname Qian?" The man was surprised and asked, "How do you know me?" They told him about the dream. The man was indeed searching for his father's coffin. They led him to the Liang Gong temple, pointed out the location, and gave him a generous gift to send him back.

    387

    黄桂圃培荣，浙之会稽人，道光末官湖北武昌府同知。咸丰二年，粤寇至鄂，城陷，桂圃衣冠坐堂皇，骂贼求死。贼絷之，并其一妾二子，挟以入舟。桂圃得间自沉于江，妾与子皆至金陵。长子启勋，时年十一二，为乡人窃负以出，逃至苏州。桂圃有弟金溪刺史，需次于苏，因抚育之。其妾为贼幽于女馆，久之放出，居乡间，以缝纫自给。幼子尚孩提，力不能养，有邻村妇乞为子，不得已许之，密以针刺“天赐”二字于儿左肩，泣而畀之。天赐者，儿乳名也。及官军收复金陵，启勋长矣，至金陵访其母，得之，迎以归。光绪四年，启勋预海运之役，溺死天津。金溪痛桂圃之无后也，其妾因言夭赐事，乃致书江宁蒋鹤庄太守物色之。得其母子，召至署中，解儿衣，验其左肩，则“天”字二画尚存，“赐”字贝旁亦未泯灭。

    Explanation

    387

    Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong was from Kuaiji, Zhejiang Province, and served as the Tongzhi (equivalent to a prefect) of Wuchang Prefecture in Hubei Province at the end of the Daoguang era. In the second year of the Xianfeng era, the Cantonese rebels arrived in Hubei, and the city fell. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong dressed in his official robes and sat in the main hall, cursing the rebels and seeking death. The rebels captured him, along with one concubine and two sons, and took them aboard a boat. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong found an opportunity to drown himself in the river, while his concubine and sons all fled to Nanjing. His eldest son, Qi Xun, was about eleven or twelve years old, and was secretly carried away by a villager and escaped to Suzhou. Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong had a younger brother, Jinxi Cishi, who was stationed in Suzhou and raised Qi Xun. His concubine was imprisoned in a women's quarters by the rebels, and after a long time, she was released and lived in the countryside, supporting herself by sewing. His youngest son was still a child, and unable to support him, a neighbor village woman asked to adopt him, and she reluctantly agreed. She secretly stitched the characters 'Tian Ci' (Heaven's Gift) on the boy's left shoulder and tearfully gave him to the woman. Tian Ci was the boy's milk name. When the government forces recaptured Nanjing, Qi Xun had grown up, and he went to Nanjing to find his mother, found her, and brought her home. In the fourth year of the Guangxu era, Qi Xun was involved in a maritime transport mission and drowned in Tianjin. Jinxi Cishi, grieving over the lack of descendants for Huang Gui Pu Pei Rong, his concubine mentioned the story of Tian Ci, and he wrote a letter to Governor Jiangning Jiang Hezhuang to search for him. He found the mother and son, summoned them to the office, undressed the boy, and checked his left shoulder, where the two strokes of the character '天' were still visible, and the '贝' side of the character '赐' was also not completely erased.

    388

    乃谓村妇曰：“此黄氏子也。”判归黄氏。桂圃为国捐躯，固宜有后，而其妾之智节则亦足多矣。此事杜筱舫方伯为余言之。筱舫又言：启勋之溺也，失其尸，求之数日不得。筱舫之弟筱珊太守，时为江苏海运津局总办，命以纸作灵位，备书官职姓名，置木盆中，旁设羹饭匕箸具焉。浮之天津插口，使人驾小舟从之。初入水，其盆向北逆流，及小舟解维，盆即顺流下行。约二里许，至宝林庵前，木盆忽自就岸，则尸已出自水中，面貌虽改，衣装宛然。乃厚敛之，并请于朝，得优恤焉。筱舫问弟何处得此法，曰：“咸丰八年七月，有直隶广文忘其姓名，奉檄押送南漕剥船。舟中携有一子，以便旋失足入水，欲觅其尸。有土人传此法云。”鬼神之事诚有不可解者，此法屡试屡验，则亦奇矣。惟启勋殁于王事，其遗魄自不应葬之鱼腹，或有神物护持，亦未可知也。

    Explanation

    388

    Then he told the village woman, "This is the son of the Huang family." He ruled in favor of assigning it to the Huang family. Guipu sacrificed his life for the country, and it was indeed fitting that he should have descendants, and his concubine's wisdom and integrity were also worthy of praise. This story was told to me by Du Xiaofang, a high-ranking official. Xiaofang also said: When Qi Xun drowned, his body was lost, and after searching for several days, it could not be found. Xiaofang's younger brother, Xiaoshan, the governor of Jiangsu, who was then the head of the maritime bureau in Tianjin, ordered a spirit tablet made of paper, with the official position and name written on it, placed in a wooden basin, and utensils for food and drink set up beside it. They floated the basin in the Tianjin estuary and sent someone to row a small boat to follow it. When it first entered the water, the basin moved northward against the current. When the small boat was untied, the basin immediately flowed downstream. After traveling about two miles, it reached the front of Baolin Temple, where the wooden basin suddenly came ashore by itself, and the body had already emerged from the water. Although its appearance had changed, its clothing remained intact. Then they buried it with great respect and requested preferential treatment from the court, which they received. Xiaofang asked his brother where he learned this method, and he replied, "In the seventh month of the eighth year of the Xianfeng era, there was a local official from Zhili whose name I have forgotten. He received an order to escort the unloading of southern grain transport ships. On the boat, he carried a child who soon lost his footing and fell into the water. He wanted to search for the child's body. A local person passed on this method, saying." Indeed, matters concerning spirits and deities are truly beyond understanding, but this method has been repeatedly tested and proven effective, so it is also quite remarkable. However, Qi Xun died in the line of duty, and his spirit should not be buried in the belly of a fish; perhaps there were divine beings protecting him, but that is also uncertain.

    389

    周镜楼，南汇县人，曾应童子试，生一女而卒。初无灵异。数年之后，其女长矣，忽大病，病中言其父实保护之，故得不死。自是目中遂能见鬼，而镜楼即附其女以与人言，灵异大著。自云在冥中签押文书，颇能作威福。有谢心畲者，娶于周氏，与镜楼兄弟也。己卯秋病甚，镜楼之女往视之，见鬼往来甚多，问之灶神，神言无有。女曰：“吾所亲见，安得云无？”于是灶神穷治之，告女曰：“是于某月日赂门神而窃入者也。”其夜镜楼至，其家人环跪求之。

    Explanation

    389

    Zhou Jingshuo, a native of Nanhui County, once took the imperial examination for young scholars, had a daughter, and then passed away. At first, he showed no signs of supernatural ability. Several years later, his daughter grew up and suddenly fell gravely ill. During her illness, she claimed that her father had protected her, which is why she survived. From then on, she was able to see ghosts, and the spirit of Zhou Jingshuo would attach itself to her to speak with others, and his supernatural abilities became widely known. He claimed that he signed official documents in the afterlife and could exercise power and grant favors. There was a man named Xie Xinyu who married into the Zhou family and was a brother to Zhou Jingshuo. In the autumn of the year Ji Mao, Xie Xinyu fell seriously ill. Zhou Jingshuo's daughter went to visit him and saw many ghosts coming and going. She asked the Kitchen God about it, but the god said there were none. The daughter replied, "I have seen them with my own eyes, how can you say there are none?" The Kitchen God then investigated thoroughly and told her, "This ghost entered through bribing the door god on a certain day." That night, the spirit of Zhou Jingshuo arrived, and his family knelt in a circle to beg him for help.

    390

    镜楼诘问鬼，鬼自言姓李名儒奎，浙江人。八世前为王氏女，潘氏妇，心畲八世前为某氏子，与之私。后有孕，服堕胎药而死。岁月虽远，怨毒未消。累世寻求，至今始遇，必索其命乃去。镜楼劝之再四，且许延道士为诵经。鬼不可。镜楼怒曰：“汝不知吾周镜楼乎！汝能出吾掌握中欤？”鬼始听命，曰：“苟延道士诵经，愿诵《大梵经》。若《玉皇经》，则在冥中为最下田土，不愿得之也。”谢氏从其言，病良已。镜楼又言有顾春船者，戊子科举人，在冥中颇有势力，己得为冥吏，乃其所荐也。以是观之，冥间营营，亦与人世无异矣。

    Explanation

    390

    Mirror Tower questioned the ghost, who claimed to be surnamed Li and named Ru Kui, from Zhejiang. Eight generations ago, she was a daughter of the Wang family and a wife of the Pan family, while Xin She, eight generations ago, was a son of a certain family, and they had an affair. Afterward, she became pregnant, took an abortion pill, and died. Though the years have passed, the resentment remains. After generations of searching, she finally meets him today and must take his life before she will leave. Mirror Tower tried to persuade her repeatedly and agreed to have a Taoist priest recite scriptures. The ghost refused. Mirror Tower angrily said, "Don't you know who I am, Zhou Mirror Tower? Can you escape from my grasp?" The ghost finally obeyed and said, "If the Taoist priest recites scriptures, I wish to have the Dafa Jing recited. If the Yuhuang Jing, it is the lowest level of land in the underworld, and I do not wish to obtain it." The Xie family followed her words, and the illness was cured.
