Zhu found it strange and asked him, "I and this old man are both from Yancheng. Last year, I brought my family, operating a small boat, came here to sell fish, and was robbed by bandits. My family of seven fortunately did not die, surviving by begging. Now, I met this old man who is about to return north, and I sought to board his boat, but he refused. I will soon become a starving corpse in a foreign land, that is why I am sad."

    433

    ”翁曰：“附舟可也，然自此至家乡千余里，尔一家七口，途中食用须钱七八千，吾亦小经纪人，岂能任欤？”朱恻然悯之，即探囊取洋钱六枚付此人，乃谢而去。茶肆中有人笑曰：“君受其欺矣。此七人者与老翁皆一党也。”朱犹未信。一夕，有事于河干，见数小舟泊焉，篷窗之下，酒肴罗列。窥之，即向之哭而求者，而老翁亦在焉，正共饮啖，谈笑甚乐。朱始知肆中人之言不谬，初甚怒之，继而笑曰：“彼自[B220]言，我则不失为义举，庸何伤乎！”越五月，寇大至，居民逃避，临河无舟楫，死者甚众。朱与妻孥正傍徨水次，忽见一小舟，其操舟者即前所遇盐城人也，呼之即至，载与俱免，复厚酬之。

    Explanation

    433

    The old man said, "Boarding the boat is acceptable, but from here to your hometown is over a thousand miles. With your family of seven, the food and expenses for the journey will require about seven or eight thousand coins. I am also a small broker, how could I possibly bear this?" Zhu felt compassion for them and immediately reached into his pocket, giving six silver coins to the person, who then thanked him and left. Someone in the tea house laughed and said, "You have been deceived by them. These seven people and the old man are all in cahoots." Zhu still did not believe it. One evening, Zhu had business by the riverbank and saw several small boats moored there. Under the awning windows, wine and dishes were arranged. Looking inside, it was the same group that had cried and begged, and the old man was also there, drinking and eating, talking and laughing very happily. Zhu then realized that the person in the tea house was not mistaken. At first, he was very angry, but then he laughed and said, "They said [B220] themselves, and I did not lose by doing a righteous deed, what harm is there!" Five months later, the bandits arrived in large numbers. The residents fled, and those near the river had no boats, many died. Zhu and his wife and children were wandering near the water when suddenly they saw a small boat. The person steering it was the same person from Salt City that Zhu had met before. He called out to him, and the person came immediately, took them all aboard, and they were saved. Zhu then generously rewarded him.

    434

    绍兴人周某，当粤贼之乱，为贼劫至湖州。贼魁甚悍，一日有狗遗矢于地，贼魁怒，尽杀其所畜狗。最后一黑狗，哀号若求免者。周颇与贼魁善，力请弗杀，从之。周以此狗寄养他所。居数月，周从贼中跳出，狗随之行。至德清，宿枯庙中。及夕，狗忽登其榻，周惊起，则闻户外有人切切耳语，盖知其自贼中来，欲害之而取其所有也。周夺门出，数人露刃追之，狗狂噬，周得免。后绕道归绍兴，大风覆舟，周溺于水，狗亦入水，衔其衣，曳之至岸，乃得不死。光绪元年，有人见周于杭州城隍山，狗亦尚在。

    Explanation

    434

    During the Taiping Rebellion, a man from Shaoxing named Zhou was captured by the rebels and taken to Huzhou. The rebel leader was very fierce. One day, a dog defecated on the ground, and the leader became furious, ordering the execution of all the dogs he owned. The last remaining black dog cried mournfully as if begging for mercy. Zhou, who was on good terms with the leader, strongly pleaded not to kill the dog, and the leader agreed. Zhou entrusted the dog to someone else's care. After several months, Zhou managed to escape from the rebels, and the dog followed him. They reached Deqing and spent the night in a dilapidated temple. Late that evening, the dog suddenly climbed onto Zhou's bed, and Zhou was startled awake by the sound of whispered conversation outside, indicating that the people knew Zhou had escaped from the rebels and wanted to harm him to take his belongings. Zhou fled through the door, and several men chased him with swords drawn. The dog fiercely bit the pursuers, and Zhou managed to escape. Later, Zhou took a detour back to Shaoxing, but a strong wind capsized his boat, and he nearly drowned. The dog also entered the water, bit Zhou's clothes, and pulled him to the shore, saving his life. In the first year of the Guangxu era (1875), someone saw Zhou on City God Mountain in Hangzhou, and the dog was still alive.

    435

    吴江黎里镇多尼庵，登徒子游其中，犹青楼也。有农家妇素与青莲庵中一尼相识，艳其所为，常寄宿庵中，尼即诱与恶少淫。其夫屡以舟来迎妇归，尼厌之，与妇谋杀其夫。妇始诺之，继而悔焉。一日薄暮，夫又以舟来，尼与所私者伪言欲至某处，使其夫载以往，并招妇偕焉。行数里，天已暝，烟水渺茫，杳无人迹，尼与所私者用力推其夫于河。妇骇甚，欲援以手，禁勿许，妇大号。尼曰：“若为人闻知，尔当膺极刑，我则无恐，王法不杀僧尼也。”妇信之，乃忍不哭，仍从尼归。居数日，其夫弟来探其兄，尼与妇均言未尝来此。其夫弟遍访，无知者。会岁将暮，妇亦归家，私具酒食祭其夫，且祝曰：“死而有知，勿我怨。

    Explanation

    435

    Wuxian County's Lili Town has many nunneries, where lecherous men frequent, much like brothels. There was a peasant woman who had long been acquainted with a nun at Qinglian Temple. She was attracted to the nun's lifestyle and often stayed overnight at the temple. The nun then tempted her into sexual relations with a bad young man. Her husband often came by boat to fetch her home, but the nun grew tired of this and plotted with the woman to kill her husband. The woman initially agreed but later regretted it. One evening near dusk, her husband came by boat again, and the nun and the bad young man claimed they wanted to go somewhere, having her husband take them there and inviting the woman to join them. They traveled several miles, and it was already dark; the water was misty and far away, with no sign of people. The nun and the bad young man pushed her husband into the river. The woman was very frightened and wanted to help him with her hand, but was forbidden from doing so and screamed loudly. The nun said, "If people find out, you will face severe punishment, but I will not be afraid, for the law does not punish monks and nuns." The woman believed her and held back her tears, returning with the nun. After a few days, her husband's brother came to visit his brother, and both the nun and the woman claimed they had never been there. Her husband's brother searched everywhere, but no one knew. When the year was coming to an end, the woman returned home, secretly preparing food and wine to offer to her husband, and said a prayer: "If you are aware after death, do not blame me."

    436

    ”为夫弟窥见，排闼入，穷究其故，妇坚不承，乃执妇并尼控于官。官严鞫之，始吐实，而苦不得其尸。时光绪三年正月十七日也，天忽大雷雨，尸浮起，距其死已五十余日，而面如生。尼所私者久亡去，官命役捕之，不获。役祷于尸，其夜死者见梦于役曰：“其人在江宁水西门外某姓饭店。”如其言，果得之，皆论如律。平望镇人王阿毛，好食蛙。制一铁针，长二尺许，每捕得一蛙，则以针穿其颈，针满始荷之而归，以充馔焉。如是者数十年矣。一日，至其亲串家，亲串止之宿。是夜有远处失火，阿毛登屋望之。其家临河而居，惧盗贼从水次攀援登屋，故于檐端列铁条数十，皆锐其末，如锋刃然。阿毛失足而坠，铁条适贯其颈，呼号甚惨。救之者无法可施，乃竖长梯于水中，众人缘梯而上，始将阿毛解下，而气已绝矣。其死状宛然如蛙也。

    Explanation

    436

    The younger brother saw and pushed open the door, entering to investigate the cause thoroughly. The wife refused to admit anything, so they arrested her and the nun and took them to the authorities. The official subjected them to a strict interrogation, and they finally admitted the truth, but they could not find the body. It was the 17th day of the first month of the third year of the Guangxu era, when suddenly a great thunderstorm broke out, and the body floated up, having been dead for over fifty days, yet its face looked like it was alive. The man the nun had been intimate with had long since died and gone away, and the official ordered the constable to capture him, but he was not found. The constable prayed to the body, and that night the deceased appeared in a dream to the constable, saying, "He is at a restaurant of a certain surname in the West Water Gate of Jiangning." Following this, they indeed found him, and both were judged according to the law.

    Wang Amo, a man from Pingwang Town, loved eating frogs. He made a steel needle, about two feet long, and every time he caught a frog, he would pierce its neck with the needle. Once the needle was full, he would carry the frogs back to use for food. He did this for several decades. One day, he went to his relative's house, and the relative asked him to stay overnight. That night, a fire broke out in a distant place, and Wang Amo climbed onto the roof to look. His relative's house was by the river, and they were afraid that thieves might climb up from the riverbank, so they placed several iron bars at the eaves, all with sharp ends, like knives or swords. Wang Amo lost his footing and fell, and the iron bar happened to pierce his neck. He cried out in great pain. Those who tried to save him had no way to help, so they set up a long ladder in the water, and everyone climbed up the ladder. Finally, they managed to take Wang Amo down, but he was already dead. His death was exactly like that of a frog.

    437

    盛凝之，苏州人，少孤贫，而性甚慧。十四岁时，《十三经》已卒业。其季父命之学贾于钱店中。一日，其主使持金赴某所，道出隘巷，见一小家数人聚哭甚哀。问其故，邻人曰：“是家某人死三日矣，遗儿女三四，贫不能敛，故聚哭耳。”盛问所需若干，曰：“非银二十两不可办。”盛所持适有此数，悉以与之，遂不敢归店，至素所熟识之僧寺宿焉。店主待久不至，问之其家，不得。乃与季父侦探三日，而始遇之僧寺。诘之，以告，征之死者之家而信。季父痛殴之。寺僧劝曰：“事虽非，而其心术可取也。”季父乃舍之去。盛既失业，遂留居寺中，仍读书。越三年，补博士弟子员，始归其家。

    Explanation

    437

    Sheng Nengzhi, a native of Suzhou, grew up fatherless and poor but was exceptionally intelligent. By the age of fourteen, he had completed his studies of the Thirteen Classics. His younger brother then instructed him to learn the trade of a merchant at a money shop. One day, his master sent him to carry gold to a certain place, and on the way, he passed through a narrow alley where he saw a family of several people gathered in deep sorrow. He asked the reason for their grief, and a neighbor replied, "A man in this family died three days ago, leaving behind three or four children. They are poor and cannot afford a proper burial, so they are gathered in tears." Sheng asked how much was needed, and the neighbor said, "At least twenty taels of silver are required." Sheng happened to have exactly this amount, so he gave it all to them and dared not return to the shop. Instead, he went to a familiar temple to stay for the night. The shop owner waited for him for a long time without success, so he inquired at the family's home but found no trace of Sheng. After three days of searching, they finally encountered him at the temple. When questioned, Sheng explained what he had done, and upon verification with the family of the deceased, it was confirmed. His younger brother, furious, beat him severely. The temple monk intervened, saying, "Though the deed was wrong, his intentions were commendable." His younger brother then let him go. Sheng, having lost his job, remained in the temple, continuing his studies. After three years, he was admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy and returned home.

    438

    至咸丰庚申岁，盛年已四十余，为贼劫至扬州，逾年而逸。流寓仪征一破庙，卖卜自活，旋病死。庙僧谋藁葬之野，忽有守备洪君至庙，问知其姓氏里居，曰：“此吾同乡也。”少顷，请其母偕来。母曰：“昔年为汝父营葬者，与此人姓名正同，未知果是否。”乃以银三十两治其丧，寄棺庙中，属僧善视之。及乱平，洪已官参将，亲至苏州访盛氏，问盛凝之无恙否，其家曰：“乱时为贼劫去，今不知存亡。”洪又细问其年貌，乃哭曰：“若然，则恩人死矣！”盖曩时隘巷中死者，即洪父也。洪年十七，投江宁督标为兵，积功至参将。其母当日即询知盛之姓名居处，时时为子言之，命其图报。前真州庙中所殡者，犹冀非是，今知果然，乃挈盛妻子至仪征，以其柩归葬，且厚恤其家。

    Explanation

    438

    In the year Gengshen of the Xianfeng era, when he was already in his forties, he was captured by bandits and taken to Yangzhou. After a year, he managed to escape and wandered to a ruined temple in Yizheng, where he sold divination to support himself. Soon after, he fell ill and died. The temple monk planned to bury him in a makeshift grave in the wilderness, but suddenly, a military officer named Mr. Hong arrived at the temple. He asked and learned the man's surname, clan, and hometown, saying, "He is from my hometown." Shortly after, he invited the man's mother to come with him. She said, "The person who arranged the burial of your father many years ago had the same name as this man; I don't know if it's truly him." Mr. Hong then arranged for the funeral with thirty taels of silver, left the coffin in the temple, and instructed the monk to look after it. When the chaos subsided, Mr. Hong had risen to the rank of Colonel and personally went to Suzhou to visit the Sheng family, asking if Sheng Ningzhi was safe. The family replied, "During the chaos, he was captured by bandits, and now we don't know if he is alive or dead." Mr. Hong then asked in detail about his age and appearance and cried, "If that is the case, then my benefactor is dead!" It turned out that the person who had died in a narrow alley in the past was indeed Mr. Hong's father. Mr. Hong, at the age of seventeen, joined the military as a soldier in the Jining garrison and accumulated merit to rise to the rank of Colonel. His mother had learned about Sheng's name, clan, and hometown at the time and often told her son about it, ordering him to seek retribution. The person who had been buried in the temple in Zhenzhou before, Mr. Hong had still hoped it wasn't him, but now he realized it was indeed him. He then brought Sheng's wife and children to Yizheng, returned the coffin for burial, and provided generous support to his family.

    439

    有友人言二事：一则前世之夫今世为女子，一则前世之妻今世为男子。二事绝奇，可纪也。云有钱某者，吴人也。妻卒，将敛矣，忽蹶然而苏，张目视其夫曰：“汝何人欤？”夫疑为谵语。妇即起坐，周视其室曰：“此何地欤？”已而引镜自照，大哭曰：“吾其为女子乎！”乃告其夫曰：“吾闽人王某也。因病而死，至冥中，冥王谓吾阳寿未终，命二鬼送之回。途遇大风，二鬼为风吹去，不知所之。我亦觉身轻如叶，随风飘堕至此，乃化为女子乎！吾本男子，读书识字，家亦小康，妻孕未产，今当奈何？”言已，复大哭。其夫劝慰百端，不应。始而不食，继觉饥饿，乃稍进饮食。夫欲与同寝，辄拒不纳。如是数月，有为其夫计者，曰：“饮食男女，人之大欲。彼既因饥饿而强进饮食，然则岂无欲念欤？当以男女之欲诱之。”夫乃觅得一说平话者，日日为演说淫亵之事。

    Explanation

    439

    A friend tells of two matters: one is that a husband from a previous life is a woman in this life, and the other is that a wife from a previous life is a man in this life. These two matters are extremely strange and worth recording. It is said that there was a man named Qian from Wu. After his wife died, they were about to prepare her body for burial when he suddenly woke up and opened his eyes, looking at his husband and saying, "Who are you?" His husband thought it was delirium. The woman then got up and sat, looking around her room and saying, "Where am I?" She then took a mirror and looked at herself, crying out, "Am I becoming a woman?" She then told her husband, "I am Wang from Fujian. Because I was ill and died, I came to the netherworld. The king of the netherworld said my life span had not ended, so two spirits sent me back. On the way, a strong wind blew, and the two spirits were blown away, and I don't know where they went. I also felt my body was light as a leaf, drifting down with the wind to this place, and now I have become a woman! I was originally a man, read books and recognized characters, and my family was also relatively well-off. My wife was pregnant but had not yet given birth. Now what should I do?" She said this and cried again. Her husband tried to comfort her in many ways, but she did not respond. At first, she did not eat, but later she felt hungry and started eating a little. Her husband wanted to share the bed with her, but she refused to let him in. This continued for several months. Someone then advised her husband, saying, "The desire for food and sex is a great desire for humans. Since she was hungry and forced herself to eat, does she not have desires? We should tempt her with the desire for sex." The husband then found a storyteller and had him tell stories of romantic and sensual nature every day.

    440

    妇始乐听之，数日后，忽曰：“吾妇人也，奈何为我说此！”麾使出。是夜，夫就之，不复拒矣，遂为夫妇如常人。岁余，情好甚笃，乃哀其夫曰：“吾家在闽，杳无消息，妻产亦不知男女。君其偕我往探之。”夫不忍拂其意，偕赴闽。至其家言之，其家初不信。妇乃历叙前生事，一一有据，且与其妻言当日房帏秘事，人所不能知者：妻乃大哭，已又破涕而笑。其前世之妻，遗腹已生男子矣。留数月，始返。

    Explanation

    440

    The woman initially enjoyed listening to him, but after several days, she suddenly said, "I am a woman, how can you tell me such things!" She waved him away. That night, the husband approached her, and she no longer resisted. They became a couple like ordinary people. After more than a year, their affection became very deep, and she pitied her husband and said, "My family is in Fujian, and there is no news from them; I don't even know if my child is a boy or a girl. Please go with me to find out." The husband could not go against her wishes and went with her to Fujian. When they arrived at her family home and told them, they initially did not believe her. She then recounted her past life, all of which were based on facts, and told her about the intimate details of their past relationship, things that no one else knew. Her wife cried, then burst into laughter. Her former wife had given birth to a son while she was pregnant. They stayed for several months before returning.

    441

    越一岁，妇受孕生一女，乃即以前生之子为婿，两家成亲串焉。又有邹某者，浙西老儒也，妻死已久，遗二子一女。二子未娶，女则已嫁矣。老儒以课徒为生，家徒壁立，衣食不充。忽一日，有一少年至，裘马甚都，揖老儒而问曰：“君其邹某欤？今年若干岁，有女一人、子二人乎？”老儒怪问：“君何知我？”少年曰：“吾前生乃君妻也。死后又生山西某姓家，某姓固钜富，吾拥赀数百万。念君寒饿，恒思周济，以父母在，未敢远游。今父母以天年终，故来相访耳。

    Explanation

    441

    After one year, the woman conceived and gave birth to a daughter. She then arranged for her daughter to marry the son from her previous life, thus uniting the two families in marriage. There was also a man named Zou, an elderly scholar from western Zhejiang, whose wife had been dead for a long time, leaving behind two sons and one daughter. The two sons were not yet married, while the daughter had already been wed. The scholar made his living by teaching students, but he was extremely poor, with bare walls and insufficient food and clothing. One day, a young man arrived, well-dressed and well-equipped. He bowed to the scholar and asked, "Are you Master Zou? How old are you this year? Do you have one daughter and two sons?" The scholar was surprised and asked, "How do you know me?" The young man replied, "I was your wife in a previous life. After my death, I was reborn into a wealthy family in Shanxi. That family was extremely rich, and I possessed millions of assets. I often thought of helping you, as you were in poverty, but I did not dare to travel far because my parents were still alive. Now that my parents have passed away, I have come to visit you."

    442

    ”乃历叙前生事，亦一一有据，相偕入室，手自检点平生故物，欷欲绝。已而二子至，皆能呼其小名，谓其夫曰：“吾携万金至此，二子可各与四千，以一千与女，而以一千为君娱老，无事更作村学究矣。”女闻其事，自夫家归，见是少年男子，羞涩不前。少年呼其乳名曰：“阿巧不识我欤？”执其手而泣。居数日，请老儒偕至前生埋骨处，蓬颗敝冢，在荒榛蔓草中，又欷久之，买地改葬，且为老儒营生圹焉。流连数月，乃始辞去。老儒命长男送之归，自此往来，亦如亲串云。淮安乡间来一瞽者，推人年命如神。有李氏子就之推算，瞽决其一生足衣食、无刑祸，有子女各六，然当有两妻。李笑曰：“吾农家子，不鳏足矣，焉有两妻？”瞽曰：“不然，命如是也。”又有陈叟延之至家，悉以其家人年命使推算之，一一不爽，至其女，则曰：“偏房命也。”陈叟怒曰：“老朽薄有田产，何至以女为人妾！”瞽曰：“不然，命如是也。即不作妾，亦非正妻。

    Explanation

    442

    A woman recounts her past life in detail, and every detail is supported by evidence. Together, they enter the room, where she personally examines her old belongings, sobbing as if she were about to die. Soon after, her two sons arrive, and they can call her by her childhood name. They tell her husband, "I have brought ten thousand taels of silver here. The two sons can each receive four thousand, give one thousand to the daughter, and use another thousand to entertain you in your old age. There is no need to worry about becoming a village scholar anymore." Upon hearing this, the woman returns from her husband's home and sees the young man. She is shy and hesitant to approach him. The young man calls out her childhood name, saying, "Don't you recognize me, Ah Qiao?" He takes her hand and weeps. After staying for several days, they request an elderly scholar to accompany them to the place where her remains were buried. The grave is in a state of disrepair, surrounded by tangled grass and overgrown weeds. They weep for a long time, then buy land to reinter her remains and construct a mausoleum for the elderly scholar. They stay for several months before finally parting ways. The elderly scholar sends his eldest son to accompany her back, and from then on, they visit each other as close relatives. In the countryside of Huai'an, there comes a blind man who predicts people's fate with divine accuracy. A son of the Li family approaches him for a reading, and the blind man predicts that the Li family will have enough food and clothing, no criminal punishment or disaster, and six children each, but they will have two wives. The Li family laughs and says, "We are just simple farmers; being unmarried is enough for us. How could we have two wives?" The blind man replies, "That is not the case. Fate is like this." Another elderly man from the Chen family invites him to his home and asks him to predict the fate of all his family members. The predictions are accurate for everyone except his daughter, for whom the blind man says, "She is destined to be a concubine." The Chen family becomes angry and says, "We have only a small amount of land and property. How could we make our daughter a concubine?" The blind man replies, "That is not the case. Fate is like this. Even if she is not a concubine, she will not be a legitimate wife."

    443

    ”已而李氏子娶于赵，甫六月生一子，乡里姗笑之。李之父惧为门户羞，归之于母家。赵女固贞淑，母家知其无他，然六月生子，无以自明。请反不可，乃留之，仍抚养其子。而李氏子所续娶，即陈叟女也，甫六月，亦生一子。于是赵女之父母兄弟咸哗于李氏之门曰：“尔谓吾女不贞，故六月而生子，今陈女亦六月生子，何也？留则俱留，逐则俱逐，一留一逐，行且兴讼。”李父子无以为计，其宗族姻亲咸谓其父曰：“若子两娶，皆六月而生子，赵、陈两姓清白旧家，两女亦皆端好，必无他故，是无可疑者也。宜迎赵女以归，使与陈女以姊妹称。”而陈女顾长赵女一岁，赵犹忿争。乃议不以长幼为次，而以先后为次，姊赵而妹陈，事乃定，两女皆婉娩颇相安。俄各受孕，逾期不育，至十二月始生，则皆女也。嗣后男女相间而生。生男皆六月，生女皆十二月，群疑尽释。两女各生三男三女。李氏子果有两妻、子女各六，陈叟之女竟为偏房，瞽者之言悉验。

    Explanation

    443

    After a while, Li's son married into the Zhao family, and within just six months, they had a son. The villagers mocked her for this. Li's father feared it would bring disgrace to the family and returned her to her mother's family. Zhao's daughter was truly chaste and virtuous, and her mother's family knew she was innocent, but the fact that she had a child within six months of marriage made it difficult for her to prove her innocence. When they asked to bring her back, the request was not granted, so they kept her and continued to raise her child. Meanwhile, Li's son subsequently married the daughter of Old Man Chen, and within six months, she too gave birth to a son. As a result, Zhao's parents and siblings loudly confronted Li's family, saying, "You said our daughter was unchaste because she had a child within six months, but now Chen's daughter also had a child within six months. Why is that? If you keep one, you must keep both; if you send one away, you must send both. Keeping one and sending the other away will inevitably lead to a lawsuit." Li's father and son had no way to resolve the situation, and their relatives and clan members advised Li's father, "Your son has married twice, and in both cases, the wives gave birth within six months. Zhao and Chen are both respectable families, and both women are virtuous and well-behaved. There must be no other reason, so there is no cause for doubt. It would be best to bring Zhao's daughter back and let her be called the elder sister to Chen's daughter." However, Chen's daughter was one year older than Zhao's daughter, and Zhao's family still contested angrily. They then decided to disregard the order of age and instead follow the order of marriage, making Zhao's daughter the elder sister and Chen's daughter the younger sister. The matter was thus settled, and the two women were quite harmonious and gentle with each other. Soon, they both became pregnant, but beyond the expected time, they did not give birth. It was only until the twelfth month that they finally gave birth, and both were girls. Thereafter, they alternated in giving birth to boys and girls, with boys born every six months and girls every twelve months. All doubts were dispelled. The two women each gave birth to three boys and three girls. Li's son indeed had two wives, each with six children, and Chen's daughter ended up as a concubine. The blind man's words were all proven true.

    444

    《右台仙馆笔记》 下 清。俞樾●卷九湖北沔阳州库藏陈友谅残甲，上缀锈铁六片，各长二寸，广寸，厚不及分。相传观者不可抚弄，违则有灾。杜筱舫观察曰：“夥颐窃据，久应火灭烟销。而腐朽之余，犹不容触忤，岂以其取精用宏，故厉气亦结而不散乎？”余谓古物流传既久，能为神怪，往往有之，非必以其人也。《隋书》开皇十一年，以平陈所得古器多为祸变，悉命毁之。顾亭林先生讥其恣睢不学。余门下士冯梦香孝廉言其族人霁亭茂才家，藏其远祖尚书公所乘肩舆二具，前明遗物也，深广皆倍于今制。其广可五六尺，其深可容人伸足而卧；以竹为之，竹皆作┇字形。相传有触之者，必头痛身热，故久而不毁。粤贼之乱，有欲薪之者。人告之故，贼始不信，俄果大病，遂不敢毁，至今犹存。孔子曰物老则为怪，岂不信夫！

    Explanation

    444

    The Right Platform Immortal Pavilion Notes
    Volume 9
    In the treasury of the prefecture of Mianyang, Hubei, there is a fragment of armor belonging to Chen Youliang, adorned with six rusted iron plates, each two inches long, one inch wide, and less than an inch thick. It is said that observers must not touch or handle it, for doing so will bring disaster. Du Xiaofang, the observer, remarked, "Chen Youliang, who seized power by force, should have long since been extinguished like a fire and dispersed like smoke. Yet, even in its decayed remnants, it cannot be touched or offended. Is it not because its essence was used widely, so that its ghostly energy is still gathered and not dispersed?" I believe that ancient objects, after being passed down for a long time, often become spirits or supernatural beings, and this is not necessarily because of the person associated with them. In the Book of Sui, during the eleventh year of the Kaihuang era, many ancient artifacts obtained after the conquest of Chen were said to bring misfortune, and all were ordered to be destroyed. Mr. Gu Tinglin criticized this act as arbitrary and unlearned. One of my students, Feng Mengxiang, a filial official, recounted that his relative, Ji Ting, a talented scholar, had in his family two sedan chairs used by his distant ancestor, a high-ranking official, which were relics from the Ming dynasty. These chairs were twice as deep and wide as modern standards. Their width could be five or six feet, and their depth could accommodate a person lying down with their feet extended. They were made of bamboo, which was shaped in the form of ┇. It is said that anyone who touches them will suffer from headaches and fever, which is why they have not been destroyed for a long time. During the Taiping Rebellion, there were those who wanted to use them as fuel. When people told them the reason, the rebels initially did not believe it, but soon fell gravely ill and thus dared not destroy them. They still exist to this day. Confucius said, "Objects that are old become strange." Is this not true?

    445

    梦香又言：其先德观察公有妾丁氏，始入室时，适购得大珠一颗，遂名之曰珠圆，而甚嬖之。早死，死后数日，观察丙夜未成寐。忽闻启户声，疑为穿窬者，披衣起坐以伺之，则丁氏冉冉至，坐床沿，默无一语。观察亦悲甚，不知所云。如是者相对甚久，至天将明，丁氏乃缩小而灭，如烟之散于地。起视，则户扃如故，初未尝启也。观察自言他人虽见鬼，无如我之真切者。惜未一叩其冥中之情状也。

    Explanation

    445

    Dream Xiang recounted: His ancestor, the Inspector, had a concubine named Ding Shi. When she first entered the household, she had just bought a large pearl, so she named it Zhu Yuan and greatly favored it. She died early, and several days after her death, in the middle of the night, the Inspector was unable to sleep. Suddenly, he heard the sound of the door opening and suspected it was a thief. He got up and sat down to wait for it, and then Ding Shi slowly appeared, sitting on the edge of the bed, silent and saying nothing. The Inspector was also very sad and did not know what to say. They were together for a long time, until dawn was approaching, when Ding Shi then shrank and disappeared, like smoke dispersing into the ground. Getting up to look, the Inspector saw that the door was still locked as before, it had never been opened. The Inspector said that others might see ghosts, but none would see it as clearly as he did, it is a pity that he did not ask about the details of her state in the afterlife.

    446

    归安县南乡有长超山，茂林修竹，境颇幽雅。山有护云庵。相传前明凌忠介公未第时读书庵中，偶于月明时闲步山麓，忽有老者揖于前，口嗫嚅若将有言。公问之，曰：“我古冢中人也。子孙零落，祭扫无人。来从公乞一盂麦饭耳。”公不之信，笑而言曰：“吾性好静，故来此读书。而四面蛙声阁阁，达旦不休，甚厌苦之。苟能为吾禁群蛙不鸣，当有以报。”老人曰：“诺。”遂不见。其夜蛙果无声，公乃具酒饭祭之于荒丘。自是庵之左右，蛙不复鸣，至今犹然。杭州望江门外民汪甲，聘陆氏女，以贫不能娶。乃纠集十余人荷轿而如汪氏，劫女以出，纳之轿中，荷以归。女初大哭，久之寂然，及至家，则空轿也。

    Explanation

    446

    In the southern suburbs of Guian County, there is a Longchao Mountain, surrounded by dense forests and tall bamboo, with a particularly secluded and elegant scenery. On this mountain stands the Huyun Hermitage. It is said that during the Ming Dynasty, when Ling Zhongjie, a respected scholar, had not yet passed the imperial examinations, he studied at the hermitage. One night, under the bright moonlight, he took a leisurely walk along the mountain foot. Suddenly, an elderly man bowed before him, his lips trembling as if he was about to speak. Ling asked him, and the old man replied, "I am a spirit from an ancient tomb. My descendants have scattered, and no one comes to offer sacrifices. I come to ask you for a bowl of millet rice." Ling did not believe him and laughed, saying, "I enjoy solitude, so I came here to study. However, the incessant croaking of frogs all around is very annoying. If you can stop them from croaking, I will reward you." The old man said, "Yes." And then he disappeared. That night, the frogs indeed stopped croaking, and Ling prepared wine and food to offer at the desolate mound. Since then, the area around the hermitage has remained free of frog croaks, and this continues to this day.

    In Hangzhou, outside the West Lake Gate, there lived a man named Wang Jia. He had engaged the services of a woman from the Lu family but could not afford to marry her. So, he gathered more than ten people and carried a sedan chair to Wang's house, kidnapping the woman and taking her out. They placed her in the sedan chair and carried her away. Initially, the woman cried loudly, but after a while, she fell silent. When they arrived at Wang's home, the sedan chair was empty.

    447

    大惊，返告之陆，适陆氏亦纠众来夺，遇诸涂，大哄而散。次日，媒妁咸集，汪言女尚在陆，陆言女已归汪，纷争数日，将讼于官。忽有人见其女僵卧海神庙外，以告陆，急舁之归。昏昏若痴，有时稍清爽，自言：“初入轿中，愤怒跳踉。忽自轿坠地，有数青衣拥之入庙。庙中神赤发垂肩，貌甚丑恶，而两耳皆聋。青衣者以女见，且以手示意，神颔之，留使执役。所食皆腥物，膏血淋漓，女饥甚而不可食。役之又甚苛，女终日哭，神亦不闻也，苛役如故。忽闻外间传言夫人自海昌还矣。众皆惊，以手作势告于神。神亦惶惧，急麾女出。遂有数人者曳女出门，置之地，即为人所见。”然女自归家后，忽歌忽泣，失其常度，父母亦厌之，归之于汪。汪与生二子一女，稍识人事，然神气之间仍痴呆也。

    Explanation

    447

    Wang was greatly alarmed and returned to inform Lu. Just as Lu's family had also gathered a crowd to come and seize, they met on the road and became very noisy before dispersing. The next day, matchmakers all gathered. Wang said the girl was still with Lu, while Lu said the girl had already returned to Wang. They disputed for several days, intending to take the matter to court. Suddenly, someone saw the girl lying stiff outside the Sea God Temple and informed Lu. Lu quickly carried her back home. She was dazed, as if mad, but sometimes a little clearer, and she said, "When I first entered the sedan chair, I was angry and leaping about. Suddenly, I fell out of the sedan chair onto the ground, and several people in green clothes carried me into the temple. The god in the temple had red hair hanging down to his shoulders, was very ugly, and both his ears were deaf. The people in green clothes presented the girl to the god and gestured with their hands. The god nodded and kept her to perform duties. What she ate were all bloody things, greasy and bloody. The girl was very hungry but could not eat. The duties were extremely harsh, and the girl cried all day. The god did not hear, and the harsh duties continued as usual. Suddenly, rumors from outside said the lady had returned from Haichang. Everyone was shocked and gestured with their hands to inform the god. The god was also frightened and quickly ordered the girl to leave. Then several people dragged the girl out of the door, placed her on the ground, and she was seen by people." However, after she returned home, the girl sang and cried intermittently, losing her usual behavior. Her parents also disliked her and returned her to Wang. Wang had two sons and one daughter, and she began to understand human affairs, but in her spirit, she remained foolish.

    448

    杭州紫阳山之麓，有林氏妇晨起汲井，忽重不可举。视之，则井中有一赤体小儿，长二尺许，以手攀绠，欲缘之上。大惊奔还，告其家人，其家人往视，则无睹矣，而妇遂得病，卧不能起。恒喃喃作呓语，曰：“吾金井神童也。方浴何得窥我？”嗣是妖异大作，室中什物辄被提掷毁坏。邻有秦生者，谓其夫曰：“吾为汝具状诉于关帝，汝可斋宿具香烛，持状至吴山关帝庙焚之。”其夫谨如所戒。越日，妇忽下床而跪曰：“关帝欲诛我，速请秦君为我一言，我即去矣！”其夫谋之秦，秦曰：“既称神童，而妄作祸祟，宜受诛殛，又何言焉？”已而妇病果瘳，秦复为文以谢。

    448

    At the foot of Ziyang Mountain in Hangzhou, there lived a woman from the Lin family who, upon rising in the morning to draw water from a well, suddenly found it too heavy to lift. Upon looking into the well, she saw a naked child, about two feet tall, using its hands to climb the rope, attempting to ascend. She was greatly alarmed and rushed back to inform her family. When her family came to look, they saw nothing, but the woman fell ill and could not get up. She constantly muttered incoherently, saying, "I am the golden well spirit child. How could you see me while I was bathing?" After this, strange and ominous phenomena began to occur, with objects in the room being constantly lifted and thrown, causing damage. A neighbor named Qin advised her husband, "I will write a petition to appeal to Guan Di. You should fast and prepare incense and candles, then carry the petition to the Guan Di Temple on Wushan Mountain and burn it." Her husband followed his advice. The next day, the woman suddenly got up from her bed and knelt down, saying, "Guan Di wants to punish me. Quickly ask Mr. Qin to speak on my behalf, and I will leave!" Her husband consulted Qin, who replied, "Since you are called a spirit child, yet have acted recklessly and caused trouble, you should be punished. What more is there to say?" Soon after, the woman's illness indeed healed, and Qin wrote a letter to express his gratitude.

    449

    海宁之硖石镇，西有紫薇山，东有审山，故亦名双山。审山之巅，有智标塔，甚壮伟。咸丰戊午岁，三月初六日黎明，居民忽见审山上白云澍渤，旭日照之，空明如镜。智标塔旁有无数塔，大小不一，倏忽万状：有金碧ピ赫者，有榱桷剥落者，有八角者，有四方者；一塔隐则一塔见，约半时许，共见六十四塔。日高云敛，乃无所睹。父老聚观，皆曰此名塔见（读如见龙在田），亦曰塔朝（读如春见日朝）。智标塔高据山巅，大逾常制，为塔之王，凡数百里内之塔，隔一甲子必来朝之。此说诞谩不足信。余门下士倪倬云钟祥家于紫薇山麓，是日实亲见之，语人曰：“云受日光与水受日光无异，塔影入云中犹塔影入水中，而云气变幻不定，故塔影亦随之而异。然形态虽异，而止见一塔，未尝两塔并见，则其为智标一塔之影，可无疑矣。

    Explanation

    449

    Xiazhi Town in Hai Ning County has Ziwensi Mountain to the west and Shenshan to the east, hence it is also known as Double Mountains. At the summit of Shenshan stands the Zhibiao Tower, which is exceptionally grand and majestic. On the sixth day of the third month in the year Wuxu of the Xianfeng era, as dawn broke, the residents suddenly saw white clouds swirling above Shenshan, illuminated by the morning sun, creating a clear and bright sky like a mirror. To the side of the Zhibiao Tower, there were countless towers of varying sizes and shapes, appearing and disappearing in a fleeting and varied manner. Some were golden and splendid, others had worn eaves and rafters, some were octagonal, and some were square. When one tower disappeared, another appeared. This phenomenon lasted for about half an hour, during which 64 towers were observed. As the sun rose higher and the clouds dissipated, the towers disappeared. The elderly gathered to watch, all saying this is called "Tower Appearance" (read like seeing a dragon in the fields) and also "Tower Morning" (read like seeing the sun rise in spring). The Zhibiao Tower stands high on the mountain peak, larger than usual, and is known as the "King of Towers." It is said that every tower within hundreds of miles must come to pay homage to it every 60 years. This belief is considered absurd and not credible. My student Ni Zhuoyun Zhongxiang, who lives at the foot of Ziwensi Mountain, personally witnessed this phenomenon on that day. He said to others: "The clouds receiving sunlight is similar to water receiving sunlight, and the reflection of the tower in the clouds is like the reflection of the tower in the water. However, although the shapes vary, only one tower is visible at a time, never two towers simultaneously. Therefore, it is undoubtedly the reflection of the Zhibiao Tower."

    450

    倪倬云又言：“硖石镇有古银杏二株，大十余围，相传皆东晋物也。一在其家，一在蒋氏之园。在其家者，恒见有白光夜出树下，其兄衷鲁闻人言银杏之下实生水银，所见白光必是也。乃掘坎埋一大石臼于中，俟其出，逐而陷之，而覆以巨石。天明发视，空无一物，而此后遂无所见。其在蒋氏园者，颇著灵异，人或便旋于旁，辄病。蒋氏筑小屋以奉神，榜曰”树神堂“。乱后，官就其园设里捐局。有李甲者役于局中，亦见白光出树下，知为水银，掘坎埋大缸焉。俗传水银能出入，则已成人形，收之之法，必以刀断之。若断其头，则所得少；断其股，则所得多。李是夕握刀以待，见白光过，猛斫之，果见其入于坎，乃覆以石，集火伴逻守之，天明发视，则一蚣遂蟠伏缸中，赤色熠熠，长二尺余。见人，一跃遂朱所在，众共惊异。翼日李大病，自言犯树神，当死。火伴多方祷禳，逾日乃瘳。

    Explanation

    450

    Nizhao Yun further stated: "In Xiazhi Town, there are two ancient ginkgo trees, each with a circumference of more than ten times that of an arm, and it is said they date back to the Eastern Jin Dynasty. One is near his home, and the other is in the Jiang family garden. The tree near his home often emits a white light at night, and his brother Zhonglu heard that ginkgo trees actually produce mercury, so the white light he saw must be mercury. They then dug a pit and buried a large stone mortar in it, waiting for the mercury to emerge, trapping it, and covering it with a large stone. The next morning, when they checked, there was nothing there, and after that, they no longer saw the light. The tree in the Jiang family garden was particularly mystical; anyone passing by it would fall ill. The Jiang family built a small house to worship the tree spirit, naming it 'Tree God Hall.' After a period of chaos, the government set up a donation bureau in the garden. A man named Li Jia, who worked at the bureau, also saw the white light and knew it was mercury. He dug a pit and buried a large jar in it. Folklore says that mercury can take human form, and the method to capture it is to cut it with a knife. If you cut its head, you get less mercury; if you cut its legs, you get more. That night, Li Jia held a knife and waited. When he saw the white light, he struck it fiercely. Sure enough, the mercury entered the pit, and they covered it with a stone, gathering his companions to guard it overnight. The next morning, they found a large, red, glowing creature coiled in the jar, about two feet long. When it saw them, it vanished, and everyone was amazed. The next day, Li Jia fell gravely ill, saying he had offended the tree spirit and would die. His companions prayed and performed rituals, and after a few days, he recovered."

    451

    长洲秦云，字肤雨。自言二十一岁时，方盛夏，至所亲范梦庵家。主人为设馄饨，甫食其一，忽眼昏耳鸣，觉神已出舍，飘飘然若御风而行，不能自主。至一处，两山夹立，中一大路，长松老柏，苍翠可爱。其左畔山巅有一亭，乃由麓而登，见亭中树一碑，碑文奇古，不可识。亭后一径颇曲折。遵径而行，径尽得一院宇，朱门洞开。入之，其庭甚宽广，左植幽篁数株，风来成韵。右一大树，绿荫满庭，树下有巨石，二童子坐其上，见客亦不问所从来。登其堂，空无一物。堂后又有庭，较小于前。

    Explanation

    451

    Qin Yun of Changzhou, styled Fu Yu, recounted that when he was twenty-one years old, during the height of summer, he went to visit his relative Fan Meng'an. The host prepared wontons for him. Just after eating one, suddenly his eyes dimmed and his ears rang, and he felt his spirit had left his body, floating as if riding the wind, unable to control himself. He arrived at a place where two mountains stood side by side, with a large road in the middle, surrounded by tall pines and ancient cypresses, lush and green, very lovely. On the left side of the mountain peak, there was a pavilion, which one could ascend from the foot of the mountain. Seeing a stele erected in the pavilion, the inscription on the stele was strange and ancient, and could not be recognized. Behind the pavilion, a path was quite winding. Following the path, at its end, he found a courtyard with a red door wide open. Entering it, the courtyard was very spacious, with several bamboo plants planted on the left, making a harmonious sound with the wind. On the right, a large tree provided green shade filling the courtyard, and under the tree, there was a large stone with two children sitting on it, who saw the guest but did not ask where he came from. Entering the hall, it was empty with nothing inside. Behind the hall, there was another courtyard, smaller than the previous one.

    452

    其石有门，门内一池清澄可鉴。旁有小楼，面池而构，上县一额，署曰“诗天”。爱其地，流连久之，及出则两童子不知何往矣。复至亭中远瞩，见有朱甍碧瓦隐见云间，叹曰：“如此境界，岂人间所有！吾其已离人世乎？”因念家中骨肉，不觉失声大哭，忽如梦醒，则卧榻上。范氏之人环立其前，皆喜曰：“生矣，生矣！”盖冥然若死者已一时许。范氏为延医诊视，汤药杂投，而秦固不知也。至是乃觉惫甚，若大病甫愈者然，三日之后始复其原。秦有《游诗天记》，叙述甚详。余撮其略如此。

    Explanation

    452

    A stone with a gate, inside which lies a clear pool that can be used as a mirror. Beside it stands a small pavilion, facing the pool, with a sign hung above it inscribed with the name "Poetic Heaven." Qin loved the place and lingered there for a long time, but when he left, the two children were nowhere to be seen. Returning to the pavilion to look far into the distance, he saw red-roofed and blue-tiled buildings hidden among the clouds. Exclaiming, "Such a realm, could it be in the human world? Have I already left this world?" he thought of his family and couldn't help but burst into tears. Suddenly, he woke up as if from a dream, finding himself lying on the bed. The people of the Fan family stood around him, all saying, "He has come back to life, he has come back to life!" He had been in a state of apparent death for a while. The Fan family called in a doctor to examine him, administering various medicines, but Qin was unaware of this. By then he felt very exhausted, as if just recovering from a serious illness, and three days later, he returned to his normal self. Qin has a detailed account called "A Visit to Poetic Heaven." I have summarized the main points here.

    453

    光绪六年二月某日，湖州府祭蚕神庙，有一乐工于四鼓时至庙伺候。庙与东岳庙邻，其地空旷，时官犹未至，夜景凄清。乐工出庙闲步，见东岳庙中灯火辉煌，人声嘈卉。窃窥之，见堂上坐者面目不甚可辨，阶下跪二囚，一不相识，一则城中开珠宝店之某甲也。俄见狱卒ㄏ某甲，投油镬中，大惊趋出，还顾则黑暗无睹矣。祭毕，归以语人，人莫之信，不三日而乐工死，人始异焉。访之珠宝店，则某甲已卧病，未几亦死。死之日，遍体糜烂如火灼者，乃悟乐工所见之不妄。然某甲平日亦无大恶，罔测其由。有知者曰：前年洞庭山有某氏妇，私托佣媪以珠玉宝石之类售于湖郡。

    Explanation

    453

    On a certain day in the second month of the sixth year of the Guangxu era, the prefecture of Huzhou was holding a ceremony at the temple of the silkworm god. A musician arrived at the temple to wait for the ceremony at the fourth drum beat. The temple was adjacent to the East Peak of Mount Tai Temple, and the area was spacious. At that time, officials had not yet arrived, and the night scene was desolate and clear. The musician stepped out of the temple to take a walk and saw that the East Peak of Mount Tai Temple was brilliantly lit with people making noisy sounds. He secretly peered in and saw that the people seated on the stage were not very distinguishable, while two prisoners were kneeling below the steps. One of them was unfamiliar to him, but the other was a certain Mr. Jia who owned a jewelry store in the city. Soon, he saw a prison guard throw Mr. Jia into an oil pot, and he was greatly alarmed and hurried out. Looking back, it was dark and nothing could be seen. After the ceremony, he returned and told others about what he had seen, but no one believed him. Within three days, the musician died, and people began to find it strange. No one could understand the reason. When they visited the jewelry store, they found that Mr. Jia was already sick, and soon he also died. On the day of his death, his entire body was rotten as if burned by fire, and people realized that the musician's vision was not false. However, Mr. Jia had no great evil deeds in his daily life, and no one could understand the reason. Someone said: "Last year, there was a woman from a certain family in Dongting Mountain who privately entrusted a maid to sell jewels and precious stones in the prefecture of Huzhou."

    454

    媪至郡城，亦无熟识者，惟与开眼镜店之某乙有旧。乙引之至甲所，议定价值洋钱一千有奇。媪先取奇数，余则甲书券付之。媪还舟，甫出太湖口，遭风而覆，同舟死者大半，其地距城止十八里。甲闻之，即往视，媪尸已为人捞至湖边。甲发其衣，则券在焉，取以归。私计此事惟乙知之，乃以洋钱一百赂乙，使秘其事，乙从之。山中妇闻媪死，知所托之物无可究诘，缢而死。甲罹阴罚，其以此欤？同跪之囚即乙无疑矣。或曰妇闻媪死而死，非因甲与乙之干没其赀而死也，甲乙宜若无罪焉。然甲一闻太湖有覆舟者，即冀幸媪死，及往视果然，遂搜取其券以归，此其居心为何如邪？妇虽不死于甲，而甲有可以死妇之道，乙之罪或可薄乎云尔，甲则难从未减矣。

    Explanation

    454

    The old woman arrived at the county town and had no familiar acquaintances, only an old connection with a certain Yi who ran an eyeglass shop. Yi brought her to Jia, and they agreed on a value of over a thousand dollars. The old woman first took the odd number, and the rest was paid by a promissory note from Jia. The old woman returned to her boat, but as soon as she left Taihu Lake, she encountered a storm and capsized. More than half of those on the same boat died, and the location was only eighteen li from the city. Jia heard about it and immediately went to see. The old woman's body had already been retrieved by someone to the lakeside. When Jia opened her clothes, the promissory note was there, and he took it back home. Privately, he thought that only Yi knew about this matter, so he gave one hundred dollars as a bribe to Yi to keep the matter secret, and Yi followed it. The woman in the mountains heard that the old woman had died and knew that the entrusted item could no longer be questioned, so she hanged herself and died. Jia suffered punishment from the underworld, perhaps because of this? The prisoner kneeling beside him was undoubtedly Yi. Some say that the woman hanged herself upon hearing the old woman's death, not because Jia and Yi embezzled her money, and thus Jia and Yi should be considered blameless. However, when Jia heard that there was a capsized boat in Taihu Lake, he immediately hoped that the old woman had died, and when he went to see, it turned out to be true. He then searched for and took the promissory note back home. What kind of heart does this show? Although the woman did not die because of Jia, yet Jia had a way to cause the woman's death. Yi's guilt might be somewhat reduced, but Jia's difficulty has never decreased.

    455

    光绪元年二月，上海招商局轮船名福星者，覆于黑水洋。江苏海运委员死者二十一人，满洲长小岩、大令长懋与焉。其眷属住江苏省城，犹未知其事。一日薄暮，其妻见大令自外入，倏不见。惊告于人，忽仆地作大令口音，泣述死状，并邀其同乡恒月坡司马恒坦至，托以后事。未几，凶问至，果如所言，月坡乃为经理其丧焉。越三年，又有朱云甫观察事，与此相类。朱云甫名其昂。浙江候补道，江苏宝山县人，侨居上海。其家世以沙船为业，谙悉海道，上海之有招商局，自朱君创之也。

    Explanation

    455

    In the first month of the first year of the Guangxu reign, a ship named "Fuxing" belonging to the Shanghai Shipping Bureau capsized in the East China Sea. Among the twenty-one officials of the Jiangsu Maritime Commission who perished were Manchu officials Zhang Xiaoyan and Magistrate Zhang Mao. Their families, residing in the provincial capital of Jiangsu (Nanjing), were still unaware of the incident. One evening, the wife of the magistrate saw him enter from outside and suddenly disappear. Shocked, she told others, and then collapsed, speaking in the voice of the magistrate. She cried and described how he had died, and summoned her fellow official, Magistrate Heng Yuesuo (Heng Tan), to entrust him with future matters. Shortly after, the news of his death arrived, exactly as she had described. Magistrate Heng then arranged for the funeral. Three years later, another incident involving Zhu Yunfu, an observer, occurred, similar to this one. Zhu Yunfu's name was Qi'ang. He was a waiting governor from Zhejiang, originally from Baoshan County in Jiangsu, but residing in Shanghai. His family had long been engaged in the shipping business, being well-versed in maritime routes. The establishment of the Shanghai Shipping Bureau was founded by Mr. Zhu.

    456

    购火轮船往来南北洋，而江浙之漕粮即附其船以达，朱君实专司之。光绪四年，住大沽分局，偶感时疾，旬日而亡，年未五十。亡之次日，由轮船寄信其家，船甫至，信犹未达也。其家一僮婢忽仆地作朱君语，告家人以死期，且云：“本尚可活十二年，为医药所误。今附某轮船南归，已至矣。”举家正共惶骇，而轮船中即送信至。盖人之初死，灵爽犹存，意所专注，虽远必达。圣人知之，故始死设重以依神，虞则有虞主，练则有练主，皆欲其灵爽有所式凭，不至遽散也。讲学家必执无鬼之说，魄降魂升归之大虚无物，由是而背死忘生者众矣。

    Explanation

    456

    Purchasing steamships to travel back and forth across the northern and southern seas, the grain from Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces was attached to these ships to reach its destination, with Zhu Junshi in charge of it. In the fourth year of Guangxu, he resided at the Dagu branch office, where he caught a seasonal illness and died after ten days, at an age not yet fifty. On the day after his death, a letter was sent to his family by ship, but the ship had just arrived and the letter had not yet reached them. A servant in his household suddenly fell to the ground and spoke as if Zhu Junshi, informing the family of the time of his death and saying, "Originally, I could have lived for another twelve years, but was misled by medicine. Now, I am attached to a certain steamship heading south, and it has already arrived." The whole family was in a state of great fear, and then the ship delivered the letter. When a person first dies, their spirit remains; if one focuses their intention, even if far away, they will surely reach. The sage knew this, so at the beginning of death, a heavy ritual is set up to rely on the spirit, during the mourning period there is a mourning host, and during the practice period there is a practice host, all wanting their spirits to have something to rely on, so that they do not scatter too quickly. Scholars who lecture must adhere to the belief that there are no ghosts, the soul descends and the spirit ascends, returning to the vast emptiness, thus many people forget death and life.

    457

    京师宣武门外有神獒，每出必于夜，千百犬随之而行。人或遇之，辄为所噬，相传久矣。杜筱舫观察言其戚储惺甫农部于冬夜饮友人所，醉而归。持灯独行，过菜市口。是日适决囚，遗血在地，有巨犬俯而易之，正当大路。惺甫叱之，犬一举首，则双目如炬，有异常犬，俄腾空而去。惺甫大骇，归即卧病，遂不起。所见岂即神獒邪？

    Explanation

    457

    In the capital city, outside the Xuanwu Gate, there was a divine dog. Whenever it appeared, it was always at night, and thousands of dogs followed it. People who encountered it were always bitten, and this tradition has been passed down for a long time. Respected official Du Xiaofang mentioned that his close friend Cui Xingfu, who worked in the Ministry of Agriculture, once encountered the divine dog during a cold winter night after drinking with friends and returning home. He was carrying a lamp and walking alone, passing through the market area. On that day, there had been executions, and blood was left on the ground. A large dog was seen eating the blood, right in the middle of the main road. Cui Xingfu shouted at it, and the dog suddenly lifted its head, revealing eyes like torches, unlike any other dog. It then suddenly soared into the air and departed. Cui Xingfu was greatly frightened, returned home, and fell ill, never recovering. Could what he saw have been the divine dog?

    458

    筱舫又言：惺甫官京师时，买妾生一女。甫生，即问：“尔家何业？”其音甚细而清脆可听。告之曰：“京官也。”遂不复言。至周岁后，牙牙学语，与常儿同。及惺甫卒，妾别嫁，挈女以俱，女时四岁矣。一夕，忽语其母曰：“明日大姊夫来，当从之南还。”明日，而筱舫之弟筱珊太守至。其妻乃惺甫之兄子也，因念此女，恐其沦落，故来相访。其母知不可留，遂使从筱珊俱南。时杜氏卷属寓居淮城，闻其至，妇女悉迎于庭。女独牵其姊之裾，投其怀中而泣。女与其姊初未尝相见也，举家咸异之。留杜氏五六年，送之归湖北。储故湖北人，其嫡妻犹在也，嗣后与杜氏不相闻。计其年当已嫁矣，不知有异人处否也。

    Explanation

    458

    Xiao Fang again said: When Xing Fu was serving in the capital, he bought a concubine who gave birth to a daughter. As soon as she was born, she asked, "What is your family's occupation?" Her voice was very soft and clear, pleasant to listen to. They told her, "We are officials in the capital." And then she stopped speaking. After a year, she began to speak in a baby-like manner, just like other children. When Xing Fu died, the concubine remarried and took the girl with her; the girl was then four years old. One evening, she suddenly told her mother, "Tomorrow, my eldest brother-in-law will come, and I will go south with him." The next day, Xiao Fang's younger brother, Governor Xiao Shan, arrived. His wife was Xing Fu's nephew's daughter, and because she remembered this girl, fearing that she would fall into obscurity, she came to visit. Her mother knew she could not keep her, so she sent her to go south with Xiao Shan. At that time, Du Shi's family was staying in Huai City. When they heard she had arrived, all the women welcomed her in the courtyard. The girl alone pulled her sister's skirt and threw herself into her arms, crying. The girl and her sister had never met before, and the whole family found it strange. They kept Du Shi for five or six years and then sent her back to Hubei. Chu was originally from Hubei, and his legitimate wife was still alive. Afterward, they lost contact with Du Shi. Calculating her age, she should have been married by now, but it is unknown whether she has encountered any extraordinary people.

    459

    王孝子，名继谷，字子诒，会稽人。父为鄞县教谕。光绪五年，教谕君病，孝子祷于城隍神，请以身代，然父竟不起。孝子大恸，曰：“神不鉴我，由我不以身先之也。”事母俞益谨。一日私语兄曰：“昨梦父告以母禄将尽，奈何？”明年三月，母果病，时其家犹在鄞，其兄子献孝廉奉父丧归葬会稽，独孝子留侍母病。已而闻兄营葬毕将归，喜曰：“事母有人矣。”于四月五日冒雨走出，至暮不归。明日，或言有素衣冠者僵立月湖贺公祠外水中；往视之，果孝子也，不知于何时死，立而不仆。祠前众乐亭上黏黄纸一幅，大书“漱六道人归真处”七字。又有小字云：“漱六道人者，会稽诸生也。年二十二，随父至鄞。己卯十月二日父卒，越一百八十日入月湖以去，时年二十九。”盖孝子于兄弟行居六，故尝自号漱六道人云。已而，又于其书案得二书，一致其兄子献，一致其弟子虞。

    Explanation

    459

    Wang Xiaozhi, named Jigǔ and styled Ziyí, was from Kuaiji. His father was the headmaster of a local school in Yin County. In the fifth year of the Guangxu reign (1879), when the headmaster fell ill, Wang Xiaozhi prayed to the City God, offering himself as a substitute, but his father still passed away. Wang Xiaozhi was deeply grieved and said, "The City God does not heed me because I did not offer myself first." He served his mother with even greater care. One day, he privately told his brother, "I dreamed that my father told me my mother's life span was nearly over, what shall I do?" The following year in March, his mother indeed fell ill, and at that time, his family was still in Yin. His brother's son, Xian, who had passed the imperial examination, was returning home to bury their father in Kuaiji. Alone, Wang Xiaozhi stayed behind to care for his mother's illness. Later, he heard that his brother had completed the burial and would soon return. He was pleased and said, "There is someone to take care of my mother now." On the fifth day of April, he walked out in the rain and did not return until late in the evening. The next day, someone reported that a person dressed in plain clothes and wearing a cap was standing stiffly outside the Water Lake Hé Gong Shrine in the water. They went to see, and it was indeed Wang Xiaozhi, who was unknown to have died at some point, standing motionless. On the wall of the pavilion in front of the shrine, there was a sheet of yellow paper with the large characters "The Place of Returning to the True Self" written on it. Below these characters were smaller ones that read: "The Sixth Taoist, a scholar from Kuaiji, was twenty-two years old when he followed his father to Yin. On the second day of October in the year Jǐ Mǎo, his father died. One hundred and eighty days later, he entered the Water Lake and departed, at the age of twenty-nine." It was because Wang Xiaozhi was the sixth among his brothers that he often called himself the Sixth Taoist. Later, two letters were found on his desk, one addressed to his brother's son, Xian, and one to his disciple, Yu.

    460

    致兄书言本欲留数日面诀，乃去来有定时，不能如愿；致弟书则处分身后事，佥用白布道袍，勿用僧道作法事。一时咸共悲叹，然莫知其何以死也。久之，于废纸簏中得其祷神疏稿，其略曰：“继谷于去年腊月之望，夜梦不祥，次旦即沥诚上疏，请折儿算以益母年。乃入春以来，母体违和，日益沉顿。因忆去秋父病，乞以身代，良由志愿未坚，以致精诚莫达，椎心泣血，悔恨何追。今母抱疴日亟，而兄在越未归，力竭计穷，泪枯肠裂。

    Explanation

    460

    To my elder brother, I wrote, saying that I originally intended to stay for several days to bid farewell face-to-face. However, departure and return have fixed times, and thus I could not fulfill my wish. In the letter to my younger brother, I instructed that all should be wrapped in white cloth robes, and no Buddhist or Taoist rituals should be performed. At the time, everyone grieved and lamented, but no one knew why he died.

    Later, I found his prayer to the gods in a discarded paper basket. The prayer stated: "Last December, I had a bad dream. The next day, I poured out my sincerity in a memorial, asking to transfer my son's lifespan to extend my mother's years. Since the beginning of spring, my mother's body has been out of harmony, growing increasingly weak and heavy. Reflecting on last autumn when my father was ill, I offered to take his place, but due to lack of resolve, my sincere wish was not fulfilled. I grieved deeply, regretting it too late. Now, my mother carries illness day by day, and my elder brother is in Yue and has not returned. I am exhausted of strength and out of ideas, my tears dried up, my heart torn apart."

    461

    若空言吁祷，恐难感格神明，股剖肝，不免伤残肢体。曷若踵汨罗之行，嗣曹江之志，削儿纪算，续母桑榆。至于晨昏侍奉，尚有诸昆，似续示祧，已延弱息。尘世利名，固非本怀，身后毁誉，在所不计。湛湛月湖，寸心可鉴，神听不远，哀此愚忱。”乃知孝子实因代母而死，遗笔不言者，恐伤母心耳。其兄在里门，梦孝子乘白马渡江而来，从者甚众，前有金牌，大书“孝子悌弟”字，已而讣至。然则所云“来去有定时”者，必非无因矣。子献寓书于余，请为铭诔，以传其弟之奇孝。余养疴吴下，未有以应，先书其大略于此。

    Explanation

    461

    If one merely speaks and prays, fearing that it would not move the gods, and if one sacrifices one's flesh and heart, one cannot help but harm one's body. Why not follow the path of Mìluó, continue the will of Cáo Jiāng, shorten one's life span, and extend one's mother's years in old age? As for serving one's mother in the morning and evening, there are still other brothers to assist, and the continuation of the family line has already been extended by a weak offspring. Worldly gains and fame are not one's true desires, and praise or blame after death are not worth considering. The clear moon lake reflects the sincerity of one's heart, and the gods are not far away, grieving for this foolish sincerity. It is known that the filial son truly died in place of his mother, and the will does not speak of it, fearing to hurt the mother's heart. His brother, at the gate of the village, dreamed that the filial son came riding a white horse across the river, accompanied by many followers, with a golden plaque in front bearing the words "Filial Son and Respectful Brother." Soon after, the news of his death arrived. Thus, the saying "there is a fixed time for coming and going" must not be without reason. Zǐ Xiàn wrote to me, asking me to compose a memorial to commemorate his brother's extraordinary filial piety. I am convalescing in Wu, unable to respond immediately, but I will first record the general outline here.

    462

    距天津百里而遥，有地曰唐官屯，亦一屯阝聚也。光绪庚辰夏，其地有民家女死于雷，年甫十五六耳。久之，有知其故者，盖其家多畜鸡，而恒为人所攘，女甚憾焉，日踪迹之，无所得。有某甲者夫妇，年四十余，甫得一子，皆大喜。乃于子生之十二日为汤饼之会，杀鸡使妇烹之，而自出外买酒肉之类。女至其门外，见有鸡毛，疑焉；入视之，妇适奏厕。北俗所卧暖坑，即与灶相连，女见锅中热气炯炯，启盖而视，鸡也，恨曰：“此必昨日窃之我家者。”顾视坑上一婴儿，方呱呱而泣，即抱儿置锅中，仍为盖之而出。妇如厕还。失儿，觉锅盖微有异，发视，则儿与鸡俱烂矣。痛甚，倒投水缸中以死。其夫持酒肉自外至，儿死于锅，妇死于缸，莫测其由，亦缢而死。其家更无余人，邻比为殡之而已，无穷究其事者。及女死于雷，邻女之与女狎者知此事，始为人言之。

    Explanation

    462

    A hundred li away from Tianjin, there is a place called Tangguantun, also a small settlement. In the summer of the Gengchen year during the Guangxu era, a girl from a common household in that place died from lightning, being just fifteen or sixteen years old. After some time, someone who knew the cause revealed that the girl's family kept many chickens, which were often stolen by others. The girl was very resentful and spent each day tracking down the thieves, but to no avail. There was a couple in their forties, who had just welcomed a son, and they were all greatly pleased. They held a soup and noodles celebration on the twelfth day after the child's birth, killing a chicken and having the wife cook it, while the husband went out to buy wine and meat. The girl came to their door, saw some chicken feathers, and became suspicious; she entered and saw the wife had just gone to the toilet. In northern customs, the warm pit where one sleeps is connected to the stove. The girl saw hot steam rising from the pot, opened the lid to look, and found a chicken inside. She grumbled, "This must be the chicken stolen from my house yesterday." Looking at the warm pit, she saw an infant crying, and immediately picked up the baby and placed it in the pot, then covered it and left. The wife returned from the toilet. She found the baby missing and noticed something was amiss with the pot lid; upon opening it, she saw both the baby and the chicken were cooked to death. She was extremely grief-stricken and threw herself into a water jar, where she died. Her husband returned with the wine and meat, finding the baby dead in the pot and his wife dead in the water jar. Unable to understand what had happened, he also hanged himself. There were no other family members left, and the neighbors simply buried them. No one further investigated the matter. When the girl died from lightning, a neighbor girl who was close to her learned of this story and began to tell people about it.

    463

    同治九年三月，绍兴府南门外从空坠一女，年十七八，貌颇娟好。问其姓氏，言语不能通，以手示意索纸笔。即与之，自书：“蜀人，距成都三千里。随母至田间，忽为狂风吹入空中，瞬息至此。”道旁观者如堵墙。有一士、一农、一贾，皆欲得之以为妇。里长闻于官，官命自择所从，颜不对；固强之，乃指为士者，遂以鼓吹送归成礼。秀水钱心庵作《莺啼序》一阕纪其事。按元郝文忠《陵川集》中，有《天赐夫人》词，亦蜀人，正与相类。又光绪五年十月初十日，京师安定门外有地名八公爷坟。是日午后，天忽起旋风。其地有十五岁之女在途，被风摄至半空，逾时落下即毙，其半面焦黑如墨。同一风吹女子，而有幸有不幸，昔人所以有茵溷之喻也。

    Explanation

    463

    In the third month of the ninth year of the Tongzhi era, a girl fell from the sky outside the southern gate of Shaoxing Prefecture. She was about seventeen or eighteen years old, with a beautiful and graceful appearance. When asked about her surname, she could not communicate verbally, but she gestured to indicate she wanted paper and ink. Paper and ink were given to her, and she wrote: "I am from Shu, three thousand miles away from Chengdu. I followed my mother to the fields when suddenly a strong wind blew me into the air, and in an instant, I arrived here." People watching from the roadside were like a wall. There was a scholar, a farmer, and a merchant, all of whom wanted to marry her. The village head reported this to the official, who ordered her to choose for herself. She blushed and did not respond; they insisted, and she finally pointed to the scholar, so they sent her back with music and completed the wedding ceremony. Xian'an of Xiushui wrote a 'Ying Ti Xu' (Eagle Cry Sequence) to record this event. According to the 'Lingchuan Collection' by Hao Wenzhong of the Yuan dynasty, there is a poem titled 'Heavenly Gifted Lady,' which is also a person from Shu, similar to this case. On the tenth day of the tenth month of the fifth year of the Guangxu era, outside the An Ding Gate of Beijing, there is a place named Ba Gong Ye's Tomb. That afternoon, a whirlwind suddenly arose. There was a fifteen-year-old girl on the road, who was carried into the air by the wind, and after a while, she fell and died, with half her face burned black like ink. The same wind blew a girl, yet some were fortunate and others were unfortunate, which is why ancient people used the metaphor of being in a bed or a toilet.

    464

    河南颍州人李麟生玉方，以县令需次江苏。自言幼时至桐城一亲串家，其家适请狐仙。狐仙乃姊妹二人，一女巫主之。巫至，先相度坐处，分前后二室，前室陈果饵，必精美，后室则粗具蔬果而已，盖以待其从者也。甲夜仙至，闻其语不见其人。俄而后室中哺啜之声大作，前之人责之曰：“此家以礼相邀，何太无状？”内竟寂然。乃诘主人所问何事，一一酬答，既毕辞去。一人曰：“可瞻仙范否？”应曰：“可。”其来时置梯于中庭，及去，见二女子拾级而登，及ニ而隐；年可十六七，容貌失丽，而身材甚短，较之常人不过三之二而已。仙畏灯烛光，虽香烟亦避之。惜麟生方龆龀，不能言其详，但记大略如此。

    464

    Li Lin, a native of Yingzhou in Henan, gave birth to Yu Fang, who was waiting in Jiangsu as a county magistrate. He once mentioned that when he was young, he visited a relative's house in Tongcheng. His family happened to invite a fox spirit. The fox spirit was a pair of sisters, and a female shaman presided over the ritual. When the shaman arrived, she first chose the seating arrangement, dividing the space into two rooms, front and back. The front room was arranged with fruits and snacks, which were always exquisite, while the back room had only roughly prepared vegetables and fruits, presumably to accommodate the spirits' attendants. At midnight, the spirits arrived, and he heard their voices but did not see them. Soon, the sound of eating and drinking in the back room grew loud. The person in the front room scolded them, saying, "This family has invited you with proper etiquette, why are you so uncouth?" The back room then fell silent. The spirits were then questioned by the host, and they answered each question one by one before departing. One person asked, "Can we see the appearance of the spirits?" They replied, "Yes." When the spirits arrived, they placed a ladder in the courtyard, and when they left, two women were seen climbing the stairs step by step, and then they disappeared. They were about sixteen or seventeen years old, with beautiful appearances, but their bodies were unusually short, being only two-thirds the height of ordinary people. The spirits were afraid of the light from candles and lamps, and they even avoided the smoke from incense. Unfortunately, Li Lin was just a child at the time and could not describe the details, but he remembers the general outline like this.

    465

    蒋清翊，字敬臣，苏人也，以知县需次浙中。性耽翰墨，注《王子安集》颇详赡，余尝为作序。知余搜访异闻，乃以纪梦数事见示，今录如左。其一事云：同治壬戌岁在安邑县，梦坐花下湖山石上。有一大池，池之北，画阁琼窗，悉以五色颇黎嵌之，中有诸女笑语声。问此何地，一女曰：“此君旧所管领之寒香洞也，何忘之邪？”梦中亦自忆为旧游之地。俄鼠触物有声，遂觉。又一事云：丙寅岁附轮船至天津，中夜梦至一处，图书满室。

    Explanation

    465

    Jiang Qingyi, courtesy name Jingchen, was from Suzhou. He served as a magistrate in Zhejiang. He was passionate about literature and calligraphy, and he annotated Wang Zifan's Collection with great detail and richness. I once wrote a preface for it. Knowing that I was seeking out unusual stories, he showed me several dream experiences, which I now record as follows. One of these dreams occurred during the year Renxu of the Tongzhi era while he was in Anyi County. In the dream, he was sitting under a flower, on a stone by a lake and mountain. There was a large pond, and to the north of the pond, there were painted pavilions and crystal windows, all adorned with colorful glass. Inside, he heard the sounds of laughter and conversation from several women. He asked where this place was, and one woman replied, "This is the Cold Fragrance Cave that you once governed. How could you have forgotten it?" In the dream, he also remembered it as a place he had visited before. Suddenly, a mouse touched something and made a sound, and he woke up. Another dream occurred in the year Binyin when he traveled by steamship to Tianjin. In the middle of the night, he dreamed of arriving at a place where books filled the room.

    466

    有人告曰：“此为少海，上帝命唐龙门王勃在此修《金台仙录》。”俄而王出，梦中以《王集》多散佚，求观其全。王抽架上一帙示之，是《太极宫飞霞阁铭》，有序文甚长，可数万字。读已，王命送出。闻舟中人语而寤，则舟进烟台口矣。又一事云：戊辰岁在苏州，梦乡人箫鼓赛神。问何神，曰：“秦穆公也。”梦中为作祭文，觉而记其一联云：“雄狐定晋，兆霸迹于韩原；雌雉兴秦，膺灵符于陈宝。”忆《封禅书》，祠陈宝是秦文公，颇悔用事之失。后读《索隐》引《列异传》，则作秦穆公，梦语固不误也。

    Explanation

    466

    Someone reported that this was the Sea of Shao, where the Supreme Deity had commanded Tang Longmen Wang Bo to compile the Jintai Xianlu (Record of the Golden Platform Immortals). Soon after, Wang emerged from his dream, lamenting that many of his works were lost or scattered, and requested to see the complete version. Wang took a volume from the shelf and showed it to him; it was the Taixing Gong Feixia Ge Ming (Inscription of the Flying Cloud Pavilion in the Palace of Supreme Harmony), with a lengthy preface and main text totaling tens of thousands of characters. After reading, Wang ordered him to be sent away. He woke up hearing people talking on the boat, and the boat had already entered the mouth of Yantai.

    Another incident is said: In the year of Wu Chen, when he was in Suzhou, he dreamed of people from his hometown playing flutes and drums to worship the gods. He asked what god it was, and they said, "It was Qin Mu Gong." In his dream, he composed an offering text. Upon waking, he remembered one couplet, saying: "A male fox determined the fate of Jin, foretelling the rise of a hegemon at Han Yuan; a female pheasant brought prosperity to Qin, receiving the divine sign at Chen Bao." Recalling the Fengchan Shu (Book of Sacrifices to Heaven and Earth), he noted that the temple of Chen Bao was dedicated to Qin Wen Gong, and he deeply regretted the mistake in his use of events. Later, he read Suo Yin (Hidden Meaning), which cited Lieyi Zhuan (Record of Strange Events), mentioning Qin Mu Gong instead. The dream was indeed accurate.

    467

    南府城外商山西北隅，有邢妃坟，虽碑志无考，而草间石兽犹存。故老相传妃即圆圆，吴梅村所为作《圆圆曲》者也。嘉庆间，苏州郑生客游滇，春日踏青商山，访圆圆墓不得，崩榛荒葛中，忽迷归路。俄而落照西沉，暮烟笼树，遥望前途，似有人家，思往借宿。至则朱门洞开，玉金铺，俨然王侯第宅。乃使阍者转达，良久而出，导入东厢，为设食樽酒，簋贰亦极精洁。饭已，有老妪出问：“客操吴音，是何乡贯？”具告之。少选，妪秉烛而出，肃客登堂。有女子容色绝代，羽服霓衣，如女冠装束，降阶而迎，曰：“妾即邢氏，埋香地下，百有余年，时移物换，丘陇就平。念君是妾同乡，有小诗十首，求为传播。”因命侍女取诗付郑。

    467

    Outside the northwest corner of Shangshan Mountain in Nanfu City, there is a grave of a concubine named Xing. Although the inscriptions on the stele are not verifiable, stone animals still remain among the grass. According to old stories, the concubine is known as Yuan Yuan, and the poem "Yuan Yuan Qu" was written by Wu Meichun. During the Jiaqing era, a man from Suzhou named Zheng was traveling in Yunnan. In spring, he hiked in Shangshan Mountain and failed to find Yuan Yuan's tomb. Surrounded by dense undergrowth and wild vines, he suddenly lost his way. The setting sun soon descended, and the evening mist enveloped the trees. Looking ahead, he saw what seemed to be an inhabited place and thought to seek shelter there. Upon arrival, he found large, open red doors, with luxurious decorations of jade and gold, resembling the residence of a noble or high-ranking official. He sent a servant to convey his request, and after a long wait, someone came out and led him into the eastern wing, where he was served food and wine. The dishes were very refined and clean. After the meal, an elderly woman came out and asked, "The guest speaks with a Wu accent. Where is your hometown?" Zheng provided detailed information. Shortly after, the woman came out with a candle and respectfully led Zheng into the main hall. There was a woman of extraordinary beauty, dressed in a feathered robe and a colorful dress, resembling a nun. She descended the stairs to greet him, saying, "I am Xing, buried underground for over a hundred years. Time has passed, and circumstances have changed, and the hills and mounds have been leveled. I remember that you are from the same hometown as me. I have ten short poems and ask you to spread them." She then ordered a female servant to fetch the poems and give them to Zheng.

    468

    其末章云：“鸳鸯化尽鱼鳞瓦，难觅当年竺落宫。”郑问竺落之义，曰：“竺落皇笳天，是南方八天之一，载在《道经》，妾旧时所居宫名也。”取翠玉笛一枝以赠，并吟一诗曰：“叹息沧桑易变迁，西郊风雨自年年。感君吊我商山下，冷落平原旧墓田。”遂命送郑出。时东方微明，向之第宅俱无所见，惟四面隐隐若有垣墉，谛视之，则深林掩映而已。然袖中玉笛故在，视其诗笺，则多年败纸，触手欲腐，墨色亦暗淡，迥非人世之物。郑以幽会荒唐，刻圆圆遗诗，托诸箕笔。东海刘古石傅会作《商山鸾影传奇》，弥失其真。苏人蒋敬臣为余言如此，因纪其大略。至其诗，世多有之，且亦不甚工，故不录也。“竺落皇笳天”，据道家书为十八色界天之一云。

    Explanation

    468

    The final chapter says: "The mandarin ducks transformed into fish-scale tiles, and the former Zhuolu Palace is hard to find." Zheng asked about the meaning of Zhuolu, and she replied, "Zhuolu is one of the eighteen color realms, known as the 'Southern Eight Heavens,' recorded in the Daojing (Daoist Canon), and it was the name of the palace where I once lived." She took a jade flute and gave it as a gift, and recited a poem: "Lamenting the easy changes of the world, the winds and rains of the western suburbs year after year. Moved by your visit to me at Shangshan, the desolate old burial grounds on the plain." Then she ordered Zheng to be escorted out. At the time, the east was just beginning to lighten, and the former mansions were all nowhere to be seen. Only the four sides faintly had walls, and upon closer inspection, it was just a deep forest. But the jade flute in the sleeve was still there, and looking at the poem, it was old and worn paper, almost rotten to the touch, and the ink was faded, clearly not of this world. Zheng thought the secret meeting was absurd, carved Yuan Yuan's farewell poem, and entrusted it to the writing brush. Donghai Liu Gushi compiled the legendary "Shangshan Phoenix Shadow," further lost its truth. The Suzhou man Jiang Jingchen told me this, so I recorded its general outline. As for the poem, there are many versions in the world, and it is not very well-crafted, so I will not record it. "Zhuolu is one of the eighteen color realms," as recorded in Daoist texts.

    469

    常州一女子，谈者失其姓氏，自幼为父母鬻于妓船。女绝色也，船媪颇珍之。稍长，导以淫，不从，日楚挞之，卒不可。而客来见女者，艳其色，争以利，冀当女意，故虽不荐枕席，而所得缠头倍他妓，媪以故稍宽之，女亦私积蓄阴自为计。咸丰己未岁，媪载女至湖州，有市人陆姓者温温然善伺人意，貌亦都雅。女年幼，于世情未阅历，见之以为佳士也，遂与订嫁娶。

    Explanation

    469

    A woman from Changzhou, whose name and surname are unknown, was sold by her parents into a brothel when she was young. She was exceptionally beautiful, and the madam of the brothel valued her highly. As she grew older, she was pressured into sexual exploitation, but she refused, leading to daily beatings until she finally could not endure it. Guests who saw her were captivated by her beauty and tried to bribe her to satisfy her desires, so even though she did not engage in sexual acts, she received more gifts than other courtesans, which led the madam to be somewhat lenient with her, and the girl secretly saved money for her own purposes. In the year 1859, the madam took the girl to Huzhou, where a man surnamed Lu, who was gentle and skilled at reading people's intentions, and also had a refined appearance, appeared. The girl, being young and inexperienced in the ways of the world, saw him as a good man and thus arranged a marriage with him.

    470

    陆去，女白媪曰：“某日陆郎以洋钱二百来赎儿，儿即辞母去，苟不见许，儿死，于母无益也。”媪知不可夺，诺之。女虑媪中变，遍诣邻舟告以故，且言别。至期，陆不至，使招之来，问爽约之故。陆言无赀。女出私蓄洋钱百五十畀之，期于明日来。陆得钱即赴博场，顷刻而尽。他妓有知其事者，以告女，女犹不信。明日，陆仍不至，使招之，则徒手来，问昨所赠，曰：“罄矣。”女哭失声。陆乘间亡去，女遂仰药死。湖郡诸耆老闻之，皆悯女而恶陆之无行，强使经纪其丧焉。余亡友徐诚庵大令为作《兰陵女子传》。

    Explanation

    470

    Lu left, and the girl told the old woman, "On a certain day, Lu will come with two hundred foreign silver dollars to redeem me. If you do not agree, I will die, which will not benefit you." Knowing she could not change the old woman's mind, she agreed. Worried that the old woman might change her mind, the girl went to all the neighboring boats to inform them of the reason and also to say goodbye. On the appointed day, Lu did not arrive, and she sent someone to call him. She asked why he had broken his promise. Lu said he had no money. The girl then took out her private savings of one hundred and fifty foreign silver dollars and gave them to him, promising he would come the next day. As soon as Lu got the money, he went to the gambling den and quickly spent it all. Some courtesans who knew what had happened told the girl, but she still did not believe them. The next day, Lu still did not come, and when she sent someone to call him, he came empty-handed. When asked about the money he had been given the previous day, he said, "It's all gone." The girl cried out in despair. Taking the opportunity, Lu fled, and the girl then committed suicide by drinking poison. The elderly and respected citizens of the Hu area heard about it and all felt pity for the girl and disapproved of Lu's behavior, forcing them to arrange her funeral. My late friend Magistrate Xu Cheng'an wrote a biography titled "The Story of the Girl from Lanling."

    471

    月桂者，直隶栾城妓也。家贫，父母强使为之，而非其志。燕赵妓多奔走逆旅，媚过客，桂独否。蜀人刘斗山明经曾诣之，欣然延接，为竟夕谈，如文士，如山人，斗山甚异之。一日，有老尼踵其门，施以钱米，俱不受，愿一见。既入，无寒暄语，辄大笑，而桂对之痛哭不已。尼曰：“无过悲，若不忘本来，某日吾迟汝于某所。”桂唯唯。届期如尼言，至其处，果得一庵。桂入庵拜佛及尼，已而出翦刀自断其发。从之往者惊阻不及，劝之归，不可，乃还告其父母。越日复往，则庵中阒然，桂与尼俱杳矣。

    Explanation

    471

    Moon桂 was a courtesan from Luan County, Zhili (modern-day Hebei Province). Born into a poor family, her parents forced her into this profession, which was not in line with her wishes. Courtesans in the regions of Yan and Zhao were often seen running around inns and flattering passing travelers, but Moon桂 did not follow this path. A scholar named Liu Doushan, who had passed the imperial examinations, once visited her. She welcomed him joyfully and they engaged in a long conversation that night, during which she spoke like a scholar and a hermit, which greatly surprised Doushan. One day, an elderly nun knocked on her door and offered her money and rice, but Moon桂 refused both. The nun wished to see her. Upon entering, the nun did not exchange any small talk and immediately laughed, while Moon桂 cried bitterly without stopping. The nun said, "Do not be too sad. If you do not forget your true nature, on a certain day I will wait for you at a certain place." Moon桂 nodded in agreement. On the appointed day, as the nun had said, she went to the place and indeed found a hermitage. Moon桂 entered the hermitage, paid respects to the Buddha and the nun, and then took out a pair of scissors and cut off her own hair. Those who followed her were too shocked to stop her, tried to persuade her to return, but she refused and they returned to inform her parents. The next day, she went back, but the hermitage was empty, and both Moon桂 and the nun had disappeared.

    472

    四川石泉县刘氏女，许嫁同邑罗氏子。罗氏子与女同龄，年十五遘疾而卒。女欲奔赴，父母不可，意常戚戚，家人虑其死，严守之。父母潜受他姓之聘，秘不使知，女微闻之，佯为欢笑，父母以为无他也。其明年三月十六日，距罗氏子殁&lt；辶周&gt；岁矣，适父母兄嫂俱外出，女挈其妹如邻媪家。先是，媪有子失足堕江死，其妇闻之亦投江以殉，盖其家屋后滨江，女素所知也。语媪曰：“顷伤于手，闻媪后园多药草，愿往求焉。”妹时年十二矣，诧曰：“姊伤手，吾奚不知？”索观之，女敛手袖中，不使见。旋乞火燃香，执之而出，媪诘何为，曰：“辟秽气耳！”妹觉有异，从之出后户，女插香于地，拜且泣。

    Explanation

    472

    A woman from the Liu family in Shiquan County, Sichuan, was engaged to a boy from the Luo family in the same town. The boy was the same age as the girl and died at fifteen from an illness. The girl wanted to go to him, but her parents refused, and she often felt deeply sorrowful. Her family worried about her well-being and kept a close watch on her. The girl's parents secretly accepted a proposal from another family, keeping it hidden from her. She heard about it indirectly and pretended to be happy, and her parents thought there was nothing wrong. A year later, on March 16th, exactly one year after the boy's death, when her parents and elder brother and sister-in-law were all out, the girl took her younger sister to the house of a neighboring elderly woman. Previously, the woman's son had fallen into the river and drowned, and upon hearing this, his wife also threw herself into the river to die as a sacrifice. The girl knew that the woman's house faced the river. She told the woman, "I recently injured my hand and heard that your back garden has many medicinal herbs, so I wish to go and find them." The younger sister, who was twelve years old, was surprised and said, "Sister, if you hurt your hand, how could I not know?" She asked to see the injury, but the girl hid her hand in her sleeve, not letting her see. Then, she asked for fire to light incense, took it out, and the woman asked what she was doing. The girl replied, "To ward off foul odors!" The younger sister sensed something unusual and followed her out through the back door. The girl inserted the incense into the ground, bowed, and wept.

    473

    妹问之，不答；趋走江边，妹掺执其祛，女急解外衣掷付妹，曰：“好事父母，吾去矣。”一跃入水。其族祖刘翁自隔江望见之，疑是女，急使人援之出，面如生而气已绝。其上下衣缝纫不可解，亦不甚沾濡，但肩背间有方尺许者，稍渍水耳。蜀俗死于外者，不以尸入室，议殡于门外。其嫂曰：“小姑从容就义，虽死犹生，请以尸入，我任其咎。”乃奉尸亻夷于堂。亲故来吊者，咸叹美之，而父母所许之某氏子亦至，愿一见其面。或曰：“不可。”或曰：“是固旧姻也，庸何伤？”时女已小敛，某氏子至女侧，女口鼻忽出血巽其面。某氏子惊仆，舆归，逾月竟死。道光壬辰年，有司以女贞烈闻于朝，旌其闾。命下之前一夕，其母梦女来，曰：“女奉上帝命，为湔江之神，不克在父母左右矣。

    Explanation

    473

    The younger sister asked her, but she did not answer; she ran to the riverside, and the younger sister held her sleeve. The girl quickly removed her outer garment and threw it to her sister, saying, "I will do good deeds for my parents, and I am leaving now." She jumped into the water. Her clan elder Liu翁 saw her from across the river, suspecting it was the girl, and quickly sent someone to pull her out. Her face was still alive, but her breath had stopped. Her clothes were sewn so tightly that they could not be undone, nor were they very wet, but there were about a square foot of her shoulders and back that were slightly wet. In Shu custom, those who die outside are not brought into the house, and they discussed burying her outside the door. Her sister-in-law said, "My younger sister-in-law calmly accepted her fate; although dead, she is still alive. Please bring her body inside, and I will take the blame." They then carried her body into the hall. Family and friends who came to mourn her all praised her, and the son of a certain family whom her parents had promised her also arrived, wishing to see her face once more. Some said, "No, you should not." Others said, "She is our old relative; what harm is there?" At that time, the girl had already been dressed in the small clothing, and when the son of that family came to her side, she suddenly bled from her mouth and nose, staining his face. The son of that family was shocked and fell, then was carried back home. After a month, he died. In the year Renchen of Daoguang, the officials reported her loyalty and bravery to the court, and honored her family's gate. On the night before the order was issued, her mother dreamed that the girl came to her, saying, "I am serving the command of the Heavenly Deity as the god of the River Jian, and I cannot stay by my parents' side anymore."

    474

    ”语已而拜，拜毕，有从者数十辈为女易冠服，色皆纯白，衣竟欲去。母挽留之，忽然而寤，以为积想所致。而湔江中有人堕水者，往往遇神人拯之而免，其神人之状，则少女而白衣，乃知即刘女也。嗣后灵迹甚著，邑人醵金建庙，颜曰“湔江水神庙”，香火颇盛。至乙未岁，其侄刘斗山明经，自他所闻其里火，不及车马，徒步而归，未至家已暮矣。至一桥，见有人迎面来，肥而短，其身正方。既过，念世间安有如此人，岂鬼邪？回顾之，见两磷火大如否，投之以石。其人复来，与相对而立，磷火荧然。目视之，则缩小如豆，稍瞬复大，如是久之，其人长啸而去。斗山不觉自随之行，俄闻水声，悟曰：“此必溺鬼也。”却立不前，而昏无所睹，觉有数人牵曳之。正危窘间，忽闻呼曰：“娘娘来矣！”皆散去。

    Explanation

    474

    After finishing his speech, he bowed. Upon completing the bow, several dozen followers appeared, dressed in pure white, and were about to leave in their clothes. The mother tried to detain them, but he suddenly woke up, thinking it was due to accumulated thoughts. In the Jian River, there were people who had fallen into the water, and they often encountered a divine person who saved them. The appearance of this divine person was that of a young girl wearing white, and he then realized it was Liu's daughter. Afterward, her miraculous deeds were well-known, and the townspeople raised money to build a temple, naming it the "Jian River Water God Temple," which became very popular.
