What will they be used for?" He informed them that it would be for the temple pillars. Shen realized that the person he saw in the dream was none other than Lord Dai, and he donated the wood to help. When the temple was completed, there was still surplus funds, so a garden was opened behind the temple as a place for the townspeople to visit. When I was young, I entered the temple and saw the hall and house were majestic, with four large pillars. The elders still recounted the story to inform others. By the end of the Xianfeng era, the town suffered from war and fire, and the temple of Lord Dai was destroyed again. After the chaos, it was rebuilt, but the beauty of the garden has not returned to its former appearance.

    518

    邑人童朴斋居新市镇，家饶于赀。其屋深邃，内楼前后各五间。童因眷口无多，于前楼作卧室，而空后楼，以储什物。一夕，闻后楼砰磅有声，犹不之异。次夕，则闻后楼语言嘈碎，达旦不休。天明，童往视之。甫上阶，闻内有人语曰：“居停主人来矣！”中扉顿启，一老翁出，颜丹发白，衣冠伟然，延童入内。童素有胆气，知其妖也，而不之惧。入则几案虽童之故物，而陈设一新。几列鼎彝，壁县字画，架庋书籍，地下厚藉氍毹，棹椅之类皆覆以锦褥。中县珠灯四，缨络烂然。坐定，翁自言：“姓锺，名紫霞，陕之华阴人。

    518

    A Townsman named Tong Puzhai lived in the new market town, wealthy in assets. His house was deep and secluded, with five rooms in front and five behind. Because he had few family members, he used the front part of the house as a bedroom and left the back part empty for storing miscellaneous items. One night, he heard a loud, heavy sound from the back part of the house, but he did not find it strange. The next night, he heard noisy and fragmented speech from the back part of the house, which continued until dawn. At dawn, Tong went to investigate. As soon as he stepped onto the stairs, he heard someone inside say, "The host is coming!" The middle door suddenly opened, and an old man emerged, with a red face and white hair, dressed in grand attire, and invited Tong inside. Tong usually had courage, knowing it was an evil spirit, but he did not fear it. Upon entering, he found that the tables, though his old furniture, were newly arranged. The tables were lined with ritual vessels, the walls hung with paintings, the shelves full of books, and the floor thickly covered with rugs. The chairs and tables were all covered with brocade cushions, and four pearl lamps hung in the middle, with elaborate decorations shining brightly. Once settled, the old man spoke about himself, saying, "My surname is Zhong, my given name is Zixia, from Huayin in Shaanxi."

    519

    携眷属遍历名区，昨从虎丘来，将游西湖，并穷天台、雁荡之胜。而山妻忽病，暂借君楼停泊行李，必有以相报，幸勿闻之外人。”辞气温雅，蔼然可亲，童敬诺之。越一日，复具衣冠通谒，老翁使一童子献茶，余无所见，而楼上则讠连讠娄细语，若有数十人者。翁语童曰：“来而不往，诚为非礼。然老夫世外人，习于疏懒，不能报谒，幸勿罪也！”自后，童或无事，辄往钟所，杯茗清谈，常至终日。时亦置酒邀童饮，酒次，纵论今古经史百家，如肉贯串。每及南宋、元、明之事，历历若亲见；至北宋以上，则言其大概而已。

    Explanation

    519

    With family in tow, I visited many famous places. Yesterday, we came from Tiger Hill and were planning to tour West Lake, as well as explore the scenic beauty of Mount Tiantai and Mount Yandang. However, my wife suddenly fell ill, so I temporarily borrowed your room to store our belongings and promised to repay you, hoping you would keep this a secret from others. Your words were warm and polite, and you seemed friendly and approachable, so the child respectfully agreed. The next day, I dressed formally and paid a visit. The old man sent a child to offer tea, and I saw nothing else, but upstairs there was soft, whispered conversation, as if there were dozens of people. The old man said to the child, "It would be impolite not to return the visit. However, I am a recluse, accustomed to a simple and lazy life, and cannot reciprocate, so please do not blame me!" After that, whenever the child had nothing to do, he would often visit me, and we would spend the whole day chatting over tea. Sometimes, I would also invite the child to drink wine, and during these times, we would freely discuss history, classics, and various schools of thought, as if the topics were connected like links in a chain. Whenever we talked about events from the Southern Song Dynasty, Yuan Dynasty, and Ming Dynasty, the details were vivid, as if we had seen them personally; but when it came to the Northern Song Dynasty and earlier periods, we would only speak of the general outlines.

    520

    童偶叩以吉凶祸福，则曰：“‘惠迪吉，从逆凶’，理有固然，毋烦推测也。”又善书画，尝写扇面赠童，一面临《兰亭叙》，一面仿南宫泼墨山水，童甚宝之。时当初夏，庭际荼蘼盛开。童之女名颖姑者，年九岁矣，独往采花。见美妇倚阑干立，乌帕裹首，紫衣而素裳。一垂髫婢侍以手招女，女不肯往，妇自出携女手，遂从之入。见老翁方危坐观书，笑谓女曰：“颖姑来，可楼上坐。”及登楼上，陈设视楼下更胜。诸婢年皆十许岁，呼妇为夫人。夫人寝室在东偏，有一小女，双足纤小，殆无甚匹。夫人曰：“此吾女也，少于汝一岁，汝宜妹之。

    Explanation

    520

    The child inquired about auspiciousness, misfortune, and fortune, and the old man replied, “‘Following the righteous path brings blessing, while going against it brings misfortune’—this is a natural order, and there is no need to speculate.” He was also skilled in painting and calligraphy, and once gave a fan to the child, one side of which he copied the Lanting Xu, and the other side he imitated the freehand ink landscape style of Nangong. The child treasured it greatly. It was early summer, and the tea vine flowers were blooming in the garden. The child’s daughter, named Yinggu, who was nine years old, went to pick flowers alone. She saw a beautiful woman leaning against the railing, her head wrapped in a black scarf, wearing a purple robe and a plain skirt. A young maid with hanging hair beckoned to the girl with her hand, but the girl did not go. The woman then came out and took the girl’s hand, and they followed her inside. They saw the old man sitting upright, reading a book, and he smiled and said to the girl, “Yinggu, come up and sit on the second floor.” When they reached the second floor, the arrangement was even more splendid than downstairs. The maids, all around ten years old, called the woman “Madam.” The Madam’s bedroom was on the eastern side, and there was a little girl with very small feet, almost unmatched. The Madam said, “This is my daughter, who is one year younger than you; you should treat her as your younger sister.”

    521

    ”至西偏，则一美少年坐而读书，夫人曰：“此吾子也，汝宜兄之。”有顷，一少妇自后房出，夫人使女呼以嫂。遂命嫂治点心。食毕，又以一篮盛食物，命小婢送至庭中，颖姑携篮归。其母视之，非藤非竹，组织甚工。启而视之，则内盛苹果四枚、西凉紫蒲萄数百颗，讶曰：“此地此时安有此物邪？”嗣后每得时物，童辄命颖姑持饷其女，而夫人亦常以异果奇花相报。一日，颖姑甫缠足已，即至钟所，不良能行。夫人笑曰：“汝母不善缠足耳，吾为若缠之，非但不痛，且有捷效。”乃解其双行缠，以口呵气，其热如蒸，足骨柔软，约缣迫袜，了无所苦。一月之后，纤小与钟女不殊，其母窃启裹视之，所渗非矾，乃白糖也。

    Explanation

    521

    When they reached the west side, a handsome young man was sitting and reading. The lady said, "This is my son; you should treat him as a brother." Soon after, a young woman came out from the inner room. The lady had her maid call her "sister-in-law" and then ordered the sister-in-law to prepare some light snacks. After eating, she gave a basket full of food to a little maid to take to the courtyard. Yinggu carried the basket back. The basket was neither bamboo nor vine, but very well made. When opened, it contained four apples and hundreds of purple grapes from Xiliang. The mother was surprised and said, "How could there be such things here at this time?"

    Thereafter, whenever she got seasonal delicacies, the child would always order Yinggu to take them to his daughter, and the lady would often send exotic fruits and rare flowers in return. One day, Yinggu had just finished binding her feet and went to Zhong's place, but she could not walk well. The lady laughed and said, "Your mother is not good at binding feet; I will bind them for you. Not only will it not hurt, but it will have quick results." She then untied the double bindings, blew warm air into the feet, which felt as hot as steam. The foot bones became soft, and she tied the cloth tightly and put on socks, without any discomfort. After one month, Yinggu's feet became as delicate and small as Zhong's daughter. Her mother secretly opened the bindings to look, and the substance used was not alum; it was actually white sugar.

    522

    其少妇则教颖姑以钅咸黹，端午日制艾虎及彩缕贻之，并极精妙，殆非人工所能为。居半年余，童所见惟翁与一僮，而颖姑则翁之全家皆得见之。童父女频如钟所，而翁与夫人则至庭中而止，未尝一诣前楼也。童虽秘其事，而仆媪辈不能无漏言，里中多有知者，人皆为童危，而童与翁益密。其友徐淳庵以刚直自负，适自山东回，语童曰：“子祸必矣，家为妖窟，而尚以为安乎？”童不应。徐自与所善贝炼师谋，贝闭目默坐，移时谓徐曰：“此狐也，然非祸人者，不久当自去，无烦驱逐也。

    Explanation

    522

    The child's young wife taught Yinggu embroidery. On the Dragon Boat Festival, she made an embroidered tiger and colorful threads and gave them to her, which were extremely exquisite, almost not something that could be done by human hands. After living there for more than half a year, the child only saw the old man and one servant, while Yinggu was seen by the entire family of the old man. The child's father and daughter frequently visited the temple, but the old man and his wife only went to the courtyard and stopped there, never once visiting the front building. The child kept the matter secret, but the servants and maids could not help but let it slip. Many in the neighborhood knew about it, and people all worried about the child. But the child and the old man became even closer. His friend Xu Chun'an was proud of his integrity and straightforwardness. He just returned from Shandong. Xu Chun'an said to the child, "You are certainly in trouble. Your home is a den of demons, and you still think it's safe?" The child did not respond. Xu Chun'an then consulted with his friend Bei Lianti, a Taoist priest. Bei Lianti closed his eyes and sat in silence for a while before saying to Xu Chun'an, "This is a fox spirit, but it is not harmful. It will leave soon, and there's no need to drive it away."

    523

    ”无何，有一道人黑面而虬须，背负一剑，过童氏之门，语阍者曰：“汝家妖气甚盛，吾能除之，无所求于主人，但妖死我愿得其皮耳。”时童适他出，阍者不告于主母，引道人自小弄而达于内。道人即于庭中，右手仗剑，向空画符，左手持水一杯，吸而喷之。闻楼上语曰：“庭中演戏剧矣，盍往观乎？”俄而道人手中之剑自坠，杯亦堕地而碎。道人大惊，若有以大杖敲其头者，踉跄走出。阍随之奔，蹶于阈，伤其股。楼上大笑。江阴陈澹山者，童之姻也，素善五雷之法，自苏至杭，道出新市，因诣童。童留之饭，而徐亦至，遂共饭焉。徐向陈述其事，童以目止之，不可，陈乃曰：“吾请尝试之。

    Explanation

    523

    Soon after, a Taoist with a black face and curly beard, carrying a sword on his back, passed by the gate of the Tong family. He spoke to the gatekeeper, saying, "Your family has a very strong demonic aura. I can eliminate it, and I do not seek anything from the master. But if the demon dies, I only wish to obtain its skin." At that time, Tong happened to be out, and the gatekeeper did not inform the mistress. He led the Taoist through a small passage to the inner part of the house. The Taoist immediately stood in the courtyard, right hand holding the sword and drawing a talisman towards the sky, left hand holding a cup of water, sucking and spitting it out. Suddenly, someone upstairs said, "There is a play being performed in the courtyard. Why don't you go and watch?" Soon the sword in the Taoist's hand fell, and the cup also fell to the ground and broke. The Taoist was greatly surprised, as if struck by a large stick on the head, and stumbled out. The gatekeeper followed and ran, tripping at the threshold and injuring his thigh. Those upstairs laughed loudly. Chen Danshan of Jiangyin, a relative of Tong, has always been skilled in the Five Thunder Techniques. He was passing through Xinshi on his way from Suzhou to Hangzhou and therefore visited Tong. Tong kept him for a meal, and Xu also arrived, so they all had a meal together. Xu told Chen about the matter, but Tong stopped him with a look, saying it was not possible. Chen then said, "I will try it."

    524

    ”时夜将半，陈命燃二烛，箧中出五色纸人五，按方位置于案，画符呵气于其上，正目视之不一瞬。有顷，纸人忽起立绕案而行，行稍缓，又呵以气，则行益加疾，其声隐隐如雷。至五更，陈曰：“妖已去矣！”以手击案，纸人皆仆，仍藏之箧中。是夜，童之内人闻后楼喧阗如初至时，窃从后窗窥之，见后楼上下皆红光围绕。闻翁与夫人语曰：“本欲俟尔病愈而行，今陈翁以真火相逼，不可留矣！惜不及与贤主人一别耳。”至五更，声乃绝。黎明入视，几榻如旧，地无纤尘，而所陈设则乌有矣。几上一小箧，启之，乃钟翁留别书也，情辞恳挚，微有怨徐之言，童读之黯然。

    Explanation

    524

    At midnight, Chen ordered two candles to be lit, and he took out five colored paper figures from a chest. He arranged them on the table according to a specific pattern, drew talismans on them, and blew his breath onto them while staring fixedly without blinking. After a while, the paper figures suddenly stood up and began to walk around the table. Their pace was slow at first, but when Chen blew breath onto them again, they walked even faster, their sound muffled like thunder. By the fifth watch, Chen declared, "The demon has left!" He struck the table with his hand, and the paper figures all fell down. He then hid them back in the chest. That night, the maid heard the back building bustling as if they had just arrived. She secretly peeked through a back window and saw the upper and lower parts of the building surrounded by red light. She overheard the old man and his wife saying, "We originally intended to wait for you to recover before leaving, but now Mr. Chen has forced us with his true fire; we cannot stay! We regret not being able to say goodbye to the noble master." By the fifth watch, the sound ceased. At dawn, she entered to see the table and bed unchanged, the floor clean, and everything else gone. On the table was a small chest. When she opened it, she found a farewell letter from Mr. Zhong, written with sincere and earnest words, slightly expressing resentment at the slow pace. The maid read it sadly.

    525

    外留定武不损本《兰亭》一册，郭忠恕山水一幅，以赠童者。又白金五斤，则赁屋之值也。夫人留珊瑚簪一枝、羊脂玉二枚、明珠四颗、碧霞宝石八块，赠颖姑为嫁资。澹山入室周视，叹曰：“此狐为我真火所逼，尚能从容而去，其道力深矣。幸其大道已成，故其临去不肯露恶状，不然吾非其敌也。”乃铸铁牌二面，书朱符其上，一钉于听事，一钉于后楼，以防复至，后竟寂然。此事在乾隆初年，邑人徐晦堂先生曾详记其事，余粗加序次而著于篇。传记所载狐居人家者多矣，未有若此之有情有礼者也。余尤叹其临去有君子交绝不出恶声之意。斯狐也，其狐而仙者与！

    Explanation

    525

    A copy of the undamaged original Lanting Xu (Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion) by Wang Xizhi, along with a landscape painting by Guo Zhongshu, was left outside to be given to the children. Additionally, five pounds of silver were left, which was the value of renting the house. The lady left a coral hairpin, two pieces of yuzhi jade, four pearls, and eight pieces of greenish-blue stones as a dowry for Yinggu.

    Dansen entered the room and looked around, sighing, "This fox was driven away by my true fire, yet it could still leave calmly. Its spiritual power is profound. Fortunately, it has already achieved its great path, so it did not reveal its evil form when it left. Otherwise, I would not have been its match." He then cast two iron tablets, inscribed red talismans on them, and nailed one in the hall of audience and one in the back building to prevent the fox from returning. Later, it remained silent. This event took place during the early years of the Qianlong reign. A local scholar named Xu Huitang once recorded this matter in detail. I have roughly arranged and recorded it in this text.

    Many foxes living among people are recorded in biographies, but none have shown such emotion and propriety as this one. I especially lament its intention to leave without uttering harsh words, as a gentleman would. This fox, could it be a fox that has become a deity?

    526

    贝炼师，不知其名，居武康之高池。有道术，人皆尊之曰“炼师”，即前所记徐淳庵与谋童氏之妖者也。时嘉兴有钜室吴姓者，居北门外，其屋历二百余年矣。是年夏间久雨，忽有黑物出于爨室，长五六尺，广一尺许，无头面手足。近之则寒气逼人，兼有冷水喷射，中之者寒颤欲绝。每阴雨则见，晴明则否。有佃人赵某宿于其家，吴氏之佣告知此事，赵有拳勇，不畏也。夜适大雨，赵与佣痛饮大醉，俱至爨室伺之。二更后，是物果出。两人直前扑之，忽冷水四射，佣者倒地，赵奋击以拳，若中绵絮者然，而臂已断矣。大呼求救，闻者奔集，则是物已杳。

    Explanation

    526

    Bai the Master of Alchemy, whose full name is unknown, dwelled in the high pool of Wukang. He possessed Daoist techniques, and people all respected him as "Master of Alchemy." He is the same as the one mentioned before, who conspired with Xu Chun'an to deal with the evil of the Tong family.

    At that time, there was a wealthy family named Wu in Jiaxing, residing outside the northern gate. Their house had been there for over two hundred years. In that year during the summer, it rained for a long time. Suddenly, a black thing appeared in the kitchen, about five or six feet long and about one foot wide, without head, face, hands, or feet. When approached, it was cold and oppressive, and cold water was sprayed. Those hit by it shivered so much they almost died. It appeared every time it was rainy or cloudy, but not when it was sunny and clear.

    A tenant named Zhao stayed at their house, and the Wu family's servant told him about this. Zhao was brave and did not fear it. That night, it rained heavily, and Zhao and the servant drank heavily and got drunk. They both went to the kitchen to wait for it. After the second watch, the object indeed appeared. The two men rushed forward to attack it. Suddenly, cold water was sprayed everywhere, and the servant fell to the ground. Zhao struck it with his fist, as if hitting a piece of cotton, but his arm was already broken. He shouted for help, and those who heard ran to gather, but the object had already disappeared.

    527

    视佣则面白&lt；面&gt；&lt；面&gt；，噤不能语；赵则倒卧血中，呻吟不辍，久之始能言所见。遍寻断臂不可得，后两人虽皆不死，而赵竟成废人焉。吴氏患之，乃至武康请于贝炼师，贝曰：“此物仅能于阴雨及夜间见形，气候尚浅，除之不难。”乃书一符，使其徒持以往，即焚于灶前。忽天色骤晦，巨雷绕屋而转，屋中诸物皆岌岌震动。俄辟历一声，硝磺之气满室，人有眩仆者，而天即晴霁。周视诸物，悉如其旧，惟有一大缸可容十余石者，本埋地中，为雷拔起，碎为数十块。视其下清水氵营氵荧，有巨蚌卧其中，其广修与前所见物等，业已断而为二，赵臂即在其旁。吴氏曰：“此缸埋自前明，贮以浊水，经宿则清而且甘，用以烹茶极佳。举家宝之，不知有怪物潜藏其下也。”乃以炭火钅蚌成灰，弃之北丽桥下，而归断臂于赵。

    Explanation

    527

    A servant, his face pale and ashen, could not speak; Zhao, on the other hand, lay on the ground in blood, groaning continuously. After a long time, he was finally able to describe what he had seen. They searched everywhere for the amputated arm but could not find it. Although neither of them died, Zhao ended up as a cripple. Wu was worried about this and went to Wu Kang to seek help from the Taoist master Bei. Bei said, "This creature can only appear during rainy and nighttime conditions. The weather is still mild, so it is not difficult to get rid of it." He then wrote a talisman, had his disciple carry it to the location, and immediately burned it in front of the stove. Suddenly, the sky darkened abruptly, and a huge thunderstorm circled the house. All the things inside the house shook violently. Soon, a thunderclap sounded, and the smell of gunpowder filled the room. Some people fainted, but the sky immediately cleared up. Looking around, everything was as before, except for a large jar capable of holding more than ten stones, which was originally buried in the ground. It was pulled up by the thunder and broke into dozens of pieces. Looking at the bottom, there was clear water, and a large clam was lying in it. Its width and length were equal to the previously seen object, and it was already cut into two. Zhao's arm was right next to it. Wu said, "This jar was buried from the Ming Dynasty. It stored turbid water, which became clear and sweet after a night and was very good for cooking tea. The whole family treasured it, not knowing that a monster was hidden underneath." They then burned the clam into ash with charcoal fire and threw it away under the North Beauty Bridge, and returned Zhao's arm to him.

    528

    归安、德清、石门三邑之交，有古墓焉，背田面河，广可二亩余。规模虽颇宏敞，而兆域久湮，宰木亦尽，惟河边华表犹存。羊虎辟邪之类皆倒于地，翁仲仆者五，其一尚垂绅端笏独立荒烟蔓草中，然陷于土者将半矣。既无碑志可稽，亦从无祭扫者，不知为谁氏之茔也。有侵其墓傍地者，往往得祸，故相戒莫敢犯。附近居民每见有青衣人往来墓域，则是日河中必有溺死者。溺人无论肥瘠，一经灭顶，虽即时拯起，皮骨仅存，若枯腊然。于是人皆远徙，遂成荒墟。墓南里许为某村，有孙姓者，其妻携女往母家。舟过墓前，忽一青色手自水出，大如箕，攫其女入水去，惊而救之，则已死矣。孙恨甚，筑土壅流而掘之，至数尺，无所见。又年余，孙之次子与一佣者，各负斫草将归矣。

    Explanation

    528

    At the intersection of the counties of Guian, Deqing, and Shimmen, there is an ancient tomb. Its back faces the fields and its front faces the river, covering an area of about two acres. Although the tomb is quite large, the boundaries have long been lost, and the sacrificial trees have also perished. Only the stone pillars at the entrance of the tomb still stand. The stone statues of mythical creatures and officials have fallen to the ground, and five stone statues of officials are lying on the ground, one of which is still standing with a sash hanging down and a tablet held in hand, alone in the midst of overgrown weeds and smoke, but half of it has sunk into the ground. There are no inscriptions or records to verify, and there has never been anyone who has visited or cleaned the tomb, so it is unknown whose tomb this is. Those who encroach on the land around the tomb often suffer misfortune, so they warn each other not to dare to violate it. The nearby residents often see a person dressed in green walking around the tomb, and on that day, there must be someone who drowns in the river. Whether the drowned person is fat or thin, once they are submerged, even if they are rescued immediately, their skin and bones are only left, as if they were dried meat. Therefore, the people have all moved far away, and the area has become a desolate wasteland. About a mile south of the tomb is a village called a certain village, where a person surnamed Sun lives. His wife took their daughter to visit her mother's house. When they passed by the tomb on a boat, suddenly a green hand emerged from the water, as large as a basket, and snatched the girl into the water. The husband was very angry, and they built up the soil to block the flow and dug it up, but they found nothing. Another year later, Sun's second son and a hired worker were carrying a hoe and chopping grass, about to return home.

    529

    骤雨忽至，见河中一人，以竹篙撑木{捭}而过。其子私计，由{捭}渡河而南，不及一里即至家矣，乃许持篙者以钱而登{捭}焉。{捭}小止容一人，故佣者仍沿河岸行。行数武，回顾之，见人与木没于水矣，急告其家。孙率家众飞棹而至，钩捞水中，仅得其子之尸，而撑{捭}者不知所之，咸以为妖鬼所幻化也。孙益愤怒，募术士治之。一道人来应募，为坛于河之南岸。方施敕勒，有暴风自北而至，一青衣人随风径扑道人。道人不能支，为其所压。青衣人倏不见，风亦寻止，众视道人，则已扁矣（扁音补典反，见《后汉书。东夷传》），流血殷然，告于官而瘗之。时乌程南浔镇有道士周静涵者，道行甚高，孙往求之。周闭目移时，谓孙曰：“此物修炼千年，吾非其敌，须召天神为助。

    Explanation

    529

    A sudden downpour came, and they saw a man in the river using a bamboo pole to paddle a small boat. The son privately thought, "If I cross the river by boat to the south, it would not take more than a mile to get home," so he agreed to pay the boatman and boarded the boat. The boat was so small it could only hold one person, so the boatman continued to paddle along the riverbank. After walking a few steps, he looked back and saw the man and the boat disappear into the water. He hurriedly informed his family. Sun led his family to row quickly to the scene, pulled out with hooks from the water, but only recovered the body of his son, and the boatman was nowhere to be found. Everyone believed it was a trick of demons or ghosts. Sun became even angrier and hired a magician to deal with it. A Taoist priest came to accept the offer and set up an altar on the southern bank of the river. Just as he was performing rituals, a strong wind suddenly came from the north, and a person in green clothes was blown straight at the Taoist priest. The Taoist priest could not withstand it and was crushed by the person. The person in green clothes suddenly disappeared, and the wind also stopped soon after. The crowd looked at the Taoist priest, who was already flat (dead). Blood was flowing profusely, and they reported it to the authorities and buried him. At that time, there was a Taoist priest named Zhou Jinghan in the town of Nanxun, Wucheng, whose spiritual practice was very high. Sun went to seek his help. Zhou closed his eyes for a while and said to Sun, "This creature has been修炼 for a thousand years. I am not his match. We must summon heavenly gods for assistance."

    530

    虽幸胜之，吾亦将不免，不可往也。”孙固请之，周不得已而往，亦为坛于南岸。甫焚一符，暴风又起，青衣人见于前曰：“吾大道垂成，须得生人气血以助之，故溺人于河，取其气血，然所溺皆应死之人，非吾故戕其生也。与汝何与，而见逼迫？”周令其远去，青衣人曰：“吾道尚未成，去将焉往？”周苦劝之，不听。周乃向空书符，以水巽之。青衣人笑曰：“汝修止三世，不满四百年，安能治我？”径前扑之。周执令牌危坐，青衣人不敢近而退。周亦下坛如孙氏，命扫除一室，凝神静坐于其中者九日，复至坛所焚符，而天神降。青衣人出，将与神敌。神发巨雷，声振原野，火随雷出，其光烛天，青衣人不支而遁。周掷剑中其首，神向周拱手，上入云际，遂不复见。

    Explanation

    530

    Even if I am fortunate to win, I will still not escape, and I cannot go." Sun persistently requested him, and Zhou had no choice but to go, also setting up a platform on the south bank. Just as he burned one talisman, a violent wind arose again, and a person in green clothes appeared before him and said, "My great way is nearly complete, and I must obtain the vital energy of a living person to assist it, so I drowned people in the river to take their vital energy. However, those I drowned were all people who should have died, not intentionally harming their lives. What do you have to do with me, and why are you being forced?" Zhou ordered him to go far away, and the person in green clothes said, "My way is not yet complete, where will I go?" Zhou earnestly advised him, but he did not listen. Zhou then wrote a talisman in the air and sprinkled water over it. The person in green clothes laughed and said, "You have only practiced for three generations, less than four hundred years, how can you control me?" He advanced directly to attack him. Zhou held the token and sat upright, and the person in green clothes did not dare to approach and retreated. Zhou also descended from the platform like Sun, ordered the room to be cleaned, and sat in meditation and quiet contemplation for nine days. He then returned to the platform to burn the talisman, and the heavenly god descended. The person in green clothes appeared, intending to fight the god. The god unleashed a tremendous thunderclap, the sound shaking the plain, and fire followed the thunder, its light illuminating the sky. The person in green clothes could not withstand it and fled. Zhou threw his sword and struck his head, and the god bowed to Zhou, ascending into the clouds, and was never seen again.

    531

    村人共见空中一物随剑堕于河干，就而视之，乃石人之半面也。渡河至墓域，则独立之翁仲其右面为剑斫去，血流至地，附以河干之半面正相吻合。周书符于其胸背，命众掘起而碎之。村人并碎其卧者而锻为灰，自是墓前无复怪异。周谓众曰：“此物再潜养五甲子，大道成矣。惜其欲速，遂至于此，深可悯也。”众送周归南浔，甫入门，见一人衣冠形状与周无异，自外而入，众皆见之。周曰：“吾元神将散矣！”取盐汤饮之，其人遂与周合为一。后周遂多病，自处分身后事，临终谓其徒曰：“吾为虚名所累，致损元功。尔等宜闭户自修，毋预外事也。”遂坐而化。此亦出徐晦堂先生所记，其事当在乾隆初年。余考之汪谢城广文所撰《南浔志》，周静涵名科耀，一名道昱，嘉兴人，习青符五雷法。初居武康之升元观，后住南浔广惠宫，著有《补闲吟草》四卷。

    Explanation

    531

    Villagers saw an object fall from the sky with a sword to the riverbank. Upon closer inspection, it was found to be half of a stone statue. Crossing the river to the tomb area, they discovered a standing stone statue with its right side cut off by a sword, as if blood had flowed to the ground. When the half from the riverbank was attached, it fit perfectly. Zhou inscribed a talisman on the statue's chest and back, then ordered the villagers to dig it up and destroy it. The villagers also destroyed other statues and turned them into ash. Afterward, there were no more strange occurrences at the tomb. Zhou said to the villagers, "This object needed to be hidden for 50 years to achieve perfection. It was too hasty, leading to its destruction; it is deeply pitiable." The villagers escorted Zhou back to Nanxun. As soon as they entered the gate, they saw a man with the same attire and appearance as Zhou enter from outside, and everyone witnessed this. Zhou said, "My spiritual essence is dispersing!" He drank saltwater, and the two individuals merged into one. Afterward, Zhou fell ill frequently, handling his affairs for after death. On his deathbed, he told his disciples, "I was burdened by reputation, which hindered my spiritual progress. You should isolate yourselves and focus on self-cultivation, avoiding external distractions." He then sat and passed away. This account also comes from Mr. Xu Huitang's records, and the events took place during the early years of the Qianlong era. I verified this information in Mr. Wang Xiecheng's Nanxun Gazetteer. Zhou Jinghan's name was Qiaoyao, also known as DaoYu, from Jiaxing. He practiced the Five Thunder Talisman techniques. Initially residing at the Shengyuan Temple in Wuchang, he later stayed at the Guanghui Palace in Nanxun, and wrote a four-volume collection titled Bu Jian Yin Cao.

    532

    吾邑乾元寺，在吴羌山之麓，背山而面河，隔河则县城环峙若屏幛然。门前有大道，然地僻山空，过者绝少。邑人某君，与其友读书寺中，其友喜习阴符奇遁之术，某恒非笑之。友尝于月夜启匣，出一剑，长二尺余，晶莹夺目。持而舞于树下，觉空中有细物簌簌落地上。舞毕，拾视之，乃树之细枝也，修短若一。某异之，欲把其剑，友曰：“剑有神光，触之恐不利于君。”仍匣之而寝。一日薄暮，散步于三门外，友笑谓某曰：“君常笑我读奇门书，盍一观我技乎？”乃中立于路，画地作圈，径可丈余；取瓦石数十块，分而为八，排列圈外，坐而俟之。

    Explanation

    532

    The Dry Yuan Temple in my county is located at the foot of Wuqiang Mountain, backed by mountains and facing the river. Across the river, the county town stands like a screen or barrier. There is a main road in front of the temple, but the place is remote and mountainous, with very few passersby. A man from the county and his friend studied in the temple. His friend enjoyed learning the mysterious and secretive arts, and the other always mocked him. One night under the moonlight, the friend opened a box and took out a sword, about two feet long, with a brilliant and dazzling appearance. He held it and danced under the tree, feeling small objects falling from the air onto the ground. After finishing, he picked them up and saw that they were small branches from the tree, all of the same length. The other found this strange and wanted to take the sword. The friend said, "The sword has divine light, and touching it might not be good for you." He put it back in its case and went to sleep.

    One evening, he took a walk outside the third gate of the temple. The friend smiled and said to him, "You often mock me for reading books on the mysterious arts. Why don't you see my skills?" He stood in the middle of the road and drew a circle on the ground, with a diameter of about one zhang (about 3.3 meters). He took several dozen pieces of tiles and stones and divided them into eight groups, arranging them outside the circle. He sat and waited.

    533

    少顷，有一人衣白衣荷糖担，手执小锣而来，走入圈中。其人素所识也，友曰：“殆矣，此人将蹶！”言未竟，果触石而颠，碎其所赍，且伤于膝，坐地良久乃去。某问故，曰：“吾所布者，武侯八门阵也。今日支干皆火，而其人所衣所持悉属金，且入惊门，出伤门，能无蹶乎！”翼日，某又请为之，布置未已，忽闻对岸有人从城上厉声呼其友姓名，曰：“汝又在此恶作剧乎？”友遽伏地叩头，曰：“不敢，不敢。”闻其声，似秦人也。遥望之，其人面，而目甚大，余为雉堞所蔽，且相距甚远，不甚了了，须臾便去。某扶其友起，面色如死灰，战忄栗不已。问之，但曰：“我师也。”诘其姓名，不告。后其友旋辞去，终莫知其师为谁也。

    Explanation

    533

    A short while later, a man dressed in white clothing, carrying a sugarcane pole, and holding a small gong approached and entered the circle. This man was someone the friend had previously known, and he said, "Danger is imminent, this person will stumble!" Before he finished speaking, the man indeed touched a stone and fell, breaking what he carried and injuring his knee. He sat on the ground for a long time before leaving. Someone asked the reason, and replied, "What I have set up is the Eight Gates Formation attributed to Wu Hou. Today, both the Heavenly Stem and Earthly Branch are associated with fire, and the person he wore and held were all associated with metal, and he entered the gate of surprise and exited through the gate of injury, how could he not stumble?" The next day, someone again requested to perform it, and the arrangement was not yet complete when suddenly a loud voice was heard from the opposite shore, calling out the friend's name from the city wall, saying, "Are you here to play tricks again?" The friend immediately fell to the ground and kowtowed, saying, "Not daring, not daring." The voice seemed to be from a person from the Qin region. Looking at him from a distance, the person had a dark face and very large eyes, but the view was obstructed by the battlements and they were very far apart, so it was not very clear. He left shortly thereafter. Someone helped his friend up, whose face was as pale as ashes and trembling uncontrollably. When asked, he only said, "He is my teacher." When asked for his name, he did not tell. Later, the friend soon left, and ultimately, no one knew who his teacher was.

    534

    绍兴昌安门外某村，有张世昌者，贾于外，恒累月不归，家中惟老母魏氏与妻同居。妻与母固姑侄也，亦甚相得。姑病思食鸡，妇杀鸡烹之，偶取一脔尝其旨否，姑适呼妇。妇未及咽而欲应，声不能出，气塞而蹶。姑频呼不应，强起视之，则已死矣。时方盛夏，疑其中暑，治之不苏。以母家相距百里，酷暑不能待，乃邀集邻比买棺敛之。又以家无停棺之所，舁而置之祖坟之侧，距其家五六里矣。而妇实未死，棺行摇动，其气复转，至夜而苏。觉身在棺中，闷不可耐，幸棺甚薄，足蹶手扌市，竟破之而出。茫茫四野，不知为何处，坐棺侧而泣。

    Explanation

    534

    A Certain Village Outside the Chang'an Gate in Shaoxing

    In a certain village outside the Chang'an Gate in Shaoxing, there lived a man named Zhang Shichang, who often traded outside and was frequently away for months. At home, only his old mother Wei and his wife lived together. The wife and mother were originally aunt and niece, and they also got along very well. One day, the mother-in-law was ill and wanted to eat chicken. The wife killed the chicken and cooked it, occasionally taking a piece to taste if it was delicious. Just as the mother-in-law happened to call the wife, the wife had not yet swallowed the piece when she wanted to respond, but her voice could not come out, and she choked and fainted. The mother-in-law repeatedly called out but received no response, so she forced herself up to see, and found that the wife was already dead. It was the height of summer, and they suspected that she had heatstroke, but despite treatment, she did not revive. Because the mother's family lived a hundred miles away, and the intense heat could not wait, they invited neighbors to buy a coffin and prepare the body. And because there was no place to keep the coffin at home, they carried it and placed it beside the ancestral grave, about five or six miles from their home. But the wife was actually not dead; as the coffin was carried, it moved, and her breath returned, and she revived at night. She realized she was in the coffin, feeling suffocated, fortunately the coffin was very thin, and she kicked and pulled with her hands until she broke through and emerged. She found herself in the vast wilderness, not knowing where she was, sitting beside the coffin and crying.

    535

    有菩提庵僧曰独修者，与佣工马四索逋而归，经由其地。闻泣声而问之，妇以实告。僧见妇有色，诡曰：“吾归必由汝村，盍从我行，我挈汝归。”妇诺之。行里许，至一村，则马四之兄马二家也。马四亦喻其意，而思独据妇，乃谓僧曰：“此妇死而复生，宜得糜粥调养。我与师亦饥矣，请至吾兄家小坐，略进饮食。”僧从其言。而是夜马二与妇均往妇家，家中阒其无人，马四固知之。及门，抉锁筒而入，邻人闻声，以为马二夫妇夜归，不问也。马四淅米溉鬻，使僧执爨，潜取析薪之斧，猛击僧头，毙之。即谓妇曰：“汝从我乎？不从者亦血吾斧。”妇惧，从之，乃席卷其兄之所有，挟妇以逃。次日马二归，见状，闻于官。

    Explanation

    535

    There was a monk named Duxiu at the Bodhi Temple, who went home with the hired laborer Ma Si to collect a debt. Passing through a village, they heard crying and asked about it. The woman told them the truth. The monk saw the woman and found her attractive, and he said, "I will return by your village. Why don't you follow me? I will take you back home." The woman agreed. After walking a few miles, they arrived at a village, which was Ma Si's brother Ma Er's house. Ma Si understood the monk's intentions and thought he would take the woman alone, so he said to the monk, "This woman died and came back to life, and she should be given porridge for nourishment. We are also hungry, so let's go to my brother's house for a moment, and we can have some food." The monk agreed. That night, Ma Er and the woman were both at her house, but there was no one there. Ma Si knew this. When they reached the door, he forced open the lock and entered. The neighbors heard the noise and thought it was Ma Er and his wife returning home, so they did not intervene. Ma Si rinsed the rice and cooked porridge, and the monk cooked the food. Ma Si secretly took an axe used for splitting firewood and struck the monk's head, killing him. He then said to the woman, "Will you follow me? If not, you will also be killed by my axe." The woman was afraid and followed him. Ma Si took everything belonging to his brother and took the woman and fled. The next day, Ma Er returned home and found the situation, and he reported it to the authorities.

    536

    而独修之徒知师被杀，马四不回，亦控于官，请捕马四。久之不获。张世昌之妻父魏翁，闻女暴卒，驰至棺所，则无尸矣，亦控于官。官诣视，罔测其故，命役缉访而已。及张世昌归，以妻死尸亡，再四寻求，将及一年，殊无踪迹。李茂元者，其素与同贾者也，是岁秋仍邀世昌同行，世昌以母病辞。茂元独行，至台州宁海县城外，见一家有妇出汲，视之世昌妻也。询之其邻，知为县中捕役许保贤之家。遽归告世昌，世昌乃诣县，请自任缉访之役，官判公牍与之。世昌与茂元偕妻父魏翁同至宁海，伺妇出，拉以赴官，并出公牍为据。

    Explanation

    536

    While the disciples of Du Xiu knew that their teacher had been killed and that Ma Si had not returned, they also complained to the authorities, requesting the capture of Ma Si. For a long time, the investigation was unsuccessful. Wei Old Man, Zhang Shichang's father-in-law, heard that his daughter had died suddenly and rushed to the coffin. However, when he arrived, there was no body inside. He then complained to the authorities. The officials went to investigate but were unable to ascertain the cause and ordered the officials to investigate and search.

    When Zhang Shichang returned, because his wife had died and her body was missing, he searched for her repeatedly for almost a year, but there was completely no trace of her. Li Maoyuan, who had always traded with him, that autumn still invited Shichang to travel together, but Shichang declined due to his mother's illness. Li Maoyuan continued his journey alone. Upon arriving at the city of Ninghai in Taizhou, he saw a woman drawing water outside a house. He recognized her as Zhang Shichang's wife. He asked the neighbors, who told him that she was the wife of Xu Baohan, a police officer in the county. Li Maoyuan hurriedly returned to inform Shichang. Shichang then went to the county and requested to take on the task of investigation and search. The official issued a public document to him.

    Shichang, along with Li Maoyuan and his father-in-law Wei Old Man, traveled to Ninghai. They waited for the woman to come out and then pulled her to the authorities, presenting the public document as evidence.

    537

    官问妇，妇直言前事，盖马四挟妇至天台，又由天台转至宁海，赀用乏绝，投许保贤家为佣。许艳妇，欲私之，妇不可。乃与马四俱出捕贼，数日，许独归，谓妇曰：“马四死于水矣。”妇知有变，未敢言也。其夜，裸妇而缚之，与淫焉，妇不得拒，遂受其污，已数月矣。官执许保贤，问马四死状，初不言。刑之，始言：“诱与外出，而毙以斧。”官验马四之尸，伤痕宛然，乃论许保贤如律，而归妇于本夫。世昌以妇遭污辱，实为强暴所迫，告于母而收之，复为夫妇如初。异哉！小家一妇，微有姿首，三人为之并命。佛言‘慎勿与色会，色会即祸生’，岂不信夫！又马四之杀独修以斧，而许保贤之杀马四亦以斧。道家言枉杀人者，是以兵刃而更相杀，亦可畏矣。

    Explanation

    537

    The official questioned the woman, who spoke frankly about the previous events: it is said that Ma Si took the woman to Tiantai, and then from Tiantai to Ninghai. When they ran out of resources, she went to work for Xu Baoxian as a servant. Xu was attracted to the woman and wanted to have an affair with her, but the woman refused. Then, he went out with Ma Si to catch bandits. After several days, Xu returned alone and told the woman, "Ma Si has died in the water." The woman knew something was wrong but dared not speak of it. That night, Xu stripped the woman and tied her up, engaging in sexual acts with her. The woman could not resist and was thus defiled, having endured this for several months.

    The official arrested Xu Baoxian and asked about the circumstances of Ma Si's death, but Xu initially said nothing. After being tortured, Xu finally confessed, "I lured her out and killed him with an axe." The official examined Ma Si's body and found the wounds clearly visible, so he punished Xu Baoxian according to the law and returned the woman to her original husband. Shichang, because the woman was defiled, was actually forced by violence, told his mother and took her back, and they resumed their marriage as before. Strange indeed! A woman from a small family, slightly attractive, caused three men to lose their lives for her. The Buddha says, "Be careful not to meet with beauty, for meeting with beauty brings calamity," is it not true? Moreover, Ma Si killed Du Xiu with an axe, and Xu Baoxian also killed Ma Si with an axe. Taoist teachings say that those who unjustly kill are killed with weapons and in turn kill others, and are also to be feared.

    538

    广西南宁府税关前有旗竿二。某年秋间，居民望见旗竿斗中焱焱有光，每夜皆然，已月余矣。一日大雨如倾，雷火下击旗竿，而斗中有赤光上冲，雷遇之辄引去，如是三四。忽辟历一声，屋瓦皆震，赤光顿息，雨亦寻止。守关者仰望斗中，若有物然。缘竿而上，见其中蟠一蜈蚣，长可三尺，赤黑斑斓，已为雷火所毙。众皆骇异，乃出而县之竹竿，以示观者。关吏之庖人曰，李元敏于是夜出户便旋，见蜈蚣头中有光，高丈许，知其有宝。窃斫破之，得一珠大如龙眼，李匿其珠，弃蜈蚣于水。次日人不见蜈蚣，疑其复活而去，亦无穷究其事者。李以珠置暗室中，可以代灯，甚宝之。

    Explanation

    538

    In front of the tax customs of Nanning Prefecture, Guangxi, there were two flagpoles. In a certain year during the autumn, the residents saw a fiery light flickering in the sky above the flagpoles every night, and this continued for more than a month. One day, a heavy rain poured down, and lightning struck the flagpoles from above. At the same time, a red light surged upward from the sky, and the lightning was deflected away by it, happening three or four times. Suddenly, a clap of thunder echoed, shaking the tiles of the house, and the red light suddenly ceased. The rain also soon stopped. The customs guard looked up into the sky and saw something there. Climbing the pole, he found a centipede coiled inside, about three feet long, with a red and black, mottled pattern. The centipede had already been killed by the lightning. Everyone was shocked and amazed, so they took it out and hung it on a bamboo pole to show the viewers. The cook of the customs guard said, "Li Yuemin went out of the door that night to relieve himself and saw light in the head of the centipede, about ten feet high. He knew that it had treasure." Li secretly chopped it open and found a pearl as big as a dragon's eye. Li hid the pearl and threw the centipede into the water. The next day, people did not see the centipede and suspected that it had come back to life and gone away. Nor was there anyone to investigate the matter. Li placed the pearl in a dark room, where it could serve as a lamp, and he highly valued it.

    539

    其妻偶出珠把玩，而邻妇适至，惧其见也，纳诸口中。少选吐出，而遍身发热，手足红肿，知中其毒，急研雄黄服之，浃旬始愈。李持珠至广州求售，西洋人见之曰：“此蜈蚣珠也。人或为毒蛇所噬，以此珠熨伤处，毒水涌出，即时平复。佩之可辟蛇虺，惟不可近口鼻耳。”琼州大贾闻之，买以千金。问所用，曰：“吾州五指山中多产奇蓝香，而恶蛇毒虺甚多，人不敢入。今持此珠往，则蛇虺远避，所获不赀矣。”欣然囊珠而去。李得金归，家遂小康。此与上二事。亦徐晦堂先生所记，当亦在乾隆间。宋洪景卢《夷坚丁志》载郑生夫妇等八事，云皆董坚老相授，故皆远年事，此亦同之也。

    Explanation

    539

    His wife happened to take out a pearl to play with, and just then a neighbor woman arrived. Fearing that the neighbor would see it, she put it in her mouth. Soon after, she spat it out, and her whole body became feverish, with her hands and feet red and swollen. She realized she had been poisoned and quickly ground cinnabar to take it, and after ten days, she finally recovered. Li took the pearl to Guangzhou to sell it, and a Westerner saw it and said, "This is a scorpion pearl. If someone is bitten by a venomous snake, use this pearl to press on the wound, and the poison will flow out, healing instantly. Wearing it can ward off snakes, but it should not be brought close to the mouth and nose." A wealthy merchant from Qiongzhou heard about it and bought it for a thousand gold pieces. He asked what it was used for, and the merchant replied, "In my state's Wuzhi Mountains, there is a lot of rare blue incense, but there are many venomous snakes. People dare not enter. Now, if you take this pearl with you, the snakes will stay far away, and you will gain a lot." He happily put the pearl in his bag and left. Li returned home with the money, and his family became moderately prosperous. This story, along with the two previous ones, was also recorded by Mr. Xu Haitang, and it is believed to be from the Qianlong period. The Yijian Dingzhi by Song Hong Jinglu records eight stories about Zheng Sheng and his wife, all of which were taught by Dong Jianlao, so they are all stories from long ago, and this one is the same.

    540

    《晋书。佛图澄传》：“刘曜攻洛阳，澄令一童子斋七日，取麻油合胭脂躬自研于掌中，举手示童子，灿然有辉。童子惊曰：”有军马甚众，见一人长大白皙，以朱丝缚其肘。“澄曰：”此即曜也。“钱辛楣先生谓此后世圆光之术所自始，然则圆光亦释氏咒术之一端也。近世挟是术者，多不甚验。大儿妇樊言在母家时，其第六嫂刘氏忽病狂易。仆媪辈荐一圆光者，延之至。先洁除一室，于棹上置大栲栳一具，满盛米麦，中安一镜，四旁遍插小旗帜及箭。乃于其前燃一灯，膏盛灯明，光彩耀目。令三童子正目视之，有见则以告。童子先见一大门圆如规，门中室宇深邃。

    Explanation

    540

    In the Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty), the biography of Fotu Cheng records: "When Liu Yao attacked Luoyang, Fotu Cheng ordered a young boy to fast for seven days. He then took sesame oil and mixed it with rouge, grinding it personally in his palm. He raised his hand to show the boy, and it shone brightly. The boy exclaimed in surprise: 'There are many soldiers and horses, and I see a tall, fair-skinned man with a red thread tied to his elbow.' Fotu Cheng replied, 'That is Liu Yao.' Mr. Qian Xinhui said that this is the origin of the divination technique known as yuan guang (circular light), and thus yuan guang is one aspect of the magical incantations of the Buddhist tradition. In recent times, those who practice this technique often find it not very effective. When Fan Yan, the eldest son's wife, was living with her mother-in-law, her sixth sister-in-law, Liu, suddenly fell ill with madness. The servants recommended a diviner of yuan guang, and they invited him to come. They first cleaned and prepared a room, placing a large basket on a stand, filled with rice and wheat, and placing a mirror in the center, with small flags and arrows inserted all around. They then lit a lamp in front of it, its bright light dazzling the eyes. They ordered three boys to look directly at it, and if they saw anything, they were to report it. The boys first saw a large circular gate, and inside were deep and secluded rooms."

    541

    有一白须老翁在其内，老翁所至，童子辄能见之。见其由堂入室，周历房闼，望之了然，无有遮碍。俄而有一物，四足而毛，大如羊豕，老翁执之，纳一大缸中。术者先藏一小瓶于棹下，闻童子言至此，即以纸封瓶口，曰：”已得之矣。“于是诸象悉隐。术者曰：”病者所苦，今已除去，不日即愈。如不见信，请以一事为验。“乃又于棹下藏一物，使童子视光中何所有。童子曰：”吾见有大钱二，大如车轮，一字而一幕。“发视所藏，果钱二文，一字一幕也。术者曰：”吾术不妄，即此可见矣。“樊氏厚赠之，不受，曰：”受人一钱，吾术即败矣。“已而刘病果愈。问其得病之由，曰：”吾见一猫跳入室中，即时迷惘。“然则光中所见四足而毛者，即是物矣。

    Explanation

    541

    There was an old man with a white beard inside. The child could always see him wherever he went. The child saw the old man enter the room, moving through the rooms and doors, and he could see everything clearly, without any obstruction. Soon, there appeared a creature with four legs and fur, as large as a sheep or pig. The old man grasped it and put it into a large jar. The magician had previously hidden a small bottle under the table. Upon hearing the child's words, he immediately sealed the bottle with paper and said, "I have obtained it." All the phenomena then disappeared. The magician said, "The patient's suffering has been removed, and he will recover soon. If you do not believe me, please allow me to demonstrate one more thing." He then hid another object under the table and asked the child to see what was in the light. The child said, "I saw two large coins, as big as carriage wheels, with one character and one blank side." When he opened the hidden object, there were indeed two coins, one character and one blank side. The magician said, "My skill is not false, and this demonstrates it." Fan offered him a generous gift, but he refused, saying, "If I accept even a single coin, my skill will be ruined." Soon after, Liu's illness indeed recovered. When asked about the cause of his illness, he said, "I saw a cat jump into the room, and immediately became confused." Thus, the four-legged, furry creature seen in the light was that cat.

    542

    秦肤雨言：七八岁时，其族父芝庭家失金钏一双，不知窃者为谁。时有扬州人王姓者，善圆光。乃延之至于室中，设香案，翦素纸，广长各二尺许，蒙覆一器上，为四方之形。其人向之诵咒，复以清水巽之，又时时取炉中所焚之香，挹其烟气，令著纸上。如是四五次，遂呼童男女数辈，使谛视之，肤雨亦与焉。纸之左畔见诸神象，或袍带而秉笏，或甲胄而执戈，无虑百余人，须眉毕见。其在后者，仅露其首而已。纸之右畔有屋字之象，即其族父家也。族父坐卧室中，于灯下作书，幼孙女旁立而观；有一妇人在内，窃钏藏怀中：一一若绘。此妇旧尝佣于其家，恒往来者也。穷究之，果其所窃。回思曩夜情事，无一不合，其技亦神矣。

    Explanation

    542

    Qin Fuyu said: When he was seven or eight years old, his uncle Zhi Ting lost a pair of gold bracelets, and no one knew who had stolen them. At that time, there was a man surnamed Wang from Yangzhou who was skilled in Yuan Guang. They invited him to their house, set up an altar with incense, cut white paper, and made it into a square shape, about two feet wide and long, covering a vessel with it. The man faced the paper, recited incantations, washed it with clear water, and then repeatedly took the smoke from the burning incense in the furnace, letting it settle on the paper. After doing this four or five times, they called several children and grandchildren to look at it carefully, and Qin Fuyu also participated. On the left side of the paper, they saw images of various deities, some wearing robes and holding tablets, others wearing armor and holding spears, without doubt more than a hundred people, all their beards and eyebrows clearly visible. Those in the back only showed their heads. On the right side of the paper, there was an image of a house, which was his uncle's home. His uncle sat in the bedroom, writing under the lamp, with his young granddaughter standing beside and watching; there was a woman inside, who had stolen the bracelets and hidden them in her bosom: each detail was as if painted. This woman used to work for their family and was a frequent visitor. They investigated thoroughly and found that it was indeed hers. When they recalled the events of the previous night, nothing did not match, and his skill was also miraculous.

    543

    生而有文在手，自古有之，今不多见。唐栖姚氏生一子，有文在手，曰：“升”。此子聪慧异常儿，读书过目成诵。即他技艺，略一研寻，无不精妙。咸以英物目之。乃年甫弱冠，以诸生终，苗而不秀，乡里惜焉。然其人亦必有宿根者与！

    　　唐西姚氏一妇，贤而且才，实管家政，春秋鱼菽之祭，皆此妇尸之。每祭前一日，必梦一妇人向之佥衽，曰：“我马氏也，凡遇祭祀，虽与诸尊属俱来，止能侍立于旁，而不得食。幸娘子哀怜，为设杯箸于末坐，且祝曰‘马氏坐此’，则我得与矣。”寤而不知马氏为何人，遍访之姚氏诸长老，始知姚氏之先有贱妾马氏，无所出，家祭不及焉。乃如其言，设杯箸，且祝曰：“马姨娘坐此。”至今循之。传称鬼犹求食，岂不信夫！

    Explanation

    543

    A Child Born with Writing on His Hand

    From ancient times, there have been children born with writing on their hands, but such occurrences are now rare. In Tangxi, the Yao family gave birth to a son who had writing on his hand, which read "升" (shēng, meaning "ascend" or "rise"). This child was extraordinarily intelligent, able to read books and recite them upon a glance. He excelled in various skills, mastering them with just a brief study. Everyone regarded him as an extraordinary person. However, he died at the age of twenty, ending his life as a student, like a plant that grows but does not bear fruit, causing the village to mourn him. Yet, he must have had deep roots, as his abilities were not merely a product of external factors.

    In the Yao family of Tangxi, there was a woman who was both wise and talented, managing household affairs. All the spring and autumn sacrifices of fish and beans were performed by this woman. Every day before the sacrifice, she would dream of a woman who approached her with folded sleeves, saying, "I am Ma. Whenever there is a sacrifice, though I come with the other respected family members, I can only stand by and do not get to eat. I hope you will have compassion and set up cups and chopsticks for me at the last seat, and say, 'Ma sits here,' then I will be able to join in." Upon waking, she did not know who Ma was. She consulted the elders of the Yao family, and they informed her that the Yao family once had a concubine named Ma who had no children and was not included in the family sacrifices. Following her instructions, she set up cups and chopsticks and said, "Ma Auntie sits here." This practice continues to this day. It is said that even ghosts seek food, how can this not be true?

    544

    河南中牟县有老孝廉郭兰可者，其始生时，父适假寐，梦有人报曰：“某科二十一名举人郭兰可。”及寤，而家人以生子告，乃即以兰可名之，后果于是科中式，名次悉符。然则科名洵由前定矣。又孟县诸生有刘藜照者，于某年乡试之前梦观榜，有其姓名，大喜，以为必中矣。是科榜出，果有刘藜照，然非孟县人也。此则鬼神以其姓名相同，聊以为戏耳。

    　　汴梁城南朱仙镇，有岳忠武庙。庙前有铁铸秦桧夫妇跪像，与吾浙西湖岳坟同。凡负贩之人至其地者，必批秦桧之颊，而摩王氏之乳，谓不如是，则是日[A13X]鬻市廛不能获利。岁月既久，颊与乳光泽可鉴。有秦氏家于庙侧，耻之，乘夜负铁像投于河中。至次日，而二像仍在水面，浮而不沉。众人环观，无不骇异，仍舁置庙前焉。

    Explanation

    544

    In Henan Province, there is an elderly scholar named Guo Lankai from Zhongmou County. When he was born, his father was just dozing off and dreamed that someone told him, "In this year's examination, the 21st place will be taken by Guo Lankai." Upon waking, the family informed him that he had been born, and they named him Lankai. As it turned out, he passed the examination in that year, and his rank matched perfectly with the dream. Thus, it was indeed predestined that he would succeed in the examination.

    In Meng County, there was a scholar named Liu Lizhao. Before the provincial examination in a certain year, he dreamed of seeing the list of successful candidates and found his name on it. He was very happy, thinking he would surely pass. When the list was released, indeed there was a Liu Lizhao, but he was not from Meng County. The spirits took advantage of the same name, just for fun.

    In the south of Bianliang City, there is a town called Zhu Xianzhen, which has a temple dedicated to Yue Fei, the loyal and brave general. In front of the temple, there are iron statues of Qin Hui and his wife kneeling, similar to those at the tomb of Yue Fei in West Lake, Zhejiang. Whenever merchants or peddlers pass by this place, they must slap the cheek of Qin Hui and rub the breasts of Wang氏, believing that if they do not do so, they will not make a profit that day. Over time, the cheeks and breasts of the statues became shiny enough to be reflected in. The Qin family, who lived near the temple, felt ashamed of this and, at night, carried the iron statues and threw them into the river. The next day, the two statues were still floating on the water, not sinking. The crowd gathered around to watch, and no one was not surprised. They still carried them back and placed them in front of the temple.

    545

    赵州城北有石狮一，满头有油污之痕，雨淋日炙，而不能去。相传其初曾幻为一少年，人民舍戏其妇，妇适夜绩，即取灯油浇其头。狮反身而遁走，至中道而鸡已鸣，遂不能还故处，至今蹲踞大道之旁焉。以上二事，并宋伯言说。奉化潘某，幼贫{宀娄}，长为县吏，遂以起家。蓄一婢，遇之虐，婢自经死，遂藁葬于野。岁余，潘经其地，见有烟气自其冢出。就视之，闻婢语曰：“且勿行，我与俱归。”大怖，奔还其家，而鬼亦随至，空中闻诟詈声。俄火发于笮，救之甫灭，而他处又焚，奔救不遑。有亲串来省视，曰：“何物鬼魅，白昼为祟！”言未已，一巨石自空坠，几中其颅，乃逃去。如是者四五年，潘氏之人死亡相踵，存者寥寥，赀产亦尽。

    Explanation

    545

    North of Zhaozhou City, there is a stone lion with stains of oil all over its head, which have not been removed by the rain and sun. It is said that the lion once transformed into a young man, and the people left their wives to play with him. One night, a woman was spinning, and she poured lamp oil on his head. The lion turned around and fled, but at the halfway point, the rooster had already crowed, and it could not return to its original place. It has been squatting by the side of the main road ever since. These two stories are both told by Song Boyan. In Fenghua, there was a man named Pan who was poor as a child but grew up to be a county official, thus rising to prominence. He kept a maid, whom he treated cruelly, and she committed suicide by hanging. He buried her in a makeshift grave in the wilderness. More than a year later, Pan passed by the place and saw smoke rising from her tomb. He went to look and heard the maid's voice say, "Do not go, I will go with you." Greatly terrified, he ran back to his home, and the ghost followed him, with curses heard in the air. Soon, fire broke out in the bamboo, and after it was just put out, another place caught fire. He ran around to save, with no time to spare. A relative came to visit and said, "What kind of ghost is this, causing trouble during the day?" Before he finished speaking, a large stone fell from the sky, nearly hitting his head, and he fled. Like this, for four or five years, people of the Pan family died one after another, and those who survived were few, and their wealth was also exhausted.

    546

    萧山某甲，家贫无以为生，乃交结吏胥，把持词讼，赀产日以饶裕，而作恶弥甚。中年后淫虐，几无人理。忽一日暴卒，未几复苏，自言：“冥司谓罪已盈贯，必置于法；但恐人间不信为恶之报，故暂令回生一年，使人观我受种种﹃辱，以警世人。见有鬼役数十挟持，我不能自主也。”言讫，即趋出跪于通衢，大呼曰：“我作恶人也，诸君看我苦否？我曾作某语，今当批颊。”乃自侧面以受刑，辅颐红肿，口中流血。又曰：“我曾为某事，今当笞臀。”即褫衣而受笞，附近之人止闻其呼吁，而一二里外，则敲扑之声清脆可听。

    Explanation

    546

    A man from Xiaoshan, poor and unable to make a living, began to associate with officials and control lawsuits, gradually accumulating wealth while his evil deeds grew increasingly severe. After middle age, he became lustful and cruel, almost devoid of humanity. One day, he suddenly died, but soon revived. He then said, "The underworld says that my crimes have reached their limit, and I must be punished; but they fear that people will not believe in retribution for evil, so they have temporarily allowed me to return to life for one year to let others see me suffer various punishments, to warn the world. I see dozens of ghost servants holding me, and I cannot control myself." After speaking, he quickly went out and knelt in the main street, shouting, "I am an evil person, can you see how I suffer? I once said something, and now I must be struck on the cheek." He then hit himself on the side of the face to receive the punishment, causing his cheek to swell and bleed from his mouth. He then said, "I once did something, and now I must be flogged on the buttocks." He stripped off his clothes and received the flogging, with those nearby only hearing his cries, while those a mile or two away could clearly hear the sound of the beating.

    547

    受一刑毕，辄曰：“苦苦，诸君见否？”如是者每日皆然，周历城厢，遍身血肉糜烂。满一年，竟毙于路。纸牌之戏，本于唐宋人叶子格，而叶子又本于骰子，说见欧阳公《归田录》。今纸牌中有红点、黑点，殆即叶子格中红鹤、皂鹤之遗乎？近世纸牌盛行，闺阁亦有行之者。余亲串中一妇，病久谵语，辄曰：“二五来矣，幺六来矣，缘我床，压我被，尔曹何不为我捉去？”所云二五、幺六，皆纸牌名目也。上二下五曰二五，上一下六曰幺六。此妇生平从不为纸牌之戏，侍疾者异之。初犹不以为意，而妇数数言之，乃大索其室中，无所得。已而于楼上一破簏中，得纸牌数十叶，多年不用，尘灰满矣。疑此物为祟，取而焚之，妇果不复言。盖纸牌常在人手中，沾人手泽，故岁久或通灵也。

    Explanation

    547

    After receiving one punishment, he would immediately say, "Suffering, have you seen this?" This was his daily routine, as he roamed around the city, his entire body covered in bloody and rotten flesh. After a full year, he finally died on the road. The game of paper cards originated from the "Yèzǐ" game of the Tang and Song dynasties, and the "Yèzǐ" game itself originated from dice, as explained in Ouyang Xiu's Guitian Lu. Today, paper cards have red and black spots, which are probably the legacy of the red crane and black crane from the "Yèzǐ" game? In recent times, paper card games have become popular, even among women. Among my acquaintances, there was a woman who, after a long illness, began to speak incoherently, saying, "Two and five are coming, one and six are coming, climbing on my bed, pressing my blanket, why don't you catch them for me?" The terms "two and five" and "one and six" are names for specific card combinations. When the top card is two and the bottom is five, it's called "two and five," and when the top is one and the bottom is six, it's called "one and six." This woman had never played paper card games in her life, and those who cared for her found this strange. At first, they didn't pay much attention, but as she repeated these words, they searched her room thoroughly and found nothing. Later, they discovered several dozen paper cards in a broken basket on the second floor, unused for many years, covered in dust. Suspecting that the cards were causing her delusions, they burned them, and the woman indeed stopped speaking. It seems that paper cards, often held in people's hands and imbued with their personal touch, may become spiritually aware after a long time.

    548

    义乌城北隅，有楼氏废园，名槐圃。地甚幽僻，颇擅泉石花木之胜，而素传有鬼，无敢居者。咸丰庚午夏，傅君锡蕃欲假其地避暑，主人以有鬼告。傅曰：“固知之，非所畏也。”主人乃扫除一室，置榻其中。傅独坐至二更，月色皎然，了无所见，将就枕矣。忽一叟从屋隅出，傅揖之曰：“君其鬼乎？”曰：“然。”“来此何为？”叟曰：“此前朝墓地，葬此者非止仆一人。但鬼之于人，亦以气类相聚，以君文士，故敢一见。”乃坐而与言，其言明季事甚悉。临去，指园中花木曰：“弹指间又成荆棘也。”时粤寇之乱已扰及浙，而义乌犹未被兵，傅不以为信。未几，寇果大至，槐圃为墟。

    Explanation

    548

    In the northern corner of Yiwu City, there is a ruined garden belonging to the Lou family, known as Huayu Garden. The area is very secluded and renowned for its springs, rocks, flowers, and trees, but it has long been rumored to be haunted, and no one dared to live there. In the summer of Xianfeng Gengwu (1850), Fu Jun Xifan wished to rent the place to escape the heat. The owner warned him of the ghosts. Fu replied, "I am aware of it, but it does not frighten me." The owner then cleaned a room and placed a bed inside. Fu sat alone until midnight, when the moonlight was bright and clear, and he saw nothing unusual, about to go to bed. Suddenly, an old man emerged from the corner of the house. Fu greeted him and asked, "Are you a ghost?" The old man replied, "Yes." "What brings you here?" Fu inquired. The old man said, "This was a burial ground from a previous dynasty, and many people were buried here, not just me. But ghosts, like people, gather according to their nature. Since you are a scholar, I dare to appear." They sat down and began to converse, and the old man spoke in great detail about events from the late Ming dynasty. As he was leaving, he pointed to the flowers and trees in the garden and said, "In the blink of an eye, it will become overgrown with thorns again." At that time, the rebellion of the Cantonese bandits had already reached Zhejiang, but Yiwu had not yet been attacked. Fu did not believe it. Soon after, the bandits indeed arrived in large numbers, and Huayu Garden became a ruin.

    549

    义乌人陈某，邑诸生也，尝送所亲冯氏之葬。既至其地，冯氏甫，掘至五尺余，忽见古砖，又二尺余，而旧椁见。工匠辈佥谓宜别治一穴，地师执不可，陈亦从而附和之。冯氏之人曰：“如旧椁何？”陈曰：“此中骸骨，一无所存，且无碑志可考。是远代不知谁何之墓，复何顾惜，扌日而去之可也。”冯氏从其言。陈既送葬而归，遂发病，谓其子曰：“吾坐冯氏事死矣，地师亦不得活。”三日之中，陈与地师相继死，惟冯氏无恙，然自葬后，其家亦日落矣。

    Explanation

    549

    A Man from Yiwu Named Chen

    Chen, a student of the county academy from Yiwu, once escorted the burial of his close friend Feng. After arriving at the burial site, Feng's coffin was just placed in the grave. They dug down more than five feet and suddenly saw ancient bricks. Another two feet or so, and the old coffin was revealed. The craftsmen all agreed that it would be better to dig a new grave, but the geomancer insisted that it was not advisable. Chen also followed and agreed with them. Someone from Feng's family asked, "What about the old coffin?" Chen replied, "There is no trace of bones inside, and there are no tombstones or inscriptions to verify. It is a tomb from a distant era, belonging to someone unknown. What is there to be concerned about? It can be removed." Feng agreed to their suggestion. Chen returned after sending off the burial and then fell ill. He told his son, "I will die because of Feng's matter, and the geomancer will not survive either." Within three days, Chen and the geomancer died in succession, only Feng remained unharmed. However, after the burial, Feng's family also began to decline.

    550

    河南孟县宋君梦兰，字香谷，同治壬戌进士。以知县分发甘肃，奉檄至凉州，适回民为乱，城陷死焉，家中尚未得信。其族子宽夫，素与同学，甚相得，忽梦其行袍行裳如军营装束（行袍，即缺襟袍；行裳，俗名战裙，并见《会典》），携一卷书而读。读已泣曰：“我已为泉下人矣。”宽夫瞿然而寤，明日果得凶问。又浙江义乌人陈君，谈者失言其名，曾为分水训导，后死于家。其子时在省城，偶昼寝，梦其父自外至，颜色惨淡，子迎问之，其父曰：“汝尚不返乎？唤汝者行且至矣。”子欲问其详，适报家中有急足至，急起问故，乃知其父死矣。盖死者之灵与赴者俱至，与九卷中所记朱观察、长大令事正相类。

    Explanation

    550

    In Henan, Meng County, there was a man named Song Jumenglán, courtesy name Fragrant Valley, who passed the imperial examination in the year Renxu of the Tongzhi era. He was appointed as a magistrate and dispatched to serve in Gansu, where he received an official order to go to Liangzhou. At that time, the Hui people were in rebellion, and the city fell, leading to his death. His family had not yet received news of his fate. His cousin, Kuanfu, had always been a fellow student and was very close to him. One day, Kuanfu suddenly dreamed that Song Jumenglán was wearing military-style clothing, carrying a book and reading it. After reading, he wept and said, "I am already a dead person." Kuanfu woke up suddenly, startled, and the next day, the bad news was confirmed.

    Another man, Chen Jun, from Yiwu, Zhejiang, whose name was forgotten by the speaker, once served as a teacher in Fenshui. He later died at home. His son was in the provincial capital and happened to take a daytime nap. He dreamed that his father came from outside and looked very sad. The son greeted him and asked, "Are you still not returning? The one who is calling you is about to arrive." The son wanted to ask more details, but just then, a messenger arrived with urgent news from home. He quickly woke up and asked the reason, and then he knew that his father had died. It seems that the spirit of the deceased and the messenger arrived together, which is very similar to the stories of Zhu Guancha and Changda Ling recorded in the ninth volume.

    551

    咸丰庚申，浙江省城失守，时何子敬观察绍祺以事至苏，其眷属留杭州。恭人陈氏闻变，语其家人曰：“主巳外出，吾曹无可求生。何氏湘南名族，长幼男女义不为贼所辱。”乃缚其幼子庆钅生、庆熙，沉之于池。自与姻家一妇朱氏者，引绳同缢。会江南援兵至，不五日而复省城，家人亟往视之，则二子以水浅未死。恭人与朱并悬于梁，朱气绝，而恭人竟以救得苏。自云：“缢后一无所苦，第见有两红灯在前，若相导者。惘惘从之行，不知所行为何地，俄而复见天日，则已醒矣。”后归原籍，以寿终。夫缢至数日，仍得不死，此事诚属罕闻。或曰恭人从容就义，节烈可嘉，宜为神鬼护持。然乱离之世，舍生取义视死如归者，夫岂乏人，何鬼神之独厚于恭人邪？闻其平日礼佛诵经，寒暑无间，大难不死，或由于此。《太平广记》中报应一门，以崇经像得福报者凡五卷，不得概以为诬妄也。杭州郑谱香都转，初应礼闱试，曾主同乡魏部郎家。

    Explanation

    551

    In the year of Gengshen during the Xianfeng era, the provincial capital of Zhejiang fell. At that time, He Zijing, an observer, came to Suzhou for official business, leaving his family members in Hangzhou. Lady Chen, upon hearing of the news, told her family, "The master has already gone out, we have no way to survive. The He family, a famous clan from southern Hunan, all ages and genders will not be dishonored by the enemy." She then tied up her young sons Qingsheng and Qingxi, drowning them in the pond. She and her friend Zhu, a woman from her in-laws' family, hanged themselves together with a rope. Just as the reinforcements from southern Jiangnan arrived, within five days, the provincial capital was recaptured. The family hurriedly went to see them, and the two sons did not die because the water was shallow. Lady Chen and Zhu were hanging together from the beam; Zhu died, but Lady Chen was saved by rescue. She said herself, "After hanging, I suffered no pain. I only saw two red lights in front of me, as if guiding me. I followed them aimlessly, not knowing where I was going. Soon, I saw the sky and sunlight again, and I had woken up." She returned to her hometown and died of old age. The lady hung herself for several days and still did not die; this is indeed a rare occurrence. Some say that Lady Chen calmly accepted her fate, her virtue and bravery are commendable, and it is fitting that she be protected by gods and spirits. However, in a time of chaos and turmoil, those who sacrifice their lives for righteousness and regard death as returning home, are there not many such people? Why did the gods and spirits favor Lady Chen alone? It is said that she regularly worshiped Buddha and recited sutras without interruption, and survived a great disaster, perhaps due to this. In the Taiping Guangji, the section on retribution, there are five volumes that describe how reverence for scriptures and images leads to good fortune; one should not dismiss them all as false. Zheng Pu Xiang, a person from Hangzhou who held a high-ranking position, initially passed the imperial examination for the position of scholar-official and once served as a guest in the home of a fellow townsman, Wei Bu Lang.

    552

    其家故有狐，谱香之未至也，狐适逐去旧塾师，而使以谱香代之。部郎曰：“其人尚未至。”狐言：“某日必来。”已而谱香果以是日至，乃从狐议定焉。入书舍，则四壁皆糊以新纸，而蝇头小字满其上，杂录诗文，殊无条理。馆人曰：“昨暮犹未见有此也。”谱香知狐所为，微哂而出，于他舍小坐。忽一淡巴菰筒破窗而入，其声甚厉，此筒乃谱香遗在书舍中者。取视，则上系一纸书，大旨谓“何以轻我”，乃亦书数语答之。

    Explanation

    552

    The household had previously been inhabited by a fox. Before 谱香 arrived, the fox had driven away the old tutor and arranged for 谱香 to take his place. The official said, "The person has not yet arrived." The fox replied, "He will definitely come on a certain day." Soon after, 谱香 indeed came on that day, and thus the arrangement was decided upon. Upon entering the study, all four walls were covered with new paper, and tiny, fine letters covered the entire surface. The writings were a mix of poetry and prose, but they were completely lacking in order. The caretaker said, "I did not see this yesterday evening." 谱香 knew it was the fox's doing and left with a slight smile, sitting in another room. Suddenly, a pipe of tobacco flew through the window, the sound being very loud. This pipe was one that 谱香 had left in the study. Upon picking it up and looking, he saw a note attached to it. The main point of the note was, "Why did you belittle me?" He then wrote a few words in response.

    553

    甫搁笔，而覆书已裹小石投入，捷速如此，宜其一夕之中书满四壁也。读之，语意仍不训。知不可以理谕，遂不复答。未久，辞魏而他就焉。谱香言此狐亦有不可及者，部郎一幼子初学为时文，甫作小讲，其文甚劣，几不可读。狐为易七八字，居然成文，真谚所谓点铁成金者也。又岁杪，部郎有故人告贷数十金，坚却之曰：“亡矣！”忽砰然一囊坠其前，白金满焉，大书其上曰：“此非蒙古儿乎？”部朗大惭。后未数年，部郎卒，家属相踵而亡，狐盖乘其衰气而侮之。余闻之杜筱舫云。

    Explanation

    553

    Upon finishing his writing, the reply was already wrapped in a small stone and thrown in, so swift was the action, and it was fitting that in one night the walls were covered with writing. When he read it, the meaning of the words was still not clear or logical. Knowing that reason could not be used to persuade, he no longer replied. Not long after, he left Wei and went to him. Pu Xiang said that this fox also had something unattainable: the official's young son was just learning to write current-style essays, and he had just written a short essay, but his writing was very poor, almost unreadable. The fox changed a few words, and unexpectedly, it became a coherent piece of writing, truly a saying that turning iron into gold. Again, at the end of the year, the official had an old friend who asked for a loan of several dozen gold pieces. He firmly refused and said, "It is gone!" Suddenly, a bag fell in front of him with a loud sound, and it was full of silver. He wrote in large letters on it and said, "Is this not a Mongol child?" The official was deeply ashamed. Not many years later, the official died, and his family members died one after another. The fox probably took advantage of his declining vitality and mocked him. I heard this from Du Xiaofang.

    554

    苑平许姓者，偶烹一鱼，已熟而食之矣，置其头于器中。至夜有光一点，荧荧如明星，持近灯，灯为之灭，家人异焉。剖而视之，得珠一颗。鱼珠不足怪，熟而犹能吐光，且有灭灯之异，则可怪矣。长洲朱我斋实亲见之。

    　　●卷十一吴县太傅潘文恭公之仲子星斋侍郎曾莹与陆夫人，于光绪四年三月三日同日而卒。夫人殁于丑时，侍郎殁于巳时，海内异之。而文恭公之冢孙东园部郎仪凤于光绪二年二月十九日卒，其配胡淑人于光绪六年六月十九日卒，年皆五十有一。世传二月十九为观世音菩萨生日，六月十九则其成佛之日也，意其夫妇二人亦皆有宿根者与？

    Explanation

    554

    In the town of Yuanping, a man surnamed Xu happened to cook a fish. It was already cooked and he ate it, leaving the head in a vessel. That night, a point of light appeared, shining brightly like a star. When he held it near a lamp, the lamp went out. His family was astonished. When they cut open the head and examined it, they found a pearl. A pearl from a fish is not surprising, but that it could still emit light even after being cooked, and that it had the strange effect of extinguishing lamps, was indeed remarkable. Mr. Zhu Wozhai of Changzhou actually witnessed this.

    In the eleventh volume, the second son of Grand Tutor Pan Wengong from Wu County, Grand Secretary Xingzai, and his wife Lady Lu, both died on the same day, the third day of the third month in the fourth year of Guangxu. Lady Lu died at the hour of Ox, and Grand Secretary Xingzai died at the hour of Snake. It was remarkable throughout the country. The eldest grandson of Grand Tutor Pan, Minister Yi Feng, died on the nineteenth day of the second month in the second year of Guangxu. His wife, Lady Hu, died on the nineteenth day of the sixth month in the sixth year of Guangxu, both being fifty-one years old. It is said that the nineteenth day of the second month is the birthday of Guanyin Bodhisattva, and the nineteenth day of the sixth month is the day he attained Buddhahood. It is thought that these two people also have deep roots in Buddhism.

    555

    元和顾子山观察，于光绪庚辰岁行年七十。偶获一龟，长身细腰，下丰上杀，形如胡庐。宾客传观，佥以为寿征也。观察名之曰大吉龟，赋诗以张之，并属善画者摹其正反侧三形，索同人题咏。余亦为赋五言古诗一章。按《太平〔广记〕（御览）》水族部载：晋义熙中，有黟县吏入山采薪，得二龟，有两树骈生，以龟侧置树间。后经十二年复入山，见一龟甲已枯，一者尚生，极长。树木所处可厚四寸许，两头厚尺余，如马鞍状。又《夷坚志》“〔吕〕（吴）”德卿绿毛龟“一条载：秀州市民社会有一龟，若药瓢然。询其人，云自始得时，以铁束其腰背，故如此。此龟殆必其类乎，然亦罕睹矣！

    Explanation

    555

    During the Yuanhe period, Gu Zishan, the governor, was seventy years old in the Guangxu reign year of Gengchen. He accidentally obtained a turtle with a long body and slender waist, fuller at the bottom and narrower at the top, shaped like a Hu庐. Guests passed it around to view, all considering it a sign of longevity. The governor named it the Great Fortune Turtle, composed poems to praise it, and commissioned skilled painters to draw its front, back, and side views, asking others to write poems about it. I also composed a chapter of five-character ancient poetry. According to the "Taiping Guangji" and "Yulu" in the Water Creatures section: During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (Jin Wudi), an official from Yi County went into the mountains to collect firewood and found two turtles. There were two trees growing side by side, and he placed the turtles on the side of the trees. After twelve years, he went back into the mountains and saw that one turtle's shell was dry, while the other was still alive and very long. The trees were about four inches thick, with the two ends more than a foot thick, shaped like a horse saddle. The "Yiji Zhi" records: Lü (Wu) Deqing had a green-haired turtle. In Xiu City, there was a turtle shaped like a medicine ladle. When asked about it, the person said that since they first got it, they had bound its waist and back with iron, hence its appearance. This turtle must be of the same kind, but it is rarely seen.

    556

    镇江有一小家，止一母一女一子。子学贾于市肆，适有苏州吴县人某甲亦在彼学贾，与其子相识，遂得识其母。时往来其家，并其女亦朝夕见焉。其女许嫁一书生，贫甚不能娶，而其年长矣。某甲与其母谋言于婿氏，请绝其婚，婿氏竟许之，某甲因自媒焉。既有成言矣，而某甲之母执不可，乃不果。未几，女归于他族，而所适者亦甚贫，且夫妇不相得，女甚以为恨。越数岁，而前所绝婚之书生举于乡，明年成进士，女闻之大悔，雉经而死。

    Explanation

    556

    There was a small family in Zhenjiang, consisting of only one mother, one daughter, and one son. The son was studying trade skills in a market shop, and coincidentally, a man from Wuxian, Suzhou, who was also studying trade skills, became acquainted with him. Consequently, he came to know his mother. He frequently visited their home, and his daughter was seen daily as well. The daughter was engaged to a scholar, but he was very poor and could not marry, and she was already old. A spoke to the fiancé's family and requested to break the engagement, which the fiancé's family ultimately agreed to. So A then proposed himself, and there was already an agreement, but A's mother insisted it was not acceptable, so it did not come to pass. Not long after, the daughter married another family, and her husband was also very poor, and the couple did not get along, and the daughter deeply regretted it. After several years, the scholar who was previously engaged passed the local examination, and the following year he became a jinshi. Upon hearing this, the daughter greatly regretted her decision and hanged herself with a rooster.

    557

    时某甲已归姑苏，忽大病，自言：“女死后讼己于镇江府。城隍以其为吴县人也，由镇江府城隍咨苏州府城隍，复由苏州府城隍札吴县城隍，遣吏卒逮问，今不得免矣。”数日竟死。其死在光绪庚辰岁，度其事之缘起，亦必在同治、光绪间。谈者失其姓名，并此女前所许嫁之书生，亦不知何人，然其事则皆云实有之也。夫破人婚姻已不可，况毁人以自成乎？甲死宜矣。至地下主者，各有疆域，簿书期会，一如人间。圣人以神道设教而天下服，其知此也夫。

    Explanation

    557

    At that time, Mr. A had returned to Suzhou, but suddenly fell gravely ill. He said, "After the woman's death, she sued me at the Zhenjiang Prefecture. The city god, considering me to be a native of Wu County, consulted the city god of Suzhou Prefecture, who then issued an order to the city god of Wu County. They sent officials and soldiers to arrest and interrogate me, and now I cannot escape it." A few days later, he died. His death occurred in the year Gengchen of the Guangxu era. It is estimated that the origin of the matter must have been during the Tongzhi or Guangxu eras. Those who talked about it did not know his name, nor did they know the name of the scholar the woman had previously promised to marry. However, everyone said that the story was true. Destroying someone's marriage is already wrong, let alone destroying someone to benefit oneself. Mr. A's death is appropriate. As for the rulers of the underworld, each has their own jurisdiction. Their records and meetings are just like those of the living world. Sages use the ways of the gods to teach and govern the world, and people submit to this knowledge.

    558

    闽中陈生，谈者讳其名。自幼佻达，喜作狎邪游，而其父督之严，始稍稍向学，然其心固常在舞衫歌扇间也。一岁上巳之日，父适他出，乃托他事绐其师，私至郊外踏青，狎鸟撩花，忘路远近。忽闻箫鼓之声，问之途人，曰：“前村演剧也”即往观之。笙歌如沸，士女如云，游目骋怀，乐而忘返。未几夕阳在山矣，顿悟身在郊外，城门将闭，惧不能归，乃遵旧路而回。行未及半，忽见路旁隘巷中有绿衣女子，从一小婢缓步而行，小腰秀颈，楚楚可人。生念野外焉得有此佳丽，然爱其美，亦不疑也，施行从之，俄至一甲第而入。

    Explanation

    558

    A Young Man from Fujian

    A young man from Fujian, whose name people hesitate to mention, was known for his carefree and licentious nature from a young age, often indulging in the company of courtesans. Despite his father's strict supervision, he began to study more seriously, though his heart still lingered in the world of dance and song. On one Shangsi Festival, when his father was away, he lied to his teacher by claiming another errand and secretly went to the outskirts for a spring outing. He wandered among the flowers and birds, losing track of time and distance. Suddenly, he heard the sound of flutes and drums, and asked a passerby, who told him, "There is a performance in the village ahead." He went to watch, where the music and singing were as lively as boiling water, and the crowd of men and women was as dense as clouds. He roamed the eyes and expanded the heart, enjoying himself so much that he forgot to return. Soon, the sun set behind the mountains, and he suddenly realized he was in the outskirts, with the city gates about to close. Fearing he couldn't return, he followed the original path back. Not having traveled halfway, he suddenly saw a green-clad woman walking slowly with a maid in a narrow alley. She had a slender waist and graceful neck, and was charming and attractive. He wondered how such beauty could be found in the countryside, but he was attracted to her beauty and did not suspect anything. He followed her, and soon arrived at a grand mansion and entered.

    559

    生徘徊门外，暮色苍然，私计不及入城，不如且谋寄宿，或有佳遇。乃叩门，以求宿告。婢出，诺之，导以入，则珠帘绣幕中绛蜡高烧，绿衣女已待于堂矣。生问姓氏，笑而不答。命置酒待客，雕盘绮食，颇极丰腆。又命婢歌以侑酒。酒阑烛炮，相携入室，红罗复帐，鸳被蛩毡，春梦香甜，不知东方之既白。忽毕爆有声，火光焱焱，照耀窗棂。疑为失火，推枕而起，则天已大明，而玉骨冰肌，俨然在抱，审视之，乃枯骸一具也。惊起四顾，则屋宇帷幕了无所睹，独立墟墓间。墓旁故有烧瓦之窑，窑破，故火光进射也。生知遇鬼，奔还其家。俄而大病，因与家人言所遇，咸以为虑，而卧病月余竟愈。于是尽涤邪念，下帷苦读，卒成进士，入词林焉。

    Explanation

    559

    The man lingered outside the gate, the evening sky dark and vast, secretly thinking he couldn't enter the city, so he decided to seek temporary lodging, perhaps encountering something auspicious. He knocked on the door, asking for a place to stay. A maid came out, agreed, and led him inside. There, in the midst of pearl curtains and embroidered screens, red candles burned brightly, and a green-clad maiden awaited him in the hall. He asked her name, but she smiled without answering. She ordered wine and food for the guest, with elaborately decorated plates and delicately prepared dishes, very abundant and rich. She also had a maid sing to entertain the wine. When the wine had run out and the candles were still burning brightly, they entered the room together, where a red silk canopy and a quilt and mattress adorned with mandarin ducks awaited. The night was filled with sweet and pleasant dreams, and he did not know that the east had already turned white. Suddenly, a loud noise erupted, and flames blazed brightly, illuminating the window frames. Suspecting a fire, he pushed aside the pillow and got up, only to find that the sky was already bright, and a woman with a delicate, cold beauty was still in his embrace. Upon closer examination, it turned out to be a withered corpse. Shocked, he looked around, but the house and its furnishings were nowhere to be seen, and he found himself standing alone in a desolate cemetery. Beside the grave, there was a kiln for firing tiles, which had broken, causing the flames to shoot out. Realizing he had encountered a ghost, he rushed back to his home. Soon after, he fell seriously ill and told his family about what he had experienced, causing them all to be concerned. After lying ill for over a month, he eventually recovered. He then completely rid himself of evil thoughts, studied diligently behind closed doors, and eventually became a Jinshi, entering the Hanlin Academy.

    560

    张仙槎习申、韩家言，游于闽中。咸丰初，就闽县令张芹香之馆。会芹香受代，而仙槎犹居署中。一夕至三更，甫就枕，忽见有二人来至床前，其人戴雨缨凉帽，如公门中装束。问来此何事，以劝捐对。仙槎曰：“吾侪橐笔依人，每岁所得亦甚微薄，其能捐乎？”二人曰：“固知汝不我信也。明日当持凭据来。”遂不见，甚异之。次日至丙夜，果见二人又与一人俱来，持簿书示之，曰：“此非凭据乎？”仙槎视之，则皆己平日所拟讼牒中批语也，其旁或注“可”字，或注“不可”字。问其故，答曰：“可者应如此办，不可者即属错误，应入犁舌地狱。

    Explanation

    560

    Zhang Xiancha, who had studied the doctrines of Shen Buhai and Han Fei, wandered in Fujian. In the early years of the Xianfeng reign, he stayed in the residence of Zhang Qinxiang, the magistrate of Min County. Just as Zhang Qinxiang was replaced, Zhang Xiancha still resided in the official residence. One night, around the third watch, just as he was about to go to bed, he suddenly saw two people come to his bedside. They were wearing rain hats with cool brims, dressed as officials. When asked what they were doing there, they replied that they were there to encourage donations. Zhang Xiancha said, "We who carry pens and rely on others earn very little each year, how could we donate?" The two people replied, "We knew you wouldn't believe us. We will bring credentials tomorrow." They then disappeared, and Zhang Xiancha found it very strange. The next day, at the third watch, indeed, two people came again, accompanied by another person, and they brought a book of credentials, saying, "Are these not credentials?" Zhang Xiancha looked at it and saw that they were all the comments he had made in the lawsuits he drafted daily, with some marked with the word "可" (possible) and others with the word "不可" (impossible). He asked why, and they replied, "Those that are possible should be handled this way, and those that are not possible are mistakes, and should lead to entering the hell of plowing the tongue."

    561
