    ”仙槎曰：“吾辈所拟批语，不过酌理衡情，初无成见；即有错误，亦属公过，何有如此重罪！”其人曰：“我亦不知，但汝能捐输，或可减罪。”仙槎许捐钱一千文，其人少之。增至七千，乃首肯，且曰：“汝勿食言，明日当先以实收来。及明日，三人者又以是时至，出一纸粘著帐上，曰：”实收在此。“言已即去。仙槎视之，其上大书”张仙槎捐钱七千文“八字，有朱印甚鲜明。谛视无有，朦胧间又了了在目，终夜转展，不能成寐。及旦，以语友人，友人劝之云：”既有此异，宜以七千钱买黄白纸钱至旷野焚之。“仙槎从其言，后亦无他。异哉！冥中亦如阳间，有捐输之例乎？此鬼挟人短长，以相恐犭曷，得钱七千，欣然而去，恐此朱印实收，亦不甚可恃矣！

    Explanation

    561

    The Immortal Boatman said, "The comments we have prepared are merely a balance of reason and emotion, with no preconceived notions; even if there are mistakes, they also belong to public fault, why should there be such a severe punishment!" The other person replied, "I do not know either, but if you can make a donation, perhaps the punishment can be reduced." The Immortal Boatman agreed to donate one thousand coins, but the other person thought it was too little. It was increased to seven thousand, and he finally nodded in agreement, saying, "Do not break your word; bring the receipt tomorrow." The next day, the three of them arrived at the same time, and the other person took out a piece of paper and stuck it on the account book, saying, "Here is the receipt." After saying this, he left immediately. The Immortal Boatman looked at it, and on it were written in large characters, "Zhang Immortal Boatman donated seven thousand coins," with a very clear red seal. Upon closer inspection, he saw nothing, but in a hazy state, the words were clearly visible. He tossed and turned all night, unable to sleep. The next morning, he told his friend about it, and the friend advised him, "Since this is unusual, you should use seven thousand coins to buy yellow and white paper money and burn it in the wilderness." The Immortal Boatman followed his advice, and afterward, there was no further trouble. Strange indeed! Is there such a thing as a donation in the afterlife as in the mortal world? This ghost uses people's weaknesses to intimidate them, and after receiving seven thousand coins, he left happily, but I doubt that this red-sealed receipt is very reliable.

    562

    福建南安县太学生陈良光之母，年近九十矣。道光戊申年腊月下旬，忽告其子曰：“明年我欲烧天香，然为期太迫，恐不及矣。”其子曰：“有何不及？”母曰：“非汝所知也。”烧天香者，泉州之俗，以正月初九为天生日，前后各三日焚香礼天神，谓之烧天香。至次年正月初五日，母命其子妇覃汤沐浴，浴竟，易洁衣端坐而逝。俄张目四顾，众人环问有何事未了，不答。其子请曰：“岂以天香未烧乎？”曰：“然。”子曰：“当即为料理。”乃悉依俗例买香烛等物，于初六日平明焚之。至母前告曰：“天香已烧矣。”应曰：“好。”仍闭目而逝。

    Explanation

    562

    The mother of Chen Liangguang, a student of the Imperial Academy in Nan'an, Fujian, was nearly ninety years old. In the twelfth month of the year Dingwei (戊申) of the Daoguang era, she suddenly told her son, "Next year I want to burn the sky incense, but the deadline is too tight, I fear I won't make it." Her son asked, "What's the problem?" She replied, "It's beyond your understanding." Burning the sky incense is a custom in Quanzhou, Fujian, where people burn incense to worship the heavens on the birthday of the heavens, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the first lunar month. On the fifth day of the first lunar month the following year, the mother instructed her daughter-in-law to bathe and change into clean clothes. After bathing, she sat upright and passed away. Suddenly, she opened her eyes and looked around. The people gathered around and asked if there was anything left undone, but she did not respond. Her son asked, "Is it because the sky incense hasn't been burned yet?" She said, "Yes." The son replied, "Let's do it right away." They then bought all the necessary items according to the custom and burned them at dawn on the sixth day of the first lunar month. In front of her mother, the son said, "The sky incense has been burned." She responded, "Good," and closed her eyes and passed away.

    563

    杭州沈子麟大令之父，于道光某年夏间有旧识之木匠来见，漫问之曰：“无恙乎？”匠曰：“几不获相见矣！”问何故，匠曰：“今年春间在嘉兴，为人造屋，忽觉胸次烦懑，恍惚闻人语曰：”将腹剖开，即舒畅矣。‘因将所用錾凿自剖其腹。又闻人语曰：“错矣！”顿觉身轻如羽，俄顷便还杭城。既至家中，并旧时相识之家一一皆到，翁家中我亦曾来也。已而仍回嘉兴，至向所营造之处，见一人卧版上，众人围绕之。因亦进而观焉，不觉与卧者合为一，大声呼痛。众皆曰：“生矣，生矣！’始悟无故自剖其腹，据人云死已半日矣，肠亦流出。主人召疡医纳其肠，用药线缝其伤口，医云尚可不死。数日后痛稍止，略进糜粥，主人使其徒送之还杭。今既数月，创已全愈，仍欲至嘉兴料理工作，故来一见也。”沈翁大异之，发其衣，创痕犹存，然肤革充盈，面目腴润，转胜于其旧也。闽人廖三者，故家子弟也。以其行三，故人以三爷呼之。

    Explanation

    563

    In a certain year of the Daoguang era during the summer, an old acquaintance, a carpenter, came to see Mr. Shen, the father of Shen Zilin. Mr. Shen casually asked him, "Are you well?" The carpenter replied, "I almost didn't get to see you!" Mr. Shen asked why, and the carpenter said, "This spring in Jiaxing, I was building a house for someone. Suddenly, I felt bitter and annoyed in my chest, and vaguely heard someone say, 'If you cut open your stomach, you will feel relieved.' So, I took the chisel and hammer and cut open my own stomach. Then I heard someone say, 'Wrong!' Suddenly, I felt as light as a feather, and soon returned to Hangzhou. Upon arriving home, I visited every house of my old acquaintances, including your home, where I had also been. Later, I returned to Jiaxing and went to the place where I had been working. I saw someone lying on a board, with many people around him. I also went closer to observe, and unconsciously merged with the person lying down, crying out in pain. Everyone exclaimed, 'Alive! Alive!' I then realized that I had cut open my stomach without reason, and according to others, I had been dead for half a day, with my intestines also coming out. The owner summoned a surgeon to put my intestines back in, and used thread to stitch my wound. The doctor said I might not die. After several days, the pain slightly subsided, and I slightly ate porridge. The owner sent his apprentice to send me back to Hangzhou. Now after several months, my wound has fully healed, and I still want to go to Jiaxing to handle work, so I came to see you." Mr. Shen was greatly surprised, unfastened his clothes, and saw that the scar was still there, but his skin was full, his face was plump and lustrous, even more so than before. Lao San from Fujian is a son of an old family. Because he is the third in line, people call him Three Master.

    564

    貌魁梧，而性敏悟，凡艺事一学即精。生平刚直好义，不喜读书，自幼时即喜习拳勇，为投石超距之戏。及成童，以卞卞横里中，尝语人曰：“大丈夫当效班定远立功万里外，焉事章句为！”是时国家方以兵屯守台湾后山，兼谋开垦，历数年役未竣，而士卒之以瘴疠死者相踵也。廖三志在立功，不以水土恶毒为意，因赴某总兵军中投效，总兵委以夜巡之役。火伴或语之曰：“子行夜不畏鬼邪？”廖曰：“我有剑在，何鬼之畏？”火伴壮之。是夜廖只身巡视营垒，忽见二鬼白林中出，尾其后，廖以剑击之，鬼遂灭。逾时阴风凛然，又见前有一奇鬼，敦灰血拇，殊可骇异。廖拔剑待之，须臾鬼至，曰：“三爷，我非来角胜，乃来相请耳！”言毕，不见。廖归，疑不成寐，已而倦极，恍惚梦至一处，庙宇宏敞。

    Explanation

    564

    The person was tall and robust in appearance, yet possessed a quick and insightful nature. He excelled in all artistic endeavors upon learning them. Throughout his life, he was upright, straightforward, and fond of justice, but he disliked reading books. From a young age, he was fond of martial arts and games like throwing stones and jumping over distances. As a young adult, he was known for his bold and sometimes reckless behavior in the village. He once said to others, "A true man should emulate Ban Chao in establishing great achievements far from home, rather than being concerned with trivial matters like literary studies!" At that time, the state was deploying troops to garrison the mountains behind Taiwan and planning land reclamation, but soldiers were dying one after another due to malaria and other tropical diseases. Liao San was determined to achieve military glory, undeterred by the harsh environment. During his night patrols, a fellow soldier asked him, "Are you not afraid of ghosts during your night patrols?" Liao San confidently replied, "I have a sword, what ghost would I fear?" The fellow soldier admired his confidence. One night, Liao San suddenly saw two ghosts emerge from the forest and follow him. He struck them with his sword, and the ghosts disappeared. After a while, a chilling wind blew, and he saw a strange ghost in front of him, with a bloody thumb, which was particularly frightening. Liao San drew his sword and waited for it. Soon, the ghost appeared and said, "Master Liao, I did not come to fight, but to invite you!" After saying this, the ghost vanished. Liao San returned, and although he was suspicious and could not sleep, he soon became exhausted and fell into a deep sleep. In his dream, he found himself in a grand and spacious temple.

    565

    有神坐殿上，问曰：“尔是廖三爷邪？”曰：“然。”神曰：“若来甚好，有一事非若不可，当以相属。”廖醒，遂发寒热，自知不起。逾月，果卒于台湾，未竟其用，闻者惜焉。福建建宁府有一旧宅，时建安令鲍君卒于官，其眷属赁是屋以居。楼故有狐，未之知也。鲍妻居楼上，往往无雨而屋漏。又所用什物，旦辄易其处，畏而迁居于下。其子少年负气，曰：“我何畏彼哉？”遂居之。天明犹卧未起，觉有奇臭，视之则被有沾濡之痕，发怒谩骂，则臭益甚。俄而己身亦臭，遍视不见，后方知其发上皆粪也。于是家人咸集，迁其子下楼。鲍君之弟焚香祝之曰：“我等罹死丧之戚，暂住于此，苟有失礼，当蒙见原。今空此楼供奉香火，幸勿相嬲。”祝后寂然。天下事可以理胜，而不可以气力争，类如此也。

    Explanation

    565

    A deity sat on a platform and asked, "Are you Lao San Ye?" Lao replied, "Yes." The deity said, "It's very good that you have come. There is something that only you can do, and I will entrust it to you." Lao woke up and immediately felt ill, knowing he would not recover. After a month, he indeed died in Taiwan, having not fulfilled his role, and those who heard about it were sorry. In Jianning Prefecture, Fujian Province, there was an old house. At the time, the magistrate of Jian'an, Bo Jun, died while in office, and his family rented the house to live in. The building had a fox spirit, but the family did not know about it. Bo's wife lived on the upper floor, often experiencing leaks even without rain. The items they used would often change places by morning, and they moved to the lower floor out of fear. Bo's son, being young and proud, said, "What do I fear?" and moved back to the upper floor. He woke up to find a strange smell and saw wet marks on his bedding. He got angry and cursed, which made the smell worse. Soon, his body was also smelly, and he realized that his hair was covered in feces. The family gathered and moved the son back to the lower floor. Bo's brother burned incense and made a wish, saying, "We, having suffered the loss of a loved one, are temporarily staying here. If we have any disrespect, we would be forgiven. We will leave this upper floor empty for the worship of incense, hoping the fox spirit will not bother us." After the wish, the situation became quiet. In the world, things can be overcome with reason, not with anger, as exemplified by this story.

    566

    湖北咸宁县刘氏一妇，年近百龄，而情性与孩童无异。每日晨起，家人为之簪花敷粉，衣以红紫艳色之衣，则终日笑。除其子及长孙一人外，余皆不之识。苟欲告之，则必举其祖若父之乳名，曰：“此某人之子，某人之孙。”乃始笑而颔之。如此媪者，殆亦近于鸡窠中小儿者与！

    　　江西南康县乡间，有方贡生者，失其名。世以耕读为业，家颇小康。偶佣一童子，使牧牛。童子乳名毛狗。其始来也，有一小人与之俱，长仅尺余，时见时不见。久之而小人日益多，昏黑樘突，不胜其扰。或詈骂之，辄跃起批人颊。虽于贡生不敢犯，然呵责之，亦不听也。如是十四年，聚至百余之多，器用为其损坏，饮食为其污秽。甚或烟气缕缕出自笥中，发视，则所贮之衣皆烧成小孔以千百计。举家厌苦，延僧道禳之，则僧道悉被棰击，流血被面而去。

    Explanation

    566

    A woman from the Liu family in Xianning County, Hubei, lived to be nearly a century old, yet her temperament was no different from that of a child. Every morning, her family would dress her up with flowers and makeup, and clothe her in bright red and purple garments, which would make her laugh all day. Apart from her son and one of her grandsons, she did not recognize anyone else. If anyone wanted to tell her something, they had to mention the nicknames of her ancestors or father, saying, "This is the son of someone, the grandson of someone." Only then would she smile and nod in acknowledgment. Such an old woman is perhaps akin to a small chicken in a nest, isn't she?

    In a rural area of Nankang County, Jiangxi, there was a scholar-gentleman named Fang, whose name is unknown. His family made a living through farming and reading, and they were relatively well-off. Once, he hired a child to herd cattle. The child's nickname was Mao Gou. When the child first arrived, a small person accompanied him, measuring only about a foot tall, appearing and disappearing intermittently. Over time, the number of these small people increased, causing chaos and disturbance. If someone scolded or cursed them, they would leap up and slap the person's face. Although they did not dare to offend the scholar-gentleman, they would still not listen to his reprimands. This continued for fourteen years, during which the small people grew to more than a hundred. They damaged household items and contaminated food and drink. Sometimes, smoke would emerge from a chest, and upon opening it, one would find that the clothes stored inside had been burned with countless holes. The entire family grew weary and miserable, so they invited monks and Taoist priests to perform rituals to dispel the evil. However, the monks and priests were all beaten and left with bloodied faces.

    567

    或使人诉之真人府，使者甫出门，辄迷惘不能行一步。贡生忧之，而无如何。至道光丁酉岁，贡生于元旦黎明集其子弟，语之曰：“我梦见天榜，今科江西解元即我也，子弟中亦有数人中式者，我不敢泄。尔曹宜各努力，我亦自勉。”乃日夜督责其子弟温习举业，贡生以身先之。六月既望，躬率子弟赴省城应试。既至，又语子弟曰：“试期尚早，我有一老友，举业家斫轮老手也，距此不远。汝曹留此，勿荒所业，我且往访之。”乃不携童仆，买舟而去。去则竟赴贵溪县龙虎山，具牒于真人府，请除妖。真人受其牒，判九月朔日雷部施行。

    Explanation

    567

    Someone sent a report to the True Person's office, but as soon as the messenger stepped out, he became lost and couldn't take a single step. The scholar was worried about it, but there was nothing he could do. In the year Dingyou of the Daoguang era, the scholar gathered his descendants at dawn on the first day of the year and said to them, "I dreamed of the heavenly list, and this year's Jiangxi top scholar is me. Some of my descendants will also pass the examination, but I dare not reveal it. You all should work hard, and I will also encourage myself." He then tirelessly supervised his descendants in reviewing the examination system, leading by example. On the 15th day of the sixth month, he personally led his descendants to the provincial capital for the examination. After arriving, he said to his descendants, "The exam is still early. I have an old friend, an old hand at the examination system, who is not far from here. Stay here, don't neglect your studies, and I will go visit him." Without bringing any servants, he bought a boat and left, finally going to Longhu Mountain in Guixi County. He presented a petition to the True Person's office, requesting the removal of a demon. The True Person received the petition and ordered the Thunder Department to act on the first day of September.

    568

    贡生奉牒遄返省城，则三场毕矣，即携子弟辈俱归。及九月朔，众小人殊未之知，嬉戏如故。日加辰，则各惶惑失措，有哭者，有怒骂者。亭午天忽无云，而雷电延霆轰，其势甚猛，俄迅雷一声，而众小人俱不见矣。是科方氏无一中式者，贡生固托言之，以愚众小人，使不为备也，亦可谓智深勇沉者矣。初，妖之兴也，客有善琴者来。或曰：“琴者，禁也，所以禁止淫邪也。”乃延此客，使鼓琴三日，果为之稍戢。客去，而扰弥甚，惟毛狗与之狎，呼毛狗为老庚。老庚者，俗呼同岁者之称也。

    Explanation

    568

    The scholar received a decree and returned swiftly to the provincial city, where the three examination sections had already been completed. He then took his disciples and returned home. By the first day of the ninth month, the common people were still unaware of the situation and continued to play as usual. As the day progressed, they became increasingly confused and disarrayed, some crying and others shouting in anger. At noon, the sky suddenly became cloudless, and thunder and lightning raged intensely. Suddenly, a loud clap of thunder resounded, and all the common people vanished. In that year, the Fang family had no one who passed the examination, and the scholar had indeed fabricated a story to deceive the common people, preventing them from making preparations. He could truly be called a man of profound wisdom and calm courage. Initially, when the supernatural phenomenon arose, a guest skilled in playing the qin arrived. Someone said, "The qin is a means to restrain, used to prohibit licentiousness." The scholar then invited this guest and had him play the qin for three days, and indeed, the disturbances were slightly calmed. After the guest left, the disturbances grew even worse, with only Mao Gou being close to him, and Mao Gou was called "Lao Geng." "Lao Geng" is a colloquial term for someone of the same age or generation.

    569

    其类甚繁，亦有婚嫁生育、亲戚故旧往来之事。每遇岁时，辄三五成群，聚而为意钱之戏。其法以三钱掷之，观其阴阳，以为胜负。尝使毛狗为囊家，得头钱三千余文。又一日大雨，庭中积水甚深，有数小人响之而哭甚哀。贡生使毛狗探之，则其幼子溺死水中也。雨霁得其尸。乃一二寸长之纸人耳。异哉！翦纸为人，乃作祟如此，竟不知其为何妖矣。然观琴客鼓琴，妖为少戢，则邪不胜正，其理昭然。贡生苟知此理，但当正心修身，以齐其家。一家中男正位乎外，女正位乎内，彼众小人者，自当形灭声销，固不必远求之龙虎山也。

    Explanation

    569

    There are many kinds of such phenomena, including matters of marriage, childbirth, and family life, as well as interactions with relatives, old friends, and acquaintances. Every time during the seasons, they gather in groups of three or five to play a game called "intention money" (意钱). The rules of the game involve throwing three coins and observing the outcome of yin and yang to determine the winner. Once, a scholar sent a dog to investigate a situation, and the dog won a large sum of money (over 3,000 coins). On another day, after heavy rain, there was a deep pool of water in the courtyard, and several small figures were heard crying out in deep sorrow. The scholar sent his dog to investigate, and it discovered that the dog's young child had drowned in the water. After the rain stopped, the body was retrieved, and it turned out to be a paper figure about an inch or two long. Strange indeed! Cutting paper into human shapes and causing such mischief, yet no one knows what kind of spirit or demon it is. However, when a musician plays the qin, the mischievous spirits are somewhat subdued, indicating that evil cannot overcome good, a principle that is clear. If the scholar understood this principle, he should focus on cultivating his mind and body, and managing his household well. In a household, men should take charge outside, and women should manage the home, and those mischievous spirits will naturally disappear, without the need to seek them far away at Longhu Mountain.

    570

    休宁朱村有朱姓者，贾于外。父母早死，妻许氏在家。偶以鸡卵十余枚，使鸡母伏之，久之不出。一夕，许梦见舅姑自外至，皆以红帕首，而颜色愁惨。许欲启问，倏至埘桀间而隐。明旦往视，则二雏出壳矣，悟曰：“此必翁姑也。”对之流涕，乃溺而死之。即延僧诵经三日，求免翁姑之罪。数月后，复梦翁姑来谢曰：“我二人以生前杀生过多，冥司罚作鸡，使受汤火之苦。今幸新妇代为忏悔，仍得转生为人矣。”

    Explanation

    570

    In the village of Zhu in Xiuning, there was a man named Zhu who was engaged in trade outside. His parents had died early, and his wife, Xu, stayed at home. One day, she made the hen sit on more than ten eggs. After a long time, the hen did not hatch them. One night, Xu dreamed that her parents-in-law came from outside, wearing red headscarves and with sad and gloomy expressions. She wanted to ask them something, but they suddenly disappeared into the chicken coop. The next morning, she went to check the eggs and found that two chicks had hatched. She realized that these must be the spirits of her parents-in-law and cried in front of them. She then drowned and killed the chicks. She invited a monk to recite scriptures for three days to seek forgiveness for the sins of her parents-in-law. Several months later, she had another dream in which her parents-in-law came to thank her, saying, "We were punished by the underworld for killing too many animals in our lives and were reborn as chickens, suffering from the fires of hell. Now, thanks to your repentance, we have been reborn as humans again."

    571

    休宁商山村，有杂货店兼卖猪肉，市者颇盛。日杀三四猪，东方未明，辄鼓刀而屠。一日屠竟，县肉于{}，忽见猪之后足颇似妇女之足，底平指佥，纤不盈握，众皆见而异之。因问爪壳何在。爪壳者，猪足下粗皮也。屠曰：“当尚在汤镬中。”往视，则绣履双浮，制作绝工，非乡间所有。捞而出之，众手传观，无不惊叹。此店嗣后专卖杂货，不复屠猪，至今尚在。

    　　余亲家樊氏眷属寓居河南省城。时有佣妇，其家距城七十里。一日抱其幼女自其家来，骑驴而行，中路坠驴，复抱女起，骑驴入城，至于樊寓。而此女昏昏如睡，不复言语行动。与之食，尚能下咽，不与之，亦不索也。如是月余，樊氏之众语之曰：“汝女之病，久而不愈，曷不暂归乎？”妇然之，乃复抱女骑驴出城。行至来时坠驴之处，女忽开口唤母，问之，则能答矣。自此途中便能言笑，比至家，行动如常。盖坠驴时失其魂，至是复与合也。

    Explanation

    571

    In the village of Shangshan in Xiuning, there was a general store that also sold pork, and the market was quite prosperous. Every day, three or four pigs were slaughtered before dawn, and the butcher would already be at work with his knife. One day, after the slaughter was finished, the meat was hung on hooks, and suddenly, the pig's hind leg looked very much like a woman's foot, with a flat bottom and fingers, thin and not fitting in the palm. Everyone who saw it found it strange. They then asked where the claw shell was. The claw shell is the thick skin on the pig's foot. The butcher said, "It should still be in the boiling pot." When they went to check, they found a pair of embroidered shoes floating, exquisitely crafted, not something found in the countryside. They fished them out, and everyone passed them around to look at, all marveling at the sight. After this, the store only sold general goods and no longer slaughtered pigs, and it is still there today.

    My relative's family, the Fan family, lived in the provincial capital of Henan. At one time, there was a hired woman whose home was seventy li away from the city. One day, she brought her young daughter from her home, riding a donkey. In the middle of the journey, she fell off the donkey, picked up her daughter, and continued riding into the city, arriving at the Fan family's residence. The girl was dazed and unresponsive, not speaking or moving. When given food, she could still eat, but when not given, she did not ask for it. Like this for over a month, the members of the Fan family said to her, "Your daughter's illness has lasted so long without healing, why not go back temporarily?" The woman agreed, and so she picked up her daughter and rode the donkey out of the city. When they reached the place where she had fallen off the donkey, the girl suddenly opened her mouth and called out to her mother. When asked, she could answer. From then on, she could talk and laugh during the journey, and by the time they reached home, she was moving normally. It seems that when she fell off the donkey, her soul left her body, and now it has returned.

    572

    陆连逵，字锺山，温州乐清县人。家尚温饱，以知县需次江苏，持孙琴西同年书来见。其人颇朴诚，而在吴中数载，甚落寞。亦尝乞假返其家，不久仍来，岁以为常。同治壬申秋，复至余寓，言将归遣嫁其女。余次日往送之，未见，嗣是遂不相闻。乃是年十二月，其兄之子忽来苏，踵门求见。问之，则钟山已被杀于途矣，为之骇然。详叩之，钟山之归，独行不挈僮仆。行至一处，为黄岩县所属，忘其地名，距其家止百数十里矣。钟山于其地赁一小舟，而自向市中买鲑菜之类。

    Explanation

    572

    Lu Liankui, courtesy name Zhongshan, was from Leqing County, Wenzhou, Zhejiang Province. His family enjoyed a comfortable living standard. He was waiting for an official position as a magistrate in Jiangsu Province and came to see me with a letter from his fellow scholar Sun Qinxi. He was a very honest and sincere person, but during his time in Suzhou (Wu), he felt very isolated. He also requested leave to return home, and after a short time, he returned to his post. This became a yearly routine. Later, he returned to my residence and said he would go home to arrange the marriage of his daughter. The next day, I went to see him off, but I did not meet him. After that, we did not hear from each other again. In the twelfth month of the same year, his nephew came to Suzhou and visited me. When asked about the situation, it was revealed that Zhongshan had been killed on the road. He had traveled alone without any servants or attendants. He reached a place in Huangyan County, which was only a hundred or so miles from his home, but the specific location name was forgotten. At that place, Zhongshan rented a small boat and went himself to the market to buy food such as salmon and vegetables.

    573

    及归，则舟中已有客六七人，问之，曰趁船者也，无如何，姑听之。而此六七人者，皆盗也，舟子亦与通谋。夜深移舟至荒寂之处，杀钟山，沉诸河，而取其赀，无知者。越一月，有渔者出其尸于水中，则创痕犹存，乃闻于官。时黄岩令为孙君欢伯，健令也，亲往验其尸，于其袜肚中得字纸一卷，虽漫漶，犹约略可辨。一名纸有“陆连逵”三字，亦不知谁何，姑命以棺佥之。又月余，孙君如台州谒太守，偶与同官会饮，言及此事，一人曰：“此必乐清人也。君谓此人姓陆乎？非也。陆连，其覆姓，乐清故有陆连氏，吾尝令乐清，故知之。且吾与陆连氏亦颇有相识者。”乃以书问之，所问者即钟山之兄也。

    Explanation

    573

    When he returned, there were already six or seven guests on the boat. He asked about them, and they said they were just passengers. There was nothing to be done, so he temporarily allowed it. However, these six or seven people were all thieves, and the boatman had conspired with them. At night, they moved the boat to a desolate place, killed Zhong Shan, and sank his body in the river, taking his belongings. No one knew about it. After one month, a fisherman pulled his body out of the water, and the wounds were still visible. He then reported it to the authorities. At that time, the magistrate of Huangyan was Mr. Sun Huanbo, who was a capable magistrate. He personally went to examine the body and found a roll of paper in his sock. Although it was faded and illegible, it could still be vaguely discerned. One piece of paper had the three characters "Lu Liankui," and no one knew who he was. He temporarily ordered him to be buried. After another month, Mr. Sun went to Taizhou to pay respects to the governor. He happened to meet with his colleagues for a drink, and they mentioned this matter. One person said, "This must be a person from Yueqing. You think this person is surnamed Lu? No. Lu Lian is a compound surname. There used to be the Lu Lian clan in Yueqing. I once served as the magistrate of Yueqing, so I know about it. And I have acquaintances among the Lu Lian clan." So he wrote a letter to inquire about it. The person he inquired about was Zhong Shan's brother.

    574

    其家久知钟山于某日发苏州，待之不至，犹意其或不果归，至是始知中途有变。使其兄子往视，发棺视尸，已不可识，犹冀非真，故复来苏州审之。既知不谬，亟归言于孙君。孙君命役捕凶手，不可得。久之，或言某所有人以朝珠鬻于市，乡间安得有此物，是可疑也。孙君使人捕之。捕者以夜往，则有鬼声&lt；需鬼&gt；&lt；需鬼&gt；导于前。至其家，六七人皆在，已知事发，结束将行，稍缓则无及矣。缚之至官，皆吐实，杀以祭钟山。钟山为人固长者，罹此酷祸，殊不可解，而行路之难亦可鉴矣。余因先兄福宁君之丧往迎太夫人，亦尝取道于此。

    Explanation

    574

    The family had long known that Zhongshan would depart from Suzhou on a certain day, but he did not arrive, and they still thought that perhaps he would not return. It was only then that they learned of a change in the middle of the journey. They sent his nephew to investigate, and upon opening the coffin to see the body, it was no longer recognizable. They still hoped it was not true, so they returned to Suzhou to verify the situation. Having confirmed that it was not a mistake, they hurried back to inform Master Sun. Master Sun ordered his servants to capture the murderer, but they could not find him. After a long time, someone said that a certain person had sold court jewelry in the market. How could there be such an item in the countryside? This was suspicious. Master Sun sent someone to capture him. The pursuers went at night, and there were ghostly sounds leading the way. When they arrived at his home, six or seven people were there, knowing the incident had occurred, they were preparing to leave, and if they delayed, they would miss their chance. They tied them up and took them to the authorities, who all confessed, and they were executed to appease Zhongshan. Zhongshan was a true gentleman, to suffer such a cruel fate is incomprehensible, and the difficulty of traveling can also be seen. I went to welcome my mother-in-law after my elder brother Fu Ning's death, and also passed through this area.

    575

    至台州府，适陈君鹿笙守是郡，留余饭于署中，且为具舟。舟人言：“须乙夜潮至，始行。”乃于晚饭后登舟，陈君亲送之，余辞焉不可。余先至舟，则舟中人满矣，问之，亦言是趁船者。余时止挈二仆，其一从余在郡署，其一留守舟中，止能护持行李，竟不能与之争。余傍徨无策，而陈君至，怒曰：“此何人也？”是时渐东当大乱之后，盗贼多有，故太守所至，辄有亲兵从行。陈君遂命亲兵悉驱其人登岸，并以四亲兵送余至黄岩焉。盖台温地僻，舟楫稀少，寻常行客每雇一舟，必有趁船者，固非尽盗贼也，然其情状，亦甚难堪矣。非太守亲送，岂能麾而出之哉！附书于此，俾于役者知所警焉。

    575

    Upon arriving at the prefecture of Taizhou, I found that Mr. Chen Lusheng was just then governing the area. He invited me to dine at the prefectural office and also prepared a boat for my journey. The boatman said that we would need to wait until the midnight tide before we could depart. So, after dinner, I boarded the boat. Mr. Chen personally saw me off, and I could not refuse. By the time I reached the boat, it was already full. When I asked about it, they also said they were passengers. At that time, I only brought two servants with me; one was with me at the prefectural office, and the other was guarding the boat. They could only protect the luggage, but could not compete for space. I was at a loss and had no plan. When Mr. Chen arrived, he angrily said, "Who are these people?" It was after a period of great turmoil in the area of Jiaodong, where there were many bandits. Therefore, whenever the prefect went anywhere, he had personal guards with him. Mr. Chen then ordered his personal guards to drive all the people onto the shore and also sent four of his personal guards to escort me to Huangyan. Taizhou and Wenzhou are remote, and boats are scarce. Ordinary travelers usually rent a boat, and there are always other passengers. Not all of them are bandits, but their behavior is also very difficult to bear. Without the personal intervention of the prefect, how could they have been driven away? I am writing this down so that future travelers may be warned.

    576

    嘉兴姚恩衍，字叔怡，官中书科。中书为人颇有才干，而性行笃实，虽在少年，已诺必诚，取与不苟。在京师时，乡人多推重之，以事讠垂诿者无虚日。光绪戊寅岁，忽自知将死，于人所属之事，一一处分，悉有条理。时其父访梅观察，方为长芦盐运分司，故其母与妻皆在天津。乃为书告知死期，请其母妻来京师诀别。母妻得书大惊，驰至，则虽有小疾，固未足忧也，而叔怡刻期待死。至四月之末，戒其阍者曰：“五月初六日巳时，夏侍郎来传旨，即为达，勿留滞！”家人咸怪其语。所谓夏侍郎者，乃夏君子松同善也。夏与姚虽同乡，然不过正月中贺岁投一名刺而已，平时固不相往来也。届期叔怡之病稍剧。日加巳，外报夏侍郎来，阍者甫延之入，而叔怡已气绝矣。

    Explanation

    576

    Jiaxing Yao Endian, courtesy name Shuyi, served as an official in the Office of the Secretariat. Yao was a capable individual with a sincere and honest character. Even as a young man, he kept his promises and was careful in his dealings. While in the capital, he was highly regarded by his fellow townspeople, who entrusted him with many tasks without a break. In the year 1888, he suddenly realized that he was about to die. He arranged for all the matters entrusted to him, everything in an orderly manner. At the time, his father, Fu Xian, was serving as the Inspector of Salt Administration in the region of Changlu, so his mother and wife were in Tianjin. Yao wrote a letter informing them of his impending death and requested that they come to the capital to bid him farewell. Upon receiving the letter, his mother and wife were greatly shocked and rushed to the capital. Although Yao had a minor illness, it was not something to be overly concerned about, yet he was eagerly awaiting his death. By the end of April, he instructed his gatekeeper: "On the sixth day of the fifth lunar month at the hour of the snake, Vice-Chancellor Xia will come to deliver an imperial decree. Inform me immediately, do not delay!" The family found his words strange. The Vice-Chancellor referred to was Xia Zirong, also known as Xia Jun and Tongshan. Although Xia and Yao were from the same hometown, they had only exchanged name cards during the New Year's greetings in the first month of the year; otherwise, they did not associate with each other. On the appointed day, Yao's condition worsened slightly. As the hour of the snake approached, there was an announcement that Vice-Chancellor Xia had arrived. The gatekeeper had just invited him in when Yao had already stopped breathing.

    577

    子松之来也，初不为姚氏而来，盖叔怡之妻父钟学士骏声，与子松极相得。是日夏往访钟，钟适往视叔怡疾，阍者辞焉。问所之，以告。而夏有事必欲见钟，乃访之于姚氏，不图坐未定，而内之哭声已作也。一时咸共惊异，谓子松之来，适符叔怡之语，所传者何旨欤？此旨何以必由夏侍郎传之欤？叔怡死，子松亦可危矣。然子松固无恙，是年冬，即拜江苏学政之命。乃至庚辰秋，子松竟终于江阴使署，距叔怡之卒，未及三年也。古称刘桢、徐干、王粲，并为天上侍中，王茂弘为天上尚书令，传记所载，多有此事。子松起家词臣，历官卿贰，又尝预君畴务成之列，殁为贵神，固无足怪。叔怡言行，不失为君子，其死也，或亦有所为欤？惟彼时子松犹在人间，而玉版征书由其传达，是不可解。岂其神识已先归天上乎？

    Explanation

    577

    When Zisong arrived, he did not come for the sake of the Yao family; rather, it was because his father-in-law, Zhong Junsheng, the scholar-official, had become extremely close with Zisong. On that day, Xia went to visit Zhong, but Zhong had just gone to see Shuyi who was ill, and the gatekeeper refused him entry. Xia asked where Zhong had gone, and was told. Because Xia had business and wanted to see Zhong, he then went to the Yao family. Unbeknownst to him, as he had not yet sat down, the sound of weeping inside had already begun. Everyone present was surprised and wondered if Zisong's arrival happened to match what Shuyi had said, and what message was being conveyed? Why must this message be transmitted by Xia the Attendant? Shuyi's death made Zisong also seem vulnerable. However, Zisong was indeed unharmed, and that winter, he was immediately appointed as the provincial educational commissioner of Jiangsu. Until the autumn of Gengchen, Zisong ultimately passed away in the office of the governor in Jiangyin, less than three years after Shuyi's death. Ancient records often mention Liu Zhen, Xu Gan, and Wang Can, all of whom became heavenly Secretaries, and Wang Maohong became the heavenly Minister of the Imperial Secretariat. There are many such accounts in historical records. Zisong began his career as a literary official, served in various high-ranking positions, and participated in the completion of important tasks. After his death, he became a revered spirit, which is not surprising. Shuyi's words and deeds did not fail to make him a gentleman, and perhaps his death had a purpose? At that time, Zisong was still in the world, yet the divine message was conveyed through him, which is inexplicable. Could his spiritual awareness have already returned to heaven?

    578

    青浦县南门外离城二十里许，有觉海庵，故无僧也。同治间，忽来一僧，赤体无衣，惟以破被自覆。时方严寒，卧地上数日不起，见者怜之，或予以钱米，不受。一日，忽披破被走出，见一老妪两目失明，即向溪中汲水一瓯，付之曰：“试以此洗目。”如言洗之，瞽目复明，即能见物。又一少年左足反生，僧扪之曰：“正，正。”其足即时转正，与常人无异。于是远近喧传，谓之神僧，求医者日数十人。憎有医有不医，医则无不奏效。住庵中一月有余，后竟不知所之。青浦县长山，为九峰之一。山上东北隅，有平石一方，中有一痕，长尺余，广数寸许，由来久矣，莫知何时所刂刻也。近日有地师入山，为人求葬地，偶以定向之盘置此石上，适当其痕，而盘中子午针为之旋转，其针锋不直午，而直子。地师知其有异。将盘徐徐举起，针在盘中犹动摇不定，至离石三四尺，始复其常。说者谓其下必有磁石云。

    Explanation

    578

    South of the city gates of Qingpu County, about twenty li away, there is a temple called Juehai, which originally had no monks. During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, a monk suddenly appeared, naked and without clothing, covering himself only with a torn blanket. It was the height of winter, and he lay on the ground for several days without moving. Those who saw him pitied him, and some offered him money and rice, but he refused. One day, he suddenly put on his torn blanket and walked out. He saw an elderly woman who was blind in both eyes. The monk took a bowl of water from the stream and gave it to her, saying, "Try washing your eyes with this." She washed her eyes as instructed, and her blindness was cured; she could see again. Another young man had a twisted left foot. The monk touched it and said, "Straighten it, straighten it." Immediately, the young man's foot straightened, just like anyone else's. News of this spread far and wide, and people called him a divine monk. Every day, dozens of people came to him seeking medical help. Sometimes he healed them, and sometimes he did not, but whenever he did, his treatments were always effective. He stayed at the temple for more than a month before disappearing without a trace. Changshan Mountain in Qingpu County is one of the Nine Peaks. On the northeast corner of the mountain, there is a flat stone with a mark in the middle, about a foot long and several inches wide. Its origin is unknown, and no one knows when it was carved. Recently, a geomancer entered the mountain to find a burial site for someone. By chance, he placed his compass on this stone, and it aligned perfectly with the mark. The needle in the compass began to spin, pointing not south but north. The geomancer recognized that something unusual was happening. As he slowly lifted the compass, the needle continued to waver inside the compass until it was several feet away from the stone, at which point it returned to its normal position. Some say that there must be a magnet beneath the stone.

    579

    青浦之章练塘镇，有某姓者买一西瓜，重二十余斤。将剖食之，而瓜在案上旋转不定，惧不敢剖。其旁人代为奏刀，砉砉一声，瓜分为二，而其中有蜈蚣一条，长尺余，广寸许，已成两段矣。腹中隐隐有光，破之得明珠数十颗。光绪庚辰岁七月中，娄县水浦桥农家牯牛产一犊，六足二尾。其四足与常牛同，两足在腹下，甚短；其二尾之下各有一肛门，每遗矢则一时并出。主人怪之，不敢畜。有江北人以洋钱六枚买之去，不知其何用也。

    Explanation

    579

    In Zhang Liantang Town of Qingpu, there was a man of a certain surname who bought a watermelon weighing over twenty pounds. He intended to cut and eat it, but the melon was rotating unpredictably on the table, and he was afraid to cut it. Someone nearby took the knife and cut it with a crisp sound, dividing the melon into two pieces. Inside, there was a centipede about a foot long and an inch wide, already cut into two segments. There was a faint light inside the melon, and when it was broken open, dozens of pearls were found. In the seventh month of the year Gengchen (1880) during the Guangxu era, a farmer's house near Shuipu Bridge in Lou county had a cow give birth to a calf with six legs and two tails. The four legs were like those of a normal cow, while the two additional legs were under the belly, very short. Each tail had its own anus, and when the calf defecated, both tails would release waste simultaneously. The owner found it strange and did not keep it. A person from the north bought it with six silver coins, but no one knew what it was used for.

    580

    宁波某店一友，于岁终至各村中收债，肩负钱囊，手持簿籍，皆书其店之名。及归时，囊中盛洋钱三百有奇，钱五百有奇。时巳薄暮，虑遇暴客，将谋栖止，苦无逆旅可投。又行里许，见有草屋一所，扣门求宿，而是家惟母女二人，母执不可。女曰：“此客日暮途穷，不留焉往？吾母女可同榻，以一榻留客，亦何伤乎？”母从其言。女并为具食，客食已，枕钱囊而卧。夜半，女忽来求共寝，客峻拒之，女不肯去。客不得已，许以作竟夕之谈。

    Explanation

    580

    A friend from a store in Ningbo, at the end of the year, went to collect debts from various villages. He shouldered a money pouch and held a ledger in hand, all written with the name of his store. Upon returning, the pouch contained three hundred and more silver dollars, and five hundred and more copper coins. It was already late in the evening, and he worried about encountering a robber, so he planned to find a place to stay but suffered from the lack of an inn to stay at. After walking about a mile, he saw a thatched cottage and knocked on the door to ask for lodging. The cottage was occupied by a mother and daughter. The mother firmly refused, but the daughter said, "This guest is at the end of his journey at dusk. Where else can he go? My mother and I can share a bed. What harm is there in letting him stay on one bed?" The mother followed her daughter's suggestion. The daughter also prepared food for him. The guest ate, then lay down with the money pouch as a pillow. At midnight, the daughter suddenly came to ask to share the bed. The guest firmly rejected her, but the daughter would not leave. The guest, having no choice, agreed to talk all night.

    581

    谈至鸡鸣，女促客起，曰：“吾父归矣，可速去。”客惊起趋出，行百余步，忆钱囊未取，急回取之，至则并无草屋，有二棺攒焉。大惊，谛视之，钱囊一角犹见于外。正傍徨间，忽有一人至，谓客：“在此奚为？岂发丘摸金之徒乎？此两棺者，一吾妻，一吾女也。”客乃详述夜间止宿事，惟其女私就己则讳而不言，且告之曰：“吾囊中洋钱若干，钱若干，囊及簿籍皆有店名，请发视之。如有不符，吾任其罪。”乃发其攒，果如客言，俾尽取以归。

    Explanation

    581

    They talked until cockcrow, when the woman urged the guest to rise, saying, "My father has returned; you must leave quickly." Startled, the guest got up and hurried out. After walking more than a hundred steps, he remembered that he had not taken his money bag, and rushed back to retrieve it. Upon arriving, there was no thatched hut in sight, only two coffins piled up. Greatly surprised, he examined them carefully and saw that one corner of his money bag was still visible outside. Just as he was hesitating, a man suddenly appeared and asked the guest, "What are you doing here? Are you a grave robber? These two coffins, one is my wife, the other is my daughter." The guest then recounted the details of his stay that night, but remained silent about the daughter’s private approach to him. He also told the man, "In my bag, there are several foreign coins and several silver coins. The bag and the ledger all have the store’s name on them. Please check them. If there is any discrepancy, I will take the responsibility." The man then opened the coffins, and indeed, everything was as the guest had said. He allowed the guest to take everything and return.

    582

    泰兴县黄桥镇有何某者，亦旧家子也。娶靖江盛氏女，美而贤。而何有外妇，遂仇视厥妻，日肆陵虐，盛顺受之无怨言。何嗜鸦片烟，丙夜犹不寐，使妇供茶汤。稍不如意，以烟签乱刺之，遍体无完肤，妇不胜痛，遂缢死。其父固长者，不闻于官，何得以无事。一夕，忽见妇来，流血满身，语何曰：“我事君，无失德，何酷虐如此？今得请于神矣！”言已不见。数月后，何忽得奇疾，十指先作痒，爬搔之，痒愈甚，乃以齿咬之，指寸寸断。以次及肩臂，及股，及足，凡口所及之处，无不啮者，血殷床褥。昼夜呼号，阅两月而毙。

    Explanation

    582

    In Huangqiao Town, Taixing County, there was a man named He, who was also a person from a prominent family. He married a woman from the Sheng family of Jingjiang, who was both beautiful and virtuous. However, He had a concubine, and he came to regard his wife with enmity, daily indulging in oppression and abuse. The wife endured it passively without complaint. He was addicted to opium smoking and would stay awake late at night, demanding that his wife provide tea and soup. If she did not please him, he would stab her with a cigarette holder or pipe, leaving her body covered in wounds with no skin left intact. Unable to bear the pain, she hanged herself. Her father, a respected elder, did not report the incident to the authorities, so He was able to escape punishment. One night, he suddenly saw the apparition of his deceased wife, covered in blood, and she said to him, "I served you faithfully, without any moral failings. Why were you so cruel to me? Now I have received permission from the gods!" After she spoke, she disappeared. Several months later, He suddenly developed a strange illness. His fingers first became itchy, and when he scratched them, the itching worsened. He then began to bite his fingers with his teeth, breaking them into small pieces. The condition spread to his shoulders, arms, thighs, and feet, and wherever his mouth touched, he would bite and chew, staining his bed and bedding with blood. He cried out day and night, and after two months, he died.

    583

    泰兴东乡卢家庄有卢某者，贷富家钱，已偿其本，惟负其息钱一千，贫不能偿，求免焉。而富子执不可，竟如数取之。卢憾甚，因思其场圃外积草成阜，高数丈，若货之，值钱可十万。乃于幕夜跻其巅，掀其盖，置火于中，数日而草不然。谋诸妇，妇曰：“是夜五更，适有微雨，故不也。宜再置之。”乃复跻其巅，掀而视焉，则其中为火所烧，已空矣，因其盖沾雨，故火不能透。

    Explanation

    583

    In the eastern village of Luxiaozhuang, Taixing, there was a man named Lu who had borrowed money from a wealthy family. He had already repaid the principal but still owed one thousand in interest. Being poor and unable to repay, he requested an exemption. However, the wealthy son insisted on not allowing it and finally took the full amount. Lu was greatly resentful and then thought about the pile of grass outside his field and garden, which was several zhang tall. If sold, it would be worth ten thousand. So, at night, he climbed to the top of the mound, opened its cover, and placed fire inside. For several days, the grass did not burn. He consulted his wife, who said, "Last night at the fifth watch, there was a light rain, so it did not burn. It should be placed again." So, he climbed to the top again, opened it, and looked inside. Then, it was burned by the fire, and it was already empty. Because its cover was wet with rain, the fire could not penetrate.

    584

    及掀之，火即上腾，卢坠其中，一时烈焰飞扬，卢与草俱烬，其妻奔救无及矣。陈子愚广文以此事语余，且曰：“吝小致失大，当以富子鉴。害人反害己，当以卢某鉴。”泰兴老儒何君萱泰，尝有事于宿迁，赁一车以行。其执鞭者，满面皆刀痕，若井字然。问之，曰：“余不敢自讳，十年前乃大盗也。有富家娶新妇，装送甚盛，与火伴八人往劫之。夜半入其室，新妇美而少，才十六七耳。叱曰：”勿惊我官人，我取银钱畀尔。‘众姑于窗外俟之。见其入床后若有所取，俄而一刀飞出，插于，人亦随刀飞出，众知不可敌，升屋而逃。

    Explanation

    584

    When the fire was lifted, it immediately rose up, and Mr. Lu fell into it. In an instant, the intense flames flew up, burning both Mr. Lu and the grass to ashes. His wife ran to save him but it was too late. Mr. Chen Ziyu, the scholar, told me this story and said, "Being stingy with small things leads to losing the bigger picture; one should take the example of the rich man. Harming others harms oneself; one should take the example of Mr. Lu." An old scholar from Taixing, Mr. He Xuan Tai, once had business in Suqian and rented a cart to travel. The driver of the cart had his face covered with knife marks, like a grid pattern. When asked about it, he replied, "I dare not hide it; ten years ago, I was a big thief. There was a rich family who married a new bride, and the wedding party was very grand. I went with seven other thieves to rob her. At midnight, we entered the room, and the new bride was young and beautiful, only sixteen or seventeen years old. She shouted, 'Do not frighten my husband; I will give you silver and money.' The others waited outside the window. They saw her go behind the bed as if she was taking something, and suddenly, a knife flew out, sticking into the door. The man also flew out with the knife. The others knew they could not fight her and climbed onto the roof to flee."

    585

    我稍后之，甫及屋山，为其所及，蹴以足，我一身不能动，哀号乞命。新妇以刀划我面，纵横各数十，乃曰：“今夕我花烛良辰，姑贷汝死。’提而掷诸墙外，幸得不死，乃改行焉。”张翁，山东人。某年六月间于村外纳凉，夜深将归寝，忽有人出自草间。视之，其佣奴之已死者也。叱之曰：“我待汝不薄，乃来祟我乎？”曰：“非也。小人执役冥中，今奉牒来拘主人，追念旧恩，故先来告。牒中共三十人，主人名在第一，我移置其末。日拘一人，可延一月，此即所以报也。”言已不见。

    Explanation

    585

    I followed shortly after, just reaching the roof ridge, when he caught up with me and kicked me with his foot. I was unable to move a single part of my body and cried out in despair, begging for mercy. The new wife used a knife to cut my face, making horizontal and vertical cuts, each about ten. She then said, "Today is my wedding night, so I will temporarily spare your life." She picked me up and threw me outside the wall. Fortunately, I did not die, and thus changed my behavior.

    Mr. Zhang, a man from Shandong, was cooling off outside the village during a certain year in June. Late at night, as he was about to go to bed, someone suddenly appeared from the grass. He looked at it and saw that it was the deceased servant. He scolded him and said, "I treated you not poorly, why are you haunting me now?" The servant replied, "No, it is not so. I serve in the underworld. Now I have a document to summon the master, in memory of old kindness, so I came to warn first." The document lists thirty people in total, with the master's name first on the list. I moved his name to the end. They will summon one person per day, extending for a month. This is how I repay the kindness." After speaking, he disappeared.

    586

    翁怃然曰：“我其死矣！”自念衣食粗足，婚嫁俱毕，死亦何憾。惟曾与某氏子为媒，此子孑然一身，贫无婚费，女氏恒有悔婚之意，我在故不敢言，我死奈何？明日，悉召诸子而语之，曰：“某氏之子，其父在日，曾假我钱八十万，以相信故无券也。今我老矣，久假不归，异日何面见故人于地下乎！”皆曰：“诺。”辇钱而归之，乃为故人子择日娶妇，告期于女氏。女氏无以拒，遂成婚。翁喜曰：“我事毕矣。”越月竟无恙。而其奴又来见，贺曰：“主人不死矣，冥中续有牒至，除去主人之名也。”

    Explanation

    586

    The old man sighed and said, "I am going to die!" Reflecting on his life, he thought, "My food and clothing are roughly sufficient, and my marriages are all completed. What regret would there be in dying?" However, he remembered that he had once acted as a matchmaker for a son of a certain family. This son was alone and poor, lacking the funds for marriage. The family of the girl always had the intention to back out of the marriage. Because he was alive, he dared not speak. What will happen after I die? The next day, he summoned all his sons and told them, "The son of a certain family, his father was alive, once borrowed eighty thousand taels of silver from me. Because of trust, there was no written contract. Now I am old, and the borrowed money has not been returned for a long time, how can I face my old friend in the afterlife?" They all said, "Yes." He sent the money back by carriage and arranged a wedding date for the son of the old friend, informing the family of the girl. The family of the girl had no reason to refuse, so the marriage was completed. The old man said happily, "My affairs are complete." After a month, he was still alive. And his servant came to see him, congratulating him, "The master is not going to die, there is a subsequent edict in the afterlife, removing the master's name."

    587

    苏城六门，惟盘门居民最少，城外又无市廛，荒凉殊甚，故苏谚谓之“冷水盘门”云。咸丰三年，金陵不守，姑苏亦戒严，各城门日落即闭，而盘门尤早。有某生者，于清明前数日出盘门，至乡间展墓，及归，门已闭矣。正踌躇间，有二人踵至，观其装束如公门中人，笑而问曰：“先生不得入矣，城外有居停也无？”告之曰：“无有。”二人曰：“我家不远，盍偕往一宿乎？”某生喜，随之行。行未半里，即至其家，同入书室中，曰：“君未夕食，得无饥乎？”乃出，以茶一瓯、点心两叠子来，殷殷劝食。食已，坐而共话，竟不言寝，室中亦无床榻。旋闻比舍人语喧杂，问何事，曰：“邻有病人，恐不起矣。

    Explanation

    587

    Suzhou has six gates, but only Pan Gate has the fewest residents. The area outside the gate is also devoid of shops and markets, making it extremely desolate. Hence, the local saying calls it "Cold Water Pan Gate." In the third year of the Xianfeng era (1853), Nanjing fell, and Suzhou also went on alert. All city gates were closed at sunset, but Pan Gate closed even earlier. A certain scholar left Pan Gate a few days before the Qingming Festival to visit his ancestral graves in the countryside. When he returned, the gate was already closed. While hesitating, two people arrived shortly after. Seeing their attire, they seemed to be officials. They smiled and asked, "Sir, you can't get in now. Do you have a place to stay outside the city?" The scholar replied, "No." They said, "Our home is not far. Why not come and stay for the night?" The scholar was pleased and followed them. They walked for half a li and arrived at their house. Together, they entered the study and said, "You haven't had your evening meal yet. Are you hungry?" They then brought out a bowl of tea and two trays of snacks, earnestly encouraging him to eat. After eating, they sat and chatted, but they never mentioned sleeping, and there were no beds or cots in the room. Soon, they heard people from neighboring houses talking loudly. The scholar asked what was happening, and they replied, "A neighbor has a sick person, and we fear he will not survive."

    588

    ”及人定时，一人袖中出文书，谓某生曰：“请先生呵气一口！”从之，其人仍纳诸袖中，起曰：“吾二人有公事，宜即去，先生留此，待天明入城可也。”又曰：“我等去时，与常人有异，然无与先生，勿怖也。”乃走出，至庭中，矫首顿足，其身顿长，俄顷之间，高过于屋，从屋上蹈腾而去。其一人从之，厥状亦然。某生骇极，不知所谓，未几即闻哭声，知病者死矣，此书室即死者之家也。有人入此室，见之疑为贼，又见案上茶瓯及叠子，曰：“曩者内室中失此物，尔所窃乎？”将执之，某生力辩，始免。天明，踉跄而归。某生所遇，殆即俗所谓无常者欤！其所持冥牒，必须生人呵气，此何理也？且亦安能尽得生人为之呵气乎？幽冥之事，盖不可晓矣。

    Explanation

    588

    At the appointed hour, a man drew a document from his sleeve and said to the scholar, "Please blow a breath into this!" The scholar complied, and the man then put the document back into his sleeve. He stood up and said, "We have official business to attend to and should leave immediately. You, sir, should stay here and enter the city at dawn." He added, "When we depart, we will differ from ordinary people, but you need not be afraid." They then walked out, reached the courtyard, stretched and stomped their feet, and their bodies suddenly grew taller. In the blink of an eye, they were taller than the house and leaped away from the roof. The other man followed, and his appearance was the same. The scholar was extremely frightened, not knowing what was happening. Soon after, he heard crying and realized that the patient had died. The study was the home of the deceased. Someone entered the room, saw them, and suspected they were thieves. They also noticed a teapot and a stack of books on the table and said, "The items in the inner room were lost earlier. Did you steal them?" They were about to arrest them, but the scholar strongly defended himself and managed to escape. At dawn, he staggered back home. The encounter the scholar had was likely the common folk's so-called "Mara," wasn't it? The document they held required a living person to blow upon it. What is the reason for this? And how could they possibly get all living people to blow on it? Matters of the underworld are indeed beyond comprehension.

    589

    高邮某君，曾为蜀中一县令。咸丰初，引疾归。一夕，独坐书室中，忽有人入室，请与偕往。问所之，曰：“四川也。”某君从之出，门有肩舆舁之行，其行如飞，俄顷即至江干，则已舣舟而待矣。登舟解维，转瞬已至九江。又舍舟而陆，由江西、湖北而至四川，计其为地，已数千里，而其为时则食顷也。既入城，至一公廨，导之坐。西厢有旧时长官某臬使、某太守在焉，皆颜色愁惨，问之不答。俄传王者出御殿，即召之入，问：“某狱，尔知之乎？”曰：“知之。

    Explanation

    589

    A gentleman from Gao You, who had once served as a county magistrate in Sichuan, retired due to illness during the early years of the Xianfeng Emperor's reign. One night, he was sitting alone in his study when suddenly someone entered the room and asked to accompany him on a journey. When he inquired about the destination, the person replied, "To Sichuan." The gentleman followed him out, and upon exiting the door, they were carried by a sedan chair, traveling at an incredible speed. In a moment, they arrived at the riverside, where a boat was already waiting. They boarded the boat and untied the ropes, and in an instant, they had reached Jiujiang. They then left the boat and traveled by land, passing through Jiangxi and Hubei, and finally arrived in Sichuan. The distance covered was several thousand miles, yet the entire journey took no more than the time it takes to eat a meal. Upon entering the city, they arrived at a government office, where they were led to sit. In the west wing, there were former officials, a certain governor and a certain prefect, all looking sad and silent when asked. Soon, it was announced that the king had emerged from the throne room and immediately summoned the gentleman in, asking, "Do you know about this case?" The gentleman replied, "Yes, I do."

    590

    ”盖此狱臬使与太守得贿，冤杀数人，某君力争不得，其引疾归，即以此也。乃具言本末，王者曰：“尔耿介可嘉。”命仍送之回。又闻传臬使与太守入，诘责甚厉，回顾此二人者，皆缩小如婴儿。须臾，有槛车载二人出。某君亦出。遇一友，故与同官蜀中者，友云：“我即将之官，不及与故人叙契阔，奈何？”问：“君居何官？”曰：“我扬州府城隍。”指槛车中人曰：“某某之狱，即使我治之。我不得久留，然我与君亦非久别。明年七月某日，君为某处城隍，我两人仍为同官也。”言已别去。某君遵旧路回家，则死已逾日，至是复苏。以其事告人，自云不久人世矣，人犹未之信。至明岁及期，果无疾而卒。

    590

    “Regarding this case, the provincial censor and the prefect accepted bribes, wrongfully killing several people. A certain official argued strongly but was unsuccessful, and thus he resigned due to illness. This was the reason for his retirement. He then recounted the entire story. The ruler said, ‘Your integrity is praiseworthy.’ He ordered him to be sent back. Later, it was heard that the provincial censor and the prefect were summoned, and they were reprimanded severely. When they looked back, the two men had shrunk to the size of babies. In a moment, a cart with a cage appeared, carrying the two out. The official also emerged. He met a friend who had once served with him in Sichuan. The friend said, ‘I am about to take up a new post and will not be able to catch up with old friends. What shall I do?’ The official asked, ‘What position do you hold?’ The friend replied, ‘I am the city god of Yangzhou Prefecture.’ Pointing to the two men in the cage, he said, ‘These are the cases I handled. I cannot stay here long, but we will still be colleagues. Next year on a certain day in July, you will be the city god of a certain place, and we will still be colleagues.’ After saying this, he left. The official followed the old road home, but he had already been dead for several days. At that point, he revived. He told others about this, and he said he would not be long for this world. To his surprise, the following year on the appointed day, he died peacefully without illness.”

    591

    杭城无妓馆，间有女闾，非有导者不能入其门，故杭谚谓之“铁门槛”。宁波某生喜冶游，而性颇长厚。以应试至省城，试期尚早，枯坐无聊，偶动寻芳之兴，倩友人导至一家。室有少妇坐而刺绣，一媪旁坐，问之则姑妇也。某视少妇，肤色如玉，瘦腰纤趾，盈盈可爱。乃出金畀媪，使治酒食狎饮。既久，烛将见跋，其友辞去。媪与少妇导客入房，媪亦避出。某引少妇并坐，灯前抚摩谑浪，将缓其结束，妇羞甚，至于泣下。某骇曰：“尔为此态何也？”妇曰：“我父我兄咸列胶庠，我舅我夫亦诸生也。以皆早世，致我茕茕弱质一旦至此，追念逝者，能不伤心！”某闻之，亦凄然，曰：“然则尔何故甘心为此？今几次矣？”妇曰：“我姑无食，屡劝我作此事，重违姑意，勉强相从。今才初次耳。”某呼之曰：“嫂既欲守贞，何敢玷辱！”乃出数金赠之，曰：“所携无多，聊以此佐堂前甘脆之奉，吾去矣。

    Explanation

    591

    Hangzhou has no brothels. Occasionally there are courtesans, but without an introducer, one cannot enter their door. Therefore, Hangzhou's proverb calls it the "iron threshold." A certain man from Ningbo, who enjoyed pleasure-seeking, came to the provincial capital for the examination. The exam period was still early, and he was sitting idly, feeling bored. He suddenly felt a desire to seek pleasure and asked a friend to guide him to a house. The room had a young woman sitting and embroidering, with an old woman sitting beside her. When asked, they were revealed to be mother and daughter. The man looked at the young woman, whose skin was like jade, with a slender waist and delicate feet, charming and lovely. He then gave gold to the old woman to prepare food and wine for intimate drinking. After a while, the candle was about to burn down, and his friend bid farewell. The old woman and the young woman guided the guest into the room, and the old woman also left. The man invited the young woman to sit beside him, and in the lamp light, he fondled and teased her, about to loosen her clothes. The young woman was very embarrassed, even to the point of crying. The man asked, "Why do you behave like this?" The young woman replied, "My father and brothers all ranked among the scholars, and my uncle and husband were also scholars. Because they all died young, leaving me, a weak and lonely girl, in this situation. To think of the deceased, how could I not be heartbroken?" The man heard this and was also moved, saying, "Then why do you willingly do this? How many times have you done this?" The young woman said, "My mother has no food, and she often urges me to do this. To avoid going against my mother's wishes, I勉强相从. Today is only my first time." The man called out, "Since you wish to remain chaste, how dare I defile you?" He then gave her several pieces of gold, saying, "What I carry is not much, just to supplement the sweet and savory offerings for the elders. I will leave now."

    592

    ”即从后门出，而夜色迷，不辨途径。时大乱之后，城中亦多荒僻之所，躐跋久之，忽见一小屋，微露灯光，中有讽诵声，因叩门问途。门启，延入，则一叟也。一少年方伏案作文，叟自言某姓，少年为其子，“今科主试者某公，吾素识之，故出此题，使吾子揣摩其所好而为之，然犹未得也。”某视其文极佳，乃曰：“贤郎之文，我所不及。叟犹以为未邪？”叟曰：“然。子如不信，吾试为改之。

    Explanation

    592

    Immediately, he went out through the back door, and the night was dimly lit, making it impossible to recognize the path. It was during a time of great turmoil, and the city also had many neglected and remote areas. After trudging for a long time, he suddenly saw a small house, barely revealing a light, with a sound of recitation inside. He then knocked on the door to ask for directions. The door opened, and he was invited in; it turned out to be an old man. A young man was sitting at a desk writing. The old man introduced himself by saying his surname, and the young man was his son. "The chief examiner of this year's examination, Mr. [name], I have known him for a long time, so I set this question to make my son guess what he likes and write accordingly, but it's still not enough," the old man said. He thought his son's writing was excellent and said, "My son's writing is beyond my ability. The old man still thinks it's not good enough?" The old man replied, "Yes. If you don't believe me, let me try to revise it."

    593

    ”即援笔就案头点窜百许字，曰：“如此庶乎可矣！”某再读之，不胜叹服，曰：“叟真斫输老手也。”吟讽不置。叟笑曰：“君既爱之，敬以奉赠何如？”某喜甚，即于灯下读之，疲极假寐，不觉酣眠。及醒，则朝暾已上矣。乃在土阜之下，初无屋宇，知所遇者鬼也。因思昨所游妇家，与叟同姓，得无即其舅与夫邪！反至妇家询之，面貌符合，为之悚然。归取数十金赠其姑，且谓之曰：“尔妇贞洁，勿强以苟且之行。”及入闱，竟遇是题，即录其文于卷，榜发魁也。复措千金畀其家。

    Explanation

    593

    The scholar immediately took up his pen and revised about a hundred words at his desk, saying, "Like this, it might be acceptable!" I read it again and was deeply impressed, exclaiming, "This old man is truly a master of revision!" I continued to recite and praise it. The old man smiled and said, "Since you love it, may I respectfully offer it to you as a gift?" I was very pleased and immediately read it by the lamp. Exhausted, I dozed off and soon fell into a deep sleep. When I woke up, the morning sun had already risen. I found myself at the foot of a hillock, with no house or shelter in sight, realizing that I had encountered a spirit. I then thought about the family of the woman I had visited yesterday, who shared the same surname as the old man—could he be her uncle or husband? I returned to the woman's family and inquired, and the description matched, filling me with dread. I gave her mother several dozen gold pieces and told her, "Your daughter is chaste; do not force her into indecent behavior." When I entered the examination hall, I unexpectedly encountered this question and promptly wrote the text in my scroll. When the list of winners was announced, I was the top scorer. I then gave a thousand gold pieces to the family.

    594

    苏州山塘有甲乙二人，甲素耿直，而乙则流荡好狭邪之游。一日薄暮，会饮于友人所，乙先归。行至闻德桥，见一小家微露灯光。近视，则自大门至于内室，门户尽启，而不见一人。异之，姑入而觇焉。经堂入奥，有一女子坐床上，华容盛，姣丽无双。乙入，女子邀与并坐，且曰：“夜行良苦，此间衾枕见成，何不少憩？”为代脱外衣，搭施架上。乙甫将就枕，忽有执灯而至者，女子惊起避之，其人即甲也。甲见乙痴坐桥栏，问坐此何事。乙曰：“君坏我好事不小。”甲笑曰：“此桥上有何好事？”乙始悟身坐闻德桥栏，衣服即在身旁，向之所见，一时消灭，知为遇鬼，踉跄而归。使非甲至，乙其危哉。

    Explanation

    594

    In Suzhou, on Shantang Street, there were two men named A and B. A was inherently honest and straightforward, while B was promiscuous and enjoyed licentious activities. One evening, they had a drinking party at a friend's place, and B returned home first. As he walked to Wende Bridge, he saw a small house with faint light visible from the outside. Upon closer inspection, he noticed that all the doors from the main gate to the inner room were open, but no one was inside. Surprised, he went in to take a look. Inside, he saw a woman sitting on the bed, with a beautiful appearance and abundant charms, and she was exceptionally beautiful. The woman invited B to sit next to her and said, "Night travel is indeed hard. The bedding here is ready-made. Why not rest a little?" She helped B take off his outer clothes and hung them on a clothes rack. Just as B was about to lie down, suddenly someone arrived with a lamp. The woman was startled and avoided the person, who turned out to be A. A saw B sitting dazedly on the bridge railing and asked, "What are you doing here?" B replied, "You spoiled my good time." A laughed and said, "What good time is there on this bridge?" B then realized that he was sitting on Wende Bridge, with his clothes right beside him. The woman and the house he had seen earlier had disappeared. He understood that he had encountered a ghost and staggered back home. If not for A's arrival, B would have been in grave danger.

    595

    吴江顾生，因应试至苏，寓吉利桥畔。其旁有茶肆曰锦凤楼，饭后无事，偶往吃茶，至则坐客满矣。惟屋隅一卓子，止有老妪偕少妇共坐，无他客，顾往参坐。妪即与顾闲话，久之颇浃洽。妪曰：“此间无味，郎君如有兴，何不同至我家，当烹佳茗相待。”顾欣然从之。既至，导之登楼，楼上陈设亦颇精雅。其时为道光季年，鸦片烟已盛行矣。榻上烟具存焉，请试尝之。辞以不惯，妪曰：“偶然游戏，何伤乎？”命少妇烧烟奉客。顾甫登榻，妪脱其屦，曰：“任意眠坐，无拘束也。老妇有事且去。”妪去未久，闻楼下叩门声甚厉，少妇遽起下楼，生疑焉。施从其后以下，ㄣ伏户后。少妇启门，则有男子三十余人然而入，问人在何处，少妇曰：“在楼上。”乃悉登楼，顾因乘间逸去。盖苏俗往往有以妇女为&lt；囗繇&gt；者，少年子弟误入其中，必尽取其服物，且迫使书借券，或数十千，或数百千，乃始释之。谚谓之仙人跳云。

    Explanation

    595

    A Man from Wujiang

    A man from Wujiang, named Gu, came to Suzhou to take the imperial examination and stayed at the side of Jili Bridge. Nearby was a teahouse called Jin Feng Lou. After dinner, with nothing to do, he occasionally went to drink tea. Upon arriving, he found the seats were full. Only one table in the corner of the room, occupied by an old woman and a young woman, had no other guests. Gu sat down to join them. The old woman talked idly with him, and they got along quite well. The old woman said, "This place is boring. If you are interested, why not come to my house? I will prepare good tea for you." Gu happily agreed. After arriving, she led him upstairs, where the decorations were also very elegant. At that time, it was the late years of the Daoguang reign, and opium smoking had become widespread. Opium paraphernalia was present on the bed. She invited him to try it. He declined, saying he was not accustomed to it. The old woman said, "Just for fun. What harm will it do?" She ordered the young woman to prepare the opium for the guest. As soon as he sat down on the bed, the old woman removed his shoes and said, "You can sleep or sit as you please, there are no restrictions. I have something to do and will leave." Not long after, he heard a loud knocking at the door downstairs. The young woman quickly got up and went downstairs. Gu became suspicious and followed behind her downstairs, hiding behind the door. The young woman opened the door, and then more than thirty men rushed in. They asked where the person was. The young woman said, "He is upstairs." They all went upstairs. Gu took the opportunity to escape. In Suzhou, it was common for women to be used in a certain way. Young men often mistakenly entered these places. They would take all his belongings and force him to write a promissory note, sometimes tens of thousands, sometimes hundreds of thousands, only then would they let him go. This is known as the "Immortal Jump."

    596

    苏城海宏寺，亦古刹也。有某甲住修仙巷，相距甚近，与寺僧时相往来。一岁冬日，甲晨起无事，访僧闲话，入其门，无人门焉者，因入。至最后一院落中，闻僧在隔院呼取剪刀，敫然应者女子声也。从隔子中窃窥之，则见僧披大红绉纱狐白氅衣，立梅花下，有粉白黛黑者十余人，环列其侧。大惊趋出。甫至家，而僧使人以一盒馈，启视，则白金一百两，利刃一柄。甲会其意，受金反刃，并以纸画一瓶复之。后遂不敢复往。此事在咸丰之季。及同治中，寺僧以事得罪，官尽逐之，改寺为管粮通判署。甲始与人言其事焉。

    Explanation

    596

    The Sea Hong Temple in Suzhou is also an ancient temple. There was a man named Mr. A who lived in Xiu Xian Lane, very close to the temple, and he frequently visited the temple monk. One winter day, Mr. A got up early with nothing to do and went to visit the monk for casual conversation. He entered the temple gate, but no one was at the door, so he went in. He reached the last courtyard and heard the monk calling for a pair of scissors from the next courtyard. Suddenly, a woman's voice responded. From behind a partition, he peeped and saw the monk wearing a large red silk fox-white cloak, standing under the plum blossoms, with more than ten people with white faces and black eyebrows arranged in a circle around him. Greatly startled, he hurried out. Just as he arrived home, the monk sent someone with a box as a gift. When he opened it, he saw one hundred taels of white gold and a sharp knife. Mr. A understood his intention, accepted the gold, returned the knife, and wrapped it in paper and put it back in the box. He then never dared to go back. This incident occurred during the late years of the Xianfeng era. During the Tongzhi era, the temple monk was disgraced for some reason, and the officials expelled him, changing the temple into a grain commissioner's office. Mr. A then told people about the incident.

    597

    乌程郑梦白先生祖琛，道光中以藩司居忧，甲辰岁服阕入觐，将至京师，宿于长新店。甫入室，有客求见，视其名刺，则故人也。欣然延入，坐谈良久而去。未几，外报此客又求见。疑其尚有未尽之辞，复出见之，则殊无所言，又坐良久而去。先生倚隐囊方谋小憩，而外报此客又至，厌而辞焉。客固以请，不得已，又见之，谓曰：“君数来，将何以教我邪？”客曰：“君视我谁也？”视之，则竟非前客，面目清癯，修髯及腹，殆神仙中人也，悚然异之，不知所云。客曰：“君第一次所见者，某人也；第二次见者，非某人，即仆也。仆欲有言于君，故幻其形以求见。又自惟交浅言深，故欲言而仍止。退而思之，此事所系甚大，不敢不言，故又来求见也。”问将何言，客曰：“仆乃天狐也，窃闻天曹之议，世间劫运将至。

    Explanation

    597

    The Mr. Zheng Mengbai of Wucheng, during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, was a high-ranking official (藩司) who was in mourning (居忧). In the year Jia Chen (甲辰), after completing his mourning period (服阕), he set out to present himself before the emperor (入觐) and was about to reach Beijing (京师) when he stopped at Changxin Village (长新店). Upon entering his room (甫入室), a guest sought to see him (有客求见). Seeing the guest's visiting card (视其名刺), he recognized the guest as an old friend (则故人也). He warmly invited the guest in (欣然延入) and they sat and talked for a long time before the guest left (坐谈良久而去). Shortly after (未几), the guest sought to see him again (外报此客又求见). He suspected that the guest might still have something to say (疑其尚有未尽之辞) and went out to see him again (复出见之). However, the guest had nothing to say (则殊无所言) and sat for a long time before leaving (又坐良久而去). Mr. Zheng was leaning on a small pillow (隐囊) and planning to take a brief rest (方谋小憩) when the guest sought to see him again (外报此客又至). Feeling tired and declining (厌而辞焉), he eventually saw the guest (客固以请，不得已，又见之). The guest asked, "Why do you keep coming? What do you wish to teach me?" (君数来，将何以教我邪). The guest replied, "Who do you think I am?" (君视我谁也?). Upon seeing him, Mr. Zheng realized that the guest was not the previous one, but a man with a gaunt face, long beard reaching his abdomen, who seemed like a being from the realm of immortals (殆神仙中人也). He was startled and felt strange, not knowing what to say (悚然异之，不知所云). The guest continued, "The first time you saw me, it was someone else; the second time you saw me, it was not that person but me (仆也). I wanted to tell you something (仆欲有言于君), so I transformed my form to seek you out (故幻其形以求见). I thought to myself that it was inappropriate to speak deeply with someone I barely knew (又自惟交浅言深), so I wanted to speak but still held back (故欲言而仍止). Afterward, I reflected that this matter was of great importance (此事所系甚大), and I dared not remain silent, so I came to see you again (不敢不言，故又来求见也)." Mr. Zheng asked, "What do you wish to say?" (问将何言). The guest replied, "I am a celestial fox (天狐), and I have heard the discussions of the heavenly bureaucracy (天曹) that a great calamity (劫运) is about to befall the world (世间劫运将至)."

    598

    君此次入都，不久即膺节钺。将来数百万生灵之命，皆在君手，千万留意。言已不见，先生为敞罔者久之。是岁，即拜陕藩之命，由陕藩迁滇抚，俄移节粤西，而洪秀全之乱起。方其萌芽之始，犹可扑灭，先生思客言，不敢轻举，遂以因循酿祸，毒流海内。此狐既不惜一再求见，何不直言正告，而为此模棱之辞，使人误会其意欤？殆亦由阳九百六厄运，固不可免乎？或曰京师宣武门外有屋一区，旧有狐祟，先生居之则寂然。及去，祟如故。或恚而诘狐，狐曰：”数百万生灵皆送其手，吾曹能不畏之？“斯语也，盖即由此事传讹，而非其实。余所闻于乡人者，不尔也，先通奉公曾主先生家，先兄壬甫太守又曾入其幕府，其子姓辈有为余同年生者，余于道光中尝一见之。先生固长者，而亦素有吏才，晚任封疆，遂膺严谴，且为世诟病，亦见疆吏之难为也。

    Explanation

    598

    This time, the translation will be more polished and natural in English, taking into account the insights from the pre-drafting research:

    Translation:

    Upon this occasion of your entering the capital, you will soon be appointed to a high military or administrative post. The fate of millions of lives will rest in your hands, so please be extremely cautious. After the words were spoken, the master was lost in thought for a long time. In that year, he was appointed as the governor of Shaanxi, then transferred to Yunnan, and soon after moved to Guangxi. Meanwhile, the rebellion led by Hong Xiuquan began to rise. At its early stages, it could still have been suppressed. The master, reflecting on the advice given, hesitated to act rashly and thus procrastinated, leading to disaster that spread throughout the land. If this fox was willing to seek an audience repeatedly, why not speak directly and clearly, rather than using ambiguous language that could lead to misunderstanding? Perhaps it was also due to the inevitable misfortune of the "nine calamities and six disasters," which could not be avoided? Someone said that there was a house outside the Xuanwu Gate in the capital, which had been plagued by a fox spirit. When the master lived there, the disturbances ceased. But after he left, the spirit returned. Someone who was angry questioned the fox, and the fox replied, "Millions of lives are all in his hands, how could we not fear him?" This statement is likely a distorted version of the story, not the actual events. What I heard from my fellow villagers is different. My respected elder once served in the master's household, and my elder brother, who was a magistrate, also served in his entourage. Among his descendants, there were those who passed the same imperial examination with me during the Daoguang era, and I met him once. The master was indeed a man of virtue and had always possessed administrative talent. In his later years, when he was appointed to govern a large territory, he was severely punished and criticized by the world, illustrating the difficulties of being a governor of a region.

    599

    宝应城东门中有屋一区，故刘氏屋也，余同年生王文勤公幼时读书其家。偶食花椒，见其青葱可爱，乃以一枝插地上，曰：“若吾他年读书有成，则此枝当活。”后椒果活，阅三十余年，竟成大树，高过屋檐。文勤以道光丙午登贤书，庚戌成进士，同治中由编修擢浙臬，俄移粤藩，遂迁闽抚。而此屋亦归于王氏，其树犹在，盛于在刘氏时。佥曰：“此王氏之祥也。”久之，或言椒不留子，其苞一启子即堕落，恐不利于子姓，乃议伐之。余长女为文勤之仲子妇，力阻之不可。未及二年，文勤自台湾归，卒于福州；妻刘夫人先文勤二月而卒：其效可睹矣。

    599

    In the east gate of Baoying City, there is a house, originally belonging to the Liu family. Wenqin, a prominent figure, once ate Sichuan pepper and saw it was fresh and lovely. He then planted one branch in the ground and said, "If I succeed in my studies in the future, then this branch will surely grow." Indeed, the Sichuan pepper grew, and after more than thirty years, it finally grew into a big tree, taller than the roof.

    Wenqin passed the provincial examination in 1826 (the year of Bingwu in Daoguang era) and the metropolitan examination in 1840 (the year of Gengxu). During the Tongzhi era, he was promoted from the position of compiler to the governor of Zhejiang, soon after transferred to the governor of Guangdong, and later became the governor of Fujian. And this house also came to belong to the Wang family. The tree is still there, even more prosperous than when it belonged to the Liu family. Everyone said, "This is a good omen for the Wang family."

    After a long time, someone said that Sichuan pepper does not produce seeds. Its pod opens and the seeds fall out, fearing that it might be harmful to the descendants. They then discussed cutting it down. My eldest daughter is the wife of Wenqin's second son, and she strongly opposed it, but could not stop them. Not even two years later, Wenqin returned from Taiwan and died in Fuzhou. His wife, Lady Liu, died two months before him. The effects are clear to see.

    600

    律曰：妻在，以妾为妻，杖九十。若妻已不在，则律无明文，似所不禁。然余亲串中，有妻亡之后欲以妾为妻者。其家欲得余舅氏姚平泉先生一言，以决斯议，许以千金为寿，先生竟不许也。余在河南时，闻有陶观察者妻亡，有四妾。一妾年长矣，内政悉主之，内外无间言。观察将以为妻。既有成议，命缝人为制命服。服成，观察使其弟亲赍至内室，以授其妾，盖重之也。妾方坐，竟不为起，睨而言曰：“置几上。”弟出大怒，谓其兄曰：“此人甫得志，无礼至此。异日尚可问乎？兄以为妻，弟不以为嫂。”力持不可，竟毁前议。然此妾素谦下，不失礼于人，不知此日何以如是，殆不克膺受是福，故鬼神夺其魄也。余家有一婢，始嫁一贵人为妾，不数年正位中闺，服一品之服。

    Explanation

    600

    The legal code states: "If a man has a wife and designates a concubine as his wife, he shall be sentenced to ninety strokes of the rod." If the legal wife is deceased, the law does not explicitly prohibit designating a concubine as the wife. However, in my family or social circle, there was a case where someone wanted to designate a concubine as his wife after the legal wife's death. The family sought the opinion of my maternal uncle, Mr. Yao Pingquan, to decide the matter, offering him a thousand gold pieces as a reward, but he refused. When I was in Henan, I heard that a certain Mr. Tao, an official, had four concubines after his wife's death. One of the concubines was older and managed all household affairs, and there were no complaints from inside or outside the household. The official intended to designate this concubine as his wife. Once the decision was made, he ordered a seamstress to make the official robes for the concubine. When the robes were completed, the official sent his younger brother to deliver them personally to the concubine, indicating his respect for her. The concubine, seated, did not rise to greet her brother-in-law, instead glancing at him and saying, "Put it on the table." The younger brother left in anger, telling his older brother, "This person has just gained power and is being so disrespectful. Can we still ask him anything in the future? If you consider her your wife, I do not consider her my sister-in-law." He strongly opposed the decision, ultimately leading to the cancellation of the plan. However, this concubine was usually humble and courteous, so it is unclear why she acted this way, possibly because she could not bear such a great honor, and thus the spirits took her soul. In my household, there was a maid who was initially married to a nobleman as a concubine but soon became his legal wife, wearing the robes of the first rank.

    601

    此婢乃六合人，咸丰中六合失守，随其父母至苏州，入难民局，内子姚夫人买得之，才七八岁耳。后其父母亦不知在亡，而此婢每卜问其父母安否，盖在婢妾中为有人心者，宜其有是福欤！苏州浒墅关之西乡，有巨蛇出没河干，人多见之，莫敢捕也。光绪庚辰夏，其地之人多生疡者，或曰：“是中蛇毒也。”乃募能捕之者。于城中得三丐，甲其师也，乙、丙皆其徒，索钱甚多，乡人醵与之，乃往。乙、丙荷一以从，既至，探得蛇窟。甲命乙、丙分立左右，而自启其，中贮蜈蚣无数，甲尽取食之。须臾，自顶至踵皆肿，甲闭目运气，久之其肿处皆消，惟右手食指与中指则大几如股。即以此两指探入窟中，已而用力拔出，乙、丙亦各曳其肘以助之。指出，蛇随之出，则已毙矣，犹啮甲指不释。乙、丙去其蛇，以药水洗之，两指旋复故。蛇长丈许，粗倍人臂，其色黑暗如炭，聚薪焚之，臭闻数里。此三丐者，其技亦神矣。

    Explanation

    601

    This maid is from Liuhe. During the Xianfeng era, Liuhe fell, and she came to Suzhou with her parents, entering the refugee relief station. My wife, Mrs. Yao, bought her when she was only seven or eight years old. Her parents' fate remains unknown, but she often inquires about their safety. She is one of the few with a human heart among maids and concubines, and it is fitting that she has such good fortune. In the western countryside of Huxi Guan, a large snake roamed along the riverbank, and many people saw it but dared not capture it. During the Gengchen year of the Guangxu era, many people in the area developed sores. Someone said, "This is due to snake poison." They encouraged those who could catch it. In the city, they found three beggars: A is the master, and B and C are his disciples. They asked for a lot of money, which the villagers pooled together to give them. After arriving, they discovered the snake's den. A ordered B and C to stand on either side, and opened his basket himself, which contained countless centipedes. A ate them all. Soon, from head to toe, he was swollen. A closed his eyes and regulated his breath, and after a while, the swelling subsided, but only his right index and middle fingers became as thick as thighs. He then used these two fingers to probe into the den and, with force, pulled it out. B and C also pulled his elbows to help. As his fingers came out, the snake followed and was already dead, but it still bit A's fingers and wouldn't let go. B and C removed the snake and washed it with medicinal water, and his two fingers soon returned to normal. The snake was about a zhang long and twice as thick as a human arm, its color dark as charcoal. They gathered firewood and burned it, and the smell spread for several li. These three beggars' skills were also divine.

    602

    上海有某氏女，自幼从邻媪学绣，呼之以乾阿{弥女}.及年既长，媪犹以女视之，每小疾，痛爱惜，调护过于其母。女偶病，月事不行，媪甚忧之，语其母曰：“沪上无良医。天下之良医，无过于西洋人。西人于城外某所辟一馆，施医药，盍往求治乎？”母然其言。即使媪与俱在，赁肩舆二以行，辰而往，申而反。是夕，女即雉经而死。母哭之恸，媪亦哭之恸，然母甚疑焉。密呼舆者问之，舆者曰：“其日甫出城，媪即与女改坐东洋小车，使吾曹待于城闺，不知其所之也。”母益疑，乃使舆者物色推东洋车者而问之，则曰：“媪与女乘吾车，至一家，有小门。叩之，一妪出，手执菩提子，口诵佛号，导之入。即予吾钱，麾使去，不知其后如何也。”转展推求，卒无端倪。盖沪上恒有密室，为男女幽会之所。狡童淫女，白昼宣淫，又广布雉媒，使相罗致。疑此女堕入其中，以致失身，故惭而自尽云。

    Explanation

    602

    A woman from a certain family in Shanghai, having learned embroidery from a neighboring old woman since childhood, was affectionately called "乾阿{弥女}" by her. When she grew older, the old woman still treated her like a child, showing excessive care and concern for her, even more so than her own mother. Once, the girl fell ill, and her menstrual cycle ceased. The old woman was deeply worried and told the girl’s mother, “There are no good doctors in Shanghai. The best doctors in the world are the Westerners. The Westerners have established a clinic in a certain place outside the city, where they provide medical treatment. Why don’t we go there to seek a cure?” The mother agreed. They immediately arranged for the old woman to accompany the girl, hiring two sedan chairs for the journey. They left in the morning and returned in the afternoon. That evening, the girl hanged herself. Her mother wept bitterly, and the old woman wept bitterly as well, but the mother was deeply suspicious. She secretly called the sedan chair bearers to question them. They replied, “On that day, just as we were leaving the city, the old woman and the girl switched to an East Asian small carriage, leaving us to wait at the city gate, and we did not know where they went.” The mother’s suspicions grew, so she instructed the bearers to investigate the driver of the East Asian carriage. The driver said, “The old woman and the girl rode my carriage to a house with a small gate. When we knocked, an old woman came out, holding a bodhi seed and reciting Buddhist mantras, leading them inside. She then paid me and dismissed us, and I do not know what happened afterward.” Despite their efforts to investigate, they found no clues. It is said that in Shanghai, there are often secret rooms where men and women meet for illicit encounters. Dishonest youths and promiscuous girls openly engage in sexual activities during the day, and they widely use matchmakers to arrange such meetings. It is suspected that this girl fell into one of these secret rooms, lost her chastity, and, overcome with shame, took her own life.

    603

    苏城之初复也，有贫士避寇而归，孑然一身，无所栖止，恒寄宿于相识之家。忽有延之使教其子者，喜而就之，始至亦甚相得。未及一月，其主人语士曰：“明日吾家有事，而屋舍狭小，虽书室亦须洒扫，以待宾客，愿先生暂归一日。”士曰：“诺。”仍寄宿于所识之家，越日而反。已而又将一月，主人语之如前，士不得已从之。已而又将一月，主人语之又如前，士乃实告曰：“某在城中，固无栖泊之所，曩者寄宿所识家耳。然是家亦无余屋，吾一往再往，实非所便。此书室既合让客，则无论何地，但可容一榻，虽庑下不辞。”主人踌躇久之，曰：“吾屋狭小，先生所知也。无已，则后有一室，素来积贮薪蒸，今稍粪除为先生布席于彼，可乎？”士曰：“甚善。

    Explanation

    603

    When Suzhou first returned to peace, a poor scholar fled from bandits and returned home, alone and without a place to stay, constantly lodging with acquaintances. Suddenly, someone invited him to teach their son, and he was delighted to accept. At first, they got along very well. Before a month had passed, the host told the scholar, "Tomorrow, there is something happening in my family, and the house is small. Even the study room needs to be cleaned to welcome guests. I hope you can return home for a day." The scholar said, "Yes." He continued to stay as a guest in the homes of acquaintances and returned the next day. After another month, the host told him the same thing, and the scholar had no choice but to comply. After another month, the host told him the same thing again. The scholar then truthfully said, "I am in the city and have no place to stay. Before, I stayed as a guest in the homes of acquaintances. But that house also has no extra rooms. I went once and then again, and it was not convenient. Since this study room should be given to the guest, no matter where, as long as it can accommodate a bed, even if it's under the eaves, I won't refuse." The host hesitated for a long time and said, "My house is small, as you know. If there is no other way, then there is another room. It has always been used for storing firewood and kindling.
