Now it has been cleaned up and can be used for you to sleep on. Is that acceptable?" The scholar said, "Very good."

    604

    ”是日初昏，主人即导之入其室，且语之曰：“先生扃户酣眠，或有所闻，切勿窥观，彼此非便。”士谨诺之，而转展不成寐。及乙夜，便闻外间人语喧杂，不知何所作。私出窥之，见中堂陈设一新，其正中安一榻，榻有小几，左右各设ブ枕。主人及其眷属，又有外来宾客甚众，皆鹄立于堂，若有所伺候者。良久，乃闻曰：“至矣，至矣。”众皆趋出待于庭。又良久，乃见一客自外入，肥黑多须，装束诡异，径登堂，坐榻之左畔。旋有一女，艳服出自西房，视之，即主人之次女也。女向客扌及地而拜，拜已登榻右畔，与客并肩而坐。于是主人及其眷属及众宾客，皆以次入拜，客与女高坐受之，略不答礼。拜已，序立两旁。旋有数人舁一案至，其上有饮食之物，凡数十品。主人奉一器跪进客，主妇奉一器跪进女，于是堂上立者皆跪。客与女略尝即止。又以一器进如前，凡进十余品，止不进，殆由客止之也。前数人仍舁案出。

    Explanation

    604

    That evening, just after dusk, the host led him into his room and said, "Please lock the door and sleep deeply, Mr. Scholar. If you hear anything, do not peek or look, as it would be inappropriate for both of us." The scholar nodded respectfully and then found himself unable to fall asleep due to restlessness. By midnight, he began to hear loud conversations outside, unsure of what was happening. Curiously, he peered out and saw the main hall newly arranged, with a couch placed centrally, a small table beside it, and pillows on either side. The host, his family, and many guests from outside were all standing upright like cranes in the hall, as if waiting for something. After a long while, they heard someone say, "Here he is, here he is." Everyone rushed out to the courtyard to await him. After another long wait, a guest entered from outside. He was stout, dark-skinned, and had a thick beard, dressed in an unusual manner. He walked directly into the hall and sat on the left side of the couch. Soon, a woman in splendid attire emerged from the western room. Upon seeing her, the scholar recognized her as the host's second daughter. She bowed deeply to the guest, then ascended to the right side of the couch, sitting beside him. The host, his family, and all the guests then entered one by one to bow. The guest and the woman sat high and received their bows, barely reciprocating with courtesy. After the bows, everyone stood in order on both sides. Then, several people carried a table into the hall, which was laden with various dishes, numbering in the dozens. The host knelt and presented a dish to the guest, while the hostess did the same for the woman. Everyone standing in the hall knelt as well. The guest and the woman tasted a little and then stopped. Another dish was presented as before, and this continued for over ten dishes, until the guest stopped them. The table was then carried out again.

    605

    客起女亦起，客携女手入东房。久之，淫亵之声达于外，主人及众客咸屏气待之。约两时许，客与女携手出，客装束如初，女则乱头粗服矣。仍坐榻上，主人主妇又跪进一瓯，不知为酒为茗Η也。客与女饮毕。皆起，客出，女跪送于堂，客掖之起；主人及众客跪送于庭，则不顾也。客去，乃始哄然而入。士惧为所见，潜入后室卧，不知其后如何。明日出视，则一切如旧，昨所陈设皆不见矣。士私计此非善地，越十许日，托故辞去。数月后又访之，则其家亦迁徙，不知所之矣。此盖所谓邪教者，但不知其名目，且踪迹诡秘，地方长吏无从知之，宜不能禁也。孙君长龄，字寿伯，乃补山相国文靖公之曾孙。与余家有连，故自幼相熟也。其生前即为冥官。每至冥中治事，辄瞑然如睡，或一宿或再宿而醒，问所事，不言。

    Explanation

    605

    A guest rose, and the woman rose as well. The guest took the woman's hand and led her into the east room. After a while, lewd sounds reached the outside, and the host and guests all held their breath in anticipation. After about two hours, the guest and the woman emerged together, the guest dressed as he had been, but the woman appeared disheveled and in plain clothes. They sat back on the couch, and the host and hostess knelt to offer a cup, but it was unclear whether it was wine or tea. The guest and the woman drank, and then they all rose. The guest left, and the woman knelt to see him off at the hall, the guest helping her up. The host and guests knelt to see her off at the courtyard, but they ignored her. When the guest departed, the others began to enter in a loud, boisterous manner. The scholar feared being seen and secretly entered the back room to lie down, unsure of what would happen afterward. The next day, when he looked out, everything was as it had been, and the items from the previous day were nowhere to be seen. The scholar privately thought this was not a good place and, after about ten days, left for a reason. Several months later, when he visited again, the family had also moved, and their whereabouts were unknown. This is likely what is referred to as a heretical sect, but its name and secretive nature made it impossible for local officials to know about it, hence they could not suppress it. Sun Junzhang, courtesy name Shoubo, was the great-grandson of Sun Yujing, known posthumously as Wenjing Gong. He was acquainted with the scholar's family from a young age. Before his death, Sun Junzhang served as an official in the underworld. Whenever he went to handle matters in the underworld, he would appear as if asleep, sometimes sleeping for one night or two before waking. When asked about his duties, he would not speak.

    606

    道光二十九年十二月十一日，孝和睿皇后崩，时江、浙未奉明文，而部中蓝印文书先至，咸知国有大丧。然其时成皇帝春秋亦高，群情拟议，未知其审。寿伯乃以《国策》触龙称赵太后之例，微语人曰：“媪也。”是夕至冥中，大受棰楚，责其漏泄。及醒，两股青紫，卧月余乃能起。或疑冥官何至受笞刑，余谓昌黎诗云“簿尉卑官不堪说，未免棰楚尘埃间”，冥中或尚沿唐制欤？惟其时诏书颁行天下，近京诸处咸已闻知，江、浙间距京师稍远，故未及耳。此非当秘密之事，虽有漏泄，未足深罪，竟被冥刑，何欤？余同年谢梦渔给谏增亦为冥官，谢性率易，每以冥中事语人，无所忌，不闻其以漏言获罪也。光绪初，盛京将军崇公卒。崇名崇实，字璞山，亦庚戌同年也。未卒前一月，谢语人曰：“璞山其不久乎，吾昨已于冥中见其魂矣。”人不以为信，逾月而遗表闻，乃信谢言之有因也。

    Explanation

    606

    On the eleventh day of the twelfth month in the twenty-ninth year of the Daoguang era, Empress Xiaoheshui passed away. At that time, Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces had not yet received official notification, but blue-stamped documents from the Ministry arrived first, and everyone knew there was a major state funeral. However, at that time, Emperor Daoguang was also advanced in age, and public opinion was speculating, unsure of the exact situation. Shoubo, using an example from the Guo Ce (Strategies of the Warring States) where Lord Chudongjun flatters Empress Dowager Zhao, whispered to someone, calling her "granny." That night, he arrived in the afterlife and was severely beaten, being punished for leaking information. Upon waking, his legs were purple and blue, and he lay bedridden for over a month before he could stand. Some wondered why a government official in the afterlife would receive a beating. I replied that a line from a poem by Han Yu says, "Low-ranking officials are not worth talking about, but they cannot escape beatings in the dust," suggesting that the afterlife might still follow Tang dynasty customs. At that time, the imperial edict had been issued and was known in areas close to the capital, but Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, being slightly farther away from the capital, had not yet heard. This was not a matter of secret importance, so even if there was a leak, it did not warrant severe punishment, yet he was punished in the afterlife. Why was this so? My contemporary, Xie Mengyu, who was also a censor in the afterlife, was straightforward and often spoke about afterlife matters without hesitation. He was never heard to be punished for leaking information. In the early Guangxu era, General Chong of Shengjing passed away. His name was Chong Shi, with the courtesy name Pu Shan, and he was also a contemporary of mine. One month before his death, Xie told people, "Pu Shan won't be here for long, I saw his soul in the afterlife yesterday." People did not believe him, but after a month, when his memorial was submitted, they believed his words had a basis in fact.

    607

    ●卷十二高玉新、贾助之，皆山东福山县人，于京师西单牌楼同开猪肉店。高行二，贾行三，各以行第相呼。始极相得，后有违言，积成仇怨。某年某日夜漏将尽，贾犹酣睡，高执利刃连斫之，伤颅及额，登时殒命，高到官自承不讳。而问官宽厚，欲改故杀为斗杀，免高之死。乃微以言导之曰：“汝两人素相得，何因口角细故顿起杀机邪？”高对曰：“昨得一梦。”官怪其言不伦，问何梦，对曰：“昨梦贾老三来，云：”高二兄，我死甚苦，汝必为我伸冤。‘“官察其声非高之声也，而高再三言之不已。官曰”然则斫下汝头颅，便可与贾老三伸冤矣。“高扌盍头曰（”扌盍头“二字，见《太平广记》卷四十二引《逸史》）：”谢大人明断。“官悚然，知附高而言者，即贾之鬼也。于是论高如律。

    Explanation

    607

    Chapter Twelve: Gao Yuxin and Jia Zhuzhi, both from Fushan County, Shandong Province, jointly operated a pork shop in the Xidan area of the capital city. Gao was the second son, and Jia was the third son, each addressing the other by their respective seniority. Initially, they were very close, but later they had disagreements that gradually turned into deep-seated grudges. On a certain night when the night watch was almost over, Jia was still sound asleep, and Gao took a sharp knife and repeatedly struck him, injuring his head and forehead, causing him to die instantly. Gao confessed to the authorities without hesitation. The magistrate, being kind-hearted, wanted to change the charge from premeditated murder to manslaughter to spare Gao's life. He gently guided Gao with the words, "Why did you two, who were once so close, suddenly kill each other over a minor argument?" Gao replied, "I had a dream last night." The magistrate found his words strange and asked what the dream was about. Gao said, "Last night, I dreamed that Jia Lao San came and said, 'Gao Er, I died very painfully, you must avenge me.'" The magistrate realized that the voice speaking through Gao was actually Jia's ghost, as it was not Gao's voice. Gao continued to speak of the dream repeatedly. The magistrate said, "Then cutting off your head would allow you to avenge Jia Lao San." Gao bowed his head and said, "Thank you, Your Honor, for your wise judgment." The magistrate was startled and realized that the ghost of Jia was speaking through Gao. He then judged Gao according to the law.

    608

    四川富顺县，民妇李氏，与佣工彭姓者有私。李有子学贾于外，其妇未婚也，而育于夫氏，名曰顺英。李遇之虐，顺英遂亡去。有木工黄姓者，留之以为妻，距其家百里矣。顺英之兄自外归，不见其妹，讼于官。官拘李与彭问之，得其奸状，则曰：“是必杀顺英以灭口也。”会其地三四里外有陂池，深丈余，浮一女尸，两手反缚，缒以巨石，其面目为鱼所唼喋，不可辨。官曰：“是必顺英。”李与彭大受笞，皆诬服。狱成，臬使苏公廷玉疑之，曰：“见尸之日，距李谋杀顺英之日，百有三旬。

    Explanation

    608

    In Fushun County, Sichuan, there was a peasant woman named Li who had an affair with a laborer surnamed Peng. Li's son was studying business away from home, and her daughter, Shunying, was unmarried and being raised by her husband's family. Li treated Shunying cruelly, and Shunying fled away. A carpenter surnamed Huang kept her as his wife, and she was a hundred li away from her home. Shunying's brother returned from outside and did not see his sister, so he sued the case to the authorities. The officials detained Li and Peng for questioning and found evidence of their adultery. They concluded, "They must have killed Shunying to silence her." Coincidentally, there was a pond several li away, deep over ten feet, where a floating female corpse was found. Her hands were tied behind her back, weighted down with a large stone, and her face was eaten by fish, making her unrecognizable. The officials said, "This must be Shunying." Li and Peng suffered severe beating and both confessed under duress. The case was finalized, but the provincial governor, Su Tingyu, doubted the verdict. He noted that the body was found one hundred and thirty days after Shunying was reported missing.

    609

    尸未溃烂，何邪？是必非顺英。”执不可，众议皆以苏为迂。俄而顺英自归，已抱子矣，乃皆叹服。而前所见女尸，则莫知为谁。久之，知有夫妇同行，中途反目，妇投池死，夫舍之去。池主畏累，以绳反缚之，将负至他处，又畏人见，遂缒石而沉之云。此事见苏公所著《从政杂录》。苏字鳌石，福建同安人。余谓李氏之狱，非得苏公力持，必有冤死者矣。尸犹未辨，而遽以定谳，可乎？惟池中女尸，恐别有故。夫妇反目，何遽轻生。妇既死矣，夫必买棺敛之，何遽掉臂而去邪？此事大有可疑，当时亦姑弗深求耳。

    Explanation

    609

    The corpse was not decomposed, what could be the reason? This must not be Shunying." They insisted it was impossible, and everyone thought Su was being impractical. Soon, Shunying returned on her own, already holding a child, and everyone then admired her. But the woman's corpse seen earlier was unknown. After some time, it was discovered that a couple had been traveling together, quarreled halfway, and the wife drowned herself in a pond, while the husband left her behind. The pond owner, fearing trouble, tied her up with rope and intended to carry her elsewhere, but fearing others would see, he tied a stone to her and sank her. This incident is recorded in Su Gong's From Politics Miscellaneous Records. Su, courtesy name Ao Shi, was from Tong'an, Fujian. I believe that without Su Gong's strong intervention, there would have been an innocent person wrongly executed in the Li case. How could a verdict be made before the corpse was even identified? But the woman's corpse in the pond might have had some other reason. Why would a couple quarrel and one of them take their own life so quickly? After the wife had died, the husband would surely have bought a coffin and buried her, so why would he just leave her behind? This matter is very suspicious, but at the time, they did not pursue it further.

    610

    光绪六年，钱唐库吏何培，以征收钱粮擅改科则，为乡民所告。巡抚谭公震怒，执何培杀之。事后，有言培于五十岁时，至天日山礼佛，山中老僧语之曰：“居士来此大善。既来此，不必下山矣！可作书寄家中处分家事，留此与老僧焚修，亦无须削发，且作灵山会上一优婆塞，亦足远祸全生也。”培不解其意，应曰：“固所愿也。然家事甚多，必须亲自料理，俟诸事粗了，当再来耳。

    Explanation

    610

    During the sixth year of the Guangxu reign, He Pei, a clerk in charge of the treasury in Qiantang, was reported by the villagers for arbitrarily altering the tax rules during the collection of money and grain. Governor Tan was furious and seized and executed He Pei. Afterward, it was said that when He Pei was fifty years old, he went to Tianri Mountain to worship Buddha. An old monk in the mountain spoke to him and said, "It is very good that a layperson has come here. Since you have come, you do not need to go down the mountain! You can write a letter to your family to handle family affairs, and then stay here and practice with the old monk. You do not need to shave your head, and you can become a lay Buddhist follower at the Ling Shan Assembly. This is enough to avoid disaster and preserve life." He Pei did not understand the meaning of the monk's words and replied, "It is what I wish. But there are many family matters that need to be handled personally. I will come again once all matters are roughly settled."

    611

    ”僧摇首曰：“此去安得再来邪？”培归，未三年而及于祸，此老僧盖先知之矣。咸阳文古玉，字严山，年逾知命，而好学愈笃。当游学浙东，至黄岩，游九峰山，见紫云峰下有洞含岈，在绝壁间，因攀援而上。至则有门甚小，伛偻入之，中多蝙蝠，色皆纯白，大有如车轮者。行百余步，又有门半阖半开，侧身而入，则风日清和，平畴千顷。时方秋杪，其中气候乃似春夏之交，桑竹阴翳。民居亦稠密，惟道上行人稀少。遥望数里外，有高山上矗霄汉。循涂而往，山麓有石坊，刻篆文曰：“无可又之乡。”即于坊下小坐，又抠衣而登。

    Explanation

    611

    The monk shook his head and said, "How could I possibly come back from here?" Péi returned, and within three years, he met disaster; this old monk must have known it beforehand. Wen Guyu from Xianyang, styled Yanshan, having passed the age of fifty, became even more devoted to learning. He traveled to study in Zhejiang, reaching Huangyan, and visited Jiufeng Mountain, where he saw a cave called Ziyun Peak below, located on a sheer cliff, so he climbed up. Upon reaching it, he found a very small door and had to stoop to enter. Inside, there were many bats, all pure white, some as large as a cartwheel. After walking more than a hundred steps, he came to another door that was half open and half closed. He squeezed through, and found the air and sun to be clear and gentle, with vast fields stretching out. It was late autumn, but the climate inside was like that of spring and summer. Mulberry trees and bamboo were lush and shady. Houses were also dense, but there were few people on the road. Looking far ahead, there was a high mountain reaching up to the sky. Following the path, at the foot of the mountain, there was a stone archway, carved with seal script that read: "The Land of No More and No Less." He sat down briefly under the archway, then pulled up his robe and climbed further.

    612

    可十余里，有一庙，庙祀许由，配以氵交长。问庙中人，乃知近村居民，皆太岳之后也。庙旁有酒卢，因入沽饮。见饮者甚众，皆衣冠都雅，言辞高古，不甚可解。末坐一少年独饮，揖而问其姓氏，自言王姓，年二十四岁。叩所学，少年云：“仆幼无所知，惟尝用力于《周易》、《老子》二书，私为之注，亦未敢自信也。”问此山何名，少年曰：“是名中天山，高逾万仞。山巅有不死之药，四方奇花珍木，无所不备，有神人守之。登高而望，则东南大海，西北河源，了了在目。

    Explanation

    612

    About ten miles away, there is a temple that worships Xu You, accompanied by a deity associated with water or longevity. Asking the people in the temple, I learned that the villagers nearby are all descendants of Tai Yue. Next to the temple is a tavern, and I entered to buy and drink. I saw many people drinking, all dressed in elegant attire, speaking in lofty and ancient language that was not very understandable. At the far end, a young man was drinking alone. I bowed and asked for his surname and name; he introduced himself as Wang, twenty-four years old. When I inquired about his studies, the young man said, "I knew nothing in my youth, but I have focused my efforts on the I Ching and Laozi, privately annotating them, though I am not very confident in my work." I asked what the name of this mountain was, and the young man replied, "It is called Zhongtian Mountain, taller than ten thousand feet. At its peak, there is the elixir of immortality, and all directions are filled with rare flowers and precious trees, with divine beings guarding it. Climbing high to look, the eastern and southern seas and the northwest river sources are clearly visible."

    613

    然仆亦未尝跻其巅也。”古玉甚异之。时日已下舂，乃解佩玉付酒家为质，别少年而归，出洞门，则依夕矣。幸有月色，扪藤引葛，仅乃得下，借宿于僧寺，与僧言之，僧皆欣然欲往。次日复寻旧径，则石壁千寻，无复洞门矣，乃以酒钱置石上而反。乃至寺，有玉自溪水顺流而下，取视，则即昨留酒家为质者也。此事王子庄孝廉曾笔之书，王姓少年，必王辅嗣无疑。《太平广记》卷三十九引《广异记》载麻阳村人事，其所遇老翁为河上公，守门童子为王辅嗣。然则此等事，古书固有之，不得竟以为虚诞也。

    Explanation

    613

    Indeed, I have not climbed to its peak either." The ancient jade greatly admired this. By the time it was late afternoon, he took off his jade pendant and left it as collateral with the tavern, parted with the young man, and returned. Exiting the cave entrance, it was already evening. Fortunately, there was moonlight, and he climbed vines and pulled on ivy, barely managing to descend. He took shelter for the night at a temple and told the monks about it. The monks were all eager to go. The next day, he again followed the old path, but there was a thousand feet of stone wall, and there was no longer a cave entrance. He then placed the wine money on the rock and returned. Arriving at the temple, a piece of jade was floating down the stream. He picked it up and saw that it was the jade he had left as collateral at the tavern the previous day. This matter was written down by Mr. Wang Zizhuang, a scholar-official. The young man with the surname Wang must be none other than Wang Fusi. In Volume 39 of T'ang Hui-yao (Comprehensive Records of the Tang), Kuang Yi Ji (Record of Strange Events) records the affairs of a village in Mayang, where the old man he met was the River Master, and the gate-keeping boy was Wang Fusi. Therefore, such matters are indeed recorded in ancient books and should not be dismissed as purely fictitious.

    614

    黄岩县濒海，其民筑塘御潮，有头塘、二塘、三塘、四塘、五塘之称，俗语转为荡。自四荡以下，其地荒凉多鬼魅。同治之末，有人家娶妇，成婚之次夕，方宴宾客，新妇忽起从后户出，家人尾之。其行如飞，竟越竹园而去，钗珥之类皆竹叶间。竹外有池，捞漉无所得。列炬四照，见田间有女人足迹，相距辄数尺，斜迤从东南去。穷所往，得之半里外溪水中，已僵立而死。此王子庄孝廉门人施榕所言。新妇之夫，乃从学于施生者也。

    Explanation

    614

    Yellow Rock County borders the sea, and its inhabitants build embankments to defend against tides, with names such as First Embankment, Second Embankment, Third Embankment, Fourth Embankment, and Fifth Embankment. Locally, these are referred to as "tangs." From the Fourth Tang onwards, the area is desolate and haunted by ghosts. At the end of the Tongzhi era, a household in the county married a woman. On the night after the wedding, they were entertaining guests when the new bride suddenly got up and left through the back door. The family followed her. She ran like the wind, crossing the bamboo garden and going away, leaving her hairpins and other ornaments stuck in the bamboo leaves. Outside the bamboo, there was a pond, and searching yielded nothing. Lighting torches to illuminate all around, they saw women's footprints in the fields, each step several feet apart, slanting and leading southeast. Following the trail to its end, they found her in a stream half a li away, already stiff and standing dead. This was said by Shi Rong, a student of Mr. Wang Zizhuang. The husband of the new bride was a student of Shi Rong.

    615

    同治中，临海县民比年疠疾，过大暑不瘳，乃于次年相约为送船之会，亦其旧俗然也。其船如商船之式，船具如桅樯舵虏，用具如桌椅床榻、枕簟被褥，食物如鸡彘鱼虾、米谷豆麦，备御之具如刀矛枪炮，无一不备。惟盛米之袋甚小，仅受一升，而数则以万计，皆村民所施也。前大暑数日，大建道场。至大暑日，送之出海，听其所之，俗呼为大暑船。尝有海盗夜遇之，以为商船，向之放炮，此船亦放炮御之。至天明，始知为大暑船，大惊而去。后此盗船为官军所败，群盗悉就俘无脱者。

    Explanation

    615

    During the Tongzhi era, the people of Linhai County suffered from a serious epidemic in recent years, and many did not recover even after the hottest day of the year, Daxu. They decided to hold a boat-sending ceremony the following year as part of their traditional customs. The boat was built like a merchant ship, with masts, sails, and a rudder, and was equipped with tables, chairs, beds, pillows, bedding, food such as chicken, pork, fish, shrimp, rice, grain, beans, and wheat, as well as weapons like swords, spears, guns, and cannons. The only exception was the small bags used to hold rice, each holding only one liter, but there were thousands of them, all donated by villagers. Several days before the hottest day of the year, they built a large temple or shrine for religious purposes. On the hottest day of the year, they sent the boat out to sea, letting it go wherever it would, and it became known as the Daxu boat. Once, some pirates encountered the boat at night, mistaking it for a merchant ship, and fired at it. The boat also fired back to defend itself. When morning came, the pirates realized it was a Daxu boat and were greatly surprised, then left. Later, the pirate ship was defeated by the official military forces, and all the bandits were captured and none escaped.

    616

    又福建某处有卖米之牙行，一夜有叩门以米来售者，担夫数十人至船中起其米，达日而米未尽。米袋大如五石瓠，荷者肩为之。天明视之，则小如碗耳。有父老识之曰：“此临海大暑船中米也。”即日疫疠大作，禳之始已。余从前客休宁汪村时，每年四月间，有打标之俗，亦所以逐疫也。糊纸为船，无物不具，但皆以纸为之耳。焚之野外，云送之游西湖，俚俗相沿，可发一噱。然亦有于徽河中亲见是船者。昔有贸布之客，曾附之至杭州。至今纸船之尾有一人负布而立，手持雨伞，如贾客之形，盖以识其灵异也。临海之船，竟是真船，宜其灵异更著矣。

    Explanation

    616

    In a certain place in Fujian, there was a middleman who sold rice. One night, someone knocked on the door to sell rice. Dozens of porters loaded the rice onto a boat, and by the next day, the rice was not fully loaded. The rice bags were as large as five-stone gourds, and the porters' shoulders were strained. When viewed in the morning, the rice bags appeared small like bowls. An elderly man recognized them and said, "These are the rice from the ship in Lianhai during the great heat." That day, an epidemic broke out, and rituals were performed to avert it. When I was a guest in Wang Village, Xiuning, every April, there was a custom of burning paper boats to drive away epidemics. The paper boats were made of paper, and everything was included, but everything was made of paper. They were burned in the fields, and it was said that they were sent to wander in West Lake. This custom has been passed down through generations and can be amusing. However, there are also those who have personally seen these boats in the Huizhou River. Once, a merchant who traded cloth accompanied the paper boats to Hangzhou. To this day, there is a figure standing at the back of the paper boat, carrying cloth and holding an umbrella, resembling a merchant. This is to recognize its supernatural nature. The ship from Lianhai is actually a real ship, and it is more likely to be considered supernatural.

    617

    王子庄孝廉，居黄岩之柔桥。其地有庙曰东阮，祀晋阮嗣宗，或曰即白龙山之神也。道光甲午岁，黄岩县中盛行花会。花会者，书三十四古人名，任取一名，纳之筒中，县之梁间，名曰作（去声）筒。主之者曰筒官。人于三十四名中自认一名，各注钱数投入匮中，谓之纳花会。所认之名适合筒中之名，则筒官如其所注钱数，加三十倍以酬之；不中，则如所注之数入钱于筒官。老少奔波，一邑若狂，因是又有降童之事。降童者，焚符降神，求示机兆也。神依人而言，所依之人谓之童身。神降，则童身自倒于地，口吐白沫，俄而跃起，竟登神坐，谓之上坛。于是众皆罗拜，求神指示，神辄示以隐语。酬对既毕，童身又倒地如初，谓之退坛。人依神言，射之往往有中者，故凡纳花会者，无不降童。所降之神非一，而小老爷尤著灵异。

    Explanation

    617

    Prince Wang Zhi, a scholar of high moral standing, resided in the soft bridge of Huangyan. In this place, there was a temple named Dongruan, which worshipped Ruan Sizong of the Jin Dynasty, or so it was said, it was the deity of Bai Long Mountain. During the year 1834 of the Daoguang era, the flower gathering was widely popular in the county of Huangyan. The flower gathering involved writing the names of thirty-four ancient people on slips, selecting one at random, placing it in a tube, and hanging the tube between the beams, known as "making a tube." The person in charge of this was called the tube official. Participants would choose one name from the thirty-four and contribute a certain amount of money to a chest, which was termed "participating in the flower gathering." If the chosen name matched the one in the tube, the tube official would pay back thirty times the amount contributed; if it did not match, the amount would go to the tube official. People of all ages rushed to join, and the entire county was in a frenzy. Consequently, the ritual of child-descending also gained prominence. Child-descending involved burning talismans to summon a deity and seek omens. When a deity possessed a person, that person was called the child body. When the deity descended, the child body would fall to the ground, foam at the mouth, and then leap up, taking the deity's seat, which was called ascending the altar. Everyone would then prostrate themselves and ask the deity for guidance, and the deity would respond with cryptic messages. After the exchange, the child body would fall back to the ground as before, which was called descending from the altar. People followed the deity's words, and their predictions often came true, so all who participated in the flower gathering also underwent the child-descending ritual. The deities who descended were not one, but the "little master" was particularly renowned for its supernatural abilities.

    618

    小老爷者，俗言观世音菩萨之弟也。人有所问，必回身向内，久之而后答，盖问其姊云。柔桥之人即降童于东阮庙中。小老爷甫降，而村民王广荣忽自田间趋而来，则东阮之神凭其身矣，小老爷仓皇退坛。广荣传神语，责父老招致邪神，亵渎灵场，父老皆叩扌盍不遑。神自言能治人病，由是问疾者麇至。广荣虽或耕于田，樵于山，贾于市，而庙中有人问疾，甫焚香默祷，无不应时立至。其人初不识字，至是辄能为人处方，悉中肯。有群医束手，服神方而即愈者。由是香火日盛，乃新殿宇，门楼郎庑，焕然一新，皆取给于香钱。而王广荣者，固不私一钱也。当是时，各村神庙俱有此等事，然皆以花会为主，间或赐方，亦不甚验。惟东阮庙，无敢以花会问者。越一二年，诸庙之神皆不降，而东阮降神者，垂三十年，至同治甲子岁，广荣死，始绝。

    Explanation

    618

    The Minor Deity, commonly known as the younger brother of Guanyin Bodhisattva, would always turn inward when questioned, seeking guidance from his sister before answering. In the village of Ruoqiao, a child was possessed at the Dongruan Temple. As soon as the Minor Deity descended, the villager Wang Guangrong hurried from the fields, and the spirit of Dongruan Temple took control of him. The Minor Deity hastily retreated from the altar. Wang Guangrong relayed the deity’s words, blaming the elders for summoning a demon and desecrating the sacred site. The elders, in haste, knocked their heads in supplication. The deity claimed to have the power to heal, and soon, people flocked to the temple seeking treatment. Despite Wang Guangrong’s daily activities of farming, chopping wood, or trading in the market, whenever someone asked for healing at the temple, after burning incense and silently praying, the deity would always appear immediately. Initially illiterate, Wang Guangrong suddenly became able to prescribe remedies, all of which proved effective. There were cases where even skilled physicians were at a loss, but patients were cured by the divine prescriptions. As a result, the temple’s incense offerings grew daily, and the temple buildings, gatehouses, and halls were renovated to焕然一新, all funded by the incense money. Wang Guangrong, however, did not keep a single coin for himself. At that time, similar phenomena occurred in the temples of other villages, but they primarily relied on folk rituals, and the prescriptions they occasionally gave were not very effective. Only the Dongruan Temple did not accept such offerings. After one or two years, the deities in other temples ceased to descend, while the Dongruan Temple’s deity continued to manifest for nearly thirty years, until the year of Tongzhi Jiazi, when Wang Guangrong died, and the phenomenon finally ceased.

    619

    王子庄孝廉于光绪丁丑岁六月十一日，梦过其友张子远所，子远示以杂文一卷，内有[C108]说二篇。其一，乃王君仁堪所作。其文云：蓬莱、方丈之间，有神人焉，造飞楼于瀛洲之溪，距水尺咫。藐姑之仙来游，坐而濯足，甚自得也。邀赤松子同观焉，赤松子曰：“是太迫水，宜升而高之。”于是神人崇其楼三尺。王乔飞舄而至，曰：“美则美矣，尚病其庳也。”于是复崇其楼三尺。洪崖子曰：“高则高矣，登楼而望，方丈蔽其前，所见不旷也。”于是复崇之三尺。

    Explanation

    619

    On June 11th of the Dingchou year during the Guangxu era (1887), Wang Zizhuang, a scholar-official, dreamed of passing by his friend Zhang Ziyuan's place. Zhang Ziyuan showed him a collection of miscellaneous writings, which contained two essays. One of the essays was written by Wang Renkan. The text reads: "Between the islands of Penglai and Fangzhang, there is a divine being who built a flying pavilion on the stream of Yingzhou, just a few feet above the water. The immortal Miao Gu came to visit and sat to wash her feet, feeling very pleased. She invited Cishengzi to join her, and Cishengzi said, 'It is too close to the water; it should be raised higher.' So the divine being raised the pavilion three feet. Wang Qiao, flying with his magic shoes, arrived and said, 'It is beautiful, but it is still too low.' So the pavilion was raised another three feet. Hongyazi said, 'It is now high, but when you climb the pavilion and look out, Fangzhang blocks your view in front, and the sight is not expansive.' So the pavilion was raised another three feet."

    620

    盖距水逾丈，而方丈之山弗能蔽矣。俄有海舟过其下，樯与甍齐，不能进也，于是神人又崇其址十倍，盖距水十有三仞六尺有咫云。既寤，其文历历可记，而莫测其旨。王君者，是年成进士，廷对第一人也。子庄曰：“此所谓‘巨鳌，首冠蓬山’者也。”王君大魁，应其兆矣。[C108]字其外类井，其中类亚，不知何字，梦中读如倪音，实字书所无也。子庄有《枝言》内外篇，皆纪异之作，以余征求异事，录以示余，余采取数则。如此篇者，与前所书文古玉事，并不知其为寓言为实事也。

    Explanation

    620

    The creature was more than ten feet away from the water, and even a small mountain could not hide it. Soon, a sea vessel passed beneath it, its masts and roof reaching the same height, unable to move forward. Then, divine beings increased the height of its base tenfold, making it stand thirteen and a half feet above the water. After waking up, the text could be clearly remembered, but its meaning remained unclear. Wang, who became the top-ranked candidate in the imperial examination that year, was said to embody the saying, "The head of the giant turtle is crowned like Mount Penglai." This was a sign that he would achieve great success. The outer part of the text resembles the character "井" (well), and the inner part resembles the character "亚" (second), but it is an unknown character. In the dream, it was read as "倪" (Ni), but it is not found in any standard Chinese dictionary. Zizhuang wrote a work called Zhiyan (Branch Words), which contains various strange stories and anecdotes. He shared this with me, and I have selected a few passages to include here. This passage, along with the story of the ancient jade inscription, is unclear whether it is a metaphorical tale or a historical fact.

    621

    翁君广平，字海村。于戊戌之春，客于乍浦。偶步野外，见众人皆西走，问之，曰：“西海塘之外，海台出见也。”海台者，俗呼海市也。因随之往。见塘外烟雾中，隐隐有楼台之象，其中有若宫阙者，有若寺观者，有若民居市廛者，有若驿舍邮亭者，以千百计，横亘十余里。是时雾气犹重，未能悉见也。少焉豁然开霁，屋舍后起一复道，如长虹，有舆马行其上。而屋舍之帘幕卷舒，窗户开阖，与夫士女之会聚燕饮，或盥漱，或栉纟徙，或坐而眺望，皆历历可数也。

    Explanation

    621

    Wang Guangping, courtesy name Hai Cun, was a guest in Jia浦 during the spring of the year Wuxu. One day, he took a casual walk in the fields and saw many people heading west. He asked them why, and they replied, "Beyond the western sea dike, the sea platform is appearing." The term "sea platform" is a colloquial name for the "sea mirage" or "Fata Morgana," which is also called "sea market" in folklore. Following their lead, he went to see for himself. In the mist beyond the sea dike, he saw faint outlines of towers and pavilions. Among them were palaces and gates, temples and observatories, residential areas and marketplaces, inns and post stations, all numbering in the hundreds, stretching for more than ten miles. At that time, the fog was still thick, so he could not see everything clearly. Soon, the mist cleared, and behind the houses, an overpass appeared like a long rainbow, with carriages and horses moving on it. The curtains and screens of the houses were being drawn and rolled, the windows opening and closing, and men and women were gathered for feasting and drinking, some washing and brushing their teeth, others combing their hair and dressing, and some sitting and looking out. All these scenes could be counted clearly.

    622

    其东有平原，若演武场，有操弓矢者，有执旌旗者，骑而驰骤者，步而击刺者。又有若大官鸣驺清道而至者。其外又有若市井交易者，负者、戴者、坐者、趋者，亦历历可数也。稍远又有若城郭者，有若大船帆风而行者，莫能窥其际矣。俄而一峰突起，有云气环绕之，殷殷然有雷声。其中忽涌出七级浮屠，上插霄汉，铃铎动摇，光彩照耀，而其云气之变幻，莫能名状也。久之昏雾复起，海风亦作，所见之象，倏归乌有矣。翁君有《观海市记》，余同年暴梅村之孙式昭字方子者，录以示余，余撮其大略如此。翁君为震泽平望镇人，盖在乾、嘉间，所云戊戌春者，不知为何年也。

    Explanation

    622

    To the east, there was a vast plain, resembling a military training ground, where some practiced archery, others held banners, some rode horses galloping swiftly, and others engaged in martial arts on foot. There was also a high-ranking official arriving with a clear path, accompanied by heralds and a retinue. Beyond this, there were scenes of market transactions, with people carrying, wearing, sitting, and hurrying, all clearly visible. In the distance, there were city walls and large ships sailing on the wind, their limits indiscernible. Suddenly, a mountain peak emerged, surrounded by clouds and accompanied by the sound of thunder. From within, a seven-story pagoda appeared, its spire reaching into the heavens, with bells and lights moving, and the clouds changing shape in a manner that defies description. After a while, the fog returned, and the sea breeze blew, causing all the scenes to vanish instantly. Mr. Wang has a record of the sea market, The Record of the Sea Market. A contemporary of mine, Mr. Fang, whose family name is Bao and courtesy name is Meicun, recorded and showed it to me. I have summarized its main points as follows. Mr. Wang is from Pingwang Town, Zhenze, and this account is said to be from the spring of the year of the earth rooster during the Qianlong and Jiaqing periods, though the exact year is unknown.

    623

    西湖南高峰之下有五老峰，相传有虎穴，俗呼谓老虎洞，然亦罕有遇之者。道光某年，有高氏于其地营葬，百杵俱举，邪许相闻。俄闻虎啸声甚厉，众皆奔走恐后，工遂辍。高氏患之，募求能得虎者。其附近有翁家山，山中人多膂力，应募捕虎。先用一木盆，置大铜镜于其中，烈日照映，光焰上腾，若火起然。于是山中之蜂飞集以万计，咸遗溺于盆中。土人言：“蜂见火起辄来救，遗溺所以救火也；”如是数日，积溺盈寸。乃捉一虾蟆，以溺少许著其身上。

    Explanation

    623

    Under the South Peak of West Lake lies the Five Elders Peak, which is said to have a tiger's den, commonly known as Tiger's Den, though it is rarely encountered. In a certain year during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, a family named Gao prepared a grave at this site, with the sound of many hammers echoing. Soon, a fierce tiger roar was heard, and everyone ran in fear, causing the work to cease. The Gao family was troubled by this and sought someone who could catch the tiger. Nearby, there is a mountain called Wangjia Mountain, where the inhabitants are known for their strength. They responded to the call to catch the tiger. First, they used a wooden basin, placed a large copper mirror inside, and under the intense sunlight, the reflection of the mirror rose like flames, as if a fire had started. Thus, countless bees from the mountain flew and gathered, all leaving their urine in the basin. The locals said, "The bees come to help when they see a fire start, and they leave their urine to extinguish the fire." For several days, the urine accumulated to an inch. Then, they caught a frog and placed a small amount of urine on its body.

    624

    虾蟆中其毒，跳一步而死，则虎中其毒，亦跳一步而死；虾蟆跳数步死，虎亦跳数步死，以是试之，率不爽。乃伏巨弩于虎出入之处，弩末以蜂溺涂之。一人升树杪观动静，又数十人执火枪伏山下，与之约，闻山上爆竹声，则奔赴。是日虎不出。次日闻虎啸声，有一伥鬼先出，至伏弩所曰：“此不利于大哥。”即移置他所。又前行，树上人俟其去远，下树置弩如故，复升树。而虎至，触机弩发，虎中其毒死。伥鬼闻声复还，见虎已死，仰视有人在树杪，踊跃欲上，人击石取火然爆竹，响振山谷。山下所伏人皆上，咸发火枪，伥乃逃去。

    Explanation

    624

    A toad that was poisoned jumped one step and died. A tiger that was poisoned also jumped one step and died; a toad that jumped several steps died, and a tiger that jumped several steps also died. This was tested, and it was always without fail. Then, a large crossbow was set up where the tiger entered and exited, with the tip coated with bee venom. One person climbed to the top of a tree to observe the movements, and dozens of others held fire guns and lay in wait at the foot of the mountain, agreeing that when they heard the sound of firecrackers on the mountain, they would rush to the scene. On that day, the tiger did not come out. The next day, they heard the tiger's roar, and a shanggui (tiger's servant) came out first, reaching the place where the crossbow was set and saying, "This is not good for the big brother." It then moved the crossbow to another place. Continuing on, the person on the tree waited until it went far away, climbed down the tree and set the crossbow back in place, then climbed back up the tree. When the tiger arrived, the trigger of the crossbow was activated, and the tiger was poisoned and died. The shanggui, upon hearing the sound, returned and saw that the tiger was already dead. Looking up, it saw someone at the top of the tree, and it jumped up eagerly to climb the tree. The person struck a stone to light a fire and set off firecrackers, and the sound reverberated through the valley. All the people lying in wait at the foot of the mountain rushed up, and they all fired their guns. The shanggui then fled away.

    625

    众人荷虎以归，高氏厚赉之。汪圻，字子封，湖州人，就馆于宣化府。偶小病月余，一夕梦有人语之曰：“请速赴任。”梦中即从之出，肩舆已俟于门。荷之者四人，其前亦有旗伞之属。俄至一处，似人间衙署，门塾堂庑规模略具，然颇隘小。堂上设公案，置印其上，赞拜如仪。遂登公坐，吏役参拜，按籍呼名，点以朱笔。事已退入内，则小屋三间，有二仆为之执役。须臾具饭，馔以四簋，切猪肉作丝，蒸鸡卵作饼，余则蔬菜，其味悉如人间。饭饱之后，一无所事，既无公牍披阅，亦无吏胥禀白。

    Explanation

    625

    A group of people carried the tiger back, and the Gao family rewarded him generously. Wang Qi, with the courtesy name Zi Feng, was from Huzhou and taught in the Xuanhua Prefecture. He suffered a minor illness for over a month, and one night he dreamed that someone told him, "Please hurry to your post." In the dream, he immediately followed the instruction, and a sedan chair was already waiting at the door. Four people carried it, and there were also flags and umbrellas in front. Soon, they arrived at a place that resembled a government office, with the layout of the gatehouse, study, hall, and corridors roughly present, though it was quite narrow and small. On the hall, a public desk was set up, with an official seal placed on it, and he paid homage according to the ritual. Then, he ascended to the official seat, and officials and servants paid homage. Names were called according to the register, and marked with red ink. After the matter was settled, he withdrew inside, where there were three small rooms, and two servants performed the duties. Soon, the meal was prepared, consisting of four bowls, sliced pork into threads, steamed chicken eggs into cakes, and the rest were vegetables. The taste was all like that of the human world. After the meal, he had nothing to do, with no official documents to read and no reports from officials.

    626

    视其印文，曰“八达岭分司之印”，印虽在匣，从不启用。两餐之后，解衣就枕，甫合眼，蘧然觉矣，则仍在宣化郡斋也。是日仍病卧如常，及夜而卧，则又在彼矣，情景一如初至时。如是月余，汪颇厌苦之，乃语二仆曰：“吾不欲来此，得乎？”仆曰：“当问之吏胥。”已而来，言曰：“官既不欲，即亦不强。”出药二丸授之，曰：“服之，则不来矣。”乃吞之，忽如梦醒，自是遂不复至，而病亦从此若失。后汪以巡检需次直隶，尝奉使至宣化，过八达岭，有旋风起于车前，疑其故吏犹来迎候也。越数年，又过之，则无复斯异，盖日久浸疏矣。

    Explanation

    626

    Wang examined the seal's inscription, which read "Seal of the Badaling Division." Though the seal was in the box, it had never been used. After two meals, he undressed and went to bed. As soon as he closed his eyes, he suddenly woke up, finding himself still in the study of Xuanhua Prefecture. On that day, he was still sick and lying in bed as usual. At night when he went to sleep, he found himself there again, and the scene was just like when he first arrived. For more than a month, Wang grew quite tired of it. He asked his two servants, "Can I stop coming here?" They replied, "We should ask the officials." Later, they came back and said, "Since the official doesn't want to, we won't force him." He gave Wang two pills and said, "If you take them, you won't come here anymore." Wang swallowed them, and suddenly it was as if he woke up from a dream. He no longer came there, and his illness also disappeared from that point on.

    Later, Wang, as a patrol inspector, needed to serve in Zhili Province. He once went on a mission to Xuanhua and passed through Badaling. A whirlwind arose in front of his carriage, and he suspected that his former officials were still coming to greet him. After several years, he passed through again, but there was no more such anomaly. It was because over time, the connection had weakened.

    627

    绍兴有孙氏妇，嫠也，年且五十矣。与比邻徐叟通，生一子，不忍弃。而妇有女已嫁，亦早寡无子，乃使女子之。女甚喜，托言得之育婴堂者，抚之如所出。子五岁不能言，而性甚悍。年浸长，恒操刀与母斗，女患之。其母适至，女以告。时女之夫族有在坐者，曰：“此儿本非己出，又悍无人理，养虎畜狼，甚无谓也，不如逐之。”母素爱此儿，不信女言，与辩。儿忽大声谓女曰：“我本尔弟也，何得子我？”母女皆失色。族人以其素不能言，亦甚骇异，细诘之，则复不语矣。知其有异，亦不穷究。于是复留数年，年益长，性益暴，而是时其母已与徐叟合而同居，若夫妇矣。女之夫族竟以此儿归之二老，二老亦受之不辞。儿归徐，遂能言，与常儿无异。今尚为木匠云。

    Explanation

    627

    In Shaoxing, there was a woman from the Sun family, a widow, who was nearly fifty years old. She had an affair with her neighbor, Mr. Xu, and gave birth to a son, whom she could not bear to abandon. The woman also had a daughter who was married and had become a widow without children. She sent her daughter to take care of the boy. The daughter was very pleased and claimed that she had obtained him from a nursery, treating him as if he were her own child. At five years old, the boy could not speak, but his nature was very fierce. As he grew older, he often wielded a knife and fought with his mother, which troubled the daughter. When the boy's mother arrived, the daughter told her about the situation. At that time, some of the daughter's husband's relatives were present, and one of them said, "This boy is not her own child, and he is also fierce and unreasonable. Raising a tiger and a wolf is very meaningless. It would be better to send him away." The mother, who had always loved this boy, did not believe what the daughter said and argued with her. The boy suddenly shouted at the daughter and said, "I am your younger brother, how can you treat me as your own child?" The mother and daughter both turned pale. The relatives, thinking he had never spoken before, were also very surprised. When they questioned him in detail, he fell silent again. Knowing that he was different, they did not investigate further. So they kept him for several more years, and as he grew older, his nature became even more violent. At that time, the boy's mother had already combined with Mr. Xu and lived together as if they were husband and wife. The daughter's husband's relatives eventually gave this boy to the two elders, and the two elders accepted him without hesitation. When the boy returned to Mr. Xu, he suddenly began to speak, just like any ordinary child. Now he is still a carpenter.

    628

    杭州清泰门外有士人徐彭宣者，夜饮醉归。过社庙之前，见庙中灯火辉煌，人语喧哄，异之，入而觇焉。见神坐堂上，吏役环侍，有按籍而呼名者，阶下鹄立之人闻呼则应，各受一卷，鱼贯而入。视其人多相识者，欲穷其异，因亦阑入其内，见诸人列坐东西。俄有人荷一牌自堂而下，牌上大书“自写履历”四字。诸人见之，皆蹙相顾，曰：“难，难！”有顿足摇头口张而不能翕者。徐意此最易事，何足为难。遥望堂西偏有一人，乃其兄也，执笔而手战，欲书又止者屡矣，曰：“岂吾兄善忘至此欤？”欲往语之，而逻者将至，惧为所见，乃循墙行。至一处，署曰“受卷所”。中一人据案坐，则其亡友曹某也。

    Explanation

    628

    In the Qingtai Gate area of Hangzhou, there was a scholar named Xu Pengxuan who returned home drunk after a night of drinking. As he passed by a local temple, he saw that it was illuminated with bright lights and filled with loud voices, which struck him as strange. Curious, he peered inside and saw a deity seated on the dais, surrounded by officials and servants. Some were calling names from a register, and those standing respectfully on the steps responded, each receiving a scroll and entering one after another. Xu recognized many of the people and, wanting to understand the mystery, slipped inside. He saw the people seated in rows on both sides. Suddenly, a person carrying a sign descended from the dais, and the sign read "Write Your Own Biography." Upon seeing this, everyone frowned at each other, saying, "Difficult, difficult!" Some stamped their feet, shook their heads, and opened their mouths but could not close them. Xu thought this was the easiest thing and wondered why it was so difficult. He noticed a person at the western side of the hall, who turned out to be his brother. His brother was holding a pen, trembling, and hesitating to write, repeating, "Could my brother have forgotten so much?" Xu wanted to speak to him, but a guard was approaching, and he feared being seen, so he walked along the wall. He came to a place marked "Place to Receive Scrolls." Sitting at a desk was a person, and it was his deceased friend Cao.

    629

    曹见徐，惊曰：“子何来此？为逻者所见奈何？速匿此！”徐入，因问其故。曹曰：“所谓自写履历者，使其人将生平行事，为善为恶，一一自书。若无所讳饰，是尚自知其过，犹可姑容，冀其悛改。小有不实不尽，则转送冥司科罪，不能生还矣。”已而缴卷者纷集，略一层卷，率多恶而少善。徐思其兄素号端人，必无遗行。及视其卷，则曾奸一处女，又尝干没人千金之赀，皆平素所未知者。未几门启，诸人皆出，曹急麾徐出，曰：“及此不出，即不能出矣。”徐趋出，履阈而仆，则身卧庙庑，东方白矣。归省其兄，则夜间寒热大作，昏不知人，黎明汗出始愈。因询以冥中事，兄茫然不知也。微以所见隐事叩之，兄大骇曰：“此十许年来未尝告人者，弟何处得之？”乃以社庙所见告，因同至庙中，立誓忏悔。今其兄年五十余尚在，或神许其改过欤！

    Explanation

    629

    曹见徐，惊曰：“子何来此？为逻者所见奈何？速匿此！”徐入，因问其故。曹曰：“所谓自写履历者，使其人将生平行事，为善为恶，一一自书。若无所讳饰，是尚自知其过，犹可姑容，冀其悛改。小有不实不尽，则转送冥司科罪，不能生还矣。”已而缴卷者纷集，略一层卷，率多恶而少善。徐思其兄素号端人，必无遗行。及视其卷，则曾奸一处女，又尝干没人千金之赀，皆平素所未知者。未几门启，诸人皆出，曹急麾徐出，曰：“及此不出，即不能出矣。”徐趋出，履阈而仆，则身卧庙庑，东方白矣。归省其兄，则夜间寒热大作，昏不知人，黎明汗出始愈。因询以冥中事，兄茫然不知也。微以所见隐事叩之，兄大骇曰：“此十许年来未尝告人者，弟何处得之？”乃以社庙所见告，因同至庙中，立誓忏悔。今其兄年五十余尚在，或神许其改过欤！

    Translation:
    When Cao saw Xu, he was startled and said, "Why have you come here? What if you are seen by the guards? Hide quickly!" Xu entered, and then asked the reason. Cao said, "What is meant by self-writing a resume is that a person records all their lifetime actions, whether good or evil, one by one. If there is no concealment or embellishment, it shows that they still know their own faults, and they can be temporarily tolerated, hoping they will repent. If there is even a little falsehood or omission, they will be sent to the underworld for punishment and cannot return to life." Soon after, those who handed in their resumes gathered, and when a layer of them was reviewed, most were filled with evil deeds and few with good ones. Xu thought his brother was always known as an upright man and surely had no hidden misdeeds. But when he saw his brother's resume, it revealed that he had once seduced a young girl and had once embezzled a thousand gold coins, all of which were unknown to Xu. Soon the door opened, and everyone came out. Cao urgently gestured for Xu to leave, saying, "If you don't leave now, you won't be able to leave." Xu hurried out, stumbled upon stepping on the threshold, and then found himself lying in the temple porch, with the east already brightening. When he returned home to visit his brother, his brother was suffering from chills and fever that night, unconscious, and only recovered when he broke out in a sweat at dawn. Xu then asked about matters in the underworld, but his brother was clueless. When Xu gently probed with the hidden matters he had seen, his brother was greatly startled and said, "These are things I haven't told anyone for about ten years, how did you find out?" Xu then told him what he had seen in the village temple, and they went together to the temple, where they took an oath and repented. Now, his brother is still alive at over fifty years old, perhaps the gods have forgiven him for his repentance.

    630

    王君半怡，乃余门下士王梦薇廷鼎之祖也。尝乘舟经太湖之筲箕，风雷交作，黑云四合，有五龙斗于湖中，忽远忽近。俄去船不及半里，湖水沸腾，船随风而上者数丈，一落则水及于，舟子惊号，不知所措。王君虽恐，犹谓死生有命，手据船舷以观其变。俄复有一龙至，满身鳞甲，灿若黄金，盘旋五龙之间，摆颔张鳞，夭矫腾跃。其时风雷益怒，湖水皆立，五龙皆不能敌。有一龙向东南而遁，捷于箭，王君之舟为其所摄，竟与俱去。约二十余里，至钱家一古墓前，龙触宰树而堕。舟亦堕地，头舻尾舳无不毁坏，同舟五人幸无伤失。龙仰卧墓上，鼻隆隆作声，若甚惫者。

    Explanation

    630

    Wang Jun, known as Ban Yi, was the ancestor of my disciple Wang Mengwei Tingding. Once, while traveling by boat through the Siao Ji Gua near Taihu Lake, a storm erupted with wind and thunder, and black clouds gathered all around. Five dragons were fighting in the lake, appearing now far, now near. Suddenly, the boat was less than half a li away from them, and the lake water boiled. The boat was lifted several zhang by the wind, and when it fell, the water reached the ship's hull. The boatman shouted in alarm, at a loss, not knowing what to do. Despite his fear, Wang Jun believed that life and death were matters of fate and held onto the ship's rail to observe the changes. Suddenly, another dragon arrived, its body covered in scales that shone like gold. It circled among the five dragons, swaying its head, spreading its scales, and gracefully leaping. At that moment, the wind and thunder grew more intense, and the lake water stood upright. The five dragons could not resist. One dragon fled toward the southeast, faster than an arrow, and Wang Jun's boat was caught by it, and went away with it. After about twenty li, they arrived in front of an ancient tomb at Qian Jia Gua. The dragon touched a tree and fell. The ship also fell to the ground, and the front and rear parts were all destroyed. The five people on the boat were fortunately not injured or lost. The dragon lay on its back on the tomb, its nose making a rumbling sound, as if very exhausted.

    631

    大雨旋至，龙展转良久，奋身一跃，复冉冉腾空而去。时已昏暮，五人者与舟子努力前行，遇男妇十数人，面目不可辨。问以途，曰：“前有陆家社，吾等皆社中人也，可从吾投之。”因尾之行。行五里许，果见一村落，及入村，男妇十数人一时不见。道旁有一庙，叩门而入，告以所苦，僧止之宿，且告曰：“君等堕舟处，即此村中陆氏之祖墓也。”王君私语同人：“顷所遇必陆墓之鬼，不然，此十数男妇何故于黑夜中冒风雨而行乎？”天明出庙，见有十余人荷畚扌局过门前，问之，皆陆氏子孙也。昨夜人定后，闻叩门甚急，启视无有。及寝，又梦其先人语之曰：“墓域为龙所毁，宜急修理。”故今往修墓也。王君语人曰：“吾言信矣！”

    Explanation

    631

    Heavy rain suddenly came down, and the dragon writhed for a long time before leaping with all its might and slowly rising into the air and departing. It was already dusk, and the five people and the boatman struggled to move forward. They encountered several dozen men and women whose faces were indistinguishable. They asked them about the way, and they replied, "There is a village called Lu Village ahead. We are all residents of the village. You can follow us to the village." So they followed them. After walking about five miles, they indeed saw a village. When they entered the village, the men and women disappeared instantly. By the road, there was a temple. They knocked on the door and entered, telling the monks of their suffering. The monks allowed them to stay overnight and said, "The place where you fell from the boat is the ancestral tomb of the Lu family in this village." Wang privately said to his companions, "What we met must be the spirits of the Lu tomb. Otherwise, why would these several dozen men and women be walking in the rain and wind at night?" When morning came, they left the temple and saw more than ten people carrying baskets and shovels passing by the temple gate. They asked, and they were all descendants of the Lu family. Last night after midnight, they heard someone knocking on the door very urgently, but when they opened the door, they saw no one. When they went to sleep, they dreamed that their ancestors spoke to them saying, "The tomb area was destroyed by the dragon. It should be repaired urgently." Therefore, they are going to repair the tomb today. Wang told the people, "My words are true!"

    632

    杭州陈香墅，四十无子。忽梦至一处，绿阴中有巨宅焉，四面皆楼，碧窗朱槛，绣幕低垂。少艾者列坐其中，脸檀眉黛，绮组缤纷，疑是青楼，徘徊不能去。突有十数人麇至楼下，或执秉秆焉，或执编菅焉，中一人持炬火指挥，使众之。一时火烈具举，焱焱炎炎，诸女号哭，有堕楼者。陈视其人，则同学友周子槿也。急叱之曰：“君何得燔人之屋？”力夺其炬，复挥众使退，于是火熄，楼中人哭声亦止，而陈顿觉，不解所谓。次日走访周，欲告以梦。至则周适因斋前榴树上有大蜂窠，偶为所螫，命家僮以火攻之，陈悟所梦即此也。乃以梦告，止勿火。是夜，陈复梦数十女子拜于前，曰：“君全吾族，德莫大焉。君善人，必有后矣！”陈五十岁后果得三子。

    Explanation

    632

    Chen Xiangshu from Hangzhou, at the age of forty, had no children. Suddenly, he dreamed of arriving at a place where, in the green shade, there was a large mansion. Surrounding the mansion were buildings with green windows and red railings, and embroidered curtains hanging low. Young and beautiful women were sitting in rows, their faces like sandalwood and eyebrows like black jade, adorned with colorful silk decorations. He suspected it was a brothel and wandered around unable to leave. Suddenly, more than ten people gathered at the foot of the building, some holding torches and others holding woven grass. One person was holding a torch and directing the others to burn it. Suddenly, the fire was intense, blazing brightly. The women cried out, and some fell from the building. Chen looked at the people and recognized them as his classmate and friend Zhou Zijin. He immediately shouted at them, saying, "How can you burn someone's house?" He struggled to take the torch and then waved the group away. Then the fire went out, and the crying in the building stopped. Chen suddenly woke up, not understanding what it meant. The next day, he visited Zhou, intending to tell him about the dream. Upon arriving, Zhou had just been stung by a bee nest on the loquat tree in front of his斋 (a room for meditation or study). He ordered his servant to attack it with fire. Chen realized that his dream was about this. Then he told Zhou about the dream, stopping him from using fire. That night, Chen dreamed again of dozens of women bowing before him. They said, "You have saved our clan, no virtue is greater. You are a good person, you will surely have descendants!" At the age of fifty, he indeed had three sons.

    633

    钱唐朱振甫，居十五奎巷。光绪己卯夏，独坐楼上，忽风雨骤至。朱起闭窗，为暴雷摄去，家人无知者。雨霁登楼，失朱所在，见窗有火灼痕，遗一履于窗下，举家惶惑大索。至楼后隙地，见朱已死，胸次尚温，面目焦黑。所衣白布短衫，苍黑成纹，似字非字，似画非画，皆莫之识。扶至床上，夜半而苏，问雷摄后如何情状，不能言也。适其家有病疟者，寒热交作之时，偶以衫覆之，病若失，遂异而藏之。自后病疟及疫疠，或为鬼魅所祟，得此衫，无不立愈，踵门来借者无虚日，有余杭某氏女祟于狐，因借衫去，甫入门，家人即见一物大如犬，自窦逃出。女在床中闻硫磺气，神识顿清，狐亦绝迹。于是朱之雷衫，益加宝贵。庚辰夏，三圣桥有许姓者，其子忽得异疾，白昼言动如常，入夜则肤冷如冰，始而寒战，继以谵语。

    Explanation

    633

    In the summer of the Geng Mao year of Guangxu, Zhu Zhenfu from Qiantang lived in Fifteen Kui Lane. One day, he was sitting alone on the upper floor when suddenly a violent storm came. Zhu Zhenfu got up to close the window, but he was struck by a sudden thunderbolt and swept away. No one in the family knew what had happened. After the rain stopped, they went up to the upper floor and found Zhu Zhenfu missing. They saw a fire mark on the window frame and a shoe left under the window. The whole family was in a state of panic and searched everywhere. Later, they found Zhu Zhenfu, whose chest was still warm, but his face was burned black. The white cotton shirt he was wearing had turned dark and formed patterns, looking like characters but not quite, and like paintings but not quite. They carried him to the bed, and he woke up in the middle of the night. When asked about his experience after being struck by lightning, he could not speak.

    Just then, there was someone in his family suffering from malaria, experiencing alternating chills and fever. He happened to cover him with the shirt, and the illness seemed to disappear. They began to treasure the shirt. The next year, a woman from Yuhang was possessed by a fox spirit. She borrowed the shirt, and as soon as she entered the door, her family saw something as big as a dog escape through a hole. The woman in the bed smelled the sulfur smell and suddenly became clear-minded; the fox spirit disappeared. The shirt gained even more value.

    In the Geng Chen year, there was a man surnamed Xu at San Sheng Bridge. His son suddenly developed a strange illness, acting normally during the day but with his skin as cold as ice at night. He started shivering and then began to speak incoherently.

    634

    其语率操北音，索酒食，索衣服玩好。不与，则詈骂其父母，甚且殴击；如命以进，则亦惟毁坏之而已。每自言吾家世大富贵，宫室如何华美，服物如何珍异，又曰：“某达官，吾门下也；某富翁，吾奴隶也。”如是者哓哓达旦，天明乃已。问之，则云：“每至黄昏，即有一人面团而ㄕ短，手持冷水一盂，泼我头上，遂昏然罔觉。凡所云云，皆不知也。”家人不堪其扰，拟借朱衫以驱之。则大骂曰：“吾乃天帝所使，风伯雨师且听我指挥，区区雷烬之余，焉能吓我邪？”ㄏ其母之发而殴之，众为跪求，始释。次夕，有所亲不谋于其家，衷朱衫而至。病者忽大喜，曰：“今夕得免矣。”问何故，曰：“此鬼缩小仅尺许，匿东北隅，股弁不止。且闻硫磺气甚烈，岂室中有朱家雷衫乎？”其所亲即出此衫，周麾于室，病者曰：“鬼巳入溺器中而没矣。”自是遂愈。

    Explanation

    634

    Translation

    Source Text (Chinese)

    其语率操北音，索酒食，索衣服玩好。不与，则詈骂其父母，甚且殴击；如命以进，则亦惟毁坏之而已。每自言吾家世大富贵，宫室如何华美，服物如何珍异，又曰：“某达官，吾门下也；某富翁，吾奴隶也。”如是者哓哓达旦，天明乃已。问之，则云：“每至黄昏，即有一人面团而ㄕ短，手持冷水一盂，泼我头上，遂昏然罔觉。凡所云云，皆不知也。”家人不堪其扰，拟借朱衫以驱之。则大骂曰：“吾乃天帝所使，风伯雨师且听我指挥，区区雷烬之余，焉能吓我邪？”ㄏ其母之发而殴之，众为跪求，始释。次夕，有所亲不谋于其家，衷朱衫而至。病者忽大喜，曰：“今夕得免矣。”问何故，曰：“此鬼缩小仅尺许，匿东北隅，股弁不止。且闻硫磺气甚烈，岂室中有朱家雷衫乎？”其所亲即出此衫，周麾于室，病者曰：“鬼巳入溺器中而没矣。”自是遂愈。

    Translation (English)

    His speech was predominantly in the northern dialect, and he requested food and drink, as well as clothes and toys. If not given, he would curse his parents, and even go so far as to beat them; if told to bring them, he would only destroy them. He often boasted that his family had been greatly wealthy and noble, describing how magnificent their palaces were and how rare and exquisite their possessions were. He also said, "Certain high-ranking officials are under my command; certain rich men are my servants." He chattered in this manner all night until dawn. When asked, he said, "Every evening at dusk, a person with a round face and short stature appears, holding a bowl of cold water, pours it on my head, and I become unconscious. All that I say, I do not know." The family could not bear the disturbance and planned to use a red robe to drive him away. He then cursed, saying, "I am sent by the Heavenly Emperor, and the Wind God and Rain God will obey my commands. How could a mere remnant of lightning scare me?" He pulled his mother's hair and beat her. The crowd knelt and begged, and he finally released her. The next evening, a relative without consulting the family, put on a red robe and came. The sick person suddenly became very happy and said, "Tonight I am saved." Asked why, he said, "This ghost has shrunk to about a foot tall, hiding in the northeast corner, trembling. Also, I smell the strong odor of sulfur. Could there be a red robe of the Zhu family in the room?" The relative took out the robe and waved it around the room, and the sick person said, "The ghost has already entered the toilet and disappeared." From then on, he recovered.

    635

    钱唐伊生，娶妻杨氏。光绪己卯乡试之前，妻忽得疾，百治不瘳。一日，作江西入口音曰：“予自江西奉真人府文牒，来浙江办闱差，与汝有缘，故寄居于此。饥矣，速为具食。”家人知其狐也，置瓜果焉。即剖一大西瓜，食之而尽，又食他果亦尽，仍索饭。饭至，尽数器。问之，则曰：“下咽即消，不觉其多也。”家人患之，而无如何。亦时见其形，一少年妇也。习见之，亦不为怪。

    Explanation

    635

    A Man from Qiantang, named Yi, married a woman surnamed Yang. Before the provincial examination in the year Gengmao of Guangxu's reign, his wife suddenly fell ill, and despite numerous treatments, her condition did not improve. One day, she spoke with a dialect from Jiangxi and said, "I came from Jiangxi with a document from the True Man's Office, to come to Zhejiang to handle the examination duties. I have a karmic connection with you, so I am staying here temporarily. I am hungry; quickly prepare food for me." The family knew that it was a fox spirit, so they placed fruits and melons there. She immediately cut a large watermelon and ate it all, then also ate all the other fruits, and still demanded rice. When the rice was brought, it was all eaten. When asked, she said, "It disappears immediately after swallowing, and one does not realize how much was eaten." The family was troubled by this, but could not do anything about it. They also sometimes saw her form, which appeared as a young woman. Over time, they became accustomed to seeing her and did not find it strange.

    636

    时学使者方录送遗才，伊患不取，问于妻，妻曰：“必取，但不高耳。”已而果然。伊又问：“场中题目，可预知乎？”曰：“不能。”再三问，则曰：“君无忧焉，吾亦当入闱，必来相助。”及入闱，无他异，但文机颇不蹇涩。既出，责其不助，曰：“已助君矣。”问中式否，曰：“恐未必也。”有虞、李二生，伊之友也，以闱中文质之。曰：“虞君，文中之豪；李君，文中之禅也。”问中否，谢不知。及榜发，皆不售。伊以告，妻曰：“余早知之矣。”榜后踪迹稍疏，至第三日辞去。从此遂绝，而妻病亦瘳。

    Explanation

    636

    During the time when the provincial examiners were selecting talented individuals, the subject was worried that he would not be chosen. He asked his wife, who said, "They will definitely select you, but not highly." And indeed, that turned out to be the case. The subject then asked, "Can the exam questions be predicted?" His wife replied, "No." After asking repeatedly, she said, "Do not worry, I will also enter the examination hall and will definitely come to help you." When he entered the examination hall, there were no unusual occurrences, but the writing was not difficult. After the exam, he blamed her for not helping, but she said, "I have already helped you." He then asked if he had passed, and she said, "I fear it might not be the case." There were two students named Yu and Li, who were friends of the subject, and they verified the exam essays. His wife said, "Yu, the hero of literature; Li, the sage of literature." When asked if they had passed, she said she did not know. When the results were announced, neither Yu nor Li passed. The subject told his wife, who said, "I knew it all along." After the results, their contact became less frequent, and on the third day, he left. From then on, the contact ceased completely, and the wife's illness also recovered.

    637

    李钦，字又安，四川新都县学生，与同学二人赁屋村中，习举子业。夜闻几案有声，以为鼠也，晨起视之，书籍笔砚，皆有移动之迹，每夜皆然。疑有鬼物为祟，召巫禳之。巫置锣鼓于地，俄而自鸣；巫又削桃为弋，植于地上，俄自拔起，击巫之头。巫无如何，竟逃去，遂不复禳。一夕，闻有磨墨声，李大言曰：“尔既磨墨，必能作字，尔究何怪？盍书以告我。”明日，视案上大书“土阜中也”四字。至夕，众乃祝之曰：“尔既为土地之神，庙在何处？”书云：“在某处吾土地。”如言发之，果得一神象。众乃集赀为建小庙，安神像于其中。

    Explanation

    637

    Li Qin, courtesy name You'an, was a student from Xindu County, Sichuan. He and two fellow students rented a house in the village to study for the imperial examinations. One night, they heard a sound on their desk, which they initially thought was a mouse. Upon waking, they found that their books and writing utensils had been moved. This happened every night, leading them to suspect that a ghost or spirit was causing trouble. They summoned a shaman to perform an exorcism. The shaman placed a drum and gong on the ground, which suddenly began to sound by themselves. The shaman then carved a peach into a wooden rod and planted it on the ground, only to have it pulled up and strike the shaman’s head. Unable to proceed, the shaman fled, and the exorcism was abandoned.

    One night, they heard the sound of grinding ink. Li Qin loudly said, “If you can grind ink, you must also be able to write. What kind of spirit are you? Why not write something to tell me?” The next morning, they found the words “土阜中也” (in the earth mound) written on the table. That night, they asked, “If you are the land deity, where is your temple?” The writing replied, “At a certain place, my land deity.” Following the message, they indeed found a statue of the land deity. They then raised funds to build a small temple and placed the deity’s image inside.

    638

    已而案上仍有声，众曰：“神必与我等有缘，能吟诗乎？”及旦，果于案上得诗二章，颇秀丽可喜。乃和其诗，写而焚之，自是日有酬唱之作。众曰：“虽见翰墨，未接言谈，能与吾辈共语乎？”室中忽有声曰：“可，然须灭烛。”于是夜间欲与神语，辄灭烛。神雅善谈论，经史异同，文章得失，下而医卜星相，江湖术士之学，无不通晓。兼之劝善规过，言近指远，众皆叹服，以为益友。远近闻风，暮夜咸集。有问疑事者，有乞书画者，皆如愿而去。如是年余，众曰：“吾辈可谓神交矣，然不睹风采，终为恨事，能使一见乎？”曰：“然烛即见。”乃然烛，而三人者所见各异：一为中年人，胜国衣冠；一为老年人，服本朝一品之服；一为少年人，巾服如明代诸生。一见之后，其象即灭，仍灭烛而与谈。

    Explanation

    638

    Afterwards, there was still a sound coming from the table. The group said, "This god must have a special connection with us, can it recite poetry?" By dawn, they indeed found two poems on the table, which were quite beautiful and pleasing. They then composed a poem in response, wrote it down, and burned it. From then on, there were daily exchanges of poems. The group said, "Though we have seen its writing, we have not yet had a conversation with it, can it converse with us?" Suddenly, a voice came from the room saying, "Yes, but you must extinguish the candle." So, whenever they wanted to converse with the god at night, they would extinguish the candle. The god was very good at discussing, covering topics such as the differences and similarities between Confucian classics and historical texts, the merits and flaws of literary works, and practical knowledge such as medicine, divination, astrology, and the arts of the itinerant scholars. It also encouraged good deeds and corrected mistakes, with its words being close but its meaning far-reaching. Everyone was amazed and respected it, considering it a beneficial friend. People from far and near heard about it and gathered at night. Some asked about doubts, others requested calligraphy and paintings, and all left as they wished. Like this, for over a year, the group said, "We can say we have a spiritual friendship, but we have not seen its appearance, it is a regrettable matter, can we see it once?" The god replied, "Yes, but you must light the candle to see it." They then lit the candle, and the three of them saw different things: one saw a middle-aged person in the attire of a noble from a previous dynasty; one saw an elderly person in the attire of a first-rank official of the current dynasty; one saw a young person in the attire of a scholar from the Ming dynasty. After seeing it once, the image disappeared, and they extinguished the candle and continued talking.

    639

    众又曰：“既见风采，益深敬慕，君居何处？可许一往乎？”曰：“可。”遂与定期。及期，诸人就寝，梦步出村中，行半日许，便非复蜀中山水，观其风景，如在燕赵之郊。乘车而行，薄暮始到。朱门洞开，绿阴币地，广庭之内鹤舞猿吟。有老翁延客入，款接殷勤，陈设富丽。次日，又张筵以宴之，佐以音乐，席上果品，皆北产也。老翁各取数枚以赠，置怀袖中。又次日，仍循原路而归，至所居始觉，以为一梦历三日矣。问之家僮，不过是日晨起稍晏耳，怀中之果累累皆在，境虽幻而物则真，咸异之。然自此神竟不复至。李晚年以医游公卿间，颇有声，为人言其事甚详，此其大略耳。

    Explanation

    639

    The group said, "Having seen your appearance, we admire and respect you even more. Where do you reside? May we come visit you?" He replied, "Yes." They then agreed on a date. When the time arrived, everyone went to bed, and they dreamed of walking out of the village. After walking for about half a day, the scenery was no longer the mountains and waters of Sichuan, but rather as if in the outskirts of Yan and Zhao. They traveled by carriage, arriving at dusk. The red gate was wide open, and green shade covered the ground. Within the vast courtyard, cranes danced and monkeys cried. An old man invited the guests inside, warmly receiving them. The arrangements were luxurious. On the next day, he set up another feast to entertain them, accompanied by music. The fruits on the table were all from the north. The old man took several pieces to give as gifts, placing them in their sleeves or pockets. On the following day, they returned along the same path, upon returning home, they realized they had dreamed for three days. They asked the servant, and it was just that this morning they had woken up a bit late. The fruits in their pockets were still there, the scenery was illusory, but the objects were real, and all found it strange. However, from then on, the spirit never came again. In his old age, Li traveled among the nobles as a doctor, gaining some fame, and spoke about the matter in great detail. This is just a summary of it.

    640

    或曰土地之神，必不如是，殆狐魅托为之，理或然也。日本国人，多嗜食鳗。然虽嗜之，又甚畏之，曰：“是有灵异，能为祟。”故不敢自杀。凡启酒肆者，必多畜鳗，以供人之求，代之操刀焉。尝有醉客三四人，夜款酒家之门，时已三更，肆中人皆睡矣。客从门外问曰：“有鳗也无？”所畜之鳗于水中同声答曰：“无。”主人大惊。至天明，尽纵其所畜之鳗，即日改业。又一酒肆善烹鳖，享其利有年矣。所生子女手足皆短小，不满三寸，厥形如鳖，彼中人皆云杀生之报也。又有鬻豆腐者，其主妇憎一狗，沃以沸汤，狗体糜烂，叫号三日而死。时妇已孕，及期生一女，自右额至右耳生黑毛长五六分，剃之复生。今已嫁矣，毛则如故。人多见之者，与狗毛无异。

    Explanation

    640

    Someone said that the land god must not be like this, it is likely that fox spirits are pretending to be it, and the reasoning might be correct. The people of Japan greatly enjoy eating eels. However, even though they love them, they also greatly fear them, saying, "They have spiritual power and can cause harm." Therefore, they dare not kill them themselves. Those who open wine shops must keep many eels, to supply people's needs and prepare them on their behalf. Once, three or four drunk guests knocked at the door of a wine shop at night, around midnight, and everyone in the shop was asleep. The guests asked from outside the door, "Do you have any eels?" The eels kept in the shop answered in unison from the water, "No." The owner was greatly surprised. By dawn, he released all the eels he had kept and changed his profession on the same day. Another wine shop was skilled in cooking turtles and had enjoyed its benefits for many years. The children they had born had short and small hands and feet, not exceeding three inches, and their shape was like turtles. People in that place all said it was retribution for killing living beings. There was also a tofu seller whose wife hated a dog. She poured boiling water over it, and the dog's body became rotten, shrieking for three days before dying. At the time, the woman was pregnant, and at the time of delivery, she gave birth to a girl. From the right forehead to the right ear, black hair grew five or six inches long, and when it was cut off, it grew back. Now she is married, and the hair is still the same. Many people who have seen it say it is no different from dog hair.

    641

    荭村翁，日本人，谈者失言其姓名，不能知也。有一奴，性刚暴，曾于竹林中得一野鸡之卵，拾归煮食之。夜分闻奴号叫之声，翁以为中其毒，往视之，则有一蛇长六七尺，盘绕膺背间，奴力解不能脱，乃以刀断之。天明复煮食其蛇，后竟无他。世传蛇雉相交，是生蛟。疑此卵雉母而蛇父，故蛇来求其子也。日本有老妪，畜一猫，甚爱之。而猫极馋，邻池所畜之鱼，为其窃食无算。邻以妪所爱，忍弗言。一日妪携猫至佛寺，寺有武夫寓焉。方招客会饮，酒肴已陈，以客未集，故未就坐。猫登筵大嚼，杯盘狼藉，武夫怒甚，拔刀杀之。妪闻猫死，走来抱猫而哭，忽中风疾，手足痿痹，寺僧送之归。妪自此日夜号叫，近听之，尚如人语；远听之，俨如猫鸣，三日亦死。

    Explanation

    641

    Mr. Red Village, a Japanese man, had his name lost in conversation, and thus it is unknown. He had a slave who was fierce and violent, who once found an egg of a wild chicken in the bamboo grove, picked it up, cooked it, and ate it. At midnight, he heard the slave's cries, thinking he had been poisoned, and went to see him. There, he found a snake about six or seven feet long coiled around his chest and back. The slave tried to free himself but could not, so he cut the snake off with a knife. The next day, he cooked and ate the snake again, and afterward, there was no further incident. It is said that when snakes and wild chickens mate, they give birth to a jiao (a type of dragon). It is speculated that this egg might have been laid by a wild chicken mother and a snake father, hence the snake came to seek its offspring. In Japan, there was an old woman who kept a cat, which she loved very much. However, the cat was extremely greedy, and it stole and ate countless fish from the neighbor's pond. The neighbor, out of respect for the old woman's affection for the cat, tolerated it without saying anything. One day, the old woman took the cat to a Buddhist temple, where a warrior was staying. The warrior was just about to invite guests for a banquet, with wine and dishes already set out, but the guests had not yet arrived, so he had not yet sat down. The cat climbed onto the table and began to feast, causing the table to be in a mess. The warrior became very angry and drew his sword to kill the cat. When the old woman heard that the cat had died, she rushed over to embrace and cry over the cat, suddenly struck by wind disease, with paralyzed hands and feet. The temple monks sent her back home. From then on, the old woman cried day and night, and when heard up close, her voice still sounded like a human's, but when heard from a distance, it sounded like a cat meowing. After three days, she also died.

    642

    日本有鬻蔬果者，夏日梨熟，晨起至园中采之。有一蛇盘绕梨树，逐之不去，乃取巨石击之，中其头，立死。及还至家，其长女呼曰：“有数蛇追逐我甚急！”然其身畔实无一蛇，知所杀者为祟。乃招佛会之友，环而诵佛号，使女坐其中。良久，蛇果不见。众方欲散，而次女又啼呼如长女，复环而诵焉，次女曰：“蛇已去矣。”而三女复然，因诵佛如初，蛇乃绝迹，遂无他异。按所谓佛会者，中国多有之，聚男妇数十人同声念佛，竟日而罢，不谓彼中亦有此风也。

    Explanation

    642

    In Japan, there was a person who sold vegetables and fruits. During the summer, when the pears were ripe, he would rise early in the morning to go to the garden to pick them. One day, he found a snake coiled around a pear tree. He chased it away, but it wouldn't leave, so he took a large stone to hit it. He struck its head, and it died immediately. When he returned home, his eldest daughter cried out, "Several snakes are chasing me very urgently!" However, there was not a single snake by her side. He realized that the snake he had killed was causing trouble. He then invited friends from the Buddhist assembly, surrounded them, and chanted the Buddha's name. He made the girl sit in the middle. After a long time, the snake finally disappeared. Just as the crowd was about to disperse, the second daughter began to cry and call out like the eldest daughter. They again surrounded her and chanted. The second daughter said, "The snakes have gone." The third daughter had the same experience. Because they chanted the Buddha as before, the snakes finally disappeared completely, and there were no other unusual occurrences. According to the Buddhist assembly, this practice is common in China, where men and women gather in groups of dozens to chant the Buddha's name all day, and it is not surprising that this custom exists in Japan as well.

    643

    日本人多食河豚而死者。俗云中河豚毒者，埋土中一昼夜必活，然未之验也。有一渔师，食河豚而死。其家极贫，邻里之人醵钱买薄棺敛之，舁而置之郊外厝屋。厝屋者，为小屋于旷野，赁于人以停棺。吾浙杭州城外多有之，谓之攒基，即此类也。夜半死者忽苏，而身已在棺中，幸棺甚薄，力掀其盖以出。闻户外人声喧杂，窥之，则数人席地而博，思借灯火归家，乃启户而出。博徒见之以为鬼也，大骇，四散而走。

    Explanation

    643

    Many people in Japan die after eating pufferfish (fugu). Folklore suggests that those poisoned by fugu will surely live if buried in the ground for one day and night, but this has never been proven. Once, a fisherman died after eating fugu. His family was extremely poor, so the neighbors collected money to buy a thin coffin and place his body in it, then carried it to a temporary storage place for coffins outside the city. A厝屋 is a small house in the wilderness, rented out to people to store coffins. Such places are common outside Hangzhou in Zhejiang province, known as攒基. At midnight, the dead man suddenly came to life, and found his body already in the coffin. Fortunately, the coffin was very thin, and he used his strength to lift the lid and came out. He heard noisy voices outside the door and peeked through, seeing several people gambling on the ground. They wanted to borrow a light to go home, so he opened the door and came out. The gamblers saw him and thought he was a ghost, greatly frightened, and ran away in all directions.

    644

    所遗银钱之类，狼藉地上，不暇收拾，渔师尽怀之以归。日本有嫠妇，甚贫，节衣缩食垂三十年，积银四十余两。人或假其银三十五两，行贾于外。俄而妇死，妇故有一女，鬻于人为婢。妇死之夜，其女梦母来告之曰：“我已死矣，有银四十余两。除某人所假外，尚存十余两在床下，汝宜速往取之，毋落人手。”女惊哭而寤，即还母家，果于床下得银如数，而假银者在数百里外，亦梦妇来言曰：“我已死矣，所假之银必归之吾女，勿与他人！”寤而访之，知妇果死，不敢负，归所假银于其女。

    Explanation

    644

    The money and valuables left behind were scattered on the ground, with no time to gather them, and the fishermen all took it with them and returned home. In Japan, there was a widow who was very poor and had saved and lived frugally for nearly thirty years, accumulating more than forty taels of silver. Someone borrowed thirty-five taels of silver from her to go trading outside. Soon after the woman died, her daughter dreamed that her mother came to tell her, "I have already died. I have more than forty taels of silver. Except for what someone borrowed, there is still more than ten taels left under the bed. You should quickly go get it, do not let it fall into someone else's hands." The daughter woke up crying in shock and immediately went back to her mother's house, indeed finding the silver in the amount mentioned under the bed. The borrower, who was hundreds of miles away, also dreamed that the woman came to say, "I have already died. The borrowed silver must be returned to my daughter, do not give it to anyone else!" Upon waking, he learned that the woman had indeed died and did not dare to be unfaithful, returning the borrowed silver to her daughter.

    645

    日本西京有所谓平野巫者，皆妇女为之。各有一木匣，方一尺许，从不启以示人，不知其中何物也，能招死者之魂，与生人问答。尝有乡间一女子，与邻舍少年通，生一子。父母耻之，蓄其子而鬻女于西京仕宦家为婢。居无何，女死，父母如西京取其丧。检箱中少一衣，疑为人所，又以女死不及面诀，亦殊念之。乃谒巫者，请招其魂。巫焚香烛，祈于神，倚匣若睡。久之为女语，谢其父母。问衣所在，曰：“此在家中某箱内，固未携来也。”又曰：“吾儿肾囊，不必医治，十三岁自愈。”盖其子贤囊大如瓠，后至十三岁，果缩小如常；衣亦果在某箱内，悉如巫言。

    Explanation

    645

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    646

    日本某氏妇，因病而死，半夜忽蹶然而起，步向后房。众大惊，疑其复生，或曰：“是尸变也。”皆从其后，以观其异。妇至后房，抱一箧而倒。抚之，其体如冰，候其口鼻，了无出入之气，未尝生也。发其箧，亦初无珍异之物，惟有书一函。读之，乃知妇生前曾与一人有私，此书即所欢者诒之。妇虽死而一灵不昧，尚念此书在箧中必为人见，欲自毁之，而力已不能，适以自发其覆，可哀亦可笑矣。

    　　日本有小村聚名曰长泽，有渠水贯其中，水极清，而深不过五六寸。村人某夜行，闻渠中有人言笑，以烛烛之，则其邻叟也，不解衣而浴，曰：“快哉快哉，惜水太冷，宜益以热水。”知其遇鬼，扶之归家，始如梦觉。问其故，曰：“有人邀我饮于酒肆，饮毕复邀我浴于浴堂，甚觉畅适，稍嫌水冷耳。”

    Explanation

    646

    A Japanese woman from a certain household died of an illness. One night, she suddenly rose up, took a few steps toward the back room, and collapsed. The people around her were greatly surprised and wondered if she had come back to life. Some said, "This is a case of shi bian (corpse transformation)." They all followed her to see what would happen. When the woman reached the back room, she fell over holding a box. They examined her, and her body was as cold as ice; there was no breath entering or leaving her nose and mouth, and she had never truly lived. They opened the box, but found no precious or unusual items inside; there was only a single letter. When they read it, they learned that the woman had been having an affair with someone before her death, and this letter was from her lover. Although she was dead, her spirit remained conscious and still thought that this letter in the box would be seen by others. She wanted to tear it up herself, but lacked the strength to do so. Just as she was about to reveal it, she was discovered, which was both pitiful and laughable.

    In Japan, there is a small village named Chōze, where a canal runs through it. The water in the canal is extremely clear, but only a few inches deep. One night, a villager was walking when he heard someone talking and laughing in the canal. He lit a candle to look, and saw it was his neighbor, an old man, who was bathing without taking off his clothes. The old man said, "How pleasant, how pleasant, but the water is too cold; it should be mixed with hot water." The villager realized he had encountered a ghost and helped him back home, where the old man finally woke up from his dream. When asked about the incident, the old man said, "Someone invited me to drink at an inn, and after we finished drinking, they invited me to bathe in a bathhouse. It felt very relaxing, but the water was just a bit too cold."

    647

    日本有一处，松树成林，广袤可二里许。吟风啸雨，不见天日，旷无人居，亦无稻田蔬圃，遂为狐魅所聚处。曾有人过其处，见一狐出自草间，采野花插头上，拾落叶化为衣，被之而行，宛然一好女子也。其人从之行，见此女入市，出钱买物，因从后呼曰：“此非人，乃狐也，勿为所惑。”俄有人击其背甚痛，女子即不见，市人视其钱，乃树叶耳。又有农夫入市，买鱼、肉诸食物，盛以竹筐，携之而归。过此松林，见有两人争论，问之，曰：“吾两人拾得银券，分之不可，故相争耳。”农夫曰：“何不易钱而分之？”两人曰：“吾侪未尝入市，未知何处可以易钱？请君携此券入市，易钱而归，当分而为三，吾两人与君各得其一。”农夫大喜，乃置筐于地，曰：“我入市易钱，君等为我守此。此地多狐魅，勿为所窃也。”两人曰：“诺。”农入市，以券示人，则一枯叶也。大惊，即走还原所，筐中之物已空，两人者俱不见。张皇四顾，杳无人迹。

    647

    In Japan, there is a place where pine trees form a dense forest, spanning approximately two li. The forest is so thick that it seems to block out the sky, with only the sounds of wind and rain audible. The area is desolate, without any human habitation or cultivated land, and has become a gathering place for fox spirits. Once, a man passed through this area and saw a fox emerge from the grass. The fox picked wildflowers to adorn its head and transformed fallen leaves into clothing, wearing them as it walked. The fox appeared as a beautiful woman. The man followed her and saw her enter the market, where she spent money to buy things. He then called out from behind, warning, "This is not a human, but a fox. Do not be deceived by her." Suddenly, someone struck the woman's back very hard, and she disappeared. The market people examined the money she had spent and found it to be nothing but leaves. Another farmer entered the market, buying fish, meat, and other foods, placing them in a bamboo basket, and carrying them home. Passing through this pine forest, he saw two people arguing. He asked them what was happening, and they explained, "We found a silver voucher and are arguing over how to divide it." The farmer suggested, "Why not exchange it for money first and then divide it?" The two people replied, "We have never been to the market and do not know where to exchange the voucher. Please take this voucher to the market, exchange it for money, and return. We will divide it into three parts, and each of us will get one share." The farmer was delighted and placed his basket on the ground, saying, "I will go to the market to exchange the money. You two will guard this for me. This place is full of fox spirits. Do not let them steal my belongings." The two people agreed, "Yes." The farmer went to the market and showed the voucher to people, who revealed it to be a withered leaf. The farmer was greatly surprised and rushed back to where he had left his basket. He found the basket empty, and the two people were gone. Looking around in panic, he found no trace of anyone.

    648

    日本国神佛之庙，皆有灵牌。其制以纸为之，大者六七寸，小者三四寸，上书某神某菩萨名号。国人咸佩带之，谓可以备不虞，名曰：“守护札。”当我朝同治六七年间，其国中一日大风，有物漫空而下，如雪花之飞舞，及坠地视之，则皆灵牌也。又有铜石佛像及金银铜钱，亦杂其中而下。其钱皆在花木之上，人家花盆中亦往往得之，黏著枝叶，摇之不落。一时举国若狂，人家皆构神坛，结神会，男服女服，女服男服，鸣钲击鼓，走于通衢，呼曰：“岂不好乎！岂不好乎！”老少奔波，填衢溢郭，如是久之始息。

    Explanation

    648

    In the shrines and temples of Japan, there are spirit tablets. These tablets are made of paper, with the larger ones measuring six or seven inches and the smaller ones three or four inches. They bear the names and titles of certain gods and bodhisattvas. All the people wear these tablets, believing they can prepare for unforeseen events, and they call them "Guardian Talismans." During the sixth and seventh years of the Tongzhi era of my dynasty, one day in Japan, a strong wind blew, and objects flew through the air, falling like dancing snowflakes.
