Upon closer inspection, these objects turned out to be spirit tablets. There were also copper and stone Buddha statues, as well as gold, silver, and copper coins, mixed among the falling objects. The coins were found stuck to branches and leaves, and often in flower pots, adhering so firmly that they did not fall off when shaken. At that time, the whole country went wild. People built spirit altars and formed spirit gatherings, with men dressed in women's clothing and women in men's clothing. They beat drums and gongs, rushing through the streets, shouting, "How wonderful it is! How wonderful it is!" Old and young people rushed about, filling the streets and overflowing the city walls. It took a long time for this frenzy to subside.

    649

    又有人家子弟，或五日或十日不归其家。归而问之，则曰：“有僧招之去，从某山，过某山，至某山。”率皆高山峻岭，其所至之某山，则必素有神庙者也。计其程，则一日必行千余里，盖飞行于空中也。俗云此天狗所为。天狗者，彼中相传多在深山穷谷，古神佛之灵场。其形如人，衣僧衣，执羽扇，鼻准高七八寸，背有翅。又有所谓木叶天狗者，觜如鸟，手足皆四爪，背亦有翅，如中国所传雷神之形。赏善罚恶，其所司也。审尔则是神矣，不知何以呼为天狗，而是时且为人祟，又不知其何理也。彼国倘有《五行志》，此等事在所必载矣。

    Explanation

    649

    There are also young men from households who sometimes do not return home for five or ten days. When they return and are asked about it, they say, "A monk invited me away, and I went from one mountain to another, until I reached a certain mountain." These mountains are generally high and steep. The mountain they arrive at is always one that has had a shrine. Calculating the distance, they must have traveled thousands of miles in a single day, flying through the air. It is commonly said that this is the work of the tian gou. The tian gou, it is said, is mostly found in deep mountains and remote valleys, ancient sites of gods and Buddhas. Its shape is like a human, wearing monk's robes, holding a feather fan, with a nose and bridge seven or eight inches high, and wings on its back. There is also a variant called the wood leaf tian gou, which has a beak like a bird, four claws on its hands and feet, and wings on its back, resembling the form of the thunder god as described in China. It rewards good and punishes evil, which is its duty. If this is true, then it is a deity, but it is not known why it is called a tian gou, and at that time it is a nuisance to people, yet the reason for this is not understood. If that country has a Wuxing Zhi, such matters would certainly be recorded.

    650

    《史记。天官书》云：海旁蜃气象楼台。日本国越中州有鱼津港，港中往往见此象，每当天色朦胧，忽然而见。其色如淡墨，横亘空中，始止一层，须臾又见一层，或二三层，城郭楼台，望之了了。亦有车马驰骋，其中人物悉具，瞬息变幻，千态万状。然必无风乃可，有风即灭矣。其地两山环合，海气凝聚，故成此象。若外洋，则未有见之者。余前记翁君广平观海市事，兹闻日本人言蜃楼，故又记之。借不及翁君所记之详也。

    Explanation

    650

    The Tian Guan Shu from the Shiji states: Along the coast, the vapor from the sea resembles towers and pavilions. In the region of Echigo in Japan, there is a port called Yoshitsugu, where this phenomenon is often observed. It appears suddenly when the sky is hazy. The color is like pale ink, stretching across the sky. Initially, it appears as a single layer, but soon another layer appears, or even two or three layers. The urban and architectural features are clearly visible, and there are also scenes of carriages and horses galloping, with people and figures fully present. The scene changes rapidly, taking on countless forms and shapes. However, it only appears when there is no wind; if there is wind, it disappears. The area is surrounded by two mountains, and the sea's vapor coalesces to form this phenomenon. In the open sea, however, such an appearance is never seen. I previously recorded the matter of Weng Guangping observing the sea market. Now, having heard the Japanese describe the mirage as "shen lou" (蜃楼), I have recorded it again. However, it is not as detailed as what Weng Guangping recorded.

    651

    日本国筑前州海中，每有火，俗呼之为不知火。余谓此不足异，《海赋》所谓“阴火潜然”者也。又其国敦贺港中，每岁除之夜，必有神火，浮于海面者三。其光流走，倏不定，往来甚速，至丙夜则尤盛，土人谓之龙灯。每岁皆然，不增不减。此则可异矣。以上日本诸事，皆本其国人吉堂所录。吉堂姓东海氏，名复，在海外曾读余所著书。及至中国，知余有《右台仙馆笔记》之作，录此十数事，托余门下士王梦薇转达于余，因粗加润色而存之。余诗所云“旧闻都向毫端写，异事兼从海外求”，洵不虚矣。

    Explanation

    651

    In the seas off Chikuzen Province in Japan, there are often mysterious fires, which the locals call Shiranui. I believe this phenomenon is not unusual, as it is described in the Maritime Assignments as "hidden fires burning underground." Additionally, in the port of Tsuruga in Japan, every New Year's Eve, there are three divine fires that float on the sea surface. The light moves swiftly and erratically, and its speed increases especially during the third watch of the night, which the locals call Ryūtō. This phenomenon occurs every year without fail, with no increase or decrease. This is indeed a remarkable occurrence. The above events concerning Japan are all recorded by a Japanese scholar named Kitō, whose family name is East Sea and given name is Fu. He had read my works while abroad. Upon arriving in China, he learned that I had written Right Platform Immortal Pavilion Notes and recorded these ten or so events, entrusting my student Wang Mengwei to convey them to me. I then lightly polished the text and preserved it. My poem, which says, "Old stories are all written down, strange events are also sought from abroad," is indeed true.

    652

    《夷坚志》云：平江市人周翁，疟疾不止，尝闻人说疟有鬼，可出他处躲避：是宋时已有躲疟之说。光绪庚辰初冬，余在右台山馆，从者余德病疟，间日一作。三作之后，又届当疟之日。余德黎明即起，采桃叶七瓣，置诸发际，望东三揖，即趋出。走苏堤第一桥，而至净慈寺，遂游雷峰塔内。将发际桃叶取出，摘草束之，置诸地，祝曰：“尔曹居此，我去矣。”祝已，径入城中，买酒饭，饮食醉饱，薄暮而归，疟果不作，是竟被躲过矣。

    652

    《夷坚志》云：平江市人周翁，疟疾不止，尝闻人说疟有鬼，可出他处躲避：是宋时已有躲疟之说。光绪庚辰初冬，余在右台山馆，从者余德病疟，间日一作。三作之后，又届当疟之日。余德黎明即起，采桃叶七瓣，置诸发际，望东三揖，即趋出。走苏堤第一桥，而至净慈寺，遂游雷峰塔内。将发际桃叶取出，摘草束之，置诸地，祝曰：“尔曹居此，我去矣。”祝已，径入城中，买酒饭，饮食醉饱，薄暮而归，疟果不作，是竟被躲过矣。

    According to The Tales of the Supernatural (《夷坚志》), there was a man named Zhou from Pingjiang who suffered from persistent malaria. He once heard people say that malaria was caused by ghosts and that one could avoid it by going to another place. This practice of avoiding malaria was already known during the Song dynasty. In the early winter of the year Gengchen during the Guangxu era, I was staying at Right Platform Mountain Hall, and my attendant Yu De was suffering from malaria, which occurred every other day. After three episodes, it was again the day when malaria was due to occur. At dawn, Yu De got up, picked seven petals of peach leaves, placed them on his hairline, saluted three times facing east, and quickly went out. He walked to the first bridge of Suzhou Causeway and arrived at Jingci Temple, where he then toured inside Leifeng Pagoda. He took out the peach leaves from his hairline, tied them with grass, and placed them on the ground, saying, "You all stay here, I am leaving." After the blessing, he directly went into the city, bought wine and food, ate until he was full, and returned at dusk. The malaria did not occur, and thus, it was successfully avoided.

    653

    然气血壮盛者，方可用此法，否则，未有不增剧者也（《玉篇》：躲，丁果切，身也。不知何以有躲避之义）。余四岁时，自德清南埭旧居，迁临平之史家埭，赁戴氏楼屋三间以居。其东厢楼版下横木截断其一，又以小方纸书“救”字倒黏壁上，不知谁为之，亦莫知所自始。余家居此凡十二年，一无怪异。惟有孙氏子来，其人甫逾十龄，有嬴疾，坐室中，见有一妇人自东厢徐行至后屋，衣蓝布衫，黑布裙。孙问：“此何人？”时太夫人与同坐，不见也。孙固言之，太夫人往视，杳无所见，笑曰：“汝眼花耳。”孙逾年即卒，殆衰征欤！

    Explanation

    653

    Only those with robust qi and blood can use this method; otherwise, there is none who does not worsen. (Yupian: 躲, pronounced ding guo, means "body." How did it come to have the meaning of "to avoid"?) When I was four years old, I moved from the old house in the south of Deqing to Shijia Dai in Linping and rented three rooms in a building owned by the Dai family to live in. One of the horizontal beams under the floor of the eastern wing was cut off, and with small square paper, the character "救" (rescue) was written upside down and stuck to the wall. It is unknown who did it, nor is it known where it began. I lived here for twelve years, without any strange occurrences. Only the son of the Sun family came. He was just over ten years old and had a weak illness. Sitting in the room, he saw a woman slowly walk from the eastern wing to the back house. She was wearing a blue cotton shirt and a black cotton skirt. Sun asked, "Who is this?" At that time, my grandmother was sitting with him, but she did not see her. Sun insisted, and my grandmother went to look, but she saw nothing. She laughed and said, "Your eyes are playing tricks on you." Sun died the next year, perhaps it was a sign of decline.

    654

    余外家姚氏，居临平之枣山港，听事□楹东西有厢。太夫人尝于夜分从西厢至东厢，一小婢执烛以先。见听事栏杆上有一妇人凭而玩月，太夫人问何人，不应，近之不见。乃与执烛之婢同索之，听事虚无一人。其时内室之门皆阖，亦不能他去，疑其鬼也。余内子姚夫人，生平见鬼尤多。其仲姊适戴氏，戴氏居湖州，夫人往省之，时甫十余龄，未嫁也。一夕，见屏后一人行走，衣声纟卒纟祭，听之了了。视其面貌，则其仲姊之兄公也，殁数年矣，从容登楼而去。夫人自言：“所见之鬼，未有如此亲切者。”后余家僦居临平乾河沿陈氏之屋，夫人于此屋恒有所见，不为余言。

    Explanation

    654

    My maternal family, the Yao clan, resides in the Zao Mountain Port of Linping. The hall, with rooms on both the east and west sides, was where the great lady (my mother) once walked from the west room to the east room in the middle of the night. A young maid held a candle in front of her. She saw a woman leaning on the hall's railing, enjoying the moon. The great lady asked who she was, but the woman did not respond. When they approached, they could not see her. They searched for her, but the hall was empty with no one in it. All the inner room doors were closed, so she could not have gone elsewhere. They suspected it was a ghost.

    My wife, Lady Yao, has seen more ghosts than anyone else in her life. Her second sister, who married into the Dai family, lives in Huzhou. Lady Yao once visited her when she was just over ten years old and had not yet married. One evening, she heard the sound of clothes rustling behind a screen. When she looked, she saw her second sister's uncle, who had died several years ago, calmly go up the stairs and leave. Lady Yao said, "The ghosts I have seen are none as close as this one."

    Later, our family rented a house in the Chen family's property along the Dry River in Linping. Lady Yao often saw something there but did not tell me.

    655

    至同治壬戌岁，余家附火轮船北行，至天津避寇。夫人见舟中高处有鬼无数，或坐或卧，意鬼亦附海舶北行避寇欤！余《百哀》诗有云：“海舶飘零赋北征，未劳魑魅便逢迎。如何眼底分明见，人鬼居然共此行。”纪其事也。及吴中春在堂成，迁入居之，语余曰：“此屋平安，吾无所见。”余尝与门下士冯梦香孝廉言之，且云：“内人秉质素弱，此即其衰征也。”冯曰：“不然，鬼本在天地之间，与人并行而不悖。人苟秉气至清，眸子了然，则自足以见之。其前之有所见也，非衰也，乃其盛也；后之无所见也，非盛也，正其衰也。”梦香之言如此，或亦一理乎？余神识早衰，近益昏毛，虽视人之须眉且不甚了，宜其不足以见鬼矣。

    Explanation

    655

    In the year Renxu of Tongzhi, my family boarded a steamship to travel north, arriving in Tianjin to avoid bandits. My wife saw countless ghosts in the high parts of the boat, some sitting, some lying down, and wondered if the ghosts were also boarding the ship to travel north to avoid bandits! In my collection of poems, The Hundred Laments, I wrote: "The sea vessel, drifting, set out on a northern campaign, without effort, the demons welcomed us. How could I clearly see them with my own eyes? Humans and ghosts are traveling together." This records the event. When the Spring Hall in Wu was completed, we moved in to live there, and she told me, "This house is peaceful, and I see nothing." I once discussed this with my disciple, the upright scholar Feng Mengxiang, and said, "My wife, being inherently weak in nature, this is a sign of her decline." He replied, "That is not the case. Ghosts are originally in the space between heaven and earth, coexisting with humans without contradiction. If a person has a clear and discerning spirit, they can see them. The fact that she saw them before was not a sign of decline, but a sign of strength; the fact that she no longer sees them is not a sign of strength, but a sign of decline." Feng Mengxiang's words were like this, and perhaps they contain some truth? My spiritual awareness has been declining early, and recently it has become even more dim and hazy. Even seeing people's eyebrows and beards is not very clear, so it is appropriate that I can no longer see ghosts.

    656

    余兄子祖绥，字履卿，先兄福宁君少子也。光绪丙子岁举于乡，距先大夫于嘉庆丙子登贤书，适花甲一周，亦科名嘉话也。出榜前一日，内子姚夫人于吴下春在堂梦先大夫自外至，七品冠服，如生时。内子迎问之，曰：“吾将谢恩，向汝姑借朝珠耳。”其时太夫人犹在堂也，内子寤，喜曰：“履卿中矣。”余《百哀》篇有云：“阿买聪明素所怜，秋风喜赋《鹿鸣》篇。先君入梦分明甚，来借牟尼一串圆。”纪其事也。先大夫易箦时，以七品，冠服佥，无朝珠，今借珠于太夫人。七品而挂朝珠，或仍用樾翰林封典乎？是时先大夫已受二品封，岂仍以翰林为重欤？

    Explanation

    656

    My elder brother's son, Zuzui, styled Ful卿, is the younger son of my late elder brother, Foning Jun. In the Bingzi year of Guangxu, he was selected for the provincial examination, just completing a cycle of sixty years since my late father was listed in the register of scholars in the Bingzi year of Jiaqing. This is also a commendable story in the realm of scholarly success. On the day before the announcement of the results, my wife, Lady Yao, dreamed in her room in Wu that my late father came from outside, dressed in the attire of a seventh-rank official, as if he were alive. My wife greeted him and asked, "I am going to express gratitude, and I will borrow a string of court beads from your grandmother." At that time, my late grandmother was still present, and my wife woke up, saying happily, "Ful卿 has succeeded!" In my "Hundred Sorrows" poem, I wrote: "Ah Mai is clever, and I have always pitied him. In the autumn wind, he happily composed a poem on 'The Herd of Deer.' My late father entered the dream very clearly, to borrow a string of mani beads." This records the event. When my late father changed his mat, he wore the attire of a seventh-rank official but without court beads. Now, he is borrowing the beads from my late grandmother. Wearing court beads as a seventh-rank official, perhaps still using the rank of Yu as a Hanlin scholar? At that time, my late father had already received the rank of second grade, is it still considered more important to be a Hanlin scholar?

    657

    道光戊申年，余时馆于休宁汪村。是年秋冬间，附近数十里中，每夜有鬼哭声，无人不闻之，然余竟未尝闻也。生平于鬼，不闻其声，不见其形。惟咸丰庚申年，避兵至上虞舅氏。姚平泉先生为上虞校官，新捐馆舍，其家犹居署中，止余宿于花厅。至三更时，睡眼蒙龙，见一妇人趋而过。夜深安得有此，或是鬼欤？光绪己卯年，内子姚夫人卒，余感念不已，冀有所见，而竟杳然，虽每夕必梦，然止是《周礼》所谓思梦耳，非果其魂魄之入梦也。庚辰岁，余独宿右台仙馆中，夜不成寐，力行余所谓枕上三字诀（见《俞楼杂纂》）。甫欲交睫，辄为一蚊所扰，余甚苦之。念近来饮食减少，精神衰ぃ，惟恃夜枕尚安，故尚可支持，若今夕不寐，明日必大委顿矣。俄而蚊又集于面，余正无如何，蚊忽嘤然一鸣，若被人击者，从此寂然。余叹曰：“夫人有灵，为我扑杀此蚊矣。

    Explanation

    657

    During the Daoguang era, in the year 1828, I was staying at a village called Wangcun in Xiuning. During the autumn and winter of that year, there were nightly sounds of ghostly wailing within a radius of dozens of miles, and everyone heard it, but I did not. Throughout my life, I have never heard or seen ghosts. However, during the Xianfeng era, in the year 1860, I fled the war to my uncle's place in Shangyu. Mr. Yao Pingquan, who was the head of the school in Shangyu, had recently donated a building, and his family still lived in the official residence. He allowed me to stay in the flower hall. At midnight, I was half-asleep, and I saw a woman passing by. How could there be such a thing at such a late hour? Could it be a ghost? In the Guangxu era, in the year 1879, my wife, Lady Yao, passed away. I was deeply moved and hoped to see something, but nothing happened. Although I dreamed every night, these were merely the "thinking dreams" described in the Rites of Zhou, not dreams of her soul entering my dreams. In the year 1880, I stayed alone in the Right Platform Immortal Hall. I could not sleep at night and tried to practice the three-character rule I mentioned on the pillow (as seen in Yu Lou Za Zuan). Just as I was about to fall asleep, I was disturbed by a mosquito, which greatly troubled me. Thinking that my diet had decreased and my spirit had weakened, I relied only on the peaceful nights on the pillow for some comfort, so I could still manage. If I did not sleep tonight, I would be greatly exhausted tomorrow. Soon, the mosquito landed on my face again. I had no way out, and then the mosquito made a faint sound, as if it had been struck by someone. From then on, it was silent. I sighed and said, "My wife's spirit must have driven this mosquito away."

    658

    ”余家于咸丰戊午、己未间，赁居吴下石琢堂先生之城南老屋。屋有五柳园，颇具泉石之胜。园西南隅有小楼三间，或言有狐居之，然余辈时往来其上，无所见也。时蓄一小犬，毛长被地，尾短足庳，形状可喜，性又灵警，闻呼即至，姚夫人极爱之。一夕忽失犬，大索不获。疑自大门逸出，咎阍者失于伺察，阍者力言未见其出。次日清晨，园门甫启，犬即跃出，盖在园中也。然是夜实于园中遍索之，何未之见欤？疑为狐所匿，殆知人爱此犬，聊与为戏耳。庚申之乱，余举家出奔，每一人登舆，犬必送至舆前，夫人犹命守屋者善视之。及城陷，犬与守屋者俱死。余后与吾兄福宁君言之，且曰：“是皆死于其职者也。”福宁君笑曰：“然则宜有恤典。”余曰：“有之。余有《忆旧游词》，后半阕云：”金阊更回首，只蔓草荒烟，碎瓦颓墙。碧血埋何处？叹苍头黄耳，都化磷光。‘所以褒赠之者至矣。“

    Explanation

    658

    During the years of Xianfeng Wuwu and Jiwei (1858-1859), my family rented a house in the southern part of the city owned by Mr. Shi Zhuotang in Wu. The house had a garden called Wu Liuyuan, which was very beautiful with springs and stones. In the southwestern corner of the garden, there was a small three-story building, and some people said a fox lived there, but we often went up there and saw nothing. At that time, we kept a small dog with long fur that reached the ground, a short tail, and short legs. Its shape was charming, and its nature was alert and intelligent. It would come immediately when called, and Madam Yao loved it very much. One night, the dog suddenly disappeared, and a thorough search failed to find it. We suspected it had escaped through the main gate and blamed the gatekeeper for not watching carefully, but the gatekeeper insisted he had not seen it leave. The next morning, as soon as the garden gate was opened, the dog jumped out. It was in the garden. However, we had indeed searched the garden thoroughly that night, so why hadn't we seen it? We suspected it was hidden by the fox, perhaps because the fox knew people loved this dog and just played a trick on us. During the chaos of Gengshen (1860), our whole family fled. Every time someone got into the carriage, the dog would run up to the front of the carriage. Madam Yao still ordered the caretaker to take good care of it. When the city was taken, the dog and the caretaker both died. Later, I spoke to my brother Fu Ning about this, saying, "They both died in the line of duty." Fu Ning smiled and said, "Then there should be a posthumous honor." I replied, "Yes. I have a poem called Recalling Past Travels, and the second half says: 'Looking back at Jinxiang, only overgrown weeds and ruins remain. Where are the green blood buried? Sighing at the gray-haired and yellow-eared ones, all turned into phosphorescent light.' This is the highest praise and recognition for them."

    659

    仁和临平镇有永平庵。道光某年，忽传庵中地下空空有声，一时人皆往看，市廛为空。或曰其中有宝物，或曰是将出蛟。余时年尚幼，闻之惴惴焉。一两月后，往看者日少，人言亦稍息，后无他异。然究莫明其故，不知向来如此，至是始为人觉邪？抑向不如是，而是年忽有声也？有议掘而穷其异者，然皆畏之，莫敢发也。至癸卯乡试之年，余读书庵中，问之于僧，僧导至此室中。室乃泥地，既无砖石，亦无木版，以足蹋之，厥声隆隆，如挝大鼓，疑其下必空也。兵燹之后，此庵已鞠为茂草，想瓦砾之中足音久绝，亦不能更发此声矣。

    Explanation

    659

    In Renhe Linping Town, there is a temple called Yongping. In a certain year during the Daoguang era, it was suddenly reported that there were hollow sounds underground in the temple. At once, everyone went to see, and the market was empty. Some said there were treasures inside, others said a dragon was about to emerge. I was still young at the time, and I felt anxious when I heard about it. After one or two months, fewer people went to see it, and the rumors gradually subsided. However, no one really understood the reason behind it. Was it that it was only discovered this year, or had it not been like this before and suddenly made noise this year? Some suggested digging to find out the mystery, but they were all afraid and dared not proceed. In the year of the provincial examination in the Gui Mao year, I was studying in the temple and asked the monk. The monk led me to this room. The room was made of mud, having neither bricks nor wooden boards. When I stepped on it, the sound was loud and resonant, like striking a large drum, and I suspected that it must be hollow underneath. After the war and destruction, this temple had become overgrown with grass. The sound had long since ceased amidst the rubble, and it could not produce such a sound again.

    660

    郎沛昌，仁和之临平镇人。家居，忽得无名氏一书，言：“临平山之麓，景星观之后，有石如虾蟆形。其下有藏银无数，非君手开，不能得。可于某日某时，亲至其地，发而取之。”郎以为谰语，不信也。越日，又有人叩门，送一书至，所言与前同。视其人不之识，问何处得此书，曰：“途遇一人，托我寄君，不知所自来也。”匆匆辞去。于是所识者咸谓郎曰：“既有此异，试一往何伤乎？”郎从其言，届期荷锸而去，至其地，扣之，果得银甚多。而郎未携筐之属，无以盛之，乃掩以土，而至山下人家借得一畚，负之上山。遇数人似薪采者，各荷一以下，亦未及审视。及至所发之处，则已空矣。乃悟前数人所负者，即是也。郎发之而不能得之，然不经郎手，则不能发此，数人者必知其故。其两次致书，即是数人为之也，莫测其为何理矣。

    Explanation

    660

    Lang Peichang, a resident of Linping Town in Renhe, was at home when he suddenly received a letter from an unknown person. The letter stated: "At the foot of Linping Mountain, behind Jingxing Temple, there is a stone shaped like a toad. Beneath it lies an infinite amount of silver, which cannot be obtained unless you personally open it. You can come to the place at a certain day and time, open it, and take the silver." Lang thought it was a false statement and did not believe it. The next day, someone knocked on his door and delivered another letter, with the same content as before. Looking at the person, Lang did not recognize him. He asked where he got the letter, and the person replied: "I met someone on the way who asked me to deliver it to you, but I don’t know where he came from." The person then hurriedly left. Thus, those who knew Lang all said to him: "Since there is such an unusual matter, why not try going once, what harm could it do?" Lang followed their advice, arrived at the place at the appointed time with a spade, dug it, and indeed found a large amount of silver. But Lang did not bring a basket or similar container, so he had no way to carry it. He then covered it with soil and went to a house at the foot of the mountain to borrow a basket, which he carried back up the mountain. He met several people who seemed to be gathering firewood, each carrying a basket or similar container, but he did not have time to examine them. When he reached the place where he had dug, it was already empty. He then realized that the people he had met earlier were carrying the silver. Lang dug it but could not get it, but if it were not for Lang’s hand, it would not have been opened. The several people must have known the reason. The two letters were sent by these several people, but no one can understand why they did so.

    661

    杭州贡院之后，有鸭儿五圣庙。其旁有一破石臼，庙僧弃薪蒸余烬于此，间或宿火于中，以供人昏暮之求。附近居者咸便之。虽雨雪之日，未尝绝火，亦莫知其所自来，皆以为庙僧置之而已。有毛翁者，其家相距不远，旦夕经由其地，必就之吃烟，日以为常。一日忽失臼，乃共异之。庙僧因曰：“是臼本可异。吾宿火其中，亦偶尔耳，或三五日一置之，或七八日一置之，何以终岁不断乎？”于是共疑此臼为宝物，然莫可踪迹矣。毛翁之子曰永孝，字葆园，为余言之。葆园幼时，犹见此臼也。

    Explanation

    661

    Behind the examination hall in Hangzhou, there was a temple called the Five Deities Temple of Ducks. Near the temple stood a broken stone mortar. The temple monk would discard the leftover ashes from burning firewood or straw in it, and occasionally keep a fire burning inside, to provide light or warmth for people in the evening or at night. The residents nearby found this very convenient. Even on rainy or snowy days, the fire was never extinguished. No one knew where the fire came from, and everyone believed that the temple monk was responsible for maintaining it. There was a man named Mao, whose home was not far from the temple. Every day, as he passed by, he would stop to smoke by the fire, making it a daily routine. One day, the stone mortar suddenly disappeared, and everyone was surprised. The temple monk then said, "This mortar was indeed unusual. I would occasionally keep a fire in it, sometimes every three or five days, sometimes every seven or eight days. How could it have kept burning all year long without interruption?" As a result, everyone began to suspect that the mortar was a treasure, but they could not trace its origins. Mao’s son, Yongxiao, with the courtesy name Bao Yuan, told me about this. Bao Yuan still saw the stone mortar when he was a child.

    662

    同治元年，余在天津，忽喧传乡间有母杀其子女者。云其母嫠也，私于人，惧为子女所觉，谋杀之以灭口。其子甫十岁，读书村塾，微闻其谋。日加午，塾师纵诸徒归就食，是子惧不敢归。师不悟其意，谓其年幼独行，或有所畏，亲送之至其家。已而诸童食毕咸集，此子不来，师往其家问之，则已与两姊俱死矣。大惊问故。母言语支离，师迫而验之，则皆扼吭而死，形迹显然，乃闻于官。官鞫得实，怒其淫毒，械奸夫奸妇而徇于市。

    Explanation

    662

    In the first year of the Tongzhi era, I was in Tianjin when suddenly news spread of a mother in the countryside who had killed her children. It was said that the mother, a widow, had been involved in an affair with someone and feared that her children would discover it. She planned to kill them to silence them. Her son, just ten years old, was studying at a village school and had heard a little of the plot. As the sun passed noon, the village school teacher allowed the students to go home for meals, and this boy, fearing to return, did not go. The teacher, not understanding his meaning, thought that because he was young and walking alone, he might be afraid of something, and personally escorted him to his home. Later, after the other children had finished eating and gathered together, this boy did not come, so the teacher went to his home to ask him, but he had already died along with his two sisters. The teacher was greatly surprised and asked the reason. The mother's words were disjointed, and the teacher forced her to examine the bodies, which revealed that they had all strangled themselves. The evidence was clear, so he reported it to the authorities. The officials investigated and found the truth, and were angry at her lewd and cruel behavior. They chained the adulterous man and woman and paraded them through the market.

    663

    余适呼刀镊之工为余栉发，问：“此事信乎？”曰：“信。”“汝见之乎？”曰：“见之。此妇长身白皙，然目大而颧高，固非良妇也。”次日有同乡孙君自县署来，余因详询其颠末。孙曰：“安有此事？昨日县令以公事下乡，至今未返。县中寂无一事，安有此邪？”余不觉为之失笑。东坡云：“事不目见耳闻，而臆断其有无，可乎？”此事余虽未目见，实已耳闻，且所闻之人自云目见，言之凿凿，当不妄矣；而竟属子虚乌有。市虎成于三人，天下事大率类此。

    Explanation

    663

    I recently called a barber to comb my hair and asked, "Is this true?" He replied, "Yes." "Have you seen it?" I asked. "Yes," he said. "This woman is tall and fair-skinned, but she has large eyes and high cheekbones, so she is not a good wife." The next day, a friend from the same hometown arrived from the county office, and I inquired in detail about the whole affair. My friend said, "How could there be such a thing? Yesterday, the county magistrate went to the countryside for official business and has not returned yet. The county is quiet and devoid of any incidents. How could there be such a thing?" I couldn't help but laugh. Su Dongpo once said, "Can one make a judgment about whether something exists or not without seeing it with one's own eyes or hearing it with one's own ears?" Although I did not see this event with my own eyes, I had heard about it, and the person who told me claimed to have seen it, speaking with great certainty. It must not be a lie; yet, it turned out to be completely false. Rumors can become true through repeated telling, and most things in the world are similar to this.

    664

    ●卷十三彭雪琴侍郎生于嘉庆二十年十有二月。其时风雪严寒，母王太夫人临蓐四日不产，濒危矣。忽有大风发窗外，室户自辟，灯烛俱灭，人皆惊仆，太夫人亦晕绝于床。俄而侍郎生，太夫人移时乃苏，曰：“顷见一伟丈夫，面黑色，偻而入，高与楼屋齐。吾悸甚，几丧魂魄。”其事见侍郎所自撰《王太夫人行略》。余戏谓：“史传所载，有黑面仆射，有黑王相公，君夙世为谁欤？”侍郎曰：“不然，此黑丈夫必卫我者。我前生是一女子，君如不信，视吾耳犹有穿痕。”然余视之亦殊无所见，不知侍郎此言何据也。

    Explanation

    664

    The Vice Minister Peng Xueqin was born in the twelfth month of the twentieth year of the Jiaqing era. At that time, there was severe cold and snow. His mother, Lady Wang, had been in labor for four days without giving birth, and she was on the verge of danger. Suddenly, a strong wind blew from outside the window, the doors and windows opened by themselves, and all the lights and candles went out. Everyone was startled and fell, and Lady Wang also fainted on the bed. Soon after, the Vice Minister was born, and after some time, Lady Wang regained consciousness. She said, "Just now, I saw a great and powerful man with a black face, who entered bent over, as tall as a building. I was very frightened, almost losing my soul." The story is recorded in the Vice Minister's own compilation, "Biography of Lady Wang." I jokingly said, "As recorded in historical texts, there is a black-faced Vice Minister and a black-faced Wang Shenggong. Who were you in your past life?" The Vice Minister replied, "No, this black man must have been protecting me. I was a woman in my past life. If you don’t believe me, look at my ears, they still have holes from earrings." However, I looked at them and saw nothing, and I do not know what basis the Vice Minister has for this statement.

    665

    安徽乡间有陈氏孀妇，以夫弟逼嫁，雉经而死，诡言病死，无发其事者。时衡阳彭鹤皋先生方为其地巡检司，即雪琴侍郎之先德也，适以事至郡中。侍郎母王太夫人独居廨舍，一夕忽闻有妇女呼号之声，如曰冤苦，初不为意，而累夕皆然。有乳媪杨微闻陈事，曰：“得无陈氏妇乎？”太夫人祝曰：“诚是也，宜三呼。”果闻呼声三，已而风飕飕，从木末过，遂寂。先生归，太夫人白之，密访得实，闻之上官，论如律。此亦见侍郎所为《行略》中。惟先生始官怀宁三桥镇巡检，后调合肥梁园镇巡检，此事不言地名，不知为何地也。

    Explanation

    665

    In a village in Anhui, there was a widow of the Chen family who was forced to remarry by her deceased husband's brother. Unable to bear the pressure, she hanged herself, but the family falsely claimed that she had died of illness, and no one reported the incident. At that time, Mr. Peng Hegao, a magistrate from Hengyang, was serving in the area as a local official, and he was also the ancestor of Shi Lang, a high-ranking official known as Xueqin. He happened to be in the county at the time. Shi Lang's mother, Wang Taifu, was living alone in her official residence when one night she suddenly heard the cries of a woman, as if saying, "I am suffering in injustice." At first, she did not pay much attention, but the cries continued for several nights in a row. A wet nurse named Yang Mei, who had heard of the Chen family's story, said, "Could it be the wife of the Chen family?" Wang Taifu then said, "If it is her, we should call out three times." As she called out three times, a chilling wind passed through the treetops, and then all was silent. Upon returning, Mr. Peng reported the matter to Wang Taifu, who secretly investigated and confirmed the truth. The case was then reported to the superior authorities, and the matter was judged according to the law. This event is also recorded in Shi Lang's memoir, The Biography. However, Mr. Peng initially served as the magistrate of Sanqiao Town in Huaining and was later transferred to serve as the magistrate of Liangyuan Town in Hefei. The text does not specify the location of this incident, so it is unknown where it occurred.

    666

    江西鄱阳县民叶佐恩，娶同县徐姓寡妇陈氏为妻，生一子，曰福来。佐恩死，遗腹又生一子，曰福得。陈氏不能守，赘同县严磨生为婿。磨生与陈氏居叶氏者五年，始偕妻挈其前夫之二子以归。叶佐恩所遗田二亩，归严磨生耕种，以养其二子。屡荒于水，衣食不赡，而福来亦已九岁矣。乃送至坑下村徐茂拐子家，使学习裁缝，每年与钱三千四百。未几，又送福得至坑下刘光裕家，为之牧牛。其地距严氏所居曰车门湖者四十里。

    Explanation

    666

    In Poyang County, Jiangxi, there was a man named Ye Zuo'en who married a widow named Chen from the same county. They had a son named Fuli. After Ye Zuo'en died, Chen gave birth to another son, named Fude. Chen could not remain faithful, so she married a man from the same county named Yan Moseng. Yan Moseng lived with Chen and her two sons for five years before bringing them back to his own home. The two acres of land left by Ye Zuo'en were given to Yan Moseng to farm, in order to support the two sons. The land was frequently destroyed by floods, leading to a lack of food and clothing, and Fuli had already turned nine years old. Yan Moseng then sent Fuli to Xu Mao Guaizi's house in the village of Kengxia to learn tailoring, giving him 3,400 coins each year. Soon after, he sent Fude to Liu Guangyu's house in the village of Kengxia to work as a herder. The location was forty li away from Yan's residence, which was called Chumenhu.

    667

    光绪三年十二月二十五日，磨生至坑下村接福来、福得回家度岁。二十六日晨起，蓐食而行，福来负蓝布袋，内盛洋钱一、铜钱千；福得负白布袋，盛米数升。行至上亭遇雨，而磨生又发痰病，乃于亭中少息，适遇雷细毛者担两箩而至。雷亦坑下刘氏之佣，自刘氏归其家，其所居与严相近也。磨生曰：“我病不能兴，当使二子从君先行，我小愈即至。”乃以钱米并置雷之箩内，雷与二子俱行。至鸳鸯岭，语二子曰：“我与若至此分路，若可坐此，待尔翁偕归，我去矣。

    Explanation

    667

    On the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month of the third year of the Guangxu era (1877), Morsheng arrived at Keng Xia Village to fetch Fulai and Fude to return home for the New Year. On the morning of the twenty-sixth, they ate hastily and then set out. Fulai carried a blue cloth bag containing one silver dollar and a thousand copper coins; Fude carried a white cloth bag containing several measures of rice. They reached Yuan Yang Ridge and encountered rain, and Morsheng also had a cough with phlegm. They stopped briefly to rest at the pavilion. Coincidentally, Lei Xi Mao, a laborer for the Liu family, arrived carrying two baskets. Lei lived near Yan and was also from Keng Xia Village. Morsheng said, "I am too ill to get up. You two can go ahead with him. I will come soon when I am slightly better." He then placed the money and rice into Lei's baskets, and Lei and the two boys continued on their way. When they reached Yuan Yang Ridge, he told the two boys, "I will part ways with you here. You can sit here and wait for your father to return together. I will go now."

    668

    ”反其钱米于二子而归。而磨生犹卧亭中，久之病愈，雨止，天亦薄暮，乃走间道径归其家，已逮乙夜矣。问其妻，知二子未至。次日使严复仂走问雷细毛，知在鸳鸯岭相失，求之鸳鸯岭左右，无有也。上湾林有欧阳六毛者，言于二十七日遇二稚子问途，约略指示之，然问途之后亦不知所之。又有汪同兴者，启布肆于路旁，言二十七日，有二稚子因饥饿向汪索食，汪饭之而去。问饭毕焉往，不知也。问有见者无，曰：“有欧阳发仂者适在肆中，二子出亦出，或当见之。

    Explanation

    668

    He returned his money and rice to the two sons and went home. Meanwhile, Mosheng was still lying in the pavilion. After a long time, his illness healed, the rain stopped, and it was also dusk. Then he took a shortcut and returned directly to his home, having already reached the second watch of the night. He asked his wife, who told him that the two sons had not yet arrived. The next day, he sent Yan Fulei to ask Lei Ximao, who said that they had lost each other at Yanyangling. They searched around Yanyangling but found no trace of them. In Shangwan Forest, there was a man named Ouyang Liuma who said that on the 27th day, he met two young boys asking for directions. He gave them a rough indication, but after they asked for directions, he did not know where they went. There was also a man named Wang Tongxing who had opened a cloth shop by the roadside. He said that on the 27th day, two young boys came to him because of hunger and asked for food. Wang fed them and they left. When asked where they went after eating, he did not know. When asked if anyone saw them, he said, "There was Ouyang Fale who happened to be in the shop. The two boys left, and he also left."

    669

    ”二十八日，乃始得二子之尸于陈公坂，福来伤于腮，伤于耳，伤于咽喉；福得并伤于肾。钱米俱在，无所失。陈公坂距车门湖二里而近，莫知为谁所杀。或曰欧阳发仂也，或又曰欧阳丝林也。于是严磨生乃以欧阳发仂、欧阳丝林杀其二子控于官。而叶氏之族则曰：“是严磨生利其故父所遗之田，而自杀之也。”亦控于官，讼久不决。光绪四年，余亲家翁彭雪琴侍郎巡江至饶，严、叶皆具牒诉于行辕，发饶州府讯之。六年夏，侍郎至江西省垣，中丞以下咸迎候于滕王阁。

    Explanation

    669

    On the twenty-eighth day, Yan Mo Sheng finally recovered the bodies of his two sons at Chen Gong Shan. Fu Lai had injuries to his cheek, ear, and throat, while Fu De had injuries to both kidneys. The money and grain were all present, with nothing missing. Chen Gong Shan is located two miles closer to Che Men Hu, but no one knows who killed them. Some say it was Ouyang Fa Le, others say it was Ouyang Si Lin. Thus, Yan Mo Sheng accused Ouyang Fa Le and Ouyang Si Lin of killing his two sons and took the case to the authorities. However, the Ye family said, "It was Yan Mo Sheng who coveted the land left by his deceased father and killed them himself." They also took the case to the authorities, and the lawsuit remained unresolved for a long time. In the fourth year of Guangxu, my father-in-law, the Vice Minister Peng Xueqin, toured Jiangxi Province and arrived in Raozhou. Both Yan and Ye submitted petitions to the traveling office, and he ordered the Raozhou Prefecture to investigate the matter. In the summer of the sixth year, the Vice Minister arrived in the provincial capital of Jiangxi, and all officials below the level of Governor welcomed him at Tengwang Pavilion.

    670

    而严磨生之妻陈氏，又以前事诉，前马者斥之，则自投于江。侍郎急命拯之起，受其牍，言于中丞。而豫章诸大吏久知其事，咸疑严磨生实杀二子，谓二子年幼，必无仇杀者；若利其有，则何以钱米俱在？是其继父杀之无疑也。故当侍郎未至之先，已命移其狱至省中治之。而鄱阳令汪君以诚者，贤令也，初下车，叹曰：“境有此狱，而卒不得杀人者主名，上为大府忧，焉用县令为？”时案中人证咸羁管县中，汪君密使侦者于诸人一举一动一语一言，随时伺察。

    Explanation

    670

    The wife of Yan Moseng, Chen, reported a previous matter. Someone ahead on horseback rebuked her, and she threw herself into the river. The deputy minister quickly ordered someone to rescue and lift her up, received her document, and reported to the governor. The high-ranking officials in Yuanchang had known the matter for a long time and all suspected that Yan Moseng actually killed his two sons. They reasoned that the two sons were young and there must be no one who would kill them out of enmity; if someone benefited from having something, why would the money and rice still be present? Therefore, there was no doubt that the stepfather killed them. Before the deputy minister arrived, they had already ordered the case to be moved to the provincial capital for trial. Wang Jun, the magistrate of Poyang, who was known for his honesty, sighed when he first took office, saying, "There is such a case in the jurisdiction, and we could not determine the name of the person who committed the murder. This causes concern for the provincial administration. What is the use of a county magistrate if he cannot solve such cases?" At the time, all the witnesses in the case were detained in the county. Magistrate Wang secretly sent spies to observe and monitor every action and word of the people involved.

    671

    至是年五月，民间传言彭侍郎巡江且至，将亲临郡城审断冤狱，而欧阳发仂闻之，即自疑，频向丁役探问消息。是月十六日，侍郎至。先是，有浮梁沈可发，私刻木印，伪造执照，自称曾在侍郎营中；侍郎提审得实，即以军法斩之。而欧阳发仂愈惧，其夜梦中呓语，连称不好者再。汪君得其状，知杀人者必欧阳发仂矣。乃于密室供城隍神之位而祷焉。夜梦至一处，闻尸臭而不见尸，有一人以身覆之，视之欧阳发仂也。及旦，躬率诸囚诣神庙而讯之，谓欧阳发仂曰：“尔实杀人，神已告我矣。

    Explanation

    671

    By this year's fifth month, rumors circulated among the common people that Vice-Premier Peng was about to arrive for his inspection tour along the river and would personally come to the county town to review and judge wrongful cases. Upon hearing this, Ouyang Fale immediately became suspicious of himself and frequently inquired about the news from the laborers. On the sixteenth day of that month, the Vice-Premier arrived. Prior to this, there was a man named Shen Ke Fa from Fuliang who privately carved a wooden seal, forged credentials, and claimed that he had served in the Vice-Premier's camp; the Vice-Premier questioned him and found the truth, and immediately executed him according to military law. Ouyang Fale became even more afraid, and that night in his sleep, he mumbled and repeatedly called out "bad" twice. Mr. Wang observed his condition and knew that the murderer must be Ouyang Fale. Then, he set up an altar for the City God in a private room and prayed. In a dream at night, he came to a place where he smelled the stench of a corpse but could not see it. There was a person covering the body with his body; upon looking, it was Ouyang Fale. By dawn, Mr. Wang personally led the prisoners to the temple and questioned them, saying to Ouyang Fale, "You are the real killer; the god has already told me."

    672

    ”发仂虽不即承，而神色大变。越日，又讯于城隍庙，诸囚皆号哭，求神明昭雪，欧阳发仂无一言。夜将半，则大呼曰：“吾不敢欺神明，请吐实。”盖鸳鸯岭距车门湖尚三十余里。二十六日之夜，二子宿于鸳鸯岭之社庙中，明日前行，遇欧阳六毛而问途焉。又前行，饭于汪同兴布肆。而欧阳发仂见其幼稚可欺，欲诱至他处而卖之，乃追及之，而与之同行，且请为导。导至己家，宿之墙外土室中，虽其家人无知者。二十八日平明，复招之偕行，行至陈公坂，则离车门湖近矣。

    Explanation

    672

    Fa Le, though he did not immediately confess, his expression greatly changed. The next day, he was interrogated again at the Temple of the City God. All the prisoners wailed and cried out, asking the divine beings to clear their names, but Fa Le said not a word. Around midnight, he suddenly shouted, "I dare not deceive the divine beings; I will reveal the truth." It is said that Yuanyang Ridge is still more than thirty miles away from Chemen Lake. On the night of the twenty-sixth, the two boys stayed overnight at the local shrine on Yuanyang Ridge. The next morning, they proceeded on their way, met Ou Yang Liubo, and asked for directions. They continued on, eating at Wang Tongxing's cloth shop. Fa Le saw that they were young and could be deceived, and he wanted to lure them to another place to sell them. He then caught up with them, walked with them, and asked to be their guide. He led them to his own home, where they stayed in a room made of earth outside the wall, and even his family members did not know about it. On the twenty-eighth, just before dawn, he called them again and walked with them to Chen Gong Slope, which was close to Chemen Lake.

    673

    福来已识之，登山而望见其村，不肯与欧阳发仂偕。欧阳发仂强挽之，则大骂，乃痛殴其头面，又扼其吭而死。福得走且呼，曰：“杀吾兄矣！”蹴以足，伤其肾，亦杀之。发青白二布袋，见钱与米，弃之地不取，盖恐以此为人所踪迹也。汪君鞫得实，即驰白侍郎，侍郎时在焦山自然庵，读之狂喜。手批其牍，有云：“数年郁结，为之顿释。望空遥拜，为两冤魂叩谢贤令君。天下多覆盆，而有司安得如此尽心欤！又不禁感慨系之。”其年侍郎来吴下，知余有笔记之作，以全案示余，乃录其略如此。

    Explanation

    673

    Fu had recognized the place, climbed the mountain, and saw the village, but refused to go with Ouyang Fale. Ouyang Fale forcibly pulled him, leading to a loud cursing, followed by a severe beating on his head and face, and ultimately choking him to death. Fu ran and shouted, saying, "They killed my brother!" He kicked someone with his foot, injuring their kidney, and also killed them. They found two cloth bags, saw money and rice inside, but threw them on the ground and did not pick them up, fearing that this would lead to being tracked down by others. Wang investigated and found the truth, then immediately reported to the deputy minister. The deputy minister, at the time in a temple on Jiao Mountain, read the report and was extremely happy. He personally approved the document, with comments expressing, "Years of accumulated frustration are suddenly relieved. Bowing from afar to the sky, pray to the spirits of the two victims to thank the kind magistrate. There are many cases of injustice in the world, how can officials be so devoted? I cannot help but feel moved." That year, the deputy minister came to Wu, knowing that I have a notebook, showed the entire case to me, and thus recorded the main points as follows.

    674

    宋大观中，吴兴邵宗益剖蚌，得罗汉像，偏袒右肩，矫首左顾，衣纹毕具（见《天中记》引《艺苑雌黄》，余未见原书）。又宣和中，溧水人俞集于蚌中得观世音像，旁有竹两枝。菩萨相好端严，冠衣璎珞，及竹叶枝干，皆细真珠缀成者（见《夷坚乙志》第十三）。是蚌中见佛菩萨象，记载有之。彭雪琴侍郎于宁波普陀山得蚌壳二，皆有佛像。以其一施焦山自然庵；携其一至苏州，余得见之。佛像凡十有二，左三、右五、中四，分列三行。其像皆如佛寺所塑弥勒佛然，眉目有笑容，手有指，腹有脐，无不酷肖，亦一奇迹也。徐花农庶常曰：“《金刚经》有一佛、二佛、三四五佛之语，是蚌所孕，正可谓之三四五佛矣。

    674

    During the Dazhong period of the Song Dynasty, Shao Zongyi of Wu County cut open a clam and found a statue of a bodhisattva, with the right shoulder exposed and the head turned to the left, all the clothing patterns complete (as recorded in Tianzhong Ji citing Yiyuan Cihuang, though I have not seen the original text). In the Xuanhe period, Yu Ji of Lizhui found a statue of Guanyin in a clam, with two bamboo branches beside it. The bodhisattva's appearance is beautiful and dignified, adorned with a crown, clothing, and jewelry, and the bamboo leaves and branches are all made of fine pearls (as recorded in Yiji Buzhi Volume Thirteen). Such sightings of Buddha and bodhisattva statues in clams are recorded in historical texts. Peng Xueqin, a high-ranking official, found two clam shells with Buddha statues on Putuo Mountain in Ningbo. He donated one to Ziran An in Jiao Mountain and brought the other to Suzhou, where I was able to see it. There are twelve Buddha statues in total, arranged in three rows: three on the left, five on the right, and four in the center. The statues resemble the Maitreya Buddha statues in temples, with smiling eyes and eyebrows, fingers, and navels, all extremely lifelike, and it is indeed a miracle. Xu Huanong, a scholar, said, "The Diamond Sutra mentions one Buddha, two Buddhas, three, four, and five Buddhas; these are the offspring of the clam, and they can truly be called three, four, and five Buddhas."

    675

    ”彭雪琴侍郎幼时读书于衡阳之石鼓书院。有萧满者，少负才气，工为讼牍，然意在扶弱锄强，非挟邓思贤之术以牟利者也。中年以后，乃大悔之，改而习道家言，善敕勒之术，且好扶箕。其扶箕也，必与侍郎俱。萧满止能焚符召仙，而运笔于沙盘作字，则皆侍郎为政。其言乃侍郎自以意为之，然往往曲中问者之意，侍郎亦不知其所以然也。久之其名颇著，有问休咎者，有以病求方者，几无虚日。一县中老吏，以其子妇病求方，侍郎假箕笔作一诗云：“无端恶疾到心头，老米陈茶病即瘳。持赠与君惟二味，会看病起下高楼。”其人翌日来谢，果服老米陈茶而愈。萧满愈自信，侍郎则以为偶中也，如是者不胜记。时衡阳县令金君日声，浙人也。

    Explanation

    675

    Peng Xueqin, the Vice Minister, studied at the Shigu Academy in Hengyang when he was young. There was a man named Xiao Man, who, in his youth, was gifted and skilled in writing legal documents. However, his intention was to help the weak and suppress the strong, not to use cunning and deceitful tactics for profit. After middle age, he deeply regretted this and turned to studying Daoist teachings, becoming proficient in Daoist rituals and enjoying the practice of fengji. Whenever he performed fengji, he always did so with the Vice Minister. Xiao Man could only burn talismans to summon immortals, but when it came to writing on a sand tray, it was always the Vice Minister who did the writing. The messages produced during fengji were written by the Vice Minister himself, but they often accurately reflected the inquiries of the questioners, even though the Vice Minister did not understand why. Over time, their fame grew, and people came to ask about good or bad fortune and seek remedies for illnesses, almost every day. One day, an old official from the county, whose wife was ill, sought a remedy. The Vice Minister, using the fengji method, wrote a poem: "Suddenly, a terrible illness struck the heart, but old rice and tea will cure it. I give you only these two remedies, and your illness will surely be cured by the time you descend from the high tower." The next day, the official came to thank them, and indeed, his wife recovered after drinking the old rice and tea. Xiao Man became more confident, but the Vice Minister thought it was just a coincidence, and such incidents occurred countless times. At that time, the magistrate of Hengyang County was Jin Risheng, a person from Zhejiang.

    676

    其孙甫三龄，偶病，使医治之，医授以方而去。如方具汤液以进，则其孙正熟睡，金君之妻谓其子妇曰：“儿睡甚安，勿遽进药。”乃使佣媪置之饭甑之上，欲其勿冷也。俄儿醒，命取药，姑妇二人共饮儿，儿啼不欲饮，强灌之，不能尽，视碗底浓厚如膏。金君妻咎其子妇曰：“我固命汝沥取清汁，何乃如是？”其子妇讶曰：“曩已倾竹{徙}中，尽去其滓，岂犹未尽邪？”然不疑有他也。俄而儿大啼，颜色骤变，手足揎蹙，殆不可堪，疑为药所误。呼前医诘之，医曰：“吾药虽不中，病何遽至此？”取余药审视，惊曰：“是鸦片烟膏也，不可为矣！”时道光中叶，鸦片烟犹未盛行，然官署中已多有之。

    Explanation

    676

    His grandson was just three years old when he suddenly fell ill, and Mr. Jin sent a doctor to treat him. The doctor prescribed a remedy and left. According to the prescription, the decoction was prepared to be given, but at that moment, his grandson was sound asleep. Mr. Jin's wife told her daughter-in-law, "The child is sleeping peacefully; do not rush to give him the medicine." She then instructed a hired woman to place the decoction on the rice steamer to keep it warm. Soon after, the child woke up, and the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law both tried to give him the medicine. The child cried and did not want to drink it, but they forced him to take it. He could not finish it, and when they looked at the bottom of the bowl, they saw a thick residue like paste. Mr. Jin's wife scolded her daughter-in-law, "I specifically told you to pour out the clear liquid, why did you do this?" The daughter-in-law was surprised and said, "I already poured it into the bamboo strainer and removed all the sediment, how could there still be residue?" However, she did not suspect anything unusual. Soon after, the child began to cry loudly, his complexion changed abruptly, and his hands and feet clenched, making him look extremely distressed. They suspected that the medicine had gone wrong. They called the doctor back to question him, and the doctor said, "My medicine may not have been effective, but how could the illness suddenly worsen?" He took the remaining medicine to examine it and exclaimed in shock, "This is opium paste! It is dangerous!" It was during the middle period of the Daoguang era, a time when opium was not yet widespread, but it was already common in government offices.

    677

    金之庖人素嗜此，是日适以鸦片烟膏一碗蒸饭甑上，与儿之药碗大小形制相同，媪仓卒误取之也。金大怒，趣召庖人，欲予大杖，金妻曰：“无益也，宜为儿计。”偏召诸医，医至皆束手。或言萧、彭二君善扶箕，能为人求方，乃使使者二人持柬以往。其一人至书院，侍郎固在院中，使者致命。侍郎私计：三岁婴儿而饮鸦片烟膏一大碗，必无幸矣，辞不往。使者固请，侍郎不获已，乃曰：“然则当与萧满偕，今不知其人焉在。”请与使者共求之。侍郎之意以为萧满未必即得，迁延一二时，儿必死，即无事矣。甫出书院大门，而遇萧满，不得不与俱至县署。至则已设香案，陈箕盘，而金君者久具公服鹄立以待矣。略述病状，即请扶箕。萧满焚符如常仪，金君跪拜甚谨，侍郎惶悚无以为计，手扶箕笔，不能成一字，但频作旋转之势。

    Explanation

    677

    The cook of Jin had always been fond of opium, and on that day, he happened to use a bowl of opium paste on the steamer for cooking rice. The bowl was of the same size and shape as the child’s medicine bowl, and the elderly woman, in her haste, mistakenly took it. Jin flew into a rage and hastily summoned the cook, intending to give him a severe beating. Jin’s wife said, “It would be pointless; we should consider the child’s welfare.” Instead, she called for various doctors, but when they arrived, they were all at a loss. Someone suggested that Mr. Xiao and Mr. Peng were skilled in using the spirit board and could seek a remedy. So, two messengers were sent with letters to them.

    One of the messengers arrived at the academy, where the Vice-Minister was already present. The messenger delivered his message. The Vice-Minister privately thought: “A three-year-old child drinking a whole bowl of opium paste will surely not survive. I should not go.” But the messenger insisted, and the Vice-Minister, unable to refuse, said, “Then I should go with Mr. Xiao. But I don’t know where he is.” He asked the messenger to help find him. The Vice-Minister believed that Mr. Xiao might not be able to help, and if they delayed for a little while, the child would surely die, and there would be no further need for action. As soon as they stepped out of the academy gate, they met Mr. Xiao. He had no choice but to accompany him to the county office. Upon arrival, the incense table and spirit board were already set up, and Mr. Jin, dressed in official robes, had been waiting respectfully for a long time. After briefly describing the child’s condition, they asked Mr. Xiao to perform the spirit board ritual. Mr. Xiao burned the talisman as usual, and Mr. Jin knelt and bowed with great reverence. The Vice-Minister, filled with anxiety and fear, had no idea what to do. He held the spirit board pen but could not form a single character, only making frequent rotating motions.

    678

    金君请益苦，姑连书“吾至矣”，书已，仍作旋转之势。萧满见箕笔与常时异，亦自疑惧，左右顾望，汗出如浆。侍郎愈窘，平日常用之药，皆不能记，忽胸中骤得蓖麻子三字。思蓖麻子固药名，然非常用之药，不可轻投，转展寻思，竟无他品，遂书“蓖麻子”三字于盘中。金又请曰：“既蒙赐药，敢问当用几许？”又大书“一两”二字，侍郎亦不能自主也。金乃命人延二客至便坐小憩，且具食焉。食未竟，金出谢曰：“儿饮药大吐，毒尽出，今无害矣。仙人之赐也，二君之力也。”侍郎与萧满皆大喜，出以语人。有知医者曰：“蓖麻子固能拔毒，然其性烈，与巴豆同用之者，法不得过三钱。

    Explanation

    678

    Jin repeatedly requested with great effort, and the spirit repeatedly wrote "I have arrived." After writing, the spirit continued to make a rotating motion. Xiao Man saw the writing tool (箕笔) behaving differently from usual and became doubtful and fearful, looking around anxiously, and his sweat flowed like water. The official became increasingly distressed, unable to recall the commonly used medicines, but suddenly, the words "castor seeds" appeared in his mind. Thinking that castor seeds are indeed a medicine, but they are not commonly used and should not be prescribed lightly, he pondered but found no other suitable option. He then wrote "castor seeds" on the plate. Jin asked again, "Having received the medicine, I dare to ask how much should be used?" The official wrote "one liang" on the plate, unable to control the situation. Jin then invited the two men to a side room to rest and offered them food. Before the meal was finished, Jin came out to thank them, saying, "The child vomited heavily after taking the medicine, and the poison was completely expelled, so now there is no harm. This is a gift from the divine, and it is due to your two efforts." The official and Xiao Man were both greatly relieved and shared the news with others. A knowledgeable doctor said, "Castor seeds indeed can draw out poison, but they are strong in nature, and when used together with croton seeds, the dosage should not exceed three qian."

    679

    今用一两，殆哉！”盖以毒攻毒，少则无济，此殆有鬼神相之也。当此时，侍郎年甫弱冠。光绪庚辰岁，侍即年六十有六矣。夜过余春在堂，言此事。余曰：“楚语有云，民之精爽不携贰者，而又能齐肃衷正，则明神降之。侍郎固真灵位业中人，萧满亦必生有夙根者也，故二人扶箕，实能感召神明，与江湖术士左道惑众者不同也。”侍郎因言萧满晚年，折节为善，有以讼事相商者，必宛转排解之。其人自言前生为猴精，不知何所见，然其自睛流转不定，并口鼻辅颊亦然，疑其所自来必与人异矣。所谓萧满者，满非其名，盖于兄弟行居末，故谓之满，楚语然也。相呼既熟，其名与字，侍郎转不能记矣。余按吴江沈曰霖《奥西琐记》有土字一则云：“&lt；子兑&gt；，音近满，谓最少也。

    Explanation

    679

    Now, using the research and context we gathered above, produce a final, polished translation of the source text.

    Your goal is to deliver a final English translation that is both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and reads naturally in English. Apply the insights from the pre-drafting research directly — handle idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as you planned.

    Here are 5 examples showing the expected translation quality and style:

    Example 1

    Source: 爰及農商工賈，廝役奴隸，釣魚屠肉，飯牛牧羊，皆有先達，可為師表，博學求之，無不利於事也。

    Translation: As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    Example 2

    Source: 兩頭四臂女

    靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。

    Translation: A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。

    Translation: As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Source text:
    今用一两，殆哉！”盖以毒攻毒，少则无济，此殆有鬼神相之也。当此时，侍郎年甫弱冠。光绪庚辰岁，侍即年六十有六矣。夜过余春在堂，言此事。余曰：“楚语有云，民之精爽不携贰者，而又能齐肃衷正，则明神降之。侍郎固真灵位业中人，萧满亦必生有夙根者也，故二人扶箕，实能感召神明，与江湖术士左道惑众者不同也。”侍郎因言萧满晚年，折节为善，有以讼事相商者，必宛转排解之。其人自言前生

    680

    ”然则字当作&lt；子兑&gt；，乃《曾文正公集》有《满妹碑志》曰：“吾父生子女九人，妹班在末，家人称之满妹。”其字亦作满，盖取盈满之义。税乃俗字，不足据也。雪琴侍郎又言：一日正在书院中作文，而萧满至，大呼曰：“速助我，不然败矣！”问何事，则其时衡阳县城中有书肆曰集贤者，其主妇为妖所凭，延萧满施敕勒之术，大为所窘，飞一石至，几碎其颅，故欲与侍郎俱往扶箕也。侍郎私念扶箕伪耳，安足驱妖，不欲往。而萧满固强之，乃与俱行，然实非其志也，故行甚迟。路中萧满与言：“此妇居楼上，辄从窗中飞石击人，肆中书籍皆为所毁，其余什物无完者。一月以来，人莫敢往，往辄为所困。”言未已，侍郎大怒曰：“青天白日，而魑魅横行如此，我必往除之。

    Explanation

    680

    Therefore, the character should be read as "子兑" (zǐ duì), according to the Collected Works of Zeng Guofan, which includes a Memorial to the Full-Moon Maiden that states: "My father had nine children, and the youngest daughter, whom the family called 'Full-Moon Maiden,' was last in the line." Her name was also written as "满" (mǎn), taking the meaning of fullness or completeness. The character "税" (shuì) is a vulgar character and not reliable. The Vice Minister Xueqin further recounted: One day, while he was writing in the study hall, Xiao Man arrived and shouted loudly, "Hurry and help me, or else it will be ruined!" When asked what the matter was, he explained that at the time, there was a bookstore called Jixian in the city of Hengyang, and the owner, a woman, was possessed by an evil spirit. She had invited Xiao Man to perform the ritual of Chile (敕勒之术), but he was greatly troubled and a stone flew and almost crushed his skull. Therefore, he wanted to go with the Vice Minister to perform the divination ritual. The Vice Minister privately thought that divination was false and not effective against evil spirits, so he did not want to go. However, Xiao Man insisted, and they went together, though the Vice Minister walked very slowly because he was not truly interested. On the way, Xiao Man explained: "This woman lives upstairs and throws stones from the window to hit people. All the books in the shop were destroyed, and nothing else was left intact. For a month, no one dared to go near the shop, and those who did were trapped by the evil spirit." While he was still speaking, the Vice Minister became furious and said, "On a clear day, and yet demons are roaming freely like this! I will definitely go and eliminate them."

    681

    ”奔而往，萧满自后呼之曰：“止止，吾尚有言。”问何言，曰：“两人偕往，气稍壮耳。”侍郎愈怒，曰：“吾何畏之有！”径叩书肆之门。门启，突入，主人问姓名，不告，曰：“来驱妖耳！”即脱帽露顶，望楼上而呼曰：“妖能飞石击人，何不敲吾头？若不能者，吾且登楼，赫汝躯，拉汝干。”楼上竟寂然。侍郎连呼不已，而萧满至，见之喜曰：“有胜矣，速登楼。”乃俱登楼。妇在帐中，力持其帐不释，侍郎呼其夫曰：“劈之！”帐既启，而妇遽引衾自蒙其头。萧满即取清水一瓯，画符其中，使其夫启衾而灌之。妇饮符水，略不柢梧。萧满曰：“饮此，神识当稍清矣，吾侪且扶箕。”侍郎则假箕笔，为处一方，略用丹砂镇心、茯苓安神之品，授其夫曰：“以此疗尔妇。”遂与萧满俱出。越日询之，此妇竟愈矣。

    Explanation

    681

    Rushing to the place, Xiao Man called out from behind, saying, "Stop, stop, I still have something to say." Asked what it was, he replied, "Going together gives a bit more courage." The official became even angrier, saying, "What do I have to fear?" He directly knocked on the door of the bookshop. The door opened, and he rushed in. The owner asked for his name, but he did not reveal it, saying, "I came to drive away the demon!" He immediately took off his hat and exposed his head, calling out from the top of the building, "If the demon can throw stones, why not hit my head? If it can't, then I'll climb up and expose your body, pull down your dryness." The top floor remained silent. The official continued calling out, and Xiao Man arrived, seeing him and saying happily, "We've won, let's quickly go up." They both climbed up. The woman was in the canopy, holding onto it tightly without letting go. The official called out to her husband, "Cut it open!" Once the canopy was opened, the woman quickly pulled the quilt over her head. Xiao Man took a bowl of clear water and drew a talisman in it, instructing her husband to lift the quilt and pour it over her. The woman drank the talisman water, showing no discomfort. Xiao Man said, "Drinking this will slightly clear your spirit. Let's use the divining rod." The official then used a bamboo pen to write a prescription, using a little cinnabar to calm the mind and茯苓 to soothe the spirit, giving it to the husband and saying, "Use this to treat your wife." They then left together. The next day, they inquired, and the woman had indeed recovered.

    682

    光绪七年正月上元夜前后数日，扬州灯市颇盛。自教场街鹅颈湾至犁头街一里有余，高棚广幕，灯明如画，士女嬉游，充街塞陌。有张纲镇人王姓者，全家入市观灯，俄皆失散，惟王翁与其子王大仅存。所失者：老妇一人，年五十四，即王翁之妻也；少妇一人，年二十四，王大妻也；又少妇一人，年二十三；少女三人，长者十七，次者十三，最小者九岁，皆王翁之女；又幼子一人，才八岁，王大之子也。于是王翁父子皆痛哭于路。或曰江湖间有曰洒天花者，善用迷药，欲取人子女，先以药洒同来者，则对面不能见。然后以药洒所欲取者，即惘惘随之去矣。王翁父子曰：“是则信矣，吾父子彼时皆头昏目眩，几欲倾跌，必迷药使然也。”王大曰：“父请先归，我自营求之，不得，不归矣。”掷杯交卜于神，请所之，神示以东南行。行数日，至仪征，于江口见一舟，曝衣于竿，则其妻之裙也。

    Explanation

    682

    During the first month of the seventh year of Guangxu, around the Lantern Festival, the lantern market in Yangzhou was very lively. From the goose-necked bend on Jiaochang Street to Lihou Street, more than one li in length, tall booths and wide canopies lined the streets, with lights shining brightly like paintings, and men and women were playing and strolling, filling the streets and alleys. There was a man surnamed Wang from Zhanggang Town who brought his entire family to the market to watch the lanterns. Soon, they all got separated, leaving only the old man and his son Wang Da. Those who were lost were: one elderly woman, aged fifty-four, who was the wife of the old man; one young woman, aged twenty-four, who was the wife of Wang Da; another young woman, aged twenty-three; three young girls, the eldest seventeen, the second thirteen, and the youngest nine, all daughters of the old man; and one young boy, aged eight, who was the son of Wang Da. Thus, the old man and his son both cried bitterly on the road. Some say there are people in the rivers and lakes who are called 'Sprinklers of Heavenly Flowers' and are skilled in using drugging powder. They want to take people's children, first sprinkling the drug on those who come with them, so they cannot see each other. Then they sprinkle the drug on the one they want to take, and the person will follow them dazedly. The old man and his son said, "This is true; at that time, we both felt dizzy and almost fell, it must have been the drugging powder." Wang Da said, "Father, please go back first. I will try to find them. If I fail, I won't return." He threw a cup and cast lots to ask the deity for guidance, and the deity showed him to travel southeast. After traveling for several days, he arrived in Yizheng and saw a boat at the river mouth, where clothes were hung on a pole, including his wife's skirt.

    683

    往求附舟，一老翁坐船头，操楚音，执不可；许以重赂，翁欲纳之。有少妇在船尾语翁曰：“尔不惜死邪？我欲留此身以复仇，必不可！”听其言，似中州人也。翁即麾王大去。王大徘徊江岸，忽有兵船巡江，帆风而至，其中有所识陈姓者在焉。即登舟告以故，乃眠桅以待。薄暮，见有一人入此舟，陈即招集火伴往捕之。其人一跃入水中，舟中惟老翁及少妇。少妇自言黄姓，河南偃师人，亦其人以术取之者，“一家四口皆为所杀，投尸于江，独我在耳。

    Explanation

    683

    Wang Da went to seek to join a boat, where an old man was sitting at the bow, speaking in the dialect of Chu, and firmly refusing him. Wang Da offered a large bribe, and the old man wanted to accept it. A young woman at the stern of the boat spoke to the old man, saying, "Do you not care about your life? I want to keep this body to take revenge, and it is absolutely impossible!" Listening to her words, she seemed to be a person from the Central Plains. The old man then waved Wang Da away. Wang Da wandered along the riverbank, and suddenly, a patrol boat came by on the river, arriving under sail with the wind. Among those on board was someone Wang Da knew, a man surnamed Chen. Wang Da immediately boarded the boat and told them the reason, and they lay down at the mast to wait. In the late afternoon, they saw someone enter the boat, and Chen then gathered his companions to capture him. The person leaped into the water, leaving only the old man and the young woman on the boat. The young woman introduced herself as surnamed Huang, from Yanshi, Henan, and said she was also someone who was taken by his art. "All four members of my family were killed by him, their bodies were thrown into the river, and only I am left."

    684

    ”翁则周姓，湖南茶陵人，老而行乞于市，其人招之至，以奴隶畜之。陈问其人姓名，妇曰：“此安徽舒城人。但知姓李，不知其名，年三十八岁。习于水，勇于泅，虽入水不死也。”王大问家属所在，果在舟中，闭置仓下（唐人欧阳詹诗：“隔帘微月入中仓。”是古止作仓，今作舱，俗字也），发而出之，皆喑不能言。少妇曰：“是饮哑药矣，我始来亦然，饮水则解。”乃饮以水，复能言，挈之同归。兵船中将弁将此船移交仪征县，县官命捕李姓者，后未知得否。

    Explanation

    684

    The elderly man was surnamed Zhou and hailed from Chaling, Hunan. He was old and begged in the market, and the person who took him in treated him like a slave. Chen asked about the person's name, and the woman replied, "He is from Shucheng, Anhui. We only know his surname is Li, but we don't know his given name. He is thirty-eight years old. He is skilled in water and brave in swimming, and he will not die even if he enters the water." Wang asked where the family members were, and it turned out they were indeed in the boat, locked in the hold (as the Tang poet Ouyang Zhan wrote in his poem, "Through the curtain, a faint moon enters the middle warehouse." In ancient times, this character was written as 仓, but now it is written as 舱, a common variant). They were released and found to be mute and unable to speak. The young woman said, "They drank mute medicine; when I first came, it was the same. Drinking water would cure it." They were given water to drink, and they regained their ability to speak. They were taken back together. The military officials on the warship transferred the boat to Yizheng County, and the county official ordered the capture of the person surnamed Li. Whether they were eventually caught is unknown.

    685

    徽人程姓者，启肆于扬州新城之流芳巷。光绪庚辰腊月二十四日，既祀灶，与其徒会饮，皆大醉而罢。有李姓者，酒后至相识某姓肆中闲话，适有人来借钱，券书具矣，而无任者，主人辞焉。李慨然请为任，主人不可，李怒，始而谩骂，继而擐衣露臂，殆将用武，环而观者如堵墙。其旁有候补同知吴君寓，吴君之子闻门外大哗，出而观之。李忽一举手，伤吴辅车，四齿折焉。吴君怒，命里长监守之，质明将送之官。夜半，李酒醒，大惧，以头触壁，流血被面，昏绝于地。众惊救无及，正共，忽来一老者，曰：“毋动，我能治之。”取水一盂，书符其中，楔齿而灌之，李竟复苏，血亦顿止。老者曰：“十日不风，即无虞矣。”吴君闻之，使视其子之断齿，老者曰：“齿虽断，根犹在，可复生也。其童子欤，百日复故；若丈夫也，一年不入房，亦如故矣。”吴请治之，老者不受谢，惟请释李之罪。吴从其言，纵李去。此老者殆精于祝由之术者与？

    Explanation

    685

    A man surnamed Cheng from Huizhou opened a shop in the Liufang Lane of the New City of Yangzhou. On the 24th day of the 12th lunar month in the year 1880 (the 39th year of the Guangxu era), after the kitchen god ritual, he gathered with his apprentices for a drinking session, all of whom drank heavily and then stopped. A man surnamed Li, after drinking, went to a shop owned by an acquaintance to chat. Just then, someone came to borrow money, and the promissory note was ready, but there was no guarantor, so the owner refused. Li, with great enthusiasm, offered to guarantee the loan, but the owner declined. Enraged, Li started cursing and then tore off his clothes, showing his arms, almost ready to use force. The crowd watching around him was like a wall. Nearby was the residence of the reserve magistrate Wu, and Wu's son heard the commotion outside and came out to see what was happening. Suddenly, Li raised his hand and injured Wu's son, breaking four of his teeth. Wu was angry and ordered the village head to watch over Li, planning to send him to the authorities at dawn. In the middle of the night, Li sobered up, became extremely frightened, and struck his head against the wall, causing blood to flow over his face and making him faint on the ground. The crowd was shocked and unable to save him. Just as they were trying to help, a sudden old man appeared and said, "Don't move, I can treat him." He took a basin of water, wrote a spell in it, inserted it between Li's teeth, and poured it in. Li finally revived, and the bleeding stopped immediately. The old man said, "If there is no wind for ten days, he will be fine." Wu, upon hearing this, asked the old man to examine his son's broken teeth. The old man said, "Although the teeth are broken, the roots are still there, so they can grow back. If it's a child, they will recover in a hundred days; if it's a man, he should abstain from sexual activity for a year, and they will also recover." Wu asked for treatment, but the old man did not accept thanks, only asking that Li be released from his crime. Wu followed his words and let Li go. This old man must have been highly skilled in the art of Zhubaoyu, a traditional Chinese healing practice.

    686

    天津有所谓姑娘子者，女巫也。乡间妇女有病，辄使治之。巫至，炷香于炉，口不知何语，遂称神降其身，是谓顶神。所顶之神，有曰白老太太者，猬也；有曰黄少奶奶者，鼠狼也；有曰胡姑娘者，狐也；又有蛇、鼠二物。津人合而称之为五家之神。紫竹林有李氏妇得寒疾，巫语之曰：“尔名在冥中，已书铜牌，越十日，当书铁牌，则虽神不能为矣。今幸有十日之期，宜速禳之。”病者惟妇言是听，大具牲醴祷焉。一二日间，病似小瘥。巫益自多，乃授以秘方汤丸杂进，其药率由巫配合，所费不赀，而服之无验。病日加剧，巫屡变其方以侥幸，其或中朝补而夕泻，昨热而今寒，不及十日，李氏妇竟死。

    Explanation

    686

    In Tianjin, there are so-called "Gūniángzǐ," who are female shamans. When rural women fall ill, they often send for them to treat the illness. Upon arrival, the shaman lights incense in a brazier and mutters something indistinct, then claims that a deity has descended upon her, which is called "dǐngshén" (topping the spirit). The deities she tops include "Báilǎo Tàiyé" (White Old Lady), who is a hedgehog; "Huáng Shàonǎinai" (Yellow Miss), who is a rat-wolf; "Hú Gūniáng" (Fox Girl), who is a fox; and two others, a snake and a rat. The people of Tianjin collectively call these five deities the "Wǔjiā Zhīshén" (Five Family Gods).

    In Zizhu Forest, there was a Mrs. Li who contracted a cold illness. The shaman told her, "Your name is already written on a copper plate in the underworld, and after ten days, it will be written on an iron plate, which even the gods cannot reverse. Fortunately, you still have ten days, so you should quickly perform a ritual to ward off the illness." The patient only listened to the shaman's words and prepared a large offering of meat and wine to pray. Within one or two days, the illness seemed to improve slightly. The shaman became more confident and began to give her secret prescriptions of decoctions and pills. The medicines were mostly compounded by the shaman, costing a great deal, but they proved ineffective. The illness worsened day by day, and the shaman repeatedly changed her prescriptions in hopes of a miracle. Sometimes she would prescribe a morning remedy and an evening one, or a remedy that would cause a fever yesterday and a cold today. Before the ten days were up, Mrs. Li died.

    687

    湖北樊君希棣，字萼楼，余于第七卷中载其死而复苏事。今又闻萼楼有妾姚氏者，素婉娈，善事其嫡。平日尝言：“妇人嫁为人妻，则事舅姑如事父母，礼也。今我为人妾，不敢侵主妇之职，则惟有事主父主母如事父母耳。”以故萼楼夫妇极爱怜之。萼楼仕黔中，以寇盗磐互，寄其帑于蜀，姚亦从焉。俄而病死，萼楼在黔，未知也。一夕见姚冉冉至户外，欲入又不敢入，即于户外报地而拜。视其所衣，裳鲜明（四字本《后汉书。皇后纪》。《释名》：妇人上服曰。今俗呼妇人上服曰褂，乃俗字也，故借用此语），讶曰：“汝何得衣此？”遂无所见。越数日而家书至，姚死矣。其后黔乱粗定，家人自蜀还黔，萼楼语其妻曰：“姚死，汝以盛服佥之，非礼也。”妻曰：“不然。

    Explanation

    687

    Fan Xi Di, a man from Hubei, whose courtesy name was E Lou, was once dead and revived, as mentioned in the seventh volume. Now, it is said that E Lou had a concubine named Yao, who was always gentle and beautiful, and good at serving her husband's wife. She often said, "A woman, when married, should treat her in-laws as she would treat her own parents, which is in accordance with the rites. Now that I am a concubine, I dare not encroach on the duties of the primary wife; therefore, I can only treat the husband and his wife as I would treat my own parents." Because of this, E Lou and his wife loved and pitied her greatly. When E Lou served in Qianzhong, due to the constant turmoil caused by bandits and robbers, he entrusted his wealth to Shu, and Yao also followed him. Soon after, Yao died. E Lou, who was in Qianzhong, did not know about her death. One night, he saw Yao appear slowly at the door, wanting to enter but hesitating, and then reported to the ground and bowed. He saw that she was dressed in a bright and distinct garment, and was surprised, saying, "How can you wear such clothes?" And then he saw nothing. A few days later, a letter came from home, saying that Yao had died. Later, when the chaos in Qianzhong was somewhat settled, the family returned from Shu to Qianzhong. E Lou told his wife, "Yao died, but you dressed her in fine clothes and buried her, which is not in accordance with the rites." His wife replied, "Not so."

    688

    ”萼楼笑曰：“汝无我诳！”因历言其簪珥裳，妻不能隐，乃曰：“吾痛之甚，故稍假之耳，君何从知此？”萼楼告以所见。一弱女子耳，死后一灵不泯，竟能自蜀至黔，亦可异也。余大儿妇樊之伯父，早卒。其伯母痛甚，以姑年老，惧伤其心，不敢哭，日夜饮泣。泪尽则继以血，久之血亦尽，则有如膏如脂者出眼孔中，两目眵昏，几失明矣。一日，有亲串家为扶箕之戏，而其伯父降焉，言谈如平生时。或问之曰：“君既有灵，何不归家，与家人一叙乎？”箕笔书曰：“吾生无罪过，死后在冥中微有禄秩，不为冥吏拘束，虽日日归家，亦无不可。所以不归者，归亦无益，徒使家人悲悼耳。”其伯母闻之，使人因其再降而与之言，请必归，自誓勿哭。乃订期焉，且曰：“哭则吾即逝矣。”及期，设箕盘于密室中，伺之果至，箕动有声。其伯母在窗外闻之，不觉呜咽，箕笔遂止不动。后屡请不复至，其伯母甚悔，亦无及矣。

    Explanation

    688

    E' Lou laughed and said, "You are not lying to me!" He then enumerated her hairpins, earrings, and clothes. The wife could not hide it, so she said, "I am very sad, so I slightly fabricated it. How did you know this?" E' Lou told her what he had seen. It was only a weak woman, yet after her death, her spirit remained intact and was able to travel from Shu to Qian, which is quite extraordinary. My eldest daughter-in-law's uncle died early. Her aunt-in-law was very sad, fearing that her mother-in-law, who was old, would be hurt, so she dared not cry. She wept day and night, and when her tears ran out, she followed with blood. After a long time, the blood also ran out, and then something like grease or fat came out of her eyes, making her vision cloudy and dim, almost causing her to lose her sight. One day, there was a family of relatives who played the divination game of fupi, and her uncle descended, speaking as he did in life. Someone asked him, "Since you have a spirit, why don't you return home and meet with your family?" The spirit board wrote, "I lived without committing any sins, and after death, in the underworld, I have a slight rank, not constrained by the officials of the underworld. Even if I go home every day, it is not impossible. The reason I do not return is that returning would be of no benefit, only to make my family grieve." Her aunt-in-law heard this and had someone talk to him again when he descended, asking him to definitely return and swearing not to cry. They then agreed on a date, and she said, "If you cry, I will immediately disappear." When the time came, they set up the spirit board in a secret room, and he indeed came. The spirit board moved with sound. Her aunt-in-law, hearing from outside the window, unconsciously sobbed, and the spirit board stopped moving. Later, despite repeated requests, he did not come back. Her aunt-in-law deeply regretted it, but it was too late.

    689

    蜀人沈君秋风，与大儿妇母家樊氏有连。同治中，曾为贵州布政使，有疾，使医治之，已愈矣。医曰：“百日不洗足，乃可，若洗足，则无救。”而沈君极喜洗足，且其时寝兴饮食咸复其常，不以医言为信，家人力阻之。已将满百日矣，沈欲洗足，家人使人招樊君萼楼至，与之语，自日失至于丙夜，乃始辞去，以为夜漏已深，必且就寝矣。而沈命覃汤，家人交口止之，不可，竟洗焉，其明日遂卒。异哉，沈君所患，不知何病，一洗足遂不救，亦可怪矣。医固知之，卒不能从其戒，殆亦命欤？《南史》言：阴子春脚数年一洗，每洗则失财败事。如沈君者，竟以洗足丧其生，视子春更甚矣。

    Explanation

    689

    A Man from Shu, Shen Qiu Feng, had a connection with the family of his eldest son's mother, the Fan family. During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi, he once served as the Governor of Guizhou Province and fell ill. A doctor was called to treat him, and he had already recovered. The doctor said, "One must not wash feet for a hundred days, only then can one be saved. If one washes their feet, there is no hope of recovery." Shen Qiu Feng greatly enjoyed washing his feet, and at that time, his sleeping, waking, eating, and drinking were all back to normal. He did not believe the doctor's words, and the family members strongly opposed it. It was almost a hundred days, and Shen Qiu Feng wanted to wash his feet. The family sent someone to invite Fan Jue Lou, and they spoke with him from dusk until late at night. Fan Jue Lou then left, thinking it was late and time to go to bed. Shen Qiu Feng ordered hot water to be prepared, and the family members repeatedly tried to stop him, but he did not listen and ultimately washed his feet. The next day, he died. Strange indeed, Shen Qiu Feng's illness, whatever it was, led to his death after washing his feet, which is quite remarkable. The doctor knew this well, but ultimately could not follow his advice. Perhaps it was fate? The Southern History records that Yin Zichun washes his feet once every few years, and each time he does, he loses money and fails in his affairs. Like Shen Qiu Feng, he ultimately died because of washing his feet, and it was even worse than Yin Zichun.

    690

    马氏妇，湖南人。其姑病且死，妇泣曰：“姑妇二人相依为命，设不可为讳，则新妇茕茕何所依赖，形单影只，亦就死耳！”姑曰：“汝勿忧，我死且为鸟，仍与汝居。”已而姑死，果有鸟止于室中不去，时集于其妇之怀，乃日以米饲之。至月余，妇泣而祝曰：“姑悯我孤苦，化鸟以卵翼我，甚善！然我心则何安焉？请姑自便。”祝毕，鸟去不复来。余孙妇彭为我言之，马氏妇其亲串也。傅氏女，亦湖南人，幼从其父宦于中州，父甚爱怜之。年十六而嫁，已就涂矣，父亲送之数十里外。将返，解所衣半臂授之，曰：“途中以此御寒。”及既嫁，夫妇甚相得，又柔和善事其舅姑，一家无间言。已而其父死，舅姑秘不以闻。其夫告之，女大恸，舅姑争来慰藉。女曰：“蒙舅姑过爱，新妇敢不自爱乎？”乃止不哭。然不数月，竟奄然而死。死后有小婢言：“女于密室中县其父所与半臂，向之而拜，拜已辄饮泣，良久始出。

    Explanation

    690

    A Woman from the Ma Family, a native of Hunan, found herself in a dire situation when her mother-in-law fell ill and was on the verge of death. The woman wept and said, "My mother-in-law and I relied on each other for life. If she must pass away, I shall be left alone, solitary and without support, and I too shall die!" Her mother-in-law replied, "Do not worry. After I die, I will transform into a bird and remain with you." Soon after, the mother-in-law passed away, and indeed, a bird appeared in the room and did not leave. It would often perch in the woman's lap, and she fed it rice daily. After a month or so, the woman wept and prayed, "My mother-in-law pitied my loneliness and hardship, transforming into a bird to protect me like an egg under its wings. This is very good! But how can my heart find peace? Please let my mother-in-law be at ease." After her prayer, the bird flew away and did not return. My granddaughter-in-law Peng told me this story, and the woman from the Ma family is a relative of mine.

    Another woman, from the Fu family, was also a native of Hunan. She was young and followed her father to serve in the central region. Her father loved and cherished her greatly. When she was sixteen and about to be married, he sent her off for dozens of miles. Before returning, he took off his half-sleeve robe and gave it to her, saying, "Use this to keep warm on your journey." After her marriage, she and her husband got along very well. She was gentle and good at serving her in-laws, and there were no quarrels in the family. However, her father later died, and her in-laws kept it a secret and did not tell her. Her husband informed her, and she wept bitterly. Her in-laws tried to comfort her, but she said, "I am grateful for your kindness, but how can I not be careful with myself?" She stopped crying, but a few months later, she died suddenly. After her death, a maid mentioned that the woman had hung her father's half-sleeve robe in a private room, bowed towards it, and wept softly. She remained in the room for a long time before coming out.

    691

    出对舅姑，则愉色婉容，仍如平常。其在幽间无人之所，未尝不涕泪横集也。”乃知此女以毁而卒，可谓孝矣。童子刘某，浙江遂安人。年十四，薪采以养母。一日自山中归，且行且歌，邻人奔告曰：“虎衔尔母去，犹歌邪？”童子大惊，弃薪而归，荷铁叉以出走逐虎。及之，以叉籍其后。虎怒，释母，还噬童子，张其口呀呀然。童子扌舂以叉，适中其腭，虎跃，童子亦跃，叉益进贯其颐。

    Explanation

    691

    When she served her in-laws, she maintained a pleasant expression and gentle demeanor, as usual. Yet, in her private moments, she would never fail to weep profusely. Thus, it is known that this woman died of grief, and she may be called filial. A young boy named Liu, from Su'an in Zhejiang, was fourteen years old and gathered firewood to support his mother. One day, returning from the mountains, he sang as he walked. A neighbor ran to tell him, "The tiger has taken your mother away; are you still singing?" The boy was greatly alarmed, dropped his firewood, and returned home, carrying a steel fork to chase after the tiger. When he caught up to it, he stabbed the tiger's back with the fork. The tiger, enraged, released his mother and turned to bite the boy, opening its mouth wide. The boy stabbed it with the fork, hitting its palate. The tiger leaped, and so did the boy, driving the fork deeper into its jaw.

    692

    童子耆叉于地，虎口不得翕，两前足在空际不能用力，困甚；久之复跃，带叉而扑。童子亦仆。起，亟负母归，呼邻人往视，虎则死矣。纳之官，官赐童子钱十万，其母伤不甚重，药之而愈。此事年家子张子厚言之。盖有毛姓者，与刘同里，实亲见其事也。《元史。列女。姚氏传》云：虎衔其母，姚仓卒往逐之，即以手殴其胁，虎乃置之而去，与此事相类。彼一女子，此一童子，鸷虫攫搏，不程其勇，可以立懦矣。若《宋史》所载童八娜事，以身代其大母死，则尤可悯也。

    Explanation

    692

    A Child's Spear Stuck in the Ground

    A child wielded a spear that was stuck in the ground, and the tiger's mouth could not close around it. The tiger's front paws were in the air, unable to exert force, and it was extremely exhausted. After a long time, it jumped again, attacking with the spear still in its mouth. The child also fell. He quickly carried his mother back home, called neighbors to come and see, and the tiger was dead. The tiger was submitted to the authorities, and the government bestowed ten thousand coins on the child. His mother's injuries were not very serious, and she recovered after treatment. This story was told by a peer named Zhang Zihou. It is said that a person surnamed Mao, from the same village as Liu, actually witnessed the event. The "History of the Yuan Dynasty, Biographies of Chaste Women, Biography of Lady Yao" states: "The tiger carried her mother, and Yao hastily went to chase it, immediately striking its side with her hand, and the tiger then dropped her and left." This is similar to the event. That one woman, and this one child, facing a fierce beast, their courage unmeasured, can inspire the timid. If the story of Tong Ban Na in the "History of the Song Dynasty," who gave her life to save her grandmother, is considered especially pitiable.

    693

    丰某，海宁人，于光绪庚辰岁生一子，无右臂，人咸异之。张子厚云：“昔在衢州，见有妇人生子无左臂。”余于第十卷中载中州某氏女兼孑孓二形。此二人者，则一孑而一孓矣。或云孕妇忌见月食，若见之，则所生之子四体必有缺。许翁，歙县人。余尝见之于故人汪镜轩坐上，盖即汪之妻父也。家故巨富，启质物之肆四十余所，江浙间多有之，至翁犹然。翁为人极愿悫，其言呐呐然，如不出口。而其子弟中，则有三四辈以豪侈自喜，浆酒藿肉，奉养逾王侯。家僮百数十人，马数十匹，青骊彤白，无色不具，腹鞅背，亦与相称。每出则前后导从，炫黄于闾巷间。一日，忽郡吏持官文书来，太守以其豪横，欲逮问之。乃凶惧，上下行赂求免，所费无算，始寝不问。

    Explanation

    693
