Someone advised the mother: "Take your son's shoes and place them under the bed, with the laces tied inward. Call out your son's name every night, and he will surely return." And indeed, this method proved effective. Another woman named Yao, who was a second wife, recounted that before the war, her husband's family lived in a village called Zhoujiawan. A neighbor's son was abducted by bandits, and someone taught the mother to beg for lamp oil and wick from forty-nine different families. At night, after everyone had gone to sleep, she would call out her son's name forty-nine times, doing so every night with a voice that was very sorrowful. After several months, the son did indeed escape from the bandits and returned home, saying that he heard his mother calling his name every night. However, by then, he was already in the jurisdiction of Dantu County in Jiangsu, a distance of several hundred miles away. It seems that the bond between mother and son is so strong that they can communicate across great distances, much like a mountain collapsing and a bell ringing, which is not a mere coincidence.

    780

    咸宁樊君，余亲家翁玉农太守之族也。宦游广东，卒于官。其子不肖，寄其父之柩于僧庐，而尽取其赀以去，不知所之。数载后，樊君之外弟以事至广东，樊见梦曰：“吾子不肖，弃吾不顾，吾柩在某所不得归葬。今幸弟至，愿与俱归。”次日其外弟访之，果得其柩，然计道路之费亦颇不细，意尚踌躇。又梦樊促之，其外弟曰：“而车远涉，事甚非易。若启君之柩，而别为以盛君骨，归葬故茔，可乎？”樊曰：“可。”濒行，又见梦曰：“凡过关塞桥梁及高山大川，必呼我姓名，庶不淹滞。”其外弟悉从之。将至家，樊先一夕示梦于其家人曰：“吾从外弟归矣！”观乎此，知狐死首丘，叶落粪本，延陵赢博之葬，虽达人高见，而孝子慈孙固不容存此心也。

    Explanation

    780

    Mr. Fan of Xianning, a member of my father-in-law's family, the Governor Yu Nong, served as an official in Guangdong and died in office. His son, being unworthy, left his father's coffin in a temple and took all his wealth with him, disappearing without a trace. Several years later, a cousin of Mr. Fan came to Guangdong on business. Mr. Fan appeared in a dream and said, "My son was unworthy and abandoned me without regard. My coffin is at a certain place and cannot be brought back for burial. Now that you have arrived, I hope to return with you." The next day, his cousin visited and indeed found the coffin. However, the cost of the journey was also quite substantial, and he still hesitated. He dreamed again that Mr. Fan urged him, and his cousin said, "Traveling such a long distance is not easy. If you open my coffin and put my bones in another box, can we bring them back to the old grave?" Mr. Fan replied, "Yes." Before leaving, he saw another dream in which Mr. Fan said, "When passing through checkpoints, bridges, and high mountains and great rivers, you must call my name, so that I will not be delayed." His cousin followed all his instructions. As they approached home, Mr. Fan appeared in a dream to his family the night before and said, "I am returning with my cousin!" From this, we can understand that just as a fox dies facing its den and a leaf falls to its root, the burial at Yanling and Yingbo, even the wise and far-sighted cannot hold such a heart, but filial sons and kind grandsons cannot bear such a heart.

    781

    咸宁余氏女既嫁而死。其母嫠也，老且病。粤寇将至，举家仓皇谋他徙。其母夜间独坐一室，忽见其女至，谓其母曰：“遍地烽烟，行将安往？母又多病，不耐奔波，与其道路流离，不如死之为乐。”其母曰：“诚然。吾自问生有何味，能从汝去，良所愿也。”旁舍之人，皆闻其母一人独语，而不闻其女语，但闻鬼声&lt；需鬼&gt；&lt；需鬼&gt；然耳。次日，问其母昨夜云何，母以告，犹不之信。越数日，其母竟死。

    Explanation

    781

    A Woman from the Yu Family in the Reign of Emperor Wu

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, a woman from the Yu family died shortly after her marriage. Her mother, a widow, was old and sick. When the southern rebels were approaching, the entire family panicked and planned to move elsewhere. One night, the mother sat alone in a room and suddenly saw her daughter appear. The daughter said to her mother, "The land is filled with smoke and fire; where will we go? Mother is also very sick and cannot withstand traveling. It would be better to die happily rather than wander on the road." The mother replied, "Indeed. I ask myself what life has to offer. If I can follow you, it is truly my wish." People from neighboring houses all heard the mother speaking alone, but did not hear the daughter's words. They only heard ghostly sounds. The next day, they asked the mother about last night, and she told them, but they still did not believe it. A few days later, the mother eventually died.

    782

    先祖母戴太夫人之侄孙骏伯茂才，早卒。其妻张氏生一女，曰慰生，适蔡氏而死。戴本德清人，然迁居湖郡数十年矣。咸丰庚申岁，粤寇犯郡城，戴氏谋他徙，张犹豫。夜间忽闻慰生疾呼曰：“速去，速去！”如是者，数夕皆然。张遂决。此与余氏女用意迥别，殆由其母衰壮不同耳。然其后赵忠节公力守湖郡，居民稍稍复集，张氏仍回城中旧居。及湖城陷，竟死于难，惜不终从其女之言也。咸宁樊氏女，许嫁李氏子，婚有日矣。而其婿病甚，婿家请改期。时女父远客于外，女母又早卒，虽有兄弟数人，皆非同母，略不顾问。女乃自主之曰：“既已许人，固无他议。

    Explanation

    782

    My great-grandmother, Lady Dai, had a nephew named Junbo, a man of talent, who died young. His wife, Zhang, gave birth to a daughter named Weisheng, who married into the Cai family and died. The Dai family originally came from Deqing but had been living in Hu Prefecture for many years. In the year 1860, the Cantonese rebels invaded the provincial capital, and the Dai family planned to move elsewhere. Zhang hesitated. At night, she suddenly heard Weisheng urgently shouting, "Hurry up, hurry up!" This happened several nights in a row. Zhang then decided to act. This is different from the intention of the Yu family girl, probably because their mothers were in different states of health. However, after that, Zhao Zhongjie Gong firmly defended Hu Prefecture, and the residents gradually returned. The Zhang family returned to their old house in the city. When Hu Prefecture fell, she ultimately died in the disaster. It is regrettable that she did not ultimately follow her daughter's advice.

    A daughter of the Fan family from Xianning, betrothed to a son of the Li family, had a wedding date set, but her fiancé was very ill. The fiancé's family requested to postpone the wedding. At that time, the girl's father was far away as a guest, and her mother had died early. Although she had several brothers, none were from the same mother, and they paid no attention to her. The girl then took it upon herself to say, "Since I have already been promised in marriage, there is no other matter."

    783

    婿虽不幸，犹将往也，况病耳，庸何伤？仍请如期。”乃粗粗成礼。礼成，婿大委顿，幸女善事之。又逾数年，生二子而后死。女独居守志，抚其二子。乃其翁非人也，以女年少有姿首，嫁之可得重聘，谋夺其志。女知其谋，遁归母家，而其母家之事悉委之其舅氏。舅氏曰：“若父以家事托我，所入不足给所出，今又增益人口，奈何？”女曰：“固也，然我亦非坐食者，请舅氏即以我当一佣可乎？”于是蚤暮力作，不遑顾其子，一子殇焉。后其母家日益落，女自食其力以终其身。余大儿妇与此女为族昆弟，故余得闻之。孤苦穷嫠，茕茕白首，姑存之笔记中，冀不泯幽光耳。

    Explanation

    783

    The husband, though unfortunate, still intended to proceed, and being ill was not a significant issue; he still requested to go as planned. Thus, the wedding ceremony was hastily performed. After the ceremony, the husband became very weak, fortunately, the wife treated him well. After several more years, they had two sons, and then he died. The wife lived alone, adhering to her vows, and cared for her two sons. But her father-in-law was not a good person; because the wife was young and beautiful, he planned to marry her off for a large dowry, intending to take away her resolve. The wife knew of his plan and fled back to her mother's home. All the affairs of her mother's family were entrusted to her maternal uncle. Her maternal uncle said, "If your father entrusted me with family matters, the income is insufficient to cover the expenses, and now there are more people to support, what should we do?" The wife replied, "That is true, but I am not a person who sits idle. Please let me work as a laborer." Thus, she worked tirelessly from morning till night, neglecting her children, and one of them died. Later, her mother's family declined further, and the wife supported herself until the end of her life. My eldest daughter-in-law and this woman were relatives, so I learned about this story. A lonely, poor widow, alone until her old age, I record this in my notes, hoping to preserve her hidden light.

    784

    余大儿妇樊之乳母刘氏，河南中牟人。嫁于湖北之兴国州，其夫与子皆前卒，常居樊氏。时余亲家翁玉农太守方知河南府，善遇之，颇有所蓄。忽有某甲自湖北至，自言为其夫兄弟之子，然族属疏远，固不可考矣。乃其事刘，则甚勤恳，抑搔疴痒，问衣燠寒，虽亲子有不若。刘甚喜之，将以为子。刘有妹亦佣于樊氏，谓刘曰：“昔吾夫有亲兄弟之子，吾以夫亡往依之，哭于门外达旦，而不见收恤。今此人与姊远矣，何其亲昵之甚也？此必不可信。姊无为所饵！”嗣后甲至与刘语，其妹辄往参焉，使不得尽其说。

    Explanation

    784

    Liu, the wet nurse of the eldest daughter-in-law of Fan, was from Zhongmou in Henan. She married into Xingguo State in Hubei, where her husband and son both died before she came to live with the Fan family. At that time, Yunan Magistrate, my father-in-law, was in charge of Henan Prefecture and treated her well, allowing her to accumulate some savings. Suddenly, someone named Jia came from Hubei, claiming to be the son of her husband's brother, though the relationship was distant and could not be verified. Nevertheless, he was very diligent in serving Liu, scratching her itches, asking about her clothes and warmth, and being more attentive than even her own child. Liu was very pleased with him and considered him like a son. Liu had a sister who also worked for the Fan family, who told Liu, "Once, my husband had a close relative, but when my husband died, I went to rely on him, cried at his door all night, but he did not receive or care for me. Now this person is far from you, why is he so close to you? This cannot be trusted. Do not be deceived by him!" Afterward, when Jia came to talk to Liu, her sister would always join them, preventing Jia from fully expressing himself.

    785

    会刘病，甲乃为赁屋于外，刘因言于主人，就外舍养病。妹又尼之，刘曰：“人固有不同，妹将以此子为犹尔夫兄之子邪？此子于我至厚，我又无子，不从之去，而尚焉往？妹勿阻我。他日必不为妹累。”妹无如何，刘乃从甲俱去。甲有所善妇人，遂以为妻，与刘同居，居然母子姑妇矣。乃稍稍蚕食，刘之所畜既尽，伪使刘至某所就医，及暮而返，则甲夫妇不知何往矣。刘亻累然一身，卒依其妹以终。嗟乎！羊肉不慕蚁，蚁慕于羊肉，醯酸不慕蚋，蚋慕于醯酸。刘氏愚妇人，固不足知此。乃其妹则何智也！三折肱知为良医，信夫！

    Explanation

    785

    When Liu fell ill, Jia rented a house outside and told the owner that Liu would stay there to convalesce. Liu's sister objected, but Liu said, "People are naturally different. Should I treat this child as if he were my brother's son? This child is very dear to me, and I have no children of my own. If I don't go with him, where else can I go? Please don't stop me. In the future, he will not be a burden to you." Seeing that she could do nothing, Liu left with Jia. Jia had a good woman friend, whom he later married, living with Liu as if they were mother, son, and mother-in-law. Gradually, Jia began to take advantage of Liu, depleting all that Liu had. He then pretended to send Liu to a certain place for medical treatment, returning in the evening. By then, Jia and his wife had disappeared. Liu, now alone, relied on his sister until the end. Alas! The lamb does not seek out ants, yet ants seek out the lamb; the vinegar does not seek out flies, yet flies seek out the vinegar. Liu's foolish sister truly did not understand this. But how wise was his sister! After three breaks of the arm, one knows how to be a good doctor. Indeed!

    786

    仁和唐栖镇乡间有沈氏者，颇有资产。生二女，无子，嗣族人子为子。父母与约曰：“所有田宅，悉与嗣子。其银钱、衣服、玩好之类，二女分之。”于是长女私计：无妹，则尽归己矣。一日与妹下楼，自后推之，坠楼下死，以妹失足告。其父母初不疑有他也。长女后归于劳氏，生子至数岁，辄死。其第五子之死也，妹附之而言前事，乃知诸子之死，皆其妹为祟也。后又举一子亦死，至第七子，得无恙。或劳氏尚不应无后，故延其一线与？然此子清狂不慧，所有财物皆为其随手散去，不及十年耗矣。后此子未死前一日，呼刀镊之工尽剃其发，亦可怪也。又闻其尚有二女，一最所怜爱，嫁于嵇氏，亦得怪疾而死。

    Explanation

    786

    In the countryside of Tangqi Town, Renhe, there was a family surnamed Shen who had considerable wealth. They had two daughters but no sons, so they adopted a son from their clan. The parents agreed with the daughters: "All the land and houses will go to the adopted son. The silver, money, clothes, and luxury items will be divided between the two daughters." The eldest daughter then thought to herself: if there is no younger sister, everything will belong to her. One day, she went down the stairs with her sister and pushed her from behind, causing her to fall and die. She reported that her sister had lost her footing. The parents initially did not suspect anything unusual. The eldest daughter later married into the Lao family. Her children died shortly after birth, one after another. When the fifth son died, the younger sister joined him and revealed the previous events. It was then known that the deaths of all the children were caused by the younger sister's mischief. Later, she gave birth to another son who also died. By the time the seventh son was born, he remained unharmed. Perhaps the Lao family should not have been left without descendants, so the lineage continued. However, this son was clearly crazy and not wise. All the family's wealth was scattered by him, and within ten years, it was all gone. On the day before his death, he called a barber to shave off all his hair, which is also strange. It is also said that the eldest daughter still had two daughters, one of whom she loved dearly. She married into the Ji family and also died of a strange illness.

    787

    胡氏，湖北人，故为倡，后倦于风尘，乃以其所生女嫁刘某，而从之居。胡颇有所蓄，刘夫妇善事之。不数年，胡所蓄尽归于刘，于是刘意日益怠，久之女亦偃蹇，不甚顾其母。胡老且病，委顿床褥，竟无过问者，虽饮食不时得，医药无论矣。胡愤恨而死。后刘所生子女，皆至数岁而死，死时辄曰：“婆来矣！”婆者，其子女呼其外祖母也。乃知胡为祟云。此与沈女事相类。传曰：子身之贰也，报之于其子女。亦犹报之于其身矣。

    Explanation

    787

    Hu was a woman from Hubei province who had once been a courtesan. Tired of the life of a courtesan, she arranged for her daughter to marry Liu, and then moved in with him. Hu had some savings, and Liu and his wife treated her well. However, not many years later, all of Hu's savings went to Liu. As a result, Liu became increasingly lazy, and after a long time, the daughter also became arrogant and disrespectful, paying little attention to her mother. Hu grew old and fell ill, lying helpless in bed, and nobody came to ask after her. Even food was not regularly provided, and medicine was not even considered. Hu died in anger and resentment. Later, Liu's biological children all died when they were a few years old. When they died, they always said, "Grandmother has come!" Grandmother was the term used by the children to refer to their grandmother. It was then known that Hu's spirit was causing trouble. This story is similar to the story of Shen's daughter. It is said in the classics: the child is a reflection of the parent; the parent's actions are reflected in their children, as if it were happening to themselves.

    788

    唐西镇王氏二女，并许嫁于姚氏，父母俱亡，无所依赖。其一女所许之姚氏，故与王氏有连，盖姚氏妇于女父为舅之子，外兄妹也。乃先取其女以归，而蓄之母氏，实女之祖母家也，戚党相依，女甚安之。其一女所许之姚氏，则初无瓜葛，而亦以为请，王氏之长老不可，曰：“俗有童养之例，小家则然耳。衣冠之族，不应如此。”姚氏固请，乃许之，使待年于夫氏。初至亦甚相得。而姚有女工为谗&lt；鬼&gt；，其姑人其女之言，遂憎疾之，久而弥甚。后避寇难，邂逅见德清蔡氏女，爱之，欲聘为子妇，乃逐此女，仍还母家。使媒氏告王氏之长老，以女游惰，绝其婚。

    Explanation

    788

    Two daughters of the Wang family from the Western Region of the Tang Dynasty were both promised to the Yao family. After their parents had both passed away, leaving them without support, one of the daughters was married to a Yao family that had a connection to the Wang family, as the wife of the Yao family was the son-in-law of the Wang family’s father, making her an aunt to the Wang daughters. They first took this daughter to their home and kept her with her maternal relatives, which was actually the grandmother’s family, and they relied on each other, and the daughter felt very comfortable there. The Yao family who was promised the other daughter had no prior connection, but they also made a request. The elders of the Wang family refused, saying, "There was a custom of betrothing children, but it was usually for poorer families. Families of higher social standing should not do this." The Yao family persisted in their request, and they were eventually allowed to wait for the daughter to reach marriageable age in her future husband's household. Initially, they got along very well. However, the Yao family had a jealous and malicious woman among the women, and the mother-in-law believed her words and came to hate the daughter, and this hatred grew over time. Later, when they were fleeing from bandits, they met a girl from the Cai family in Deqing and fell in love with her, wanting to marry her to their son. They then sent away this daughter and returned her to her maternal home. They sent a matchmaker to inform the elders of the Wang family, claiming that the daughter was idle and thus breaking off the engagement.

    789

    俄姚氏子以暴疾死。死之日，彼姚氏之妇与女父为外兄妹者，见女父自外至，呼曰：“二妹，吾已捉毛毛去矣，敬使妹知之。”毛毛，即姚氏子之小名也。夫绝其婚，未伤其命，似不应遽杀其婿。且以谗言构其女者小姑也，婿何罪乎？然世俗于童养之女，往往虐遇之。盖女子而至待年于夫氏，其孤苦可知。既无顾忌，遂相凌践。君舅君姑，本非骨肉；女公女叔，竞构萋菲。饮食每至不周，鞭棰在所恒有，饮恨吞声，宛转而死者，比比然也。王女之父之杀其婿，或亦得请于神矣。观其呼彼姚氏妇而告之，盖假此以警彼也。呜呼！风俗浇漓，人心凉薄，则鬼神之事，固有足以辅政教之所不及者矣。

    Explanation

    789

    Soon, the son of the Yao family died suddenly of a violent illness. On the day of his death, the sister-in-law of the Yao family, who was also the wife of the daughter's father, saw her father-in-law coming from outside and called out, "My dear sister, I have taken away Mao Mao, respectfully informing you of this." Mao Mao was the nickname of the Yao family's son. The engagement was broken, but no harm was done to his life, so it seems unjust to hastily kill the son-in-law. Moreover, the one who slandered the daughter was the sister-in-law, so what crime did the son-in-law commit? However, in the secular world, girls raised in families for marriage are often mistreated. Being a girl who waits for her husband's family, her loneliness and misery are evident. With no caution, they are bullied and oppressed. The father-in-law and mother-in-law are not blood relatives; the aunts and uncles of the husband's family compete in spreading gossip. Their diet is often inadequate, and they are frequently beaten, suffering in silence and dying tragically, which happens frequently. The father of the Yao family's daughter killing his son-in-law might have been requested by the gods. Observing him calling out to the sister-in-law of the Yao family and informing her, it seems he was using this as a warning to her. Alas! Decadent customs and cold hearts mean that matters related to ghosts and deities can indeed supplement the inadequacies of political and moral education.

    790

    洛阳县令秦某，以事至汴梁，久留不返。其妻在署中，与女及赘婿同居。俄秦在汴梁大病，妻往视之。妻故有一婢，爱之等于女，居处必与偕，至是因仓卒不及挈。此婢留居署中，而婿频烦役使之。女疑其有私，欲杀婢，伺婢睡熟，以翦刀刺之，力薄不能遽断其吭，区刂俞刂良久，血肉狼藉然后死。遽以棺敛之，舁置野外。比及秦妻返，家人皆见，独不见此婢。问之，女以暴疾死对。其旁有一长橙（都邓切，几橙也。见《广韵》四十八嶝），忽起而人立，且行数步。正共骇异，一小婢哭而跪秦妻前，曰：“主母不知邪？婢子死矣，死甚苦！”秦妻惊曰：“汝即某邪？”曰：“然。

    Explanation

    790

    The magistrate of Luoyang, Mr. Qin, went to Bianliang because of some matter and stayed there for a long time without returning. His wife remained in the county office, living with his daughter and a son-in-law. Soon after, Qin fell seriously ill in Bianliang, and his wife went to see him. She had a maid whom she loved as much as her daughter, and they always stayed together. In the hurry, she did not have time to take the maid along. The maid was left in the county office, and the son-in-law frequently used her. The daughter suspected that the son-in-law had an affair with the maid and wanted to kill her. She waited until the maid was asleep, then stabbed her with a pair of scissors. The force was weak, and she could not immediately cut through her throat. She cut for a long time, and the blood and flesh were in disarray before the maid died. The daughter quickly placed the maid in a coffin and carried her to the wilderness. By the time Qin's wife returned, all the family members were present except for this maid. She asked about the maid, and the daughter said that the maid had died suddenly. Suddenly, a long bench (which was placed near the maid's body) stood up and walked several steps. Everyone was shocked and amazed. A small maid cried and knelt before Qin's wife, saying, "Mistress, don't you know? The maid has died, and she suffered greatly!" Qin's wife was shocked and asked, "Are you the maid?" The small maid replied, "Yes."

    791

    ”乃具言其事，且曰：“主人之女夫，亦即主人也，有所役使，敢不承奉。其有无私情，固不可知。至于婢子，循分执役，实无他念。乃不加察，遽施毒手，睡梦之中，横被翦屠。杀人者死，虽逃王法，岂无冥谪，今已得请于神，必索其命。”秦妻抚之而泣曰：“汝诚苦矣，然吾平日待汝不薄，幸以吾故，曲恕吾女。”婢曰：“此固不能。虽然，婢子不敢于主母之前加无礼于女公子，一离膝下，恐不复能忍矣！”言已倒地，扶起复问之，则茫然不知所对。秦妻于是为此婢大作佛事，冀仗佛力销释冤愆。此事盖在咸丰初，至今二十余年，未知其女无恙否？然恐秦妻死后，终不得幸免也。

    Explanation

    791

    The maid then fully explained the matter and said, "The daughter-in-law of the master is also the master herself. If there is any service required, how dare I not obey? Whether there are no private feelings, it is certainly unknown. As for me, I follow my proper role and perform my duties, truly having no other thoughts. Yet, without investigation, they hastily used violent means, and in my sleep, I was suddenly brutally slaughtered. The killer shall die; even if they escape the law, how can there be no punishment in the afterlife? Now I have appealed to the gods, and they will certainly demand their life." Qin's wife comforted her and wept, saying, "You truly suffer greatly, but I have treated you kindly in my daily life. If only for my sake, show leniency to my daughter." The maid replied, "This I cannot do. However, I dare not be disrespectful to the young lady before my mistress. Once away from her side, I fear I will no longer be able to endure!" After she finished speaking, she collapsed to the ground. When lifted up and asked again, she was blankly unaware of how to respond. Qin's wife then performed extensive Buddhist rituals for the maid, hoping to rely on the power of the Buddha to resolve her grievances and sins. This matter probably occurred in the early years of the Xianfeng reign. Up to now, more than twenty years have passed, and it is unknown whether her daughter is safe. But I fear that after Qin's wife dies, she will ultimately not be spared.

    792

    中牟县典史张某，疽发于背，召巫者视之。巫出而语人曰：“张公必死矣。吾至冥中，见其父母甚怒，言其子不孝，闻父丧不发而之官，及其居官，又贪暴无人理。今在冥中受罪甚苦，导吾至一处，见张公裸而仆地，其背上凿一小孔，实薪蒸于中，焚其脂膏。岂有如此而得活者乎？”不数日，张果死。死后有人自其家乡来，乃言张甫补官，父即死于家，张秘不言，仍挈妻子赴任。此事其乡人固有知之者，然在河南，则莫之知，在中牟更莫之知也，而巫乃能言之。然则其所见情状，必非伪造矣。

    Explanation

    792

    Zhang Gong, the County Magistrate of Zhongmou, developed a boil on his back. He summoned a witch to examine him. After the witch left, she told others: "Zhang Gong will surely die. When I arrived in the underworld, I saw his parents very angry, saying that their son was disobedient. He did not inform the authorities about his father's death and continued to serve in office. While in office, he was greedy and violent, lacking moral principles. Now he is suffering greatly in punishment in the underworld. The sorcerer guided me to a place where I saw Zhang Gong naked and lying on the ground. There was a small hole drilled into his back, filled with kindling, and his fat was burning. How could anyone survive such a condition?" A few days later, Zhang Gong indeed died. After his death, someone came from his hometown and said that Zhang Gong had just been appointed to his position when his father died at home. Zhang Gong kept this secret and took his wife and children to take up his new post. This fact was known to some people in his hometown, but in Henan, it was unknown, and in Zhongmou, it was even more unknown. Yet, the sorcerer was able to speak of it. Therefore, what the sorcerer saw must have been real.

    793

    《清异录》云：“菌蕈有一种，食之令人得干笑疾，土人戏呼为”笑矣乎“，不言其可以致死也。然此菌实有毒，笑而不已，久之必死。余居吴下马医科巷，邻潘氏有一媪，其妻母也。食菌后觉腹中有异，乃至床上卧，俄而吃吃笑，俄而大笑，惊谓其女曰：”殆矣，吾食笑菌，死矣。“其言虽如此，而笑仍不绝声，未几起而立，旋仆，遂伏地狂笑。其女见此状，惊惶失措，以余家时有药饵馈送邻比，乃踵门问焉。余二儿妇检视沪上所刊经验良方，知食笑菌者，薜荔可以治之。墙头适有此种，乃采一束，煎汤与之饮之，须臾笑止。至今无恙。

    Explanation

    793

    Qing Yi Lu records that there is a type of mushroom, which, when eaten, causes one to suffer from a laughing disease. The locals jokingly call it "Xiaoyihu" (笑矣乎). However, this mushroom is actually poisonous. If one laughs uncontrollably, they will eventually die. I live in the Ma Weike Lane in Wu (Wuxi), and my neighbor Pan has an elderly woman who is my mother-in-law. After eating the mushroom, she felt something unusual in her abdomen and lay down on the bed. Soon after, she started to laugh, then laughed loudly, and said in shock, "I'm in danger, I ate the laughing mushroom, I'm going to die!" Although she said this, her laughter did not stop. After a while, she stood up, then fell down again, and finally lay on the ground, laughing uncontrollably. Her daughter saw this and was shocked and at a loss, as our house often sends medicine to the neighbors. She then went to our door to ask for help. My two daughters-in-law checked the experience and good remedies published in Shanghai and found that the climbing fern (薛荔) can treat this condition. There happened to be some climbing fern on the wall, so they picked a bunch, boiled it into a decoction, and gave it to her to drink. Soon, her laughter stopped. She is still fine today.

    794

    咸丰间，粤贼陷浙江绍兴。诸暨之包村有义民包立身起乡兵，与贼相持几一年，杀贼无算，浙东西人归之者甚众。及包村破，乃尽歼焉。有魏某者，亦绍兴人，自包村逸出，为贼所追，自其后以刀连斫之，而首不殊。又一贼自前解其衣扣，欲使受刃，而衣扣纠缠不可解，乃以利刃刺其喉。魏仆地，颈血模糊，贼以为死矣，委之去。而魏实未死，俟贼去远，复起立，以手扶其头而行。行数里，稍一释手，首忽下坠倒悬于膺，大惊，仍掀之起，按捺颈上，幸不复坠。然饥甚，盖不食饮者数日矣。偶掬溪水饮之，甘如饴，见田间有荑稗，采而生食之，其香美不可名言也。数日至其家，竟不死。

    Explanation

    794

    During the reign of Emperor Xianfeng, the Taiping rebels captured the city of Shaoxing in Zhejiang province. In the village of Baocun in Zhuji, a righteous civilian named Bao Lishen raised a local militia to resist the rebels. They held out against the rebels for nearly a year, killing countless enemies, and many people from eastern and western Zhejiang flocked to join them. When the village of Baocun was finally breached, they were completely wiped out. There was a man named Wei from Shaoxing who managed to escape from Baocun. He was pursued by the rebels, who slashed him repeatedly with swords, but his head did not fall off. Another rebel from the front tried to unbutton his clothes to make him vulnerable to the sword, but the buttons were tangled and could not be undone. The rebel then stabbed him in the throat with a sharp blade. Wei fell to the ground, his neck covered in blood. The rebels thought he was dead and left him. However, Wei was not actually dead. After the rebels had gone far away, he got up again, supporting his head with his hand as he walked. After walking several miles, when he slightly let go of his head, it suddenly fell down and hung upside down from his chest. He was greatly surprised, lifted it up again, and pressed it onto his neck, fortunately it did not fall again. He was very hungry, having not eaten or drunk for several days. He scooped water from the stream and drank it, finding it sweet like honey. He saw some wild grass in the fields and picked it to eat raw, its fragrance and taste were indescribably delicious. After several days, he returned home, and ultimately did not die.

    795

    初时每食，尚有热气鬻然自喉间溢出，盖即贼刃所刺处也，久之创合，亦不复然矣，惟头稍偏，常若左顾耳。其嫂佣于杭州许氏，余次女家也，故得详知之。异哉！贼斫不死，神扶持，裴公圣相故然；一介细民，何以致此，岂其人有阴德邪？《太平广记》卷三百七十六引《广异记》云，太原王穆，唐至德初于南阳战败，贼以剑自后斫穆，&lt；角力&gt；骨俱断，惟喉尚连。初冥然不觉，食顷乃悟，而头在脐上，遂以手力扶头附颈，须臾复落，以发分系两畔，左膊发解，头坠怀中，复系发正首，行四十里。麾下散卒见之，载还军，养病二百余日方愈。绕颈有肉如脂，头竟小偏。与此事绝相类。然则《广记》所载，尚有郑会、邵进等头断而仍活者，亦未必竟无其事也。

    Explanation

    795

    At first, when he ate, there was still hot air gushing out from his throat, which was where the bandit's sword had pierced him. After a long time, the wound healed, and the condition no longer occurred, but his head was slightly tilted to the left, as if always looking to the left. His sister-in-law worked for the Xu family in Hangzhou, which is where my second daughter is married, so I was able to learn the details. How extraordinary! Surviving a fatal wound, it must have been divine support, as the saintly and wise official Pei Gong would have it; how could a common person achieve this, unless he had hidden virtue? According to Taiping Guangji, volume 376, citing Guangyi Ji, during the early years of the Tang Dynasty, Wang Mu of Taiyuan was defeated in battle in Nanyang. The bandit attacked him from behind with a sword, breaking all his bones except for his throat. At first, he was unaware, and only realized it after a while. His head was on his navel, so he had to use his hands to hold his head to his neck. Soon after, it fell again, so he tied his hair on both sides to keep it in place, but his left arm's hair came loose, and his head fell into his chest. He then tied his hair again to straighten his head and walked forty miles. His subordinates saw him and helped him back to the army, where he spent over two hundred days recovering. His neck had flesh like fat, and his head was slightly tilted. This story is very similar to the one about Wang Mu. Indeed, Taiping Guangji also records cases of Zheng Hui and Shao Jin, who survived decapitation, so such events might not be entirely fictional.

    796

    江西有谭仙姑者，不知何时人。或云孝女，或云贞女，盖女子而夭折者，其事不得而详也。在江西颇著灵异。嘉庆、道光间，湖北大旱，或言江西谭仙姑求雨最灵，乃往迎之，以纸书谭仙姑之位，置肩舆中。复使人执雉尾导其前，言神所凭依也。神至，果得大雨，于是遂为立庙。其后祈报者无虚日。遇有水旱偏灾，乡民辄迎其神祷焉。有舆夫戏言：“空舆耳，舁此何为？”言甫出口，神舆顿重，压此舆夫仆地；再舁之，不能举矣。咸知获罪于神，使叩首谢过，乃复如初焉。余按陆鲁望《野庙碑》云：“江浙间山椒水滨多淫祀，妇而尊严则曰媪，女而容艳则曰姑。然则此风自唐已然矣。惟闻谭仙姑之庙，凡素行不修者，皆不得入，入必为神所谴。有三妇人入庙瞻礼，归经路厕，旋风忽起，粪秽激射。中间一妇人衣裙皆污，而前后两妇相去咫尺，略不г濡。群以为异，后知此妇素悍也。然则如谭仙姑者，尚不失聪明正直之义，或未可以淫祀例之与？

    Explanation

    796

    In Jiangxi, there is a Maiden Tan, whose exact time period is unknown. Some say she was a filial daughter, others say she was a chaste maiden, but it is known that she died young, and the details of her life are not fully known. She is quite renowned in Jiangxi for her supernatural abilities. During the reigns of Jiaqing and Daoguang, a severe drought struck Hubei, and it was said that the Maiden Tan of Jiangxi was the most effective at summoning rain. So, they went to invite her, writing "Maiden Tan's position" on a piece of paper and placing it in a palanquin. They also had someone hold a peacock feather in front of the palanquin, saying that this was where the spirit resided. When the spirit arrived, it indeed brought heavy rain, and thus a temple was built for her. Afterward, there was no day without those coming to pray and offer thanks. Whenever there were localized floods or droughts, the villagers would always welcome her spirit to pray. One of the palanquin bearers made a joking remark: "It's just an empty palanquin, why are we carrying this?" As soon as the words were spoken, the palanquin suddenly became heavy, pressing the bearer down to the ground; when they tried to lift it again, they could not. Everyone knew they had offended the spirit, so they made him kneel and apologize, and then it returned to normal. I refer to Lu Ruxiang's Wild Temple Stele, which says: "In the hills and waters of Jiangsu and Zhejiang, there are many excessive and inappropriate worship practices. Women who are dignified are called 'Aunt,' and women who are beautiful are called 'Maiden.' Thus, this custom has existed since the Tang Dynasty. However, I have heard that in the temple of Maiden Tan, anyone who has not behaved properly is not allowed to enter, and if they do, they will be punished by the spirit. Once, three women entered the temple to pay their respects. On their way back, a whirlwind suddenly arose, and fecal matter was sprayed. The woman in the middle had her clothes and skirt soiled, while the two women in front and behind her, though only a short distance away, were not affected at all. Everyone found it strange, and later it was known that this woman was usually fierce. Thus, like Maiden Tan, she still upholds the virtues of wisdom and righteousness, and perhaps she cannot be categorized as one of those excessive and inappropriate deities."

    797

    孟县刘某，生二子，皆成立矣。又娶妾，生一女，女患目疾，使巫视之。巫曰：“祖坟风水与此女不利，不迁葬，目且盲。”妾乃与刘计，将如巫言。二子执不可，曰：“巫言固不可信，吾家幸平安，祖坟年代久远，岂可轻动？”而妾日夕聒其夫曰：“吾无子，止此一女，不幸而盲，是无女也。二子但知自为计，君幸为我计！”刘纳其言，乃不与二子谋，故使二子他适，即发祖坟。甫起其棺，而女在家大叫，登时目盲。迁葬之后，刘不久死，二子亦相继死，遂无后。余按《仪礼。丧服篇》“改葬缌”，是古人固有改葬之礼。然郑康成注曰：谓坟墓以他故崩坏，将亡失尸柩也。是古人改葬，非如后世之以风水起见。惟唐李阳冰《扌西先茔记》，则实用方士邵权之说，谓干温冥之禁，非窀穸攸宜，盖唐时已有此等谬说矣。《春秋》庄〔三〕（二）年，书“葬桓王”，《公》《谷》皆以为“改葬”。何劭公谓恶录之。仁人孝子无轻言改葬哉！

    Explanation

    797

    A Man from Meng County, Liu, had two sons who had both grown up. He then married a concubine and had a daughter, who suffered from an eye disease. He sent a witch to examine her, and the witch said, "The feng shui of the ancestral grave is unfavorable to this daughter; if not reburied, her eyes will become blind." The concubine then planned with Liu to follow the witch's advice. The two sons firmly opposed it, saying, "The witch's words are certainly not credible. Our family is fortunately safe and sound, and the ancestral grave has been there for a long time, how can it be moved lightly?" But the concubine incessantly nagged her husband, saying, "I have no sons, only this one daughter. Unfortunately, if she becomes blind, it’s as if I have no daughter. The two sons only know how to think of themselves, please think of me!" Liu accepted her words and did not consult with the two sons, deliberately sending them elsewhere. He immediately exhumed the ancestral grave. As soon as the coffin was opened, the daughter at home screamed and immediately became blind. After the reburial, Liu did not live long, and the two sons also died one after another, leaving no descendants. I examined the Yili (Rites of Zhou) Sangfu (Funeral Rites) chapter, which mentions "simplified burial after relocation," indicating that ancient people had the practice of reburial. But Zheng Xuan commented, "It refers to graves that have collapsed due to other reasons, fearing the loss of the coffin and remains." Thus, ancient reburial was not like the later practice of considering feng shui. Only in the Xianying Ji (Record of the Ancestral Grave) by Li Yangbing of the Tang Dynasty, it actually used the words of the Taoist scholar Shao Quan, saying that violating the rules of the underworld is not suitable for burial, it seems that such false beliefs already existed in the Tang Dynasty. In the third year of Duke Zhuang of Lu, it is recorded "the burial of King Huan," both the Gongyang and Guliang commentaries consider it "a reburial." Mr. He Shao criticized it as a bad recording. Righteous and filial people should not lightly speak of reburial.

    798

    绍兴乌某，私其婢，生一子。妻恶之，归其婢于母家。婢乞以子从，许焉。婢嫁于房氏，子亦从之，遂为房氏子，不知有乌矣。后乌妻死，无子，乌欲返其子，时婢已死，请于房氏，房氏不可。有术者为乌谋曰：“吾能致之。”于乌氏祖墓之前，削木为代，埋其左畔。旬日后侦之房氏，则其子左目盲焉，乃使人告曰：“不归乌氏，右目亦且盲。”未信。复削木为弋，埋其右畔。旬日后，其子右目亦盲矣。大骇，就乌问故，乌谓之曰：“尔实吾子也。

    Explanation

    798

    In the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, there was a man named Wu who had a relationship with his concubine and she bore him a son. His wife disliked the son and returned the concubine to her family. The concubine requested that her son follow her, and Wu agreed. The concubine was married into the Fang family, and the son followed her, becoming a son of the Fang family and unaware of his true parentage. Later, Wu's wife died, and he had no children, so he wished to reclaim his son. At that time, the concubine had already died. He approached the Fang family, but they refused. A diviner proposed a plan to Wu, saying, "I can bring about the desired outcome." The diviner carved a wooden substitute and buried it on the left side of Wu's ancestral tomb. Ten days later, when he investigated with the Fang family, the son's left eye became blind. The diviner then warned, "If you do not return the son to the Wu family, his right eye will also become blind." The Fang family did not believe him. The diviner then carved another wooden substitute and buried it on the right side of the tomb. Ten days later, the son's right eye also became blind. The Fang family was greatly alarmed and approached Wu to ask the reason. Wu then revealed, "You are indeed my son."

    799

    ”因告以术者所为，曰：“尔非吾子，则乌氏之墓何预尔房氏之子哉？”子乃泣下，愿归乌氏。房氏知不可留，亦听之。于是先起左代，后起右代，旬日之间，两目复明如故。绍兴城中有天官第，相传为吏部陈公宅。陈先官江西，曾至贵溪龙虎山谒张真人。有童子献茶，失于碎其杯，真人怒，命左右执之去。陈曰：“童子无心之失，可恕也。”真人首肯，命释之。后陈老而家居，有老人求见，以玉如意献云：“此玉可御火灾。昔蒙活命之恩，今效衔珠之报，幸勿却也。”陈惘然不识所谓，老人曰：“曩日真人府中献茶童子，即我也。

    Explanation

    799

    In Shaoxing city, there is a residence called the Heaven Official's Mansion, which is said to be the former residence of Mr. Chen, the head of the Ministry of Personnel. Mr. Chen was previously an official in Jiangxi and once visited Longhu Mountain in Guixi to pay respects to the True Person Zhang. A young boy offered tea, but accidentally broke the cup. The True Person became angry and ordered the attendants to take the boy away. Mr. Chen said, "The boy's mistake was unintentional; it can be forgiven." The True Person nodded in agreement and ordered the boy to be released. Later, Mr. Chen retired and lived at home. An old man sought an audience and presented a jade如意, saying, "This jade can ward off fire. In the past, I was saved from death; now I offer this as a token of gratitude, please accept it." Mr. Chen was puzzled and did not understand what was meant. The old man revealed, "That boy who offered tea at the True Person's residence was me."

    In another story, a fortune-teller told a boy from the Fang family about the actions of the fortune-teller, saying, "If you are not my son, then how does the tomb of the Wu family concern you, who are a member of the Fang family?" The boy wished to return to the Wu family. The Fang family knew they could not keep him and also agreed. First, the left eye opened, then the right eye opened. Within ten days, both eyes were as clear as before.

    800

    微公一言，必为真人所斩矣！”陈因诘其姓名，老人曰：“实告公，我乃龙也。天下之龙，各分疆域，亦如人间州县。然其未有分地者，必先服役于真人府，役满乃得受地。我已役满，奉真人文牒，来为此地龙王矣。”陈曰：“君既龙王，可容我一见真身乎？”老人曰：“可。”期于某日至瓜子湖相见。瓜子湖在绍兴南门外，广六七里，其西岸故有龙王庙。陈届期至庙中，凭栏而望。时方盛暑，烈日当空，万里无云。午后忽起微风，俄而风甚，黑云四起，转瞬之间，雷电大作，急雨骤至。

    Explanation

    800

    But for your words, I would certainly have been executed by the True Person! Chen then asked for his name. The old man replied, "I will tell you honestly, I am a dragon. Dragons across the world each have their own territory, just like human administrative divisions. However, those without assigned territories must first serve in the True Person's office; only after completing their service can they receive territory. I have completed my service, and with the True Person's document of appointment, I have come to become the Dragon King of this place." Chen said, "Since you are the Dragon King, may I see your true form?" The old man replied, "Yes." They agreed to meet on a certain day at Guaizi Lake. Guaizi Lake is located near the south gate of Shaoxing, spanning about six or seven miles. There used to be a Dragon King temple on its west bank. Chen arrived at the temple on the agreed date and leaned on the railing, looking out. It was the height of summer, with the sun blazing overhead and the sky clear with no clouds. In the afternoon, a gentle breeze suddenly arose, soon becoming stronger, and dark clouds gathered from all sides. In an instant, thunder and lightning struck fiercely, and a sudden downpour came.

    801

    湖中白浪滔天，遥望有一物蜿蜒而出，长十余丈，粗可数尺，在水际转侧不定。初时止见其身，不见首尾，后微露其爪，形如鸡距，色黑如漆。旋又露两角，未几，并首亦全见，与世间所图画不甚异也。向陈若叩首者，陈亦举手答礼。已而复没于水，雨止风定，晴霁如初。陈归，以其所赠玉如意缄庋梁间，每遇火灾，辄至陈第而灭，故至今存焉。此事乃沈君祖炜言之。吏部陈公，不知何人，案头无《绍兴府志》，不可考也。其情迹颇近荒诞，盖故老相传如此。沈君即绍郡人，言之凿凿，姑为记之。《齐谐》志怪之书，原不必其为信史也。

    Explanation

    801

    In the lake, the waves were as high as the sky, and in the distance, something emerged, winding and twisting, measuring over ten zhang in length and several feet in thickness, turning and shifting uncertainly at the water's edge. At first, only its body was visible, with its head and tail unseen, but later, its claws were slightly revealed, shaped like a chicken's spur, black as lacquer. Soon after, two horns appeared, and shortly, the entire head became visible, not much different from what was depicted in the world. The one who bowed deeply to Chen, Chen also raised his hand in return as a gesture of respect. Afterward, it submerged back into the water, the rain stopped, the wind calmed, and the sky cleared as it had at the beginning. Chen returned home, sealing and storing the jade如意 (a symbol of good fortune) given to him in the rafters of his house. Whenever there was a fire, it would appear at Chen's residence and extinguish the flames, so it still exists to this day. This story was told by Mr. Shen Zuwei. The official from the Ministry of Personnel, Mr. Chen, is unknown, as there is no Shaoxing Fu Zhi (local gazetteer of Shaozhou) on his desk, making it impossible to verify. The events described are somewhat close to the fantastical, as they have been passed down by the old people. Mr. Shen is from Shaozhou and spoke with great certainty, so I will record it here. The Qi Hua (Strange Tales) is a collection of supernatural stories, originally not claiming to be a reliable historical record.

    802

    沈君祖炜又言：昔有航海者，帆风而行，正极顺适，忽闻其后声如怒雷。回顾，见波浪汹涌，一大鱼尾舟而来。张其口，含岈若巨壑，皓齿排列若霜戈雪戟，一翕一辟，海水从之出入，洪流氵养，涛作风兴。舟人大恐，而鱼行益近。时舟中有猪百头，羊半之，米面称是，乃谋以此等物稍稍掷与之，冀其得食则止。始而猪羊，继而米面，随投随食，在鱼不过一哆口而已，不费咀嚼。须臾诸物皆尽，无物可投。鱼意虽未餍，而其腹亦似果然，缩鳞卷鬣，悠然而逝。舟中人乃相庆，若更生云。此所谓吞舟之鱼，在海中固有之，不足为异也。沈君又言：其友陈姓者，曾至台湾。一日，见海面有青气上腾，须臾四散，因问舟人：“此何物？”舟人曰：“皆蚌也。群龙欲夺其珠，故逃耳。请看后面，龙至矣。”语未已，果见群龙数十，或青或黑，四面飞去。

    Explanation

    802

    Señor Shen Zuwěi also said: Once there were sailors who were sailing with the wind, enjoying perfect smoothness and comfort. Suddenly, they heard a sound like a furious thunder behind them. Looking back, they saw turbulent waves and a huge fish trailing the boat. The fish opened its mouth, which looked like a vast ravine, and its white teeth were arranged like spears of frost and snow. It opened and closed its mouth, causing water to enter and exit, creating a massive flow and waves that rose like wind. The sailors were greatly frightened, and the fish came even closer. At that time, the boat had 100 pigs, 50 sheep, and an equivalent amount of rice and flour. They then planned to throw these things to the fish little by little, hoping that it would stop when it got food. They started with the pigs and sheep, followed by the rice and flour. The fish ate everything immediately, and for the fish, it was just one gulp, without chewing. Soon all the things were finished, and there was nothing left to throw. The fish's desire was not yet satisfied, but its belly seemed full. It curled its scales and mane and left calmly. The people on the boat then celebrated, as if they had been reborn. This is what is called a fish that can swallow a boat; in the sea, such things are common and not surprising.

    Shen Zuwěi also said: His friend, a man surnamed Chen, had once gone to Taiwan. One day, he saw a green vapor rising from the sea, which soon dispersed. He then asked the boatman, "What is this?" The boatman said, "All are clams. A group of dragons wanted to take their pearls, so they fled. Please look behind; the dragons are coming." Before the words were finished, indeed, dozens of dragons, some blue and some black, flew around from all sides.

    803

    湖北武昌县所属有狼子湖，故多鱼，渔舟聚焉。光绪辛巳四月朔，雷雨交作，见云雾中隐隐有物，众以为龙，皆伏而观之。俄霹雳一声，此物下坠，雨亦旋止。逼视之，乃大鲢鱼也，长丈余，其头尚仰视，似未即死。渔人或以叉{措}之，仍不动，再{措}籍之，始垂首而毙。于是居民集，各持刀割其肉去，烹而食之，味甚肥美，亦无他异。此事见上海所刊新闻纸，因上二事类志之。

    Explanation

    803

    In Wuchang County, Hubei Province, there is a lake called Wolf Son Lake, which is known for its abundance of fish and attracts many fishing boats. On the first day of the fourth month in the year 1891 during the Guangxu era, thunder and rain occurred simultaneously. People saw something faintly visible in the clouds and fog and thought it was a dragon, so they all crouched and watched it. Suddenly, a loud thunderclap was heard, and the thing fell down; the rain also stopped soon. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a large carp, about ten feet long, with its head still looking up, as if it had not died immediately. Fishermen used spears to stab it, but it still did not move. They stabbed it again with force, and it finally lowered its head and died. The residents then gathered, each taking a knife and cutting off pieces of its meat. They cooked and ate it, finding the taste very rich and delicious, with no other unusual occurrences. This incident was reported in a newspaper published in Shanghai, and it is included in a broader collection of similar stories.

    804

    湖北武昌有杨姓者，设肆于汉口，夫妇皆居肆中，年逾四十，尚无子。其弟有一子，杨夫妇皆甚爱之，言于弟，欲以为嗣，弟亦许焉。杨妇每自汉口回家，辄为儿制新衣履，且多以果饵饲之，儿视伯母如母也。某年杨妇回，抚爱此儿与平时无异，居已逾月矣。一日，儿至妇卧室，嬉戏甚乐。妇忽出，至厨下取刀而入，力斫儿。儿大叫倒地，未死，又连斫之，乃死。其弟夫妇闻声趋至，见状大惊，曰：“谁杀吾儿？”妇曰：“我也。”弟夫妇莫测其意，惟抱儿而哭。久之，妇亦抱儿而哭。弟夫妇因问：“嫂何故杀儿？”妇曰：“吾讵知之邪？儿死，吾亦不欲生矣。”以头触墙壁，欲自杀。

    Explanation

    804

    In Wuchang, Hubei, there was a man surnamed Yang who ran a shop in Hankou. He and his wife lived in the shop. They were over forty years old but still had no children. His younger brother had a son whom the Yang couple loved very much. They told the younger brother their intention to adopt the boy as their son, and he agreed. Whenever the Yang wife returned from Hankou, she would make new clothes and shoes for the boy and often feed him fruits and candies. The boy treated his uncle's wife as if she were his mother. One year, after the Yang wife returned, she treated the boy as she usually did, and he stayed with them for more than a month. One day, the boy went to the wife's bedroom and played very happily. Suddenly, the wife came out, went to the kitchen to get a knife, and returned to strike the boy forcefully. The boy screamed and fell to the ground, not dead yet, but she struck him repeatedly until he died. The younger brother and his wife rushed over upon hearing the noise. Seeing the scene, they were greatly shocked and asked, "Who killed my child?" The wife replied, "I did." The younger brother and his wife could not understand her intention and only held the child and cried. After a while, the wife also held the child and cried. The younger brother and his wife then asked, "Why did you kill my child?" The wife said, "How could I know? With the child dead, I no longer wish to live." She struck her head against the wall, attempting to commit suicide.

    805

    弟夫妇反共劝慰，曰：“固知嫂无他意，此必别有故。吾夫妇年尚少，儿死可再生。愿嫂无介意！”妇乃出所制衣履，为儿著之，且买良材为作椟。既敛，妇百计求死，弟夫妇防之甚严。城外故有田宅，妇托故至城外，弟夫妇亦从之往。妇乃绝食，弟夫妇执饮食苦劝，曰：“嫂不食，吾二人亦不食。”妇不得已，为举箸，然曰：“吾杀此儿，何面目见尔夫妇！且亦何以见吾夫哉！吾夫妇老矣，不能生子，万一尔夫妇竟无子，杨氏血食不以吾斩乎？”其弟夫妇知其必自杀，使人旦暮守之，刀绳之属皆为藏┑。妇伺守者稍懈，取竹一节，踏碎之，以竹末自刺其喉，断吭而死。此妇之杀此儿，必有厉鬼凭依之。袁子才先生《新齐谐》载有舅杀其甥事，与此类也。

    Explanation

    805

    My brother and his wife came to console her, saying, "We know you have no other intentions; there must be some other reason. We are still young, and our son can be reborn. We hope you won't be upset!" The woman then took out the clothes and shoes she had made, dressed them on him, and bought good materials to make a coffin. After the burial, the woman sought death in every way, and my brother and I guarded her very strictly. There was land and property outside the city, and the woman pretended to go to the countryside. My brother and I followed her. The woman then stopped eating, and my brother and I held food and urged her, saying, "If you don't eat, we won't eat either." The woman, unable to resist, picked up the chopsticks, but said, "I killed this child. How can I face you both? And how can I face my husband? My husband and I are old and cannot have more children. If you two never have a child, will the Yang family's sacrifices be cut off because of me?" My brother and I knew she would kill herself, so we had people watch her day and night, hiding knives and ropes. The woman waited for the guards to relax slightly, took a piece of bamboo, crushed it, and used the bamboo splinters to stab her throat, cutting it and dying. This woman's killing of the child must have been influenced by a malevolent spirit. Mr. Yuan Zicai's New Qi Xie records a case of an uncle killing his nephew, similar to this.

    806

    朱某，苏州人，设肆于临顿路。咸丰庚申年，贼陷苏州，朱于道路间得人家所弃女子，遂收养之。居数载，女年十六七，遂以为妻，时朱则年逾四十矣。生子女数人，长女嫁于湖州，子皆学贾于外。一岁，妇携其二幼女至湖州视其长女，久而不反，朱使人问女，言母归数月矣。朱各处侦探，杳无踪迹。久之，有人言于金匮县见其妻。朱使导往，则其妻果在，盖已别从一人矣。朱讼于官，判归朱，未几又亡去，朱郁郁不乐。又以道路往返耗其资，不复能设肆，恒至余家助爨烹之役。婢媪辈言其事如此。老夫女妻过以相与，在朱诚不为无过，然既生育子女，且皆成立，乃舍之而去，遂同陌路。

    Explanation

    806

    Zhu, a man from Suzhou, ran a shop on Lindun Road. In the year of Xianfeng Gengshen (1860), when the rebels occupied Suzhou, Zhu found an abandoned girl on the road and took her in to raise. After several years, the girl was around sixteen or seventeen, and Zhu married her, at which time he was over forty years old. They had several children, with their eldest daughter marrying in Huzhou, and their sons studying trade outside. One year, the woman took her two young daughters to visit her eldest daughter in Huzhou, but did not return. Zhu inquired about her, and it was said that she had returned home after several months. Zhu searched everywhere but found no trace of her. After some time, someone told him that they had seen his wife in Jinkui County. Zhu sent someone to guide him there, and indeed, his wife was there, having already taken up with another man. Zhu took her to court, and the court ruled in his favor, but soon after, she left again. Zhu became depressed and unhappy. Moreover, the back-and-forth travel had depleted his resources, and he could no longer run his shop. He would often come to our house to help with cooking and other chores. The maids and female servants spoke of the matter in this way. The old man and his young wife lived together, and while Zhu was not without fault, since they had already raised children, and all had grown up, she left him and went her own way, becoming strangers.

    807

    杜老诗云“轻薄桃花逐水流”，此女之谓矣。余家有佣媪朱氏，亦苏州人。当庚申之乱，负其一子一女仓皇出走。已而惫甚，度不能兼全，拟弃其女。而女已ΝΜ能步，每置之地，辄牵衣不释，不得已，仍负之行。至一桥，高甚，朱乃置其女于桥堍（堍，俗字，然吴文英词云“乍凌波断桥西堍”，则宋时已有此语矣），疾负其子度桥而去。行数十武，犹闻其女在隔岸唤母呼兄也。及乱定，朱复还苏州，积佣值，颇有所蓄。为子娶妇，而女家索陪门财，其余衣裙钗钏，所费颇巨，数年积累，一朝而罄。妇至，又不顺其姑，时有谇语。朱每为余儿妇辈言之，辄呜咽而哭。计此妇生年正在庚申之后，或疑即其所弃女转世也。虽出臆度之辞，不为无见矣。

    Explanation

    807

    A woman like the one described by Du Fu in his poem, "Light and frivolous like peach blossoms that drift with the current," is exactly what this woman was. In my household, there was an elderly servant named Zhu, who was also from Suzhou. During the chaos of the Gengshen year, she carried her son and daughter in a panic, fleeing for their lives. Soon afterward, she was extremely exhausted and judged that she could not save both. She planned to abandon her daughter, but the girl, though unable to walk, would cling to her mother's clothes and refuse to let go. Unable to do otherwise, she continued to carry her. They reached a high bridge, and Zhu placed her daughter on the bridge's base (the term "堍" is a colloquial word, but it appears in Wu Wenying's poem, "Zha Lingbo Duanqiao Xitù," indicating that it was already in use during the Song dynasty). She quickly carried her son across the bridge and left. After walking several steps, she could still hear her daughter calling for her mother and brother from the other side of the river. When the chaos subsided, Zhu returned to Suzhou and accumulated her wages, saving quite a bit. She arranged for her son's marriage, but the daughter-in-law's family demanded a dowry, and the rest of the expenses for clothes, skirts, hairpins, and bracelets were substantial. Years of savings were depleted in one go. The daughter-in-law did not obey her mother-in-law and often had quarrels. Zhu would often speak of this to her grandchildren, and she would weep bitterly. Calculating the daughter-in-law's age, she was born after the Gengshen year, and some suspected that she might be the reincarnation of the daughter Zhu had abandoned. Though this is speculative, it is not entirely without merit.

    808

    沈某，亦苏州人，当兵乱时，逃至上海，颇有口辩。泰西人之商于沪渎者，延之至其寓，使佐会计，大有所获。偶见一舟人妇，艳之，欲娶为妻；而此妇喜其多金，亦愿委身，惟故有夫在，计无所出。妇有所善妇曰长子者，素以姊称之，因与谋焉。长子曰：“易耳。”乃赁其舟，伪欲如某处。至黄浦，风浪颇急，舟人持篙立船头，长子曰：“帆太高，宜下之。”亲出指点，突挤舟人坠江中，反号呼拯救，则无及矣。妇既丧其夫，遂归于沈。及乱定，沈与妇返苏州，生计充裕，甚自得也。妇忽得怪疾，辄自语曰：“来矣！来矣！”遂瞑眩而仆，如是者几年余。一日张目曰：“今日真来矣！”仆地遂绝。长子仍居沪上，于妇死前数日，忽若与人争辩者，曰：“此尔妇之谋，何预我事？”又曰：“无多言，从尔去。”遂赴水而死。

    Explanation

    808

    Se, also a native of Suzhou, fled to Shanghai during the turmoil and was quite eloquent. Western merchants who were trading in Shanghai invited him to their residence and made him assist with accounting, which brought him substantial financial gain. One day, he saw a boatman's wife and found her attractive, wanting to marry her. The woman, pleased by his wealth, was also willing to submit herself, but since she already had a husband, she had no solution. She had a friend, whom she always called "Elder Sister," and they devised a plan together. Elder Sister said, "It's easy." So they rented the boat, pretending to go to a certain place. When they reached the Huangpu River, the wind and waves were quite fierce. The boatman stood at the bow holding a pole. Elder Sister said, "The sail is too high, it should be lowered." She personally went to point it out, suddenly pushing the boatman into the river. Then she shouted for help, but it was too late. The woman, having lost her husband, then married Se. After the turmoil ended, Se and his wife returned to Suzhou, where their livelihood was comfortable, and they were very content. The woman suddenly developed a strange illness, often speaking to herself, saying, "Here you are! Here you are!" She then fell into a faint, like this for several years. One day, she opened her eyes and said, "Today, indeed, here you are!" She fell to the ground and died. Elder Sister still lived in Shanghai and, several days before the woman's death, suddenly acted as if he were arguing with someone, saying, "This is your wife's plan, what does it have to do with me?" Then he said, "Don't talk more, follow me." He then drowned himself.

    809

    苏州又有陈氏子者，当兵乱时，随其母避乱至上海。一日语母曰：“门前大树上有红衣人招我。”母叱曰：“勿妄言，目眩耳。”次日，陈子竟攀援登树，坐义桠中，俄枝断，坠地死。大儿妇因言，其同乡余氏女，方与母食，食毕，投箸遽出。乡俗谓“饭后不饮茶，所食不化”，母乃呼其子曰：“汝焉往？且饮茶去。”子曰：“门外有人呼我，我出即返耳。”匆匆出门，投水而死。此等事，殆所谓夙冤者与？

    Explanation

    809

    In Suzhou, there was another son of the Chen family who, during a time of military chaos, fled with his mother to Shanghai. One day, he told his mother, "There is a red-clothed person waving at me on a large tree in front of our house." His mother scolded him, saying, "Don't speak nonsense, you're hallucinating." The next day, the Chen son climbed the tree, sat in the fork of a branch, and suddenly the branch broke, causing him to fall to the ground and die. The eldest son's wife then mentioned that her fellow villager, a daughter of the Yu family, was having a meal with her mother. After finishing the meal, she quickly put down her chopsticks and rushed out. According to local custom, "one should not drink tea after a meal, as it prevents digestion." Her mother called out to her, "Where are you going? Go drink some tea first." The daughter replied, "There's someone calling me outside; I'll just go out and come back right away." She hurriedly went out and threw herself into the water, drowning. Such events, perhaps, are what is meant by a long-standing grudge or karmic debt?

    810

    湖北咸宁余氏女，嫁为石氏妇。石故无赖，日事饮博，余女生一子而卒，余氏收而蓄之。既长，使出而学贾。其子颇勤谨，师甚爱之，垂三年矣。石闻其子稍有所蓄，又余氏所具衣装冬夏皆备，乃自诣其师，欲以其子归。师留之，石不可，曰：“吾子与汝为徒耳，非与汝为奴也，汝安得留？”师知不可夺，厚赠而遣之。其地距石所居三四百里，石挈其子至半途，适阻小水，乃赁一舟尽载所赍。父子俱登舟矣，石语其子曰：“吾遗一物在某处，可往取之。”子登岸至其地，遍觅无有，将返问之父，比至水滨，则舟已开矣。沿岸追寻，无所睹，乃大窘，亻累然一身，进退俱不可。

    Explanation

    810

    A Woman from Xianning, Hubei, and Her Son

    A woman from the Yu family in Xianning, Hubei, married into the Shi family. Shi was a good-for-nothing, daily engaged in drinking and gambling. After the Yu woman gave birth to a son and died, the Yu family took him in and raised him. When the boy grew up, they sent him out to learn trade. The boy was diligent and careful, and his teacher loved him very much, for nearly three years. Shi heard that his son had accumulated some wealth, and the Yu family had all the necessary clothing for both seasons ready. Shi then went to see his teacher and wanted to take his son back. The teacher tried to keep him, but Shi refused, saying, "My son is your apprentice, not your slave; how can you keep him?" The teacher knew he could not change Shi's mind and gave him generous gifts and let him go. The place was about three to four hundred miles away from where Shi lived. Shi took his son to the halfway point, just happened to be blocked by a small stream. He then rented a boat to carry everything he had. Both father and son got on the boat. Shi told his son, "I left something at a certain place; you can go get it." The son went ashore to the place, searched everywhere but found nothing. He was about to return to ask his father, but by the time he reached the water's edge, the boat had already set sail. He tried to follow along the shore but saw nothing. He was greatly distressed, alone and helpless, neither advancing nor retreating was possible.

    811

    不得已，乞食于途，久之跖穿膝暴，胫骨腐烂生且，病不能行。群モ怜而饮食之，得不死。有咸宁人过其地，或告之曰：“此子尔同乡也，能挈之归乎？”其人就而问之，曰：“是吾乡人余氏之甥也，吾归当为言于余氏。”及归，如余氏告之，余氏急使人迎以归，已憔悴无人状矣。持养数月，乃始能行。后余氏又使学贾于他处，戒之曰：“汝父若来，勿再为所诳也。”夫道路之间，杀越人于货，尚且有之，况止于欺诳，又何足异！所异者，以父诳其子而取其资，是不可以人理论矣。余书为搜神志怪而作，然记及此等事者，人无人理，人即妖也，正不必魑魅罔两，然后可铸之禹鼎也。

    Explanation

    811

    Out of necessity, he begged for food on the road, and after a long time, his soles were worn through and his knees were exposed to the elements, his tibia bones rotting and decaying, leaving him sick and unable to walk. The common people pitied him and fed him, saving his life. A person from Xianning passed by his place, and someone told him, saying, "This boy is from your hometown. Can you take him back with you?" The person approached and asked him, and he replied, "He is the nephew of my fellow townsman Mr. Yu. When I return, I will speak to Mr. Yu about him." When he returned, he told Mr. Yu as he had been told. Mr. Yu quickly sent someone to bring him back, but by then, he was already emaciated and looked like a ghost. He was cared for for several months before he could walk again. Later, Mr. Yu sent him to learn trade in another place and warned him, saying, "If your father comes, do not be deceived by him again." On the road, there are those who kill strangers for their possessions; let alone mere deception, what is there to be surprised about? What is remarkable is that the father deceived his son to take his money; this cannot be explained by human reasoning. My writing is for recording strange and supernatural events, but when it comes to such matters, people lack reason, people are like demons; it is not necessary to cast them in the Yu Ding only after they become demons or spirits.

    812

    咸宁余氏有一妇，不知其母家何姓也。妇极短小，坐椅橙上，足不及地，然性柔婉，舅姑皆喜之，夫妇亦相得。久之无子，妇语其夫曰：“我自度不能生育，君家又贫，不能纳妾媵，不如我死，君得续娶。虽有雁之费，较买妾为省也。”夫曰：“是何言与？”而妇自是遂阴有自杀之意。乃归宁，从容语其母家曰：“妇人内夫家，外父母家。既已嫁人，其生其死，无与父母家矣。每见人家因女死，辄登夫氏之门，相嬲不休，此最无理。不幸我死，愿吾家勿蹈此恶习也。”其家曰：“汝幸未死，何出此不祥语？”妇笑曰：“我未死，故及言之，若我死，尚及言邪？”及归，又语其夫：“万一我死，君勿伤悼。续娶得子，我死胜于生！”夫初不知其有死志也。越数日，乘人不觉，竟缢而死。余氏大恸，母家之人果无一言，反相劝慰，此妇可谓贤矣。其夫续娶，亦无子，惟所娶者装送颇盛，余氏赖以温饱。后嗣族人子为子，已抱孙矣，亦足慰此妇于地下乎。

    Explanation

    812

    During the Xining period, the Yu family had a wife whose maiden name is unknown. The woman was very short, so short that when she sat on a chair, her feet did not touch the ground. Despite her small stature, she was gentle and amiable, and both her in-laws and husband loved her. After a long time, she had no children. One day, she told her husband, "I believe I cannot bear children. Your family is also poor and cannot afford to take concubines. It would be better if I were to die, so you could remarry. Although there would be expenses for a formal marriage, they would be less than the cost of buying a concubine." Her husband replied, "What are you saying?" From that day on, the woman secretly harbored the intention of committing suicide. She returned to her natal family and calmly told them, "A woman is part of her husband's family and separate from her parents' family. Once married, her life and death have nothing to do with her parents' family. I often see families visit the husband's home after a woman's death, pestering them endlessly. This is the most unreasonable behavior. If I were to die, I hope my family will not follow this bad custom." Her family said, "You are fortunate not to have died. Why do you speak such unlucky words?" The woman smiled and said, "I am still alive, so I can speak about it. If I were dead, would I still be able to speak?" Upon returning home, she again spoke to her husband, "If I were to die, do not grieve. If you remarry and have a son, my death would be better than my continued life!" Her husband did not know that she had such a death wish. A few days later, taking advantage of no one noticing, she hanged herself. The Yu family was greatly saddened, and her natal family did not say a word, instead offering comfort. This woman can truly be called virtuous. Her husband remarried, but still had no children. Only the new wife brought a substantial dowry, which allowed the Yu family to live comfortably. Later, a son was adopted from the extended family, and they already had a grandson. Is this enough to comfort this woman in the afterlife?

    813

    《列女传。母仪篇》云：古者妇人妊子，目不视于邪色，耳不听于淫声。此特言胎教之法耳。《博物志。杂说篇》云：妇人妊娠，不欲令见丑恶物异类鸟兽。又云：不可见兔，令儿唇缺；不可啖生姜，令儿多指。此则近于避忌之说，然犯之亦未必遽验也。独湖北妇人妊子避忌最甚，有所谓换胎者，言所见之物，入其腹中，换去其本来之胎也。故妇人妊子，则房中所有人物画像，悉藏┑之。或以针刺其目，云其目破，即不为患矣。有一妇卧室县一美女像，及生子，厥状肖焉。美女屈右臂，伸三指，作指物状，此子亦屈右臂，伸三指，终身如此。

    Explanation

    813

    The Lie Nü Zhuan: Mu Yi Pian states: "In ancient times, when a woman was pregnant, she did not look upon improper sights, nor did she listen to lewd sounds." This passage merely refers to the method of fetal education. The Bó Wù Zhì: Zá Shuō Pian mentions: "When a woman is pregnant, she should not be allowed to see ugly or strange animals." It also states: "She should not see rabbits, as this might cause her child to have a cleft lip; she should not eat ginger, as this might make her child have extra fingers." These are close to superstitious beliefs about avoidance, but even if one violates these rules, it may not necessarily be immediately verified. However, women from Hubei Province take such precautions most seriously. There is a belief known as "huàn tāi" (changing the fetus), which suggests that what a pregnant woman sees can enter her womb and replace the original fetus. Therefore, when a woman is pregnant, all portraits of people and objects in her room are hidden or covered. Some even pierce the eyes of the images with needles, believing that if the eyes are damaged, it will not cause harm. One woman had a portrait of a beautiful woman hanging in her bedroom, and when she gave birth to a son, he resembled the woman in the portrait. The beautiful woman in the image had her right arm bent and extended three fingers, as if pointing at something. This child also had his right arm bent and extended three fingers, and he did this throughout his life.

    814

    又一妇偶观优，及生子，头上有肉隆起，如戴高冠，两耳旁各有肉一片下垂，如以巾幂之者然。因忆观优时，有优人之冠如是，为其换胎矣。其地每有游僧，担荷衣装，乞食村落，担上有弥勒像，此则尤在所忌。孕妇见之，生子必肖弥勒像矣。故此僧所至，村人辄噪而逐之。孕妇或不及避，猝与相遇，则坐地上，自解其履，以左履换至右足，右履换至左足。此僧亦必将所荷之担，从右肩换至左肩，从左肩换至右肩。如此相持，及人众咸集，逐此僧去，乃得无事。其理真不可究诘也。中牟县有兄弟二人，谈者失其姓名，兄弟同时病死。俄其弟复苏，则实其兄附之而生也。弟妻引之入室，不可，却入兄室，兄妻又拒之。

    Explanation

    814

    A woman once happened to watch a performance, and when she gave birth to a child, there was a swelling of flesh on her head, like wearing a tall hat, and on each side of her ears, there was a piece of flesh hanging down, like a cloth covering it. Because she remembered the performance, there was an actor's hat like that, and it must have been a case of fetal transformation. In that place, there are often wandering monks carrying clothes and begging for food in villages, with a statue of Maitreya on the shoulder pole, which is especially to be avoided. If a pregnant woman sees it, her child will surely resemble the image of Maitreya. Therefore, wherever this monk goes, the villagers always make a commotion and chase him away. If a pregnant woman does not manage to avoid it, she might suddenly encounter it, and she sits on the ground, takes off her shoes, and switches her left shoe to her right foot and her right shoe to her left foot. The monk will also switch the pole he carries from his right shoulder to his left shoulder and vice versa. They hold this position like this, and when the crowd gathers, they chase the monk away, and only then is there no trouble. The reason for this is truly impossible to investigate. In Zhongmu County, there were two brothers, and the names of the speakers are unknown, who died of illness at the same time. Soon after, the younger brother revived, and it turned out that his older brother had attached himself to him and was reborn. The younger brother's wife tried to bring him into their room, but he refused, and instead went into his brother's room, where his brother's wife also rejected him.

    815

    乃徙居于外，旬日之后，气体充实，如无病人。而一入内，则娣姒皆避之。不得已，出家为僧焉。按段成式《酉阳杂俎》载：上蔡县李简病卒，托汝阳县张宏义身以生，并引扁鹊易鲁公扈赵齐婴之心，及寤互返其室为证。扁鹊事未知出何书，段博极群书，必有所本也。是此等事，古固有之，古但谓之易心。《太平广记》再生部则谓之易形，至今人则谓之借尸还魂。按《金史。五行志》，大定十三年正月，尚书省奏：“宛平张孝善有子曰合得，大定十二年三月旦以疾死，至暮复活，云本是良乡人王建子喜儿。而喜儿前三年已死，建验以家事，能具道之。此盖假尸还魂，拟付王建为子。

    Explanation

    815

    A man moved to live outside, and after ten days, his body felt full of energy, as if he were a healthy person. However, as soon as he returned home, his sisters-in-law all avoided him. Unable to help it, he left home to become a monk. According to Duan Chengshi's Youyang Zazu, there was a man named Li Jian from Shangcai County who died of illness but was reborn into the body of Zhang Hongyi from Ruyang County, and the story also mentions Bian Que exchanging the hearts of Lu Gonghu and Zhao Qiying. When they woke up, they returned to their respective rooms as evidence. It is unknown from which book the story of Bian Que comes, but Duan Chengshi was well-versed in many books, so it must have a basis. Such events have existed in ancient times, and they were simply called "heart exchange." The Taiping Guangji in the "Rebirth" section refers to it as "body exchange," and in modern times, it is called "borrowing a body to return to life." According to the Jin Shi in the "Five Elements" section, in the first month of the thirteenth year of Emperor Shizong's reign (1173 CE), the Shangshu Province reported: "Zhang Xiaoshan from Wanping had a son named Hedé, who died of illness on the third day of the third month of the twelfth year of Emperor Shizong's reign (1172 CE) but revived in the evening, claiming to be Xier, the son of Wang Jian from Liangxiang. However, Xier had died three years earlier, and Wang Jian verified this with family matters, and Xier could recount them in detail. This is likely a case of borrowing a body to return to life, and it is intended to give Wang Jian a son."

    816

    ”上曰：“若是则奸幸小人，竞生诈伪，渎乱人伦，止付孝善。”此借尸还魂之事之见于正史者也。余尝论死生之事，以为鬼神之说，圣人固言之。吾人束发读《中庸》，即知“鬼神之为德，其盛矣乎”，安可不信？至轮回之说，圣人所不言，惟《列子》书言死之与生，一往一反。死于是者，安知不生于彼？此为佛氏轮回之说所祖。后世若羊祜、顾非熊事，炳然记载，即世俗所谓投胎者也。实则投胎犹之借尸耳。人之初死，一灵未泯，或适遇初产婴儿，一无知识，附之而生，则谓之投胎；或适遇新死之人，形骸未坏，附之而生，则谓之借尸：其理一也。不可谓竟无是事。然必谓人之生死悉如是，则又不可矣。

    Explanation

    816

    The Emperor said, "If so, then the cunning and fortunate small people will vie to produce deceit and falsehood, disrupting human relationships, and only reward filial piety and goodness." This is the case of possession of a corpse that is recorded in official history. I have often discussed matters of life and death, believing that the doctrine of ghosts and spirits, sages have indeed spoken of it. When we begin reading the Zhongyong at the age of coming of age, we immediately know that "the virtue of ghosts and spirits is truly great," how can we not believe it? As for the doctrine of reincarnation, sages do not speak of it, only the Liezi (a Daoist text) speaks of death and life as a cycle of going and returning. How can we be sure that those who die here do not be reborn there? This is the origin of the Buddhist doctrine of reincarnation. In later times, stories of Yang Hu and Gu Feixiong are clearly recorded, which are what the common people call reincarnation. In fact, reincarnation is still possession of a corpse. When a person first dies, their soul has not yet perished, and if they happen to encounter a newly born infant, who knows nothing, and attach themselves to be reborn, it is called reincarnation; or if they happen to encounter someone who has just died, whose body is not yet decayed, and attach themselves to be reborn, it is called possession of a corpse: the principle is the same. It cannot be said that there is no such thing. However, to say that all human deaths and rebirths are like this, then it is not so.

    817

    元吴自牧《梦粱录》云：杭城小民家，多无坑厕，只有马桶。每日自有出粪人氵蹇去，谓之倾脚头。按此俗，今苏、杭皆然。杭州有一细民，以倾脚头为业，因出入于横河桥许氏。许故钜族，民入其家，辄慕之曰：“吾死后得生许氏，虽数日而死，吾亦乐之。”后许氏一妇孕，将产，梦见是人来，遂生一子。使人问之，则是人果即于是日死矣。许氏素闻其语，咸知即是人托生也。子生七日而殇，其愿虽偿，其生何太促欤？

    Explanation

    817

    According to Wu Zimu's Menglianglu, it is said that in Hangzhou, many ordinary households lacked traditional toilets and instead used chamber pots. Each day, there were people who collected and removed these chamber pots, a job known as "倾脚头." This custom is still prevalent in Suzhou and Hangzhou today. In Hangzhou, there was a man who made a living by collecting chamber pots and frequently visited the Xu family in Henghe Bridge. The Xu family was a prominent clan, and the man, upon entering their home, expressed a wish to be reborn into their family, saying, "If I could be reborn into the Xu family, even if I only lived for a few days, I would be happy." Later, a woman from the Xu family became pregnant and dreamed of the man. She then gave birth to a son. When they asked about the man, they learned that he had indeed died on that very day. The Xu family, having heard his wish, believed that the newborn was his reincarnation. However, the child died after only seven days, fulfilling the man's wish but raising the question of why his life was so brief.

    818

    无锡杨氏女，许嫁江西程氏子。程故钜族，而程子因父母早亡，自幼失教，既长则骄乐佚游，从流忘反。程之长老寓书于杨，愿为赘婿，庶有所检束，杨氏许之，然不与女言其故也。程书在书室，为女所窃发，遂得其详，郁郁不乐，寝以成疾。及程子至，翩翩裘马少年也，而举止轻浮，语言讠鬼诨。女知所适非人，疾益增剧，程子殊不顾瞻。晨出则至暮不归，夕出则达旦未反，非博场即倡家也。如是年余，其所赍罄矣，并女之奁中物，亦且垂尽，自是女病益笃。临死前一日，已沉沉若死矣。程子偶入视之，见女腕上有金条脱一事，捋取而去。已而婢媪辈见失此物，诧曰：“谁所邪？”将议大索。

    Explanation

    818

    A girl from the Yang family in Wuxi was engaged to be married to a son from the Cheng family in Jiangxi. The Cheng family was an old and powerful clan, but the Cheng family's son, due to the early death of his parents, lacked proper education from a young age. As he grew older, he became骄奢淫逸，随波逐流。The elders of the Cheng family sent a letter to the Yang family, wishing for their son to become a son-in-law, hoping that this arrangement would bring him some restraint. The Yang family agreed, but they did not tell the daughter the reason behind the change. The Cheng family's letter was kept in the study room, where it was secretly opened by the girl, and she learned the details. She became deeply unhappy and fell ill due to prolonged depression. When the Cheng family's son arrived, he was a dashing young man dressed in fine clothes and riding a fine horse, but his behavior was frivolous, and his speech was full of jokes and innuendos. The girl realized that she was marrying the wrong person, and her illness worsened. The Cheng family's son paid no attention to her. He would leave in the morning and not return until evening, and leave in the evening and not return until dawn, often spending his time in gambling houses or brothels. After a year or so, his resources were exhausted, and even the dowry items that the girl had brought with her were nearly gone. From then on, the girl's illness became more severe. On the day before her death, she was deeply unconscious, as if dead. The Cheng family's son happened to visit her and saw a gold bracelet on her wrist. He took it away. Later, the maidservants and nurses noticed the missing item and wondered, "Who took it?" They were going to conduct a thorough search.

    819

    女犹知之，微摇其首曰：“不必寻矣。”女死，程亦辞去，不知所之。后杨氏次女婿秦君至京师，见之于正阳门外，则已为モ矣，叹曰：“吾僚婿也，乃至此欤？”赠以银十两，裘一袭，且曰：“亟还江西。果行也，当为办装。”程曰：“尔为尔之官，我为我之モ，何预尔事邪？”不顾而去。此子甘处下流，真别有肺肠者。其初至杨氏为赘婿也，杨氏宴之，且陈梨园之戏。优人进点（唐崔令钦《教坊记》云：凡欲出戏，先进曲名，上以墨点者即舞，不点者即否，谓之进点），程点《拾金》。《拾金》乃乞儿事也，众宾愕然。或病其不祥，或赏其通脱，而程竟以乞儿终，非其朕兆与？山东有陈媪者，老寡妇也。有二子，其长子甲早卒，而甲妇甚贤。

    Explanation

    819

    The woman still knew, and she slightly shook her head, saying, "There is no need to look further." After the woman died, Cheng also left, and no one knew where he went. Later, the Yang family's second son-in-law, Qin, arrived in the capital and saw Cheng outside the Zhengyang Gate, where he had become a beggar. Qin sighed and said, "He was my colleague's son-in-law, and now he has come to this?" He gave Cheng ten taels of silver and a fur coat, and said, "Hurry back to Jiangxi. If you do go, I will prepare your travel arrangements." Cheng replied, "You do your job, and I will do mine. What concern is it of yours?" He turned away and left. This man was content to remain in a lowly position, truly having a different kind of heart. When he first came to the Yang family as a son-in-law, the Yang family entertained him and performed music and dance from the imperial music bureau. The performers selected plays by marking them with ink, and Cheng chose the play "Picking Gold." "Picking Gold" is a play about a beggar, and the guests were startled. Some criticized it as inauspicious, while others praised it for its openness and ease. Cheng ultimately became a beggar, which was foreshadowed by his choice of the play. In Shandong, there was an old woman named Chen, an elderly widow. She had two sons, and the eldest, Jia, died early, while Jia's wife was very virtuous.

    820

    甲死后遗有千金，贷于人而食其息，姑妇二人颇足自给。次子乙及妇均不孝。陈媪自从甲妇居，乙不顾问。无何，甲所遗千金尽为人干没。甲妇无以为生，郁郁不乐，久之遂成迷惘之疾，饥饱寒燠皆不自知。媪怜之，昼夜调护之，又苦无食，力作以自食。甲妇之母族谓陈媪曰：“此非可长也，且以久疾累尊章，岂母家无人乎？”迎之归。媪自是从其次子居，乙夫妇虽不乐，然无以拒也。而乙妇悍甚，乙亦畏之。乙恒外出，乙妇亦时还母家，每为姑具五日之粮，则其返也必十日；具十日之粮，则其返也必二旬，媪于是恒不得饱。甲妇虽还母家，然犹念其姑，月必一来，来必居数日。

    Explanation

    820

    After A's death, he left behind a fortune of a thousand gold, which was borrowed and lived off the interest. The two women, A's wife and her mother-in-law, were quite able to support themselves. The younger son B and his wife were both unfilial. Tao Aunty lived with A's wife, but B did not ask about her. Soon, the thousand gold A left behind was all lost to others. A's wife had no means of living, she was sad and unhappy, and after a long time, she developed a state of confusion, unable to distinguish between hunger and cold. Tao Aunty pitied her and took care of her day and night, but she also suffered from hunger and worked hard to feed herself. A's wife's maternal family told Tao Aunty, "This cannot last long, and moreover, the long-term illness is a burden on your mother, is there no one in the maternal family?" They welcomed her back. Tao Aunty then lived with the younger son B, who was not happy, but they could not refuse. B's wife was very domineering, and B was also afraid of her. B often went out, and B's wife also sometimes returned to her maternal family. Whenever she prepared five days' worth of food for her mother-in-law, she would return after ten days; whenever she prepared ten days' worth of food, she would return after twenty days. Tao Aunty thus always went hungry. Although A's wife returned to her maternal family, she still thought of her mother-in-law and came once a month, staying for several days each time.

    821

    每日巡行庭除间，见地上破瓴&lt；瓦&gt；，或败絮、或故纸、或枯本朽株，一一拾起，以奉其姑。且呼之曰：“娘，俟此等物化为金银，我与娘皆不饿死矣。”邻舍人闻之，皆为叹息。而乙妇殊厌恶之，不为具食。媪节食食之，故不能久留。留数日，辄好言遣之，曰：“吾儿家去，勿念老身。”如是者十余年矣。陈氏之邻，故客舍也，会有达官税驾于此，以病小留，尽得其状，叹曰：“是其罪在干没者。夫两嫠妇所恃以活命者，而干没之，此非人矣。”命有司穷治其事，悉反其金。陈媪得金，亟召甲妇告之。甲妇见多金，大喜，神识顿清，夙疾若失。仍奉姑同居，且嗣族人子为子，娶妇生孙焉。乙客死于外，乙妇挟其资再嫁，而后夫好饮博，不事恒业，不数年，罄其所赍。俄后夫亦死，乙妇不能自存，乞食于路，甲妇时时饮食之，未几以痢死。

    Explanation

    821

    Every day, she patrolled the courtyard and steps, picking up broken tiles, old rags, discarded paper, and dead branches or rotten trees, and offered them to her mother-in-law. She would also call out to her, saying, "Mother, wait until these things turn into gold and silver, and neither I nor you will starve to death." The neighbors heard about it and all sighed. However, the second wife especially disliked this and refused to provide food. The elderly woman reduced her own food to feed her, so she could not stay long. After staying for a few days, she would kindly send her away, saying, "Go back to my son's house, do not worry about me." This pattern continued for over ten years. The neighbor of Chen's family used to be a guesthouse. One day, a high-ranking official stayed there temporarily due to illness, learned the entire situation, and exclaimed, "His crime lies in embezzlement. The two widows relied on these things to live, but he embezzled them, which is not human." He ordered the officials to thoroughly investigate the matter and return all the gold. When Chen the elderly woman received the gold, she quickly called the first wife to inform her. The first wife, seeing the large amount of gold, was greatly delighted, her spirit suddenly became clear, and her long-standing illness disappeared. She continued to serve her mother-in-law and lived together with her, adopted a son from the clan, married a wife, and had grandchildren. The second wife's husband died outside, and she took her wealth and remarried. Her new husband enjoyed drinking and gambling, did not engage in a steady occupation, and within a few years, emptied all his possessions. Soon after, her new husband also died, and the second wife could not support herself, begging for food on the streets. The first wife would often feed her, but she died of dysentery shortly after.

    822

    张翁，闽人也。少时至河南中牟县访所亲，而所亲已他去，遂留落不得归。久之无以为生，乃鬻其衣装，得钱万余，设星货铺于市廛间（星货铺，见李济翁《资暇集》，言列货丛杂，如星之繁）。会有某翁者，亦闽人，游于市。与之语，闽音也，喜曰：“吾同乡乎？”因叙桑梓之谊，时与往来。见张翁诚实，乃谓之曰：“如此琐琐，岂足生活？君旧时在闽，曾作何业？”张曰：“吾旧学于药肆，颇亦有年。”某曰：“然则启肆卖药，岂不胜此？”张曰：“固也，苦无资耳。”某曰：“以吾之资，藉君之力，同启一肆，有利均之。

    Explanation

    822

    Zhang Gong, a native of Fujian, traveled to Zhongmu County in Henan as a young man to visit his relatives, but found them absent. He remained there and could not return home. After a long time, having no means of livelihood, he sold his clothes and belongings for more than ten thousand coins and set up a shop selling miscellaneous goods in the market (a "star shop," as described in Li Jiweng's Zixiaji, where goods are displayed in a crowded, star-like manner). One day, an old man from Fujian also wandered in the market. When they spoke, they recognized each other's Fujian dialect, and the old man happily said, "Are we from the same hometown?" They then discussed their ties to their hometown and often visited each other. Seeing that Zhang Gong was honest, the old man said to him, "Such petty matters are not enough for a living. What did you do in Fujian before?" Zhang replied, "I used to learn in a pharmacy for quite a few years." The old man said, "Then why not open a pharmacy to sell medicine? Wouldn't that be better than this?" Zhang agreed, saying, "Indeed, but I lack the funds." The old man proposed, "With my financial support and your assistance, we can jointly open a pharmacy, and the profits will be shared equally."

    823

    ”次日，即出千金授张，曰：“好为之，吾今有事且回闽，异日再相见。”张于是即在中牟启药肆焉，买药者踵于门，利市三倍。张虽一钱之人，必籍之，岁终会计，有所赢余，则分为二，曰：“此肆吾与某君共之也。”而某君竟不复至。张自至闽访之，亦不得其人，归而叹曰：“吾何以报某君乎？”乃辜较某君岁所应得，别储之，榜于门，大施舍。病者药之，物故者之，寒饿者衣之食之；岁大无，振之行之；数十年不倦，中牟称善人焉。后其家益饶衍，其子于同治壬戌入词林，咸知其为行善之报。惟所谓某君者，见首不见尾，亦殊可异也。

    Explanation

    823

    The next day, he gave a thousand gold coins to Zhang and said, "Do well by it. I have something to attend to and will return to Fujian. We will meet again some other time." Zhang then opened a pharmacy in Zhongmou, and people buying medicine came in a steady stream, making a profit three times over. Zhang, despite being a person with very little money, recorded everything meticulously. At the end of the year, when accounts were settled, if there was a surplus, he would divide it in half, saying, "This pharmacy is shared between me and a certain gentleman." However, the gentleman never came back. Zhang went to Fujian to find him but could not locate him. Returning home, he sighed, "How can I repay the gentleman?" He then calculated what the gentleman should have received annually, set it aside separately, posted it on the door, and distributed it generously. He gave medicine to the sick, buried those who had died, clothed and fed those who were cold and hungry; in times of great famine, he relieved and distributed aid. For many years, he did not tire, and in Zhongmou, he was called a good man. Later, his family became increasingly prosperous, and his son entered the Hanlin Academy in the Ren Xu year of the Tongzhi era. Everyone knew it was the reward for doing good deeds.
