As for the so-called gentleman, he appeared at the beginning but disappeared at the end, which is very strange.

    824

    余外家临平姚氏，其疏族中有一妇，于吾外王父为尊属，余不及见矣。传闻其生前姣丽无双，且双趾纤小，每制履倦，则以钅咸线插髻上，帮帛垂耳后，才如一叶，人不见也。以故不良能行，行必以婢媪扶掖之，姚美人之名闻于乡里。今临平有地曰美人埭，以此妇名也。其子妇悍甚，恒与其姑立而谇语，妇懦不能与争，郁郁久之，竟雉经死。妇工翰墨，临死自书一纸，详述其子妇勃溪之状，置怀中。其子搜得，燔之。其子妇曰：“凡缢死者，下有遗魄，不掘出，且为祟。”乃掘地深数尺，果得如炭者一段，亦燔之。余幼时曾过其家，家尚温饱。乱后复访之，则无一人矣。有字蓉斋者，于余为舅氏，为贼掠去，不知所终。嗟乎！其先世有此等事，而望其子孙之克昌，则是无天道矣。

    Explanation

    824

    My maternal family, the Yao clan of Linping, had a distant relative, a woman who was a senior relative to my maternal grandfather. I never had the chance to meet her. It is said that she was exceptionally beautiful, with small feet that were highly valued in ancient China. She often grew tired from making shoes, so she would use a needle to pierce her hair bun, and a piece of cloth would hang behind her ear, as thin as a leaf, unseen by anyone. Because of this, she was not good at walking and had to be supported by maids or old women when she walked. She was known as the "Yao Beauty" in her village. Today, there is a place in Linping called "Beauty Dike," named after this woman. Her daughter-in-law was very fierce and often argued with her mother-in-law. The woman was weak and could not argue back, and she became depressed for a long time, eventually committing suicide by hanging. She was skilled in writing and, before her death, wrote a document detailing the quarrels with her daughter-in-law, placing it in her bosom. Her son found and burned the document. Her daughter-in-law said, "Those who die by hanging have lingering spirits. If we do not dig them up, they will cause trouble." So, they dug the ground several feet deep and found a piece that looked like charcoal, which they also burned. When I was young, I visited her family, and they were still comfortable. After a period of chaos, I visited again, but no one was left. There was a man named Rongzhai, who was my maternal uncle, and he was taken by bandits, and his fate is unknown. Alas! If such things happened to his ancestors, and we hope for the prosperity of his descendants, then there is no justice in heaven.

    825

    道光间，临平乡间有一妇，中年以后得一怪疾，口不能言，肢体不能运动，其耳目亦似无所见闻，昼夜卧床中，块然似已死者。而肌肤仍温和，口鼻仍有出入之息。使医切其脉，盖无病也。其子妇辈按时以糜粥饮之，尚能下咽，饭则不能咀嚼矣。如是五六年。一日晨起，饮之不受，抚之则冷，始知其已死也。其时喧传，以为奇事。余按纪文达笔记中有一事，与此相似，但彼男子，此妇人，为异耳。文达谓其心死而形生，余则以为此乃魂去而魄存也。《左传》载子产之言曰：“人生始化曰魄，既生魄，阳曰魂。”余尝以火譬之，人之形质，犹聚薪蒸而束之也。

    825

    During the reign of Emperor Daoguang, there was a woman in the village of Linping who, after reaching middle age, contracted a strange illness. She could no longer speak, her limbs were paralyzed, and her ears and eyes seemed to have no perception. She lay in bed day and night, appearing like a dead person. Yet her skin remained warm, and her mouth and nose still had breathing. If a doctor examined her pulse, it seemed there was no illness. Her children and relatives fed her porridge at regular intervals, and she could still swallow it, but she could not chew rice. This state persisted for five or six years. One morning, when they tried to feed her, she refused to drink, and when touched, her body was cold. Then it was known that she had died. At that time, the news spread widely, and it was considered a strange event. I recall an event in Jiwenda's notes that is similar, but that one was a man, this one a woman, which is the difference. Ji Yun said her heart was dead but her form was alive, but I believe this was a case where her soul had departed but her spirit remained. Zuo Zhuan records Zichan's words: "When life begins to take form, it is called '魄'; once '魄' is formed, the yang aspect is called '魂.'" I have often compared the human form to a bundle of firewood and steam.

    826

    置火于中，俄而薪化为火，此即所谓“人生始化曰魄”也；俄而炎炎焱焱，光焰上腾，此即所谓“既生魄，阳曰魂”也。人之死也，魂升而魄降，亦犹灯之熄也，烟上散而煤下坠，乃其常也。若此妇者，其魂已去，而其魄犹存，正如光已灭而火未绝耳。因记姚氏掘遗魄事，并记此事，且说魂魄之义如此。又魂魄者，生时之名，若死后则为鬼神矣。孔子曰：“众生必死，死必归土”，此之谓鬼，其气发扬于上为昭明，蒿凄怆，此百物之精也，神之著也。可知生为魂魄，死则魂升而为神，魄降而为鬼，盖魂升魄降，姑以生时之名名之。其实，魂者火之光也，死而升者止其烟也；魄者火之所以为火也，死而降者止其煤也。观姚氏所掘其形如炭，正可证明其理矣。

    Explanation

    826

    Place fire in the center, and shortly afterward, the wood turns into fire, which is what is meant by "the beginning of life is called魄 (pò)"—shortly thereafter, the flames rise and the light ascends, which is what is meant by "once魄 is formed, the yang aspect is called魂 (hún)." When a person dies, the soul ascends while the body descends, just as when a lamp goes out, the smoke disperses upward while the coal falls downward, which is the natural order. In the case of this woman, her soul has departed, but her body remains, just as the light has gone out while the fire is not completely extinguished. Therefore, I recorded the story of Yao's excavation of the remaining body, and also this incident, and thus, the meaning of soul and body is as follows. Moreover, soul and body are names used during life, but after death, they become ghosts and spirits. Confucius said, "All living things must die, and upon death, they return to the earth," which is what is meant by ghosts, whose qi rises above and becomes bright and clear, sad and mournful, which is the essence of all things, the manifestation of the spirit. It is known that life is soul and body, and death means the soul ascends to become a spirit, and the body descends to become a ghost, as the soul ascends and the body descends, temporarily using the names of life. In reality, the soul is the light of the fire, and what rises after death is the smoke; the body is the essence of the fire, and what falls after death is the coal. Observing the shape of what Yao excavated, it resembled charcoal, which can indeed prove this principle.

    827

    唐西镇人伊介春，有女曰翠姑，自幼许嫁木氏子。木所居曰锁条桥，相距非远。后木氏家日落，介春私与其妻议，欲绝其婚。翠姑微闻之，郁郁不乐。咸丰之季，贼陷杭州，乡间皆大乱，介春率妻女奔沪渎，遂别为女相攸，有成议矣。翠姑知事迫，缢而死。此事余闻之二儿妇，适草笔记第十五卷竟，因记之卷末。虽其事平平无奇，似不合志怪之例，然稗官小说，未始不以表章节义为事。先大夫《秦淮烈女篇》云：“我无风化权，仅操笔一枝。”余笔记中载此等事，犹此意也夫。

    Explanation

    827

    A Man from Tang West Town named Yi Jiechun had a daughter named Cuigu, who was betrothed since childhood to a son of the Mu family. The Mu family lived in a place called Suotiao Bridge, which was not far away. Later, the Mu family's fortunes declined, and Jiechun privately discussed with his wife the idea of breaking off the marriage. Cuigu, hearing of this, became deeply unhappy. During the late years of the Xianfeng era, bandits occupied Hangzhou, causing great turmoil in the countryside. Jiechun led his wife and daughter to flee to Shanghai, where he arranged for his daughter to marry someone else, and the agreement was made. Knowing the matter was urgent, Cuigu hanged herself. I heard this from my second son's wife, just as I was finishing the fifteenth volume of my rough notes, and recorded it at the end of the volume. Although the story is rather ordinary and unremarkable, it seems not to fit the examples of strange tales. However, minor officials' novels do not necessarily avoid showing loyalty and righteousness. My late father in his "Loyal Women of Qinhuai" wrote: "I have no power to influence morals, only a pen." I recorded this in my notes, following the same intention.

    828

    ●卷十六武林徐氏，为浙西望族。其故第在姚园寺巷，乱后虽毁于兵火，而门楣及听事无恙。圣祖御书“凛存堂”额及“清德镇俗”额，高宗御书“清慎可风”额，至今犹存。余门下士花农庶常琪，乃文穆公来孙也，稍加葺治而居之。余每过其家，辄兴故家乔木之思。偶阅定远方莲舫太守《蔗余偶笔》，载其家文敬公轶事云：文敬之封翁居钱唐江边，每子午潮退，将沿岸数十里水族，亲扫入江，自少至壮无间。一夜潮极大，巡江武官见火光一团，涌入徐氏，叩门以告，适文敬公生，因以潮名。及观平江李次青廉访《国朝先正事略》，则云：钱唐徐文敬公，先世业渔。公生日，江潮大上，一舟为风漂没。封翁急拯之得免。僦舟者为闽省孝廉，入都应试者也。翁延至家，致鸡黍之敬。

    Explanation

    828

    ●Volume Sixteen: The Xu Family of Wulin, a distinguished family in western Zhejiang, once had their ancestral home located in Yao Yuan Si Xiang. After the chaos, although the mansion was destroyed by war, the door lintel and the main hall remained intact. Inscriptions on plaques written by the Kangxi Emperor, such as “Linsuntang” and “Qingde Zhen Su,” and those by the Qianlong Emperor, like “Qingshen Ke Feng,” still exist today. My student Huā Nóng (花农), a scholar-official, is a descendant of Wen Mu Gong (文穆公). He slightly repaired and lived in the mansion. Whenever I visited his home, I always felt a sense of nostalgia for the old family and its tall trees. I happened to read in the Zheyu Oubi (蔗余偶笔) by the governor of Dingzhou, Dìng Fāng (定方), known as Lianfang (莲舫), which records some anecdotes about Wen Jing Gong (文敬公) of the family. It states that Wen Jing’s father-in-law lived by the Qiantang River, and every time the high tide receded, he would personally sweep the aquatic creatures along the coast for dozens of miles and sweep them into the river, from youth to adulthood without interruption. One night, the tide was extremely high, and a military officer patrolling the river saw a ball of fire rush into the Xu family’s residence. He knocked on the door to report, just as Wen Jing Gong was born. Thus, he named him after the tide. When I read the Guochao Xianzheng Shilüe (国朝先正事略) by Li Ciqing (李次青), a censor from Pingjiang, it states that Wen Jing Gong of the Xu family from Qiantang had ancestors who were fishermen. On the day he was born, the river tide rose greatly, and a boat was swept away by the wind. His father-in-law hurriedly rescued him and saved him. The boat owner was a scholar from Fujian who came to the capital to take the exams. The father-in-law invited him home and showed respect by serving him chicken and millet.

    829

    次日洗儿，请孝廉命名，以江潮之异，名之曰潮。其后孝廉累不第，而文敬早达。分校会试时，孝廉竟出其门云。此二说未知谁得其实。花农距文敬已六世，恐亦未必能详矣。花农之生也，其先德若洲先生梦一道士化为鹤而生，故小字鹤根，意花农亦生有自来者与！徐花农云：吾家旧祀花园土地。相传文敬公晚岁家居时，招邻叟饮，一叟年最高，延之首坐，叟辞。公曰：“君乃吾乡老土地，何辞焉？”叟笑而就席。老土地者，杭人谚语犹言前辈耳。是夜，公梦叟来，云：“公命我为土地，愿即公花园中乞一席地，公其许我乎？”公愕然而寤，犁旦使人侦之，则叟死矣。问何病，其家曰：“叟固无病，但自言欲往徐氏，如是者三，遂卒。”公叹息久之，祀之于后圃。游戏一言，遂为鬼神所凭藉，卿相造命，信矣！

    829

    The following day, after the child's head was washed, a scholar of high repute was invited to name the child. The child was given the name "Tide" due to the unusual behavior of the tides. Later, the scholar repeatedly failed the imperial examinations, while Wenjing achieved early success. When Wenjing served as an examiner in the provincial-level imperial examination, the scholar ended up as his student. It is unclear which of these two stories is true. By the time of Huafarmer, six generations had passed since Wenjing, and it is unlikely that Huafarmer could provide detailed information. Huafarmer was born when his grandfather, Mr. Zhou, dreamed of a Taoist priest transforming into a crane and giving birth to him, hence his nickname "Hegrassroots." It seems that Huafarmer was also born with a purpose! Xu Huafarmer said: Our family has traditionally venerated a garden land deity. It is said that when Wenjing was in his later years and living at home, he invited a nearby old man to drink. The old man was the oldest in the village, and Wenjing invited him to the main seat, but the old man declined. Wenjing said, "You are my village's old land deity, why refuse?" The old man laughed and took his seat. The old land deity is a proverb in Hangzhou, meaning "forefather." That night, Wenjing dreamed that the old man came to him and said, "You have appointed me as the land deity, I wish to request a small plot of land in your garden. Will you grant my request?" Wenjing woke up in surprise and, the next morning, sent someone to investigate, and found that the old man had died. When asked about the cause of death, the old man's family said, "The old man was not ill, but he said he wanted to go to the Xu family, and he repeated this three times before he died." Wenjing sighed for a long time and worshiped the old man in the back garden. A casual remark, and it became the basis for a ghost or deity. High officials have the power to determine fate, indeed!

    830

    花农又言：文敬公未达时，为赘婿于同里殳氏。殳媪以公贫，弗善也。殳夫人卒，公遂归江干故居。一日，殳翁遇诸涂，曰：“尔以吾女亡，遂不过我乎？”强邀俱归，于殳媪前亟称公贤，媪微哂。翁曰：“尔不兼于此子乎？吾行且妻以次女。”媪怒甚，曰：“尔好为之，吾不与闻。”翁曰：“敬诺。”遂以次女为公继室，媪无如何也。公不复居殳氏，与夫人俱归。他日夫人归宁，偶语诸嫂曰：“今日肩舆及门，触于枨，几覆。”嫂曰：“吾家荜门，安容高车？”夫人曰：“庸讵知吾不高车乎？”诸嫂皆干笑，曰：“尔乘高车来，当毁垣以迎之。”夫人遂辞去。及文敬公贵，夫人始再至母家，诸嫂置酒相款洽。夫人从容语诸嫂曰：“垣未毁乎？”余谓江氏槟榔之诮，韦家鹳雀之称，士方贫贱，见轻于妻族，自古有之。史家载此等事者，欲使人知白屋之中大有青云之士，勿以高门鼎族而蔑视寒微耳。余记殳夫人之言，亦此意也。

    Explanation

    830

    The Flower Farmer further recounted: When Wen Jing Gong was not yet established in his career, he was a son-in-law to the Shu family in the same village. The Shu matriarch, disliking him for his poverty, treated him poorly. After the death of the Shu wife, Wen Jing Gong returned to his former home by the riverbank. One day, the Shu patriarch met him on the road and said, "Did you think that because my daughter has passed away, you would no longer visit me?" He forcibly invited him to return, praising Wen Jing Gong's virtues in front of the matriarch, who gave a slight smile. The patriarch then said, "Do you not hate this son-in-law? I will marry him to my second daughter." The matriarch was greatly angered and said, "Do as you please, I will not interfere." The patriarch replied, "I agree." Thus, he married his second daughter to Wen Jing Gong as his second wife, and the matriarch had no choice. Wen Jing Gong no longer lived with the Shu family and returned home with his wife. One day, the wife visited her mother's house and spoke to her sisters-in-law, saying, "Today, my sedan chair hit the threshold and almost tipped over." Her sisters-in-law said, "Our humble door cannot accommodate a high carriage." The wife responded, "How do you know I wasn't riding in a high carriage?" Her sisters-in-law all laughed and said, "If you were really riding in a high carriage, we would tear down the wall to welcome you." The wife then left. When Wen Jing Gong became powerful, the wife returned to her mother's house, and the sisters-in-law prepared a feast to welcome her. The wife spoke to her sisters-in-law in a calm manner, asking, "Has the wall been torn down?" I have heard of the Jiang family's reprimand and the Wei family's derogatory nickname. Scholars, when poor and lowly, are often looked down upon by their in-laws, and this has been the case since ancient times. Historians record such stories to teach people that even among poor families, there can be people of high status, and they should not look down on the less fortunate. My recollection of the Shu wife's words also carries this meaning.

    831

    杭州姚园寺巷有旌德观，祀疫神曰温元帅。按元人吴自牧《梦粱录》已载有此神，云是东岳所部，疑必起于宋世矣。每岁五月，居民舁神巡行市廛，盖亦逐疫之意，而过徐氏之门必疾趋而过。相传徐文敬公官冢宰，是为天官，主天下神只，故神过其门必致敬焉。国家定制，凡坛庙祭祀及僧道等事，皆掌于礼部祠祭司，实与吏部无涉。巫觋之见，良可一噱，然亦可见故家之重矣。

    　　徐少薇，名章，花农之再从兄也。嘉庆戊寅，年十四，应乡试，中副榜。其明年举于乡，应礼部试，不第，留京师。未几卒。卒之前数月，梦至一所，修篁夹路，中有棋声，不觉朗吟曰：“飞来碎玉度棋声，修竹婵娟画不成。”下句未就，而竹中有人续之曰：“惆怅碧溪相别后，烟霞深处五峰青。”寻声而往，则一樵者，方倚树而吟。见徐至，惊曰：“华林子来何速乎？钱某犹未至也。子既来此，吾导子至一处观之。”遂与俱往。至则高门岌で，若官廨然。

    Explanation

    831

    In Hangzhou, there is a street called Yao Yuan Si Lane, where there is a temple named Jingde Guan, which worships the deity of epidemics known as Wen Yuan Shuai. According to Wu Zimu's Menglianglu from the Yuan dynasty, this deity has already been recorded, and it is said to be part of the Eastern Mountain's division, likely originating during the Song dynasty. Every year in May, the residents carry the deity through the marketplaces, presumably to drive away epidemics. When passing by the Xu family's gate, the deity must hurry past. It is said that Xu Wenjing, who served as the Grand Master of Ceremonies, a position overseeing all divine beings, was highly respected. Therefore, the deity shows respect when passing his gate. By national decree, all temple and shrine rituals, as well as matters concerning monks and Taoists, are managed by the Ministry of Rites' Sacrificial Affairs Office, which is not related to the Ministry of Personnel. Such beliefs of shamans are truly laughable, but they also reveal the respect given to old families.

    Meng Shaowei, whose name is Zha, is the second cousin of Hua Nong. In the year Jiaqing Wuyin, at the age of fourteen, he took the provincial examination and was listed on the supplementary list. The following year, he passed the provincial examination and took the Ministry of Rites examination but did not pass, remaining in the capital. He passed away shortly after. A few months before his death, he had a dream in which he arrived at a place with tall bamboo groves lining the road, and there was the sound of chess being played. He unconsciously recited aloud, "Flying fragments of jade carry the sound of chess, tall bamboo graceful beyond painting." Before he could finish the next line, someone from within the bamboo continued, "Charming, the green stream after parting, the five peaks deep in mist and clouds." Following the sound, he went to see a woodcutter leaning against a tree and reciting. Seeing Meng arrive, the woodcutter was surprised and said, "How quickly you have come, Scholar Hua! Mr. Qian has not yet arrived. Since you are here, I will guide you to see a place." They went together. Upon arriving, they saw a tall gate, resembling an official building.

    832

    入门，有女子六七人执帚扫花，视徐而笑，不出一语。登堂见楹间县一联云：“天下今宵共明月，人间何处有仙山。”堂之左右，列书橱八九，缥囊缃帙，充刃其中。有角巾野服者数辈，挟册讽诵。樵者即取一卷，授徐视之，其生平所作诗文皆载其上，惊而寤，不解所谓。惟樵者所云钱某，则固知之，乃其同砚之友也，其时固无恙。未几而钱某之讣至，叹曰：“曩樵者言钱某未至，吾来何速，今钱某往矣，吾其继之乎？”因记所梦，┑书箧中。及卒，家人发箧得之，乃知石曼卿芙蓉之城，王平甫灵芝之宫，非小说家伪造也。

    Explanation

    832

    Entering the door, there were six or seven women holding brooms to sweep the flowers, looking at Xu and smiling, but not speaking a word. Upon entering the hall, Xu saw a couplet hanging between the pillars that read, "Under the same moon tonight, where in the world is there a fairy mountain?" On both sides of the hall, there were eight or nine bookshelves, filled with books of colorful bindings and covers. Several people wearing headbands and rustic clothing were holding books and reciting. The woodcutter immediately took a scroll, gave it to Xu to look at, and all of Xu's poems and essays written throughout his life were recorded on it. Xu was startled awake, not understanding what it meant. Only the woodcutter's mention of Mr. Qian, who he knew was a friend from his studies and was still alive at the time, stood out. Soon after, news of Mr. Qian's death arrived, and Xu sighed, saying, "The woodcutter said that Mr. Qian had not arrived, why did I come so quickly? Now that Mr. Qian has passed away, will I follow him?" Xu then recorded his dream in a box. Upon his death, his family found it in the box and realized that the city of lotus flowers of Shi Manqing and the palace of lingzhi of Wang Pingfu were not fabricated by novelists.

    833

    杭州武林门内，有长寿桥。桥之左别有一桥，无名，但以小桥呼之。其地有某甲者，素无赖，恒集徒众，携锹锄于无主荒地，扌窟石卖钱。阶砌柱础，爬罗殆尽，乃窃小桥上石栏石循，载以小舟，至唐西镇卖之。甲有子七岁，是夕忽得疾，次日弥甚，药之不瘳，自言：“有神人击我。”甲使卜者卜之，曰：“桥神为祟。”甲大惊，具牲醴祷焉，无效，不数日，其子竟死。嗟乎！乱后桥梁半就倾圮，比来稍稍修葺。乃余往来江、浙间，见有甫修而即圮者，推原其故，盖有负之而趋者矣。安得处处有桥神，为之呵护哉！

    　　苏州山塘有某甲者，以卖鱼为活，人颇长厚，家亦粗足自给。惟嗜酒，每醉则言语不伦，家人习之，亦不怪也。一夕酩酊而归，坐未定，复趋出，家人问焉往。曰：“吾且赴水死。明日买棺至兴隆桥边敛我可也。”咸以为醉语耳。良久不返，踪迹之，则宛在水中央矣。钩捞出之，已不可救。竟如其言，敛于兴隆桥边云。

    Explanation

    833

    Inside the Wulin Gate of Hangzhou, there is a Changshou Bridge. To the left of the bridge, there is another unnamed bridge, often called the Small Bridge. There was a man named Jia who was always morally corrupt. He gathered followers and carried shovels and picks to abandoned lands, digging stones to sell. He dug up stone railings and balustrades from the small bridge, loaded them onto a small boat, and sold them in the market of Tang West Town. One night, Jia's seven-year-old son suddenly fell ill. The next day, the illness worsened, and despite medical treatment, he did not recover. He said, "There is a deity striking me." Jia consulted a diviner, who said, "The bridge deity is causing harm." Jia was greatly alarmed and prepared sacrifices and wine to pray, but it was ineffective. Within a few days, his son died. Alas! After the chaos, half of the bridges have collapsed. Recently, they have been repaired slightly. However, I have observed that recently repaired bridges immediately collapse. Tracing the cause, it seems that someone is carrying away the materials. How can there be bridge deities everywhere to protect them?

    In Suzhou's Shantang Street, there was a man named Jia who made a living selling fish. He was a decent person, and his family was somewhat self-sufficient. However, he loved alcohol, and when drunk, he would speak incoherently. His family was accustomed to this and did not find it strange. One evening, he returned drunk and sat down but then rushed out again. When asked where he was going, he said, "I am going to drown myself. You can buy a coffin for me tomorrow and bury me near Xinglong Bridge." Everyone thought it was just drunken talk. After a long time, he did not return. When they searched for him, he was indeed in the middle of the water. They pulled him out, but he could not be saved. As he had predicted, he was buried near Xinglong Bridge.

    834

    李翁，广东从化县人，迁居省城。年五十余，无子，买某姓女子为妾。女见李老，意不乐，呼使侍寝，掩面而泣。问之不答，固问，始微露其意。李翁曰：“老夫女妻，固非偶也。若既不愿，吾何敢强！”召其父至，以女归之。李翁可谓长者矣。此女失此良人，转似可惜也。

    　　许仁山阁学之女，嫁海宁孙氏，有女名小翠，生于京师。襁褓中，父母以奔丧归，因小翠稚小，寄养于余次女处，盖余次女即仁山之从弟妇也。后余女挈之南旋，以还其父母。小翠秀外惠中，眉目如画，然自幼多病，年七岁殇焉。其临死时趺坐榻上，闭目合掌，家人不知其已死，候之则气已绝矣。其殆有宿根者与？昙华一见，亦可异也。

    Explanation

    834

    Li翁, a man from Conghua County, Guangdong Province, moved to the provincial capital. He was over fifty years old and had no son, so he bought a girl of a certain surname as a concubine. When the girl saw the old man, she was not happy. He called her to serve in bed, but she covered her face and wept. When he asked her, she did not answer, and when he persisted, she finally hinted at her feelings. Li翁 said, "An old man with a young wife, it is not a proper match. If you do not wish to, how dare I force you!" He called her father and returned the girl to him. Li翁 can be called a person of virtue. This girl lost this good man, and it seems regrettable.

    Xu Renshan, a scholar-official, had a daughter who married into the Sun family of Hai Ning. She had a daughter named Xiao Cui, who was born in the capital. When Xiao Cui was in swaddling clothes, her parents returned home for a funeral, and because Xiao Cui was too young, they entrusted her to be raised by my second daughter, who is Xu Renshan's cousin's wife. Later, my daughter took her southward and returned her to her parents. Xiao Cui was beautiful on the outside and wise within, with eyebrows and eyes like a painting. However, she was sickly from a young age and died at the age of seven. When she was on her deathbed, she sat cross-legged on the bed, closed her eyes, and clasped her hands. Her family did not know she had already died; when they waited, they found that her breath had already left. She must have been a person with a predestined fate. A fleeting encounter, indeed, is also remarkable.

    835

    小儿出痘，谓之出花，不知所自始，然南北并有此语矣。一家有娣姒二人，其子皆出痘，娣之子颇危险，姒之子已上浆矣。其夜，姒梦见一卖花媪，娣姒各就之买花，已而娣以所买之花易姒之花，遂寤。次日视儿，则娣之子颇有起色，而姒之子痘皆下陷，未几竟死，娣子反无恙。乃悟梦中易花，是其兆矣。扬州甘泉县陈生端甫，居仙女庙镇之南，母与妻皆熊氏，盖姑侄也。妻病，母移榻于其房。一夕睡醒，见火光荧荧，自下而上，惊而谛视，乃一大鼠，衔灯草缘壁而行。急呼其子起，则火已及承尘矣，须臾遂炽。陈负母出，又使人舁病妇出，幸皆无恙，屋竟毁焉。夫鼠衔灯草常有之事，而陈氏竟以致焚如之祸。欧阳公云“祸患常起于细微”，信矣。

    Explanation

    835

    Children contracting smallpox are referred to as "出花," a term whose origin is unknown, yet it is used both in the north and south. In one family, there were two sisters-in-law, both of whose children had contracted smallpox. One sister-in-law's child was in grave danger, while the other's child had progressed to the "上浆" stage. That night, the sister-in-law whose child was in danger dreamed of an elderly woman selling flowers. Both sisters-in-law went to her to buy flowers, and later, the sister-in-law whose child was in danger exchanged her flowers for the other's, and then woke up. The next day, when she looked at her child, it had improved somewhat, while the other sister-in-law's child's pustules had all sunk, and soon after, the child died, whereas the first sister-in-law's child remained unharmed. She realized that the exchange of flowers in her dream was a sign of the changing conditions of their children. In Yangzhou's Ganyuan County, there was a man named Chen Sheng Duanfu, who lived south of the Fairy Temple Town. His mother and wife were both surnamed Xiong, making them aunt and niece. When his wife fell ill, his mother moved her bed into the room. One night, after waking from sleep, she saw a faint, flickering light moving from the bottom up. She was startled and looked closely, and saw a large rat carrying a piece of burning material running along the wall. She quickly called her son to wake up, but by then the fire had already reached the ceiling. In a short while, the fire grew intense. Chen carried his mother out, and then had someone carry out his sick wife. Fortunately, they were all unharmed, but the house was completely destroyed. The incident of a rat carrying burning material is a common occurrence, but for Chen, it led to a devastating fire. As Ouyang Xiu said, "Disasters often originate from small, seemingly insignificant events," and indeed they do.

    836

    苏州有王媪者，少寡而贫，无子女，惟为人缝纫以糊其口，垂三十年矣。其邻某翁，颇饶于资，有一子甫十岁，自幼多病。病作，辄有鬼凭其身，招巫者施敕勒之术，竟不能遣，惟王媪偶过其家，则鬼即避之。如是者数年，其子心体烦，日就羸瘠，翁甚忧之。梦神语之曰：“以此子为王节妇后，病即愈矣。”翁因商于王媪，愿以此子与媪共之，承两家香火。媪大喜。子自母王媪，病果不复作，娶妻生子，奉王媪终其身。然则此子之病，殆鬼神哀怜节妇，不忍其老而无依与？

    　　先大夫《印雪轩随笔》载：临平乡间，有兄弟四人而同岁者，盖二子于正月孪生，二子于十二月孪生也。乃又有兄弟二人，同年同月，而弟之生日顾先于兄者。盖此二人为异母兄弟，皆生于三月，兄于三月中旬生，弟于闰三月上旬生，相距二十日。至无闰之年，则弟先于兄者十日矣。其事本无奇，姑记之为谈助。惜谈者不言其姓氏。

    Explanation

    836

    In Suzhou, there was a woman named Wang Ao who became a widow at a young age and lived in poverty, with no children. She made a living by sewing and mending for nearly thirty years. Her neighbor, a wealthy old man, had a son who was just ten years old and had been ill since childhood. Whenever the boy fell sick, a ghost would possess his body, and despite the old man calling upon a witch to perform exorcism rituals, the ghost could not be driven away. However, whenever Wang Ao happened to pass by the house, the ghost would immediately flee. This went on for several years, and the boy's heart and body became increasingly troubled, gradually becoming weak and emaciated. The old man was very concerned.

    One night, he dreamed that a god told him, "If you let this son be the heir of Wang's chaste widow, his illness will heal." The old man then discussed this with Wang Ao and wished to give his son to her to share, to carry on the ancestral rites of both families. Wang Ao was overjoyed. After the boy was adopted by Wang Ao, his illness indeed did not recur. He married and had children, serving Wang Ao until the end of her life. Perhaps the spirits pitied the chaste widow and could not bear to see her old and without support.

    In addition, my late father's Yinxue Xuan Suibi (Notes from the Snowy Pavilion) records: In the countryside of Linping, there were four brothers born in the same year. Two of them were twins born in January, and the other two were twins born in December. There were also two brothers born in the same year and month, but the younger brother's birthday preceded the older brother's. These two brothers were half-brothers, born in March. The older brother was born in mid-March, while the younger brother was born in early April of a leap year, with a twenty-day gap between them. In non-leap years, the younger brother's birthday would be ten days before the older brother's. This story is not particularly remarkable, but it is recorded here for amusement. Unfortunately, the names of the individuals are not mentioned.

    837

    湖北有张氏女，许嫁胡氏子，婚有日矣。而胡氏子病甚，乃使媒妁请绝于张，张女不可，曰：“女子既以身许人，死生以之。”遂适胡氏。而胡氏子病有间，又逾数年，生二子，始死，张女抚其二子以终。此与第十五卷所载樊氏女事相类，然樊女茕茕孤苦，张女则非止有节操，且有才干。其父仕于闽为县令，资财巨万，惟挟二妾以从，弃厥妻于家不顾，无以为生。族中人咸不平，纵臾其妻，使如闽。辞无资，则为醵资，又惧不礼于夫。女乃曰：“母无惧，我侍母以行，然须具来往之资。合则留，不合则返耳。”于是母女俱如闽，未至其所三十里，使人以告。

    Explanation

    837

    In Hubei, there was a daughter of the Zhang family who was betrothed to a son of the Hu family, with the wedding date set. However, the Hu son fell gravely ill, and his family sent a matchmaker to ask Zhang to call off the engagement. Zhang's daughter refused, saying, "Having already pledged my body to another, I accept life and death with it." She then married into the Hu family. After the Hu son's illness improved somewhat, several years passed, and she gave birth to two sons. He eventually passed away, and Zhang cared for her two sons until the end of her life. This story is similar to the account of Fan's daughter in the fifteenth volume, but while Fan's daughter was lonely and suffering, Zhang not only had integrity but also talent. Her father served as a county magistrate in Fujian, possessed vast wealth, and took only two concubines with him, abandoning his wife at home without concern, leaving her destitute. Members of the community were all outraged and encouraged her to go to Fujian. Her mother declined due to lack of funds, but they raised money for her. She also feared she would not be treated respectfully by her husband. Zhang then said, "Mother, do not fear. I will accompany you. However, we need to prepare the necessary funds. If we are welcomed, we will stay; if not, we will return." Thus, mother and daughter traveled to Fujian, stopping thirty miles short of their destination to send a message.

    838

    父闻妻至，将不纳，闻女与俱至，始大具车徒迎之。既至，果相安居。数月，女辞欲返，母留之女曰：“家有尊章，岂能居此长奉母乎？”母泣曰：“汝在，故我无苦。汝去，彼将鱼肉我矣，吾从汝归耳。”遂与俱返。后其父以赃败，诏下原籍簿录其家财。县官奉檄至门，母皇恐伏灶下，余人悉走匿。女盛服出见县官，言父平时弃母不顾状，且曰：“父盛时，母不同其乐。今父败，母岂得同其苦？请公入视，如有铢金寸锦之储，甘受隐匿之罪。”声情慷慷，县官为之动容。入视之，破屋数椽，疏帐缥被而已。叹曰：“诚如汝言，诚如汝言。”遂去。山西一富翁，年四十无子，每至亲串家见有小儿，辄喜而抱弄之。

    Explanation

    838

    The father heard that his wife was coming, and he was initially reluctant to accept her. Upon learning that his daughter was coming with her, he finally prepared a grand procession to welcome them. After they arrived, they indeed lived together in harmony. After several months, the daughter requested to return home, and the mother tried to persuade her to stay. The daughter said, "There is a respected elder in our family. How can I stay here and continue to serve you?" The mother wept and said, "As long as you are here, I have no worries. But if you leave, he will treat me like fish and meat. I will follow you back." And so, they returned together. Later, the father was disgraced for corruption, and an imperial decree was issued to his hometown to inventory his property. The local official arrived at the door, and the mother, in fear, hid under the stove, while the others all fled. The daughter, dressed in her best clothes, came out to meet the official and described how her father had neglected her mother in the past. She also said, "When my father was prosperous, my mother did not share in his happiness. Now that he is disgraced, can my mother share in his suffering? Please enter and see for yourself. If there is any hidden wealth, I am willing to accept the punishment for concealing it." Her words were resolute and moving, and the official was deeply moved. Upon entering, he saw only a few dilapidated rooms, a sparse curtain, and a simple bed. He sighed, "Truly as you say, truly as you say." And he left. In Shanxi, there was a wealthy man who was forty years old but had no son. Every time he visited relatives and saw a child, he would always be happy and play with the child.

    839

    尝于役于中州，骑马过中牟县城外，见树阴之下一小儿，才四五岁，馆纳可爱。下马就视，儿初不啼，抱之上马，幸无见者。谛视此儿，眉目姣好，肥白如瓠，爱不忍释，竟拥之怀中，束马而去。比至家，遂以为子。而翁自得此子后，连举二子，三子皆长，并为娶妻。然翁之家人每窃窃私语，谓翁既有子矣，焉用此他人子为？翁微闻之，自度己死后，必不相容。于是召此子语之曰：“吾自中牟携汝来，今幸成立。然久居此，终少味也。叶落粪本，汝其归乎！”乃为具万金之装，使携妇俱去，戒之曰：“汝至中牟，但至某所访之，必有骨肉相逢。此吾遇汝处也。”子不得已，涕泣辞去。

    Explanation

    839

    Once while serving in the Central Plains, I was riding a horse past the outskirts of Zhongmou County. Under the shade of a tree, I saw a little boy, just four or five years old, who was cute and lovable. I dismounted to look at him. The child did not cry at first, so I picked him up and put him on the horse. Fortunately, no one saw. I closely examined this child, noting his beautiful eyebrows and eyes, and his fat and white face like a gourd. I loved him and could not bear to let go, so I finally held him in my arms and tied up the horse and left. By the time I arrived home, I adopted him as a son. After adopting this son, the old man had two more sons, and all three sons grew up, and they were all married. However, the old man's family often whispered among themselves, saying that since the old man already had sons, why did he need this other son? The old man heard it faintly and realized that after his death, they would not accept him. Then he called this son and said to him, "I brought you from Zhongmou, and now you are fortunate to have grown up. But staying here for a long time, it has become less interesting. Leaves fall back to the root, you should return home!" He then prepared a thousand-gold outfit, sent him away with his wife, and warned him, "When you reach Zhongmou, just go to a certain place to visit, and there will be a reunion of flesh and blood. This is where I met you." The son had no choice but to cry and leave.

    840

    既至中牟，止其妻于逆旅，而自至城外访求，逢人必问，冀有所遇。久之，有人语之曰：“吾村某氏老嫠妇，曾失一子，得毋是乎？”其子喜甚，亟使导之往，至则一老妇出曰：“吾夫亡后，遗腹生一子，至某岁失之，今且二十年，虽见之，固不识矣。然有可辨认者，此子面有豆花（唐陈黯有《咏豆花》诗，即痘瘢也，今谓之面麻），吾曾识其数，今虽久远，犹未忘也。”乃言其数。众人视此子之面，果有豆花，数之与妇言合，皆喜曰：“此真是矣。”其子泣拜，历言前事，即从逆旅中迎其妻至。老妇家本贫，自失子后，又悲思成疾，一旦有子，又有子妇，且有多金，乃大喜慰。“塞翁失马，未始非福”，此之谓矣。或亦造物哀怜节妇而曲成之乎？

    Explanation

    840

    After arriving in Zhongmou, he left his wife at an inn and went himself to the outskirts of the city to search, asking everyone he met, hoping to find something. After a long time, someone told him, "In my village, there is an old widow from the family of a certain surname who once lost a son. Could this be him?" The man was very happy and quickly asked the villager to guide him there. Upon arrival, an old woman came out and said, "After my husband died, I gave birth to a son by posthumous conception. He went missing at a certain age, and it has been nearly twenty years since then. Even if I see him now, I would not recognize him. However, there are things that can be recognized. This child has marks on his face like those described in the poem '咏豆花' by Táng Chén Àn, which are smallpox scars, and I remember how many there were. They still haven't faded." She then described the number of scars. The crowd looked at the child's face and indeed saw the marks, and the number matched what the old woman had said. Everyone was delighted and said, "This is truly him." The man wept and bowed, recounting all the previous events. He immediately brought his wife from the inn to join them. The old woman's family had been poor since she lost her son, and her grief had made her ill. Now, with the return of her son and his wife, and the sudden wealth, she was overjoyed. "A loss may turn out to be a gain," as the saying goes. Perhaps the Creator pities the chaste widow and helps her in a roundabout way.

    841

    光绪辛巳岁，花农与倪儒粟茹及孤山寺僧本慧，同至俞楼，于楼后山上西爽亭小坐。既下山，僧自后招花农曰：“来看，来看！”花农视之，见松树上一蛙，浅绿色，竟体滑泽如碧玉琢成，无磊之状，与常蛙异。儒粟曰：“此非金华将军邪？”僧点首曰：“无多言。”次日，花农至右台仙馆以告余。按杭城涌金门内之有金华将军庙，旧矣。据《咸淳临安志》，神姓曹，名杲，真定人，仕后唐为金华令。吴越王擢宋婺，钱氏入朝，委以国事。杲即城隅浚三池，曰涌金。既殁，民为立祠池上，此固有功烈于民，宜在祀典者也。其神乃化身为蛙，何邪？然鬼神之事有不可以常理测者。即如黄河之神，化身多为蛇，见于记载，登之奏牍。

    Explanation

    841

    In the year of Xin Si during the Guangxu era, the flower farmer, Ni Ruxu, and the monk Benhui from the Solitary Mountain Temple, accompanied by others, visited Yu Lou. They sat in the Xishuang Pavilion on the hill behind the building. After descending the mountain, the monk called out to the flower farmer from behind, saying, "Look, look!" The flower farmer turned to see a frog on a pine tree. It was a light green color, its body smooth and lustrous like jade carved into a precious stone, without any rough or uneven texture, unlike ordinary frogs. Ni Ruxu remarked, "Is this not the General of Jinhua?" The monk nodded and said, "Do not speak much." The next day, the flower farmer went to the Right Platform Pavilion to inform me of the incident. According to the Xianchun Lin'an Zhi (Imperial Capital Lin'an Gazetteer), the deity's name is Cao Gao, from Zhending, who served as the magistrate of Jinhua during the Later Tang dynasty. The King of Wu and Yue promoted him to serve in Cangzhou (Dust), and the Qian family entered the imperial court, entrusting him with state affairs. Cao Gao dredged three ponds in the city, naming them "Yongjin." After his death, the people built a shrine on the pond to honor him, as he had made significant contributions to the people. The deity transformed into a frog, which is unusual. However, matters concerning ghosts and deities cannot be measured by ordinary reasoning. For example, the Yellow River deity often transforms into a snake, as recorded in official documents.

    842

    同年生薛世香廉访，河南灵宝人也，尝语余云：“黄河神，有大王，有将军。每见形，则官吏必以盘承之，大设牲牢，广陈优戏。蛇在盘中端然不动。其蛇有大小，小者大王，大者将军。大王蛇不过尺许，头外尾内，其直如矢，虽至数日之久，小有转侧，而终不易其方。将军蛇，或数尺，或丈余，蟠曲如纠纟墨，首则昂然上出，而不见其尾。大王蛇必居盘中，将军则稍偏焉。数日后辄失所在，莫知何往。河工官吏及滨河之民，咸崇奉之，无敢亵视。有所谓栗大王者，乃国朝栗恭勤公，其神亦蛇也。”然则金华将军之为蛙，何异之有？推而上之，知古书所载，如伯鲧为黄熊，望帝为杜鹃，女娃为精卫，钦丕为大鹗，盖亦理之所有。一孔之儒，固不足以知之。

    842

    Contemporary official Xue Shixiang, an inspector from Lingbao, Henan, once told me: "The deities of the Yellow River include a Great King and a General. Whenever they appear, officials must present them with a tray, set up elaborate sacrifices, and stage performances. The snake lies calmly in the tray, unmoving. The snakes vary in size; the smaller ones are the Great Kings, and the larger ones are the Generals. The Great King snake is no more than a foot long, with its head outside and its tail inside, as straight as an arrow. Even after several days, it may turn slightly, but it never deviates from its direction. The General snake, however, can be several feet or even over a zhang long, coiled tightly like a tangled thread, with its head raised proudly, and its tail unseen. The Great King snake must remain in the center of the tray, while the General snake is slightly off to the side. After several days, they vanish without a trace, and no one knows where they go. Officials and people living near the Yellow River all revere them, and no one dares to look at them with disrespect. There is also a deity known as the Litchi Great King, who is said to be the spirit of Litchi Gongqin of our dynasty, and whose form is also a snake." Thus, what is there to be surprised about if the General of Jinhua transforms into a frog? By extension, we can understand that ancient texts record transformations such as Boyu becoming a yellow bear, King Du becoming a cuckoo, Nüwa becoming the magpie, and Qin Pi becoming a great eagle—all of which are within the realm of possibility. A scholar with a narrow perspective is certainly not sufficient to comprehend such matters.

    843

    同治十年冬，余自西湖精舍还吴下寓庐，迂道德清，省视先人丘陇。泊舟城中，自坐小舟出南门，至金鹅山，展先通奉君之墓，留奴子沈贵守舟。忽有一人司四五十岁，口操德清土音，至舟求见。沈诘其姓氏，其人告之，约略是刘字。沈粗解翰墨，问曰：“卯金刀乎？”曰：“非也，木字偏旁耳！”余归，沈以告，余亦漫置之。然以木旁字而音似刘，且为姓氏所有，惟楼及柳耳。吾邑素无此二姓，莫知何人也。

    Explanation

    843

    In the winter of the tenth year of the Tongzhi era, I returned from West Lake Academy to my temporary residence in Wu, taking a detour through Deqing to visit the graves of my ancestors. I moored my boat in the city and, sitting in a small boat, left the city through the southern gate, arriving at Jinge Mountain to pay respects at the tomb of the late official. I left my servant, Shen Gui, to guard the boat. Suddenly, a man in his forties or fifties appeared, speaking with a local accent from Deqing, and came to the boat to request an audience. Shen questioned his name, and the man replied that it was approximately "Liu." Shen, who had a basic understanding of writing, asked, "Is it the 'Liu' composed of the characters for 'mouse,' 'gold,' and 'knife'?" The man replied, "No, it has a 'wood' radical!" I returned home, and Shen reported this to me. I dismissed it casually. However, considering that the surname has a 'wood' radical and sounds like "Liu," and that it is one of the two surnames that exist, it must be either "Lou" or "Liu." Our town has never had these two surnames, so I do not know who this man is.

    844

    及归吴下，适同县江子平孝廉、蔡瑜卿秀才皆在，因以问之，两君曰：“此事绝异，岂柳侯乎？”柳侯者，吾邑总管神也。吾邑总管神三，曰戴，曰柳，曰叶，而柳实先于戴、叶。唐天宝中初设德清县，柳侯即来为县令。侯名察躬，乃柳子厚之祖，集中《先侍御史神道表》所称德清君者是也。有惠政，既殁，而邑人祠之。岁久祠废，及戴侯兴，而邑人即以柳祠故址为之祠，于是祀戴兼祀柳，后又附以叶，并详县志。余流寓四方，久不获躬拜祠下，而文章道义，又不足以感动幽明，两君所言盖谰语也。其事载《春在堂随笔》卷第五，今因金华将军事，又类记之。

    Explanation

    844

    Upon returning to Wu, I happened to be in the same county as Jiang Ziping, a scholar of high moral standing, and Cai Yuqing, a talented young scholar. I asked them about the matter, and the two gentlemen replied, "This is quite unusual. Could it be Lord Liu?" Lord Liu is one of our town's guardian deities. Our town has three patron gods: Lord Dai, Lord Liu, and Lord Ye, with Lord Liu being the first among them. During the Tianbao era of the Tang Dynasty, when Deqing County was first established, Lord Liu came to serve as the magistrate. His name was Cha Gong, and he was the grandfather of Liu Zihou. He is the "Degan Jun" mentioned in the Xian Shiyushi Shendaobiao (Inscription on the Spirit Road of the Former Magistrate) in Liu Zihou's collected works. He had benevolent policies, and after his death, the people of the town built a shrine for him. Over time, the shrine fell into disuse, and when Lord Dai became active again, the people of the town used the old site of the Liu shrine to build a new one. Thus, they began to worship both Lord Dai and Lord Liu together, and later added Lord Ye to the worship. This is recorded in the county records. I have been living away from home for a long time and have not been able to personally visit the shrine. Moreover, my literary and moral principles are not enough to move the spirits of the living and the dead. What the two gentlemen said is probably just idle talk. This matter is recorded in the fifth volume of Chunzaitang Suibi (Spring in the Hall Miscellaneous Notes). Now, because of military affairs in Jinhua, I am recording it again.

    845

    光绪五年十月乙丑，余葬内子姚夫人于钱唐右台山之原，余即自营生圹于其左。既葬三日，有蝶见于坟茔，黑质而黄章。越三日，又见，亦如之。时虽十月小春，然已交大雪，晨起严霜满地，不应有此，亦可异也。及归吴下，以语老友吴平斋，平斋决为太常仙蝶，然未敢遽信也。其事见《春在堂诗编》卷第九，今亦类记于此。余作《金华将军》诗，中四句云：“我泛余不溪中舟，疑有神人来同游。我筑右台山下圹，疑有仙蝶来送葬。”即谓此二事也。

    　　陆锦斤，长洲学生，以高等食饩，有声庠序间。光绪辛巳岁，感疾颇剧，妻子为祷于神，陆曰：“可不必矣，吾顷见冥吏持牒来，有吾姓名。锦下一字本非斤字，后涂去之，而注斤字于旁，则是我也，其能免乎？未几果卒。陆氏兄弟行以锦字为名者，尚有数人，不知其涂去者为何字也。陆年仅二十四，是岁甫新昏，同学之士颇惜之。

    Explanation

    845

    In the fifth year of the Guangxu era, on the day YiChou of the tenth month, I buried my wife, Lady Yao, in the original of Right Platform Mountain in Qiantang, and I myself built my own grave to the left of it. Three days after the burial, a butterfly appeared at the tomb, with a black body and yellow patterns. Three days later, it appeared again, just as before. Although it was the small spring of October, it was already the time of great snow, and in the morning, the ground was covered with frost, which should not have been the case, and it was also strange. When I returned to Wu, I told my old friend Wu Pingzai about it, and Pingzai decided it was a divine butterfly from Taichang, but he did not dare to believe it immediately. This matter is recorded in the ninth volume of Spring in the Hall Poetry Collection, and it is also recorded here. I wrote a poem called General of Jinhua, in which four lines say: "I floated in a boat on the Yubei River, suspecting that a divine being came to accompany me. I built a grave at the foot of Right Platform Mountain, suspecting that a divine butterfly came to accompany the burial." These two events are referred to here.

    Lu Jinjin, a student from Changzhou, was a high-ranking student, and he was well-known in the school. In the year Xisi of the Guangxu era, he fell seriously ill, and his wife and children prayed to the gods. Lu said: "There is no need to do so, for I recently saw a messenger from the underworld holding a document with my name on it. The character 'jin' below 'Lu' was not originally 'jinjin,' but it was later crossed out and 'jinjin' was written next to it, which is me, how can I escape? Soon after, he died. There were still several people in the Lu family who were named with the character 'jin,' but it is unknown what the original character was. Lu was only twenty-four years old, and that year he had just recently married, and his fellow students were very sorry for him.

    846

    顾某，苏人，官浙中，与于海运之役。自浙至沪，偶与数友饮于茶寮，忽得狂易之疾，其僚送之还苏，而疾益甚。百计求死，刀绳之属家人悉为藏┑，甚至不敢以陶器盛饮食。饭碗茶否一入其手，辄啮而碎之，或不及备，则吞咽入腹者，亦竟有焉。医巫杂进，久而不瘳。会有请箕仙者，乃就之问故。箕笔书曰：“病由鬼祟，鬼亦顾姓，浙江平湖市井中人。顾某前生为其主会计，干没其千金，致彼郁郁而死，故今来索偿。然顾某止于侵蚀其资，究未戕害其命，此鬼自以病终，理难议抵。果能广作功德，当可消释冤愆。”顾氏从之，用浮屠氏法，诵经施食，七日夜而顾病果已，仍至沪上，终海运之事。然未及三年，即以他疾卒。此鬼殆乘其衰气而来，《左氏》所谓“其气焰以取之”者也。

    Explanation

    846

    Gu, a man from Suzhou, served as an official in Zhejiang and participated in the maritime transport mission. While traveling from Zhejiang to Shanghai, he occasionally drank tea with several friends at a teahouse. Suddenly, he fell ill with a severe and sudden disease. His colleagues sent him back to Suzhou, but his condition worsened. He tried every means to commit suicide, and his family hid all the weapons and ropes. They were so afraid that they even dared not use pottery to serve food and drink. Whenever he got hold of bowls or cups, he would bite and crush them. If they were not prepared in time, he would swallow them whole. Doctors and shamans tried various treatments, but his illness did not improve. One day, someone invited a bamboo basket spirit (箕仙) to inquire about the cause. The spirit wrote with the basket: "The illness is caused by a ghost. The ghost is also named Gu, a commoner from Pinghu, Zhejiang. In his previous life, Gu was the accountant for the ghost's master and embezzled a thousand gold pieces, causing the ghost to die in sorrow. Therefore, the ghost has come to seek retribution. However, Gu only embezzled the assets and did not cause the ghost's death. The ghost died of illness, so it is difficult to argue for compensation. If Gu can perform many good deeds, he can resolve the grudge." Following the advice, Gu's family performed Buddhist rituals, reciting scriptures and offering food for seven days and nights. Gu's illness was cured, and he returned to Shanghai to complete the maritime transport mission. However, he died of another illness within three years. The ghost likely took advantage of his weakened state, as the Zuo Zhuan says, "taking advantage of his weakened state."

    847

    某甲，扬州人，寓于苏，家中惟一母一妻。偶外出，其妻为之阖户，甫还入内，骤得狂疾，载号载呶，不可向迩。母急，使人趣甲返。甲察其声，则男子也，问曰：“汝何人耶？”妻大声曰：“不识我乎？我某岁与汝别于某所，汝忘之乎？”甲乃恍然知为某乙。先是，甲在扬州时，曾借乙钱十千，及粤寇之乱，甲与母妻逃至某处，适乙亦至焉。

    Explanation

    847

    Mr. A, a native of Yangzhou, resided in Suzhou. At home, he had only his mother and wife. Occasionally, he went out, and his wife closed the door for him. Just returned inside, he suddenly fell ill with madness, shouting and grumbling, making it unbearable. The mother was alarmed and sent someone to urge Mr. A to return. Mr. A noticed the voice and realized it was a man. He asked, "Who are you?" His wife shouted, "Don't you recognize me? I parted from you at a certain place in a certain year, have you forgotten it?" Mr. A suddenly realized it was Mr. B. Previously, when Mr. A was in Yangzhou, he had borrowed ten thousand coins from Mr. B. During the chaos caused by the Cantonese rebels, Mr. A, his mother, and his wife fled to a certain place, and Mr. B also arrived there.

    848

    其时乙无一钱，乃从甲索所借钱，甲橐中尚有洋钱四十，欲许之，妻曰：“乱离如此，生死未卜，岂偿债时邪？”甲从其言而止，自后亦不复见。至是乃问之，曰：“与尔别后，尔何所往？”妻作鬼语曰：“彼时吾与母同行，尔不还吾钱，母子二人饥饿不得食，努力至江边，贼已大至。江中虽有渡船，以吾无钱不肯载，乃负母循江岸而行，力竭仆地，呻吟三昼夜，母子俱毙。每念死时之苦，恨尔入骨，十数年来在扬州寻觅，竟不可得。后知在苏州，又在苏州寻觅数年矣。今得相逢，必偿我命！甲叩首谢曰：”曩者诚我负心，然吾夫妇死，尔母子仍不得活，于事无益；且我负尔钱，究未害尔命也。

    Explanation

    848

    At that time, B had no money and asked A for the borrowed money. A still had forty silver dollars in his bag and wanted to agree, but A's wife said, "The chaos and separation are so great, and life and death are uncertain. Is it a time to repay debts?" A followed her words and stopped, and afterward, they did not see each other again. At this point, A asked about it and said, "After we parted, where did you go?" A's wife spoke in ghostly language and said, "At that time, I was with my mother. You did not return my money. My mother and I were hungry and could not eat. We struggled to the riverbank, and the bandits had already arrived in large numbers. There were ferries in the river, but because I had no money, they would not take us. I carried my mother along the riverbank, exhausting ourselves and falling to the ground, moaning for three days and nights. Both my mother and I died. Every time I think of the suffering at the time of our deaths, I hate you to the core. For over ten years, I have been searching for you in Yangzhou, and for several years in Suzhou. Now that we have met again, I must take your life." A bowed and said, "In the past, I truly acted out of heartlessness, but if my wife and I die, your mother and child would still not survive, which would be of no benefit. And I owe you money, but ultimately, I did not harm your life."

    849

    我愿数倍曩日所借之钱，为尔作功德，凭仗佛力，托生善地，不亦美乎？“鬼始不可，既而曰：汝言亦有理，好为之。”甲于是出钱大作佛事，妻病果愈。中山君有言：“与不期众少，其于当厄；怨不期深浅，其于伤心。”甲与乙既素有通财之谊，危迫之际，虽无所负，犹宜分财以济之，况实负其钱乎？乃妇言是用，毛不拔，遂使母子流离，挤于沟壑，固宜其饮恨九原矣。虽仗佛力消释前愆，吾犹未保其卒也。赵某，苏州人，当兵乱时，甚踔城外无所依。有农人李姓，怜而留之，赵因与李女通。

    Explanation

    849

    I wish to repay several times the money borrowed in the past, to perform good deeds for you, relying on the power of the Buddha, to be reborn in a good place, isn't that beautiful? "The ghost initially refused, but then said: Your words also make sense, let's do it." Jia then spent money to perform large-scale Buddhist rituals, and his wife's illness was indeed cured. Duke Zhongshan once said: "Whether many or few, they are all in trouble; whether deep or shallow, they all cause heartache." Jia and Yi have always had a relationship of sharing wealth, in a time of crisis, even if there is no debt, one should still share wealth to help, how much more so if there is actual debt? But the wife's words were followed, and not a single hair was pulled out, thus causing mother and child to be displaced and pushed into the ditches, it is natural that they would harbor resentment in the underworld. Even though relying on the power of the Buddha to forgive past sins, I still cannot guarantee the final outcome. Zhao, a person from Suzhou, during times of military turmoil, leaped over the city walls with nowhere to rely on. There was a farmer named Li, out of pity, he kept him, Zhao then had an affair with Li's daughter.

    850

    李无如何，遂以为婿，赘于其家，夫妇颇相得。赵有季父，避兵宁波，使人访知赵所在，招使往。赵与妻谋曰：“吾季父颇有资，今来招我，我且先往。往而得所，再来迎汝，并迎翁也。”及赵至宁波，其季父议为娶某氏女，赵闻某女美，且装送甚丰，欣然从之。及乱定，遂依季父居，不复还苏。至光绪辛巳春，赵偶至门外眺望，有阴风吹面，通体生粟，乃入室蒙被而卧。夜半忽作谵语曰：“与汝别二十余年，绝不一念前情，致吾父抱愤而死，吾亦饮恨而亡。负心若此，吾今得请于神矣，汝尚望活邪？”言毕遂绝。

    Explanation

    850

    Li Wuhuo was then taken as a son-in-law and lived in his wife's family, and the couple got along well. Zhao had a younger uncle who fled to Ningbo to avoid the war. He sent someone to find Zhao and invited him to come. Zhao told his wife, "My younger uncle has considerable wealth. He has come to invite me, so I will go first. If I succeed, I will come back to get you and my father-in-law." When Zhao arrived in Ningbo, his younger uncle proposed marrying him to a certain family's daughter. Zhao heard that the girl was beautiful and that the dowry was very rich, so he happily agreed. After the war ended, Zhao settled with his younger uncle and did not return to Suzhou. In the spring of the XinSi year during the Guangxu era, Zhao went out to gaze at the scenery and suddenly felt a cold wind on his face, causing him to shiver all over. He then went back inside, covered himself with a blanket, and lay down. In the middle of the night, he suddenly spoke deliriously, saying, "It has been over twenty years since we parted, and I have not thought of our past once. This caused my father to die in anger, and I also died in anger. To be so unfaithful, I have now obtained permission from the gods. Do you still hope to live?" After speaking these words, he died.

    851

    扬州市井中，有王、陈二人共启一肆。肆中畜一猫，毛色甚美，两家皆奇爱之。猫一乳生四子，皆肖其母，逾月之后跳踉于地，见者每注目焉。邻有某甲乞其一，许之，以不能离母，故未将去。一日，陈他出，王倦而假寐，惟陈母、王妻坐守肆中。忽有少年突入，攫二小猫去，母妻皆大号，王惊起追之，某甲亦助之追。须臾，陈亦继至。少年见追者三人，知不能脱，弃一猫于途，甲抱之归。陈与王仍相逐不舍，少年窘甚，并所存一猫亦去之，二人抱猫俱返。而少年不知，犹以为有追者，狂奔不已，触一孕妇仆地，竟践其身上而过。于是市人大哗，要遮少年，使不得逸。视妇，已垂欲绝矣，觅其夫至，缚少年送官，如妇死，当论如律云。嗟乎！鲁以斗鸡而出昭公，郑以逐狗而杀子阳，小事而启大衅，微物而酿巨祸，自古有之矣。此犹其小焉者也。

    Explanation

    851

    In the bustling market area of Yangzhou, there were two people named Wang and Chen who jointly operated a shop. In the shop, they kept a cat whose fur was exceptionally beautiful, and both families loved it very much. The cat gave birth to four kittens, all resembling their mother. After a month, the kittens were leaping and playing on the ground, drawing the attention of everyone who saw them. One day, a young man suddenly entered the shop, grabbed two of the kittens, and ran off. The mother and wife both cried out loudly, and Wang, who had nodded off lightly, woke up and chased after the young man. Someone named Mr. A also helped chase him. Soon, Chen arrived as well. The young man, seeing that three people were chasing him, realized he could not escape, so he abandoned one kitten on the road, and Mr. A took it back. Chen and Wang continued to chase him relentlessly, and the young man, in great distress, ran off with the remaining kitten. The two men returned with the kitten. The young man, not knowing that they had stopped chasing, still thought they were after him, and he ran wildly, accidentally colliding with a pregnant woman and knocking her down. He even stepped on her body as he ran past. The crowd then surrounded and blocked the young man, preventing him from escaping. They saw that the woman was on the verge of death, and her husband was found and brought to the scene. The young man was tied up and taken to the authorities. If the woman died, he would be judged according to the law.

    Alas! The Duke of Lu was exiled due to a cockfight, and the ruler of Zheng was killed due to a dog chase. A small matter can lead to a major conflict, and a small thing can cause a great disaster. This has been the case since ancient times. This is still a minor example of such incidents.

    852

    某甲，湖北人，居扬州为鹾商，喜谈内外丹之术。偶与友饮于茶寮，见一老翁支布为棚，卖药其下，有一客与之谐价，未定。就视之，则一何首乌，宛然人形。问所值，钱四千耳。大喜，如数与之。友曰：“此必伪也，岂有何首乌已成人形者，而所值止此邪？”甲曰：“不然，仙人卖药，论有缘与否，不计值也。”抱之而归。是夕宿于花园，不入内室，次日视之，死矣。家人大骇，穷究其故，友以告。亟使人觅卖药翁，不知所之矣。古诗云：“服食求神仙，多为药所误。”诚哉是言也。

    Explanation

    852

    A certain person from Hubei, residing in Yangzhou as a salt merchant, enjoyed discussing the practices of internal and external alchemy. Once, while drinking with a friend at a teahouse, he saw an old man setting up a tent with cloth, selling medicine underneath. A customer was haggling with him, but the price had not yet been settled. Upon closer inspection, it was a He Shou Wu root that resembled a human form. He asked about its price, and it was four thousand coins. He was greatly pleased and paid the amount. His friend said, "This must be fake. How could there be a He Shou Wu that has become a human form, and it costs so little?" The person replied, "Not so. Immortals sell medicine, depending on whether there is a karmic connection, not counting the value." He carried it back. That night, he stayed in the garden, not entering the inner room. The next day, he saw it had died. His family was greatly alarmed and thoroughly investigated the cause. His friend informed them. They quickly sent someone to search for the medicine seller, but he had vanished. As the ancient poem says, "Seeking immortality through elixirs often leads to being misled by medicine." Truly, these words are accurate.

    853

    同治之初，滇中大乱，贼所到之处，杀人如麻，白骨盈野，通都大邑，悉成丘虚。乱定之后，孑遗之民稍稍复集，扫除骼，经营苫盖，时则又有大疫。疫之将作，其家之鼠无故自毙，或在墙壁中，或在承尘上，人不及见，久而腐烂，人闻其臭，鲜不病者。病皆骤然而起，身上先坟起一小块，坚硬如石，颜色微红，扪之极痛。旋身热谵语，或逾日死，或即日死，诸医束手不能处方。有以刀割去之者，然此处甫割，彼处复起，其得活者，千百中一二而已。方疫盛时，村民每于夜间见鬼火，数百数千，成队而行，近之则闻锣声、鼓声、铃铎声、吹角声、马蹄声、器械摩扌介声，月夜并见有旗帜之象。又往往有人忽然倒地，如酣睡者，越日而苏，辄言有兵马经过，被其捉去搬送什物，至某处而返。

    Explanation

    853

    In the early years of the Tongzhi era, a major uprising broke out in Yunnan. Wherever the rebels went, they killed people like hemp, leaving fields full of bones. Major cities and towns were all turned into desolate ruins. After the chaos subsided, the few survivors began to return, cleaning up the bones and remains, and rebuilding the ruins. At this time, a major epidemic broke out. Before the epidemic began, rats in the households died without apparent cause, sometimes in the walls and sometimes on the ceilings. People did not see the dead rats, which then rotted over time. Anyone who smelled the rotting rats rarely escaped illness. The disease started with a hard, red, painful lump on the body. Soon after, the patient developed fever and delirium, and some died within a day, while others died within a few days. Doctors were unable to treat the disease. Some people tried to cut off the lumps, but even if one lump was cut off, another would appear elsewhere. Only a few out of many survived. During the height of the epidemic, villagers often saw ghostly lights at night. The lights appeared in groups and moved in formation. When people approached, they heard the sounds of drums, bells, horns, horse hooves, and metal clashing. During moonlit nights, people also saw the appearance of flags. Sometimes, people would suddenly fall to the ground as if sleeping deeply and then wake up after a day. When they woke up, they would say that they had been captured by soldiers and transported goods to a certain place before returning.

    854

    又或言令其荷送传牌，牌上大书“某官带兵若干，赴某处，仰沿途供应如律”。及数日之后，其所言某处某处，无不大疫矣。疫起乡间，延及城市，一家有病者，则其左右十数家即迁移避之，踣于道路者无算，然卒不能免也。甚至阖门同尽，比户皆空，小村聚中绝无人迹。老子云：“师之所处，荆棘生焉。”信矣！马星五观察驷良，南人，为余说如此，盖其所亲见也。观察又言：咸丰八九年间，李树往往不实，而反生王瓜；楸树本无实，而反结实如豆，好奇者收而藏之。厥后疫起，百药不效，或谓李瓜、楸豆，自古未闻，天地既生此异物，谅非无用，或可以治此异疾乎？试之果验。而当时收藏者少，甚不易得，得之者珍逾参苓矣。近年来久无此疾，而李与楸之生瓜豆亦不再见。于此又见天心仁爱，虽大劫之中，未始不寓挽回之意也。

    Explanation

    854

    Some say that they were ordered to carry and deliver an official edict, which prominently stated, "A certain official is leading a number of troops to a certain place, and it is ordered that they be supplied along the way according to law." After several days, the places they mentioned all suffered great epidemics. The epidemic began in the countryside and spread to the cities. If one family had a sick person, then their neighbors, numbering in the dozens, would move away to avoid it. Countless people fell ill and died on the roads, but ultimately they could not escape it. Even to the point where entire families perished, and every household was left empty, small villages became completely deserted. The Dao De Jing says, "Where armies have been, thorns and thistles grow." Indeed! Magistrate Ma Xingwu, from Yunnan, told me this, as he personally witnessed it. The magistrate also said: During the years 1858–1859 of the reign of Emperor Xianfeng, pear trees often did not bear fruit, but instead grew melons; catalpa trees, which normally do not bear fruit, instead produced seeds like beans. Curious people collected and preserved them. Later, an epidemic broke out, and all medicines were ineffective. Someone suggested that these pear melons and catalpa beans, which had never been heard of before, might be useful. Since heaven and earth have produced these strange things, they must have a purpose, perhaps they could cure this strange disease? When tested, it proved effective. But at the time, there were few who collected them, making them very hard to obtain. Those who obtained them valued them more than ginseng or lingzhi mushrooms. In recent years, this disease has not occurred for a long time, and pear trees and catalpa trees no longer produce melons or beans. This shows the benevolence of heaven, even in great disasters, there is always a hint of redemption.

    855

    叶某，吴人，官于中州。有三子，皆在十龄内外，年相若也。一日，戏于庭中，其仲子手执一铁钉，谓季子曰：“吾此钉一发，即成弩箭，且中汝目矣。”季子曰：“兄试为之。”仲子一举手，钉即飞去，仲季二子相去颇远，中间又隔一人，而钉竟从此人腋下穿过，飞集季子之目。目睛即时突出，大如鸡卵，五色烂然。幸其系未绝，累然下垂且四五寸。季子走还内室，投其母；母大惊，以手握其睛，纳入眶中。季子初不觉痛，至是始大痛而倒，良久乃苏，后幸无恙，而此目则盲矣。方扰攘时，忽失仲子所在，大索之，则在一神庙中，厥角稽首，求其弟不死。扶之还，色若死灰，额上血漉漉然。其母不忍责，转以好言慰之。此殆有鬼神使之然乎？

    Explanation

    855

    A Man from Wu

    A man from Wu, serving as an official in the Central Plains, had three sons, all around ten years old, and of similar age. One day, they were playing in the courtyard. The second son, holding a nail in his hand, told the youngest son, "If I shoot this nail, it will become a crossbow bolt and hit your eye." The youngest son replied, "Brother, try it." The second son raised his hand, and the nail flew away. The two younger sons were quite far apart, with another person in between, but the nail actually passed through this person's armpit and stuck in the youngest son's eye. The eyeball immediately popped out, as large as a chicken's egg, brightly colored. Fortunately, the connection was not broken, and it hung down about four or five inches. The youngest son ran back to the inner room and threw himself into his mother's arms. His mother was greatly shocked and held the eyeball with her hand, putting it back into the socket. At first, he didn't feel pain, but then he started feeling intense pain and fell down. After a long time, he regained consciousness, fortunately unharmed afterward, but this eye became blind. While everyone was in a state of confusion, the second son suddenly disappeared. They searched for him thoroughly and found him in a temple, kneeling with his head bowed, praying to protect his younger brother from dying. They helped him back, and he looked ashen, with blood dripping from his forehead. His mother could not bring herself to scold him and comforted him with kind words. This is probably due to the intervention of ghosts and spirits.

    856

    潘氏，徽州大姓也。其族人检视谱牒，知乾隆间族中有一妇殁于杭州，即浅葬于城外，今绝无后矣。而此妇生前颇有恩德及其族人，于是举族聚谋，欲归其骨，合葬于其夫之墓。乃共醵金，使一人往。其人于光绪七年四月至杭州，依谱牒所载，营求其葬处，果得之。发视，则其棺乃楠木，所为尚完好，而其底则已朽矣。尸卧棺中，容色如生，衣服亦未坏。视其棺和所题识，盖已一百二十八年，而俨然如新死者。遂舁而置之新制之棺。有与其役者，见尸右手中握一小盒，封缄甚固，乃窃而怀之，莫知何物。或曰：“其中有宝珠，尸之不坏，职是故也。”吴下有邵氏旧仆，实从之往，亲见其事，归为奴辈言之如此。

    Explanation

    856

    Pan was a prominent family in Huizhou. One of their clan members examined the genealogical records and discovered that during the Qianlong era, there was a woman in the family who died in Hangzhou and was buried shallowly outside the city. She had no descendants. However, she had shown great kindness and virtue to her family. Therefore, the entire clan gathered to discuss and decided to bring her remains back and bury them together with her husband's tomb. They collected money and sent one person to Hangzhou. This person arrived in Hangzhou in the fourth month of the seventh year of Emperor Guangxu's reign, followed the records in the genealogy, and successfully found the burial site. When they opened the tomb, they found that the coffin was made of camphor wood, which was still in good condition, but the bottom had rotted. The body was lying in the coffin, with a natural appearance as if it were newly deceased, and her clothes were also undamaged. There was someone who saw that the deceased's right hand held a small box, which was sealed very tightly. This person secretly took the box, not knowing what was inside. Some said, "There must be a treasure inside the box, which is why the body remains uncorrupted." A servant from Wu actually accompanied them and personally witnessed the event, and he told the story to others upon his return.

    857

    李石泉孝廉，钱唐人，以道光丁酉科举于乡，余同岁生也。初名人杰。未几，湖北有反者名钟人杰，石泉不欲与同名，乃改名峨。或曰：“峨字，山在我上，以山压我，岂得为安？不易此名，必大不利！”石泉不从其言，逾年竟卒。余按《太平广记》引《感定录》曰：有进士李岳，连举不第，夜梦人谓曰：“头上有山，何以得上第？”及觉，不可名岳，遂更名言，乃中第。此与石泉事正相类，或人所言，不为无见也。

    Explanation

    857

    Li Shi Quan, a scholar-official from Qiantang, passed the provincial examination in the Ding You year of the Daoguang era and was of the same age cohort as I. Initially, he was named Ren Jie. Shortly thereafter, there was a rebel named Zhong Ren Jie in Hubei, and Li Shi Quan did not want to share a name with him, so he changed his name to E. Someone said, "The character 'E' (峨) means 'mountain above me,' which presses down on me. How can this be safe? If you do not change this name, it will surely bring great misfortune!" Li Shi Quan did not heed this advice and died the following year. I consulted Taiping Guangji, which cites Gand Ding Lu and says: There was an imperial scholar named Li Yue who failed to pass the examinations multiple times. In a dream, someone told him, "With a mountain on your head, how can you achieve the top rank?" Upon waking, he could no longer be named Yue, so he changed his name to Yan and then passed the examination. This is exactly similar to Li Shi Quan's story, and the advice given is not without insight.

    858

    《通俗文》曰：“梦语谓之[C204]（引见《一切经音义》），字亦作呓。《拾遗记》云吕蒙呓语通《周易》，是也。今人亦往往有然。湖北有一妇，不见容于夫，夫外出，辄弃妇不顾。妇食贫自守，无他志。夫偶归，与之宿，闻妇呓语，皆处分家事，勤勤恳恳，夫大感动，曰：”谚云柴米夫妻，信矣！“遂相爱重如初。此亦至诚动物之一证也。又有一贵家妾，平日事其大妇，谬为恭敬，夫或于无人处抚问之，曰：”夫人遇汝何若？“则谨对曰：”甚善！“然其梦中，呓呻呼，辄若见督责于大妇，恐怖乞怜，觳觫万状。夫呼问之，笑曰：”无他，呓语耳！“固问之，终不言。夫叹曰：”积威所劫，一至此乎？“由是浸疏其妻，实则妾之呓语，皆伪为之也。此视骊姬之夜半而泣，用意更阴险矣。其妻竟以此郁郁成疾而死，临死谓侍者曰：”吾死，终不令此妾得志。“未几，妾亦死。

    Explanation

    858

    According to the Shuowen Jiezi, dream speech is referred to as [C204] (引见《一切经音义》), which can also be written as "呓" (yì). The Shiji records that Lu Meng's dream speech was related to the I Ching. This phenomenon is not uncommon in modern times. In Hubei, there was a woman who was mistreated by her husband. When he went out, he would abandon her. She lived frugally and had no other aspirations. One day, her husband returned home and spent the night with her. He heard her speaking in her sleep, discussing household matters with diligence and care. He was deeply moved and said, "The proverb says, 'Chai and Mi are a couple,' indeed!" Thus, they fell in love and respected each other as before. This is another example of how sincerity can move things. Another story involves a concubine from a noble family who, during the day, pretended to be respectful to her senior wife. Her husband sometimes asked her privately, "How does the senior wife treat you?" She would reply, "Very well!" However, in her dreams, she would cry out as if being scolded by the senior wife, filled with fear and begging for mercy, trembling uncontrollably. When her husband called out to her, she smiled and said, "Nothing, just dream talk!" He persisted in asking, but she never revealed the truth. He sighed, "Has the accumulated power of the senior wife overwhelmed you to this extent?" As a result, he gradually grew distant from his wife. In reality, the concubine's dream speech was all false. This is even more cunning than Lady Li Ji's weeping at midnight. Her senior wife eventually died of sorrow and illness, saying to her attendants, "Even if I die, I will not let this concubine achieve her goals." Not long after, the concubine also died.

    859

    光绪辛巳岁四月，苏州西米巷民家，有母彘产一豚，一首、二身，二尾、八足。其家以为不祥，毙而投之水。群モ取食之，亦无他异。按《明史》，天启四年，榆林生豕，一首、二身、二尾、八足，事与此类，在五行家所谓豕祸也。纪文达自言于汉儒之学，最不信五行传说，余亦云然。夫孔子作《春秋》，未尝不纪异，然必如刘向、董仲舒诸儒之说，以某异为某事之应，则不能无附会，亦不能无牾。《明史。五行志》但纪祥异，不载事应，所见卓矣。

    Explanation

    859

    In the Xinsi year of the Guangxu era, in April, a household in Ximi Lane, Suzhou, had a sow give birth to a piglet with one head, two bodies, two tails, and eight legs. The family considered this inauspicious and killed the piglet, throwing it into the water. The dogs took it to eat, and there were no other unusual events. According to the History of Ming, in the fourth year of the Tianqi era, a pig was born in Yulin with the same unusual characteristics, and the matter is similar to this, which the Five Elements school calls a "pig disaster." Ji Wen Da, in his study of Han Confucianism, is most skeptical of the Five Elements legends, and I agree with him. Confucius, in writing the Spring and Autumn Annals, did not fail to record unusual events, but if one follows the sayings of scholars like Liu Xiang and Dong Zhongshu, interpreting a particular unusual event as a response to a particular event, one cannot avoid being speculative and contradictory. The History of Ming: Five Elements Chapter records only auspicious and unusual events without recording the corresponding events, which is a very insightful view.

    860

    张氏子，楚人，生而蕴藉，父母奇爱之。年十六七，忽有神附其身，言语不伦，歌哭无度。平时婉娈如处女，至是则距跃曲踊，捷于猿猱，百尺之树可登其巅，视隆楼杰阁，犹几案也。楚俗，为神所附者，即使为巫，乡里皆信奉之，所获颇丰。而其父母雅不欲为此，力请于神，曰：“吾子眇小，不足奉大神，愿神舍旃。”神不许。乃邀集其族姻之长老，罗拜于前，请益哀。神附其子，言曰：“既众人苦求，吾即去矣。然此子不永年，吾在能护持之，吾去恐不免耳。”言已果去。其子逾年而卒，众以神言为信，然安知非此子已精神越渫，故邪得乘之邪？其子言神初降时，见有若猴ㄑ者。疑为猴精，故し捷若斯也。

    860
    Chinese原文 English翻译 翻译备注
    张氏子，楚人，生而蕴藉，父母奇爱之。 Zhang's son, a native of Chu, was refined and well-mannered from birth, and his parents loved him dearly. "蕴藉" is translated as "refined and well-mannered" to capture the sense of being cultivated and gentle.
    年十六七，忽有神附其身，言语不伦，歌哭无度。 At the age of sixteen or seventeen, he suddenly became possessed by a spirit, speaking incoherently and weeping or singing without restraint. "言语不伦" is translated as "speaking incoherently" to convey the erratic nature of his speech.
    平时婉娈如处女，至是则距跃曲踊，捷于猿猱，百尺之树可登其巅，视隆楼杰阁，犹几案也。 Normally gentle and demure like a maiden, he then began to jump and dance wildly, swift as a monkey, climbing the top of a hundred-foot tree, and seeing tall buildings as if they were tables. "婉娈如处女" is translated as "gentle and demure like a maiden" to emphasize his usual demeanor.
    楚俗，为神所附者，即使为巫，乡里皆信奉之，所获颇丰。 In Chu, those possessed by a spirit were made into mediums, and the villagers all believed and revered them, gaining a lot. "巫" is translated as "medium" to reflect the role of a spiritual intermediary.
    而其父母雅不欲为此，力请于神，曰：“吾子眇小，不足奉大神，愿神舍旃。” But his parents did not wish for him to become a medium, and they earnestly pleaded with the spirit, saying, "Our son is small and insignificant, not worthy to serve the great spirit; we hope the spirit will leave." "雅不欲为此" is translated as "did not wish for him to become a medium" to convey their reluctance.
    神不许。乃邀集其族姻之长老，罗拜于前，请益哀。 The spirit refused. They then invited the elders of his family and relatives, and they prostrated themselves before the spirit, pleading even more earnestly. "罗拜于前" is translated as "prostrated themselves before the spirit" to show their deep respect and plea.
    神附其子，言曰：“既众人苦求，吾即去矣。然此子不永年，吾在能护持之，吾去恐不免耳。” The spirit possessed his son and said, "Since everyone has been earnestly requesting, I will leave. But this child will not live long; I can protect him while I am here, but I fear he will not escape death when I leave." "既众人苦求" is translated as "Since everyone has been earnestly requesting" to convey the collective effort.
    言已果去。其子逾年而卒，众以神言为信，然安知非此子已精神越渫，故邪得乘之邪？ After speaking, the spirit indeed left. His son died after a year, and the people believed the spirit's words, but how can we know that this child's spirit had not been exhausted, allowing the evil spirit to take advantage of it? "精神越渫" is translated as "spirit had not been exhausted" to suggest a possible alternative explanation for the possession.
    其子言神初降时，见有若猴ㄑ者。疑为猴精，故し捷若斯也。 His son said that when the spirit first descended, he saw something that looked like a monkey. He suspected it was a monkey spirit, which is why he was so agile. "猴ㄑ" is translated as "monkey" to maintain the imagery of the spirit's form.

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and natural in English, capturing the nuances and cultural context of the Classical Chinese source.

    861

    楚人有娶新妇者，甫导引入房，将行合卺之礼，新妇忽发狂，号呼欢呶，癸瞿奔触。举家皇皇，莫知为计，不得已，迓其父至。新妇见之，则厉声曰：“汝来邪？”父察之，盖其兄之声也。先是，其父有兄早死，以二子为托，父乾没其资，而虐遇兄子，毙其一人，至是其兄之鬼附新妇之体，历数其罪，父俯首不能出一语。夫氏之人乃责之曰：“此女既归我家，则我家人也，岂复与尔家事？且此女在室时，尔何不附之而言？今乃牿乱人家嘉礼，无乃不可欤！”鬼曰：“此女在室，我附之而言，谁其闻之？今两家族姻咸在，是非曲直必有公论，我故不辞搪突，一抒愤懑，幸诸长老哀怜，为我善处之。”众谓其父曰：“汝诚无状，今尚何言？”乃使悉反其所侵蚀，且召其兄子至，推择族中一长者，俾鞠育之，以待其成立。鬼又附新妇，叩首致谢，遂去。后亦无他。然其父自此不齿于乡里矣。

    Explanation

    861

    A Man from Chu Marries a Bride

    A man from Chu was about to marry a bride. Just as he was leading her into the bridal chamber to perform the union ritual, the bride suddenly went mad, shouting and making loud noises, rushing and colliding. The entire family was in a state of panic, not knowing what to do. They had no choice but to bring the bride's father to the scene. When the bride saw him, she spoke harshly: "You have come?" The father realized that it was the voice of his older brother. Previously, the father had an older brother who died early, and he entrusted him with two sons. The father embezzled their assets and mistreated the older brother's son, killing one of them. At this point, the spirit of the older brother possessed the bride's body, listing the father's crimes. The father bowed his head and could not say a word. The family of the groom then blamed him, saying: "This woman has already come to our home, so she is part of our family. How can we involve ourselves in your family's affairs? And when this woman was still a maiden, why didn't you possess her and speak through her? Now you are disrupting our family's good ceremony, isn't that unacceptable?" The spirit replied: "When this woman was still a maiden, I possessed her and spoke through her, but who would have heard it? Now both families and relatives are present, there must be a fair judgment. I therefore do not hesitate to be impolite, to vent my anger. I hope the elders will pity me and handle the situation well." The crowd told the bride's father: "You are indeed unreasonable. What can you say now?" They then made him return everything he had embezzled and summoned the older brother's son, choosing an elder in the family to raise him until he grew up. The spirit again possessed the bride, bowed his head, expressed gratitude, and then left. There were no further incidents. However, from then on, the father was no longer respected in the village.

    862

    湖北江夏县民祝某，于兵乱时娶一妇，江西人，嫠也。然此妇自归祝氏，甚爱敬其夫，善事其舅姑，先后宛若，女公女妹无间言。又甚勤恁，凡女事虽素所不习，必勉为之，一家皆喜，以为得贤妇，不以其再嫁而轻之也。居数载，忽得暴疾，有鬼凭之而言，责其不义，察之，乃其故夫也。祝氏之人，环而祷曰：“乱离如此，君又早世，茕茕刍孀，将可倚赖？其不能守，非其罪也。”鬼曰：“不然，吾未死时，固尝问之，能为吾守否？彼自誓不嫁，奉吾老母。吾骨未寒，而遽背之，致吾老母流离道路，死于饥寒，吾在九原恨入骨髓。今得相逢，必取其命。尔家若哀怜此妇，为治后事可矣。忽召医巫，徒费无益也。”妇病数日竟死。此妇在祝氏甚贤，度其事故夫必无失礼，乃以负其诺责，遂膺忄替之诛。传曰：“食言者不病”，可为倍死忘生者戒矣！

    Explanation

    862

    A man from Jiangxia County in Hubei Province, named Zhu, married a woman from Jiangxi Province during a time of war. She was a widow. However, this woman, upon joining the Zhu family, deeply loved and respected her husband, and she treated his parents-in-law with great care, just as if they were her own. Her relationship with her brother-in-laws' wives was harmonious, with no complaints or criticisms. She was also very diligent, and although she had never practiced many of the tasks typically performed by women, she would always do them with effort. The entire family was pleased, considering her a virtuous wife, and they did not look down on her because she had remarried.

    After living together for several years, she suddenly fell gravely ill. A ghost possessed her and spoke, accusing her of being unrighteous. Upon investigation, it turned out to be her former husband. The people of the Zhu family gathered around and prayed, saying, "In such a time of chaos, you have passed away, leaving this lonely and destitute widow. Who can she rely on? It is not her fault that she could not stay faithful." The ghost replied, "That is not true. When I was alive, I asked her if she could remain faithful to me. She swore she would not remarry and promised to take care of my elderly mother. Before my bones had even grown cold, she broke her promise, causing my mother to wander homeless and die of hunger and cold. I am filled with hatred in the underworld. Now that I have found her again, I will take her life. If your family feels pity for this woman, you can arrange her funeral. Do not waste your money on doctors and sorcerers, as it will be of no use." The woman fell ill for several days and eventually died. This woman was very virtuous in the Zhu family, and it is believed that her former husband had not acted improperly. She was punished for breaking her promise, and thus suffered the retribution of a broken vow. The classics say, "Those who break their word do not suffer," but this serves as a warning to those who would die or live without regard for their promises.

    863

    道光中，安徽太和县令某君幕客有能扶箕者，长夏无事，辄与诸友召箕仙，以诗歌唱和为乐。一日仙至，作诗云：“城郭人民异昔时，战场衰草尚离离。平生家国无穷恨，清颍亭边夜月知。”自署蘧蘧子，众莫知为谁。箕笔又书曰：“诸君不知有李栩乎？吾即栩也。”众曰：“敢问何时何地人？”箕曰：“旧事回思，至今心痛。诸君固好事者，不妨略言之。吾生于明万历之季，明代颍州隶凤阳府，吾乃颍州人也。吾父名精白，官山东巡抚。其时魏忠贤颛权，风示督抚，呈进祥瑞，而山东民间，适有牛产一犊，厥状诡异。吾父为僚友所惑，遂以麒麟生闻于朝，阁臣票旨有云‘厂臣修德而生仁兽’。海内忠义之士闻而恶之，吾父遂为清议所不齿。思陵登极，逆奄伏诛，诏定逆案，吾父与焉。吾诚私心痛之，因念《周书》有言：”尔尚盖前人之愆，惟忠惟孝。‘然则干父之蛊，惟忠孝而已。

    Explanation

    863

    During the reign of Emperor Daoguang, one of the county magistrates in Taihe, Anhui, had a retainer skilled in mediumship with a spirit board. During the long summer days when there was nothing to do, he would often invite his friends to summon a spirit from the board, and they would engage in poetic exchanges for amusement. One day, when the spirit arrived, it composed a poem: "The city walls and people are different from those of old; the battlefield is still covered with withered grass. All my life, I have harbored endless sorrow for my family and country, known only by the night moon at Qingyin Pavilion." The spirit signed itself as "Ququzi," and none of the guests knew who it was. The spirit then wrote on the board: "Do any of you know of Li Xu? I am Xu." The guests asked, "May we inquire about his time and place of birth?" The spirit replied, "Reflecting on these old matters, I still feel pain in my heart. Since you are all interested in such things, let me briefly tell you. I was born at the end of the Wanli era of the Ming dynasty. At that time, Yingzhou belonged to Fuyang Prefecture, and I was from Yingzhou. My father's name was Jingbai, and he served as the governor of Shandong. During that period, Wei Zhongxian held absolute power and instructed the governors and provincial administrators to present auspicious signs. Coincidentally, a strange calf was born in Shandong, and my father, influenced by his colleagues, reported it to the court as the birth of a qilin (a mythical unicorn). The cabinet ministers drafted an edict stating, 'The eunuchs' virtue has led to the birth of a benevolent creature.' The loyal and righteous scholars of the land were outraged, and my father was subsequently disapproved of by the public. After the enthronement of Emperor Siling, the eunuchs were executed, and the court issued a decree to compile the list of those implicated in the scandal, and my father was included in it. I truly feel sorrow for him, and I recall what the Zhou Book says: 'You should cover up the sins of your ancestors; only loyalty and filial piety.' Thus, correcting one's father's mistakes can only be done through loyalty and filial piety."

    864

    每读书见古忠臣烈士之事，未尝不慷慨太息也。崇祯八年，流贼陷颍州，吾念徒死无益，跳而免。走山东，招集旧时部属，得死士数百人，与之俱还，收复颍州。又与贼战茨河铺，大破之。又战焦陂集，禽贼首闯塌天，贼由是不敢犯颍。吾守颍凡八载，名震江淮，后因降贼袁时中复叛，吾出不意，为所戕害。以死报国，亦复何恨。万不料贼中有李信者，故杞县举人也，降贼之后，改名曰岩，力劝闯贼勿多杀人，以收人心。又造’迎闯王，不纳粮‘之谣，以荧惑人听，名列贤书，而甘为贼党，非人类矣。然彼为李信，吾为李栩，彼为河南开封府杞县人，吾为直隶凤阳府颍州人，若凤马牛不相及也。且吾自惭不学，少年时一纨子弟，好驰马试剑，博弈饮酒，间或与幕府诸客为歌诗，酒后耳热，拔剑狂歌，如此而已。又何尝折节读书，应场屋之试，登孝廉之科乎？乃举世以讹相传，竟谓吾即李信，数百年来牢不可破，皆言当时贼中之李信，即山东巡抚李某之子也。

    Explanation

    864

    Whenever I read about the deeds of ancient loyal ministers and valiant heroes, I could not help but sigh deeply with emotion. In the eighth year of the Chongzhen era, the rebel forces captured Yingzhou. I thought that dying in vain would be useless, so I jumped and escaped. I fled to Shandong, where I gathered my former subordinates and recruited several hundred loyal warriors. Together, we returned and recaptured Yingzhou. I then fought the rebels at Zihuapu and defeated them decisively. I fought again at Jiaobeiji and captured the rebel leader, Chuantatian, after which the rebels dared not attack Yingzhou again.
